小豆棚 - The Little Bean Trellis

Translation created with model: together-qwen3-14b-3step-fewshot / step-by-step-3step-fewshot

    Home
    1

    小豆棚 清 曾衍东

    传
    曾七如，名衍东，字青瞻，号七道士，山东嘉祥人。乾隆壬子举人，为楚北江夏令。诖误戍温。居郡西曾氏依绿园之旁，名其地曰“小西湖”。性落拓不羁。工诗及书画，笔墨狂放，大致以奇怪取胜。镌图章“摩古出奇”。自榜其门曰：“挂冠自昔曾骑虎，闭户于今好画龙。”慕郑板桥为人，常谓曰：“难得糊涂”。遇赦后，贫老不能归，卒于温。著有《小豆棚》八卷，《哑然集》一帙。尝作《元宵灯鼓图》，题云：“惊人岁月千挝鼓，老我乾坤百盏灯”。读之令人有“胜事长新，年华暗老”之感也。嘉庆丙子永嘉彭左海撰

    Explanation

    1

    The Small Bean Garden
    By Zeng Yandong, Qing Dynasty

    Zeng Qiru, also known as Yandong, with the courtesy name Qingzhan and the pen name Qi Daoist, was from Jiaxiang, Shandong. In the year of Renzi during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, he passed the imperial examination as a juren and served as the magistrate of Jiangxia in Chu North. He was exiled to Wenzhou due to a mistaken charge. He lived west of the city, near the Zeng family's Yilv Garden, and named the place "Little West Lake." By nature, he was unrestrained and unbound by convention. He was skilled in poetry, calligraphy, and painting, with a bold and unrestrained style that generally won admiration for its strangeness. He engraved seals with the inscription "Mogu Chuoqi" (Mimic the Ancient and Create the Novel). He inscribed his gate with the words: "In the past, I once rode a tiger; now, I close my door and enjoy painting dragons." He admired Zheng Banqiao's character and often said, "It's hard to be oblivious." After being pardoned, he was poor and old, unable to return home, and died in Wenzhou. He wrote the Eight Volumes of "The Small Bean Garden" and one collection titled "Yaran Ji." He once painted a "Lantern and Drum Figure for the Lantern Festival," with the inscription: "Thousand drum beats shock the passing years; a hundred lamps illuminate my old world." Reading it evokes the feeling of "good things grow new, the years darken old." This was written in the year of Bingzi during the reign of Emperor Jiaqing by Peng Zuo Hai of Yongjia.

    2

    余家有《豆棚闲话》一编，爱其自出机杼，成一家言，暇时尝玩适之。阅数年，客有谈及曾七如居士所撰《小豆棚闲话》，其义类颇相似，亦即取前书“豆棚“之名而名之矣。七如系东鲁嘉祥人，工诗文及书画，尤精古篆，笔墨豪放不羁。由乾隆壬子举人任楚北江夏令，诖吏议戍温。每因行踪所至，见夫山川古迹、人事物类，或取一二野史家钞本剩录及座客谈论，博采旁搜，辑成一部，十余万言，奇奇怪怪，若无关于世故者。

    Explanation

    2

    At my home, I have a collection of Dou Peng Xian Hua. I love it for its originality and the fact that it forms its own school of thought, and I often read and enjoy it in my leisure time. After several years, a guest mentioned Xiao Dou Peng Xian Hua, written by Master Zeng Qiru, saying that its meaning and style are quite similar. It also takes the name "Dou Peng" from the earlier book. Zeng Qiru is from Jiaxiang in Donglu (eastern Lu). He is skilled in poetry, prose, calligraphy, and painting, especially ancient seal script, and his brushwork is unrestrained and free-spirited. He was a juren (provincial examination graduate) from the year Renzi during the Qianlong era and served as the magistrate of Jiangxia in northern Chu. He was implicated in a case and exiled to Wen. Whenever he traveled, he encountered mountains, rivers, ancient sites, and various people and things, and sometimes took notes from a few wild histories, family copies, and conversations with guests. He gathered and compiled these into a book, which consists of over ten thousand words, full of strange and unusual stories, as if they have nothing to do with worldly affairs.

    3

    其自目为“闲书”，意在此欤？余从友人处借读一过，觉众妙毕具，层见叠出，以为得未曾有。然原本随得随录，意义尚烦寻绎。因为之分门别类，诠次成帙，计十六卷。大而忠孝节义之经，次而善恶果报之理，常而艺文珍宝，变而神鬼仙狐，以及山川风土、鸟兽虫鱼、诗词杂记诸凡备载。虽曰“闲书”，而无不可于花晨月夕展玩流连，可以助谈笑，可以长识见，并可以寓劝惩。较《豆栅闲话》更觉取精用宏，安得以“闲书’目之乎！余获是书，不敢秘诸所有，亟为校雠付梓，公诸同好。他日腾贵鸡林，亦未可知。博古君子，可藉以略识七如氏之梗概矣。光绪六年岁次庚辰孟春月项震新东垣谨识。

    Explanation

    3

    He (or they) call it "idle book," does this mean that? I borrowed it from a friend and read it through, and found that all wonders are present, recurring and layered occurrences, which I consider to be unprecedented. However, the original text was recorded as it was obtained, and its meaning still requires careful interpretation. Therefore, I classified and compiled it into volumes, totaling sixteen. The book covers large themes such as the classics of loyalty, filial piety, righteousness, and integrity, followed by the principles of good and evil retribution, common topics like arts, literature, and treasures, and changing subjects like gods, ghosts, immortals, and foxes, as well as landscapes, local customs, birds, beasts, insects, fish, poetry, and miscellaneous records—all are fully documented. Although it is called "idle book," it is not without its uses for flower-filled mornings and moonlit evenings, where it can aid conversation, expand knowledge, and also convey moral lessons. Compared to "Bean Fence Idle Talk," it seems to be more refined and comprehensive, how can it be called an "idle book"? I obtained this book and did not dare to keep it to myself; I quickly corrected and printed it, publishing it for the enjoyment of like-minded people. In the future, it may also rise in value in Chicken Forest (Korea). Scholarly gentlemen can use it to briefly understand the outline of the Seven Li Family. Respectfully recorded in the first month of spring in the year of Gengchen during the sixth year of Guangxu, by Xiang Zhen of Dongyuan.

    4

    《小豆棚》，闲书也；我，忙人也。作此等书，必其人闲、其所遭之时闲、其所处之境闲，而后能以闲心情为闲笔墨。我为秀才忙举业，为穷汉、为幕、为客忙衣食，那得工夫闲暇，作一部十余万言的闲书？即偶有闲时候、闲境地，又焉能忙里偷闲，向百忙中草草干这闲事？然则我何以有是书？我问之我，我亦不解。我平日好听人讲些闲话，或于行旅时见山川古迹、人事怪异，忙中记取；又或于一二野史家抄本剩录，亦无不于忙中翻弄。

    Explanation

    4

    《小豆棚》, an idle book; I, a busy person. To write such a book, one must be idle, the time one encounters must be idle, and the place one finds oneself must be idle, only then can one write with a leisurely mood and idle words. As a scholar, I am busy with the imperial examinations; as a poor man, an official, or a guest, I am busy with food and clothing. How could I have the time to be idle and write a book of over a hundred thousand words? Even if I occasionally have some idle time or idle surroundings, how could I find time to relax amidst the bustle and hastily do this idle thing? Then, how did I come to have this book? I ask myself, and I do not understand. I enjoy listening to idle talk from others, and during my travels, I might see ancient sites and unusual human events, jotting them down amidst my busy schedule; or I might occasionally flip through some leftover copies of wild history books, and I do this amidst my busy life as well.

    5

    且当车马倥偬，儿女嘈杂之下，信笔直书。无论忙之极忙，转觉闲而且闲。盖能用忙中之闲，而闲乃自忙中化出；无他，贵心闲耳。心一闲，则无往不得其闲。将所有诸般贪、嗔、爱、恶、欲，种种不可思议，而我心闲闲，不与之逐而与之适；把那些闲情、闲话、闲事、闲人，竟成一部闲书于我这忙人之手。或有谀余者曰：“七如，闲人也，闲者而后乐此。”余唯唯否吾。或有诮余者曰：“七如闲乎哉？夫我则不暇。”余亦唯唯否否。乾隆六十年岁在乙卯九月曾衍东七如氏书。

    Explanation

    5

    Under the chaos of carriages and horses, amidst the noise of children, I write freely. No matter how busy, one feels increasingly idle. It is because one can use the leisure in the midst of busyness, and the leisure arises from the busyness itself. There is no other reason, it is precious to have a leisurely mind. Once the mind is leisurely, then everywhere one goes, one finds leisure. All the various desires, anger, love, hatred, and lust, all the incomprehensible things, my mind remains leisurely, not chasing after them but adapting to them; those idle feelings, idle words, idle matters, idle people, are transformed into an idle book in the hands of a busy person. Someone might flatter me by saying, "Qiru, you are an idle person, and only an idle person can enjoy this." I nod and shake my head, disagreeing. Someone might mock me by saying, "Is Qiru really idle? I have no time." I also nod and shake my head, disagreeing. In the year 1795, during the lunar year of the Rabbit (乙卯), in the month of September, by Zeng Yandong, pen name Qiru.

    6

    卷一 忠孝部(节烈、贞女附)
    颜氏忠孝录
    颜公衍绍，复圣六十五世裔。后曲阜，少孤，读书攻苦。举崇桢进士，出知凤阳令，有能声。会流寇横行江淮，公练兵，浚隍城，为战守计。贼知有备，不敢逼。已而内召，将入都，适上遣宦者杨显名监鹾政，议行属礼。公厉声曰：‘何议为？宁不做官，不失我身。议则终当屈膝耳。”遂束装北京。累试，当改官翰林。
    时淮安陈启新给事吏垣，欲交结公。公以其大言，舆榇上封事，又矫着布絮见上，公曰：“此罔上者，又沽名，小人也。”屡谒公，不报。陈怒，遂劾选擢诸臣，多大吏私人。率罢归。公左迁广平府经历。

    Explanation

    9

    Chapter Sixteen: Miscellaneous Notes

    Zheng Banqiao, Zhu Gao'an, Yuan Shuofu, Jian Wang, Liu Xiaolian, Xiao Huangliang, Lü Gongzi, Shao Siyao, Shao Shangmei, Jia Xiucai, Mài Cài Lǎo Lǎo, Li Fu, Zhang Zhaofu, Yě Sì Sù, Shēngyuán Yǒu, Shāng Lǚ Fū, Nán Zhōng Xínglǚ Jì, Hàn Bá Biàn, Shù Yì

    Appendix: Record of a Gift of a White Banana Stone Inkstone, Segment on the Dangerous Point, Note on the Proofreading

    Chapter One: Loyalty and Filial Piety (with Appendices on Chastity and Virtuous Women)
    Record of the Loyalty and Filial Piety of the Yan Family

    Yan Gong Yanshao, the sixty-fifth descendant of the Second Sage, was born in Qufu. Orphaned at a young age, he devoted himself to studying with great diligence. He passed the imperial examination during the reign of Emperor Chongzhen and was appointed as the magistrate of Fuyang, where he earned a reputation for his ability. During the time when rebel forces were rampant in the Jianghuai region, the official trained his troops and fortified the city walls, preparing for both defense and attack. The rebels, knowing that the city was well-prepared, dared not approach. Soon after, he was summoned to the capital. Just as he was about to enter the capital, the emperor sent a eunuch named Yang Xianming to oversee the salt administration and proposed a ceremony of submission. The official sternly replied, "Why discuss it? Rather than holding office, I will not lose my integrity. If I were to submit, I would ultimately have to kneel." He then packed his belongings and traveled to Beijing. After several attempts, he was promoted to a position in the Hanlin Academy.

    At that time, Chen Qixin, a censor from Huai'an, sought to befriend the official. However, the official disapproved of his grandiose claims. Chen had a coffin carried to present a memorial to the emperor and wore coarse clothing made of hemp and cotton to see him. The official remarked, "This man is deceitful and seeks fame, making him a small person." Despite repeated visits by Chen, the official did not respond. Chen became angry and subsequently impeached several officials who had been promoted, many of whom were close associates of high-ranking officials. As a result, the official was demoted to the position of magistrate in Guangping Prefecture.

    10

    是时，王师入关，所向皆摧。邯郸直其冲，吏部请以习兵事者，公前守御江淮，故补邯郸。城庳薄，势在旦夕。公驰就谯门，到任部署。日夜募得乡勇者千人。邑人张执塘统之。塘，故兵校也。勉以大义，咸勇跃思奋。开公帑给军，守者不可。公曰：“此城失，皆非我有也。”公犒千金。有两士夜缒欲遁，逻得之。诸生多为丐免。公曰：“吾治军，当行军法。”即拔佩刀，斩二人。人心肃然。三日，兵薄城，不下，解去。时各城失守，村堡被焚。执塘寻获数人至县。嗔目曰：“吾高总兵部兵也。”公曰：“吾治焚劫吾民者。”鞭之，极刑，列其罪状。太监高起潜怒。适部将侯拱极败绩，起潜劾公阻挠，冀卸其罪以归公。抚按皆力为辩，始从薄罚，镌三级守城之功，不叙。

    Explanation

    10

    At this time, the imperial army entered the pass, crushing all that was encountered. Handan was directly in the path, and the Ministry of Personnel requested someone experienced in military affairs. The official, who had previously guarded the Yangtze and Huai rivers, was therefore appointed to Handan. The city was low and thin, and the situation was precarious, ready to collapse at any moment. The official rushed to the city gate and made arrangements upon arriving. Day and night, he recruited a thousand local volunteers. A local man named Zhang Zhitang led them. Zhang was formerly a military officer. He inspired them with great principles, and they all became brave and eager to fight. He opened the public treasury to fund the troops, but the guard objected. The official said, "If this city falls, it will not be mine." He rewarded them with a thousand gold pieces. Two soldiers tried to escape by lowering themselves down at night, and they were caught by the patrol. Many students begged for their pardon, but the official said, "In managing the army, military law must be enforced." He immediately drew his sword and executed the two. The hearts of the people were filled with awe. After three days, the enemy surrounded the city but could not take it and withdrew. At that time, other cities had fallen, and villages and fortresses were burned. Zhang Zhitang captured several people and brought them to the county. He glared and said, "I am a soldier under the Grand Commander of the Ministry of War." The official said, "I will punish those who burned and looted my people." He whipped them, subjected them to the harshest punishment, and listed their crimes. The eunuch Gao Qiqian was angry. Just as the division commander Hou Gongji suffered a defeat, Gao Qiqian accused the official of obstructing, hoping to shift the blame onto him. The governor and the censor-general vigorously defended him. Finally, he received a mild punishment, his rank was reduced by three levels, and his defense of the city was not recorded.

    11

    将告归，西山盗发。受命迁真定府同知，往捕之。贼曰：“颜邯郸安在？”公跃马而出曰：“汝欲识颜公耶？”贼望见，投戈罗拜，曰：“我辈恨不为邯郸民。公至，自能活我。”皆乞降，盗悉平。时公冢子伯璟、次子伯玠，皆家居，三子伯珣随任，甫六岁。壬午，公知河间府。闰十一月，王师再入关，攻河间。城急，公纵火焚其梯，反风吹火，烧延楼橹。公知势不可支，趋署，令诸仆拒门守，乃集家人一室中，积薪纵火，火烈，公衣冠北面，再拜，跃入自焚。仆吕有年冒焰负公季子出。上闻嘉悼不已，敕予优恤。

    Explanation

    11

    He was about to report back when bandits from the Western Mountains arose. He was then ordered to transfer to the position of Deputy Governor of Zhen Ding Prefecture and was tasked with capturing them. The bandits asked, "Where is Official Yan Handan?" The official leaped onto his horse and said, "Do you want to meet Official Yan?" Upon seeing him, the bandits threw down their weapons and prostrated themselves, saying, "We regret not being citizens of Handan. When you arrived, you saved us." All surrendered, and the bandits were completely pacified. At that time, the official's eldest son, Bo Jing, and second son, Bo Jie, were both at home, while his third son, Bo Xun, accompanied him in his duties and was just six years old. In the year Ren Wu, the official became the Governor of Hejian Prefecture. In the leap month of the eleventh lunar month, the royal army entered the pass again and attacked Hejian. The city was under siege, and the official set fire to the ladders, but the wind blew back, causing the fire to spread to the watchtowers. Knowing that the situation was hopeless, the official rushed to the office, ordered the servants to guard the door, and then gathered his family in one room, piling up firewood and setting it ablaze. The fire raged, and the official, dressed in formal attire, faced north, bowed twice, and leaped into the flames to commit suicide. The servant Lu You Nian,冒着火焰, carried the official's youngest son out. The emperor heard of this and mourned continuously, issuing orders for generous condolences.

    12

    初，公有幕客严柏令，善察休咎。及之河间，密言此城不可居。公佯不省，阴使人护之出，柏令挥涕去。又，公赴河间，时长子伯璟在兖，夜梦一人僵卧，支体焦烂，不可识，一人指曰：“此太守也。”明日公除河间；信至，璟涕泣不食，寄书极谏不可往。公笑曰：“儿曹欲吾为自全计，此方百姓安所逃死乎？”视事如故。夫人颜氏，亚圣裔也。公举乎乡，喜甚，典簪珥佐觞客。及捷南宫，卧不起。姻党相贺，答曰：“国家多难而遽以身许人，吾滋惧焉，何以贺为？”

    Explanation

    12

    In the beginning, the official had a retainer named Yan Bailin, who was skilled in observing auspicious and inauspicious signs. When the official arrived in Hejian, Yan Bailin secretly said that the city was not suitable for residence. The official pretended not to understand, but secretly had someone escort Yan Bailin out, who shed tears as he left. Later, when the official went to Hejian, his eldest son, Bojing, was in Yan. One night, Bojing dreamed of a person lying motionless with scorched limbs, unrecognizable, and another person pointed and said, "This is the governor." The next day, the official was appointed as the governor of Hejian. When the news reached Bojing, he wept and refused to eat, sending a letter strongly advising against the official's going. The official smiled and said, "My children want me to consider my own safety, but where can the people of this region escape death?" He continued to handle affairs as usual. His wife, Lady Yan, was a descendant of a secondary sage. When the official was recommended by his hometown, he was very happy, managing his hairpins and earrings to assist in entertaining guests. After passing the imperial examination, he lay down without getting up. Relatives and friends congratulated him, but he replied, "In times of national crisis, to quickly offer oneself to others, I am even more afraid of this. What is there to congratulate about?"

    13

    当公之未遇难也，伯璟既得恶梦，日夜忧虑。道阻，事不相闻。未几兵至兖，城破，兵民皆走窜。璟体肥，不良于行，玠掖璟疾走，璟麾之曰：“吾等父在河间，存亡不可知，汝当速去。兄弟并命，于此无益也。”玠泣不去。璟绐之，使他顾，遽自睥睨间跃下。玠遂死乱兵；璟仆地，伤左足，至夜乃苏，为逻者所得。见其修髯广颡，状甚伟，不敢害，车舁以告其帅，不为屈，帅惊曰：“吾自入关，未尝见如此人。

    Explanation

    13

    When the public official had not yet met with disaster, Bo Jing had already had a bad dream and was worried day and night. The road was difficult, and nothing was heard about the matter. Shortly afterward, the army arrived at Yan, the city was breached, and the soldiers and civilians all fled. Jing was overweight and not good at walking, so Jie supported Jing and ran quickly. Jing waved his hand at Jie and said, "Our father is in Hejian, and his fate is unknown. You should leave quickly. The fate of brothers is intertwined here, and it would be of no benefit." Jie cried but did not leave. Jing deceived him, making him look elsewhere, and suddenly jumped down from the parapet. Jie was killed by the chaotic soldiers; Jing fell to the ground, injured his left foot, and revived until night. He was captured by the guards, who saw his long beard and broad forehead, and found his appearance very impressive. They dared not harm him and carried him in a cart to report to their leader. Jing did not yield, and the leader was surprised, saying, "Since I entered the pass, I have never seen such a person before."

    14

    ”既知为颜子后，遂留帐中。有人语璟：昨日驱妇数辈，一妇骂，不肯行，卒反刀击其背。骂不已，卒杀之墙下。有媪指曰：“此颜氏妇。”璟曰：“必吾妻也。”璟告帅，至墙下觅之，果然。盖刃伤已四日矣。试其息，犹未绝，载还曲阜。而帅告璟曰：“汝日念父，兖州破时，破河间已一月矣。”璟闻痛哭投地，绝复苏，告帅曰：“吾父素矢忠贞，义无苟全。我幸遇公，得不死，曷从吾去，俾收骸骨。”帅怜而许之。因得间道归曲阜。己遂匍匐赴河间。

    Explanation

    14

    After learning that he was the descendant of Yan Zi, Jing stayed in the tent. Someone told Jing: "Yesterday, several women were driven away, and one of them cursed and refused to go. Finally, he drew his sword and struck her back. She continued to curse, and finally, he killed her under the wall." An old woman pointed and said, "This is the wife of Yan." Jing said, "This must be my wife." Jing reported to the general, and they went to the wall to look for her. Indeed, it was her. The wound had been inflicted four days ago. He tested her breath, and it was not yet extinguished. He carried her back to Qufu. The general then told Jing, "You have been thinking of your father every day. When Yanzhou was destroyed, it had been a month since Hejian was destroyed." Jing heard and wept bitterly, falling to the ground. He nearly died but revived, and told the general, "My father always vowed to be loyal and steadfast. It was not right to preserve life by any means. I was fortunate to meet you and was saved from death. Why not let me go with you to collect his remains?" The general pitied him and agreed. Jing was able to take a secret route back to Qufu and then crawled to Hejian.

    15

    当是时，室人朱氏创剧，二子患痘，毅然不顾。兵火充斥，尝积日不得食，或被执。璟慷慨与语，声泪皆迸，辄为感动，释去，达河间。得遗骸灰烬中，躄踊惨怛，观者泣下。先是，仆有年负伯珣走，道中流矢，至珣窜民间。璟访得之，携与归。因悲玠之死，而愈笃珣之爱也。
    鼎革后，暇辄读书鼓琴。平生坦易，遇人甚温。家法严以肃，友爱季弟，三十年无闲言。恒自言年至六十一卒。后果验。有子六人，朱淑人出者三，皆知名。长运使公，次考功公，三学使公，时人尊为“一母三进士”。后科第连绵，至四世。今崇芳、崇简、崇芬：“一母三孝廉”云。盖忠孝之遗泽长也。

    Explanation

    15

    At that time, Lady Zhu, the wife of the subject, was gravely ill, and the two sons suffered from smallpox. Despite these hardships, the subject remained steadfast and unhesitant. Amidst the chaos of war and fire, they often went days without food, and there were times when they were captured. The subject spoke earnestly and passionately, his voice and tears both bursting forth, which moved those around him, leading to their release and allowing them to reach Hejian. They found remains in the ashes, weeping bitterly and lamenting deeply, causing spectators to shed tears as well.

    Prior to this, a servant had carried Mr. Boxun on his back as they fled, but along the way, they were hit by flying arrows, forcing Mr. Boxun to hide among the common people. The subject eventually found him and brought him back home. Moved by the death of Mr. Jie, the subject's love for Mr. Boxun grew even stronger.

    After the change of dynasties, the subject would often read and play the zither in his leisure time. He was known for being open and easy-going, treating people with great warmth. His family rules were strict and disciplined, and he loved and cared for his younger brother deeply, with no quarrels over the course of thirty years. He always predicted that he would die at the age of sixty-one, and indeed, his prediction came true. He had six sons, three of whom were born to Lady Zhu, all of whom were well-known. The eldest son was Mr. Yunshi, the second son was Mr. Kaogong, and the third son was Mr. Xueshi. At the time, people revered them as "Three Scholars from One Mother." Later generations continued to succeed in the imperial examinations, up to the fourth generation. Now there are Chongfang, Chongjian, and Chongfen, who are known as "Three Filial Officials from One Mother." This is the long-lasting legacy of loyalty and filial piety.

    16

    (余读《唐书》，天宝河北之变，忠节公父子死节负骨，与此事吻合。是颜氏之子忠孝，有所由来者矣。此传盖采贻上、彝尊诸传合成，最称详确。
    我五世祖宏毅公，字泰东，为宗圣六十三代嫡裔。袭世职。内遭家变，外侮凭凌，负奇慨，有胆略。崇祯八年，公行取入京陪祀，过归德州。时登州游击孔有德叛，骚动州邑，遇总兵杨御蕃为贼所困。公素与蕃善，遂与贼战，身中流矢。活抉其一贼归，掷于马前，已毙。后十四年，嘉邑满家碉土寇龚二麻作乱，先后攻城。公率家丁与阖邑绅士逻守之，保无恙。竟以劳瘵，三十二而卒。
    其义勇，直可与颜氏比烈。惜子孙微薄，无传之者。东不文谨附篇末，用备采访。)

    赵孝子传
    赵江，商丘人。性方执而慈善，读书不求仕进。常见贫者、丐子，辄与一钱。即百丐与百文，亦不吝。外此，盐米自供，皆谨细，因是多营。妻李氏，贤且美。有二妾。李氏生二子，二妾各生一子，陶陶遂遂，乡邻称之。

    Explanation

    16

    I read the Tangshu (Book of Tang), and during the Tianbao era, the rebellion in Hebei, the loyal and righteous father and son who died heroically and had their bones carried back home aligns with this event. The filial piety and loyalty of the descendants of Yanzi have a long-standing tradition. This biography is compiled from various sources, including those of Yi Shang and Yi Zun, and is considered the most detailed and accurate.

    My fifth-generation ancestor, Hongyi Gong, with the courtesy name Tai Dong, was the 63rd direct descendant of Confucius. He inherited a hereditary official position. Internally, he faced family turmoil, and externally, he was threatened by invasions. He possessed extraordinary courage and strategic thinking. In the eighth year of the Chongzhen reign (1635), he was summoned to the capital to participate in a sacrificial ceremony and passed through Guizhou. At that time, Kong Youde, a military officer from Dengzhou, rebelled, causing unrest in the region. He encountered General Yang Yufan, who was besieged by the rebels. Since he had always been close to General Yang, he fought the rebels, and was hit by an arrow. He captured a rebel alive, brought him back, and threw him in front of his horse, where he died. Fourteen years later, in 1649, Geng Erma, a local bandit, caused chaos in the area, repeatedly attacking the city. He led his family retainers and local gentry to defend the city, ensuring its safety. Eventually, he died of overwork at the age of 32.

    His bravery and righteousness are comparable to that of the descendants of Yanzi. Unfortunately, his descendants were not prominent enough to continue his legacy. I, Dong Bawen, respectfully add this at the end of the text to serve as a record for future reference.

    Biography of Zhao the Filial Son

    Zhao Jiang, from Shangqiu, had a straightforward and charitable nature. He studied but did not seek official positions. He often gave money to the poor and beggars. Even if a hundred beggars came, he would give them a hundred coins without hesitation. Outside of this, he carefully managed his household, ensuring that basic needs were met. His wife, Li, was both virtuous and beautiful. He had two concubines. Li gave birth to two sons, and each concubine gave birth to one son. He lived a peaceful and contented life, and was praised by his neighbors.

    17

    后李氏又生一子。娩之夕，室有异香。落草后，一足短，为跛。周岁又坏一目。江恶之，以为不祥，欲弃之榛莽中，其母不可，因名曰“榛”。会当旱魃为灾，连岁不登，荒脊，流亡者十室九空。赵幸温饱，赖以存。至夏，疫行，一村传染。李氏病，继而两妾并诸子，下暨仆婢牛马，无一不尪瘠枕藉乎其间。时榛已七岁，江与榛独无病，而药炉鼎沸，巫医相望。旬日之间，妻丧妾死子殇畜毙，江虽殷实，罹此百凶，亦不能支。抑且吊问皆绝，榛又幼孤，有残疾。江于此时，呼天惨地，抚境捶胸，自问生平固无大善，亦无大恶，何降祸之烈一至此极？每每愤不欲生。既而幡然有迁引之志，乃束资欲发。榛牵其衣，曰：“阿爹去，儿焉往？”江曰：“去去当复回。”榛曰：“行行恐别离。”泣不已。江诳之，绝裾以去。出门，惘惘不知所向。

    Explanation

    17

    After Li gave birth to another son, on the night of his delivery, the room was filled with a strange fragrance. After being born, one foot was shorter, making him a cripple. In his first year, one eye became blind. Jiang detested him, considering him an omen of ill fortune, and wanted to abandon him in the wilderness, but his mother refused, so he named him "Zhen." When drought caused disaster, for several years, there was no harvest, the land was barren, and nine out of ten households were empty due to famine and migration. Zhao was fortunate to have enough food and warmth, relying on it to survive. By summer, an epidemic spread, infecting the entire village. Li fell ill, followed by both concubines and all the children, down to servants, maids, cattle, and horses, none of whom were spared from wasting away. At this time, Zhen was seven years old, and only Jiang and Zhen remained healthy, while the medicine furnace was boiling, and sorcerers and doctors were everywhere. Within ten days, his wife died, his concubines died, his children died, and his livestock perished. Although Jiang was wealthy, facing such numerous calamities, he could not bear it. Moreover, all condolences and inquiries ceased, and Zhen, being young and orphaned, had a disability. At this time, Jiang cried to heaven and earth, lamented his circumstances, and beat his chest, asking himself if he had ever done great good or great evil, why the calamity was so severe. He often felt so angry that he wanted to die. Later, he suddenly changed his mind and decided to leave, gathering his belongings. Zhen pulled his clothes, saying, "Father, if you leave, where will I go?" Jiang said, "I will go, but I will return." Zhen said, "If you go, I fear we will part forever," and he wept uncontrollably. Jiang lied to him and left, tearing his clothes, and left the house, wandering aimlessly without knowing where to go.

    18

    斯时巷无居人，僵尸在室。榛以巾兜土掩其母、兄，反阖其户，竟出渡河，奔外氏家。畜养焉。阅月，其外氏携榛返舍，门庭扃如故。自窗棂视内，床上皆土垒如邱。同榛，榛泣告。外氏怜之，遂为之营葬，经理家事。仆婢亦渐渐归。外氏乃与延师。年十三，入庠，以家务，弃举业。而恒产十倍于前。十六毕姻。十八育一男。逾年，谓其妻曰：“死者已矣，生者曷归？人之所以异于禽兽者，不过此伦常，此彝教。吾父弃家避难，亦越于今盖十有二年，其杖履如故乎？音容如故乎？未尝一刻去诸心。今我有子，而我无父；天下岂有无父之人哉？我将不容于世矣！”乃治任，誓以不见父不返。其妻及外氏戚党劝之，不听，因饯于野亭。家人送之，皆白衣冠。

    Explanation

    18

    At that time, the alley had no inhabitants, and zombies were in the rooms. Hēn covered his mother and brother with soil using a cloth, then closed the door, and finally went out and crossed the river, running to his maternal grandfather's house. He was raised there. After a month, his maternal grandfather took Hēn back to their home, where the door and courtyard were locked as before. Looking inside through the window lattice, the bed was piled with soil like a mound. Together with Hēn, Hēn wept and reported. His maternal grandfather pitied him, so he arranged for the burial and managed the household affairs. The servants and maids gradually returned. His maternal grandfather then arranged for a teacher. At thirteen, Hēn entered the school, but because of household affairs, he gave up his studies. However, his constant property was ten times more than before. At sixteen, he married, and at eighteen, he had a son. After a year, he said to his wife, "The dead are gone, but where do the living return? What distinguishes humans from animals is nothing but these moral norms and these established teachings. My father abandoned our home to avoid disaster, and it has been twelve years since then. Are his staff and shoes still the same? Are his voice and appearance still the same? He has never for a moment forgotten. Now I have a son, but I have no father; how can there be a person in the world without a father? I cannot tolerate being in the world anymore!" So he prepared his belongings, swearing not to return until he sees his father. His wife and his maternal grandfather's relatives tried to persuade him, but he did not listen. So they held a farewell banquet at a pavilion in the wild, and the family saw him off, all wearing white clothes and hats.

    19

    榛乃一肩行李，彳亍伶仃，飘然南下。以南方多佳山水，意其父或隐于僧。至维扬，登金、焦之山，访吴淞诸名胜，探禹穴，上九嶷，入闽峤而跨粤峒。凡有茅庵卓锡之地，莫不遍访周咨。星霜寒暑三易，而迄无消耗。既而行资告匮，乃背书其寻亲之由，招摇于市。乞食蓝缕，夜宿破庙败堵。闲人见之，皆以为假乞怜而绐人者。用是，行益困。

    Explanation

    19

    Zhen, with a single shoulder of luggage, walked slowly and alone, drifting southward. Believing that the south had many beautiful landscapes and mountains, he thought his father might be hidden among monks. Upon reaching Yangzhou, he climbed Jinshan and Jiuzong Mountain, visited the scenic spots around Wusong, explored the legendary burial place of Yu the Great, ascended Jiuyi Mountain, entered the mountainous region of Fujian, and crossed the mountainous region of Guangdong. Everywhere he found places where monks had settled, he thoroughly visited and inquired. After three years, he still found no trace of his father. Soon after, his funds ran out, and he began to write about his search for his father, attracting attention in the market. He begged for food in tattered clothes, and at night, he slept in dilapidated temples and ruined walls. Bystanders saw him and all thought he was pretending to beg for pity to deceive others. Thus, his journey became increasingly difficult.

    20

    一日暮，至赣江，将趁渡，舟人以为丐，不与济。其舟至中流，风起而覆，人尽祸。榛望而喟然曰：“天不死我，我必见父生还也。”其志愈坚。又三年，而西自蜀、黔入滇。古云“蜀道青天”，而滇、黔更难于蜀道。复出铁关，达野人居。其地产生银、宝石，榛得之，返售于都市，稍壮行色。如是，由西欲出汉中。度阴栈，天晦，雾径滑，坠悬崖下，了无损伤。寻樵路出，乃至太原。当大雪，榛冻馁，行僵卧。忽见一人，峨冠朱绯，舆马甚都，指曰：“此吾孙也。”从人急救之。至一廨，冠者抚之曰：“儿寻尔父，当出口，不在此。会不远矣。”一丸纳榛，吞之苏，起身便不寒，亦不饥，且畅支体，可数日不食。

    Explanation

    20

    One evening, Zhen arrived at the Gan River, intending to take a ferry. The boatman, thinking he was a beggar, refused to let him cross. His boat reached midstream when the wind arose and capsized, putting all on board in peril. Zhen looked and sighed, saying, "Heaven did not kill me; I will surely see my father return alive." His resolve became even stronger. After another three years, he traveled west from Sichuan and Guizhou into Yunnan. As the ancients said, "The road through Sichuan is as clear as the sky," but the roads through Yunnan and Guizhou are even more difficult than those through Sichuan. He then passed through the Iron Pass and reached the wild settlements. The region produced silver and gemstones, which Zhen obtained and sold back in the city, gradually gaining strength and purpose. Thus, he intended to leave from the west to reach Hanzhong. He crossed the dark栈, where the sky was dark and the fog made the path slippery. He fell off a cliff but suffered no injury. He followed a woodcutter's path and eventually reached Taiyuan. During a heavy snowfall, Zhen froze and starved, lying motionless. Suddenly, he saw a person with an elaborate cap and red robe, accompanied by a splendid carriage and horse. The person pointed and said, "This is my grandson." His attendants rushed to help Zhen. They arrived at a government office, where the person with the cap comforted Zhen, saying, "My son is looking for your father. He should be outside, not here. He is not far away." The person gave Zhen a pill, which he swallowed and then recovered. He felt no cold, no hunger, and his body was invigorated, allowing him to go several days without food.

    21

    乃逾燕都，出居庸，又东至辽阳。关东丰胰地，人物蕃阜，无殍丐，粟烂鸡黄，且多豫人为贾。询厥由来，命曰：“吾乡人可屈指，独无赵姓，当他处觅耗。”榛终以神语为异，迟徊不能去。忽一日，见一翁，年七旬内，白须，行甚驶。遇榛，辄投数镪而去。榛甫欲问，而翁已远。榛急追之。三里许，至一篱落柴门，翁即入。

    Explanation

    21

    Zhen then passed through Yan capital, exited Juyong Pass, and continued eastward to Liaoyang. The land east of the pass was rich and fertile, with abundant people and things, no beggars or starving individuals, and plentiful grain and chickens. Additionally, there were many merchants from Henan. Inquiring about the reason, someone said, "My fellow villagers can be counted on one hand, but there is no surname Zhao; they must be looked for elsewhere." Zhen finally found the divine words strange and hesitated, unable to leave. Suddenly one day, he saw an old man, who was in his seventies, with a white beard, walking very fast. When he met Zhen, the old man immediately threw several coins and left. Zhen was just about to ask, but the old man had already gone far away. Zhen hurriedly chased after him. About three miles away, he reached a fence and a thatched door, and the old man immediately entered.

    22

    榛闻内书声朗朗，少顷翁出，见榛，曰：“适遇诸途，今又过门耶？”榛曰：“闻长者口音似豫人，敢以一事动问：此地未审有河南赵姓寓者否？”翁异之，又见其蹩，曰：“尔榛儿耶？”榛闻声一号，气噎欲绝。江亦泣曰：“吾以汝为死矣。是吾之过也。”掖之入内，少息，哭诉颠末。十五年浮萍浪迹，海角天涯，靡所不到。江解颜曰：“吾自离乡井，别故土，便欲南辕。闻其地浇漓浮侈，俗不长厚，因转念而北。然虽余生放废，终不肯以清献世裔，甘心黄冠缁流，乱我儒风之素守。沈阳敦庞之所，食裕人和，作童蒙馆，教小儿识字。乡俗与河南颇异，每晨来学，以一钱识一字，十字十钱，百字百钱，日可青蚨数百。二十余年，饘粥于斯。计所积，可千金。”旋问榛家计，则对以十倍从前。乃劝其父归，父许之。

    Explanation

    22

    Hazel heard a loud sound inside the house, and shortly after, an old man came out and saw Hazel. He said, "I just met you on the road; are you passing by again?" Hazel replied, "I heard that your accent sounds like someone from Henan. I dare to ask if there is a man named Zhao living here?" The old man was surprised and noticed Hazel's limping, then asked, "Are you my son, Hazel?" Hazel heard his voice and cried out once, choking with emotion. Jiang also wept, saying, "I thought you were dead. It was my fault." He led Hazel inside, where they took a brief rest. Hazel then cried and recounted the details of their separation. For fifteen years, Hazel had wandered like a floating duckweed, traveling to the ends of the earth, never staying in one place. Jiang relaxed his expression and said, "After I left my hometown and parted from my homeland, I wanted to head south. But upon hearing that the place was decadent and luxurious, with customs not lasting or thick, I changed my mind and headed north. Although my life has been neglected, I have never willingly accepted the life of a Taoist or Buddhist monk, disrupting the traditional Confucian values. In Shenyang, a place of peace and prosperity, I taught children to read in a school for young children. The local customs are quite different from those in Henan. Every morning, children would come to learn, paying one coin for each character, ten coins for ten characters, and a hundred coins for a hundred characters, earning several hundred coins daily. For over twenty years, I lived on porridge and rice. Calculating the accumulated wealth, it could amount to a thousand gold pieces." He then asked Hazel about his family's financial situation, and Hazel replied that it was ten times better than before. Jiang then advised Hazel's father to return home, and his father agreed.

    23

    先是，江翁不言豫人，又讳其姓，号“天水江先生”，人咸以为“江”也，今始知之。其居停梓里，争相延誉，为之赆钱甚众。一月而行旌甫劝。抵家十里许，其家人已候于道。问其何以预知？家人云：“十日前，村中同梦多人，云朔越某日，赵孝子迎其父归。前夜，旧茔上有慈乌千百集杨树巅。”是时其孙赵环已成人，将婚，乡党艳其事，数百里皆来观，云：“赵榛不惟眇躄，且又黝缩。独能担荷大任，立身修行，为第一流人。斯亦奇矣！”

    Explanation

    23

    Previously, Old Master Jiang did not mention his origins from the state of Yu and avoided discussing his surname, instead being called "Master Jiang of Tianshui." Everyone thought he was surnamed Jiang, but now it is known otherwise. In his hometown, people vied to praise him and gave him farewell gifts in great quantity. After a month, he was only encouraged to leave. Upon arriving about ten miles from home, his family was already waiting on the road. When asked how they knew in advance, his family said, "Ten days ago, many villagers had the same dream, saying that on a certain day in the lunar calendar, Zhaò the filial son would welcome his father back. The previous night, thousands of birds gathered at the top of a willow tree near the old grave." At that time, his grandson Zhaò Huán had grown up and was about to get married. The villagers admired this event, and people from hundreds of miles away came to see. They said, "Zhaò Zhēn is not only blind in one eye and lame but also dark and hunched. Yet, he alone could shoulder great responsibilities, cultivate himself, and become among the best people. This is truly extraordinary!"

    24

    嘻！宁残其形而不残其性乎？将不全于人而独全于天乎？曾生寓曹南，邻其地，戚其事不传，求其乡之父老，津津道之者，以书。铭曰：
    “眇能视，跛能履。不盲于心，而不坠于行止。视履考祥纯孝之子。”
    (按：孝子有万里寻亲录，实纪其太翁卒于滇，孝子负骸以归。与此传小异。)

    李将军全城纪略
    李将军，名士元，字小溪，直隶通州人也。长身鸠面，有膂力，以胆略自雄。起行伍，至裨将，守备青州。值明季，州县吏咸以笔墨抑制武职，士元郁郁无所施。
    崇祯壬午冬，大兵略东，士元登陴，誓守城。北隅有庳圮处，士元率众逻守。众戒严，士元独不寐。夜半，闻城外犬狺狺。俯堞而窥，则甲声铮然，万骑屯集壕堑。士元大呼，众惊欲散。士元立馘一人，乃止。急燃火炮击之，腾而过，不能中。黄指挥桓立陴间，放“万人敌”皆顿地熄。士元乃倒提炮尾，以毡帽窒其口，附堞而发，而桓以束薪投城下，

    Explanation

    24

    Ah! Should one rather harm the form than the nature? Or is it better to be incomplete in human terms but complete in the eyes of heaven? Zeng lived in Cao Nan, near the place, but his sad story was not passed down. I sought out the elders of the village, who spoke of it eagerly, and recorded it in writing. The inscription reads:

    "Though blind in one eye, he can still see; though lame, he can still walk. Not blinded in the heart, and not faltering in one's steps. Examining the steps and the omens, this is a truly filial son."

    (Notes: There is a record called 'A Son's Journey of Ten Thousand Miles to Find His Parents,' which truly records how his great-grandfather died in Yunnan, and how the filial son carried the bones back home. This account is slightly different.)

    The Account of General Li's Defense of the City
    General Li, whose name was Shiyuan and courtesy name Xiaoxi, was from Tongzhou, Zhili Province. He was tall with a crow's face and possessed great strength, taking pride in his courage and strategy. Rising from the ranks, he reached the position of deputy general and was stationed as the defender of Qingzhou. During the late Ming dynasty, officials in the counties and prefectures all used their pens to suppress military officials, leaving Shiyuan feeling depressed and without a place to apply his talents.

    In the winter of the Renwu year of Chongzhen, the main army advanced eastward. Shiyuan climbed onto the city wall and swore to defend the city. At the northern corner, there was a section of the wall that was low and crumbling. Shiyuan led the troops to guard it. The soldiers were on high alert, but Shiyuan alone did not sleep. At midnight, he heard barking dogs outside the city. Leaning over the battlements to look, he saw the sound of armor clanking, with ten thousand riders gathered in the moat. Shiyuan shouted loudly, and the soldiers were startled and wanted to disperse. Shiyuan immediately beheaded one of them, and they stopped. He quickly lit a firecracker and fired it, but it flew over and failed to hit. Huang, the commander, stood between the battlements and fired "ten thousand enemies," all of which fell to the ground and extinguished. Shiyuan then grabbed the tail of the firecracker and stuffed a felt hat over its mouth, firing it against the battlements. Meanwhile, Huang threw a bundle of firewood down from the city wall.

    25

    “万人敌”忽响如轰雷。云梯环攻者，歼焉。敌兵雨射城中，桓与士元袒而立。桓中流矢殪，士元屹而不动，矢纷纷不及身，意义愈壮。抵明，敌以城坚不可攻，拔营东去。城中百姓咸以手加额曰：“微将军，城其屠矣！”
    癸未三月，大兵率众东返。去青州六十里，下砦于弥水之涯四十余日。而明怀宗遣重兵护卫，衡藩师范志宅顿兵王乘埠，钟将军晓东门，经略王永吉、赵敬塘军车辕门，总兵刘清驻师古西关，相联络，为犄角之势。众凡数万，视兵士焚戮，毁庐舍，牵持累累以去，莫敢如何。而泽清一部，尤横恣狡谲，反首鼠两端，为袭城之计，乘夜假冒大兵攻城。士元备预綦严，燃“万人敌”焚杀数百人，遂宵遁。
    逾年，甲申三月，李自成陷京师，僭尊号，建国大顺，改元永昌。遣伪官姚将军将以五百人填青，皆铁衣绣鬣，以红帕首，势焰张甚。而藩王家有献女为其小妻者，城中惴惴不自保。未几，吴三桂由宁远抵关门，请我大兵蹴燕都，自成西走。

    Explanation

    25

    The "Ten Thousand Men's Enemy" suddenly roared like a thunderclap. Those attacking with ladders were annihilated. The enemy's arrows rained down on the city, and Huán and Shìyuán stood exposed and ready. Huán was struck by an arrow and died, but Shìyuán stood firm and unmoved, the arrows failing to hit him, which made his resolve even stronger. By dawn, the enemy, finding the city too well-defended to attack, withdrew their camp to the east. The city's inhabitants all raised their hands in relief, saying, "Without our general, the city would have been destroyed!"

    In the third month of the year Guiwei, the main army led their troops eastward. They camped sixty miles from Qingzhou for over forty days along the banks of the Mi River. The Ming Emperor sent heavy forces to guard the area, with Balancing Prince's military commander Zhi Zhai stationed at Wang Chengbu, General Zhong guarding the eastern gate, Strategist Wang Yongji and Zhao Jingtang stationed at the chariot gate, and General Liu Qing stationed at the ancient western pass, all linked together in a defensive formation. The enemy numbered tens of thousands, burning and slaughtering soldiers, destroying homes, and dragging people away, leaving the city helpless. However, one unit under Ze Qing was particularly arrogant and cunning, hesitating and acting deceitfully, planning to attack the city by night, impersonating the main army. Shìyuán prepared with utmost vigilance, lighting the "Ten Thousand Men's Enemy" to burn and kill hundreds of them, and they fled at night.

    A year later, in the third month of the year Jiashen, Li Zicheng captured Beijing, usurped the imperial title, founded the Shun Dynasty, and changed the era name to Yongchang. He dispatched a false official, General Yao, with five hundred men to fill Qingzhou, all clad in iron armor with embroidered manes, wearing red headscarves, their presence menacing. Some of the princes' families were forced to offer their daughters as concubines, causing the city to tremble with fear. Soon after, Wu Sangui arrived from Ningyuan at the pass, requesting our army to attack Yanjing, and Li Zicheng retreated westward.

    26

    士元遣急足侦探，一日夜至青。士元私计贼，觇知非内溃即外逸，青人必罹其害。适贼于是日设宴于邢尚书宅，士元率标下数十人来进谒，姚仓皇离席起立。士元直前，踊身越几，挥斩姚于席上，左右皆披靡。士元大呼曰：“若知吴某引兵百万已灭闯贼乎？动者如姚罪。去留悉听之。”是时，城中万户，莫不屏息以遵士元。士元介胄见衡王，曰：“神京失守，闯贼西窜，社稷无主，中原鼎沸。王亲宪皇之子孙，据全齐形胜之地。山东豪杰，荷戈砺刃，大者数万，小者千百为群，引领以望王义师之起胜兵，百万可传檄而集。南塞大岘之山，北扼河济之冲，迤逦而西，以光复大物。将见燕蓟士女，皆箪食壶浆以迎王师。此诚光武中兴汉柞，肃宗再造唐室之业也。否则坐失事机，鹿死谁手，瞻乌靡定，异姓代兴。彼下尺书以征王，王其犊车出郭，乌能享此藩封乎？”王素懦，又吝财自封，乃趦趄曰：“卿言大不是。”士元知事不可为，乃弃官怏怏归里。

    Explanation

    26

    Shi Yuan dispatched a swift messenger to gather intelligence, who arrived in Qing within a day and night. Shi Yuan secretly calculated that the rebels would either collapse from within or flee outward, and the people of Qing would surely suffer from their actions. Coincidentally, on that day, the rebels held a banquet at the residence of Grand Secretary Xing. Shi Yuan led dozens of his subordinates to pay their respects. Yao, the rebel leader, hastily left his seat and stood up. Shi Yuan rushed forward, leaped over the table, and beheaded Yao right there at the banquet, causing all around him to scatter in fear. Shi Yuan then shouted, "Do you know that General Wu has led a million troops to defeat the rebel bandits? Anyone who moves like Yao will be punished. You may choose to stay or leave." At that time, all the households in the city held their breath and followed Shi Yuan's orders. Shi Yuan, clad in armor, met with King Heng and said, "The capital has fallen, and the rebel bandits have fled westward, leaving the state without a ruler and the Central Plains in turmoil. As a descendant of the reigning emperor, you hold the strategically advantageous territory of all Qi. The heroes of Shandong, bearing arms and sharpening their blades, gather in groups of tens of thousands or hundreds, eagerly awaiting your righteous army to rise. A million troops can be gathered with an official edict. By blocking the southern pass of the Daxian Mountains and controlling the northern strategic location at the confluence of the Yellow River and the Ji River, and gradually advancing westward, you can restore great things. Soon, the people of Yan and Jilin will offer food and drink in baskets and jars to welcome your army. This is indeed the glory of Emperor Guangwu of Han, who restored the Han dynasty, and Emperor Suzong of Tang, who revived the Tang dynasty. Otherwise, you will lose the opportunity due to inaction, and it is uncertain who will end up with the prize. The rise of a different family is a possibility. If they send a letter to summon you, you will have to leave the city in a small carriage and will no longer be able to enjoy this fief." The king was naturally timid and also stingy, unwilling to share his wealth. He hesitated and said, "Your words are greatly mistaken." Shi Yuan knew that the situation could not be salvaged and resigned in disappointment, returning home.

    27

    洎我朝定鼎，遣两固山安集青郡。士元亦随固山至，逾月，有赵应元之变。应元为自成余党，持伪符乘传至。太守张文衡出迎，而应元声言报谒，随文衡肩舆，并从卒数百，拥而入，因据城以叛。杀总督王鳌永，而欲挟衡藩南渡。事出仓卒，人情汹汹。两固山以士卒少，思檄诸路重兵围城以济。士元方剿高密土寇，闻变疾驰至青，入谏两固山曰：“城中居民皆胁从，非诚心事贼。脱大兵至，城破则玉石俱焚，滥及无辜，岂安集之谓哉？”曰：“如君言，计将若何？”曰：“应元以败亡之余，祚有青州，本出愿外。观其入城，封府库，禁杀戮，其意大有所在。但在骑虎之势，急则拚死。缓则可以计图。某将以利害祸福动之，诸公请按兵以待。其计如是，如是。”皆曰：“诺。”

    Explanation

    27

    Since the establishment of our dynasty's capital, two generals were sent to pacify Qingzhou. Shi Yuan also followed the generals, and after a month, there was the rebellion of Zhao Yingyuan. Zhao Yingyuan, a remnant of Li Zicheng's forces, arrived carrying a false document and traveling by official post. The governor of Qingzhou, Zhang Wenheng, went out to greet him, but Zhao Yingyuan claimed he came to pay respects, following Zhang Wenheng's sedan chair, with hundreds of attendants, and entered the city. He then seized the city to rebel, killing the governor-general Wang Aoyong, and intended to take the Prince of Heng south across the river. The situation arose suddenly, causing widespread panic. The two generals, with few troops, considered calling in reinforcements from various routes to besiege the city. Shi Yuan, who was then fighting local bandits in Gaomi, heard of the rebellion and rushed to Qingzhou. He entered and advised the two generals, saying, "The residents of the city were all coerced into following, not genuinely loyal to the rebels. If the main army arrives, the city will fall, and both the good and the bad will be destroyed, harming the innocent—how can this be called pacification?" The generals replied, "If what you say is true, what plan should we follow?" Shi Yuan said, "Zhao Yingyuan, having suffered defeat, unexpectedly gained control of Qingzhou, which was not his original intention. Observing his entry into the city, he sealed the treasury and prohibited killing, suggesting he has deeper intentions. He is in a situation where he must fight to the death if pushed too far, but if we act slowly, we can plan our strategy. I will use the balance of benefits and harms to influence him; please the generals hold back your troops and wait. The plan is as follows, as follows." They all said, "Agreed."

    28

    遂缓装徒步，通谒。应元素耳其名，欢然迎迓。曰：“李小溪为两固山作说客耶？”曰：“为将军计耳！将军据青州已月余，孤城自画，不能拓尺寸地以张威令。将坐守青州，南面以自王耶？抑或藉朝廷之命，专制一道之为得耶？将军士卒不满千人，为将军城守者，不过慑将军威。为目前自守计，非能拊循而用之也。战则不能，守则不可，援兵外集，内变将作，必有以将军为奇货者，譬如阱中之虎，坐受缚矣！”应元士卒少，又传禁兵将至，闻言色变，曰：“将军为我谋，奈何？”曰：“是莫若与诸帅和。令抚军疏请于朝，言公入青州，只以总督虐民，诛之，其余不戮一人。今复以全城归命，则通侯之赏可立至也。”应元喜曰：“唯君命。”士元乃导应元出谒诸帅，甲士皆随之。

    Explanation

    28

    Li Xiaoxi, dressed in plain attire and walking on foot, made a formal visit to Ying Yuan. Upon hearing Li Xiaoxi's name, Ying Yuan joyfully received him. He asked, "Is Li Xiaoxi acting as a mediator for the two garrisons?" Li Xiaoxi replied, "It is for the general's benefit, after all! The general has been holding Qingzhou for more than a month, isolated and unable to expand even a small area to extend one's authority. Will the general sit and defend Qingzhou, facing south as a king? Or will the general rely on the imperial command to rule an entire region? The general's troops number less than a thousand, and those guarding the city are only intimidated by the general's power. For the current defense, they cannot be properly managed and used. Unable to fight and unable to defend, with reinforcements gathering outside and internal changes likely to occur, there will certainly be those who view the general as a valuable asset, like a tiger in a trap, sitting and waiting to be captured!" Ying Yuan, with few soldiers and hearing that imperial troops were approaching, looked alarmed and said, "What should the general do?" Li Xiaoxi said, "The best course is to reconcile with the other commanders. Have the pacifying general submit a petition to the court, stating that upon entering Qingzhou, the general only punished the cruel governor and did not harm anyone else. Now, by surrendering the entire city, the general can receive the reward of a marquis." Ying Yuan was pleased and said, "I will follow your advice." Shi Yuan then led Ying Yuan to meet the other commanders, with armed soldiers following.

    29

    晚，遂令应元张筵招饮，宴于郡北门之瞻辰楼。随从者止许各一人。参议韩昭宣素勇将，专席坐，应元军师杨王休与士元各东西向坐，而士元与应元贴肩坐，以示亲昵。至则钻刀歃血而誓。两固山各伏兵城外，以俟士元。业先与城中居民约闻炮声则启扉，再则各家以床几之属顿衢中，三则阖户寝息，听街市有声，勿哗。时夜漏二下，酒酣乐作，金鼓暄阗，与城柝相乱，而炮响忽发。

    Explanation

    29

    In the evening, Ying Yuan was invited to a banquet arranged by the host, held at the Zhan Chen Tower on the north gate of the city. Each attendee was allowed only one attendant. Han Zhaoxuan, a brave general and advisor, sat at a special seat. Ying Yuan's military advisor, Yang Wangxiu, and Shi Yuan sat facing east and west respectively, while Shi Yuan sat shoulder to shoulder with Ying Yuan, indicating their close relationship. Upon arrival, they performed a ritual of cutting their flesh and drinking blood together to swear an oath. Two military units were stationed outside the city, waiting for Shi Yuan. The host had previously arranged with the city residents that upon hearing the first cannon shot, they should open their doors; upon the second shot, they should place beds and tables in the streets; and upon the third shot, they should close their doors and sleep, listening for any sounds in the streets without making noise. It was past midnight when the feast reached its peak, with music and drums playing loudly, mixed with the sound of city guards' drums. Suddenly, the cannons fired.

    30

    士元佯惊曰：“此何为者也？”应元曰：“岂营卒有窃发者乎，行诛。”再发，士元起谓应元曰：“君当有他谋。信誓旦旦之谓何？而乃中变乎？”应元方错愕，无以应。倏而三发。士元乃以左手握应元右臂，怒目左右视，伪为与应元耳语状。因携手，睥睨间，辄以右手掣刀斩应元于城上。而昭宣以铜锏踣王休于坐。从者潜抽利刃，所杀凡数十人，余皆散走。而三炮时，先约伏兵杀守城卒，纳我军。诸从贼以通衢什器隔阂，无一人得脱。抵晓居民启户，皆毙横尸于市，方藉藉言今夜三鼓李将军已斩赵贼首矣。
    方是时，微士之计，加兵围城，困兽犹斗，势必多杀良民。则活青州之数万生灵者，非士元而谁哉！事既定，部牒新选一参戒至。当时亦未有表其功者。士元仍随迹归田里。后二十年，有人于粥市见士元鬻马络自给云。

    Explanation

    30

    Mr. Shì pretended to be surprised and said, "What is this?" Mr. Yìng replied, "Could there be a traitor among the camp guards? I will execute them." Suddenly, he said it again. Mr. Shì stood up and said to Mr. Yìng, "You must have another plan. What is the meaning of your earnest and sincere vows? Have you changed your mind?" Mr. Yìng was taken aback and had no response. Suddenly, it happened three times. Mr. Shì then grabbed Mr. Yìng's right arm with his left hand, glared around, and pretended to whisper to Mr. Yìng. They walked together, and in the midst of looking around, he suddenly drew a knife and beheaded Mr. Yìng on the city wall. Mr. Zhuo knocked Mr. Wáng Xiū to the ground while he was seated. The followers secretly drew sharp blades, killing a total of dozens of people, and the rest fled. At the third watch of the night, they had previously agreed to have hidden troops kill the guards and let their army in. The followers were blocked by the bustling streets and equipment, and none could escape. Until dawn, when the residents opened their doors, all were killed and lay dead in the streets. People were whispering that at the third watch of the night, General Li had already beheaded the leader of the rebels.

    If it were not for Mr. Shì's plan, adding troops to besiege the city, the trapped beast would still fight, and it would inevitably result in the killing of many innocent civilians. Then the one who saved the tens of thousands of lives in Qingzhou, who else but Mr. Shì? After the matter was settled, a new edict appointing a subordinate official arrived. At the time, no one recognized his achievements. Mr. Shì still returned to his village. Twenty years later, someone saw Mr. Shì selling horse gear at the porridge market to support himself.

    31

    (读《李将军传》，三全青城，功盖齐地。卒之不获封赏，湮没以终，是岂当轴壅于上闻？抑如田畴辈不受爵耶？噫！李广难封，生不遇时，将军之时为何时，厥功虽伟而沦落不遇，遂令英雄坐老市廛间。可胜叹哉！)

    王世名
    陈惺斋有《杏花村传奇》，载王世名报父仇，多失。附会传信，适足滋疑。余略言其概焉。
    王世名，武义人。年十七。父良，为族俊殴死。已成讼，而父尸暴露。世名跐颡颤心，急欲掩盖。会族尊者议输田以和。世名遂佯应之。凡田所入，辄易价封识。乃下帷攻苦，冀得志遂而叩阍庭，以大雪冤。既而游庠，不第，即弃举子业。与猛士游习拳勇，阴铸一刀，镂曰“报此”。又绘文像，又绘佩刀者在侧，其妻俞氏问之，曰：“刀剑，古人所常佩者，余何独不然？”妻颔之，而泪荧荧，亦不言。

    Explanation

    31

    Reading the biography of General Li Guang, one sees that he was a hero of Qingcheng, whose achievements overshadowed those of Qi. Yet, in the end, he did not receive a title or reward, and was forgotten until his death. Is it because the powerful officials blocked his achievements from reaching the emperor? Or is it like Tian Chou, who refused titles? Alas! Li Guang's difficulty in receiving a title, being born in an unfavorable time, what was the time of the general? His achievements were great, yet he was overlooked and unrecognized, thus making heroes grow old in the marketplace. How can one not sigh!

    Wáng Shìmíng
    Chén Xīngzhāi wrote The Legend of Xinghuacun, which recounts Wáng Shìmíng's quest for revenge against his father's killers, but much of it is unreliable. Fabricating and spreading rumors only adds to the doubts. I will briefly outline the general outline here.

    Wáng Shìmíng was from Wuyi. At the age of seventeen, his father, Liáng, was beaten to death by a prominent member of the family. The case was settled in court, but his father's body was left exposed. Wáng Shìmíng, deeply distressed and anxious, wanted to quickly cover his father's body. Coincidentally, an elder in the family suggested donating land for reconciliation. Wáng Shìmíng pretended to agree. For all the land he donated, he would mark the value and seal it. He then retreated to study diligently, hoping to achieve his goal and petition the court to clear his father's name. Afterward, he attended school, but failed the exams, and decided to abandon the pursuit of becoming a scholar-official. He trained with brave warriors in martial arts, secretly forged a knife, and engraved the word "revenge" on it. He also painted a portrait and another image of someone wearing a sword beside it. His wife, Yu, asked him about the knife, and he replied, "Swords and knives, which ancient people often carried, why should I not do so?" She nodded, but her tears flowed, and she remained silent.

    32

    逾年，生一子，乃曰：“王氏其有后乎！”嗣是，常出，不以时。两月之后，遇俊暮归。世名挟刀，伏而刺之死，遂斩其头于蝴蝶山下。世名乃出其向所对识租银，及宿构百状，赴邑请死。邑夸廉，得其情，别馆之，上其事于大吏。大吏欲检其父尸。尸伤重，则世名罪缓。盖欲以死者而生之也。世名曰：“吾所以忍痛至今始发者，不忍残我父尸也。本吾杀仇命，情罪允当，何必曲原，奚检为？但母恩未断，祈归别母。”吏从之。世名归，母见之泣。世名曰：“儿身乃父之遗也。以父之遗，为父死，虽生离母。得死从父，何憾焉？”邑中直世名者，几千万人。邑令始舁其父棺至。世名见即大痛，以头触阶，血喷如雨死。环观者悉为之恸，邑令亦泣。

    Explanation

    32

    After a year, he gave birth to a son and said, "Will the Wang family have descendants!" From then on, he often went out at irregular times. Two months later, he encountered Jun returning in the evening. Shiming took a knife, crouched down, and stabbed him to death, then decapitated his head at the foot of Butterfly Mountain. Shiming then produced the rent silver he had previously acknowledged and various fabricated scenarios, going to the county to request execution. The county magistrate, known for his integrity, discovered the truth and had him detained separately, reporting the matter to the higher officials. The higher officials wanted to examine his father's body. If the body was severely injured, Shiming's punishment would be mitigated. It was to save the dead by making the living appear dead. Shiming said, "I have endured the pain until now to avoid desecrating my father's body. I killed my enemy as I should have, the crime is just, why should I be spared, and why check the body? But my mother's affection is not yet severed, I ask to return to bid her farewell." The official agreed. Shiming returned, and his mother saw him and wept. Shiming said, "My body is my father's legacy. To die for my father with his legacy, even if I leave my mother alive, dying for my father, what regret is there?" In the county, there were nearly a million people who supported Shiming. The county magistrate finally had his father's coffin brought. Shiming saw it and was greatly grieved, he struck his head against the steps, and died with blood spraying like rain. Those around him all wept, and even the county magistrate cried.

    33

    当世名饮恨于嬉笑而誓必报也，妇俞氏知之，曰：“君为孝子，妾必为烈妇。”及世名归别母，时以母老儿幼嘱之，俞氏曰：“为君忍三载，过此以往，非君所能禁也。”逾三年，俞氏果绝粒死。后有直指马君闻於朝，旌王之庐曰“孝烈”。

    人耳
    文登黄光灿，幼负至性。年十三，值母病，百里外匍匐延医。医言调治必须人参。黄询人参何物，价值几许。家人正以贫不能购，绐之曰：“人参，人耳也，那可得？”黄乃密赴僻处，以利刃自割其耳，持告其父曰：“母病可疗矣！”父惊恻怜，邻里共异之。
    噫！黄之天性纯笃，出自髫龀，非愚孝可比也。

    Explanation

    33

    A person in the world held a grudge due to mockery and vowed revenge. A woman named Yu knew about it and said, "You are a filial son, and I will be a loyal wife." When the man returned to bid farewell to his mother, she entrusted him with her concern about her elderly state and young children. Yu said, "I will endure for three years for you, but after that, it will be beyond your control." After three years, Yu indeed starved herself to death. Later, a magistrate named Ma reported the matter to the court, and the man's home was honored with a plaque that read "Filial and Loyal."

    A boy named Huang Guangcan from Wendeng had a strong sense of filial piety from a young age. At the age of thirteen, when his mother was ill, he crawled over a hundred miles to seek a doctor. The doctor said that the treatment must include ginseng. Huang asked what ginseng was and how much it cost. His family, unable to afford it, lied to him, saying, "Ginseng is human ears, which cannot be obtained." Huang then secretly went to a remote place and cut off his own ear with a sharp blade, holding it up to his father and saying, "Mother's illness can now be treated!" His father was shocked and pitied him, and the neighbors were all amazed.

    Indeed, Huang's pure and sincere nature was evident from a very young age, and his filial piety was not comparable to mere foolishness.

    34

    吴老人诸子妇轮养传
    崇明吴老人者，生四子。家贫，鬻子自给。四子咸为富家奴，及长皆自立，赎身娶妇，同居奉养父母。始每月轮养，其媳曰：“一月一轮，必历三月后方得侍颜色，太疏，当每日轮养。”继以一日一轮，亦必历三日。乃以一餐为率，如早餐伯，则午餐仲，晚餐叔，明日早餐则季。周而复始。逢五日十日，四子共设食于中堂，父母南向坐，东则四子及诸孙、西则四媳及孙媳坐。以次称觞上寿。
    老人饮食之所，后置一厨。厨中每家各置钱一串，老人每食毕反手于厨中随意取钱一串，往市中嬉买果饼啖之。厨中钱总无匮。则其子潜补，不令老人知也。老人间往知交游，或博弈，或樗蒲。四子知其所往，随密持钱二三百文，安置所游之家，并嘱其并输于老人。老人胜，踊跃自喜。持归，告其孙稚。或买嬉食之物，以为娱。亦知其子为之也。尝终日怡然，一家喜气溢于庭楣。昔子舆曰“曾子养志”，斯之谓与！

    Explanation

    34

    The Story of the Wu Elder's Sons and Daughters-in-Law Taking Turns to Care for Their Parents

    In Chongming, there was an elderly man from the Wu family who had four sons. Due to poverty, he sold his sons into servitude to make ends meet. As the sons grew up, they all became self-reliant, bought their freedom, married, and lived together to care for their parents. Initially, they took turns caring for their parents every month. One of the daughters-in-law said, "If we take turns every month, it will take three months before we can serve our parents, which is too infrequent. We should take turns every day." They then started a system where each son took care of the parents for one day at a time, but even this was found to be too infrequent. They decided to take turns based on meals: if the eldest son took care of them for breakfast, the second son would do so for lunch, the third son for dinner, and the fourth son would take care of them for breakfast the next day. This cycle continued. On the 5th and 10th days of the month, the four sons would gather in the central hall to prepare a meal for their parents, with the parents seated facing south, the four sons and their grandsons to the east, and the four daughters-in-law and their granddaughters to the west. They would then take turns offering wine to wish their parents longevity.

    In the area where the elderly man ate, there was a kitchen behind it. Each family placed a string of coins in the kitchen. After each meal, the elderly man would reach into the kitchen and randomly take a string of coins, go to the market, and playfully buy fruit and cakes to eat. The kitchen never ran out of money, as the sons secretly replenished it without letting the elderly man know. Occasionally, the elderly man would visit his friends or play games like Go or dice. The four sons knew where he was going, so they secretly brought him two or three hundred coins and instructed the places he visited to give them to the elderly man. If he won, he would return happily, holding the coins and telling his young grandsons. Sometimes, he would buy toys or snacks for the children as a treat. He also knew that his sons were providing him with the money. He often spent the entire day in a state of contentment, and the joy of the family overflowed from the courtyard. As the ancient sage Zi Yu said, "Zengzi nurtured his parents' spirits," this is what it means.

    35

    老人年九十九，妇年九十七，长子七十七，次子七十六，余皆颁白，五世一堂。曾、元绕膝，约二十余人。
    崇明镇刘公兆表其门曰：“百令夫妇，齐眉五世，儿孙绕膝，此岂非人生第一乐事哉！”凡为人子者，皆当如是竭力尽孝，及时奉养。诚以軎在此，而惧亦在此。不见世之失怙者乎，欲孝父而何追也？不见世之失恃者乎，欲孝母而何由也？甚至双亲永诀，劬劳之恩徒存梦想，又何可言？世有居高官，食厚禄，席丰履厚，父母已不获身受其奉，回忆贫贱时，又不克以甘旨承欢。即今日椎牛诹祭，而黄土长埋，绿醑空奠：“一滴何曾到九泉”，不更令我恸不能禁，泪尽而继之以血乎！吴老人诸子之传，可以风矣。

    Explanation

    35

    The old man was ninety-nine years old, and his wife was ninety-seven. Their eldest son was seventy-seven, and their second son was seventy-six. The rest of the family, including grandchildren and great-grandchildren, were all gray-haired, living together in five generations under one roof. There were about twenty people in total, with grandchildren and great-grandchildren surrounding the elders.

    Mr. Liu of Chongming Town inscribed the gate with the words: "A couple blessed with a hundred years, harmonious for five generations, with children and grandchildren surrounding them—this is not the greatest joy of life?" All sons should strive to be filial, exerting all efforts to fulfill filial piety and providing timely support and care. The moment is here, and the fear is also here. Have you not seen those who have lost their fathers and can no longer honor them? Have you not seen those who have lost their mothers and can no longer honor them? Even when both parents have passed away, the efforts of filial piety are only a dream, and what can be said? There are those who hold high positions, enjoy generous salaries, and live in abundance, but their parents have not been able to share in their success. When they recall their days of poverty, they cannot even offer delicious food to bring joy to their parents. Even if they sacrifice a bull and consult the spirits today, the parents are long buried in the ground, and the offerings of green wine are empty. "Not a single drop has ever reached the netherworld"—does this not make me weep uncontrollably, until my tears are exhausted and replaced by blood? The accounts of the sons of the old man Wu serve as an example to inspire others.

    36

    (此段文字，如和靖诗。)

    封邱陈女纪事
    曰豫封邱，二人为俦。不出其乡，农家者流。略纪姓氏，曰陈与刘。声气投洽，往还绸缪。如兄如弟，相爱相将。朝偕轵里，暮聚井乡。三里而遥，衡宇相望。
    陈育一女，刘诞一郎。相与谈宴，约为婚姻。交换酒盏，爱割衣襟，昔为密友，今其至亲。两姓永好，愿结同心。人事变易，不可终量。生死难齐，厥有彭殇。陈也日富，而寿而康。刘也日贫，曷云沦亡。妻其以嫠，之子幼孤。数年之中，口弗可糊。陈翁古道，不以其富。女守乃贞，盟缔弗渝。岁月其梭，之子岐嶷。女兮及笄，嫁当以时。

    Explanation

    36

    In the style of 和靖's poetry, the story of the woman from Chen in Fengqiu is told:

    In the county of Fengqiu in Yu, two people became companions. They did not leave their hometown and were of peasant origin. Their surnames were briefly recorded as Chen and Liu. They were harmonious in voice and spirit, frequently visiting each other in an intimate manner. Like brothers, they loved and supported each other. In the morning they gathered in the same village, in the evening they met in the same well. Though three miles apart, their houses faced each other.

    Chen raised a daughter, and Liu gave birth to a son. They talked and feasted together, and agreed to marry. They exchanged cups of wine, and affectionately cut their sleeves. Once close friends, now they were the closest relatives. The two families will always be well, and they wished to unite their hearts. Human affairs change, and cannot be fully measured. Life and death are hard to equal, and there are those who live long and those who die young. Chen became increasingly wealthy, and lived long and healthy. Liu became increasingly poor, and what can be said about his demise? His wife became a widow, and their son was an orphan. In several years, they could barely make ends meet. The old man Chen, adhering to ancient ways, did not exploit his wealth. The daughter maintained her chastity, and the vows were not broken. Time flies like a shuttle, and the son grew wise and capable. The daughter has come of age, and it is time for her to marry.

    37

    刘母心惄，念兹冻饥。萧萧四壁，儿何以妻。适有大买，欲佣老妇。浣洗炊厨，十千而募。刘母忻然，往投其户。取缗与儿，亟其完娶。子负镪归，乃易衣裳。葺尔舍宇，洁尔酒浆。乞诸邻里，借彼车行。陈女于归，夜其未央。入此室处，欲拜高堂。皇皇四顾，问我姑嫜。刘曰我母，佣而求赏。然后得娶，言罢心仿。女曰我郎，何须忱切。我有余资，完彼富室。我母其旋，同侍朝夕。以安子心，尽我妇职。
    翌日之辰，女曰归宁。往彼母家，密挟私金。置诸缇筐，覆之饼蒸。及暮而还，女也一人。来叩我门，胠箧扃关。询诸其邻，婿在田间。女手欲倦，庋筐石端。莲步出村，招招夫还。邻窥其远，为之倒倾。始贪菜馐，倏睹多金。利令心愦，慢藏抽身。

    Explanation

    37

    Liu's mother was grieved, thinking of her son's cold and hunger. Her house was bare and desolate, with four empty walls; how could her son marry? Just then, a big buyer came, wanting to hire an old woman for washing, cooking, and kitchen work, offering ten thousand coins. Liu's mother was happy and went to the buyer's house. She took the money and gave it to her son, urging him to quickly complete the marriage. The son returned with money and changed into new clothes. He rebuilt their house and cleaned the wine and drinks. He asked the neighbors for help and borrowed a cart. Chen's daughter returned to her husband's home at night. She entered the room and wanted to pay respects to her in-laws. She looked around anxiously and asked about her mother-in-law and father-in-law. Liu said, "My mother was hired and asked for a reward." After that, he was able to marry, but after saying this, his heart was troubled. The daughter said to her husband, "Why do you need to be so earnest?" She had extra money and would complete the marriage for a wealthy family. Her mother should return, and they would serve together day and night to comfort his heart and fulfill her duties as a wife.

    The next day, the daughter said she was going back to her parents' home. She secretly carried private money, putting it in a red basket and covering it with steamed buns. By evening, she returned alone. She knocked on the door, opened the chest, and locked the door. She asked the neighbors, who told her that her husband was in the fields. Her hands were tired, and she placed the basket on a stone. She walked gracefully out of the village, waving to her husband to return. The neighbors saw her go far and gave generously to her. Initially, they were craving vegetables and delicacies, but suddenly noticed a large sum of money. Greed made her mind confused, and she slowly concealed and left.

    38

    夫与偕返，虚筐在门。夫为启管，女入房帏。笑言宴宴，挈金而回。迅赎我母，迎奉春晖。女兮孝思，夫曰贤哉。孰知中变，谁料非灾。启视笯笼，不见其金。魂丧魄失，血泪盈盈。明知有偷，富以其邻。呼而相问，不知以应。夫曰毋庸，尔实诳许。焉得蓄存，付之筐筥。言词讥讪，女羞不语。中心恻怛，无以自处。夫也宴息，女独酸悲。
    夜如何其，徘徊以思。沉冤覆盆，何说之辞；何说之辞，胡以生为。仰视屋梁，俯解衣带。投诸其环，断情割爱。父兮母兮，生我何赖。夫兮姑兮，鉴我怨艾。鸡鸣咿晤，之子梦清。披衣瞿觉，起不见人。在榱在桷，延颈结绳。惊心却走，奔告其亲。陈翁顿颡，陈母涕洟。群奔婿所，解悬而梯。婿跽陈词，诉厥金遗。赎姑不遂，痛溢长辞。翁曰命也，嗟予爱女。婿尔何仇，宁忘旧雨。号泣相随，殉其钗履。言舁女棺，葬之村墟。

    Explanation

    38

    When he returned with her, an empty basket lay at the door. He opened the tube, and she entered the inner chambers. They spoke cheerfully, and with gold in hand, they returned. Quickly, she redeemed her mother, welcoming and serving her with the warmth of spring. The woman's filial piety was evident, and her husband praised her virtue. Who could have known that a sudden change would occur? Who could have predicted such a disaster? Upon opening the cage, they found no gold. Her soul was lost, and tears flowed freely. They knew someone had stolen it, and blamed their neighbor for their misfortune. They called out to each other, but received no response. The husband said there was no need, and she had lied to him. How could it be stored, when it was placed in the basket? Her words were mocking, and she was too ashamed to speak. Her heart was filled with deep sorrow, and she could not find a way to cope. The husband rested, while she alone was filled with deep sorrow.

    How is the night, and she wandered in thought. A deep wrong was covered over, and what words could be said? What words could be said, and why should she continue to live? She looked up at the ceiling and untied her belt. She threw herself into the ring, severing all ties and love. Father and mother, what good did you do in giving birth to me? Husband and mother-in-law, see my grievances and regrets. The rooster crowed, and the child dreamed clearly. She suddenly woke up, but found no one there. On the beams and rafters, she extended her neck and tied a rope. Shocked, she ran away, and rushed to tell her parents. Mr. Chen bowed his head, and Mrs. Chen wept. They rushed to the husband's place, untied the rope, and climbed down. The husband knelt and spoke, pleading about the lost gold. They failed to redeem the mother-in-law, and deep sorrow filled the long farewell. Mr. Chen said it was fate, and lamented his beloved daughter. What enmity do you have, husband, that you could forget our past? They wept together, and followed her in death, carrying her coffin to bury it in the village.

    39

    恶邻恶邻，又生觊觎。闻有埋衾，丧中多具。夤衣畚来，新坟顿圮。开棺出尸，剥肤拔珥。怀资竟去，尸移墓旁。皇天湛湛，洞鉴其僵。歘然女起，魂返其乡。依稀行路，曰归迷茫。黯黯古道，若识母闾。抵门呼款，父母惊惧。疑之为祟，回煞来居。女也泣告，生转非虚。父审母谛，开户始纳。
    重生相逢，悲喜言答。所失多金，邻人实挟。翁闻女云，纠党排闼。其来汹汹，奔尔邻东。倾箱倒柜，窃金出笼。更有衣饰，得自柩中。乃知其恶，厥罪重重。羁彼凶顽，讼之公庭。邑宰眦裂，笞挞交惩。按律以定，环首相停。官乃止谳，翁亦释宁。归寻其婿，女返其夫。解囊出镪，方赎其姑。

    Explanation

    39

    Evil neighbor, evil neighbor, again harboring covetous thoughts. Hearing there is a buried quilt, many items are prepared in the funeral. Dressed in secret, he comes with a basket, the new grave suddenly collapses. Opening the coffin, he takes out the body, peels off the skin, and pulls out the earrings. Carrying away the wealth, he leaves, the body moved to the side of the grave. The vast sky, clearly sees his frozen state. Suddenly the girl rises, her soul returns to her hometown. Vaguely on the road, saying to return, she is lost. Dimly on the ancient road, as if recognizing her mother's gate. Arriving at the door, calling gently, her parents are terrified. Suspecting it to be a ghost, thinking the return of the dead has come to live. The girl weeps and tells, her revival is not false. The father examines, the mother listens, they open the door to admit her. Reborn and reunited, they speak with mixed emotions. The lost gold, the neighbor indeed holds. The old man hears the girl's words, gathers a group to break in. They come fiercely, rushing to the neighbor's east. They empty the boxes and cabinets, steal the gold and leave. There are also clothes and decorations, obtained from the coffin. Thus knowing his evil, his crimes are numerous. Imprisoning the wicked, bringing him to the public court. The magistrate's eyes widen, he beats and punishes severely. According to the law, the verdict is decided, all heads nod in agreement. The official stops the trial, the old man is also released and calmed. Returning to find her husband, the girl returns to her husband. Unfastening the bag, taking out the money, to redeem her mother-in-law.

    40

    天道昭彰，善旌暴锄。生死暖昧，剖晰不模。恶兮邻人，善夫陈翁。慈如刘母，孝妇克恭。言报其慈，用惩厥凶。呜呼噫嘻，纪之谈丛。

    烈女铭(并序)
    潍邑孤山，有烈女墓。不详其姓氏里居。崇祯辛未，登兵之变起，吴桥破新城而东下也，潍人在女墙见大队整列。忽一旅数标，拥肩舆而北。约二三里许，倏而烟焰弥空。不知何故兵去，乃问，始知。
    此女自新城来，初，诸贼掠得之。强以马，不乘；强之车，不登。呼天触地，誓不欲生。诸卒以为奇货，欲追献主帅。乃觅一大轿，强舁之行。女连日不饮食，唯求一死。诸卒使同掠诸妇百方劝谕，皆不应。追及主帅，帅大叱曰：“谁教尔为此者？亟返之！”启轿，女已自劚死。乃舁野中，积薪而焚。邑之士大夫义之，为碑瘗其骨焉。铭曰：

    Explanation

    40

    The way of heaven is clearly evident, virtue is commended and evil is punished. Life and death are obscure, but must be dissected and clarified without ambiguity. The evil neighbor, the virtuous old man. As kind as Liu's mother, the filial woman is respectful. To repay her kindness with words, to punish the wicked. Alas, record it in the collection of conversations.

    Inscription for a Chaste Woman (Preface)
    On Lonely Mountain in Weizhou, there is a tomb of a chaste woman. Her name and place of origin are unknown. In the year Xinwei of Chongzhen, the rebellion of the bandits began, and Wuqiao broke through Xincheng and moved eastward. The people of Weizhou saw a large formation of troops on the defensive wall. Suddenly a group of several banners, carrying a sedan chair, moved north. After about two or three miles, suddenly smoke and flames filled the sky. No one knew why the soldiers left, so they asked, and then they learned.
    This woman came from Xincheng. Initially, the bandits captured her. They forced her to ride a horse, but she refused; they forced her into a carriage, but she refused. She cried to heaven and earth, vowing not to live. The soldiers considered her a valuable prize and wanted to present her to the chief commander. They found a large sedan chair and forced her to go in it. The woman did not eat or drink for several days, only seeking death. The soldiers tried to persuade her with the help of other captured women, but she refused. When they caught up with the chief commander, he shouted, "Who taught you to do this? Return her immediately!" When they opened the sedan chair, the woman had already slit her own throat. They carried her body to the wilderness and burned her with wood. The local scholars and officials honored her, erecting a stele and burying her bones. The inscription says:

    41

    骨如雪，心如铁。真金入炼金不折，沉香遇爇香不灭。黄犀辟尘尘不生，白璧绝玷玷不涅。浩浩元气还太虚，短碣孤山同嵽嵲。

    马姑
    崇祯末年，高杰等为乱，兖豫不靖。盗贼蜂起，肆掠城邑。掳玉帛子女，所过一空。贼寇金乡，有贼部将翻天鹞等。金乡令韩键能兵，在邑多美政。闻警，先激父老以忠，以重赏募敢死士，设战守具。及贼薄城，攻数日，不能下。夜，贼以牛车数十辆，直拥城下。贼伏辕底，挖垣。令以灰瓶硫掷车上，贼多烟毙。旦，贼哗曰：“弹丸小邑，悉力死守。得尔城不足以威，吾去矣。”遂哄而散。众曰：“寇退，应樵汲。”令不可，曰：“诈也。吾见其散而整也严备之。”日昃，闻钲声自西北来，令即率众登陴以观。逾时，尘扬马骤，旗帜鲜新。众疑之，及临城堑，声言曰：“鲁王师至，来护民。”众皆喜，即令初不料贼之伪也。

    Explanation

    41

    The Bone White, the Heart Iron

    The bone white as snow, the heart iron. True gold, when refined, does not break; sandalwood, when burned, does not lose its fragrance. Yellow rhinoceros horn repels dust, and no dust remains; white jade, flawless, does not become tainted. Vast primordial energy returns to the void, and a short stone monument and a solitary mountain stand as high as the lofty peaks.

    Mazu

    In the last year of the reign of Emperor Chongzhen, Gao Jie and others rebelled, causing unrest in the regions of Yan and Yu. Bandits rose up like swarms of bees, plundering and ravaging cities. They took away precious goods, women, and children, leaving nothing behind. The bandits attacked Jinxiang, and among them was a bandit commander known as "Fantiyao."

    The magistrate of Jinxiang, Han Jian, was skilled in military affairs and had implemented many good policies in the city. Upon hearing the alarm, he first inspired the elders with loyalty, offering heavy rewards to recruit brave soldiers and setting up defensive preparations. When the bandits approached the city and attacked for several days, they failed to capture it. At night, the bandits brought dozens of ox-drawn carts, driving them directly to the city walls. The bandits hid under the carts and dug at the walls. The magistrate threw incendiary devices at the carts, causing many bandits to die from the smoke.

    By dawn, the bandits shouted, "This small city, with all its might, is dying to defend itself. Capturing this city would not be enough to impress us. We will leave." And so, they scattered. The people said, "The bandits have retreated; we should go out to gather firewood and water." The magistrate refused, saying, "It is a trick. I see that they are still well-organized. We must remain vigilant." As the sun began to set, he heard the sound of bells coming from the northwest. The magistrate immediately led the people to the city walls to observe. After a while, dust rose and horses galloped, with fresh flags fluttering. The people were suspicious, and when they reached the city moat, the bandits announced, "The army of the Prince of Lu has arrived to protect the people." Everyone was overjoyed, but the magistrate initially did not realize that the bandits had disguised themselves as the Prince of Lu's army.

    42

    方欲启管，忽队中一妇女，颧面猿臂，骑铲马冲而出，大呼曰：“是贼也，将赚尔城。何王师之有！”贼闻之怒，围之三匝，脔斩于马下。令与城上人皆见之，守益力。贼无计，乃去。三日，士民出城敛其尸，视衣幅上有小字一行云：“济宁城南马防屯马思敬之女，誓不从贼。”邑人感其义，葬而祀焉。颜曰：“忠义烈马姑祠。”
    前不载邑乘。闻济宁潘兆遴《芳晨小记》有之。今秀水盛百二修《济宁志》载入此条，惜太略。呜呼，妇人女子之德，恭顺慈贞以为贤；至若流离颠沛，明大义，救全城，勇烈凛凛，此须眉丈夫之所难能。马姑之行，虽古仲连何以加兹，况又蹈郦生之祸也哉！

    张烈妇

    Explanation

    42

    As they were about to open the tube, suddenly a woman emerged from the ranks, with prominent cheekbones and monkey-like arms, riding a plow-horse and charging out. She shouted loudly, "These are the rebels, planning to deceive your city. Where is the imperial army?" The rebels, upon hearing this, became furious and surrounded her three times before cutting her into pieces right there on the horse. The order was given that all the people on the city walls should witness this, and as a result, the defenders fought even harder. The rebels, having no strategy left, retreated. Three days later, the townspeople went out of the city to collect her body. On her clothing, they found a line of small writing that read, "Daughter of Ma Sijing from the Ma Fang屯 in the south of Jining, swearing not to follow the rebels." The people of the town were moved by her loyalty and buried her, building a temple to honor her. They named it the "Temple of the Loyal, Righteous, and Valiant Lady Magu."

    This story was not recorded in the local annals. It is said that it was mentioned in Pan Zhaolin's Fangchen Xiaji from Jining. Today, it has been included in the Jining Zhi edited by Sheng Bai'er from Xiu Shui, but it is too brief. Alas, the virtues of women, which are considered noble when they are respectful, obedient, compassionate, and chaste, are even more remarkable when they are displaced and in distress, yet they understand great righteousness, save the entire city, and display bravery and valor that even men find difficult to match. Lady Magu's actions, though compared to ancient figures like Zhonglian, are no less commendable, and she faced the same fate as Li Sheng.

    Lady Zhang, the fierce woman.

    43

    义登成山张烈妇，同邑孙士奎之妻。适孙后，不数载，孙岁试入郡，染疾甚危。烈妇闻之，即欲以死自决。未几，孙病小愈，归。然日抱沉疴，奄奄在床第。烈妇焚香告天，乞代夫死，不得死。烈妇左右药炉。五年，昼夜不少懈。孙病愈，而烈妇劳，无子，为孙纳妾。丙子，孙疾复作，烈妇日夜悲号，欲先引颈以报夫子于地下。孙曰：“妾有娠，倘得育男，我死之后，孤谁与守？”烈妇遵夫命，又不死。是月，果举一男。孙病又瘥已。冬十一月，疾大渐，不复可治。烈妇以抚孤故，不敢死。
    三年，藐孤殇，烈妇复欲死，曰：“有孤不死，守孤也，孤殇，何守？当死报夫子命。”亲故解之曰：“死后矣，死夫乎？死子乎？当日死夫，烈也；今不死子，为节也。且茕茕一柩，独不当守其晨夕耶？何取乎死！”言近义，且防之。于是烈妇又不得死。后贫甚，妾不得已，遂嫁去。烈妇独与一婢拾穗采薇，日用益苦，而节益坚。凡朔望必哭奠，有事必于柩前禀命而行。

    Explanation

    43

    Lady Zhang of Yideng Chengshan, a woman of great virtue, was the wife of Sun Shikui from the same county. After marrying Sun, she did not spend many years with him before he passed the annual examination and entered the county administration. Soon after, he contracted a serious illness. Upon hearing this, Lady Zhang immediately wished to die in order to follow her husband. Not long after, Sun's condition improved slightly and he returned home. However, he continued to suffer from a grave illness, lying bedridden and weak. Lady Zhang burned incense and prayed to heaven, asking to die in place of her husband, but she was not allowed to die. She attended to the medicine pot day and night for five years without rest. When Sun recovered, Lady Zhang was exhausted and they had no children, so Sun took a concubine. In the year of Bingzi, Sun's illness relapsed, and Lady Zhang cried and wailed day and night, wishing to stretch her neck to repay her husband in the afterlife.
