Sun said, "I am pregnant, if a boy is born, who will guard the orphan after my death?" Lady Zhang followed her husband's command and did not die. That month, she indeed gave birth to a boy. Sun's illness healed again. In the eleventh month of winter, he fell gravely ill and could no longer be treated. Lady Zhang, because of her duty to care for the orphan, dared not die.

    Three years later, the young orphan died, and Lady Zhang once again wished to die, saying, "Since there is no orphan left, what am I guarding? If the orphan died, what should I guard? I should die to repay my husband's command." Her friends and relatives tried to dissuade her, saying, "It's too late now, should you die for your husband or for your son? When you died for your husband, it was heroic; now not dying for your son is a form of chastity. And with a single coffin alone, should you not guard him day and night? What is the point of dying?" Their words were reasonable, and they also tried to prevent her. Thus, Lady Zhang could not die again. Later, they became extremely poor, and the concubine had no choice but to leave. Lady Zhang was alone with a maid, picking wild rice and herbs, and daily life became increasingly difficult, but her chastity became even stronger. On the first and fifteenth days of the lunar month, she would cry and offer sacrifices, and for any matters, she would consult the coffin and proceed accordingly.

    44

    甲申盗起，人民逃窜，烈妇仰天叹曰：“未亡人从人避乱乎？此我死时矣！”避绝粒不食，出妆奁鬻，制棺椁，营双穴，以迄柳车丹旐，无不毕备。卜葬五月六日。偏辞亲串，如归宁者。时水浆不入口，已十四日，声若金石，神色满眉睫间。至此转无一毫悲切状。知之者以为屡死不死，终不至死；不知者以为绝无死意，何至于死。五日，日昃，后事嘱切犹子侄辈。夜半呼婢子出，闭户。六日，昧爽，启视，端坐孙子柩旁，白练绕颈，竟瞑目含笑死。
    先是，一犬当烈妇绝粒时，犬亦不食。烈妇语之曰：“吾将死，与尔别觅一主栖托，可乎？”犬呜咽，掉尾，若不忍去。至是，犬亦死。
    呜呼，忠臣节妇，有始矢一死，而终竟不死，有初事逶迤，而终能决然一死者。虽曰性也，亦有命焉。因缘机会一不凑合，则不能死，且不敢死。烈妇屡死不死，而终于一死。可谓当死而死，是死固其性也，亦死之而得正命者矣。

    Explanation

    44

    In the year of Jia Shen, when bandits rose and the people fled, a noblewoman sighed to the heavens and said, "Am I to follow others in fleeing from chaos? This is the time for my death!" She abstained from food, sold her dowry, made a coffin and prepared a double grave, and arranged for the funeral carriage and the red banner, everything was fully prepared. She chose the date for burial as May 6th. She bid farewell to her relatives, as if returning home. At this time, she had not eaten or drunk water for fourteen days, her voice was as clear as gold and stone, and her spirit was evident in her eyes and eyebrows. Here, she showed no signs of sorrow or distress. Those who knew her thought she had many opportunities to die but ultimately chose to die; those who did not know her thought she had no intention of dying, so why did she die? On the fifth day, at midday, she entrusted her after affairs to her nephews and younger brothers. At midnight, she called out to her maid and closed the door. On the sixth day, at dawn, when the door was opened, she was seen sitting upright beside her grandson's coffin, with a white cloth tied around her neck, and she died with closed eyes and a smile.

    Before this, when the noblewoman abstained from food, a dog also refused to eat. The noblewoman spoke to the dog, saying, "I am going to die, can you find another master to rely on?" The dog wept and wagged its tail, as if reluctant to leave. When this happened, the dog also died.

    Alas, there are loyal ministers and chaste wives who vow to die at the beginning but never do, and there are those who initially hesitate but ultimately decide to die resolutely. Although it is said to be their nature, it also depends on fate. If the right opportunity does not align, they cannot die, and they dare not die. The noblewoman had many opportunities to die but ultimately chose to die. She can be said to have died at the right time, and her death was indeed her nature, and she died in a way that was fitting.

    45

    (其笔意奇绝，可与烈妇俱传。)

    义夫烈妇
    莱州雄崖守御所屯民陈三义，幼聘同里女王氏，已而氏病，目失明。氏父谓陈：“吾女瞽，不可妻。”图辞婚，三义执不可，卒娶瞽女归。伉俪笃甚。
    一日，氏晨起，讶目中有光，渐辨物，久之，炯炯如幼时。当三义之娶瞽女也，里中或义之，或以为非人情，有匿笑者，至是咸惊叹，谓夭实怜其义而使之明也。无何，三义家日落，负贩转徙，滞京师十年余。氏键户纫针，恃十指自活。
    岁甲戌八月，三义客死。氏闻讣，长号绝食，请其亲党易所居室。鬻棺二，作三义木主纳一棺，其一自殓。分室中敝衣物以酬瘗葬者。亲党惊怪，且劝阻百端。氏哽咽曰：“吾夫义，不瞽弃我。我何忍独活！”闻者皆泣下。九月自缢死。

    Explanation

    45

    The style of his writing is extraordinarily unique, worthy of being passed down alongside that of a chaste widow.

    Chaste Widow and Loyal Husband
    In the town of Xingya, a garrison under the command of the Xingya Defense Post in Laizhou, there lived a man named Chen San Yi. As a young man, he was betrothed to a woman from the same village, Lady Wang. However, she fell ill and became blind. Her father told Chen, "My daughter is blind; she cannot be a wife." Chen, however, refused to break off the engagement, insisting that he would marry her regardless. Eventually, he married the blind woman and brought her home. Their marriage was very close and devoted.

    One morning, the woman woke up and was surprised to find light in her eyes. Gradually, she could distinguish objects, and after some time, her vision was as clear as it had been in her childhood. When Chen San Yi married the blind woman, some in the village praised his loyalty, while others thought it was against human nature, and there were even those who secretly laughed. However, everyone was amazed and praised him, saying that heaven truly favored his virtue by restoring her sight.

    Soon after, Chen San Yi's family fortunes declined, and he had to engage in petty trade and move around. He remained in the capital for more than ten years. His wife, Lady Wang, locked herself in her room and sewed, relying on her own hands to make a living.

    In the year Jia Xu, during the eighth month, Chen San Yi died as a guest in the capital. Upon hearing the news, Lady Wang wept bitterly and refused to eat. She asked her relatives to exchange their house. She sold two coffins, made a tablet for Chen San Yi, and placed it in one coffin. She prepared her own body for burial in the other. She divided the old clothes in her room to reward those who buried her. Her relatives were shocked and tried to dissuade her in many ways. Lady Wang, choking with sobs, said, "My husband was loyal, and he did not abandon me when I was blind. How can I bear to live alone?" Those who heard her all wept. In the ninth month, she committed suicide by hanging.

    46

    呜呼！三义不弃瞽女，其瞽复明，是天不难取已瞽之目，使之复明；何独不能使三义有中人产，夫妇白首牖下！虽然，三义不穷，则不客死；不客死，则氏不能以烈见。天或者使义夫烈妇相报若影响，以厉世而磨钝，未可知也。时学使刘公嘉其事，檄司是土者转三义榇归，与王氏合葬焉。

    金贞女传
    贞女金氏，江阴观山村人也。世为农家，幼许婚于武进杨氏子。子十岁，忽失去，其母寻之不得，久绝影音。遂来金家，为金翁言：“儿子亡矣，大约为奸拐所略卖，否则为虎蛇所吞噬。吾不忍淑女劳华，摽梅期愆。请返聘书，另择高门可也。”
    金翁归，从容为女言之。女曰“不可”。翁不听，强谋择婿。女涕泣，以死自誓。父怒曰：“我不能畜汝，农家谁不食力，尔能耕乎？”女曰“能”。使同诸兄力作，女则躬胼胝。祈寒署雨，勤恳过于男子，无怨言。父视其意决不可回，乃动怜念。翁有四子，各分田十亩，以五亩分女。女遂安焉。

    Explanation

    46

    Translation:

    Alas! The three righteous ones did not abandon the blind girl, and her blindness was restored. If God could restore the girl's sight, then it is not beyond divine power to do other things; why could not God also make the three righteous ones have normal children and live out their lives together? Yet, if the three righteous ones were not poor, they would not have died as travelers; if they did not die as travelers, the lady could not be seen as chaste. Perhaps God arranged for the virtuous husband and chaste widow to repay each other like echoes, to warn the world and sharpen dullness, and this is unknown. At that time, the provincial examiner Mr. Liu praised the matter, and issued an edict to the local officials to return the coffins of the three righteous ones to their hometown, and bury them together with the Wang family.

    The Story of Jin's Chaste Maiden

    Jin's Chaste Maiden was from the village of Guanshan in Jiangyin. Her family had been farmers for generations. When she was young, she was betrothed to a son of the Yang family in Wujin. When the boy was ten years old, he suddenly disappeared. His mother searched for him but could not find him, and soon lost all contact. She then came to Jin's house and told Jin's father: "My son has died. He was likely abducted by criminals and sold, or perhaps eaten by tigers or snakes. I cannot bear to see my virtuous daughter suffer, and the time for her marriage has passed. Please return the betrothal documents, and let her choose a better family."

    Jin's father returned home and calmly told his daughter about the situation. The daughter said, "I cannot do that." The father did not listen and insisted on arranging another marriage for her. The daughter wept bitterly and vowed to die rather than agree. Her father became angry and said, "I cannot keep you if you refuse. In our family, who does not work hard? Can you plow the fields?" The daughter replied, "I can." He made her work alongside her brothers, and she worked harder than any of the men, without complaint. Seeing her determination, her father felt pity. Jin had four sons, and each was given ten acres of land. He gave five acres to his daughter. The daughter then settled down.

    47

    杨母又来言：“近得儿子消息，言被人赚去，流转于浙东。今剃发于天台某寺为僧，无株待也。”女乃见杨母，曰：“母之子不犹在乎？盍寻之归？儿坚守至今，愿终为母家妇，无他适之理也。”杨母深感其贞，且并以乏资寻找，告女助以金。寻之，则僧出游，闲云野鹤，无定踪焉。
    数年中，杨母贫益困，尝携少子来女家。女厚给之，至母殁不衰。女为人强力俭啬，历年置沃产将百亩。因自作蔬，遣人往天台供佛饭僧，冀杨氏子知之而返也。其略云：“常州府江阴县观山村金女，未适武进之杨氏，皈心志礼于四大法王牟尼释迦诸佛。前氏以未嫁，夫当龆龀，出亡不归。今四十余年行将就木。呜呼，女未嫁而守，夫不死而为嫠者也。闻杨氏夫在台为僧，访寻又不得耗，岂辞世乎？抑尚在人间乎？今姑且贫死，我之以为姑者，即杨氏夫所自出之母，生我之爱之谓何？且杨门无可抚之孤，其先人将为若敖之鬼矣！顾晋瓣香，广施大众菩萨鉴照愚忧。”云云。

    47

    Yang's mother came again and said, "Recently, I received news of my son, who was deceived and has been wandering in eastern Zhejiang. He has now shaved his head and become a monk at a certain temple in Tian台, with no means of support." The girl then saw Yang's mother and said, "Is your son not still alive? Why not seek him and bring him back? I have remained steadfast until now, wishing to remain forever as a daughter-in-law of the mother's family, with no intention of remarrying." Yang's mother deeply admired her chastity and, also due to her own lack of funds for searching, asked the girl to help with money. When they searched for him, the monk was out traveling, like a cloud in the sky or a crane in the wild, without a fixed trace.

    Over the years, Yang's mother became increasingly poor and often brought her younger son to the girl's home. The girl generously provided for them, even after her mother's death. The girl was strong-willed and thrifty, accumulating a fertile property of nearly a hundred acres over the years. She would personally prepare vegetables and send people to Tian台 to offer food and support to the monks, hoping that Yang's son would learn of this and return. Her letter roughly stated: "Gold Girl from Guanshan Village, Jiangyin County, Changzhou Prefecture, has not yet married into the Yang family of Wujin, and has devoted herself to the worship of the Four Great Kings of Buddhism, including the Buddha (Shakyamuni). Before, her husband, who was still a child, went away and did not return. Now, over forty years old and nearing the end of life, I say, 'Alas, I remain chaste while unmarried, and my husband, though not dead, is like a widower.' I heard that Yang's son is a monk in Tian台, but my search has yielded no news. Has he passed away? Or is he still in the world? I will die in poverty, but the one who considers me as her aunt is the mother from whom Yang's son was born. What does it mean for me, who was born and loved by her? Moreover, the Yang family has no orphan to care for, and their ancestors will be like the ghosts of Ifao! I offer my incense, hoping that the great bodhisattvas will see my humble concern."

    48

    村中，一日忽有一僧，须发皓然，自言杨氏子，来自天台。感金女义而恤其母，望门稽首，不敢请见。女知之，使人问之曰：“师何来暮也？独不堕今生孽乎？”僧反命曰：“此前世因也。”女又使人告之曰：“师之母死，已土侵，想师在莲花座上，当不念地狱中苦。试问灵台方寸中，师亦有过不去处否？”僧闻言汗下，怃然而为问曰：“女圣贤命我矣！”遂去，不知所终。女年至七十余。有兄子九人，各分余田十余亩，使营葬祭云。

    Explanation

    48

    In the village, one day there suddenly appeared a monk with white beard and hair, who introduced himself as a descendant of the Yang family, coming from TianTai. Grateful for the kindness of the woman named Jin Nü towards his mother, he bowed respectfully at the door but dared not ask for an audience. The woman learned of his presence and sent someone to inquire, asking, "Why have you come so late, Master? Are you not falling into the sins of this life?" The monk replied through the messenger, "These are the results of my past actions." The woman then sent another message, saying, "Your mother has passed away and is now buried. I imagine you are seated on a lotus throne, not thinking about the suffering in hell. Let me ask you, do you still have unresolved issues in your heart?" Upon hearing these words, the monk broke out in a sweat and, looking thoughtful, asked, "The virtuous woman has given me a profound lesson!" He then left, and his final destination was unknown. The woman lived to be over seventy years old and had nine nephews. Each nephew received more than ten acres of land, and they were instructed to manage the burial and memorial services.

    49

    (夏虚泉曰：“古者女嫁，有吉日而夫死，女服斩衰而吊。既葬，除之，无守贞之说也。”盖圣人缘情定礼，不强人以所难耳。然有人行人之所难，岂不可贵！故后世旌闾之典，同于节妇焉。顾凡贞女必未嫁夫死，奔丧守贞，孝养其父母。
    今金氏又贞女之变者矣。夫当杨母告绝，及知婿已为僧，理固可以不变。父迫之嫁，势又不得以守，而断断乎守之。倘所谓过于中行者耶？然终始冀其婿之来，而坚守以待，竭力躬耕，卒养其姑以终老。其介性所至，是难能也！是难能也！)

    Explanation

    49

    Xia Xuquan said: "In ancient times, if a woman was to be married on a good day but her husband died, she would wear the heaviest mourning attire and attend the funeral. After the burial, she would end the mourning period, and there was no requirement for her to remain chaste." It is said that sages establish rituals based on human emotions and do not impose unreasonable demands. However, those who go beyond what is expected are highly commendable! Therefore, in later generations, the tradition of honoring widows who maintain their chastity is similar to that of chaste women. Generally, a chaste woman must have died before her marriage, mourn her husband, remain chaste, and care for her parents.

    Now, Jin's case is a variation of the typical chaste woman. When Jin's mother-in-law rejected her, and she learned that her fiancé had become a monk, she could have remained unchanged. Her father forced her to marry, and she could not refuse, but she firmly decided to remain chaste. Is this what is meant by going beyond the middle way? Nevertheless, she persisted in hoping for her fiancé's return, remained steadfast, worked hard at farming, and ultimately cared for her mother-in-law until her death. Her steadfast nature is rare and admirable! Indeed, it is rare and admirable!

    50

    陈戍节妇
    妇，甘肃回部落人，为常州江阴陈四之妻。陈获罪而戍于边，故娶之。生二子焉。惟是远徼穷边，人烟绝少，阴风尽号，朔雷夏飞。不则饥餐青稞，渴饮潼酪。晓暮不闻鸡犬，但闻野马之群嘶。至其地者，虽负强力、拥厚资，无不为之消沮竦飒，丧厥生平。况茕茕一戍如陈四者，又乌足道。然其得以优游化外十余年，受妻子庸庸之福者，盖非陈妇之力不及此。
    有年，邀恩放陈，得赦。陈故有母在籍，今幸得归，乃遘疾不能疗。濒危，谓妇曰：“天乎？命也？生为异域之人，死犹不免他乡之鬼。哀哀我母，十年违定省，边庭音耗断绝，以为遇赦得归，一见慈颜。何期病入膏肓，捶床抚胸，生还无日，伤如之何！”妇曰：“无忧，汝但将息。汝既有此孝心，汝生，汝行之；汝即不生，我成之。我以一匹缣裹君骸骨，返吴门见母也。我岂效人家妇使妪，煦得小儿度日子，即称完节人哉？”陈泣谢曰：“我死得瞑目矣！”翌旦，遂死。

    Explanation

    50

    陈戍节妇
    妇，甘肃回部落人，为常州江阴陈四之妻。陈获罪而戍于边，故娶之。生二子焉。惟是远徼穷边，人烟绝少，阴风尽号，朔雷夏飞。不则饥餐青稞，渴饮潼酪。晓暮不闻鸡犬，但闻野马之群嘶。至其地者，虽负强力、拥厚资，无不为之消沮竦飒，丧厥生平。况茕茕一戍如陈四者，又乌足道。然其得以优游化外十余年，受妻子庸庸之福者，盖非陈妇之力不及此。
    有年，邀恩放陈，得赦。陈故有母在籍，今幸得归，乃遘疾不能疗。濒危，谓妇曰：“天乎？命也？生为异域之人，死犹不免他乡之鬼。哀哀我母，十年违定省，边庭音耗断绝，以为遇赦得归，一见慈颜。何期病入膏肓，捶床抚胸，生还无日，伤如之何！”妇曰：“无忧，汝但将息。汝既有此孝心，汝生，汝行之；汝即不生，我成之。我以一匹缣裹君骸骨，返吴门见母也。我岂效人家妇使妪，煦得小儿度日子，即称完节人哉？”陈泣谢曰：“我死得瞑目矣！”翌旦，遂死。

    The Chosen Wife of Chen Shu
    The woman was from the Hui tribe of Gansu province and was the wife of Chen Si from Jiangyin, Changzhou. Chen was exiled to the frontier due to his crimes, and thus she married him. They had two sons. The frontier was remote and desolate, with few people, and the cold winds howled while summer thunderstorms raged. They often had to eat barley and drink dairy products, and they could not hear the sounds of chickens or dogs, only the distant neighing of wild horses. Even those who were strong and wealthy could not help but feel discouraged and lose their spirits upon arriving there. How much more so for Chen Si, who was alone and exiled? Yet, he was able to live there for over a decade, enjoying the simple happiness of family life, which could not have been possible without the efforts of his wife.
    One year, Chen was granted clemency and released. He had an elderly mother who was still alive, and he was fortunate to return home. However, he fell ill and could not be cured. As he lay on the brink of death, he told his wife, "Alas, is this fate? I was born in a foreign land, and even in death, I cannot escape being a ghost in a strange place. My dear mother, I have been away from her for ten years, unable to fulfill my filial duties. The news from the frontier has been cut off, and I thought that with clemency, I would be able to return and see her once more. Who would have thought that I am now gravely ill, pounding the bed and clutching my chest, with no hope of returning alive, what sorrow!" The wife said, "Do not worry, just take care of yourself. You have such filial devotion, if you live, you will do it yourself; if you do not live, I will carry it out. I will wrap your bones in a piece of silk and return to Suzhou to see your mother. How could I be like other women who remarry after their husbands die, live a comfortable life, and then claim to be a woman of perfect virtue?" Chen wept and thanked her, saying, "I can die with peace of mind!" The next day, he died.

    51

    妇请于官，求咨负殖归吴。西陲荒窎，一邑境常数百里。妇又步如飞，护役皆瞠乎后也。一日暮，遄行不得栖所。二役曰：“惫矣。盍夜宿。”妇不可，役皆呶呶，不欲走。妇喝曰：“汝等以我为囚耶？”遂批一役，如弄小儿。众乞饶曰：“愿走。”妇负骨抱儿，役为之负一儿，行冥冥中。山僻不辨凸凹，妇前导，喑呜叱咤，以壮之。俄见一灯，荧荧在望。妇喜，谓役曰：“都向灯处走。”遂踉跄行，不解倦，而东方渐白。歘见一斑斓大虎，跃入丛莽，咆哮而去。乃知即此照行人者，众咸以为神。自此不惟畏妇，实且敬之。

    Explanation

    51

    The woman petitioned the official, requesting permission to take her deceased husband's body back to Wu. The western frontier was remote and desolate, with the territory of one county often spanning hundreds of miles. The woman walked as fast as flying, leaving the guards far behind. One evening, as they hurried along, they could not find a place to rest. Two of the guards said, "We are tired. Why not stay for the night?" The woman refused, and the guards, grumbling, did not want to continue. She shouted, "Do you think of me as a prisoner?" Then she struck one of the guards, treating him like a child. The others pleaded for mercy, saying, "We will keep going." The woman carried her husband's bones and held her child, while the guards carried another child, walking through the darkness. In the remote mountains, it was hard to distinguish the terrain, but the woman led the way, roaring and shouting to boost morale. Soon she saw a faint light in the distance. Delighted, she told the guards, "Let's all head toward the light." They staggered forward, never tiring, and the eastern sky began to lighten. Suddenly, they saw a striped tiger, which leaped into the dense undergrowth and roared away. They then realized it was the one causing trouble for travelers, and everyone considered her a deity. From then on, the guards not only feared the woman but also respected her.

    52

    至山西首站。役携妇投文，并告其夜行之事。官异之，赐妇以金，长批递送之，外复致书前途，为之旌焉。故路多余资。抵江南，邑尹青州蔡公澎闻其事，异其人，令其入内宅，为之风示闺阃。太夫人及夫人群拔钗珥，多所赠遗云。其人面长颧高，鼻钩目圆，漆身修伟如丈夫，余亦了了，不异于人。
    呜呼！古今之天于英奇瑰特，往往置之寥廓之区。又或不畀于男子，而钟于妇人。岂天之不爱才耶？抑才之不限地以生耶？彼陈妇所为，其高出华周杞梁之妻又乌可以道里计哉？当时自大尹以下，皆有所赐。

    Explanation

    52

    Upon arriving at the first station in Shanxi, the laborer brought his wife to submit a document, along with an account of their night journey. The official found this strange and rewarded the wife with gold, sending a lengthy letter along with it, and also dispatched a message ahead to commend them. As a result, there was extra money on the road. Upon reaching Jiangnan, the magistrate of Qingzhou, Cai Gong Peng, heard of the incident and found the person remarkable. He ordered her to enter the inner quarters of his residence, setting an example for women. The lady and her entourage of women removed their hair ornaments, giving her many gifts. The person had a long face, high cheekbones, a hooked nose, and round eyes, painted her body, and was tall and strong like a man, but otherwise was quite normal.

    Alas! Throughout history, the heavens have often favored extraordinary and unique talents, placing them in remote areas. Sometimes, they are not bestowed upon men but upon women. Is it that heaven does not love talent? Or is it that talent is not limited by place of birth? What she did surpasses the deeds of the wives of Hua Zhou and Qi Liang, how can it be measured in terms of distance? At that time, from the magistrate down, all were given gifts.

    53

    妇式闾里，问其老母，已终，堂在殡有年矣。妇为姑及夫营葬。乡人义之，乐为佽助。并得所赐金，置屋数椽。田几亩，大尹表其门曰：“健节可风。”妇六十余死。其二子不才，惟灌园作菜佣。抑岂其笃生不凡，英雄无用之之地，而以畎亩终欤？

    薛鲁氏
    寿张薛志仁妻鲁氏，生一女。薛故家子，读书未成名，年二十，妻同庚。
    一日，薛以事入城。归暮，雨阻，宿外村。薛有中表弟霍某，亦以事遇雨，奔薛村，叩户求宿。鲁氏曰：“兄不在家，盍他往？”雨盆注，霍请以爨下歇。鲁许之。晨霍起，隔窗呼曰：“弟去矣，”鲁应曰：“反关笆篱。”薛归，见门未扃，院有男子履迹。正猜疑间，鲁氏又曰：“尚未去耶？”薛不答，鲁氏开门。薛怒入，曰：“昨夜何人酣睡卧榻？”鲁亦忿曰：“私汉耳！”薛入厨，持刀相向，妻笑曰：“拿贼拿赃，捉奸捉双。杀一人是诬也，有罪。”薛语塞，掷刀，出门。既而悔之，又不任咎。

    Explanation

    53

    The woman respected the neighborhood, asking about her old mother, who had passed away, and noting that the hall had been in mourning for many years. She arranged the burial for her mother-in-law and husband. The villagers admired her and were willing to help. With the aid they received, she built several rooms and acquired a few acres of land. The governor inscribed at the gate: "Her strong virtue is worthy of praise." The woman died in her sixties. Her two sons were not capable, only tending the garden and working as vegetable sellers. Or is it that she was born with great virtue but found no place to display it, and thus ended her life tending fields?

    Xue Lushi
    Luoshi, the wife of Xue Zhiren from Shouzhang, gave birth to a daughter. Xue was a descendant of a noble family, but he did not achieve fame through his studies and was twenty years old when he married a woman of the same age.
    One day, Xue went into the city for an errand. Returning late in the evening, he was caught in the rain and stayed at a village outside the town. Xue had a cousin, Huo, who also encountered rain while on an errand and rushed to Xue's village, knocking on the door to request lodging. Luoshi said, "My husband is not at home, why don't you go elsewhere?" The rain was pouring down, and Huo asked to rest under the stove. Luoshi agreed. In the morning, Huo got up and called through the window, "I'm leaving now," to which Luoshi replied, "Close the gate and the bamboo fence." When Xue returned, he saw that the door was not locked and noticed footprints of a man in the courtyard. As he was guessing and suspecting, Luoshi said again, "Have you not left yet?" Xue did not answer, and Luoshi opened the door. Xue angrily entered and asked, "Who was sleeping on the bed last night?" Luoshi also became angry and said, "It was a private man!" Xue went to the kitchen, holding a knife toward her, and his wife laughed, saying, "To catch a thief, you need evidence; to catch an adulterer, you need witnesses. Killing one person is slander, and you are guilty." Xue was speechless, threw down the knife, and went out. Later, he regretted it but could not take responsibility for his actions.

    54

    抵京师，无所事。有卖布放印钱者，多山东人。薛与识，代其奔走劳。陕人梅某，寓张相公庙，设局放钱。操印子者，皆假梅以归其息。时见薛，与语，察其诚笃，能书算。询其家，对以孤，父母荒岁皆饿死。梅曰：“盍就食于我？”薛拜谢，遂随梅。数年，薛事梅犹父。梅故无子，后梅携薛归陕。梅老迈不出门，义子之。遂姓梅，名雪。复入都，竟改父业，以资斡旋一部书，期满得山西杂职。买妾施氏，生一子。善夤缘，升榆次令。梅老就养，卒于官。

    Explanation

    54

    Upon arriving in the capital, Xue found himself without any occupation. There were many people from Shandong who sold cloth and issued printed money. Xue became acquainted with them and assisted them in their errands and labor. A man named Mei from Shaanxi lived in the temple of Zhang the High Official and set up an office to issue money. Those who handled the seals lent money to people from Shandong under Mei's name to earn interest. Xue frequently met with Mei, talked to him, and found him sincere and honest. Xue could write and calculate. When asked about his family, he replied that he was an orphan, with both parents having starved to death during a famine. Mei said, "Why not come and eat with me?" Xue bowed and thanked him, and so followed Mei. After several years, Xue served Mei as if he were his father. Mei had no children, so later he took Xue back to Shaanxi. As Mei grew old and weak, he treated Xue as his adopted son. So Xue took the surname Mei and was named Xue. He returned to the capital, eventually changed his father's profession, and used his resources to mediate a book, completing the term and obtaining a miscellaneous position in Shanxi. He bought a concubine named Shi, who gave birth to a son. Xue was good at networking and was promoted to the magistrate of Yuci. Mei grew old and was taken care of, dying in office.

    55

    薛丁外艰，来寓京师。薛偶于顺治门，见一人似霍。霍亦伫视薛，薛曰：“非霍家表弟耶？”霍曰：“二十年，兄何处去！”薛携霍至一酒楼，命仆归寓。问霍曰：“吾家固无恙乎？”霍曰：“诬嫂哉，诬嫂哉！当日雨中之印履，是我留踪，帘外之启闩，伊谁假乎？淅沥止而弟遄行，猜疑见而兄难发。生语猝投，操戈相向。既乃飘然远引决去，无端彼在室者，廿年守不志之贞。襁褓者及笄，误于归之候。兄独何心，不念穷庐中尚有沉渊之苦节哉！”薛闻言泣下，归寓摒挡行李，克日偕霍东归。

    Explanation

    55

    薛丁因父亲去世而回到京城。一天，薛丁在顺治门偶然遇见一个人，看起来很像霍。霍也盯着薛丁看，薛丁说：“难道不是霍家的表弟吗？”霍说：“二十年了，哥哥你去哪儿了！”薛丁带霍到一家酒楼，吩咐仆人回去收拾行李。薛丁问霍：“我们家还好吗？”霍说：“冤枉嫂子了，冤枉嫂子了！那天雨中的脚印，是我留下的，门外的锁是谁开的？雨停了，我匆匆离去，哥哥你却因为猜疑而难以启齿。突然间说出的话，就像拿起武器相对。之后我飘然远去，不知为什么，那个未婚的嫂子，二十年来坚守着不屈的贞操。孩子从小到大，错过了归家的时机。哥哥你到底怎么想的，难道不记得穷苦人家中还有深沉的苦节吗！”薛丁听后泪流满面，回到住处收拾行李，决定尽快与霍一起东归。

    56

    至家门，薛与霍入。鲁见之大骂曰：“何物伧楚，无故入人家。岂不闻寡妇之门，无疾风暴雨耶？”薛曰：“我薛志仁。”鲁曰：“薛郎以我不贞，绝裾去，客死久矣。何得复有其人？”霍为之缓颊，备言其悔，兼述廿年遭际，及现在更姓得官之事。薛涕零请罪，女拜认父。邻人见车马，咸来看视。鲁严拒之，逐出门外。薛不得已，寄邻家数日，挽亲戚关说。鲁氏以死誓不与薛合，曰：“彼其之_子，以尔贸迁。谓他人父，西土是冒。祈雨祈雨，反以我为仇。我躬不阅，实命弗犹。”竟不纳。薛乃为女择配，遗以多金，嘱善其母。居弥月，恐嫌者以假籍揭。辞鲁，鲁不见。

    Explanation

    56

    Upon arriving at the door, Xue and Huo entered. Lu, upon seeing them, loudly scolded, "What kind of uncouth person are you, entering someone's home without reason? Have you not heard that a widow's door should not be subjected to sudden storms?" Xue replied, "I am Xue Zhiran." Lu said, "Xue Lang accused me of being unfaithful, tore off his clothes, and left, dying as a guest long ago. How can there be another of him?" Huo interceded on his behalf, explaining his regret in detail and recounting his experiences over the past twenty years, as well as his current situation of changing his name and obtaining an official position. Xue wept and asked for forgiveness, and the woman bowed and acknowledged him as her father. Neighbors, seeing the carriages and horses, all came to look. Lu firmly refused and drove them out. Xue, having no choice, stayed at a neighbor's house for several days, appealing to relatives to mediate. Lu vowed to her death that she would not reconcile with Xue, saying, "That man, trading you for gain. Calling someone else's father, claiming to be from the West. Praying for rain, praying for rain, yet turning me into an enemy. I have not been favored, truly my fate is not the same." She refused to accept him. Xue then arranged for the woman to marry, gave her a large sum of money, and instructed her to treat her mother well. After a month, fearing that others might expose her with false documents, he bid farewell to Lu, but she did not see him.

    57

    后薛服阕之任，使人来寿迎之，终不去。曾接其女及婿之署，如霍者皆往来如织焉。惟鲁氏闭门纺绩，环堵萧然。初，晋人来，有所馈遗，悉掷之；继至，则但致安否而已。今鲁年七十余，乡里钦之。
    (“忍”之一宇，忠孝节义可结而成；即害理悖德，亦此一字阶之厉。薛之去妻，认假父，甘薄幸而隳身名者，不能忍而已矣。鲁之守贞甘贫，凌冰霜而挺松筠者，能忍而已矣。人亦善用斯忍哉。)

    张氏(附单廷玑事)
    单廷玑，顺天人。幼即为丐。年四十，转徙而丐于江南芜湖。日乞食，夜枕藉人家屋檐下。夜寒甚，茧缩栗起。见一人提灯导一老者过其前，问：“何人在我檐下？”单对以丐。翁怜而呼入门，止于旁舍，啖以粥，令寄宿。主人入，佧亦去。

    Explanation

    57

    After Xue completed her mourning period and resumed her official duties, someone sent to invite her back, but she refused to return. She had previously met her daughter and son-in-law at their residence, and many visitors came to see her, as dense as a web. In contrast, Lu remained secluded, spinning thread and living a simple, austere life. Initially, when people from Jin came to visit, she rejected all gifts, throwing them away. Later, when they returned, she only exchanged pleasantries. Now, at the age of seventy or so, Lu is respected in her hometown.

    ("Endurance" is a word that can bind loyalty, filial piety, righteousness, and virtue together; it can also lead to harm and moral corruption. Xue's ex-wife, who chose a false father and endured hardship and disgrace by marrying a man of low moral character, did so only because she lacked endurance. Lu, who remained chaste and endured poverty, standing firm like pine and bamboo in the face of harsh conditions, did so only because she possessed endurance. People should also wisely use this virtue of endurance.)

    Zhang (with the story of Single Tingji)
    Single Tingji was from Shuntian. As a child, he became a beggar. At the age of forty, he traveled to Wuhu in southern Anhui and continued to beg. He begged for food during the day and slept under people's eaves at night. One particularly cold night, he contracted and trembled with cold. He saw a man carrying a lamp leading an old man past him and asked, "Who is under my eaves?" Single Tingji replied that he was a beggar. The old man felt pity for him and called him inside, offering him a place to stay in a side room, feeding him porridge, and allowing him to stay for the night. When the owner returned, Single Tingji left.

    58

    单出，行其庭而伺焉。仆出见之曰：“鼠偷将欲暗中摸索耶？”单不服。诸仆集，将挝。及主人出，呼单曰：“吾恤尔寒，与汝舍，怜汝饥，与汝粥，何忘恩而背德？”单曰：“丐感翁德，反盗翁物。丐虽不齿，丐不为也。丐固无行，试问贵爪牙，我窃安在？是诬也。翁恶乎听。”翁曰：“是奴亦给于口。汝年强，奚而丐？”单曰：“丐五岁丐至今心目间无非是丐。故丐之外，未尝设想也。”翁问姓氏，曰：“单姓，名廷玑，京中人。”翁曰：“尔父何业？”曰：“幼不悉记，但知开银号于某胡同。父死时，家业罄，戚族无一人。乃为王氏奴，为假子。又见弃，遂为丐。”翁点首曰：“汝即单廷玑乎？且去。”即命仆送旅店。诘旦，持裘帽来服单。

    Explanation

    58

    Dan stepped out, walking in his courtyard and waiting. The servant came out and saw him, saying, "The rat thief is about to secretly feel around in the dark?" Dan did not admit it. The servants gathered, ready to beat him. When the old man came out, he called Dan and said, "I was concerned about your cold and gave you shelter, pitied your hunger and gave you porridge. Why do you forget my kindness and betray my trust?" Dan said, "The beggar is grateful for the old man's kindness, but instead steals the old man's things. Even though beggars are not respected, beggarly behavior is not something I would do. Beggarly behavior is indeed base; try asking your henchmen, where is my theft? This is slander. Old man, why listen to this?" The old man said, "You are strong, why beg?" Dan said, "I have been a beggar since I was five. In my heart and mind, there is nothing but begging. Outside of begging, I have never thought of anything else." The old man asked for his name and surname, and Dan replied, "I am Dān, named Tíngjī, from the capital." The old man asked, "What did your father do for a living?" Dan said, "I don't remember much from when I was young, but I know he ran a silver shop in a certain alley. When my father died, our family business was ruined, and no relatives came to help. I became a servant of the Wang family and was adopted as their son. And was abandoned again, so I became a beggar." The old man nodded and said, "Are you Dan Tíngjī? Go ahead." He immediately ordered a servant to take him to an inn. The next morning, the old man held a fur coat and hat to clothe Dan.

    59

    单不解。服而见翁。曰：“汝知我为汝翁，汝为我婿乎？”单曰：“不知也。”翁曰：“我姓张，关吏也。昔奉使令解银入都，道被窃，银不足兑。无可计，觅死。所遇汝父慨赠四百金，得竣事归。三年复入都，访汝父。时汝已二岁，我女亦二岁，遂与订婚姻。后四年，又进京，则汝父死，遍问汝。佥曰：单贾非土著，比死则散，无可访问。数十年来，音耗歇绝。然吾女为汝守贞至今，宁知汝流离若此也！”单軎，拜翁，叙舅礼焉。

    Explanation

    59

    Dan was puzzled. He dressed himself and went to see the old man. The old man said, "Do you know that I am your father-in-law and you are my son-in-law?" Dan replied, "I do not know." The old man said, "My name is Zhang, and I am a customs official. In the past, I was sent on a mission to deliver silver to the capital. Along the way, the silver was stolen, and there was not enough to fulfill the requirement. I had no choice but to contemplate suicide. Fortunately, your father generously gave me four hundred gold pieces, allowing me to complete the task and return home. Three years later, I returned to the capital to visit your father. At that time, you were two years old, and my daughter was also two years old. We then arranged the marriage. Four years later, I came to the capital again, and your father had died. I asked everyone about you, and they all said that the single merchant family would scatter upon the father's death, making it impossible to find you. For several decades, we have lost all contact. However, my daughter has remained chaste for you until now, and I never knew that you would be in such a state of wandering and hardship!" Dan was moved and bowed to the old man, acknowledging him as his uncle and performing the appropriate rituals.

    60

    初，翁最爱女，为访单，久无消息。欲嫁之，又恐背单氏约。女乃守志不二，且不弓其足，以示其贞。至是始赘单，而女年四十矣。尝问单何能，单告翁曰：“惟善走南北，道颇熟也，懂得些满洲话。”翁笑置之。
    会关督某欲接官眷，将遴一干事者，张以其婿对。入见，关督悦。即命遄发。单归，谓其妻曰：“泰山汲引我所事，我意非徒效奔走也。京师官眷初来南地，诚能趁此机会于道中迎奉之，他事可图也。奈乏资何？”妇曰：“当竭力办。”乃出其蓄数百金付单。遂行。

    Explanation

    60

    Initially, the father loved his daughter dearly and sought a match for her, but there was no news for a long time. He wanted to marry her off, but feared breaking a prior agreement with the Dan family. The daughter remained steadfast in her resolve, and she did not bind her feet, demonstrating her chastity. It was only then that she married into the Dan family, and by that time, she was already forty years old. Once, he asked what his son-in-law could do, and the son-in-law replied to his father-in-law: "I am good at traveling north and south, I know the routes well, and I understand some Manchu." The father laughed it off.

    When a certain customs supervisor wanted to welcome his official family, he was looking for someone capable of handling affairs, and the father-in-law recommended his son-in-law. Upon meeting the customs supervisor, the supervisor was pleased. He immediately ordered him to leave quickly. The son-in-law returned home and told his wife: "My father-in-law has recommended me for this position. My intention is not just to serve as a messenger. Since the official family from the capital is coming to the southern region for the first time, if we can take this opportunity to welcome them along the way, other matters can be planned. But what about the lack of funds?" The wife said: "I will do everything possible." She then took out her savings of several hundred gold coins and gave them to her husband. They set off on their journey.

    61

    至山东泲上遇官舫。一路解资奉承，凡器用饮食游观，能使上下男女尽得欢心。夫人大喜，抵署，盛称廷玑能；且言其币重，当厚赏之。督即命单代张之关吏。单乃受张之教，张所阅历数十年，关钞机宜，悉为指示，不留余蕴。
    为吏三年，复为鹾，积万金，遂报捐通判。值南河清发人员，单得拣河工。未逾年，为淮安府山安通判。张以女年余四十，恐不育，又以次女女焉。单尝与同官说丐之事，甚悉。计为倅时，去丐之日才六年耳。后迁襄河同知。不数年，卒于官。闻张翁每岁置锦衣裤施丐者，至今不倦云。
    (单廷玑固无足道，独其父遇素不相识之人，慨助多金，以济急难。其好义有足多者。张翁不以丐婿为辱，收恤而教之，亦不谓负德矣。至其女，以一言之约，数十年乃贞不字，誓心守义，岂不贤哉!卒之守义者得适所天，好义者终取其报。而张翁且好施不倦，其事均可以劝善。)

    卷二 义勇部(侠附)

    Explanation

    61

    Upon arriving at the banks of the Ji River in Shandong, they encountered an official boat. Throughout the journey, they offered gifts and flattery, providing all kinds of utensils, food, and entertainment, which made everyone, both men and women, happy. The lady was very pleased and, upon arriving at the office, highly praised Tang Ji's abilities; she also mentioned that his gifts were valuable, so he should be generously rewarded. The supervisor then ordered Dan to take Zhang's place as a customs officer. Dan then received Zhang's instruction, which Zhang had gained through decades of experience, and thoroughly explained the customs procedures and mechanisms, leaving nothing unsaid.

    After serving as an official for three years, he returned to salt trading and accumulated ten thousand gold. He then offered to donate money to be appointed as a magistrate. When there was a recruitment for personnel to clear the Southern River, Dan was selected to work on the river project. Within a year, he was appointed as the magistrate of Shan'an in Huai'an Prefecture. Zhang, fearing that his daughter, who was over forty, might not bear children, gave his second daughter in marriage to Dan. Dan often spoke with his colleagues about the affairs of beggars, knowing them very well. Calculating from when he became an assistant magistrate, it had only been six years since he started dealing with beggars. Later, he was promoted to the position of magistrate of Xianghe. Within a few years, he died in office. It is said that Master Zhang, every year, prepared fine clothes and trousers to give to beggars, and he has continued this practice without tiring to this day.

    (Dan Tang Ji himself is not worth mentioning, but his father, who had never met the person before, generously helped with a large sum of money to relieve an urgent difficulty. His kindness is worthy of much praise. Master Zhang did not consider the beggar son-in-law a disgrace, but instead took care of and educated him, and he did not think he was ungrateful. As for his daughter, she kept her promise of a single word for decades, remaining chaste and unmarried, vowing to uphold her virtue—how virtuous she was! In the end, the one who upheld her virtue was able to find her true match, and the one who showed kindness ultimately received her reward. And Master Zhang, who continues to give generously without tiring, all of these things can serve as a model for good deeds.)

    Volume Two: The Department of Righteous Courage (with a note on chivalry)

    62

    常运安
    常运安，永平府人。性刚猛，与人谈古今不平事，辄呈愤，终日不怿。看《精忠传》，凡集中秦桧字样，皆手抠之。
    一日，观剧演《如是观》，至“标本”一出，常大怒，跃登台上，夺武穆王椎，痛击假桧几毙。讼于官鞫，知与伶素无宿怨。实深恶当日之桧，故今日见其似者而扑之耳。搜其家，所藏传本皆无“桧”字，官释之。由是乡人推服。
    常与人排难解纷，不避嫌怨。每常所到，角者即散。常独悻悻，寻殴不已。里有邱三，邪无行，遇一孀妇吴姓者，与常同巷居。偶过市，邱尾之，披襟而歌淫亵之词。常促邱三，唾其面目：“人尚恤孤矜寡，汝凌之耶？请试老拳。”邱不能敌，鼻遭之而梁折，目遇之而珠流。邱归，平复后，约党群殴，常亦颅破而臂伤，众救舁返，其妻劝之曰：“琴不对驴鼓，力不与牛斗，奈何与无赖狯争强弱？”常曰：“挞我于市，是可忍孰不可忍？”常起，日伺邱。人见其色厉，不敢问。邱闻之，请肉袒，不许。遂远避之。

    62
    Chang Yun'an, from Yongping Prefecture, was a man of strong and fierce nature. Whenever he discussed ancient and modern injustices, he would become furious and remain unhappy all day. When he read "The Story of Loyalty," he would tear out the characters for Qin Hui with his hands whenever he saw them.

One day, while watching a play performance of "As It Is Seen," he became extremely angry when the scene "The Model" was performed. He leaped onto the stage, seized the mallet associated with the Duke of Wumu (Yue Fei), and severely beat the actor playing Qin Hui, nearly killing him. He was brought before the authorities for questioning. The judge knew that there was no prior grudge between Chang Yun'an and the actors. It turned out that he deeply hated Qin Hui from that time, and thus today he could not bear to see someone resembling him and struck him.

The authorities searched his home and found that none of the books he owned contained the character for "Hui." They released him. As a result, the villagers respected and admired him for this act.

Chang Yun'an was known for resolving conflicts and difficulties, and he did not avoid potential conflicts or grudges. Whenever he arrived at a dispute, the parties involved would disperse. However, Chang would remain angry and continue to fight. There was a man named Qiou in the village who had a bad reputation. One day, Qiou encountered a widow surnamed Wu, who lived in the same alley as Chang Yun'an. Qiou followed her into the market, where he stripped off his robe and sang obscene songs. Chang Yun'an confronted Qiou, spat in his face, and said, "Even people still pity widows and orphans; do you dare to bully them? Try my fists!" Qiou could not withstand Chang's strength, and he was hit so hard that his nose was broken and his eyes were swollen shut. After recovering, Qiou gathered a group of friends to attack Chang Yun'an, who was also injured in the head and arm. The villagers rescued and carried him back home. His wife advised him, "Don't play the lute for a donkey, nor fight a cow with your strength. Why bother to argue with a scoundrel like him?" Chang Yun'an replied, "If they beat me in the marketplace, is this something I can tolerate? What else cannot be tolerated?" He then waited for Qiou every day. People saw his stern expression and dared not ask about it. Qiou, upon hearing this, asked to be stripped naked as a sign of submission, but Chang refused. Eventually, Qiou fled and stayed away from him.
    63

    常一日甫出门，见孀妇跪于前。常问其故，孀曰：“前日邱某之辱，多蒙义愤。今遘祸愈烈，实望大力拯救，生死含感。”旁有人曰：“孀有族中侄吴乔，窥妇无子，欲夺妇产，计无所出，乃诬妇以不洁，将逐之以自肥。”常闻言，眦睛暴裂，气冲于冠，攘臂寻吴。时吴正在市口，喋沓其嫂，手持一纸若状词。众哗然曰：“常运安来。”吴望而欲去。常曰：“唶，休走！”吴曰：“此吾家事，汝不得预。”常曰：“汝欲霸汝兄之白产，何至玷汝嫂之苦节？”突飞一掌，吴仰仆。常进步踏其胸，指吴曰：“汝是吴乔否？”曰：“是。”常曰：“这回当不是场上之伪丞相也。”拳脚交加，登时立毙。常乃谓孀曰：“娘子请回，杀人者常运安也。管领疾风暴雨，再不入寡妇之门矣。”孀泣谢曰：“累君哉！”

    Explanation

    63

    One day, as Chang was just about to leave, he saw a widow kneeling in front of him. Chang asked her the reason for her actions. The widow said, "Yesterday, I was insulted by Mr. Qiu, and I greatly appreciated your righteous indignation. Now, my misfortune has become even more severe, and I truly hope for your great assistance in saving me, filled with gratitude and emotion." Someone nearby said, "The widow has a nephew named Wu Qiao in her clan. He has been spying on the woman because she has no children, wanting to seize her property. With no other options, he slandered the woman as impure and plans to expel her for personal gain." Upon hearing this, Chang's eyelids burst open, his breath surged to his head, and he flung up his arms to find Wu. At that time, Wu was at the market entrance, chattering with his sister-in-law, holding a piece of paper that looked like a complaint. The crowd exclaimed loudly, "Chang Yun'an is here." Wu looked and wanted to leave. Chang said, "Hey, don't run!" Wu said, "This is my family's matter, you cannot interfere." Chang said, "Do you want to seize your brother's property? Why go so far as to defile your sister-in-law's virtuous conduct?" Suddenly, he flew a fist, and Wu fell backward. Chang stepped forward and kicked his chest, pointing at Wu and saying, "Are you Wu Qiao?" Wu replied, "Yes." Chang said, "This time, you should not be the fake chancellor on the field." He pummeled Wu with fists and feet, and instantly, Wu died. Chang then told the widow, "Madam, please return. The killer is Chang Yun'an. I will endure violent storms and will never enter the door of a widow again." The widow cried and thanked him, saying, "Troubling you!"

    64

    常慨然自首于庭。官义之，人哀之，而莫能救。遂问抵。逾年，援赦得释。夫妻抱痛，如逢隔世。第以逮罪，产业荡尽。妻乃日夜号泣，劝其改行。常亦力悔前非，誓不再蹈，卒为善士。然而闻鸡起舞，终未免有冯妇之见存，其妻实忧之。当夜缋，见火珠满地，累累行入墙隅中。妻告常，掘而视，得窖金，不可测。常喜曰：“吾得金，金得所用矣！使此傥来者，俾一二钱虏得之，将不知几经慢藏，几经严密，势不至不及于祸不止。何如今日假我行义，不以利为利，而以义为利之得哉！”妻曰：“行之利，勿暴其气，是集义所生者。”常自此遇人急难，脱骖留佩，往往出诸水火之中，即或偶逢按剑，一钩金尽可冰消。故常君晚岁躁释矜平，义士而有蔼如之容。称之者谓其半生仗义，半世疏财，其克保首领终也宜矣。

    Explanation

    64

    Chang, with a sigh of sorrow, confessed in the courtyard. The official regarded it as just, and the people pitied him, but no one could help. He was then accused of a crime. After a year, he relied on amnesty and was released. The couple grieved together, as if they had met in another life. Only because of the accusation of a crime, all their property was lost. His wife wept day and night, advising him to change his ways. Chang also strongly regretted his past mistakes and swore not to repeat them, ultimately becoming a good man. However, despite his efforts to rise early and practice martial arts, he still had the inclination of Feng Fu, which worried his wife. One night, she saw fire pearls covering the ground, lined up and going into the corner of the wall. She told Chang, and they dug and looked, finding hidden gold, the amount of which was incalculable. Chang said joyfully, "I have obtained the gold, and the gold can be used properly! If this unexpected gain were to fall into the hands of a few copper coin thieves, it is impossible to know how many times it would be carelessly stored and how many times it would be strictly secured, but it would not stop until it reached disaster. How much better is it today that I am allowed to act righteously, not treating profit as profit, but treating righteousness as the source of profit!" His wife said, "The benefit of acting, do not let your temper get the better of you; this is the result of accumulating righteousness." From then on, whenever Chang encountered people in urgent need, he would dismount from his carriage and leave behind his jewelry, often rescuing them from the midst of danger. Even if he occasionally met someone drawing a sword, a small amount of gold could melt like ice. Therefore, in his later years, Chang released his impatience and became proud and calm, a righteous person with a gentle and kind appearance. Those who praised him said that he spent half his life practicing righteousness and half his life being generous, and it was appropriate for him to live to a ripe old age.

    65

    秃梁
    秃梁者，乞人也，张姓，不知何许人。自幼独身行乞。其顶无发，自呼为“秃梁”。人亦以“秃梁”呼之。魁梧，有膂力，声粗猛，一呼彻巷无不知为秃梁至。有钱则买食，余以分人。或佣工，工资不计多寡。遇人呼修桥梁道路，不索直。
    有年大饥，梁乞至夷潍，忽大恸，诘之曰：“我思家。”遽归。春，人相食，弃婴儿满道。梁以两筐贮十数人，担之乞食食之。有死者，旋补之。五阅月，无怠容。生平不饮、不博、不盗、不与人斗。人托之馈遗，虽重资，一无所苟；即大风雨，不爽期。有欲授以室者，笑而不答。
    雍正七年，病死于高密，年已七十矣。

    Explanation

    65

    Tubiang
    Tubiang was a beggar, surnamed Zhang, and his origin is unknown. From a young age, he lived alone and begged. He had no hair on his head and called himself "Tubiang." People also called him "Tubiang." He was tall and strong, with a loud and fierce voice. One call would echo through the alley, and everyone would know Tubiang had arrived. When he had money, he bought food and shared the rest with others. He sometimes worked as a laborer, and his wages were not counted in terms of amount. When people asked him to repair bridges and roads, he did not ask for payment.

    During a great famine, Tubiang begged in Yiwai. Suddenly, he burst into tears, asked why, and said, "I miss home," then hurriedly returned. In spring, people were eating each other, and abandoned babies were everywhere on the road. Tubiang used two baskets to carry several people, carried them to beg for food, and fed them. When someone died, he immediately replaced them. For five months, he showed no signs of fatigue. Throughout his life, he did not drink, gamble, steal, or fight with others. People entrusted him with gifts, even if they were valuable, he did not take advantage of them; even in heavy rain or strong winds, he never missed his appointments. When someone wanted to give him a wife, he smiled but did not respond.

    In the seventh year of Yongzheng's reign, he died of illness in Gaomi, and he was already seventy years old.

    66

    二班头
    粤之潮属揭阳，大邑也。有二班皂沈清者，修髯大耳，为人憨直，綦贫。衙退，一醉之外，正不问事大如天也。其妻交谪之，沈尝曰：“杖头钱不可用，但添我一杯酒，独不顾人家覆盆冤耶？”妻乃去闱，沈一人愈觉无累。
    有邑豪李姓，艳邻家霍氏女。女父固穷，不愿为李妾。李厚以金，不可；挟以势，亦不可。李又拆邻堵，霍出，堵乃覆。霍惧及祸，携女避之。李复诉霍负李债，赇行于官，欲诬服之。李托沈，倘责霍，重毙之，遗以锭金。及讯，果下霍于阶。沈乃左手执杖，右手持金，而告尹曰：“李以金赂我杖，我不敢以杖售其金。杖则犹是也，而金曷反诸？”尹惭，遂释霍。故李谋终不能伤霍者，沈之力也。
    后人以沈行事不同乎役，且有大远于役者。死之日，塑其像于城隍庙之东廊，犹左杖右金，青衣爪牙之态，宛肖其生。今邑人有以斗酒豚蹄供其前，提其耳而视之曰：“沈二班头，某事乞为佑之。”事多应云。

    Explanation

    66

    In the region of Chaozhou, Guangdong Province, there was a city named Jieyang, a large and important place. There was a low-ranking official or servant named Shen Qing, who had a long beard and large ears, and was honest and straightforward, though extremely poor. After the officials returned home from work, he would drink himself into a stupor, ignoring even matters as significant as the heavens. His wife often scolded him, and Shen Qing once said, "Money obtained through bribes or improper means cannot be used; it only adds to my cup of wine. Would you not care about the hidden grievances of others?" His wife then left the household, and Shen Qing felt even more unburdened living alone.

    There was a wealthy and influential man named Li in the town. He was attracted to his neighbor's daughter, Ho. Ho's father remained poor but content, and did not wish to become Li's concubine. Li offered him a large sum of money, but he refused; Li then tried to use his power and influence, but Ho's father still refused. Li then damaged the neighbor's wall, and when Ho came out, the wall collapsed. Ho feared that he would bring disaster upon them, so he took his daughter and fled. Li then accused Ho of owing him money and bribed the officials, attempting to make Ho confess falsely. Li entrusted Shen Qing with the task of punishing Ho, offering him a large sum of money in return. When Shen Qing was called to question Ho, he brought him down to the ground. Shen Qing then held a stick in one hand and money in the other, and told the magistrate, "Li bribed me with money to use this stick. I dare not sell my stick for his money. The stick is still the same, but where is the money returned?" The magistrate was ashamed and released Ho. Thus, Li's schemes could not harm Ho, thanks to Shen Qing's intervention.

    Later, people believed that Shen Qing's actions were different from those of a typical low-ranking official, and even went beyond the duties of such a role. On the day of his death, a statue of him was created in the eastern corridor of the City God Temple, still holding a stick in one hand and money in the other, dressed in the attire of a low-ranking official, resembling him in life. Today, people in the town offer him a cup of wine and a pig's trotter, pulling his ear and looking at him, saying, "Shen, the low-ranking official, please help us with this matter." Many things are answered or resolved through his intercession.

    67

    送匾
    太仓张受先，名采，事母至孝。性耿介，好面斥人短。乙酉元旦，梦关公送一“乾坤正气”匾到家。张大喜，逢人说项，以为神之格思也。
    先是，州中有豪仆某，为民蠹，且为衙鹯，人莫之撄。张与州侯钱希声善，以公愤，曰之钱。侯怒，案治其罪。夫投鼠必忌其器，毁椟恐伤其珠。哲人达士，以此为箴。张公之白于州牧，虽曰义愤，亦深不自韬晦。宜宵小私仇焉，将含沙而射之，果皆衔恨欲甘心于张。
    是岁五月，张公至小教场，群党要而劫之。丛殴聚击，血肉糜烂，备极惨酷。见其僵仆不动，以足蹴之，身直仰，咸以为死，哄而散。侧有关帝庙，庙僧谓同住者曰：“张公，正人也，尸弃于野，恐有毁伤。吾侪当舁还其家。”无以盛之，乃下一乾坤正气匾，舁焉。到家，灌之酒，得苏。明年，捕凶党，置诸极刑。

    Explanation

    67

    Receiving a Plaque

    Zhang Shouxian, from Taicang, whose given name was Cai, served his mother with utmost filial piety. He was upright and straightforward, and he liked to openly criticize others' faults. On New Year's Day of the Year of the Ox (Yi You), he dreamed that Guan Gong delivered a plaque inscribed with "Heavenly and Earthly Righteousness" to his home. Zhang was greatly delighted and spoke highly of it to everyone, believing it to be a manifestation of divine will.

    Before this, there was a powerful servant in the state who was a pest to the people and a scourge to the government, and no one could touch him. Zhang was on good terms with the state governor Qian Xisheng. Out of public indignation, he reported the servant to Qian. The governor was angry and investigated and punished his crimes. When throwing at a mouse, one must be careful not to damage the vessel; when destroying the box, one might damage the pearl. Wise and enlightened people use this as a warning. Although Zhang reported it to the state governor out of righteous anger, he also did not keep a low profile. It was appropriate for petty people to harbor private grudges and plot harm. They all harbored hatred and wanted to take pleasure in taking revenge on Zhang.

    In the fifth month of that year, Zhang went to a small training ground, and a group of his enemies ambushed and attacked him. They beat and attacked him until his flesh was torn and bloodied, suffering the utmost cruelty. Seeing him lying motionless, they kicked him with their feet, and when he did not move, they thought he was dead and scattered. There was a Guan Di temple nearby, and the temple monk told the other monks, "Zhang is a righteous man, and if his body is left in the wild, it might be damaged. We should carry him back to his home." Having no container, they used the "Heavenly and Earthly Righteousness" plaque to carry him. When they got home, they gave him wine to drink, and he revived. The next year, they captured the criminals and sentenced them to the harshest punishment.

    68

    嘻，是梦也，谓之神兆也可，即以为旌善也亦可。

    张二唠
    张二唠，名景仪，行二，潍之东关外人。以其好言，故称唠。凡与人共一事，论一物，必穷诘再再，亦究乎其至极而后已。然其行皆韪乎正。乡之少年后辈，或遭于道，必趋而避之，盖恐与之言而剌剌不休也。
    有杜祥，唠同里，客死于都。其妻高氏与姑及三幼子居。乾隆十二年饥，姑令高醮。适二唠丧偶，有媒之者，遂聘焉。择吉，高氏至，张迎入。高氏坐床隅，唠曰：“新人年几何？”妇羞不言。唠固问，妇素知其唠，乃曰：“三十二。”唠曰：“三十二。前婚杜时年几何？”妇曰：“一十六。”唠曰：“十六年中，尔夫妇亦相得否？”妇笑曰：“夫妇有何不得之有？”唠曰：“恐不得。

    Explanation

    68

    Ah, it is as if a dream, it can be called a divine omen, or it can also be considered a sign of good deeds.

    Zhang Er the Talkative
    Zhang Er, whose given name was Jingyi, was the second son and lived outside the eastern gate of Weifang. Because he loved to talk, he was called "the Talkative." Whenever he shared an activity or discussed an object with someone, he would question them repeatedly, delving into the matter to its very extremities. However, his actions were always upright and proper. The young people and younger generation in the village would avoid him when they met on the road, for they feared engaging in conversation with him, as he would talk incessantly.

    There was Du Xiang, a fellow villager of Zhang Er, who died as a guest in the capital. His wife, Gao Shi, along with her mother-in-law and three young children, lived together. In the twelfth year of the Qianlong reign, during a famine, the mother-in-law ordered Gao Shi to perform a wedding ceremony. At that time, Zhang Er had lost his wife, and a matchmaker arranged for him to marry Gao Shi. They chose a lucky day, and when Gao Shi arrived, Zhang Er welcomed her in. Gao Shi sat at the edge of the bed, and Zhang Er asked, "How old are you, the new bride?" She was too shy to answer. Zhang Er persisted in asking, and knowing that he was talkative, she finally replied, "Thirty-two." Zhang Er said, "Thirty-two. How old were you when you married Du?" She answered, "Sixteen." Zhang Er then asked, "Did you and your husband get along well during those sixteen years?" She laughed and said, "What could there be to not get along with in a marriage?" Zhang Er replied, "Probably not."

    69

    既相得，当死守，宁改适？”妇曰：“姑老矣，不能养，子皆幼，不能抚，故醮而得金，以养以抚。”唠曰：“金有尽，姑与子畴抚养？”妇曰：“不贫不醮。”唠曰：“醮亦终贫，何如不醮？”妇泣曰：“醮岂我之愿哉！而迫我以不得不醮之势。”言罢大恸，以袖掩面，不能成声。唠曰：“夫如是，不须悲。尔急归，孝尔姑，抚诸子。”妇曰：“聘难偿。”唠曰：“不尔索。”持灯引妇出门，送之归，告其姑而抚其子。皆涕洟拜谢。唠曰：“如有急，唯我恤，可遣告，赍尔缺。”乡里称善。今三子皆力食，能养母矣。
    (此文有声有色，简古可诵。七如慧心绣口，得这一种笔墨。)

    Explanation

    69

    Since we have already become acquainted, I should die to defend you, rather than change my mind." The woman replied, "My mother-in-law is old and cannot be cared for, and my children are young and cannot be raised. Therefore, I remarried to obtain money to support and raise them." The man said, "Money is finite. What will happen when the money runs out, and who will care for your mother-in-law and children?" The woman said, "One does not remarry unless one is poor." The man said, "Remarrying will still lead to poverty. How is it better not to remarry?" The woman wept bitterly and said, "Remarrying is not what I desire! But I was forced into a situation where I had to remarry." After she finished speaking, she wailed loudly, covering her face with her sleeve and unable to form words. The man said, "If it is as you say, there is no need to grieve. You must return quickly, be filial to your mother-in-law, and care for the children." The woman said, "The dowry is difficult to repay." The man said, "I will not demand it from you." He held a lamp and led the woman out the door, sending her back home, informing her mother-in-law, and caring for the children. They all wept and bowed in gratitude. The man said, "If there is any urgency, only I can help. You may send word, and I will provide for your needs." The village praised his kindness. Now, the three sons are all strong and capable, able to support their mother.

    (With its vivid imagery and concise, ancient style, this text is both moving and memorable. The author's skillful use of language and his ability to capture the essence of the situation are truly remarkable.)

    70

    叶禄
    我曾祖闻进公有仆名叶禄者，善走，一日夜行五百里。视其胫骨，较人长挺，又无肉，多黑毛，皆二三寸，自膝以下卷连而生。与人同行，初亦不惊其捷，一眨眼间，皆瞠乎后。望之者觉其足不着地，脑后辫横直流矢。闻进公以恩贡准入成均，叶随往。公家贫亲老，尝以膏资之半寄家。一日一次，命叶归里。吾嘉邑去京师千二百里有奇，叶以七日作往返，无愆期。
    一日，行至夜，过郑州洼，二十里无人居。有打短棍者，伺叶至，击叶，叶仆。棍者剥其资去。叶狼狈起，失资，视南北只两条路，无岔杂口，于是望北追之，约五六里，不见；急反南路，未八里，而荷棍者隐隐在前焉。叶呼曰：“还我行李。”棍者见叶狂奔，叶转缓行随之，棍者反身欲击叶，叶亦缓行而退，棍者不能得。棍者又走，叶又随之。如是者再，棍者惊，乃还其资。叶收资而棍者自去。叶思竟夜无事且又馁，何不戏之？

    Explanation

    70

    Leaf Lu
    My great-grandfather, Wenjin Gong, had a servant named Leaf Lu who was exceptionally fast, capable of traveling five hundred miles in one night. When examining his shin bones, they were longer and more prominent than those of ordinary people, with little flesh and many black hairs, each about two or three inches long, curling from below the knees. When walking alongside others, they initially did not notice his speed, but in the blink of an eye, he would be far behind. Observers felt his feet did not touch the ground, and his long braid flowed backward like an arrow. Wenjin Gong was admitted to the Imperial Academy through the system of recommending scholars based on merit, and Leaf Lu accompanied him. The master was poor and had elderly relatives to support, often sending half of his salary home. Once a day, he would send Leaf Lu back to his hometown. Our town is about one thousand two hundred miles from the capital, and Leaf Lu completed the round trip in seven days without any delay.
    One day, while traveling at night, they passed through Zhengzhou洼, a desolate area twenty miles from any settlement. A short stick wielder lay in wait for Leaf Lu, attacked him, and knocked him down. The stick wielder took his belongings and fled. Leaf Lu, disheveled and without his possessions, looked around and saw only two roads leading north and south, with no side paths. He then looked north and chased after the thief, covering about five or six miles, but saw no sign of him. He quickly turned back south, but before he had gone eight miles, the stick wielder was faintly visible ahead. Leaf Lu shouted, "Return my belongings!" Seeing Leaf Lu running wildly, the stick wielder slowed down, and Leaf Lu followed at a slower pace. The stick wielder turned around to strike Leaf Lu, but Leaf Lu also slowed down and retreated. The stick wielder could not catch him. The stick wielder ran again, and Leaf Lu followed. This happened twice, and the stick wielder was surprised, returning Leaf Lu's belongings. Leaf Lu collected his belongings, and the stick wielder left on his own. Leaf Lu thought that nothing had happened all night, and he was also hungry, so why not play a trick on him?

    71

    乃随棍者，棍者惫甚，又闻咿喔鸡声已唱村落，明星巳烂，天色欲曙，仓皇言曰：“壮士何相逼太甚耶？”叶曰：“终曾往返，未及握手晤面，拟欲登堂识荆耳。”棍者无奈曰：“毋哗，舍下不远，倘承枉顾，愿为前导。”
    时天已大亮，棍者引至一村，入室。良久，有老翁出，延叶登堂，问姓名。老翁曰：“老夫段姓，夜间豚子误犯，望宽恕焉。”叶唯唯。复问叶，知为曾氏仆，且为曾氏寄膏金归养。翁甚爱叶之忠于主，又敬闻进公之能孝于亲也。且馔饮叶，醉饱辞去。翁且赠行资，又与叶一小旗，曰：“凡贵相知过此，或车或马或行李，皆插此旗一面，直抵济上，可无虞也。”

    Explanation

    71

    As Ye followed the stick-wielder, the stick-wielder was extremely exhausted and, hearing the roosters crowing in the village, saw the stars fading and the sky about to dawn. He hastily said, "Why are you pressing me so hard, sir?" Ye replied, "We have met before, but we haven't had a proper meeting. I wanted to come to your home to meet you properly." The stick-wielder, with no choice, said, "Do not make a fuss. My place is not far away. If you would grace me with your presence, I would be happy to guide the way."

    By this time, the sky was already bright, and the stick-wielder led Ye to a village and into a house. After a long time, an old man came out and invited Ye to enter the hall, asking for his name. The old man said, "I am surnamed Duan. My son made a mistake at night. I hope for your forgiveness." Ye nodded in agreement. The old man then asked Ye and learned that he was a servant of the Zeng family and had been entrusted with the task of returning the gold and medicine to the Zeng family. The old man greatly admired Ye's loyalty to his master and respected hearing about Jin Gong's filial piety. He treated Ye to food and drink, and after being well-fed and satisfied, Ye took his leave. The old man also gave him some travel money and a small flag, saying, "For anyone who knows you and passes by here, whether by carriage, horse, or luggage, they should all insert this flag. All the way to the city of Ji, there will be no worry."

    72

    后叶常往来其家，数年后，段氏富，改行，而我闻进公已选云梦宰。会集差使都门，重过其地，翁已作古。棍者与叶叙旧交，且见其二子，为鸡黍云。

    张陈武
    文登协镇张陈武，有绝力过人。康熙壬子来登。当时营兵悍不奉法，以哗噪为常。公抵任，未几，有无赖多人倡乱，约公于五月五日黎明，举石为号。有其党醉，泄谋。公于二鼓始闻信，率亲丁驰而往，群凶方歃血。公入，叱之。众鼓刀而前，公以手扑之，刀纷纷落，如狂风之飘屋瓦。皆就缚。其两首不受缚，公以左右肋，挟之上马，归至城下，二凶巳受挟死。

    Explanation

    72

    Translation:
    After Ye often visited his home. Several years later, the Duan family became wealthy and reformed their behavior, while I heard that Jin Gong had been selected as the governor of Yunmeng. When we gathered for a mission in the capital and revisited the place, the old man had already passed away. The man nicknamed "Stick" had a reunion with Ye, and met his two sons, who prepared a meal of chicken and millet.

    Zhang Chenwu, the assistant commander of Wendeng, had extraordinary strength beyond others. In the year 1682, he came to Dengzhou. At that time, the soldiers were fierce and did not follow the law, often causing disturbances. The official arrived at his post, and shortly after, many ruffians started a rebellion, planning to attack at dawn on the fifth day of the fifth month, using a stone as a signal. One of his followers got drunk and revealed the plan. The official only heard the news at the second drumbeat (midnight), led his personal guards to rush there, and found the ruffians just about to take an oath. The official entered and shouted at them. The crowd brandished their knives and rushed forward. The official used his hands to knock them down, and knives fell one after another, like tiles being blown off by a strong wind. All were bound. Two of them refused to be bound, and the official used his left and right ribs to lift them onto the horse. When they returned to the city gate, the two ruffians had already died.

    73

    次日，公升帐传集营弁，各带器械者，听骂曰：“余结发从戎，身经百战。汝曹竖子，何敢当万人敌？”遂试武于庭，距跃过辕门。复袒衣，创痕瘢癜。众皆啮齿咋舌，罗拜于地。乃追其余党，各重捶之。自是营伍辑服，奉张公约。

    断头兵
    乾隆甲午秋，山左寿张逆伦之变夜发。仓皇无备，满城奔窜，军民杂沓。有兵张某，奋勇登陴，遇贼于巷。一人力战，为贼所歼。觉贼刀劈脑后如切瓜声，遂昏去，倒地上。移时苏，痛极，血涔涔，狼狈而起。其首坠胸前，不能举，张以手托起，加于脰，复坠。张乃挟其首，又拽其辫结，系于后腰带间，遂不坠。
    视市上无一人，踉跄归。家人见之，惊，掖榻内。验其项上刀痕，深入骨里，皮不断者仅寸许。所幸气食二嗓无伤，于是灌以米汁。月余创合，亦能言语。唯不敢俯视一切，恐低首仍挂胸前耳。

    Explanation

    73

    The next day, the general ascended to the tent and summoned the camp officers, ordering them to bring their weapons. He then subjected them to verbal abuse, saying, "Since I began my military career as a young man, I have fought in countless battles. How dare you, mere boys, face the enemy of ten thousand?" He then demonstrated his martial skills in the courtyard, leaping over the gate. He then bared his chest, showing the scars of his wounds. Everyone bit their lips and gasped, prostrating themselves on the ground. He then pursued the rest of his followers, beating each of them severely. From then on, the camp was orderly and disciplined, following the general's orders.

    A Headless Soldier

    In the autumn of the year Jia Wu during the reign of Emperor Qianlong, a night of chaos erupted in Shou Zhang, Shandong, where a family member committed an act of filial impiety. The city was caught unprepared, and people fled in panic, with soldiers and civilians mixed together. There was a soldier named Zhang who bravely climbed onto the city wall and encountered the enemy in the alley. Alone, he fought valiantly and was killed by the enemy. He felt the enemy's sword cut his head off like slicing through a melon, and he fainted. He fell to the ground but regained consciousness after a while, suffering from extreme pain. Blood was dripping from his neck, and he stumbled to his feet. His head had fallen to his chest and could not be lifted, so Zhang held it up with his hand and placed it back on his neck, but it fell again. Zhang then grabbed his head and tied his braid to the back of his waistband, and it no longer fell.

    When he looked around the market, he found no one, so he staggered home. His family was shocked to see him and helped him to bed. They examined the deep gash on his neck, which had cut through the bone but left only an inch of skin unbroken. Fortunately, his respiratory system was unharmed, so they gave him a drink of rice gruel. After a month, his wound healed, and he could speak again. However, he could no longer look down, fearing that his head would still hang in front of him.

    74

    今张年近七旬，犹善饭。有人自寿张来者，见之，无异词。

    周劈刀
    汝宁赵若水，名进士也。为枣强令，兴文教，奖励后进，爱才如命，一时之彦，无不乐被其容接。簿书鞅掌之暇，长吏之堂不啻师儒之室，百里中盖彬彬如也。
    会当放衙之期，捕缉者获一窃牛马贼，名周劈刀。吏抱牍比赃按律，俄请鞫。俄而羁至，长跪阶下。赵视之，虽屈下膝，犹昂藏高出几案，须长，飘脑后。赵异其相，先问捕者曰：“尔从何处缉得？毋误捉好人。”捕告曰：“若囚常往来于燕南赵北之间，得人牛马，辄骑而去。有追之者，周即挟刀劈斗，勇不可当。‘劈刀’之名自此有。昨大醉鼾雷，卧野庙中，故得就缚。否诚不可与争锋。”赵乃指周曰：“囚，何说之辞？”周慨然曰：“大丈夫磊磊落落，何可一世。今不幸被羁，岂等鼠窃狗偷辈作乞怜惫赖状？窃诚是囚，谅大官不至以杀牛马之人加杀人之罪。

    Explanation

    74

    Zhang, who is nearly seventy years old, still enjoys eating well. Someone who came from Shouzhang saw him and had no different words.

    Zhou Pidao
    Zhao Ruoshui from Runing, known as a Jinshi (imperial scholar), served as the magistrate of Zaoqiang. He promoted cultural education, rewarded and encouraged young scholars, and loved talent as if it were his life. The talented people of the time were all delighted to be received by him. In his spare time from handling official documents, his office was no different from a teacher's or scholar's room, and within a hundred miles, the area was generally refined and cultured.

    It was the time when he was about to dismiss his officials. The captors captured a thief who stole cattle and horses, named Zhou Pidao. The officials brought the case files, compared the evidence with the law, and soon requested to interrogate. Soon, Zhou Pidao was brought in in chains, standing with long legs, kneeling on the steps. Zhao looked at him, though his knees were bent, he still stood tall, towering over the table, with a long beard flowing behind his head. Zhao found his appearance unusual and first asked the captors, "Where did you catch him? Don't mistake a good person." The captors replied, "The prisoner often roamed between the southern part of Yan and the northern part of Zhao. Whenever he got cattle or horses, he would ride off immediately. If anyone tried to chase him, Zhou would draw his knife and fight bravely, making him invincible. The nickname 'Pida' originated from this. Yesterday, he was drunk and snoring loudly, sleeping in a wild temple, so they were able to capture him. Otherwise, he would not have been easy to capture."

    Zhao then pointed to Zhou and said, "Prisoner, what do you have to say?" Zhou responded with a sigh, "A true man is open and upright. How can one live a life like this? Now unfortunately, I am in chains. I am not like a petty thief begging for mercy. I admit I am a thief. I believe you would not punish me for killing cattle and horses as if I had committed murder."

    75

    ”赵曰：“囚亦知夫窃之轻重乎？”周曰：“既为窃，岂不知窃？愿为大官陈之：天下古今纷纷多窃者也，独囚也乎哉？窃也者，职彼所有，济我所无。初不必明彰其劫夺之嫌而阴成以投赠之好。况放牛世替，借马人亡，偶值以事之所必需者，宁复计其风之不相及？至先天义蕴，往哲名言，人能窃之，即可以为圣。日月精华，阴阳奥窍狐能窃之，即可以为仙。极之，窃宝玉，窃大弓，窃虎符，或作权奸，或作义侠；等而下之，王朝升斗之糈，爵秩之荣，窃位者一旦藉手，固莫不名显当时，荫及后世。区区慢藏匿迹于马渤牛溲之下，又何窃之义类之足充与？窃狁囚乎哉？”赵益异其言，薄责而释之。

    Explanation

    75

    Zhao said, "Does the prisoner understand the gravity of theft?" Zhou replied, "Since it is theft, how could one not know it is theft? I wish to present this to the high official: Throughout history, there have been many thieves; is it only the prisoner? Theft, by its nature, takes what belongs to others to satisfy one's own needs. At first, it is not necessarily obvious as a violent act but can be seen as a covert act of generosity. Even more so, when cattle are replaced by generations, and horses are lent and lost, occasionally, what is needed for a purpose, how can one consider the lack of connection between them? As for the profound meanings of the ancients and the wise sayings of the past, if one can steal these, one can become a sage. The essence of the sun and moon, the mysteries of yin and yang—if a fox can steal these, it can become a deity. Extending this, stealing precious jade, a great bow, or a tiger talisman, some may become powerful villains, others righteous heroes. And so on, down to the meager rations of the imperial court, the honors of rank and title. Those who steal positions, once they gain power, will certainly be famous in their time and benefit future generations. Such trivial acts of theft are hidden among the mundane. How can such acts fill the category of theft? Is theft a prisoner?" Zhao was increasingly amazed by his words and lightly rebuked him before releasing him.

    76

    后十年，赵犹子官于粤，有平倭寇周将军者，通款。接问：“若水是君何人？”答：“以诸父行。将军何以识之？”曰：“我师也，感恩知己，兼而有之。林下先生，颇记忆否？倘有便鸿，余有一函并薄物申敬。”因念叔多门墙，未闻有武弟子。后寄书，邮返，其叔字中始缅述之，乃知其人。犹录其寄诗一首以示，云：
    学书不就剑无成，曾向燕南草泽行。命也何如拼一醉，薄乎云尔感余生。
    海门蛟射秋风壮，圣主龙飞野鹙平。今日功名铜柱表，愿从桃李报恩情。

    乔一琦
    一琦，字伯珪，上海人。膂力过人。邑中有石坊，尝乘马过坊下，以手援坊足。夹马，起半空中，久之乃下。又尝坐舟中，势欲前则舟即前，欲后则舟亦退。奇勇如此，亦一奇也。事见乔氏《最乐堂家传》。

    Explanation

    76

    Ten years later, Zhao's nephew was in office in Yue, where there was General Zhou who had pacified the Wokou (Japanese pirates). When they communicated, he asked, "Who is Ruoshui to you?" He replied, "He is my senior relative. How do you know him?" The general said, "He was my teacher, and I am grateful to him for his kindness and understanding. Do you remember the recluse scholar? If you have a letter, I have a letter and some small gifts to express my respect." Reflecting on his uncle's many disciples, he had not heard of any martial ones. Later, when he sent a letter, it was returned, and his uncle's handwriting began to recall the story, and he finally knew the person. He still recorded the poem he sent, which said:
    I did not succeed in calligraphy, nor did I achieve with the sword; I once wandered in the grasslands of Yan. Fate has it that I might as well drink to forget, and I feel grateful for my remaining life.
    The dragon shot at the sea gate in the autumn wind, and the wise ruler flew like a crane in the wild. Today, his achievements are inscribed on the copper pillar, and I wish to repay his kindness by becoming a student and flourishing.

    Qiao Yiqi
    Yiqi, courtesy name Boge, was from Shanghai. He had extraordinary strength. In his county, there was a stone archway; he once rode a horse under it and used his hand to lift the horse's hooves. He lifted the horse, rising into the air, and remained there for a long time before descending. He also once sat in a boat, and when he wanted to go forward, the boat moved forward, and when he wanted to go backward, the boat also moved backward. His extraordinary bravery was indeed a marvel. This is recorded in the Qiao family's "Zui Le Tang Jia Chuan."

    77

    浣衣妇
    江西抚军某，骄恣甚，道路以目，总藩某，则政多美誉。会有大谳。两人意见牴牾，案牍上下，遂两焉。藩执不附抚，而抚因以怼藩，且图杀藩。藩滋惧，谋所以避之，不得，欲解组，又不能。尝于空庭月白脱帽无人之际，浩然长叹。
    月前有浣衣妇进藩署，夫人见之喜，询其里居，夫人之桑梓也。年约三十，孀寡无依，随帆下豫章，谋为妪而标洁谨悍，不同凡妇，言语亦爽利可喜。藩亦异其为人。
    一日，藩抑郁，书空咄咄。妇前致词曰：“大人屏藩宣化当敷政优优，不使丛脞斯已耳。何终日颦蹙。若有大不得已于中者然？妾闻主忧臣辱，盍为贱妾言之？毋谓裙钗中无解环法也。”公曰：“尔穷庐嫠妇，何足与语。有怀莫白，奚词费为？”妇曰：“监军将不利于大人乎？”公愕然，妇曰：“无忧。监军酒色徒，未能远谋。妾将为大人释此厄。”藩喜问计，妇曰：“请俟诘朝。”

    Explanation

    77

    A Washerwoman

    In Jiangxi, there was a governor-general who was extremely arrogant and domineering, causing people to communicate by glances out of fear. The governor of the province, on the other hand, was often praised for his governance. There was a significant legal case, and the two officials had conflicting opinions, leading to a dispute in the exchange of official documents. The governor refused to align with the governor-general, and as a result, the governor-general harbored resentment and even plotted to kill the governor. The governor became increasingly fearful, seeking ways to avoid the situation but finding none. He considered resigning but was unable to do so. Once, in the empty courtyard under the bright moonlight, he removed his hat and sighed deeply in solitude.

    A month ago, a washerwoman entered the governor's office. The lady of the house was pleased to see her and asked about her hometown, which turned out to be the same as the lady's. The washerwoman was about thirty years old, widowed and without support, having traveled by boat to Yuzhang (Nanchang). She was distinguished, clean, cautious, and strong-willed, unlike an ordinary woman, and her speech was sharp, clear, and pleasant. The governor found her character unusual.

    One day, the governor was deeply depressed and writing in the air, expressing his frustration. The washerwoman approached and said, "Your Excellency, in promoting governance, you should act with grace and not let trivial matters distract you. Why do you frown all day as if you are deeply troubled? I have heard that when the ruler is in trouble, the minister is disgraced. Why not tell me your concerns? Do not think that among women there are none who can solve problems." The governor replied, "You are a poor, widowed woman, what do you know? If I have something to say but cannot express it, why should I waste words?" The washerwoman said, "Is the military commander planning harm against you?" The governor was startled, and the washerwoman said, "Do not worry. The military commander is obsessed with wine and women and is unable to plan for the long term. I will help you resolve this crisis." The governor was pleased and asked for her plan. The washerwoman said, "Please wait until tomorrow morning."

    78

    妇早起，捧雕盘，盛熊燔一{目廷}，炙馨欲染指。使驰馈。受而甘之，报谢。及公谒抚，抚曰：“承贶嘉珍，安得此善庖丁？我府中刀俎不及也。”藩曰：“适来浣妇，初不知其工调剂。宪军如适口，当使其越俎而代。”抚喜。
    藩归告妇，妇欣然舆往。抚见之心荡，妇承以目。抚乐甚，留不返。且邀藩饮，一切酸咸，皆出妇手，不假咄嗟。抚每往狎妇，妇固黠甚，抚不可耐，要于槛而约之。妇曰：“大人高贵，贱妾躯龌龊，不足荐枕席。”抚坚之，妇乃约曰：“室南绮轩，薄暮请俟妾于轩中。”抚候之晚。时当秋凉，日甫昵，抚纱夹摇羽箑，大椅坐夜番棚下。

    Explanation

    78

    The woman rose early, carrying a beautifully carved plate, and served roasted bear meat that smelled so good it made one want to lick their fingers. She sent someone to deliver the food. The recipient received it with pleasure and expressed gratitude. When the public official (抚) visited, he said, "I have received this fine delicacy. How did you find such a skilled chef? My kitchen cannot match this." The official (藩) replied, "The woman who washed clothes was unexpectedly skilled in cooking. If the military official (宪军) likes her cooking, I will have her take over the kitchen." The public official was captivated by the woman’s beauty, and she reciprocated with her eyes. The official was very pleased and decided to stay. He also invited the other official to drink, and all the dishes were prepared by the woman, without any delay. The official frequently visited the woman, who was very clever, and the official became impatient, making a promise to her. The woman said, "You are of high status, and I am unworthy to serve you in bed." The official insisted, and she agreed to meet him in a pavilion in the south of the room in the evening. The official waited for her in the evening. It was a cool autumn day, with the sun setting, and he sat on a large chair in a bamboo hut, fanning himself with a feather fan.

    79

    俄妇至，持盘水向抚曰：“少坐，俟妾拂试以请。”抚颔之，妇入轩。顷见窗如针乱刺孔，抚视孔中出白气，缕缕如丝突出，旋绕抚身上下，不绝若网。既乃渐取渐缚，身不敢动，而芒刃往来，间不容发。妇曰：“贪婪贼，欺心太甚，将脔切尔，为豫章人泄忿。”抚战栗，哀恳，呼之以神，号之以仙，且尊之以菩萨，百千万意，不可思议。妇曰：“方伯，民望也，汝仇之何？今与汝约，勿贪、勿忌、勿淫、勿酷，我处曲山颠，朝朝暮暮，往来爽气，可鉴尔形，可烛尔心。千里万里，能呼吸至。”抚唯唯自誓。妇出轩曰：“好自为之，我去矣。”遂绕于白光中，长旦向西而灭。

    Explanation

    79

    A woman arrived shortly, holding a basin of water toward the governor and said, "Please sit a moment while I clean and prepare." The governor nodded, and the woman entered the pavilion. Soon, the window appeared as if it had been poked with needles, creating numerous holes. The governor saw white vapor coming out of the holes, like threads emerging and swirling around his body, forming a continuous net. Gradually, she began to bind him, and he could not move. The sharp blade moved back and forth, leaving no room for error. The woman said, "Greedy thief, your deceitfulness is excessive. I will cut you into pieces to avenge the people of Yuzhang." The governor trembled, pleaded, called her a deity, a fairy, and even a bodhisattva, expressing countless intentions, beyond comprehension. The woman said, "Governor, you are the people's hope. Why do you hate them? Now I make a pact with you: do not be greedy, envious, lustful, or cruel. I dwell at the top of Qu Mountain, where the fresh breeze blows day and night, reflecting your form and illuminating your heart. No matter how far, I can reach you with a breath." The governor nodded in agreement and swore. The woman left the pavilion, saying, "Take care of yourself. I am leaving." She then disappeared into the white light, moving westward until she vanished.

    80

    抚之发髯须眉衣裳，层层剥削，满地如尘。抚之身，如剥卵，如刮瓠，三月不视谒。后其行顿改，与某藩前怨亦释。

    齐无咎
    齐无咎，字冠卿，金陵人。性谨持，举优贡。客京师之粉坊胡同南口。邻多隙地，近苇塘。
    初夏午凉，齐独步，见一板扉，内败屋数间。无男子，有少妇，年二十许，好容色，一女奴。齐数见而访之，为孀。嘱媒妪通其意，求为妻。媒告妇，妇曰：“齐，贵人，非吾偶也。吾非大家世族，恐贻他日羞，不可。”后齐求为妾，许之。妇归，齐诘其邦族姓氏，妇曰：“买妾可不知其姓。”终不肯言。

    Explanation

    80

    Stripping away his hair, beard, eyebrows, and clothes, layer by layer, he left behind a mess that covered the ground like dust. Stripping himself, like peeling an egg and scraping a gourd, he remained isolated for three months without receiving visitors. Later, his behavior changed dramatically, and the grudge he had with a certain noble was also resolved.

    Qi Wujiu, courtesy name Guanqing, was from Jinling. He was cautious and disciplined, having been selected as a "优贡" (a type of scholar-official). He resided at the southern entrance of Fenfang Hutong in the capital city. His neighborhood had many vacant lots and was near reed ponds.

    One midsummer afternoon, Qi Wujiu took a solitary walk and saw a wooden door. Inside were several dilapidated rooms. There was no man, only a young woman in her twenties, with a beautiful appearance, and a female servant. Qi saw her several times and inquired about her, learning that she was a widow. He asked a matchmaker to convey his intentions and seek to marry her. The matchmaker told the woman, who said, "Qi is a noble person, not my match. I am not from a prominent family, and I fear it would bring shame in the future, so it is not possible." Later, Qi asked to become her concubine, and she agreed. When Qi asked her about her family name and background, the woman said, "When buying a concubine, one does not need to know her surname." She ultimately refused to reveal it.

    81

    妇不苟于言笑，而事齐颇勤，谓齐曰：“郎君客囊萧索，京城米珠薪桂，居大不易，且食指又增，当思所以治生者。”妇乃买磨一具，驴二头，麦数斛。磨得面，辄用驴驮，自鬻于市。至晚归，则麦囊中垂垂皆钱也。
    齐入课成均，多不家，又复得膏金。妇善生理，由是齐之客旅将丰于其家，从无柴米拮据。
    一日，墙外有腰斩一尸，无上段，京师汹汹然，而客坊初不以为齐妇，即齐亦断不以为妇之为之也。逾年未缉获，事宕。后妇产一子，齐肄业将满。每言欲与妇同归江南，妇但微哂，亦不答。时夜半，齐寝，闭户垂帏，忽失妇所在。齐惊怪，以为有奸，颇发怒愤。问其婢，曰：“娘子每常如是，不知所为。郎君特不知觉耳。”

    Explanation

    81

    The wife was not frivolous in her speech and laughter, but she served Qi diligently. She said to Qi, "My dear, your travel expenses are scarce, and the cost of living in the capital is high, making it difficult to make ends meet. Moreover, the number of people to be fed has increased, so you should think of ways to make a living." The wife then bought a mill, two donkeys, and several measures of wheat. She ground the wheat into flour, used the donkeys to carry it, and sold it in the market herself. By evening, when she returned, the wheat bag was full of money.

    Qi entered the Imperial Academy and spent little time at home, also receiving a substantial sum of money. The wife was skilled in managing the household, so Qi's travels would make the family prosperous, and they never lacked basic necessities.

    One day, outside the wall, there was a decapitated body with no head, causing panic in the capital.
