The innkeepers initially did not suspect Qi's wife, and Qi also did not think it was his wife who did it. For over a year, the case remained unsolved. Later, the wife gave birth to a son, and Qi was about to complete his studies. Every time he mentioned wanting to return to Jiangnan with his wife, she only smiled slightly and did not respond. One night, Qi slept, closed the door, and hung the curtain, but suddenly found his wife missing. Qi was shocked and suspected she was having an affair, becoming very angry. He asked his servant, who said, "Madam often does this, I don't know why. You just don't notice it."

    82

    齐起立庭院，傍徨蹀躞，月色如画。忽闻飞隼突落，一人自屋而下，红绢裹头，大部虬须，右手持一匕首，左手携二人首。齐方惊顾，其人相对摘须，乃妇也。妇曰：“郎君无怪也。”遂入室，告齐曰：“妾父宦于闽之长汀，为上官所枉，奇冤刻骨。数年以来，此仇已报，克不可留。”齐视其二首，则已劓鼻抉睛，糊不可辨。妇更以白练束身，取灰革囊函首携之，曰：“妾幸托小星得所栖止，报我大仇。女奴是妾数年所抚，郎可纳之，以代我任，且育汝子。”言讫收泪，逾重垣，莫知其向。齐甚惊愕。少顷妇却至，曰：“适去忘哺得孩子。”良久出，便对齐拱手去。齐悚立一晌，入室不闻儿啼，视之儿已身首异处矣。呼女奴询其故，女奴曰：“妾十岁，父母鬻于娘子。娘子育之，五年而不知娘子为谁也。”齐令女密其事，纳之为妾。
    是年，齐得官，为东川云阳丞。后终不闻妇之音问也。
    (传奇中《锁云囊》有女盗挂须髯，绝相类。)

    Explanation

    82

    A Woman of Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    83

    卷三 报应部(善恶并附)
    陆修
    临安马指挥某，未尝读书，而雅欲教子。因延师于槜里之陆修。修固名士也，马耳其名，丰馆谷以相招。陆就马，马亦礼敬陆。陆固检束自持，馆政之外，不与他事。马一子，名骥良，让梨之岁，其父母爱如掌上珍。乳妪婢女，日往来于绛帏皋比之间，如莺梭鱼贯，杂沓不休。陆唯端坐正襟，静翻书卷，丝毫不为之动。
    一日，有婢湘青，送梅子于其徒，因取一枚向陆曰：“先生梅之。”陆摇首曰：“毋庸。”婢笑目：“不用梅，用我杏否？”陆持戒扑几上，訇然有声。婢咋舌去。自此馆内肃然，不敢驰驱，皆奉先生。约半年，其徒颇循师范。陆每当课余，辄命骥良隅坐，喜讲古今孝悌故事，媚娓不倦。陆尝语人曰：“蒙以养正，为圣功之始。故幼稚之年，实为终身成败相关。必先正其心性，而文艺其后焉。如始基不正，虽异时才华震世，大节有亏，何足重也。”马及其妻，咸爱陆之能善诱。

    Explanation

    83

    Chapter Three: The Book of Retribution (Good and Evil, with Appendices)
    Lu Xiu

    In Lin'an, there was a certain commander named Ma, who had never studied but earnestly desired to educate his son. Therefore, he invited a teacher from the town of Zui Li named Lu Xiu. Lu Xiu was indeed a renowned scholar, and Ma, hearing of his name, provided generous accommodation and food to invite him. Lu Xiu accepted the invitation and was treated with great respect by Ma. Lu Xiu maintained a disciplined and self-controlled demeanor, focusing solely on his duties in the academy and avoiding other matters. Ma had one son named Ji Liang, who, in the year he was given pears (a symbol of filial piety and sibling harmony), was cherished by his parents as a precious treasure. The wet nurses and maidservants would continuously move in and out of the room, bustling like birds weaving or fish swimming in a row. Despite this, Lu Xiu remained seated upright and dignified, quietly reading books, showing no reaction to the commotion.

    One day, a maid named Xiang Qing brought some plums to the students and took one to offer to Lu Xiu, saying, "Teacher, here is a plum for you." Lu Xiu shook his head and said, "No need." Xiang Qing smiled and said, "If you don't want a plum, do you want an apricot from me?" Lu Xiu, maintaining his discipline, slapped the table, making a loud sound. Xiang Qing left with a startled expression. From then on, the atmosphere in the academy became solemn and respectful, and everyone was afraid to act recklessly, all showing respect to the teacher. After about half a year, the students began to follow Lu Xiu's example and behave properly.

    Whenever there was a break from lessons, Lu Xiu would often seat Ji Liang at the corner of the room and happily tell him stories about filial piety and brotherly love, speaking sweetly and tirelessly. Lu Xiu once told others, "Nurturing the young with righteousness is the beginning of saintly virtue. Therefore, the early years of childhood are truly related to one's success or failure throughout life. One must first correct one's heart and nature, and then pursue literary and artistic studies afterward. If the foundation is not right, even if one becomes famous for their talents later in life, there will be flaws in one's major principles, and how can it be valued?" Ma and his wife both admired Lu Xiu's ability to educate effectively.

    84

    时秋深绵雨，陆偶感寒疾，卧榻。晚课毕，良归告其母。马妻闻之，恐陆生衾薄，乃命婢袱新绸被送斋中。陆卧覆榻上。晨，马来视疾。陆未起，马见床边有一红女舄，窃拾而视之，乃其妻物，袖而返。以馆后有径通内，诘妻。妻告以送被误。马不之信。及夜，命婢诡传主母命邀之，己操刃往，开门，即杀陆。陆闻命，怒曰：“咄，是何言？明日告汝主，当挝杀汝。”马返，疑未释，更逼其妻往，陆曰：“吾承贤夫延为西席，讵以冥冥堕行哉？贤夫受朝廷官，一生名义，汝为之丧尽矣！”妻请开门，陆曰：“此门生死之关，人禽之界，速请回步。先生休矣，断不为夫人启也。”马疑释而弃刀焉。

    Explanation

    84

    In the depths of autumn, with continuous rain, Mr. Lu accidentally caught a cold and lay bedridden. After the evening lesson, the servant returned and told his mother. Upon hearing this, Mr. Ma's wife was concerned that Mr. Lu's quilt was too thin, so she ordered a maid to wrap a new silk quilt and send it to the study. Mr. Lu lay on the bed. In the morning, Mr. Ma came to visit his illness. Mr. Lu had not yet gotten up, and Mr. Ma saw a red woman's shoe beside the bed. He secretly picked it up and looked at it, realizing it belonged to his wife, and put it in his sleeve before returning. Knowing there was a path behind the school that led to the inner courtyard, Mr. Ma questioned his wife. She told him that she had mistakenly sent the quilt. Mr. Ma did not believe her. By night, he ordered a maid to falsely claim that the mistress had invited Mr. Lu, and he himself went with a knife. He opened the door and immediately killed Mr. Lu. Upon hearing the command, Mr. Lu angrily said, "What nonsense! I will tell your master tomorrow, and he will beat you to death." Mr. Ma returned, but his doubts were not resolved, and he further pressured his wife to go. Mr. Lu said, "I accepted your husband's invitation to teach here. How could I fall into wrongdoing in the dark? Your husband holds an official position from the imperial court, and his reputation for life is ruined by you!" His wife asked to open the door, and Mr. Lu said, "This door is the gateway to life and death, the boundary between humans and beasts. Quickly step back. Mr. Ma, stop, I will never open this door for you." Mr. Ma's doubts were resolved, and he discarded the knife.

    85

    翌旦，陆借故辞馆。马谢曰：“先生君子也。”为之备述昨夕颠末，方悟送被卷鞋之误所自来耳。
    甚矣，吾为陆生危矣。馆扉一启，祸何可言。不特立丧其元，抑且枉害彼妇。尝谓陆生能绝邪径之履，义也；申宾主之正，礼也；晨告辞，智也；托他故，仁也。有君子之道四焉，可以为师矣。世之下榻东家者，正宜自检瓜田李下，用防未然。正不得藉口坐怀，反诮鲁男子之闭门为迁也。

    Explanation

    85

    The next day, Mr. Lu left the residence, citing an excuse. Mr. Ma thanked him, saying, "You are indeed a gentleman." He then recounted the entire incident from the previous evening, and finally understood the origin of the misunderstanding regarding the bedding and shoes.

    How great is my concern for Mr. Lu! Once the door of the residence is opened, what kind of disaster can be imagined? Not only would he lose his head, but also wrongfully harm that woman. I have often said that Mr. Lu avoids taking the wrong path, which is righteousness; he upholds the proper relationship between host and guest, which is propriety; he announces his departure in the morning, which is wisdom; he uses an excuse, which is benevolence. There are four ways of a gentleman, and he can be a teacher. Those who stay at the east host's place should indeed check themselves in the melon field and under the plum tree to prevent something before it happens. One should not use the excuse of being in a relationship to justify inappropriate behavior, nor criticize the virtuous man's action as excessive.

    86

    或云，此万历丁丑进士陆世科事，后官至大理正卿，不附魏党而归。
    (吾乡富甲某，忽欲延师课子。会当夏月，晒麦于场，雨骤来，诸佣工皆为之盖藏。富甲问曰：“教书匠何以不至？”师闻之，怒而去。嘻，可怪也。师也者，言其文章品行足以矜式后人，故延之，尽礼以特之，折节以求之。宁为过情，毋为不及。情则尊师之道得，乃有以获书香之报。
    今富甲以教诗说礼之儒，侪之梓匠轮舆之列，猥曰其志将以求食也？夫亦思一器一物，倩人成就，尤必殷勤至而款洽申。况以子弟受裁于师，何等关系，何等慎重，顾以轻薄相尝耶？而师之所以为师者，亦贵自尊其道，以为养正圣功之本，方不愧北面西宾之称。不然，亦适宜为富甲打麦场尔，又何常师之有!)

    Explanation

    86

    Some say, this is the story of Lu Shike, who passed the imperial examination in the year Ding Chou during the Wanli era and later held the position of Justice of the Supreme Court, refusing to align with the Cao Wei faction and eventually returning home.

    (A wealthy person from my hometown suddenly decided to hire a teacher for his son. It was during the summer, and they were drying wheat in the courtyard when the rain came suddenly. All the hired workers covered the wheat to protect it. The wealthy person asked, "Why hasn't the teacher arrived?" The teacher heard this and became angry, leaving. How strange! A teacher is someone whose writings and moral character are worthy of being a model for future generations, so one should invite them with the utmost respect, treat them specially, and humble oneself to seek their guidance. It is better to go beyond what is expected than to fall short. By showing genuine respect for teachers, one can gain the benefits of scholarly success.

    Now, the wealthy person treats the teacher as a carpenter or wheelwright, suggesting that the teacher's only goal was to earn a living? One should also think about how even a simple object requires careful craftsmanship and attention. How much more so, then, for a student receiving instruction from a teacher, which is of great importance and should be taken seriously, not treated lightly? A teacher should also hold themselves in high regard and take their role seriously, so as not to be unworthy of the title of teacher. If not, the teacher is just another worker in the wealthy person's wheat field, and there is no real teacher-student relationship!)

    87

    掷狐裘
    福建孝廉林某，会试北上，舟泊吴江一高楼下。夜半楼中火起，岸上鼎沸。忽一少妇单衣坠于舟中。林急掷狐裘一袭，与之蔽体，置令坐于仓中，自挑灯出立船头以待之。
    天明，令登岸，送之归。返，即解缆去。林以是科成进士。因偕同年谒房师，拜谢毕，房考曰：“子有大阴德。前阅卷时，见此卷，油污，已置落卷中。假寐时，梦一长髯赤面人阅此卷，且批云：‘裸形妇，狐裘裹。秉烛达旦，汝与我。’醒时，卷已在案，因荐中焉。”林因述前事，公啧啧称奇。内有一吴江同年，向林下拜曰：“坠楼人，即我妻也。是夕，某赴酌于外，闻失火，亟归。一婢一仆已为灰烬，度妻亦必罹于难。平明，见妻归，狐裘灿然，问所从来，云是舟中人所赠。我疑必有所污，斥归母家，自谓恩断义绝之意。年兄即活其命，又全其节，真恩重抵山，宜为天神所钦也。”房考叹：“此若非圣帝显灵，吴江生不兔为负心人，而夫人终抱不洁之名矣！宜速归作好合计。”

    Explanation

    87

    Translation:

    Throwing the Fox Fur Coat

    A scholar from Fujian, who had passed the provincial examination and was heading north for the metropolitan examination, moored his boat under a high building in Wujiang. At midnight, a fire broke out in the building, causing great commotion on the shore. Suddenly, a young woman in a single garment fell into the boat. The scholar quickly threw her a fox fur coat to cover her body, seated her in the hold, and lit a lamp to stand watch at the bow of the boat.

    At dawn, he helped her ashore and sent her home. He then untied the mooring ropes and set off. That year, he passed the metropolitan examination and became a Jinshi (imperial scholar). He went with his fellow examinees to visit their examiner, and after bowing and thanking him, the examiner said, "You have done great good deeds. When I was reviewing the exam papers earlier, I saw one that was oil-stained and had been placed among the failed ones. While napping, I dreamed of a man with a long beard and a red face who was reading this paper and wrote, 'A naked woman, wrapped in a fox fur coat. Holding a candle until dawn, you belong to me.' When I woke up, the paper was already on the desk, and I recommended it for admission." The scholar recounted his earlier experience, and the examiner marveled at it. One of the fellow examinees from Wujiang knelt before the scholar and said, "The woman who fell from the building is my wife. That night, I went out drinking, and upon hearing about the fire, I rushed home. My maid and servant were already reduced to ashes, and I feared my wife must have perished too. At dawn, I saw her return, wearing a bright fox fur coat. She said it was given to her by someone on the boat. I suspected she must have been defiled and sent her back to her mother's house, thinking our relationship was over. You have saved her life and preserved her virtue, truly a debt of gratitude as heavy as a mountain, worthy of the admiration of the gods." The examiner sighed, "If it were not for the divine intervention of the Holy Emperor, the man from Wujiang would have been a disloyal person, and his wife would have been left with an unfortunate reputation! You should quickly return and make amends."

    88

    生泣谢。后归，夫妇如初。林榜下除浙令，便道往访，夫妻出拜欢谢。犹出其狐裘相示，以志感佩不忘云。

    一枝花
    福州生员林涛，少年美貌，如粉妆玉琢，艳丽胜于裙钗。因下乡庄收租，宿于佃家。
    晚间，偶出垅上闲步，归见案上有兰花一枝，鲜香可爱，不知从何处来。明日，见一小女垂髻，窗前窥探。林就窗而语，女即笑，步而去，振振有声。继而复来，曰：“昨日有一枝花落在此，着我来讨还。”林曰：“在此。”问：“此花为谁之物？”女曰：“我姊昨来看汝住处，落在此。”林笑还之。女去，又持花来掷林曰：“我姊说这花教你一夜便弄得此等模样儿。晚间月上，姊约你到东厢赔花问罪。”女去，灯静，林至东厢。

    Explanation

    88

    Lin cried and thanked her. After returning, the husband and wife were as they were at first. Lin, who had been appointed as the governor of Zhejiang, took a detour to visit. The couple came out to bow and express their joy and gratitude. The fox spirit still took out her fox fur coat to show, to mark the gratitude and admiration that would not be forgotten.

    In another story, Lin Tao, a scholar from Fuzhou, was young and handsome, like jade carved in fine detail, more beautiful than a woman. Because he was collecting rent in the countryside, he stayed at a tenant's house. In the evening, he happened to go out for a walk in the fields and returned to see a fresh and fragrant orchid on the table, not knowing where it came from. The next day, he saw a little girl with a hanging braid peeking at him from the window. Lin spoke to her through the window, and the girl smiled and walked away, making a clear sound. Then she came back and said, "My sister dropped a flower here yesterday, and I came to return it." Lin said, "It's here." She asked, "Whose flower is this?" The girl said, "My sister came to see you yesterday and dropped it here." Lin laughed and returned the flower to her. The girl left, then came back and threw the flower at Lin, saying, "My sister said this flower made you look like this last night. When the moon rises tonight, my sister will meet you in the east wing to settle the matter with the flower." The girl left, and as the lamp went out, Lin went to the east wing.

    89

    移时，果见一女，嫣然而来，年十七八，俊俏无比。林一见销魂，携手并肩，觉香气馥郁，竟体如脂。彼此各道衷曲，真如胶漆。歘闻有呼“荷姑”声，女曰：“空庭冷露，不可为欢。明日父兄入城，舍下无人，郎可从屋后绕入内房，当焚香扫榻以待。”叮咛而别，林归室卧，辗转思慕，一夜自不交睫。继闻枕上鸡鸣，树头鸦叫，旦气澄然，中怀顿释。自念：“我已有妻，彼尚未嫁，一时乱之，实为损德。明岁科场岂可望乎？”遂披衣早起，匆匆入城，自此足迹不至，女亦无由寄讯。闻其一病几死，林毅然不顾也。丙子遂捷乡书，人以为不淫之报云。
    (人有转念遂成恶道，然必察其初心之是否。若林子之竟夜低徊，卒成正果，可谓善补过者。)

    Explanation

    89

    After some time, a charming young woman appeared, around seventeen or eighteen years old, with a beauty that was unmatched. Upon seeing her, Lin was utterly captivated, and they walked side by side, feeling the sweet fragrance and the smooth, delicate texture of her body. They shared their deepest feelings, truly inseparable like glue and lacquer. Suddenly, they heard someone calling out, "Hegu." The woman said, "The courtyard is empty and the dew is cold; we cannot indulge in our desires. Tomorrow, my father and brothers will enter the city, and there will be no one at home. You may enter the inner chamber by going around the back of the house. I will burn incense and tidy the bed to wait for you." After earnest instructions, they parted ways, and Lin returned to his room, tossing and turning with longing, unable to sleep all night. Later, he heard the rooster crowing near his pillow and the crows calling from the trees, the morning air was fresh and clear, and his heart suddenly felt relieved. He thought to himself, "I already have a wife, and she has not yet been married. If I act rashly for a moment, it is actually a loss of virtue. How can I hope to pass the next year's exams?" So, he got up early, dressed quickly, and hurried into the city, and from then on, he stopped visiting. The woman also had no way to send messages. He heard that she fell ill and nearly died, but Lin firmly disregarded it. In the year of Bingzi, he passed the local exams, and people believed it was a reward for not indulging in lust. (When people change their minds, they may fall into evil ways, but one must examine whether their initial intentions were good. If Lin's night of wandering finally led to a successful outcome, he can be considered a good person who corrects his mistakes.)

    90

    冬烘生
    吾乡有前辈者，饩于庠，诚笃太古风，教胄为业。三十而鳏，终日静坐。课读之外，一无所问，亦一无所事事。与人言谈，蔼如也。尝自塾中归，手持一卷书，行路诵之，失足坠眢井中。自妻没后，皆就馆谷。东家某，爱敬之。
    一日，其东纳一姬，家人哄其事。老生微闻之，嘱其徒曰：“请若翁来，告一事。”顷东至，相对坐。半晌，老生注视之，不发一语。东人曰：“师适召何事？”老生曰：“无甚事。”东人以冗辞之出。老生蹀躞沉思，又以指圈画空赴，复命其徒：“请若翁。”东再至，曰：“师有何事？直言毋隐。”老生乃趦趄曰：“闻君纳一新宠，有诸？”东曰：“然，适买得一村女子耳。”老生曰：“女来几日矣？”曰：“昔者。”老生乃曼声曰：“昔者，盍与我？”东笑谓之曰：“吾亦知师鳏居久，当为吾师娶一佳偶，此特奔走婢，不足当师中馈主，容再图之。”老生起谢。家人闻而粲然，在老生固不以为非。

    Explanation

    90

    The Pedantic Scholar

    In my village, there was a respected elder who was supported by the local school. He was deeply committed to the ancient way and made teaching the young his profession. At the age of thirty, he became a widower and spent his days in quiet contemplation. Beyond his lessons, he asked about nothing and did nothing else. When he spoke with others, he was gentle and kind. Once, returning from the school, he held a scroll of books in his hand and recited it as he walked, only to lose his footing and fall into an abandoned well. After his wife's death, he relied entirely on the fees from his students. His host, a certain gentleman from the east, loved and respected him.

    One day, the host took in a concubine, and the household was excited about the matter. The old scholar heard it faintly and instructed his student to say, "Please ask your father to come, as I have something to tell him." Soon, the host arrived, and they sat facing each other. After a while, the old scholar stared at him without speaking a word. The host asked, "Teacher, what did you call me for?" The old scholar replied, "Nothing much." The host dismissed him with some excuse. The old scholar paced back and forth in deep thought, drawing circles in the air with his fingers, and then told his student, "Please ask your father again." The host returned and asked, "Teacher, what is it? Speak plainly without hiding anything." The old scholar hesitated and said, "I heard that you have taken in a new concubine, is that true?" The host replied, "Yes, I just bought a village girl." The old scholar asked, "How long has she been here?" The host answered, "From the past." The old scholar then said in a drawn-out tone, "From the past, why not give her to me?" The host smiled and said, "I also know that you have been a widower for a long time. I will arrange for you to marry a good match. This girl is just a servant who runs errands and is not suitable to be your main wife. Let me think about it again." The old scholar got up and thanked him. The household heard and laughed, but the old scholar did not consider it wrong.

    91

    会前村有新孀，其东遂与老生媒焉。媒，婚于馆后小园。屋一椽，釜、杓、床、帐，悉东与之办。合卺之夕，老生簪花衣蓝，中坐青庐，行交拜扎，而腼腆胜于少年。观者殊不以为再访蓝桥也。三朝谢客，老生喜形于色。后其妻欲归宁，老生亲为控驴，妻至前夫墓所，下驴而泣。老生亦泣，妻呼夫而恸，老生则呼之为兄云。
    时妻煮麦缕，少齑辛，欲乞诸邻。嘱老生视，勿过火。老生酣读忘之。及妻归，而缕亦成糊矣。邻女子汲于井上，裙幅为风飏起，老生就而下之，女诟厉焉。老生曰：“妇道衣裙不当如是。我不为整，是我之过也。”乡人知其诚，而不之咎。其生平大率类是。

    Explanation

    91

    There was a recently widowed woman in a nearby village. The East arranged for her to marry in a small garden behind the inn. A single-roomed house, a pot, a ladle, a bed, and curtains were all provided by the East. On the wedding night, the old man wore a flower in his hair and blue clothes, sat in the middle of a blue pavilion, performed the exchange of bows, and was more bashful than a young man. The onlookers did not consider it a second chance at love. Three days after the wedding, the old man refused visitors and showed joy on his face. Later, his wife wanted to visit her parents' home, and the old man personally led the donkey. When the wife arrived at the grave of her former husband, she dismounted and wept, and the old man also wept. The wife called out to her husband and mourned deeply, and the old man then called her husband "brother" and said so.

    At that time, the wife was cooking wheat noodles with little seasoning, and she wanted to ask the neighbors for help. She instructed the old man to watch the noodles and not to burn them. The old man was so engrossed in reading that he forgot about it. When the wife returned, the noodles had turned into a paste. A neighbor woman was drawing water from the well, and her skirt was blown up by the wind. The old man went over to help her down, and the woman shouted angrily. The old man said, "A woman should not dress like that. If I had helped her, it would have been my fault." The villagers knew his sincerity and did not blame him. His life was mostly like this.

    92

    举一子，有夙慧，长能文。会徵宏博，擢第二。晚岁至滇黔节制，咸以为忠厚之报。
    (七如氏曰：冬烘一生行谊，皆如老树着花，无一丑枝而古艳，跃跃纸上。盖悃款出于自然，风流亦自不免。时对此篇犹令人神往于函丈春容际耳。)

    江善人
    豫章省城外，有黄牛洲，江姓家于此。尝商于闽、广间，航海上下，数十年也。江生平好善，不欺童叟。见人捕燕雀，必售而放之生。每曰：“乌语数间，乐意可聆。今人笼之棘中，以听其呼朋哀怨之声，亦复何也？”
    一年，自闽抵粤，过大矶岛。飓风突起，四顾冥合。长虹挂天，海水震荡。舟师入，向顺风入大洋，罔知其所。既而桅折舶裂，百人皆溺，而江亦赴涛中。自揣万无生理，忽觉身畔有木。江抱之，木起江起，浪落身落。浮沉出没一日夜，江力尽，风愈狂。江随波至岸，觉水浅，身不自持，海浪推沙於身际，犹相击也。

    Explanation

    92

    Translation:

    He had one son, who from a young age showed innate intelligence and grew up to be skilled in writing. When he was selected for the grand examination, he was ranked second. In his later years, he reached a position of military control in Yunnan and Guizhou, and everyone believed it was a reward for his loyalty and kindness.

    (Seven-Like Master said: A life of conduct as bland and unremarkable as winter fog, yet like flowers blooming on an old tree, not a single ugly branch, yet anciently beautiful, leaping out of the paper. Sincerity and honesty come naturally, and he could not escape being elegant and refined. At that time, reading this piece still makes one yearn for the springtime atmosphere of the master's presence.)

    The Good Man Jiang

    Outside the provincial capital of Yuzhang, there was a place called Huangniuzhou, where a family surnamed Jiang lived. Jiang once traded in Fujian and Guangdong, sailing up and down the seas for many years. Jiang was always kind-hearted and never deceived children or old people. When he saw people catching sparrows and swallows, he would buy them and set them free. He often said, "The calls of the birds in a few trees can be pleasant to listen to. Now people cage them in thorns to hear their cries of longing and sorrow, what for?"

    One year, while traveling from Fujian to Guangdong, he passed by Dajishi Island. Suddenly, a hurricane arose, and all around was pitch black. A rainbow hung in the sky, and the sea was violently agitated. The ship's crew headed into the open sea with the wind, unaware of their destination. Soon after, the mast broke and the ship split apart, and all aboard were drowned, including Jiang, who was swept into the waves. Thinking to himself that there was no chance of survival, he suddenly felt something wooden beside him. Jiang clung to it, and as the wood rose, so did he; as the waves fell, so did he. He floated and sank for a whole day and night, exhausted, with the wind becoming even more fierce. Jiang was carried by the waves to the shore, feeling the water was shallow, unable to support himself, with the sea waves still pushing sand against him.

    93

    顷而势暂杀，潮当寅遇，暴定日晴。江已匍卧沙岸。风余威尚呼呼，满身衣夹可半干，幸秋初不寒。神定举目望北，皆巉巉岩石，匐走圈豚。依附藤葛而上，及巅，三面皆汪洋，水天相接。独岛后西向，草满石礧，不辨径路。江忖云：“我江某不死鱼鳖，讵独吝于虎狼？望洋无益也，且腹中枵。”于是缘磴下，入草窠杂树之中。见山枣殷红，脱落满地。江啖之，不饥，望岩际茸茸处，微露一线行迹。江尾之二三里，闻鸡犬声，渐亦隐隐似屋角出丛莽。江喜而奔，无何，居然村落也。户烟虽少，而守望皆整。村外一翁策杖来，长须髯，飘飘然道妆，与中华无异。江前致词，告以舟遭风坏，望乞怜收。翁曰：“听尔声口，似江西人。”江曰：“南昌郡。”翁曰：“我乡里也。”引之入村，村中老少见翁，皆拱立。江忆翁必林下绅。至门，入内，登堂，甚巍焕。江匍匐，翁掖之起，曰：“乡里也，何必尔尔？既至此，可暂栖身。”指耳室居之，衣具悉备。

    Explanation

    93

    A sudden lull in the storm ensued, and the tide met at the hour of Yin. The storm settled, and the sun came out. Jiang lay crawling on the sandy shore. The wind still blew strongly, and his clothes clung to his body, half-dry. Fortunately, it was not cold at the beginning of autumn. Calming down, he looked up and looked north, seeing steep and rugged rocks that resembled a pig enclosure. He climbed up, relying on vines and creepers, and reached the summit, where three sides were vast and boundless, with the water and sky meeting. Behind and facing west, there was an island, covered with grass and stone heaps, making it hard to distinguish the path. Jiang thought to himself, "I, River Jiang, am not meant to die by fish and turtles, so why should I spare even tigers and wolves? Looking out to sea is no use, and I am also hungry." Therefore, he climbed down the steps and entered a thicket of mixed trees. He saw deep red mountain jujubes fallen all over the ground. He ate them, and his hunger was satisfied. He looked at the soft and dense edge of the rock, where a faint trace of a path appeared. He followed it for two or three miles and heard the sounds of chickens and dogs. Gradually, the corners of houses emerged from the dense vegetation. Jiang was happy and ran. Soon, he indeed saw a village. Although the smoke from the houses was sparse, the guarding and watching were all orderly. An old man came outside the village with a cane, having a long beard and wearing Taoist attire, no different from a Chinese person. Jiang went forward and spoke, telling him that his boat was damaged by the wind and begged for mercy and shelter. The old man said, "Listen to your voice, you seem to be from Jiangxi." Jiang replied, "Nanchang Prefecture." The old man said, "I am from your hometown." He led him into the village, and the elders and young in the village stood respectfully when they saw the old man. Jiang remembered that the old man must be a retired gentleman. Upon reaching the door, they entered and went inside, where the hall was grand and splendid. Jiang knelt, and the old man helped him up, saying, "We are from the same hometown, why be so formal? Since you are here, you can temporarily stay." He pointed to a room in the ear and arranged for clothing and utensils to be prepared.

    94

    江居半月，每日蔬菜饭颇洁精，不及荤酒。往来仆御，皆江西声口。江因询其众，去中华几远？众含糊答之。而翁一日呼江曰：“尔能会计，为我司日掌记。”江诺。惟日记数百人米菜而已。至晨，有人舁买物至江所，所过数登簿而已。如是者年余，江固诚悫，翁喜之，问江曰：“汝亦念故乡否？”江泣曰：“蒙长者留养，实所心愿；惟家有慈亲，望子不归，恐断肠耳。”翁曰：“此地亦好，欲归亦不难事。”江闻言，跪请归省。翁许以异日。

    Explanation

    94

    Jiang stayed for half a month, during which he ate vegetable rice every day that was quite clean and refined, without meat or alcohol. The servants and drivers who came and went all had a Jiangxi accent. Jiang then asked the group how far they were from China, and the group answered vaguely. One day, the old man called Jiang and said, "You can handle accounts and manage my daily records." Jiang agreed. His task was simply to record the daily meals of several hundred people. In the morning, someone carried purchased goods to Jiang's place, and the items were simply recorded in the ledger. This went on for over a year. Jiang was indeed honest and sincere, and the old man liked him. The old man asked Jiang, "Do you also miss your hometown?" Jiang cried and said, "Being taken care of by the elder, it is indeed my wish; but my kind mother at home, hoping for my return, is afraid that she will break her heart." The old man said, "This place is also good, returning is not a difficult matter." Upon hearing this, Jiang knelt and requested to return home to visit. The old man agreed for another day.

    95

    晨，江抱簿登堂，一一交翁讫。翁乃策杖出门，至海边，杳无舟楫。翁掷杖波中，即化一巨舰。翁与江登之，令江闭目勿启，但闻风声浪声。既而渐远渐微，而乡音市语隐约来前。翁曰：“至矣。”江瞪望惊喜，则“滕王高阁临江渚”也。翁入阁，江随之，见阁下神案香楮布满符箓。翁取案上供神柑，剖其瓤，与江。江食之，翁仰以空皮合置俎间。江又随翁至厨下，见刀俎满前，砧烹错杂。翁持一纸函与江曰：“人问汝，以此贻之。”江纳于袖中，翁即翻身入灶而没。江急曰：“长者赴火。”而厨师执之曰：“此天师洁斋之所，闲人何擅至此？”江曰：“适与长者至，忽入灶内矣。”
    遂出封函以验，拆之，即早间天师祈雨表文。中有两错字，特为圈出。又指供上柑果，空一枚。江抚询之，详知其好善，署石表于州曰“善人处”。而江始知翁之为旌阳许真人也。益修善行，母子悉登上寿云。

    Explanation

    95

    In the morning, Jiang carried a ledger and ascended the hall, completing the transactions with Old Man one by one. Old Man then leaned on a cane and went out the door, reaching the seaside, where no boats were in sight. Old Man threw his cane into the waves, and it instantly transformed into a giant ship. Old Man and Jiang boarded it, and Old Man ordered Jiang to close his eyes and not open them, but only to hear the sounds of wind and waves. Gradually, they became distant and faint, and local dialects and market language began to隐约 come before them. Old Man said, "We have arrived." Jiang gazed with surprise and joy, and they found themselves at the high pavilion of Duke Teng overlooking the riverbank. Old Man entered the pavilion, and Jiang followed him, seeing an altar with incense, paper money, and talismans. Old Man took a citrus fruit from the altar, cut open its flesh, and gave it to Jiang. Jiang ate it, and Old Man placed the empty skin on the altar. Jiang then followed Old Man to the kitchen, where knives and cutting boards were everywhere, and the preparation of food was杂乱. Old Man handed a paper document to Jiang, saying, "If anyone asks you, give this to them." Jiang put it in his sleeve, and Old Man turned around and disappeared into the stove. Jiang hurriedly said, "The elder is going to the fire." The cook grabbed him and said, "This is the place of the Heavenly Master's pure fasting, how dare an outsider擅自 come here?" Jiang then took out the sealed document to verify it, opened it, and it was the early morning prayer document for rain. There were two typos, which were specially marked. He also pointed to the missing fruit on the altar. Jiang inquired about it, and learned in detail about Old Man's good deeds. Old Man carved a stone tablet in the state proclaiming "Land of the Virtuous." Jiang then realized that Old Man was the Immortal Xu Shu of Jingyang. He increased his good deeds, and both mother and son lived to old age.

    96

    (七如氏曰：“云中鸡犬，合宅飞升，岂清虚之表，有一境位置之耶？据此，则神仙踪迹，仍在人间。第为桃花流水杳然洁处耳。”是说亦近理。)

    墙折弄
    吴门陆采侯者，慷爽人也，顺治年间，有某商主其家置绸缎诸货，已毕，欲束装行。采侯止之曰：“诘朝重阳佳节，客不囊萸山上，而反载月船头，不诚太煞风景耶？”商颔之，乃移货贮他寓，为便行计。
    明日，携斗酒登治平寺，相与尽一日之欢。晚归，他寓火，千金物付之一炬。采侯叹惋，且伤客之荡尽也。语商云：“是非客之过，我贻之咎。若货未登舟，货犹我货也。且我若不强留，又安及火。”竟偿其值。商感谢而去。采侯与其弟俊侯同居，邻家火，左右俱烬，独陆氏之庐无恙。
    未几，邻再火，两邻又荡然，而陆氏之庐仍无恙。时左邻高墙已倾，采侯兄弟正覆其下，佥曰：“陆氏昆仲不得正命死。”及锄，视之，见墙倾如折，中一弄然。两人战栗危坐，了无损伤。

    Explanation

    96

    Translation:

    (Seven Ru said: "The chickens and dogs in the clouds, along with their homes, flew into the heavens. Could there be a specific place in the pure and empty realm where they are positioned? According to this, the traces of immortals still remain in the human world. They are merely in a place as clean and remote as the peach blossoms and flowing water." This explanation is also reasonable.)

    Wall Collapse

    Lu Caihou of Wu Gate was an open and straightforward man. During the reign of Emperor Shunzhi, a merchant who had finished setting up silk and satin goods in his home wanted to pack and leave. Caihou stopped him and said, "The next day is the Chongyang Festival. Why not carry chrysanthemums to the mountain instead of carrying the moon on the boat? Isn't that too spoiling the scenery?" The merchant nodded in agreement, and Caihou moved the goods to another residence to facilitate the journey.

    The next day, Caihou carried a pot of wine to Zhiping Temple and spent the day in joy together. On returning late, the other residence caught fire, and thousands of gold worth of goods were reduced to ashes. Caihou sighed and lamented the loss of the guest's goods. He said to the merchant, "This is not the guest's fault; it is my responsibility. If the goods had not been loaded onto the boat, they would still be my goods. And if I had not insisted on staying, how would the fire have occurred?" He ultimately compensated the guest for the value. The merchant thanked him and left. Caihou lived with his younger brother Junhou. When the neighbor's house caught fire, everything around it was burned to ashes, but the Lu family's house remained unharmed.

    Not long after, the neighbor's house caught fire again, and both neighboring houses were completely destroyed, but the Lu family's house remained unharmed. At that time, the high wall of the left neighbor's house had collapsed, and Caihou and his younger brother were just underneath it. Everyone said, "The Lu brothers will not die a natural death." When they dug it up and looked, they saw that the wall had collapsed as if it had been cut, and there was a gap in the middle. The two men were trembling and sitting upright, with no injuries at all.

    97

    金驼子
    洞庭东山金驼子，背曲如弓，心性灵敏，人多爱之。肖其形，呼为“金元宝”。人家有喜庆事，总得金元宝到门，以为佳谶。金复能为谀词祝焉，故远近争致之。金一一至其家，莫不醵金钱、具酒食，欣然醉饱，盈袖而归。
    数年，家渐裕，有田二十亩，皆膏腴地，旱潦无虞，乡人号曰“米囤”。里有某甲，富而贪，涎之，求售于驼，驼不卖。谚曰：“乡里老儿生得怪，越贵越不卖。”甲意甚恨，转辗寻思，乃与役勾，使人讼驼，驼倾囊，遂欲鬻田，甲贱得之，价不及半也。驼自此贫，无有再问元宝来者；既自送元宝上门，而人亦视之为楮镪也。
    他日，伛偻田所，见秀颖连阡，曾辍耕之，几时他人将饱其实，不觉咨嗟太息。锄禾者，驼旧佃客也。相与语，因谈及为讼某者即某甲，以此数十亩故。不然，无妄之灾何因而至前耶？佃原委甚悉，驼愤然归，磨利刃出入挟之，思得之而甘心焉。

    Explanation

    97

    In Dongting Mountain, there was an old man named Jin, whose back was curved like a bow, but he had a sharp and quick mind. People often called him "Gold Ingot" because of his hunched back, which resembled a gold ingot. Whenever there were joyful occasions in the village, people would invite Jin to their homes, believing that his presence brought good luck. Jin was also skilled at flattering words, so people from far and near would come to seek his blessings. Over the years, Jin became prosperous, owning twenty acres of fertile land that was not affected by drought or flooding. The villagers called him "Rice Bin" because of his wealth.

    There was a wealthy and greedy man in the village who coveted Jin's land and tried to buy it, but Jin refused. The villagers had a saying: "The old man in the village is strange; the more valuable something is, the less likely he is to sell it." The greedy man was very angry and thought deeply about how to get the land. He eventually involved officials and had Jin taken to court, forcing him to sell his land. The greedy man bought the land for less than half its value.

    Jin became poor after this, and no one else came to ask for his blessings. People began to see him as worthless, like paper money. One day, Jin walked bent over to his field, where he saw a field of lush, well-grown crops. He remembered that he had once stopped farming, and now others would reap the benefits of his hard work. He sighed deeply with regret.

    A tenant farmer who had once worked for Jin approached him and began to talk. They discussed the lawsuit, and Jin learned that the greedy man had caused all this trouble because of the land. Jin, fully understanding the cause and effect, became angry. He sharpened a blade and carried it with him, determined to get revenge.

    98

    一日，侦知其饮于姻家，夜候道旁檐下。更余，驼忽转念曰：“贫，我命也。某谋产而得产，渠自昧心，我复舍命而杀人。我仍无产且亦丧命，何益之有？”遂掷刀于河，返走暗中，度石桥，忽闻人语曰：“这里是金元宝。”觉有人自驼后扳倒仆地。又似一人持二板至，遂置驼于板上，复以一板压之，缚自勒板，如榨油麻。
    驼本枉者，而使之直，是犹以桮棬为杞柳也。驼觉腰背悉为夹碎，痛急昏去，复苏，一无所有。反手腰背，大异于前。疾返叩门，妻见而讶之，曰：“汝何颀然而亭亭，橛然而矗矗也？”惊笑达比邻，共走视，果无复拳曲故态。远近传为异事。稍有周给之者，驼又小康。人问之，诡言得一秘方，而挟刀事密不言。

    98

    One day, he discovered that she was drinking at her relative's house, and he waited under the eaves by the roadside at night. After the first watch, the camel suddenly changed his mind and said, "Poverty is my fate. Someone schemed to acquire wealth and succeeded; he himself has lost his conscience. If I give up my life to kill someone, I will still have no wealth and also lose my life. What benefit would there be?" He then threw the knife into the river, returned to the darkness, crossed the stone bridge, and suddenly heard someone say, "This is a golden ingot." He felt someone from behind pull him down and fall to the ground. Then he saw another person holding two boards come. They placed the camel on the board and put another board on top of him, bound him tightly to the board, like pressing out oil from flax.

    The camel, originally crooked, was made straight, it is like turning a cup and basin into willow branches. The camel felt his waist and back completely crushed, the pain was so intense that he fainted, then regained consciousness, and nothing was left. Touching his waist and back, they were very different from before. He quickly returned and knocked on the door. His wife saw him and was surprised, saying, "How tall and upright you are, how straight and tall you stand?" The neighbors were surprised and laughed, they all went to see, indeed, no longer the same bent and twisted state. Near and far, it was passed on as a strange event. Those who helped him a little, the camel became moderately prosperous. When people asked him, he falsely said he had obtained a secret formula, but kept the matter of the knife secret.

    99

    数月，仇某甲忽至，馈遗殷勤。逾日又来，邀幸其家，初竣拒，而请之者益力，不得已。治具中堂，丰腆周洽。酒酣，又延之别馆，把臂捉膝而语。驼心疑之，夜深，欲别，甲曰：“自君蠲除痼疾，深自欣慰。仆不量，有恳于君，君其无吝教。”驼问所欲，甲跪曰：“鄙人年逾五十，只一子，七令。生而娟秀，前月嬉于灯下，足挂屏风而仆，遂如钩焉。其母日夜怜念，思所以疗之，非君神方不可。如肯援手，当奉百金为寿。”驼闻言仰天直视，默默不语。甲笑曰：“岂薄百金耶？不靳益也？”驼曰：“妄取人财，恐腰之再折耳！”不觉慨然叹息，涕泗交颐。甲怪，问，驼乃罄吐详悉。计掷刀桥头之日，正其子屏风得疾之夜。甲闻之憬然，继且痛哭，深以为悔。乃载驼之夫妇，养于家，归其米囤之田。其子遂瘳。

    Explanation

    99

    Several months later, Qiu Qimao suddenly appeared, offering gifts with great care. The next day, he came again and invited Camel to his home. Initially, Camel refused, but the requests became even more insistent, and he found himself unable to refuse. Qiu prepared a feast in the central hall, which was abundant and generous, with everything in order. After drinking to fullness, Qiu invited Camel to another building and they held arms and knees while talking. Camel felt suspicious, and as the night deepened, he wanted to leave. Qiu said, "Since you cured my chronic illness, I am deeply relieved. I do not measure my worth, but I have a request for you, please do not hesitate to teach me." Camel asked what he wanted, and Qiu knelt and said, "I am over fifty years old, and I have only one son, who is seven years old. He was born beautiful and delicate. Last month, while playing under the lamp, his foot got caught in the screen and he fell, and his leg became bent like a hook. His mother pities him day and night, thinking of ways to treat him, but nothing but your divine remedy will work. If you help, I will offer a hundred gold pieces as a gift." Upon hearing this, Camel looked up to the sky and remained silent. Qiu laughed and said, "Do you think a hundred gold pieces are too little? I will not hesitate to give more!" Camel replied, "To take someone else's money, I fear my back will break again!" Unconsciously, he sighed deeply and tears streamed down his face. Qiu was surprised and asked, and Camel then fully revealed the details. The day he threw the knife at the bridge was the same night his son was injured by the screen. Qiu was startled and then wept bitterly, deeply regretting his actions. He then took Camel and his wife into his home, raised them, and returned the rice granary fields. His son then recovered.

    100

    由是观之，损人利己之不可也。彼小人者，占人之物，诓以为己物；占人之财，骗为己财，谓非损在人而利在己欤。以此家室丰腴，安享其亨，岂能久乎？藉曰能之，而人之因是贫乏，我其坦然而对之乎？吾恐屏间颠仆，有不旋踵而至者矣。

    Explanation

    100

    From this perspective, it is clear that harming others for one's own benefit is unacceptable. Those petty people take others' possessions and deceive themselves into thinking they are their own; they take others' wealth and deceive themselves into thinking it is their own. Is it not that harm is done to others while benefit accrues to oneself? Thus, a household that is prosperous and well-off, enjoying comfort and prosperity, how could it last long? Even if one could do it, and people become impoverished because of this, how could I face them calmly? I fear that disaster will strike swiftly and unexpectedly.

    101

    (此文笔亦简淡。)

    孙元昌
    孙元昌，字大山，益都人。刚直果毅。与人洞达而隐回，至其意之所是，则断辞一迹，虽贲育不能夺也。读书好深湛之思，刻文切理，不喜滑泽枝叶。久于庠序，屡进不偶。终不易其所学，论难衎衎，确如也。壮年论事，慷慨激发，无所施试。年未五十，婚嫁粗毕。遂闭门却扫，渐疏外事，门前种柳，堂后刈葵，署其门曰：“辟俗理肱枕，隐心问药笼。”有贫贱交。
    一日，豪富车马过存，将入门，一闻其声，即飘然逾垣引去。终不复接对。其愤时迕俗，皆此类也。性好综详，临事必先立矩度。即断竹败瓦，处之必安其据，用之必当其才。晚营孝水之滨，俯仰静观。穷年兀对，倦则策杖独寻，从容信步，山边林下，邂逅忘机，辄为盘桓。

    Explanation

    101

    This writing is also simple and understated.

    Sun Yuanchang
    Sun Yuanchang, courtesy name Dashan, was from Yidu. He was upright, resolute, and decisive. He was open and sincere with people but also subtle and indirect. When it came to what he believed was right, he made a firm decision and followed through, even if the strongest and bravest could not change his mind. He enjoyed deep and profound contemplation while reading, and his writing was precise and insightful. He did not like superficial or ornate writing. He had long-standing service in schools and academies, but his repeated attempts were unsuccessful. He never changed his approach to learning, and his debates were engaging and convincing. In his prime, he argued passionately and inspired others, but he had no opportunity to put his ideas into practice. Before the age of fifty, he had completed his marriages and family affairs. He then closed his door, swept the floor, and gradually distanced himself from external matters. He planted willows in front of the door and harvested calabashes behind the hall. He inscribed on his door: "Shun the俗, rest your arm on the pillow; hide your heart, ask the medicine cabinet." He had friends from humble backgrounds.
    One day, wealthy and powerful people came to visit. As soon as he heard their voices, he would suddenly leap over the wall and leave. He never resumed communication with them. His anger at the times and his disregard for俗were all like this. He had a nature that liked to be thorough and detailed. When faced with matters, he would first establish rules and standards. Even when dealing with broken bamboo and tiles, he would ensure they were placed properly and used according to their capabilities. In his later years, he settled by the banks of the Xiang River, observing calmly, looking up and down. He spent the whole year in quiet contemplation. When tired, he would take a staff and walk alone, strolling leisurely. He would meet people by chance in the mountains and forests, forgetting all worldly concerns, and would linger there.

    102

    移日，儿辈念其劳，间以仆马追随，却不御，怅然独返。亦其素怀微尚然也。孤情自照而隐不违亲，矫时砺俗而动不惊众。年七十有三。生平未尝一衣帛乘马。临病笃，尚自点检余稂，代诸弟偿负，亦未尝挂一人钱。有四子，以长子廷铨，官封光禄大夫。

    张民感
    张民感，安邱人。少孤，为诸生，不屑事章句。尝曰：“情非捧檄，礼岂翘弓，何数数于禄为？”因静以修身，俭以养德，乡党共推长者。中岁无子，妻王氏为购一妾。入门，见其泪痕盈颊，哀苦动人。问，知为名家女。立遣去，不索直。女谢归，面使者曰：“愿祝张公三子成名。”后果三子，孝廉嗣伦，明经继伦，侍御绪伦，遂符其言。

    Explanation

    102

    For several days, his children remembered his labor and occasionally followed him with servants and horses, but he did not accept them, and returned sadly alone. This also reflected his usual modest aspirations. He lived alone and reflected on himself, yet did not abandon his family; he corrected the times and refined customs, but his actions did not startle the people. He was seventy-three years old. In his lifetime, he never wore silk or rode a horse. When he was gravely ill, he still checked his remaining grain and paid off his younger brothers' debts, and he never owed anyone a penny. He had four sons, with his eldest son Tingquan, who was granted the title of Guanglu Daifu.

    Zhang Minggan
    Zhang Minggan was from Anqiu. He lost his father at a young age and became a scholar, disdaining to study the details of classical texts. He once said, "If one's heart is not moved by a summons, how can one bow to a bow? Why bother with official salary so much?" Therefore, he cultivated himself through quietude and frugality, and the local community unanimously respected him as a virtuous elder. In middle age, he had no son, and his wife Wang bought a concubine for him. When she entered the house, she saw tears streaming down her face, her sorrow and grief touching. When asked, she learned that she was a daughter from a distinguished family. Zhang immediately sent her away without demanding payment. The woman thanked him and returned, speaking to the messenger face-to-face: "I wish that Mr. Zhang would have three sons who would achieve fame." Indeed, he later had three sons: Xianlian Sili, Mingjing Jilun, and Shiyu Xulun, thus fulfilling her words.

    103

    先是，公病革，诸子幼，乃呼其从子孝廉书绅至。屏人，出橐千金付之曰：“嬛嬛藐诸孤，岂能守？此付汝，待其长。可予则予之，如不可予，汝其自享之，毋以多金累也。”书绅唯唯，诸季长，悉以原橐归焉。闻者称公诚能格人，智足庇后。

    小李儿
    从来男子宜室，女子宜家，婚姻之事，自古皆然。闻此事者，不必尽为媒之正。当巧言以讽其成，或微言以劝其成。往往有一种天性残忍之人，不但不为撮合，且为之拆破者。如当夫妻反目，偶尔生离，年岁凶荒，甘心死别。因造无稽之言，设断情之语，观其镜破鸾分，以为快意，何所利而为之耶？
    昔有德州小李儿，初为人运船，偶一商登岸，遗金十笏，李得之，船主许妻以女。阅数日，商追至，值船主他往，李慨然悉还之。船主有戚某，乘间破之，曰：“此儿薄福，一钩金且不能承受，况欲得妻乎？终必饿死。”船主感其言，遂逐李。

    Explanation

    103

    Before his death, a public figure, gravely ill and with his young sons, called upon his younger brother, a man of virtue named Shushen, who had passed the local recommendation system. In private, he handed over a bag of a thousand gold, saying, "These frail and insignificant orphans cannot manage this wealth. I entrust it to you, to be given to them when they grow up. If they are worthy, give it to them; if not, enjoy it yourself, but do not burden yourself with too much wealth." Shushen agreed and, when the children grew older, returned the money in its original form. Those who heard of this praised the public figure as truly able to influence people, wise enough to protect the future.

    As for Xia Li'er, from ancient times, men are suited for marriage, and women for a proper household; marriage has always been so. Those who hear of such matters do not necessarily need to be matchmakers. They should use clever words to encourage its completion or subtle words to persuade its completion. Often, there are people with a naturally cruel disposition who do not only fail to bring people together but actively break them apart. When a husband and wife quarrel, they may part ways occasionally; during years of famine and hardship, they may willingly accept a deathlike separation. They fabricate baseless rumors and use words that sever emotional ties, watching as the mirror shatters and the phoenixes separate, finding it pleasing. What benefit do they gain by doing so?

    Once, there was a Xia Li'er from Dezhou who, when he first worked as a boatman, encountered a merchant who left a pouch of ten gold pieces on the shore. Xia Li'er found it and returned it to the merchant, who then promised to give his daughter in marriage. After several days, the merchant returned, but the shipowner was elsewhere, so Xia Li'er generously returned the money. The shipowner's relative, taking the opportunity, said, "This boy has little fortune; he cannot even bear a single hook of gold, so how could he possibly marry? He will eventually starve to death." The shipowner was moved by these words and expelled Xia Li'er.

    104

    李去，是日浴桥下，有物碍足，摸之，银也。悉取之，可数百金，用以市贩。遇前失银之商，教以脱货，利倍息。船主闻其富，乃以女归之，乃逐其戚。此天之报施善人岂爽哉！彼破人之婚者，曷利焉！

    张二棱
    张姓，行二，济上人。性凶悍，故以棱名，书法也。为州小捕，乡人怖之。值岁奇荒，人相食，流亡遍野，民不聊生。而张乃安享丰裕，自鸣其得意。
    张尝在道旁俟往来行车，有推载小男女四五人者，知其为贩，截路而呼曰：“何处私来人口，敢从官道扬鞭耶？随我官廨报验。”方出境，贩者恐，贿之如所愿，乃释。垂毙乞儿载满道路，张掖之，投乡中大户家。无何乞死，张必诈索，尽致方舁去。又或至乡中，与大户无故口角，或以石自破其颅，血横渍，得金以供十日醉。

    Explanation

    104

    Li departed, and on that day he bathed under the bridge. He felt something obstructing his feet, and upon touching it, he found it to be silver. He took all of it, amounting to several hundred gold coins, and used it for trading. He met a merchant who had lost the silver and taught him how to sell his goods, earning double the interest. The boat owner, hearing of his wealth, gave his daughter to him in marriage and thus distanced himself from his relatives. Is this not the way the heavens reward good people? As for those who break others' marriages, what benefit do they gain?

    Zhang Er Ling
    Zhang, the second son of his family, was from Jishang. He was fierce and brutal, hence the name "Ling," which is a calligraphic term. He served as a small-town constable, and the villagers feared him. During a year of severe famine, people ate each other, and refugees were everywhere, the people could not live. But Zhang enjoyed abundant wealth and boasted of his success.

    Zhang once waited by the roadside for passing carriages. He saw a group pushing a cart with four or five children, knowing they were peddlers, he blocked the road and shouted, "Where did these private individuals come from, daring to whip their horses on the official road? Follow me to the official office for verification." Just as they were leaving the city, the peddlers were afraid, bribed him as he wished, and he released them. Dying beggars filled the roads, Zhang blocked them, and sent them to the homes of wealthy families in the village. Soon the beggars died, Zhang must have extorted money, and they were all taken away. Sometimes he would go to the village and argue with the wealthy families without cause, or hit his head with a stone, causing blood to stain, and he would get money to drink for ten days.

    105

    城中有张姓商人，张思得其钞，觅一妓候之城隅。俟商过，妓肩挤之而喊。张诬商白昼戏良家妇，绁之当官，用数百缗赎免，以所获半入官衙，所以官知不治，反倚为鹯，且任其蠹也。前村有乡甲，买一妾，张知其为远来逃亡者。携其夫往，初念无非索几缗以为快，遂排而入曰：“尔何恃，娶活汉妻耶！”其妻闻之出，与其夫抱头哭，甚惨。张悯之，纵其夫妇。甲不敢声，复解囊令其圆聚而去。张乃醉饱于乡甲之家，以防其袭。乡甲固畏其悍，莫之何。尝剥牛卖诸市，识者不敢指证其局。吓乡愚等事，张谓之为“配药”；而破颅舁尸等事，张谓之为“打锅”。皆实录也。

    Explanation

    105

    In the city, there was a merchant surnamed Zhang. Zhang thought of obtaining the money and sought out a prostitute to wait at the city corner. Waiting for the merchant to pass, the prostitute pushed him and cried out. Zhang accused the merchant of playing with a virtuous woman during the day, had him bound and taken to the authorities, paid several hundred strings of cash to be released, and half of what he gained went into the government office. Therefore, the officials knew not to punish him and instead relied on him as a hawk, and allowed him to be a pest. In a nearby village, there was a man named Xiang who had bought a concubine. Zhang knew that she was a runaway from afar. He took the husband with him, at first, he thought no more than to demand a few strings of cash for fun, and then pushed his way in and said, "What do you rely on, marrying a living man's wife?" The wife heard this and came out, crying with her husband, which was very sad. Zhang pitied them and let the couple go. The man dared not speak, and then took out his purse and let them reunite and leave. Zhang then got drunk and full at the man's house to prevent an attack. The man in the village was naturally afraid of Zhang's ferocity and could do nothing. Once, he skinned a cow and sold it in the market, and those who recognized it dared not point out the scam. Scaring the villagers and other such things, Zhang called them "matching medicine"; and breaking skulls and carrying bodies, Zhang called them "beating the pot." All of these are true accounts.

    106

    一日午醉，休后园柳树下，忽二皂衣至，腰间出铁索，套其项。张曰：“二位何事？我即有罪，曷缓此小青龙，为我留一线光。狐兔相怜，何太逼耶？”二皂曰：“吾非阳世役隶，尔恶贯满盈，冥府察之，来勾尔魂。尚梦梦作呓何为？”张自思：“我出入衙门数十年间，不怯官长，撞成把势，岂冥地阴曹便打不开去？况阴阳并无二理，吾将试之。”曰：“去固易易，但二位远来，曷少作浆水以劳困乏，可乎？”二皂许之。张入厨，先取灶灰，于前后门铺散满地，复持长鞭而入，曰：“何物鬼魅，敢来恐吓老张！”遂挥鞭按迹而捶。二皂号啕万状，夺门，不敢履灰上，从窗隙中逸走，如人狼狈鼠窜去。张计得，嗣后尝以灰围其寝所。

    Explanation

    106

    One day, after drinking to intoxication, Zhang rested under a willow tree in the rear garden. Suddenly, two individuals dressed in black approached, drawing an iron chain from their waists and placing it around his neck. Zhang said, "What business do you have with me? If I have committed a crime, why not show some leniency towards this small green dragon and leave me a thread of light? How cruel are you, fox and rabbit alike?" The two individuals replied, "We are not servants of the earthly realm. Your evil deeds are complete, and the underworld has summoned your soul. What are you still babbling about?" Zhang thought to himself, "I have spent decades in government offices, unafraid of officials, and have become a tough guy. Can the underworld truly overpower me? Moreover, the principles of the earthly and spiritual realms are the same. I shall test this." He said, "Leaving is indeed easy, but you have come from afar. Why not prepare some drink or food to alleviate your fatigue?" The two individuals agreed. Zhang went into the kitchen, first taking some ash from the stove, and spread it around the front and back doors. Then, holding a long whip, he entered and said, "What kind of ghosts dare to frighten old Zhang!" He swung the whip, striking the trail they had left. The two individuals wailed in agony, fleeing through the door, unwilling to step on the ash, and escaped through the window, like a panicked rat. Zhang, satisfied with his plan, subsequently often surrounded his sleeping area with ash as a precaution.

    107

    越数日，如厕，一昂首，见马面者捉之，竟去。张欲言不得，至官庙，见南面怒容狰狞像，颇不似世间笑面官。曰：“汝即拒捕者？罪恶累累，不自悛改，害人横暴，合置油铛。”南面笔判油单百斤，镬焉。众鬼牵至铛前，焰烈，鬼担油入。张曰：“诸位，一言奉赠，镬一人，奚事百斤油？半用之，余者诸公携归，可以代膏灯半月。”众喜，张又曰：“相煎略缓，假我一见阎君，返即就死，甘心也。”众以其减油，牵之堂下。王曰：“复有何言？”张曰：“油镬二棱，定以百斤，贵爪牙私吞其半。四体肥，入鼎不完其肤，乞赐灭顶之凶，较甚涸辙之苦，感德无既。

    Explanation

    107

    After several days, Zhang went to the toilet and, upon lifting his head, saw a horse-faced person who caught him and finally left. Zhang wanted to speak but could not. He arrived at the temple of the official and saw a furious and ferocious-looking image facing south, quite unlike the smiling-faced officials of the world. The image said, "Are you the one who resisted arrest? You are laden with countless sins, not repenting on your own, causing harm and being violent; you should be placed in the oil cauldron." The southern-facing official wrote a decree for one hundred catties of oil, and the cauldron was prepared. The ghosts led him to the front of the cauldron, where the flames were fierce, and the ghosts carried the oil in. Zhang said, "Ladies and gentlemen, a word of advice: to fry one person, why so much oil? Use half of it, and the rest, gentlemen, take back with you, which can light a lamp for half a month." The ghosts were pleased, and Zhang added, "Let the roasting be a bit slower. Let me see the Lord of Hell, and I will return to die, content with my fate." The ghosts, because of the reduced amount of oil, led him downstairs. The king said, "What more do you have to say?" Zhang replied, "The oil cauldron has two sides and was determined to use one hundred catties, but your henchmen privately consumed half of it. Your four limbs are fat, and entering the cauldron will not fully burn your skin. I beg for the punishment of being drowned, which is more severe than the suffering of a dried-up ditch. I will be forever grateful."

    108

    ”王大怒，众鬼慑然，令以蒺藜挝其鬼卒，流血满庭。一判稽簿进曰：“此人尚有两善，合不当休。所以哓哓于鼎镬间也。”王阅薄，稍霁，点首曰：“囚固狡狯，亦挝四十，始放还阳。”众按之阶下，捶楚交加。张固常受杖，鬼力尽而张亦不甚惫。杖毕，数十鬼呵逐之。张曰：“何所见而拘谙幽？何所见而还诸阳？望明示我。”判乃指簿示云：“张某生平无一淫行，为第一善；又于某年月日，救人夫妻完聚，亦一善事。有此二条，准上百恶。但当痛改前非，否则重愆俱罚也。”张亦骇异。出，众鬼拦之，索讨钱文。张曰：“我张二棱纵横一世，门中朋党，未有不拜下风者。一文钱真不费，尔等游魂饿鬼，亦敢手中讨生活乎？”众恐其嘶喊，任其去。

    Explanation

    108

    The king was furious, and the ghosts trembled in fear. He ordered them to strike the ghost soldiers with thorny plants, causing blood to fill the courtyard. One judge presented a record and said, "This person still has two good deeds, so he should not be punished. That is why he pleaded so desperately in the face of severe punishment." The king reviewed the record, his anger slightly subsiding, and nodded, saying, "The prisoner is indeed cunning. Strike him forty times as well, and then release him back to the living world." The ghosts restrained him at the stairs, and the beatings were relentless. Zhang, who was accustomed to being beaten, was not very tired despite the ghosts' exhaustion. After the beating, dozens of ghosts chased him away. Zhang asked, "Why am I being detained in this dark place? Why am I being sent back to the living world? Please explain clearly to me." The judge pointed to the record and said, "Zhang has never committed any immoral acts, which is the first good deed. On a specific date, he also helped a couple reunite, which is another good deed. These two good deeds balance out his many bad deeds. However, he must deeply repent his past wrongs, otherwise he will face severe punishment again." Zhang was also surprised and amazed. As he left, the ghosts blocked his way and demanded money. Zhang said, "I, Zhang Erle, have been powerful and influential in my lifetime. No one in my circle would not submit to me. A single coin is not worth the trouble, and you, wandering spirits and hungry ghosts, dare to beg for a living?" The ghosts were afraid of his shouting and let him go.

    109

    张苏时，而鸡已喔喔鸣矣。身热，两肘青肿，三十日痛苦不起床。张自此颇能改悔，誓行善事，以赎前愆。有人向张谈及往事，则如批其颊，赤頳不自容。后竟以寿终焉。

    薛清来
    薛清来，豫章人，明经，为江苏邑令。记三生事，前两世皆为女身。初生在浙秀水，为贫女。父业渔，尝药鱼鳝，不留孽，涸其沼。夜以火灼蛙蟹，后不能给。遂鬻女，甫六岁，为勾栏买去。十三称佳丽。里有巨室沈二官，为之梳拢，情好最密。女号锁二姑娘。尝遇胡僧，受采补术，挟以纵淫，一宵可敌十健男。城中有学舍，众子弟来饮女所，谑浪备极。众素知女能，欲困之。坐中倡连横之说者，杨生也，年老而倔。女解衣延敌，烛不移影，众皆披靡，鸟兽散。独杨生危坐不前，女招之，而杨已倒戈漂杵。女笑释之曰：“杨先生何兵气不扬也。”

    109

    During Zhang's tenure, the rooster had already crowed. Zhang was feverish, with his elbows swollen and bruised, and he suffered for thirty days, unable to get out of bed. From then on, Zhang was able to repent sincerely, swearing to do good deeds to atone for his past sins. When someone mentioned Zhang's past to him, he felt as if they had slapped his face, turning red with embarrassment. He eventually died of old age.

    Xue Qinglai
    Xue Qinglai was from Yuzhang, a scholar who had passed the imperial examination in Confucian classics, and served as the magistrate of a county in Jiangsu. He recorded his three lives, in which his two previous lives were that of a woman. She was born in Xiushui, Zhejiang, as a poor girl. Her father was a fisherman who used herbs to treat fish and eels, did not harm others, and dried up the pond. At night, he burned frogs and crabs with fire, but later could not provide enough. He then sold his daughter, who was just six years old, to a brothel. At thirteen, she was considered beautiful. In the village, there was a wealthy man named Shen Er Guan, who arranged for her to be introduced into the brothel, and they had the closest relationship. She was called Lock Er Girl. She once met a foreign monk and learned the technique of taking essence, which she used to indulge in lust, capable of matching the strength of ten strong men in one night. In the city, there was a school building where young men came to drink at her place, indulging in lewd and unrestrained behavior. They always knew her abilities and wanted to test her limits. Among them, the one who advocated the strategy of horizontal alliance was Yang Sheng, who was old but stubborn. She undressed and invited him to fight, and in the time it took for the candle to not move its shadow, all the others were defeated, scattering like birds and beasts. Only Yang Sheng sat upright and did not move forward. She invited him, but Yang had already fallen and was useless. She laughed and released him, saying, "Mr. Yang, why is your fighting spirit so weak?"

    110

    后女以荒淫，十九岁死。至冥司，王怒曰：“尔前生作县令，有秽政罚尔娼，偿厥罪愆。今又纵淫害人，将议加。”女曰：“王罚我为女，何不令我为妻为妾为婢，奈何令我为娼？是假我淫具，诲我以淫也。欲加之罪，不亦冤乎？”王沉思曰：“此前官原错断。今尔复作女，当为尼，守清规，忏悔己过。否则坠入种种恶道。”女叩头去。途中见一棚如茶肆，多人环向一池，执杓饮。有令女饮者，女嫌其浊，乃虚其杓，作饮状。
    去至一篱落，忽跌，已在蓐中，不敢声。一妇抱之起，用兜出弃诸野。盖私胎也。女冻冷，又惧，乃犬声呼，耳中仍作儿音。顷，人至曰：“阿弥陀佛。”怀之去。女审之，老尼也。中心了了，但口不能言，及长，名锁云。每忆前生，痛心忏悔。静中偶动，强自敛抑。惟沈二官来庵，颇怀旧雨，不能恝然，亦未说破。月下禅关，甘心孤寂而已。十八岁，晨起，沐浴更衣，无疾而逝。

    Explanation

    110

    After the woman died at the age of nineteen due to her debauchery, she arrived at the realm of the dead. The judge there was furious and said, "In your previous life, you served as a county magistrate and were punished for your corrupt governance by being forced into prostitution to atone for your sins. Now, you have indulged in lust and harmed others, and I will add to your punishment." The woman replied, "Why did you punish me by making me a prostitute? Why not make me a wife, concubine, or servant? Why must I be a prostitute? This is tantamount to giving me the tools for debauchery and teaching me to indulge in it. To add to my punishment, is that not unjust?" The judge pondered for a moment and said, "The previous judge made a mistake in judgment. Now that you have been reborn as a woman, you should become a nun, observe the pure precepts, and repent for your past mistakes. Otherwise, you will fall into various lower realms of existence." The woman bowed her head and left. On the way, she saw a pavilion that resembled a tea shop, where many people were gathered around a pool, holding ladles to drink. Someone ordered her to drink, but she found the water turbid and instead pretended to drink by holding an empty ladle.

    Later, she stumbled and fell into a bed of grass, too afraid to make a sound. A woman picked her up, carried her in a basket, and left her in the wilderness. She was an illegitimate child. The girl was cold and frightened, and she cried out in the voice of a dog, though her ears still sounded like a child's. Soon, someone approached and said, "Amitabha Buddha." The woman took her in and carried her away. The girl realized that the woman was an elderly nun. She understood everything in her heart, but she could not speak. When she grew up, she was named Lock Cloud. She often remembered her past life and deeply repented. In moments of stillness, she occasionally felt desires but forced herself to suppress them. Whenever Second Official Shen visited the temple, she felt a connection to him, but she could not help herself, and she did not reveal her feelings. Under the moonlight at the Zen gate, she willingly embraced solitude. At the age of eighteen, she bathed, changed her clothes, and passed away peacefully in the morning.

    111

    女飘飘出庵，如识故道。倏忽间，又至幽都。群鬼识之，曰：“锁姑娘，锁姑娘！”咸来相狎。女合掌宣佛，悉散去。及见冥王，嘉其悔过修行，许转男身，给青衣。女谢去，投生豫章薛家，即今生也。
    长聘同里沈氏，十六完娶。沈柔婉，事薛颇谨。薛固知其为沈转生也。后以廪贡，出为邑宰。在江苏诸邑，宦囊多盈余。好置姬妾，先后去留不计其数。凡置一人，价必廉，且多凑合。现在者，十余人，皆殊姿，善承迎。屋中设一大床，可半间，历十余级，每级卧一人。自卧于没阶。早起，众妾环侍，为之沃盥更衣履。凡餐，一妾为之置味一品。薛有未尝之羹，司庖者心向隅终日。薛虽安享其丰实，乃应接不暇。沈氏夫人本不妒，而众妾又相和处，可乐也。独薛以为是孽障缠绕，摆脱不开，总无一刻清净，空诸色界。

    Explanation

    111

    The woman floated out of the nunnery, as if she knew the old path. In the blink of an eye, she arrived at the underworld. The ghosts recognized her, saying, "Lock girl, Lock girl!" They all came to play with her. She bowed her hands and recited Buddhist prayers, and they all dispersed. When she saw the King of Hell, he praised her for repenting and practicing Buddhism, allowed her to be reborn as a man, and gave her a blue robe. She thanked him and departed, being reborn into the Xue family in Yuzhang, which is her current life.

    She was engaged to a local woman named Shen and married at the age of sixteen. Shen was gentle and diligent in serving Xue. Xue knew for sure that she was reborn as Shen. Later, he passed the imperial examination and became a county magistrate. In various counties in Jiangsu, his official coffers were often overflowing. He liked to have concubines, many of whom came and went, too numerous to count. Whenever he took one, the price was always low, and often combined. Currently, there were more than ten, all of them beautiful, and good at pleasing. In the room, there was a large bed that could fit half the room, with more than ten steps, each step occupied by one person. He lay at the bottom step. In the morning, the concubines surrounded him, serving him with water and changing his clothes. For every meal, one concubine prepared a dish. Xue had never tried the soup, and the cook felt sad all day. Although Xue enjoyed his wealth and abundance, he was overwhelmed by the constant attention. Shen's wife was not jealous, and the concubines also got along well, making it a pleasant situation. However, Xue felt that this was a burden of karma, from which he could not escape, and he never had a moment of peace, unable to empty the realm of desire.

    112

    或在锦瑟繁弦绣衾款语之时，不禁意趣索然。因得痪疾，告归，日剧。十余妾皆给妆资，遣之去，曰：“夫死无子之妾，不必守，不能守，且不可守。我死卿必去，卿留我亦死。与其离于死后，不若别于生前。卿等待我十余年，皆不知我为谁，故作此痴想打算。我固知卿为何者人，因何者事，以偿我，以报我，抑以累我者。今不去，将何为？”妾有誓不去者，薛必遣之，不一留。沈氏以为忍，薛笑曰：“不用留，不用留，我已归荒邱，留他正到头。半夜无人私听处，柳梢月上黄昏候。梦到春深先唤醒，黄莺打起认归舟。做鬼也风流，免得儿孙后日忧。”薛止一子，沈夫人出，亦邑庠生。
    (凡事太明白，皆无味。薛之前生了了，将一切夫妇子女，如稽簿欠，有何乐境？诚不若糊涂之为得也。)

    Explanation

    112

    Sometimes, during the intimate moments of playing the qin with its intricate strings and talking tenderly under a finely embroidered quilt, one cannot help but find it dull and uninteresting. Due to contracting paralysis, I resigned and returned home, and my condition worsened day by day. I provided dowries to my ten concubines and sent them away, saying, "A wife without a son has no obligation, ability, or reason to remain faithful. If I die, you will leave; if you stay, I will also die. It is better to part ways while we are still alive than to be separated after death. You have waited for me for over a decade, not knowing who I really am, so you have these foolish thoughts and plans. I know very well who you are, what you do, and whether you are here to repay me, to thank me, or to burden me. If you do not leave now, what will you do?" Those who swore they would not leave, Xue would definitely send them away, and would not keep them. Shen considered this as being cruel, but Xue laughed and said, "There is no need to keep her, there is no need to keep her, I have already returned to the grave. There is no need to keep her, there is no need to keep her, I have already returned to the grave. In the quiet, secluded place at dusk, the moon rises above the willow branches. In dreams, spring deepens and wakes me up first, the oriole sings and recognizes my returning boat. Even as a ghost, I will be romantic, sparing my descendants future worries." Xue had only one son, and Shen's wife, who came from the same village, was also a scholar.

    (All things that are too clear are tasteless. Xue, in his previous life, had everything clear and accounted for, treating all matters of marriage and children like a ledger of debts. What joy could there be in such a life? Indeed, it is better to be somewhat vague and ignorant.)

    113

    李湘
    甚矣，口生诟而口戕口。有吴慎修者，针工也，宁波人。妻袁氏，本苏宦之婢，即如苏人，面凹而口阔，身肥而足大，性荡佚。吴素不如所好。
    邻有回人马姓，伟而壮，屠羊为业。袁素倚门见之，喜其准高而力硕，以指示后，又掠裙跨步作态。马喜。屋后固有短垣，夜，马逾墙相从。且数，吴觉之，不敢发，诚以妻悍而马恶。
    吴有友李湘，好事而多言，且好雌黄人。一日，吴就李饮，将醉。吴忽垂首，咨嗟而涕洟。李问之。不答。固问，吴曰：“汝度人心事，试一猜之。”李曰：“汝不过意马而心猿。”吴愕然，既请受命。李笑曰：“是不难，闻汝妻悍，且凌汝，何不赠马，则马德汝，而妻不仇汝。”吴怪曰：“汝浑家何不赠之。”李曰：“我妇若此，刃之，如烹小鲜，岂似汝瓮中鳖缩缩然，使背高于首者！”吴曰：“我诚拼以命，何不可歼。但恐官方絷因耳。”李乃以指点吴曰：“汝好不惶愧，几曾见杀奸而抵者？且将邀厚赏焉。”

    Explanation

    113

    A Man with a Restless Mind

    How great is the power of words to give rise to slander and harm one another! There was a man named Wu Shenshou, a needleworker from Ningbo. His wife, surnamed Yuan, was originally a servant of a Suzhou official and thus had the manners of a Suzhou woman. She had a sunken face with a wide mouth, a plump body, and large feet, and she was lewd and licentious. Wu had never been fond of her.

    In their neighborhood, there was a Hui person named Ma, who was tall and strong, and whose profession was slaughtering sheep. Yuan often leaned on the door to look at him and was pleased by his height and strength. She would point to him and make suggestive gestures with her skirt, and Ma was attracted to her. There was a short wall at the back of their house, and at night, Ma climbed over the wall to meet her. Wu discovered this affair but did not dare to act, for he feared both his wife's temper and Ma's strength.

    Wu had a friend named Li Xiang, who was fond of gossip and often spoke ill of others. One day, Wu went to drink with Li, and they were about to get drunk. Suddenly, Wu lowered his head, sighed, and shed tears. Li asked him what was wrong, but Wu did not answer. Li persisted, and Wu said, "Guess what’s troubling my mind." Li replied, "You’re just restless, like a horse and a monkey in your mind." Wu was startled and asked for an explanation. Li laughed and said, "It’s not difficult. I hear your wife is fierce and often mistreats you. Why not give yourself to Ma? Then Ma would be grateful to you, and your wife wouldn’t hate you." Wu was surprised and asked, "Why didn’t your wife give herself to him?" Li said, "If my wife were like that, I would kill her as easily as cooking a small fish. How can you be like a turtle in a jar, with your back higher than your head?" Wu said, "I am willing to risk my life. What’s wrong with killing an adulterer? But I’m afraid the authorities might arrest me for it." Li pointed at Wu and said, "You’re not even ashamed. Have you ever seen someone killed for being an adulterer and rewarded for it? You’ll just get a big reward!"

    114

    吴归，告其妻有夜工，伪出，抉刀俟于墙隅。更深，袁氏掩扉而脱衣，马来入室，即与妇奸，立于床下。吴挺刃入，马执灯檠格之，刀落，马夺门走。吴拾刃杀其妇而函其首，诣李曰：“如命，将求赏于官。”示以首。李大惊曰：“马首安在？”吴曰：“马逸去。”李曰：“无马首，必不可。”吴曰：“汝使我杀妇，固未言马。无已，请以君首代。”遂欲杀李。李曰：“姑徐徐。今汝即杀我，不能移我尸于汝妇寝所。为汝计，莫若汝妇候于门，有过者，乘黑杀之，移尸而入室，方可以代。”吴释李，仓皇归。

    Explanation

    114

    Wu returned home and told his wife that he had night work, which was a lie. He secretly left and drew out a knife, waiting in the corner of the wall. As the night deepened, Lady Yuan closed the door and took off her clothes, when Ma came into the room and had sexual relations with her, standing under the bed. Wu thrust the knife in, but Ma held the lampstand to block it, causing the knife to fall. Ma then fled through the door. Wu picked up the knife and killed his wife, putting her head in a box, and went to Li, saying, "As ordered, I will seek a reward from the authorities." He showed Li the head. Li was greatly surprised and asked, "Where is Ma's head?" Wu replied, "Ma has escaped." Li said, "Without Ma's head, it cannot be done." Wu said, "You told me to kill my wife, but did not mention Ma. If there is no other way, please use your head instead." He then threatened to kill Li. Li said, "Let's take it slowly. If you kill me now, you cannot move my body to your wife's sleeping chamber. For your sake, why not have your wife wait at the door? When someone passes by, kill them in the dark, move their body into the room, and this way, it can be substituted." Wu released Li and hurried back home.

    115

    适一人暗中来，甫及门，吴促之入。其人慑栗不敢声，杀之。火而视，僧也。吴乃移尸扫迹，以二首鸣官，云其妻与僧奸宿，杀之当场。官抵吴所检焉。妇赤身而僧裹衣，于是解衣剥肤。仵者喝报曰：“衣者亦女也。”盖僧而尼。官大骇，鞠吴。吴不能讳，供以初谋于李，妻杀而马逸，继复谋于李，杀僧而化尼。
    官乃捕马至。马伏罪律，以和奸而酿命，戍焉。而吴以擅杀而故杀，抵焉。至于李，始也戏吴杀妻，而类同谋；继也诡吴杀尼，而甚加功，亦拟辟，谳遂定。

    Explanation

    115

    At the moment, a person came in the dark, just as they reached the door, Wu urged them inside. The person trembled with fear and dared not speak, so Wu killed them. They lit a fire to see, and it turned out to be a monk. Wu then moved the body and cleared the traces, and reported the incident to the authorities, saying that his wife had an affair with the monk and killed him on the spot. The official investigated what Wu reported. The woman was naked, but the monk was wrapped in clothes. They then undressed and peeled off the skin. The coroner shouted out, "The one in the clothes is also a woman." It turned out that the person was a nun disguised as a monk. The official was greatly shocked and interrogated Wu. Wu could not hide the truth and confessed that he had planned with Li at first, his wife killed, and the horse escaped. Then he planned again with Li, killed the monk, and transformed into a nun. The official then captured the horse. The horse confessed guilt according to the law, for consensual adultery that caused a crime, and was exiled. Wu was punished for unauthorized killing and intentional murder. As for Li, at first he played a trick on Wu to kill his wife, and was similar to a co-conspirator; then he tricked Wu to kill the nun, and did much more, and was also sentenced to death. The verdict was finalized.

    116

    嗟乎，李惟口之故，出好兴戒。

    徐国华
    扬州徐国华，虎而冠，以雄称，食鹾商俸。自仪征盐河至扬，多爬盐贼。徐得俸，则窃匪便不上某船，否则群集蹂躏，不可当，用是而富。匪徒皆赖之，尊若盟长，见者必卑词屈奉，稍有睚眦，则殴辱立至，并不用徐亲觌面，自然能以毒中之。
    生一子，不能继父业。徐每授之方略，则殊不了了。徐叹曰：“英雄豪杰，问世一生，甚矣，是父是子之难也。”
    其妾名二侉者，本山东道上娼户，为徐所强占，颇爱嬖。妻怼之，遂凌妻。徐病革，问其妾曰：“我死后，汝为我守乎？”妾乃以指竖鼻端曰：“俺这一朵花才半开，遂守空房耶？看你的行为，伸伸腿，大家都撒手。我不打诳语欺瞒死人。”徐哭曰：“枕边恩爱何顿忘耶？”妾曰：“三伏天，炎炎炙背，想你的好情儿。”冷笑而出。至晚，与一仆怀细软走矣。徐知之，愤急，气如牛喘，暴亡。

    Explanation

    116

    Alas, because of Li Wei Kou, good is promoted and evil is warned against.

    Xu Guohua
    Xu Guohua of Yangzhou, a tiger in a hat, was known for his ferocity and lived off the wealth of salt merchants. From the salt river of Yizheng to Yangzhou, there were many salt thieves. When Xu received his salary, thieves would avoid his ship, but when he didn't, they would gather and cause trouble, making it impossible to resist. Thus, he became wealthy. The thieves all relied on him, respecting him as a leader. Those who met him had to bow and show deference. If there was any slight provocation, they would be beaten and humiliated immediately, without Xu having to see them in person, as he could harm them from afar.

    He had a son who could not continue his father's business. Whenever Xu tried to teach him strategies, the son could not understand. Xu sighed, "How difficult it is to pass on the legacy of a hero and a mighty man to a son!"

    His concubine, named Erkua, was originally a prostitute from Shandong, who was forcibly taken by Xu. She was greatly favored by him. His wife resented her and treated her poorly. When Xu was gravely ill, he asked his concubine, "After I die, will you stay with me?" She raised a finger to her nose and said, "My flower is only half-bloomed, and I will not stay in an empty house? Watch your behavior. When you stretch your legs, everyone will let go. I do not tell lies to deceive the dead." Xu cried, "How can you suddenly forget our pillowside affection?" She replied, "On the hottest days of summer, the sun scorches my back, and I think of your good feelings." She laughed coldly and left. By evening, she and a servant left with valuable items. When Xu found out, he was furious and died suddenly.

    117

    当徐气绝时，徐之子尚在某家豪赌云。且其子又愚，不知生理，尝为人所市弄而鱼肉之。是昔父之所取而施诸人者，竟今子之所受而还诸已。年余，有宿迁人至，谓其子曰：“宿某家，产一豕，身有白毛成字，作‘徐国华’，非汝尊者名乎？”与其子往宿，果见豕，如所云。抱豕痛哭，若见所生，乃欲售之。其家曰：“徐我仇也。生前曾诈我二百金，今天罚以假手于我，将碎脔以雪愤，奚售为。”于是往来关视，终以二百金赎之，圈而归，敬以豢之，别犬马之养。后豕肥腯，毛尽脱，浑变黑，字迹全无。始知宿迁某以术弄也。彼盖素悉其父之恶，而又知其子之愚，以火烙豕身，掺药而字，使白毛焉。夫而后招摇于市，使之闻之，复假一叶之舟，偕来审视。玩徐子于股掌之上，计亦巧矣。

    Explanation

    117

    When Xu's breath ceased, his son was still gambling at someone's house. Moreover, his son was foolish and did not know the way of life, often being treated like a commodity and butchered. What the father took from others and inflicted on them, his son now receives and returns to himself. After a year or more, a person from Suqian arrived and told his son, "At a certain family in Suqian, they have given birth to a pig. Its body has white hair forming characters, spelling out 'Xu Guohua.' Isn't that the name of your father?" Along with his son, they went to Suqian and indeed saw the pig, just as described. They hugged the pig and cried bitterly, as if seeing their own offspring, and then wanted to sell it. The family said, "Xu is my enemy. Before he died, he cheated me out of two hundred gold pieces. Today, heaven is punishing him by giving me the opportunity. I will cut him into pieces to vent my anger, why sell him?" They then came and went, watching over it, and finally redeemed it for two hundred gold pieces. They put it in a pen and returned home, treating it with respect and feeding it well, different from how they treated dogs and horses. Later, the pig became fat and its white hair fell out, turning completely black, and the characters disappeared. It was then known that the person from Suqian had used magic to do this. He must have known about Xu's father's evil deeds and also knew that Xu's son was foolish. He burned the pig's body with fire, mixed in medicine to make the characters, causing the white hair to grow. Then he flaunted it in the market, letting people hear about it, and pretended to come in a small boat to inspect it. He played with Xu's son at his fingertips, the plan was also clever.

    118

    噫，徐即非是豕，要必为豕以偿人。观其正罪输金，冥冥中岂漫然乎？
    (近日卖骡马者，尝作伪色，即此掺药否？何官常乌须之难耶？)

    大算盘
    单有益，宛平人。重利放债，算析秋毫。凡有远者铨选，借伊银钱，甚至三扣，人号为“单算盘”。与之交者，无不吃亏。见人一器一物，亦设计获职，因而家遂丰。起盖房廊，陈设玩好，居然豪富。家有一妻四妾三子一女，而且婢仆舆马无不如意。

    Explanation

    118

    Ah, if Xu is not a pig, then he must be a pig to compensate others. Observe his formal punishment of paying a fine; in the unseen realm, is it done carelessly?

    (Recently, those who sell mules and horses have been altering their appearance; is this the same as adding poison? Why is it so difficult for officials to dye their mustaches black?)

    The Big Abacus

    Shan Yiyi, from Wanping, was known for lending money at high interest rates and calculating with great precision. Whenever someone far away needed to be selected for a position and borrowed money from him, they often had to pay back triple the amount, earning him the nickname "Shan Abacus." Those who dealt with him always ended up losing. He would also devise ways to acquire things he saw, leading to his family becoming prosperous. He built houses and arranged furnishings, becoming a wealthy and powerful figure. His household included one wife, four concubines, three sons, and one daughter, and he had no shortage of servants, carriages, and horses, all of which met his every need.

    119

    一日，单于庭前睡，午见一青衣舁一大算盘至，庋桌上，两头宽尺余。盘中算子大于梨桉，横枨上并无百十分两字样，皆号妻妾子女房产地土之类。其人对单曰：“尔剥众小财为一人大财，则削众小家成一大家。今以总算扣你零算，以恶算罚尔刻算也。”于是手推指挪，满盘皆动，既而一一打去，止有“女”字上，一子尚存。其人以手捏子曰：“即去此，亦不足偿，曷留之？适所以偿也。”乃举盘令单视，单忽醒。由是病疫，家尽死亡。又遭回禄，产业荡然。剩一女，遂流为娼，而单亦至于丐云。

    三生赘
    丹徒张映薇，游于越。同舟有王姓者，越人也。通款洽，颇相投契。而王之左手，尝以帛缠，捉之袖中，不见其肘。终吴越之路，虽欲握手道欢，皆虚其左。张异之，问曰：“足下袖手而旁观，见疑也？何不直臂请拳，使我瞭如指掌。指头禅好教人难猜也。”王曰：“倘我如出一手，何妨把臂相示。诚以指不若人，则知恶之。”遂脱襟相示，盖人腕而豕蹄。

    Explanation

    119

    Translation

    One day, the Chanyu slept before the court. At noon, he saw a man in a blue robe carrying a large abacus to the court, placing it on the table. The abacus was over a foot wide at both ends. The beads were larger than pears, and there were no marks for hundredths or tenths on the crossbar. All the beads were labeled with the names of wives, concubines, children, property, land, and so forth. The man said to Dan, "You strip many small fortunes into one great fortune, then reduce many small households into one large household. Now use the total to offset your individual accounts, and use evil calculations to punish your cruel calculations." He then pushed and moved the beads with his fingers, and all the beads moved. Then, one by one, he removed them, leaving only one bead on the 'woman' character. The man picked up the bead and said, "Even if removed, it is still insufficient to compensate. Why not leave it? It is just to compensate." He then lifted the abacus and showed it to Dan, who suddenly woke up. Thereafter, Dan fell ill with an epidemic, and the entire family died. Then he suffered from fire, and his property was completely destroyed. Only one daughter remained, and she became a prostitute, while Dan also became a beggar.

    Three Lives of a Ghost

    Dan Mu Zhang Yingwei traveled to Yue. On the same boat was a man surnamed Wang, a native of Yue. They got along well and became quite close. However, Wang's left hand was often wrapped in cloth and hidden in his sleeve, and his elbow was not visible. Throughout the journey from Wu to Yue, even though they wanted to shake hands and share joy, Wang kept his left hand hidden. Zhang found this strange and asked, "Are you hiding your hand and watching from the side, making me suspicious? Why not extend your arm and show me your fist, so I can understand clearly. The finger Zen is good at making people guess." Wang replied, "If I were to extend my hand, what harm would it do to show my arm? Truly, if my fingers are not as good as others, I know it is not good." He then took off his robe to show Zhang, revealing a human wrist with pig hooves.

    120

    张惊怪，王曰：“坐。我明告子：此三生孽报，犹未脱然也。前再生为邳州役隶。有同村霍姓，欠粮，捕甚急，曾揭备银拾两，托余代为完纳。余侵蠹之，不为给完。逾年，催旧欠，羁霍去，备楮栳掠至死，诉于冥司。寻勾余至阴曹，对质，实我所侵。冥王怒，谓椽曰：‘与其阴惨以刑，不如阳受以报。’遂笔判一狴牌，絷我至一处，阴霾无光，隐隐一石，圈门如城圈。铁扇有守者，见牌发钥，门开则湿热之气隆隆蒸起，背后一推，两耳闻啼豕声，即落一娄猪腹中。自觉在其腹内，辘辘不得舒展，且膨闷。排挤有日，砉然委地，乃见身在笠中，与诸小豭呶呶，始悟人化为豕。恨不食乳，馁甚，有人以水拌粒饲我，匍匐往食；又善饥，如是日厌糖粃，数月而硕大无朋矣。

    Explanation

    120

    Zhang was startled and questioned, and Wang said, "Sit down. I will clearly inform you: this is the karmic retribution of three lifetimes, and I have not yet escaped it. In my previous life, I was reborn as a low-ranking official in Pizhou. There was a man from the same village named Huo who owed grain, and the capture was very urgent.
