He posted a guarantee of ten taels of silver and entrusted me to complete the payment on his behalf. I embezzled the money and did not fulfill the payment. After a year, when they came to collect the old debt, they detained Huo and took him away, beating him to death with a bundle of straw. He appealed to the underworld officials. Soon, I was summoned to the underworld, where we confronted and verified the matter, and it was indeed my embezzlement. The underworld king was angry and said to the magistrate, 'Rather than punishing in the darkness with harsh penalties, it is better to receive retribution in the light.' He then wrote a judgment and issued a prison card, binding me to a place that was dark and gloomy with no light, with a gate like a city wall. There were guards with iron fans who saw the card and opened the lock. When the door opened, a damp and hot vapor rose, and I was pushed from behind. I heard the squealing of pigs and fell into a pig's belly. I felt inside the pig's belly, unable to stretch out comfortably and feeling bloated and suffocated. After being pushed and squeezed for some time, I suddenly fell to the ground and saw myself in a bamboo hat, with other small male pigs making noise. I finally realized that a person had transformed into a pig. I hated not being able to drink milk and was very hungry. Someone fed me with water mixed with grains, and I crawled to eat. I was prone to hunger, and after several months, I grew accustomed to sweet grains and became large and unmatched."

    121

    尝触篱，见园中多苦瓜甕菜，始知为豫章地。既乃肥腯好睡，而懒腹垂在地。当暑热，无可为法，于水塘涸厕伏滚一大泥窝，稍觉凉爽。一日，有一人绳我至案上，其貌酷类霍姓者，出屠刀，篦诸石上，铮铮然。吾第知一刀之惨有不能免，孰知江西人每生剥豕皮以蒙钲鼓。屠乃自我颔下以刀中裂分许，直至尾闾，痛如火线一条。又以铁挝分剥，自腹及脊，以及于臀，如脱裹衣。其疼苦初在皮裹膜外，继即万镝攒心。所最难忍者，至蹄足如沸扬一滚，姑徐徐褪落耳。至第三只，皮断身坠，而心气遂绝。又见冥司王者曰：‘霍负既偿，若挥之去。’旁一鬣须者，引入一圭窦，不觉落地，呱呱而泣。自幸复为人身，迄于今，一豚蹄犹不敢交于右手。呜呼！我负我友，实有豕心，而况于手，故缠之不可以示人。”

    沈肯堂构堂录
    沈肯堂、构堂，兄弟也。幼不率教，长不循礼，略识之无，遂至不安恒业，而机心生焉。一为医，一为幕，彼两人未尝无苟合之时。

    Explanation

    121

    Once I touched the fence, I saw many bitter melon and water spinach in the garden, and then realized I was in the land of Yuzhang. I was fat and plump, loving to sleep, with my lazy belly hanging on the ground. During the hot summer, there was no way to cool off, so I rolled around in a large mud nest by the dry pond and toilet, feeling slightly cool. One day, a man tied me to a table, his appearance very similar to someone named Huo. He took out the slaughter knife and sharpened it on the stone, making a ringing sound. I only knew that the pain of one cut was unavoidable, but who knew that people in Jiangxi often peeled the pigskin alive to cover drums and gongs. The butcher made a cut from under my chin to the tail, the pain like a burning line. He then used an iron tool to peel the skin from my belly to my back and buttocks, as if removing a wrapped garment. The initial pain was on the outer layer of skin, but soon it felt like thousands of arrows piercing my heart. The hardest part was when he removed my legs, like boiling water rolling over, slowly and carefully. By the third leg, the skin was torn off, and I fell, my life force leaving me. I then saw the king of the underworld say, "The debt to Huo has been repaid, so dismiss him." A bearded man beside him led me into a small opening, and without realizing, I fell to the ground, crying "gua gua." Feeling fortunate to be human again, up to now, I dare not use my right hand to touch a pig's hoof. Alas! I betrayed my friend, truly having the heart of a pig, and even more so my hands, so they are bound and cannot be shown to others.

    Shen Kenting and Shen Guting were brothers. They were unruly as children and did not follow the rules as adults, barely knowing anything, so they could not settle into a stable career, and cunning thoughts arose in them. One became a doctor, and the other became a clerk, but they were not without moments of opportunism.

    122

    肯堂始轫药肆，悬壶都市，秋蜂之房，枯鱼之牙，以及宿草败皮，堆满瓶盎。间设一二方书，临时剽窃。偶有所得，秘不传人。
    一少年项间偶患热节疮，来求肯视。肯见其衣服华好，吓之为疽，重其售，许以三十金。肯阴以毒置油膏中。敷之，一夜而肿紫。患者呼号达旦，急舆请沈。辞之，赴宦家酒，更阑不至。乃以百金为寿，方为之解此痛厄，犹自啧啧为良国手。时盛夏，邻人贫者有阴症，其子踵门跽请。肯醉中往视，略一诊切，曰：“此中暑也，宜用香薷饮。”服后气将脱，始惶恐，急以八味附子投之，乃苏。继连服十剂，瘳。又尝取荠苨蒸晒，充作人参；桂皮以胡桃浸刷，假号清花。并合宫方，纵人淫恶，夺人寿箕。由此利倍起家，而其术终不精，往往误。症疑，难下手，后乃专用平药数味，创为两歧之论，以待病者之自痊，作藏拙计，甚得也。

    Explanation

    122

    Dr. Ken began his medical practice in the city, opening a pharmacy and hanging his medicine bag as a sign. His jars and containers were filled with worthless items such as honeycombs of autumn bees, teeth of dried fish, and old grass and rotten skin. Occasionally, he would set up a few medical texts and copy them without attribution. Whenever he made a discovery, he kept it secret and did not share it with others.

    One day, a young man came to Dr. Ken with a boil on his neck. Seeing the young man's fine clothes, Dr. Ken scared him into thinking it was a more serious condition (carbuncle) and increased the price, asking for thirty gold coins. Dr. Ken secretly put poison in the ointment. After applying it, the patient's neck swelled and turned purple within a night. The patient cried out all night, and his family carried him to ask for help from another doctor. The other doctor refused and went to a wealthy family's banquet instead, staying late and not arriving. The patient's family then offered a hundred gold coins as a gift, and the other doctor finally agreed to help, relieving the patient's painful condition and praising himself as a good doctor.

    During the hot summer, a poor neighbor came to Dr. Ken with a cold condition. Dr. Ken, drunk, went to examine him and briefly checked his pulse, saying, "This is a case of heatstroke; you should use the Fangfeng Tang formula." After taking the formula, the patient nearly lost consciousness. Dr. Ken became frightened and quickly gave him the Ba Wei Fu Zi formula, and the patient recovered. The patient continued to take the formula ten times, and eventually, he recovered completely.

    Dr. Ken also tried to steam and dry the herb Ji Ni to use as a substitute for ginseng and soaked cinnamon bark with walnuts to create a substitute for high-quality cinnamon, calling it Qing Hua. He combined imperial prescriptions and indulged in immoral behavior, shortening people's lives. From this, he made a fortune and rose to prominence, but his skills were never truly refined, and he often made mistakes. When he doubted the diagnosis or was unsure how to proceed, he would later use a two-pronged theory to wait for the patient to recover on their own, as a strategy to hide his lack of skill. This approach was very effective.

    123

    至若构堂之伪幕也，与肯堂之术则殊途而同归。医可以庸死人，幕则以劣杀人。其初游保定，录陈案；继入京师，为科吏。精熟律例，强记无遗。怀之径寸，遨游当事。一得馆地，始则高抬声价，以耸东人，而隐则逢迎居停之意，倡导主人之非，串官婪财，通役作弊。每致徇私死公，强词夺理，立成铁案，牢不可破。覆盆之下，永载沉冤。曾为石城史公幕，一富贾过境，有车夫坠车碾死。

    Explanation

    123

    Regarding the deceptive facade of a structure, and the skill of the knowledgeable hall, they differ in approach but lead to the same end. A doctor can kill with mediocrity, and a lawyer can kill with inferiority. Initially, he traveled to Baoding and recorded old cases; subsequently, he entered the capital and became a clerk. Thoroughly familiar with legal codes, he memorized everything without omission. Carrying it in his heart, he roamed among the powerful. Once he obtained a position, he first inflated his reputation to impress the locals, but secretly flattered the host's intentions, promoting the host's faults. He connived with officials to seek wealth, colluding with subordinates to cheat. Often leading to favoritism at the expense of public interest, he twisted words to win arguments, quickly forming ironclad cases, which are unbreakable. Under the overturned pot, forever bearing deep grudges. He once served as a lawyer for Shi Gong of Shicheng. A wealthy merchant passed through, and a coachman fell off the carriage and was run over.

    124

    构堂以其富，过为推敲，安生疑窦，使东家逐节严鞫风之，以诈其财至千金，则构堂一举笔之劳，杯酒释之矣。又为闽中某公幕，一人命为某殴死，构堂初以为误伤致死。后府司行驳，东家覆讯，实为殴死无疑。而竟执以案由已定，不欲申文详辨，以形其短。且曰：“失久不如失出，节屈法，宽之未为不可。”在泉州署，妄以海滨贫人，诬之为盗。心知其冤，欲为官邀功，不之救，且实其辞，尽诛之。每闻狱有未定谳而死者，必抚掌称快，以为“又省我许多笔墨，便可早结。”是何复知朝廷明慎详刑之义，务期情实罪允，方正典刑。苟有矜疑，犹予缓决，以延旦夕之命，而顾草菅视之乎？

    Explanation

    124

    Goutang, because of his wealth, was excessively meticulous in his writing, causing doubts to arise. He instructed his master to investigate step by step, probing into the matter, in order to swindle a fortune of a thousand gold pieces. Thus, Goutang's mere act of writing was enough to secure a lenient resolution with a cup of wine. Later, serving under a high-ranking official in Fujian, he was involved in a case where someone was accused of causing a death by assault. Initially, Goutang believed it was an accidental injury leading to death. However, after the provincial office reviewed the case and the master re-investigated, it was clear that the death was indeed caused by assault. Despite this, Goutang insisted that the case was already settled and did not want to submit a detailed report to highlight his shortcomings. He even said, "A long-term loss is not as bad as a short-term loss; bending the law to be lenient is not wrong." While serving in Quanzhou, he recklessly accused poor people by the sea of being thieves. Knowing in his heart that they were innocent, he wanted to gain credit for the official and did not save them, instead confirming their accusations and having them all killed. Whenever he heard of executions of those whose cases were still undecided, he would clap his hands and feel pleased, thinking, "It saves me a lot of writing, and the case can be settled quickly." How could he understand the principles of the court, which emphasize careful and thorough administration of justice, ensuring that the facts are clear and the punishment is just? If there is any doubt, one should still grant a delayed verdict to extend the accused's life for a few days, rather than treating them like weeds!

    125

    夫幕犹医也，良相之无异于良医者，不以其事之悬绝，而力之足以活人，一也。士之不得志于时，借术托途，岂但糊口，最好积善。肯堂分文不费，可以救人之危；构堂声色不动，可以全人之命。顾何惮而不为，乃刻薄若是？无他，见利而忘义也。故肯堂半年，家遭回录，荡然一烬，妻子俱焚；构堂今将六十，流寓岭表。虽称名幕，而搁笔辄穷老而潦倒。
    (七如氏曰：“医与幕，唯恐伤人，亦唯恐不伤人。慎斯术也。存乎其人，择之而已矣。”)

    125

    A counselor is like a doctor. There is no difference between a good chancellor and a good doctor; they both have the ability to save lives, despite the differences in their fields. Scholars who are unsuccessful in their careers often use their skills as a means of survival or advancement, but it is not just about making a living; it is best to do good deeds. 肯堂 can help in times of danger without spending money, and 构堂 can remain calm in the face of temptation and preserve lives. Why would anyone hesitate to do good when they are so stingy? There is no other reason than that they see profit but forget righteousness. Therefore, 肯堂's family suffered a disaster within half a year, and his home was reduced to ashes, with his wife and children all perishing. 构堂 is now sixty years old and lives in exile in the southern regions. Although he is a famous counselor, he is now poor and disheveled. (七如氏 said, "Doctors and counselors are cautious not to harm people, but they are also cautious not to avoid helping them. The success of these professions depends on the person practicing them, and one should choose wisely.")

    126

    李可久
    李可久，祖母于氏，生三日，言前世姓陈，行三。由进士授洪洞令。以接按院，坠马死，见冥司，云：“以刑酷，好使罪囚跪美人椿，尝彻夜不释。因罚为北地女，使其缠足穿耳，生产秽亵种种罪恶道。限二十三年而返。”
    七八岁，山东臬司王某，因公过境，传呼于于氏之门，女望见之，曰：“王年友犹识陈某乎？”王停舆，惊询。女备道生前，缕晰可据。王知其前生善画兰，给笔札，令作。女笔拳屈指不随腕，遂相向大哭。及长，面麻大于钱，项有宿瘤。见恶于其夫，年二十三果血崩死。

    颈上痒
    萧山屠户张六，性凶暴，宰牲为业。日必宰猎十数，以此获利。遂娶妻，数年无子。后身体日渐臃肿，头项亦自短缩，遍胸生毛如鬣，两目眶俱深陷，逼肖豕形。
    六月间，门首肉案旁独坐，觉颈上偶痒，张以屠刀搔之，朗朗有声，忽狂风吹坠檐木，一击而首落。其妻坐产招夫，改业谋生。

    Explanation

    126

    Li Kejiu
    Li Kejiu, whose maternal grandmother was surnamed Yu, spoke on the third day of his birth that in a previous life he was named Chen and was the third in his family. He passed the imperial examinations and was appointed as the magistrate of Hongtong. Due to an official inspection, he fell off his horse and died, appearing before the underworld. There, it was said to him, "Because of your cruel punishment, you made prisoners kneel before a beautiful woman, keeping them kneeling all night without release. Therefore, you are punished to be a woman in the north, made to bind her feet and pierce her ears, and to engage in various sins and evils related to childbirth and sexual misconduct. You are limited to return in twenty-three years."

    At the age of seven or eight, Wang, a provincial censor from Shandong, passed through the area on official business and summoned Yu at her door. The girl saw him and said, "Do you still remember Chen from before, Wang?" Wang stopped his carriage, surprised and asked. The girl recounted her previous life in detail, which could be verified. Wang knew that in her past life she was skilled in painting orchids, so he gave her brushes and paper, asking her to paint. Her fingers were clenched and did not follow her wrist, and they both wept together. As she grew older, her face was covered in large patches of pockmarks, and she had a tumor on her neck. She was disliked by her husband and indeed died from a hemorrhage at the age of twenty-three.

    Neck Itch
    Zhang Liu, a butchering butcher from Xiaoshan, was fierce and violent, making a living by slaughtering animals. He slaughtered dozens of animals each day, thereby profiting. He then married and had no children for several years. Later, his body became increasingly obese, his neck shortened, and he grew hair all over his chest like a mane, with deeply sunken eyes, closely resembling a pig in appearance.
    One June, he sat alone next to a meat counter at the entrance of his shop, feeling an occasional itch on his neck. He scratched it with a butcher's knife, making a clear, loud sound. Suddenly, a strong wind blew down a beam, striking his head off in one blow. His wife, after giving birth, sought a husband and changed her occupation to make a living.

    127

    手掌痕
    湖州凌汉章，见一丐者，形躯长大而凶恶，面颊上天生一手掌痕。有十余丐从之，观者如市。里人有知之者，谓此丐聂姓，父为刑曹员外。曾因一过掌击一仆仆地死。后家居，白日见其仆入门，继无所睹，妻即生一子，掌痕宛然在面。父乃指其掌之见于面，而悔其行之疚于心也。比长，日以杀父为事，父忧死。子荡产，遂为丐。
    呜呼！缙绅之子多丐也，丐固不止一聂也。夫官至贵而丐至贱，不能长守贵者，贱不旋踵矣。世之丐者，沿市哀号，称谓无所不呼。亦犹之乎高官显爵，端拱衙堂，嗤嗤者咸尊崇之，百千万声，无量称道。苟为不慎，则出乎尔者，亦反乎尔。不丐而何？

    黑毡帽
    山左有包揽钱粮者，士庶家多为之设肆于市。或兑换银钱，或打造首饰。置一大熔炉于室中，如浮图，名为倾宝于官，而实则消髓于民也。又串通胥吏，使衙官出示：不准自封投柜，复不准他人开设。此铺而后得龙断焉。是以犯禁之揽人，反视为奉官之包户矣。

    Explanation

    127

    Palm Mark
    In Huzhou, Ling Han-zhang once saw a beggar who was tall and fierce in appearance, with a palm mark naturally present on his cheek. He was followed by more than ten beggars, and spectators gathered like a market. Among the locals, some knew that this beggar was surnamed Nie, and his father had been a judicial official. The father once struck a servant so hard that the servant died. Afterwards, while living at home, the father saw his dead servant enter the house during the day, and his wife gave birth to a son with the same palm mark on his face. The father pointed to the mark on his son's face and felt remorse for his actions. As the son grew older, he made it his business to kill his father, and the father died of worry. The son squandered his wealth and eventually became a beggar.
    Alas! Many sons of the gentry become beggars, not just one named Nie. From high officials to lowly beggars, those who cannot maintain their status fall quickly. In the world, beggars walk through the market, calling out in sorrow, using all kinds of names to solicit alms. This is similar to high-ranking officials who sit in their offices, admired by all, with countless praises and endless commendations. If one is not careful, what one does can come back to haunt them. How can one avoid becoming a beggar?

    Black Felt Hat
    In Shandong, there were those who monopolized grain and money matters, and many households set up shops in the market. Some exchanged money, while others made jewelry. They placed a large furnace in the room, resembling a pagoda, claiming to serve the government, but in reality, they exploited the people. They colluded with officials to issue orders, prohibiting self-sealing and depositing money into a cabinet, as well as preventing others from setting up their own businesses. After this shop, they gained a monopoly. Thus, those who violated the law were seen as serving the government, despite their illegal activities.

    128

    乡人负镪入城，登门请纳，任意倍算，不可测度。有乡人无钱者，请为代纳，其毒更甚。当麦熟，则贱索其麦；谷熟，则贱索其谷，以至棉烟丝布，及于车牛田土，无不设法取之。而被害者犹曰：“官项也。”吾乡有愚老，有田数十亩。城中有包管其事者，五年荡其产。老饮恨日甚，以致病渐。将死，曰：“吾必作恶犬嗾杀之。”其家殓以黑毡帽，紫花布袍。未几，来一犬，黑头毼身，遂不去。家之人亦忘此老之言矣。及犬壮，包者又来索其子之物，犬闻其声，跃而出，啮其腓，不释，百计不能脱。门前故有积水一池，遂相滚入水，犬竟曳至深处，两毙焉。闻于官，具述冤报。官令其妻自行收敛，且埋其犬，毋再结冤。

    Explanation

    128

    The villagers carried their money into the city, knocked on the door to request payment, and faced arbitrary double demands that were unpredictable. There were also villagers who had no money and were asked to pay on behalf of others, which made the situation even more severe. When the wheat was ripe, they would seize it at a low price; when the rice was ripe, they would seize it at a low price. This continued to cotton, tobacco, silk, cloth, carts, oxen, land, and soil—nothing was spared from being taken by any means. Yet, the victims still claimed, "It's an official tax." In my village, there was a foolish old man who owned dozens of acres of land. In the city, there was someone responsible for managing this matter, and over five years, they drained his property. The old man grew increasingly resentful, which gradually worsened his health. On his deathbed, he said, "I will act like a wicked dog and bite to death the one who wronged me." His family buried him in a black felt hat and a purple flower-patterned robe. Soon after, a dog appeared, with a black head and shaggy body, and it did not leave. The family members had forgotten the old man's words. When the dog grew strong, the one who had been responsible for the matter came again to demand the possessions of the old man's son. The dog heard the sound and jumped out, biting his calf, refusing to let go, and no matter what, could not be freed. There was a pool of stagnant water in front of the house, and they rolled into the water together. The dog eventually dragged him to the deep part of the pool, and both died there. The incident was reported to the authorities, and the story of revenge was fully narrated. The official ordered the wife to bury both the dog and her husband, and not to make further grudges.

    129

    偿负驴
    吾乡刘心木者，家素封，好济贫乏，有善人之耳。时有田姓，济宁人，单寒，流落井里间。刘翁与之语：“几聿云暮，云胡不归？想尔家亦不远，岂无父母兄弟，而踽踽若是？”田姓以负逋告。翁曰：“几何？”田曰：“十五缗。”翁归出镪金八两与之，田曰：“予负不能偿而避于此。今复负翁，以偿负，是一负也。徒多此转移耳，不如不偿。”翁曰：“彼求偿急，汝不得归。我求偿缓，汝得归。且偿不偿任汝也。”田喜，谢而去。则不知田之果归？果偿？所负与否？且不知果有是负否也？后翁遂置之。

    Explanation

    129

    Repaying a Debt

    In my hometown, there was a man named Liu Xinmu, whose family was wealthy and he was known for his kindness and generosity. At that time, there was a man surnamed Tian from Jining, who was lonely and poor, wandering aimlessly in the countryside. Liu Xinmu spoke to him, saying, "The day is nearly over, why not return? Your home must not be far, and you surely have parents and siblings, yet you wander alone like this?" The man replied, "I am in debt and unable to repay it, so I am avoiding it here." Liu Xinmu asked, "How much do you owe?" The man said, "Fifteen strings of coins." Liu Xinmu returned home and gave him eight taels of silver. The man said, "I cannot repay my debt and have avoided it. Now, if I repay you, it is still a form of debt, just an unnecessary transfer. It is better not to repay." Liu Xinmu said, "He demands repayment urgently, and you cannot return. I demand repayment slowly, and you can return. Whether you repay or not is up to you." The man was pleased and thanked him before leaving. However, it is uncertain whether the man eventually returned home, whether he repaid the debt, or whether he still owed it. And it is even more uncertain whether the debt actually existed. After this, Liu Xinmu let the matter rest.

    130

    数年，翁偶坐，夜半闻叩扉声，且呼刘。翁启户，无所见。是夜槽间老蹇下一黑驴。阅月而驳唇，皆白皙，浑身如墨，且善伺人意，呼之即来，童稚任控辔，从无蹄啮事。秋夏场圃，每系凉于柳阴下。有晋人过，爱之，曰：“噫！个粉眼粉嘴好，愿以八金求售。”翁与之。翁即于是夜梦田姓人来偿负云。

    男女变易
    郓城李常和，居城，开药肆。家迄可四十，无子，娶妾，三年诞一儿，李甚喜，时时抚弄。尝使其妻服侍绷褥，稍不慎，则骂其不贤。弥月，把儿尿，视其蛹，缩小如豆。越日，内陷，旬而沟，男化为女，哇声转雌。

    Explanation

    130

    After several years, the old man happened to be sitting when, at midnight, he heard knocking at the door and someone calling for Liu. He opened the door but saw nothing. That night, the old donkey in the stall gave birth to a black donkey. After a month, its lips turned white, and its entire body was as black as ink. It was good at understanding people's intentions, coming when called, and children could ride it without any trouble from kicks or bites. In autumn and summer, the donkey was often tied to rest under the shade of willow trees in the fields. A man from the state of Jin passed by and loved it, saying, "Ah! This donkey with its pink eyes and mouth is wonderful; I would like to buy it for eight coins." The old man sold it to him. That night, the old man dreamed that a man surnamed Tian came to pay off his debt.

    Gender Change
    In Yuncheng, Li Changhe lived in the city and ran a pharmacy. He was about forty years old and had no son, so he took a concubine. After three years, she gave birth to a boy, and Li was very happy, often fondling the child. He once made his wife attend to the baby's swaddling and diaper, and if she was slightly careless, he would scold her for being unwise. When the child was a month old, Li held him and noticed that the navel was shrinking like a bean. The next day, the navel sank inward, and after ten days, it became a small hole. The boy transformed into a girl, and the crying sound changed to that of a female.

    131

    城西乡之方大头，不知其名，农也。亦无子，产五女。是年又生一女，其妻恶之，欲溺毙，方曰：“子女皆肉也，与其子不肖，欲逆覆吾宗，何如多有女安而绝我后？”遂育之。
    忽一夕，大风动屋，其女哭声壮，辰视之，变成男。哄其乡里，咸以为奇。有自城中来者，言李药铺同日男而女，交相诧也。
    (得子薄妻，如之何不女？爱女若子，如之何不男？是在乾隆辛亥九月间事。
    嘉庆十一年丙寅二月，余代理湖北江夏事廿三日。看城外金沙州民人熊万兴呈称：其长女金姑，年十七岁，许字城内李宏声之子为妻。忽于十八日变为男子。熊故无子，其二女，恐李戚诬以赖婚，且此事合郡皆知，报明在案耳。余曰：“此事之异，亦人之妖也。毋用报。如恐李氏诬，签目俱在，可指而验；如何等系念姻娅，何不以未字之次女续之耶？”熊叩头欣谢，撤其报呈而去。)

    拔一毛
    陈眉公继儒，优游林下，声誉一时。当时皆倚重其言，有山中宰相之目。

    Explanation

    131

    A Man from the West of the City

    In the western suburbs of the city, there lived a farmer named Fang Daitou, whose name was unknown. He had no sons and had given birth to five daughters. That year, he had another daughter, but his wife disliked her and wanted to drown and kill her. Fang said, "Children are all flesh and blood. Rather than having a son who is unworthy and might bring ruin to our family, how is it better to have many daughters and peacefully end my lineage?" So, he raised her.

    One night, a strong wind blew through the house, and the girl cried loudly. In the morning, they found that she had turned into a boy. The villagers were shocked and thought it was strange. Some people from the city also heard about it and said that on the same day, a boy turned into a girl at Li's pharmacy, and they were all amazed.

    (If having a son diminishes one's wife, how can one not have a daughter? If one loves a daughter as much as a son, how can one not have a son? This strange event occurred in the ninth month of the year Xin Hai during the reign of Emperor Qianlong.)

    In the second month of the year Bing Yin in the eleventh year of the reign of Emperor Jiaqing, I was acting as the magistrate of Jiangxia, Hubei. On the twenty-third day, I reviewed the petition of Xu Wanxing, a resident of Jinsha State outside the city. He stated that his eldest daughter, Jingu, aged seventeen, was engaged to marry the son of Li Hongsheng in the city. Suddenly, on the eighteenth day, she turned into a man. Xu had no sons and two daughters, and he feared that Li's relatives might accuse him of breaking the engagement. Moreover, this matter was known throughout the county and had been reported and recorded. I said, "This strange event is also a human anomaly. There is no need to report it. If there is a fear of Li's accusations, all the signatures are present, they can be pointed out and verified. How can one not care about family ties? Why not marry the younger daughter who is not yet engaged?" Xu knelt and happily thanked me, withdrew his report, and left.

    Pulling Out a Hair

    Chen Meigong Jiru lived a leisurely life in the mountains, enjoying a good reputation at the time. Everyone relied on his words, and he was known as the "Prime Minister of the Mountains."

    132

    毛文龙总制三边，会母寿，思得陈一言以为荣。特遣将校赍重币往求。陈迟欠未予，将校恐误期，登堂坐索，颇事罗唣。陈大怒，斥逐之，迁怒于毛。是岂毛之罪哉？即将校之索文亦不过党将军帐下羔酒习气耳，何足挂怀？适门人某，为兵部尚书，过访求教。陈遽语曰：“拔一毛可以利天下。”门人再拜谢曰：“谨受教。”履任，诬毛以罪状而诛之。毛既被诛，边事大坏。论者以明三百年天下，实眉公一言亡之也。
    (近有殿元公某遭雷殛死。成殓后，雷复震其尸。闻其生平，止莅荆宜观察一任。说者谓其曾准人筑州种苇，以致堵截江流，遂贻灌城决堤之患，故有此谴。嘻！若据数世诛锄，如白起牛，曹瞒豕，则殿元公又安知非眉公后身耶？)

    鳖僧
    余杭一僧，极奢侈，穷极其嗜，因之巧极其饪。好食鳖，于斧顶开一孔，火盛水沸，鳖头出口张，僧以醢酱姜桂之属，杓而饮之。鳖熟而味已入矣。如是有年。

    Explanation

    132

    Mao Wenlong, the Grand Coordinator of the Three Borders, was celebrating his mother's birthday and sought a word from Chen to bring honor. He specially sent officers with heavy gifts to seek Chen. Chen delayed and did not give the word, and the officers, fearing they would miss their deadline, entered the hall and sat to demand it, causing a lot of disturbance. Chen became very angry, scolded and drove them away, and transferred his anger to Mao. Was this Mao's fault? The officers' behavior was merely a reflection of the indulgent habits of the general's entourage, and what was there to worry about? Just then, a disciple, who was the Minister of War, visited to seek instruction. Chen suddenly said, "To pluck a single hair for the benefit of the world, I would not do it." The disciple bowed twice and thanked, saying, "I have received your teaching." After taking office, he imputed crimes to Mao and had him executed. With Mao's execution, the border situation deteriorated greatly. Those who commented on it said that the Ming dynasty's three hundred years of rule was actually lost due to one word from Mei Gong.

    (Recently, a状元 (zhuangyuan) was struck by lightning and died. After the funeral, lightning struck his body again. It was said that during his lifetime, he held the position of an observer in Jing and Yi provinces. Some said that he once allowed people to build a state and plant reeds, which blocked the river flow and caused flooding and dike breaches, leading to this punishment. Ah! If punishment is carried out across generations, like the cruelty of Bai Qi or the ruthlessness of Cao Cao, who knows if the状元 (zhuangyuan) was not a reincarnation of Mei Gong?)

    A Monk from Yuhang
    A monk from Yuhang was extremely extravagant and indulged in his passions to the extreme, and thus he was very skilled in cooking. He loved to eat turtles and would open a hole at the top of an axe. When the fire was intense and the water was boiling, the turtle's head would emerge and open. The monk would pour various seasonings like fermented meat, soy sauce, ginger, and cinnamon over it and drink it. By the time the turtle was cooked, the flavors had already been absorbed. This went on for many years.

    133

    一夕，火发。僧故楼居，仓猝间，思钻月窗以遁。窗小，仅容一首，竟烧死。观者曰：“今日之烧死僧，如当日之活煮鳖。”
    (按<洗冤录>，甲鱼同苋菜食，生鳖，茅舍潺滴肉上，皆可杀人；又有一种毒蛇，与鳖交，精入地三尺，凝结鳖形，其名曰“蝎”。往往不辨，食之主血胀死。)

    李五
    济宁三井闸，为运河蓄泄湖水而筑。粮艘至，起板迎溜以上，千夫牵挽，声振断流，如闻鼞鼓。行而引者谓之“短纤”，止而提者谓之“排夫”。饿鬼道中，往往托生于此。因忆友人有悯粮艘纤夫、集唐一首云：
    西江运船立红帜(王建)，落帆渡桥来浦里(张籍)。送风上水万斛里(王建)，自怜淮海同泥滓(李绅)。
    计合一条麻绳挽(韩文)，有力未免遭驱使(张籍)。邮夫防吏急喧驱(张籍)，夜间鼍声人尽起(钱起)。
    不辞手足皆胼胝(李温)，趚趚踏沙人似鬼(子厚)。尔来气少筋骨露(吴融)，因风因雨更憔悴(元稹)。

    Explanation

    133

    One night, a fire broke out. The monk, who lived in a tower, in the midst of the sudden emergency, thought of drilling through the moonlit window to escape. The window was small, only large enough for one head, and he ultimately burned to death. The observers remarked, "The monk who burned to death today is like the turtle that was alive and then boiled." (According to the Washing Away of Wrongs, if a turtle is eaten with water spinach, if it's a live turtle, or if it's placed on meat in a thatched cottage where water drips, all can kill; there is also a kind of venomous snake that mates with a turtle, and its essence penetrates three feet into the ground, forming a turtle shape, called 'scorpion.' Often indistinguishable, eating it causes blood to swell and die.)

    Li Wu
    The Three Wells Lock in Jinan was built to store and release lake water for the canal. When grain ships arrive, boards are raised to meet the current and move upstream, and thousands of people pull and tug, the sound shaking the broken flow, like hearing drums. Those who pull while moving are called 'short纤,' and those who hold back and lift are called '排夫.' Often reborn here in the realm of hungry ghosts. Recalling a friend who, moved by the plight of the grain ship纤夫, compiled a poem from Tang verses:
    Ships on the West River stand with red flags (Wang Jian), lowering sails to cross the bridge and enter the harbor (Zhang Jie). Sending the wind to push the ships up the water, carrying ten thousand measures (Wang Jian), lamenting that the Huai and Hai rivers are like mud and silt (Li Shen).
    Calculating that one hemp rope can pull (Han Wen), even the strong cannot avoid being driven (Zhang Jie). Postal workers and guards rush and shout to drive them (Zhang Jie), at night, the crocodile sounds make everyone wake up (Qian Qi).
    Not sparing hands and feet, all calloused (Li Wen), stepping on the sand, people look like ghosts (Zi Hou). Since then, their strength has diminished, and their bones show (Wu Rong), due to wind and rain, they become even more emaciated (Yuan Zhen).

    134

    茫茫漫漫方自悲(韦应物)，顽钝如船命如纸(白傅)。柳丝挽断肠牵断(来鹏)，千声万血谁哀尔(韩文)。
    呜呼余心诚恺悌(温飞卿)，莫言自古皆如此。谁人为奏圣天子(陆龟蒙)？
    有纤夫而又作排夫名李五者，满面斑大于钱，一目，鼻两孔如突黔，唇齿皆随意布置，如今水墨画中写意人。余从泲水之旁，往往见之，未尝不曰：“此不全于天者也。”李曰：“人为之也。”问其故，李曰：“我河内人，家有薄产，耽于赌，故种麦一年，供骰一箝；种秫一秋，打叶一周……”
    岁将暮，家家办酒果，而李冰釜冷灶，若度寒食禁烟。妻骂曰：“酒肉，朋友也；柴米，夫妻也。我自嫁汝家，终岁操作，不曾换得一餐饱。今岁将尽，尔其与之俱尽乎？”李绐之曰：“我将觅自尽。”妻指窗前一小树曰：“尽在树间。”李愤然取厨刀，断其树，睨而视之，窃有所喜，以为可使制梃而御人于国门之外矣。乃芟繁柯，伐碎叶，应手而去。妻亦不问其所之。

    Explanation

    134

    In the vast and boundless expanse, one feels sorrow (Wei Yingwu), stupid as a boat and life as thin as paper (Bai Juyi). The willow threads can tear the heart apart (Lai Peng), who will mourn you in your thousands of cries and bloodshed (Han Yu).

    Alas, my heart is truly kind and gentle (Wen Tingyun), do not say that this has always been so. Who will present this to the holy emperor (Lu Guimeng)?

    There was a man named Li Wu who worked as both a rudder puller and a boat pusher. His face was covered in spots larger than coins, one eye, and his nostrils were like protruding black holes, with his lips and teeth arranged haphazardly, much like a person depicted in ink wash painting. I often saw him by the Ji River and would always say, "This is a person incomplete by nature." Li said, "It was done by humans." When asked why, he replied, "I am from the He River area, my family has meager possessions, and I am addicted to gambling, so I plant wheat for a year to provide enough for one round of dice; I plant millet in autumn, and it lasts for a week of gambling..."

    As the year comes to an end, every household prepares wine and fruit, while Li's home remains cold with ice in the pot and a cold stove, as if observing the Cold Food Festival. His wife scolded him, saying, "Wine and meat are friends; firewood and rice are spouses. Since I married into your family, I have worked hard all year without ever being able to eat my fill. Now that the year is coming to an end, is your life coming to an end along with it?" Li lied to her, saying, "I am going to commit suicide." The wife pointed to a small tree in front of the window and said, "It's all in the tree." Li angrily took a kitchen knife, cut down the tree, looked at it, secretly pleased, thinking he could use a stick to fend off people at the city gate. He then pruned the branches, chopped the leaves, and they fell away with a snap. His wife did not ask where he was going.

    135

    出官道，伏柳树下。夜北风凛凛，一人负行李踉跄来，意其为岁暮遄归者。棒喝之，其人惧，遗所负以逸。李喜，固利在物不在人。归，启视钱物，新衣，颇足办五辛盘。夫妻皆欣欣度乐岁。第倘来物，不甚爱惜。曾几何时，瓶罍告匮。李复技痒，妇谏曰：“得意不宜再往。”不听，复要于路。月朦胧上，见驴背大囊，一老叟盹而骑。去三步，击之，不中。叟下，撤梃，前步，提李发立起，曰：“若是谁？”李不答，复问，李亦不答。叟以足略拨，李仆地仰，叟踏李胸，曰：“汝不言，且试汝梃。”一梃而齿牙脱，再梃而鼻梁折，三梃而眉飞目去，如荠辛臼，千捶百捣，至无口无耳无鼻舌身意，更幻出一切不可思议诸般色相。叟兴尽，复跨蹇迢迢而去。
    李死而复苏，血与泪迸，曰：“我复有何面目返家门对妻子耶？”遂流于今盖二十年。余异其状，故备书之。

    Explanation

    135

    Leaving the official road, he lay in ambush under a willow tree. At night, the northern wind was harsh, and a person carrying luggage stumbled along, seeming to be someone hurrying home at the end of the year. Li struck and shouted at him, and the person was afraid, abandoning what he was carrying to escape. Li was pleased, believing the gain was in the objects, not in the person. Returning, he opened to see the money and goods, new clothes, quite enough to make a five-spice dish. The husband and wife both happily spent the happy year. But the things that came by chance were not cherished very much. Not long after, the jars and bottles were empty. Li again felt the urge to act, and his wife advised, "It's not right to go again when you're already satisfied." He didn't listen and again set out on the road. The moon was dimly rising, and he saw a large bag on a donkey, with an old man dozing and riding. Taking three steps, he struck, but missed. The old man got off, pulled out his staff, stepped forward, and pulled Li up by his hair, asking, "Who are you?" Li didn't answer, and when asked again, Li also didn't answer. The old man slightly kicked him, and Li fell face up. The old man stepped on Li's chest and said, "You won't talk? Let me try your stick." One strike and his teeth fell out, another and his nose broke, and a third and his eyebrows and eyes flew away, like a radish in a mortar, beaten a thousand times, until he had no mouth, no ears, no nose, no tongue, no body, no mind, then he transformed into all kinds of unimaginable forms. The old man, having had his fill, remounted his donkey and went away slowly. Li died but revived, with blood and tears gushing out, saying, "I have no face to return home to my wife and children!" And thus he has been wandering for about twenty years. I find his appearance strange, so I have written it all down.

    136

    卷四 祥瑞部
    祈梦事征
    无锡惠山，有于少保忠肃公庙。二八月闻，苏人多斋宿庙中，祈神以梦。
    夫祈梦者何？定终身、卜休咎也。梦之必属于公者何？说者曰：“于公年少时好梦，尝宿壮缪庙，梦神告之曰：‘汝终身，归问汝嫂，则知之。’公归而问日，‘嫂嫂，试猜我异日作何等官。’嫂曰：‘夭杀的，不过与你一二品小官足矣。’后至少保。英宗复辟，遂及难。后人哀之，若怨于梦，而因以天下后世之梦，皆司之于公。此于公祈梦之由来也。”
    周清源者，常州秀才，穷于遇，且困于学。幼婚于富伧张姓。张有两坦，周次倩，其大婿为鹾商子，任姓。张翁每爱任而鄙周，周亦遂不敢与任齿。会张翁寿辰，张女先归，周无以为仪。作诗一章，令其妻献嘏焉。翁笑曰：“半张纸值不得两瓯面。”其大姊曰：“想妹夫已呕尽心血矣。”周妻惭甚，惟于无人处潸潸襟泪而已。

    Explanation

    136

    Translation

    Chapter Four: Portents and Auspicious Signs

    Records of Dream Seeking

    In Wuxi, on Mount Huishan, there is a temple dedicated to Yu Shao, the Righteous and Upright Public Servant. During the second and eighth months of the lunar calendar, people from Suzhou often fast and stay overnight in the temple, seeking dreams from the gods.

    What is the purpose of seeking dreams? To determine one's fate and predict good or bad omens. Why are dreams necessarily attributed to Yu Gong? According to some, "When Yu Gong was young, he had frequent dreams. Once, he stayed overnight at the temple of Zhuangmiao, and in a dream, a deity told him, 'Your entire life, return to ask your sister-in-law, and you will know.' He returned home and asked his sister-in-law, 'Sister-in-law, guess what rank of official I will become in the future.' She replied, 'Goodness, no more than a second or third-rank minor official is enough for you.' Later, he became a Righteous and Upright Public Servant. When Emperor Yingzong was restored to the throne, Yu Gong met his fate. Later generations mourned him, as if blaming the dream, and thus entrusted the dreams of all under heaven and future generations to Yu Gong. This is the origin of Yu Gong's practice of seeking dreams."

    Zhou Qingyuan, a scholar from Changzhou, was unlucky in life and struggled with his studies. He married at a young age into a wealthy merchant family named Zhang. Zhang had two daughters-in-law; Zhou was the second son-in-law, and the first son-in-law was a salt merchant's son, surnamed Ren. Old Master Zhang always favored Ren and looked down on Zhou, so Zhou also dared not compare himself to Ren. On the occasion of Old Master Zhang's birthday, Zhang's daughter-in-law returned home first, leaving Zhou with nothing to offer as a gift. He wrote a poem and had his wife present it. Old Master Zhang laughed and said, "Half a sheet of paper is not worth two bowls of noodles." His eldest daughter-in-law said, "I suppose your brother-in-law has poured his heart and soul into it." Zhou's wife felt very ashamed and could only shed tears silently when no one was around.

    137

    翌朝，寿客哄堂，眷属皆从屏后觑，独不见周生。周妻侦诸仆，仆曰：“来也，翁不令预席，置秘小阁中。一人独酌，想已酣矣。”乃令仆导往视之。至阁上，见周方以箸缴缕吞咽，呜呜有声。女顾而唾，周噎于颡。女泣曰：“奈何幽诸室而尝丈人之羹也？”周曰：“聊供一饱，初何尝不当食而兴叹也。”女曰：“诚如是，尚有羞恶之心也？”生投著起，欲去。女曰：“焉往？”生曰：“我将入长安取富若贵来相。”女曰：“良佳，无徒托诸空言。”乃拔一钗，与周为行资。
    周袖之出，售于市。方欲行，瞥见一皂衣人曰：“粮急矣，絷欠户。”周未及答，遂夺其银，且拥之去。至役家，抵暮。及晨，皂谓其妻曰：“夜梦神告我，周相公贵人也。”妻曰：“我亦与子同梦，当善视之。”皂谓周曰：“我为相公代杖久矣。我今若使相公见官长，将及辱，我不忍。”且还其金。遂饭周，役夫妻甚殷勤匕箸间。周感谢去，因下杭州，访故人某。

    Explanation

    137

    The next day, the guests at the celebration were in high spirits, and the family members peered from behind the screen, but Zhou was nowhere to be seen. Zhou's wife inquired of the servants, who said, "He has come, but the master did not allow him to join the table; instead, he was placed in a secret small room. He is drinking alone, probably already drunk." She then sent a servant to take her to see him. Upon reaching the room, she saw Zhou using chopsticks to pick up food and swallow, making a gulping sound. The woman looked at him and spat, causing Zhou to choke. She wept and said, "How could you be confined in this room and taste your father-in-law's soup?" Zhou replied, "I just wanted to satisfy my hunger; I never thought to lament while eating." The woman said, "If that is true, then you still have a sense of shame and disgust!" Zhou put down his chopsticks and stood up, ready to leave. The woman asked, "Where are you going?" Zhou said, "I will go to Chang'an to gain wealth and status for us." The woman said, "That is good, but do not just talk about it in vain." She then pulled out a hairpin and gave it to Zhou as travel money.

    Zhou took the hairpin and went out, selling it in the market. Just as he was about to leave, he suddenly saw a man in black clothing say, "The food is urgent; we need to detain you." Before Zhou could answer, the man took his silver and took him away. They arrived at the master's house by evening. By morning, the man in black clothing told his wife, "I dreamed that a god told me, 'Zhou is a noble person.'" The wife said, "I had the same dream; we should treat him kindly." The man said to Zhou, "I have served as your representative for a long time. If I make you meet with officials, it will be humiliating, and I cannot bear it." He then returned Zhou's money and fed him. The master and his wife were very kind to Zhou. Zhou thanked them and left, then went to Hangzhou to visit an old friend.

    138

    过无锡，舟人有祈梦于少保庙者，生亦与焉。是夜，梦于公揖而坐曰：“清华挺贵之选，异日我有一事，不能不烦足下锦心也。”周醒不解。
    至杭州。友人某者，三年前已作古矣。周大丧气，不唯不能北上，更无面目返江东。遂就寓武林两月，而行资匮，逆旅主人将不容周。当此时，椎心饮泣，生不如死。尽醉出城，至湖心，望深青之处，一跃入水。其初不觉沉溺，栩栩然如在空中，既而身若负重，以为是殆死矣。乃一举目，则身丽于网，为渔者所救。周苏，以为不能遂沉，误其死期，大骂渔者，渔者不能辨，乃携网认罪而去。周仍复入水，又觉有人亟曳其辫发而起，置之亭中。周又苏，则见一头陀，筋骨纠纠，手执念珠，跏趺地上。周不言，惟眶视。僧曰：“若善男子，有何大不得已，必沉沦而不返耶？”周呻吟涕洟，告请颠末。僧起曰：“曷随贫僧往？”周随登一小舟。

    Explanation

    138

    Passing through Wuxi, there were boatmen who sought dreams at the Temple of the Shao Bao, and the protagonist also joined them. That night, he dreamed of the official bowing and sitting, saying, "You are chosen for a noble position, and one day I have something that cannot be done without troubling your fine mind." Zhou awoke without understanding.

    Upon arriving in Hangzhou, a friend of his, who had died three years ago, came to mind. Zhou was deeply disheartened, not only unable to travel north but also ashamed to return to Jiangdong. He then stayed in Hangzhou for two months, but his travel funds were depleted, and the innkeeper was about to evict him. At that time, he gnashed his heart and wept, feeling that life was no longer worth living. Drunk, he left the city and reached the center of the lake, where he saw a deep green area and leaped into the water. At first, he did not feel sinking, feeling as if he were floating in the air. Then he felt as if he were carrying a heavy load, thinking he was almost dead. When he raised his eyes, he saw himself caught in a net and was rescued by a fisherman. Zhou revived, thinking he had not drowned and had misjudged his time of death, and he cursed the fisherman loudly. The fisherman could not explain, and he took the net and admitted guilt before leaving. Zhou then dived back into the water, and he felt someone pulling his queue and lifting him up, placing him in a pavilion. Zhou revived again and saw a monk who was strong and sinewy, holding prayer beads, and sitting in the lotus position on the ground. Zhou said nothing, only staring intently. The monk said, "You are a good man, what great necessity makes you sink and not return?" Zhou groaned, wept, and recounted the whole story. The monk stood up and said, "Why not follow this poor monk?" Zhou followed and climbed onto a small boat.

    139

    僧本从五台来，字超然，卓锡于水仙庵者。周自此居庵中，僧见其能书，遂令其写经十余部。僧一日谓周曰：“求利于市，求名于朝。足下何不作京中游，以图进取？老衲于都，颇多熟识，当为书致某喇嘛寺僧，自能为足下谋一居停也。”并厚赠周。

    Explanation

    139

    The monk, whose name was Ben, came from Mount Wutai and settled in the Water Fairy Temple. Zhou, who lived in the temple, was known for his calligraphy, and the monk commissioned him to write over ten Buddhist scriptures. One day, the monk said to Zhou, "Why not travel to the capital to seek profit and fame? This old monk has many acquaintances in the capital. I will write to a lama temple there, and they will surely arrange for you a place to stay." The monk then generously gave gifts to Zhou.

    140

    周抵京，某喇嘛遂为图于某王府佐领下一拨什库作冬烘生。一日，周与东家至王府闲游，王归问门内者为谁？告以某拨什之延师。王呼周见，大喜，曰：“我有小贝勒，命尔傅之。”周谢出，后遂入王府为贝勒傅。会考博学宏词，命之应试。遂蒙擢用，授词林。
    逾年，督学闽省，假归省墓，盛仪卫，过岳门而不入焉。至某役家，登堂拜其夫妇。三年差满，复馆职。逢上命修明史，周所签分烈传，恰当得忠肃公名下。周始悟当年祈梦之征，于是尽心搜罗校纂，是传称详确焉。
    (余于庚申秋，梦青龙在天，群鸡绕地。次年，获祧楚北，以为佳兆。何官运坎廪一至于此？岂尚有转机者乎？^#^)

    青阳
    安徽青阳县，国初至乾隆年，从无甲第一人。有汪生邑庠某，发奋为雄，下帷攻苦。然十年文场，三战三北。汪之壮志亦全灰于鸡鸣夜雨时矣。

    Explanation

    140

    Zhou arrived in the capital, and a certain lama arranged for him to become a tutor for a low-ranking official under the control of a prince's household. One day, Zhou accompanied his employer to visit the prince's residence. When the prince returned and asked who was inside, he was told that it was the tutor for the low-ranking official. The prince summoned Zhou and was greatly pleased, saying, "I have a young prince; I order you to teach him." Zhou thanked and left, and later entered the prince's household to serve as the tutor for the young prince. When the opportunity arose for the examination for the highest degree in literature and rhetoric, the prince ordered Zhou to take the test. Zhou was then promoted and appointed to the literary elite.

    After a year, Zhou was appointed as the supervisor of education in Fujian province. He took leave to return home to visit the ancestral graves, accompanied by a grand procession. He passed by his father-in-law's gate without entering. Upon reaching his father-in-law's house, he went into the hall and paid respects to his father-in-law and mother-in-law. After three years of service, he returned to his position in the academy. When the emperor ordered the compilation of the Ming History, Zhou was assigned to compile the biographies of loyal and valiant figures, and he was specifically given the task of writing the biography of the "Zhong Su" official. Zhou then realized that his earlier dream was a sign or omen, and he diligently collected, edited, and compiled the biography, which is noted for its detail and accuracy.

    (In the autumn of the year Gengshen, I dreamed of a blue dragon in the sky, with a flock of chickens circling the earth. The following year, I was promoted to a position in the northern part of Chu, which I considered a good omen. How could my career path be so difficult? Could there still be a turning point?)

    Qingyang
    In Qingyang county, Anhui province, from the founding of the Qing dynasty to the reign of Emperor Qianlong, there was never a top-ranked scholar. There was a man named Wang, a student in the local school, who was determined to excel and studied diligently. However, over ten years of taking the imperial examinations, he failed three times. Wang's ambition and spirit were completely extinguished by the sound of chickens crowing in the rain at night.

    141

    有年春宵，汪生忽梦揭榜中式本年二十一名举人，醒而异之。晨告同人，佥曰：“汪生抱屈已久，且吾邑素无科第，若有必当推汪。今汪生既有登科之梦，即不得以登科作梦论，是直登科而已矣。”遂传合邑，咸以为兆。而汪生族党乃设大礼于宗祠，树旗横匾，贺者接踵。
    会安抚道出青阳，过汪氏之祠。见门贴报单，异之。问汪姓，以梦告。抚以为妄，饬邑令止之。及秋，抚军监临闱事，填榜之夕，将拆弥封。至二十一名，抚军起言于主试者曰：“下官春巡，道经青阳，见有汪姓祠堂贺新举人者，并署二十一名。问其故，则以梦征。今拆号至此，恐前日之托于梦者，或今日之不免于贿也。”主试者曰：“是不难，力破其关节。请易之。”乃取其备荐各卷，悉阵抚前，抚喜，信手拈出一卷。主试填注中式，及拆弥封，恰是青阳汪某。抚大骇，至公堂上，无不以为大奇。自汪姓一第之后，青阳科甲至今不绝。

    141

    A certain spring night, Wang gave a dream in which he saw the list of the twenty-first successful candidate this year. He awoke and found it strange. In the morning, he told his peers, who all said, "Wang has been wronged for a long time, and our county has never had a successful candidate. If there is one, it should be Wang. Now that Wang has had a dream of passing the examination, it cannot be considered just a dream; it is as if he has already passed the examination." The news spread throughout the county, and all believed it to be a good omen. Wang's relatives and clan members then set up a grand ceremony in the ancestral hall, raised flags and hung banners, and people came to congratulate in a continuous stream.

    Just then, the governor passed through Qingyang and went to Wang's ancestral temple. Seeing a notice posted on the door, he found it strange. He asked about the Wang family, and Wang told him about the dream. The governor considered it absurd and ordered the county magistrate to stop it. When autumn came, the governor oversaw the examination. On the night of filling the list, he was about to open the sealed envelopes. When he reached the twenty-first name, he spoke up to the chief examiner, saying, "Your servant during the spring tour, I passed through Qingyang and saw a Wang family temple celebrating a new successful candidate, with the number twenty-one written on it. When I asked why, they said it was based on a dream. Now that we have reached this point in the list, I am afraid that the one who relied on the dream the other day may be involved in bribery today." The chief examiner said, "It is not difficult. We can break the connections. Please change it." Then, he took out the recommended scrolls, all arranged before the governor. The governor was pleased and randomly picked one scroll. The chief examiner filled in the successful candidate, and when the seal was opened, it turned out to be Wang from Qingyang. The governor was greatly shocked. On the public hall, everyone found it extremely strange. After Wang passed the examination, Qingyang has had continuous success in the imperial examinations.

    142

    夫汪生未第之前，何其难；既第之后，又何以如此联翩接踵之易？当其初，先兆以梦而群相和之者，翻不以梦视梦，而且以真视梦，又何其愚？是不惟汪生愚，一邑之中皆愚也。迨至朱衣一点，天下知名，然后叹物造之于人亦巧矣。

    玉钩形
    浙之辛得仁廪生，馆于富家。岁暮解馆，得束脯八金。归至渡口，见夫妇二人痛哭岸旁。辛问之，夫诉云：“岁将尽，责逋者日逼于门，欲卖此妇。妇又不愿弃买臣而去，甘心与冀缺同终。不得已相率赴水耳。”辛恻然，尽与八金。二人泣谢，辛负担晚归，言遇妇投水事。妻曰：“何不周之？”辛曰：“倾囊与之矣。”妻亦甚欣。

    Explanation

    142

    Before Wang passed the imperial examinations, how difficult it was; after he passed, how easy it became for him to achieve success one after another! When he first began, the signs of his future success were foretold in dreams, and everyone around him agreed, yet they did not treat the dreams as mere dreams, but rather as if they were real. How foolish were they! It was not only Wang who was foolish, but the entire town was as well. When he finally received the red robe (a symbol of passing the imperial examinations), the world recognized his talent, and then he marveled at the skill of fate.

    The shape of a jade hook.

    A scholar named Xin Deren from Zhejiang, who was a student in a wealthy household, was paid eight gold pieces at the end of the year when his teaching contract ended. On his way back to the ferry, he saw a couple crying by the riverside. He asked them why they were crying, and the husband explained, "The year is coming to an end, and our creditors are pressing us for payment. We want to sell this woman. But she refuses to leave with Bi Gan (a historical figure known for his loyalty) and is willing to die with Ji Que (a historical figure known for his wisdom) rather than live in dishonor. So, we have no choice but to jump into the water together." Moved by their plight, Xin gave them all the eight gold pieces. The couple thanked him tearfully, and Xin returned home late at night, recounting the incident to his wife. His wife asked, "Why didn't you help them more?" Xin replied, "I gave them all the money I had." His wife was also very pleased.

    143

    至除夕，不能给。妻出红裙一条，以贳酒食。辛口占曰：“红裙沽清酒。”妻曰：“黑箸蘸白盐。”相与欢宴，初不知床头米罄，灶底烟寒也。夜梦至一处，琼楼玉宇，有联续其句曰：“关关金锁户，卷卷玉钩帘。”辛觉，书于壁间。
    明春赴馆，居停延地师葬母。辛以二亲未殡，请师留意。至一处，见鹿卧地，人至奔去。师曰：“此金锁玉钩形，吉地也。”因忆与梦合，但不知为谁氏地。适前与金人之至，见辛曰：“先生得非恩人乎？自得金完债，今稍温饱。常念未能报德，今何事至此？”辛言求葬地，指鹿眠处。人曰：“此吾业也。”即邀至家，愿书契与之，且为助工营葬。数年后，辛联捷，官至都宪。

    Explanation

    143

    By the end of the year, Xin could not afford to provide for the family. His wife brought out a red skirt and exchanged it for wine and food. Xin improvised a verse: "Red skirt buys clear wine." His wife responded: "Black chopsticks dipped in white salt." They enjoyed a feast together, initially unaware that the rice in the bed was gone and the fire in the stove was cold.

    That night, he dreamed of a place with jade towers and crystal palaces. There was a couplet continuing the verse: "The door is locked with golden locks, the curtain is rolled with jade hooks." Xin woke up and wrote it on the wall.

    In the following spring, he went to the academy. The host invited a geomancer to bury his mother. Xin, because his parents had not yet been buried, asked the geomancer to pay attention. They arrived at a place where a deer was lying down, and when people approached, it ran away. The geomancer said, "This is the shape of a golden lock and jade hook, an auspicious site." He remembered that it matched his dream, but he did not know whose land it was. Just then, a person named Jin arrived and saw Xin. He said, "Sir, aren't you my benefactor? Since I received the gold to pay off my debt, I am now slightly better off. I often think I have not been able to repay your kindness, so why are you here now?" Xin explained that he was looking for a burial site and pointed to where the deer was lying. The person said, "This is my business." He immediately invited him home, willing to sign a contract with him and also help with the work of the burial.

    Several years later, Xin succeeded in the imperial examinations and became an official up to the rank of Grand Censor.

    144

    亦畅园芝鹤小辨
    吾乡庶常孙某，早年读书其妇翁亦畅园中。夏日有芝一本，生于斋。未几，有鹤止斋前树。既赴省而宿，桂齐花。北方桂隔年不花，仅有叶在，而庶常是科果捷。乙未联榜，人遂以妇家之瑞瑞其倩。
    噫！何不瑞其子，而瑞其倩？宁若芝若鹤若桂，庶常斋头独不当位置一本耶？

    瓮走
    莱阳张允捷，丙子入秋闱。家中注水瓮自门外走入门里，旋转如篷，格磔有声。见者惊视，瓮忽爆碎，水流满地。方取土覆之，而报中者已轰于门。
    (贺康侯云：某年月日，在江宁宴其亲家某姓书斋。亭午，见其院中山石响走丈余，同人群集往观。初亦无异，后其家自败云。)

    Explanation

    144

    A Brief Discussion on the Luminous Garden, Orchid, and Crane

    My hometown's scholar-official, Mr. Sun, used to study in the Luminous Garden of his father-in-law's house when he was young. During the summer, an orchid sprouted in his study. Soon after, a crane perched on a tree in front of the study. After he went to the provincial capital to take the exam and stayed overnight, the osmanthus flower bloomed. In the north, the osmanthus tree does not bloom every year; only the leaves remained, but Mr. Sun indeed passed the exam that year. In the year of Yiwèi, he was listed on the imperial examination results, and people attributed the auspicious signs to his wife's family.

    Ah! Why were the auspicious signs not attributed to his son, but to his wife's family? How could the orchid, the crane, and the osmanthus not have a place in Mr. Sun's study?

    A Moving Water Jar

    In the year of Bingzi, Zhang Yunjie entered the provincial examination. At home, a water jar filled with water moved from outside to inside the house, spinning like a sail and making a sound. Those who saw it were startled, and the jar suddenly exploded, spilling water all over the ground. Just as they were covering the broken jar with soil, the news of Zhang Yunjie's success came.

    (He Kanghou said: On a certain day in the year, he was dining in the study of a relative in Jiangning. At noon, he saw a mountain stone in the courtyard move several feet, and a crowd gathered to watch. Initially, there was nothing unusual, but later the family declined.)

    145

    卷五 艺文部
    文酒
    蜀人万秋池，工诗文，豪于饮。饮少，为诗文辄艰涩，饮能尽其量，则下笔有神，文异水而涌泉矣。穷于遇，世无知者。值闱中酒禁綦严，不得携杯酌往，一畅其志，遂屡踬名场。然性酣曲蘖不顾也。继且饮资匮乏，无所为谋，往往去衣去食。
    一日游郊外，见一人坐石上，倚巨罂，瓢而饮，酣笑自得，旁若无人。万涎之，曰：“饮士无伴，孤哉孤哉！”其人曰：“子欲饮乎？先酬以文。”万曰：“身将饮，焉用文之？”乃假瓢而吸，顷刻告罄。万呼曰：“酒之兴也，其于中古乎？饮酒者，其有忧患乎？屈原宜醉而独醒，故沉汩罗而不悔；李白宜醒而长醉，故溺采石而不辞。山石之贵，吾弗为之；嵇康之祸，庶几免失。阮籍胸中块垒，自取浇焉；刘伶醉后吱唔，妻难戒也。谢朏告弟，此中惟宜饮酒；袁种谓盎，但云日饮几何。古之人皆然，如之何而不饮？予岂好饮哉？予不得已也。”其人喜曰：“饮者也！”

    Explanation

    145

    Translation:

    Volume Five: The Department of Literature and Arts
    Literature and Wine

    A man from Shu named Wan Qiu Chi was skilled in poetry and prose, and he excelled in drinking. When he drank little, his writing became stiff and awkward; but when he drank to his full capacity, he wrote with divine inspiration, and his words flowed like water from a spring. Despite his talent, he was unfortunate in his opportunities, and the world did not know him. During the strict prohibition on wine in the examination halls, he was unable to bring a cup of wine to quench his thirst and fulfill his aspirations, leading to repeated failures in the imperial examinations. Nevertheless, his passion for wine remained undiminished. Later, he found himself lacking the means to drink, often sacrificing his clothes and food to do so.

    One day, while walking in the outskirts, he saw a man sitting on a stone, leaning against a large jar, drinking with a ladle. The man was laughing heartily, seemingly oblivious to anyone around him. Wan Qiu Chi was drawn to him and said, "A drinker without companions, how lonely you are!" The man replied, "Do you wish to drink? First, pay with a piece of writing." Wan Qiu Chi said, "I am about to drink, why do I need to write?" The man then handed him a ladle, and Wan Qiu Chi drank it all in a moment. He exclaimed, "The joy of wine, could it be from the Middle Ages? Do those who drink wine have concerns? Qu Yuan should have been drunk but remained sober, so he drowned in the Miluo River without regret; Li Bai should have been sober but remained drunk, so he drowned in Cai Shi without hesitation. The value of rocks, I would not seek; the disaster of Ji Kang, perhaps I can avoid. Ruan Ji had inner turmoil, which he relieved by drinking; Liu Ling, after getting drunk, muttered, and his wife found it hard to stop him. Xie Fei advised his brother that this was the only suitable activity; Yuan Zhong asked about the amount of wine consumed daily. All the ancients were like this, how could I not drink? Am I fond of drinking? I cannot help it." The man was pleased and said, "You are indeed a drinker!"

    146

    遂与订交，问其姓名，曰：“公孙氏，字伯雅。”期以诘朝，相与痛饮。如是者，常相过从，遂无虚日。公孙问万曰：“有舍宇否？”万曰：“聊蔽风雨。”公孙曰：“我当移樽就教，庶几卜昼而兼卜夜也。”是夕，伯雅至。万曰：“我贫不能为酒，奈何？”伯雅指几上何书，万曰：“醉中草耳。”伯雅展读，至《红梅花赋》，曰：“此篇可酿一罂，以尽今宵之乐。”万不之信。

    Explanation

    146

    He then became friends with him, asking for his name. He replied, "I am of the Gong Sun clan, and my courtesy name is Bo Ya." They agreed to meet the next morning and drink together. Thus, they often visited each other, meeting every day. Gong Sun asked Wan, "Do you have a place to stay?" Wan replied, "Just a modest shelter from wind and rain." Gong Sun said, "I will come to your place to seek instruction, and we can plan for both day and night activities." That night, Bo Ya arrived. Wan said, "I am too poor to provide wine, what shall we do?" Bo Ya pointed to a book on the table and asked, "What is this?" Wan replied, "It was just scribbled in a drunken state." Bo Ya read it and reached the Red Plum Blossom Ode, saying, "This piece can be brewed into a large jar of wine to enjoy the full joy of tonight." Wan did not believe him.

    147

    公孙令汲泉一器，投以赋稿，斟之杯中，沉碧芳香，不同凡酒。万狂喜，味之微觉酸苦。伯雅曰：“苦为上，辣次之，酸又次之，甜斯下矣。然亦足药为文之病也。”万曰：“古人之文，胜我者多，皆可为用乎？”伯雅曰：“为陈言务去之，其精气皆久耗矣。足下文，只一半可用，余则糟粃，即成之，亦索然无味耳。虽然，靠此区区心血，安能填我二人溪壑？且有旨酒，必得佳肴，焉得瓮中常满，杯底不空，取不穷而用不竭，若水之原原来？计惟以是子母权之，乃可为常，否则锦囊不足恃也。”

    Explanation

    147

    Gong Sun ordered a single container to be filled with spring water, into which he threw a draft of a poem or prose. He then poured it into a cup, and the liquid became dark green and fragrant, unlike ordinary wine. Mr. Wan was greatly delighted, but tasting it, he slightly felt a sour and bitter taste. Mr. Boya said, "Bitterness is the best, spiciness comes next, sourness follows, and sweetness is the lowest. However, it is also sufficient to cure the ailments of writing." Mr. Wan asked, "There are many ancient writings better than mine, can they all be used?" Mr. Boya replied, "To remove all old cliches, their essence and vitality have long been exhausted. Your writing is only half usable, the rest is dross, even if completed, it will be tasteless. However, relying on such a small amount of effort, how can it satisfy our great desires? And for good wine, there must be fine food, how can a jar always be full, a cup never empty, taken without end and used without exhaustion, like the source of water? The only way is to use this as a mother and child relationship, then it can be sustained, otherwise, a treasure bag is not reliable."

    148

    公孙乃于临市筑一小楼，挂青帘焉。一时沽者饮者接踵相望，咸啧啧为饮中第一之楼。夜则二人杯盆错杂，倚栏豪吟，相与枕藉乎其中。偶有佳作，即成醇醴。伯雅又以二人寂寞，呼弟仲雅、季雅至，从此四痤不虚，满浮大白。尝于更阑月上，谈宴衷怀，无不倾倒。仲雅忽曰：“万兄年四十，尚未占凤。吾有一婢，名婪春，年及笄，颇不俗恶，诚未敢以文君自诩，但作当炉人甚妙，更善酿事。”万起谢。逾夕，季雅携一婢来，见万展拜。万见女美无伦比，真如荷粉露垂，杏花烟润，嫣然欲绝。万就内寝，伯雅楼居。仲季时往来其间。婪春自入厨后，司酒政，指点渑醑，法无不备。又或投以名花，杂以异香，液为琼玉，滴以珍珠，并各标题名目。有一种丰亭白者，昧之多脂粉香，此婪眷自为也。婪春曰：“吾之为酒也，砚田以种之，墨池以漉之，笔花以灌之，书仓以储之。又使刘，李诸仙，拍浮其中，岂仅淳于、高阳之徒狂饮一石哉？”

    Explanation

    148

    Gong Sun built a small pavilion near the market and hung a green curtain. People buying and drinking were so many that they were shoulder to shoulder, and all praised it as the best place for drinking. At night, the two would have cups and dishes mixed together, lean on the railing and recite poetry, and lie together in a disordered manner. When there was a good poem, it was like fine wine. Bo Ya, seeing the two were lonely, invited his brothers Zhong Ya and Ji Ya. From then on, the four of them were never empty-handed, always raising their cups to drink. They often talked and drank late into the night, sharing their deepest feelings, and everyone was deeply impressed. Zhong Ya suddenly said, "Brother Wan, at forty, you still haven't married. I have a maid named Lan Chun, who is of marriageable age, and she is not bad. I don't dare to compare her to Wen Jun, but she is very good at brewing wine and managing the household." Wan thanked him. The next day, Ji Ya brought a maid to see Wan. Wan saw the girl, who was beautiful beyond compare, like a lotus flower with dew droplets, an apricot blossom in misty light, and she was so charming that she seemed about to melt away. Wan went to the inner room, and Bo Ya stayed in the pavilion. Zhong Ya and Ji Ya often came and went among them. After entering the kitchen, Lan Chun took charge of the wine, pointing out the best wines, and all methods were available. Sometimes she added famous flowers and exotic fragrances, making the liquid like jade and pearls, and each had its own title. There was a type called Feng Ting Bai, which had a scent of powder and perfume, made by Lan Chun herself. Lan Chun said, "My wine is made by planting it in the ink fields, filtering it in the ink pools, watering it with ink flowers, and storing it in the book warehouses. Even the immortals of Liu and Li play in it. How could it be just for the likes of Chun Yu and Gao Yang to drink a stone of it?"

    149

    一日，楼中有饮者至，豹头虬须，呼酒频频，几尽百盏。既而使酒骂坐，拍案惊人。时伯雅已作醉乡侯，闻喧出曰：“何物伧楚，饮吾酩而噂沓为？”饮者掀须曰：“吾饮乎尔，敢醉吾乎？”伯雅倾倚而前，欲与之较。饮者大吼，奋拳一击，伯雅仆地，成一铜爵。饮者怀之，下楼欲去。季仲趋而出，跪乞其还。饮者怒，从袖中掷出石阶上，铛然裂而为二。仲、季惊惧，亦遂杳然。回顾饮者，已不知其所往。万拾爵归而合之，款识完好，上有箴曰：“无怒恶，无思虑。辑尔颜，柔尔气。君子欢焉，小人是戾。汉初平三年，伯雅之箴。”万告婪春，婪春泣曰：“物之成毁，各有其时。乃知一齐彭殇，皆为妄作。伯雅其亡乎！”乃作醮祭而招之，曰：

    Explanation

    149

    One day, a drinker arrived at a building, with a tiger-like head and twisted beard. He called for wine repeatedly and nearly finished a hundred cups. Soon, he became drunk and began cursing the company, slamming the table to startle everyone. At that time, Boya had already become the Lord of Drunkenness. Hearing the commotion, he came out and said, "What kind of uncouth barbarian are you, drinking my wine and making a mess?" The drinker raised his beard and said, "I drink my own wine, how dare you get drunk?" Boya leaned forward, intending to argue with him. The drinker roared and struck him with a clenched fist, causing Boya to fall to the ground and transform into a copper vessel. The drinker carried it and went down the stairs, wanting to leave. Ji Zhong hurriedly went out and knelt, begging him to return. The drinker, angered, threw the vessel onto the stone steps, where it rang and split into two. Ji Zhong and Ji Qi were filled with fear and shock, and they also vanished. Looking back at the drinker, he was already gone. Wan picked up the vessel and brought it back, then put it together. The inscription was intact, and on it was a saying: "Do not be angry or evil, do not be anxious or worried. Smooth your face, soften your breath. The gentleman will be happy, the small person will be angry. In the third year of the Chuping era of the Han dynasty, Boya's admonition." Wan told Lan Chun, who cried and said, "The formation and destruction of things each have their own time. Thus knowing that equating longevity and short life is a mistake, are all false. Is Boya dead?" Wan then performed an offering and summoned him, saying:

    150

    呜呼！伯雅，尔为才子，奈何碎首于阁下；尔非美女，奈何坠身于楼头？尔何不邀鸿门之赐，而适类钓台之会？奈之何濡首不戒，腐胁痛伤。我登糟邱之上，呼曰：魂归来兮，吾知其一滴九泉，举杯对月而骑鲸。吁嗟乎！如范亚父之撞玉斗，岂淮南王之遗金臼？
    后仲雅、季雅皆绝迹焉。万诘婪，婪亦不答。
    婪育一子，名衡，亦能传其业。万年七十四卒。婪送葬之墓，哭于旁，遂殁。咸以为仙。次年春，万墓旁发芍药一枝，洁白可爱，名为婪尾春者是也。至今狁传成都佳酿，盖万之遗制与。

    Explanation

    150

    Alas! Ber Ya, you were a talented man, how can it be that your head was crushed beneath the hall; you were not a beautiful woman, how can it be that your body fell from the top of the tower? Why did you not seek a favor like at the Feast of Hongmen, and instead achieve a peaceful resolution like at the fishing platform? How could it be that you failed to heed warnings, leading to severe suffering and pain? I climbed to the top of the wine cellar, calling out: "Soul, return! I know that even a single drop will reach the underworld, so I raise my cup to the moon and ride the whale." Ah! Like Fan Zeng's attempt to strike Liu Bang with a jade goblet, how could it be compared to the forced suicide of the King of Huainan? Afterward, Zhong Ya and Ji Ya both disappeared. Wan questioned Lan, and Lan did not answer. Lan raised a son named衡, who could also carry on his father's legacy. Wan, at the age of seventy-four, passed away. Lan attended his funeral, wept beside his tomb, and then died as well. All believed him to have become a deity. The following spring, a branch of a white and lovely peony sprouted from Wan's grave, named "Lanwei Chun," symbolizing the last remnants of spring. To this day, the fine wines of Chengdu continue to be passed down, a legacy of Wan's craftsmanship.

    151

    梦花记摘略
    乙酉岁，史子小峰馆授生徒。课业之余，摊书藜床，梦游一山，芙蓉秀削。循麓陟登，抵山腰。缭垣袤延，朱扉洞开。仰视额书悬云际，为“碧落九层天源”。疑古刹也。入观之，假山面起，青童倚立，顾笑无言。又过溪湾，清泚水，虢虢鸣，度略彴。中起大殿，西转精舍，翳修竹，题“紫萼房”。踅入，阒无人。设铅椠。旋闻弹指声，窥之，一小鬟，徒倚阶除，冉冉殿后去。蹑其迹，随而入。宏敞深邃，旁夹花树。循中迤逦行，遥望隆楼杰阁，磊块崇敞。堂中二姝，对弈门角。女侍若招手状。二姝睨而弗动。及檐，榜曰“定宫延史”。入见，其灵颜玉莹，真天人也。室东西壁，牙签插架，目不给赏。旭彩射晶，窗光灿灿照人。几玉钩金，琉璃砚盒，翡翠笔床，精丽不类人世。案头铺笺，题一七律云：
    画帘不启旧朱门，谁向春衫问泪痕。自是冯元生命簿，何劳宋玉赋招魂。

    Explanation

    151

    Dream of the Lotus: A Brief Account

    In the year of Yiyou (1905), Shi Zixiao, known as Xiaofeng, served as a tutor to his students. After completing his lessons, he would spread his books on a bed of millet stalks and, in his dreams, wander through a mountain where lotus flowers were elegant and slender. He followed the path up the mountain, reaching its waist. The winding walls extended far, and the red doors stood wide open. Looking up, he saw a signboard hanging high in the sky, inscribed with "Biluo Jiu Ceng Tianyuan" (Azure Sky Nine Layers Heavenly Source). He suspected it to be an ancient temple. Upon entering, he saw artificial hills rising up, with young boys leaning against them, smiling without speaking. He passed a stream where clear and cold water flowed, making a gentle sound as he crossed a small bridge. In the middle stood a grand hall, and to the west, a small pavilion surrounded by tall bamboo, inscribed with "Zi'e Fang" (Purple Calyx Room). He entered, finding it quiet and empty, and set up his writing tools. Suddenly, he heard the sound of snapping fingers. Looking around, he saw a young girl leaning idly by the steps, slowly moving away behind the hall. He followed her trail and entered. The interior was spacious and deep, with flowers and trees on the sides. As he walked along, he saw tall and majestic buildings, grand and open. In the hall, two young women were playing chess in the corner of the door. A female attendant seemed to beckon, but the two women looked aside without moving. He reached the eaves, where a signboard read "Dinggong Yan Shi" (Settled Palace Inviting History). He entered and saw a face that was radiant and pure, truly a celestial being. The walls on both sides of the room were lined with books, their labels inserted into the shelves, too many to take in with the eyes. Sunlight shone through crystal, and the window light shone brightly. A desk with a gold hook, a glass inkstone box, and a jade pen holder, all exquisitely beautiful, unlike anything in the mortal world. On the desk, paper was spread out, and a seven-character regulated verse was written:
    "Drawn curtains do not open the old vermilion gate, who asks of the spring robe for traces of tears? It is the fate of Feng Yuan to have a thin destiny, why bother to compose a poem to summon the soul?"

    152

    森森暮雨花犹落，草草西风日易昏。多感跫音相过赏，此身虽死性常存。
    款书“延陵花史吴慕娥”。心异之，询焉。
    娥敛衽曰：“妾恨人也，幼随任会稽。十二父死，旋归苏阆。慈母爱女如珠，阿兄挥金如土。不数年，家资荡尽，计难全活。适有驻钺之武夫，纳为小星，获金八百。妾不羞为下贱者，为母兄计耳。谁知一入侯门，便成苦海。远人之别泪未干，狮子之吼声顿起。从此朝朝暮暮，尽是愁魔；月月花花，都无颜色。百年薄命，片刻秋风；一缕芳魂，半场春梦。既已不乐有生，宁复悲夫就死。红炉焰烬，伤弱骨之能灰；白练丝长，悼幽情之未泯。”娥又吟二绝云：
    一纸西风薄命词，空怜藕折短于丝。伤心泪渍黄泉水，六十娘今没女儿。
    又云：日暮空山海气昏，野篱零落水为村。片风吹散朝云影，不必闲寻觅返魂。
    前一姝对弈者号冰夫人，把诗观玩，微吟云：秋水盈盈写泪痕，春山淡淡锁愁魂。无端题起伤心事，肠断江南乌桕村。

    Explanation

    152

    The dense evening rain still causes the flowers to fall, the grassy west wind makes the day easily darken. One often feels grateful for the footsteps that pass by to admire, even though this body may die, the nature or essence remains.

    Written by Wu Mu'e, the Flower Historian of Yanling. I found it strange and asked about it.

    Wu Mu'e bowed and said, "I am a woman of sorrow. I was young when I followed my father to Shaoxing. At twelve, my father died, and I returned to Suzhou. My loving mother cherished me like a pearl, while my elder brother spent money like dirt. Within a few years, our family wealth was depleted, and it became difficult to survive. Fortunately, a military officer took me as his concubine, and I received eight hundred coins. I do not feel ashamed to be a lowly concubine; it was for the sake of my mother and brother. Who knew that entering the noble household would turn into a sea of suffering. The tears of parting from distant loved ones had not dried when the roar of the lion suddenly arose. From then on, every day and night were filled with sorrow; every month and flower lost their color. A life of short-lived fate, a moment of autumn wind; a fragrant soul, a half-dream of spring. Since I no longer enjoy living, why should I grieve over dying. The red furnace's flame has turned to ash, injuring the weak bones; the white silk is long, mourning the deep emotions that have not faded."

    Wu Mu'e then recited two verses:

    A single sheet of paper written in the west wind, a fate of thin destiny. In vain, I pity the broken lotus root shorter than silk. Tears of sorrow stain the yellow spring water, the sixty-year-old woman now has no daughter.

    Another verse: At dusk, the empty mountain and sea are shrouded in mist, the wild fence is scattered, and the water forms a village. A gust of wind disperses the morning cloud shadows, there is no need to search for a return to life.

    The woman who was playing chess before, known as Ice Lady, held the poem and admired it, softly reciting: The autumn water, full and clear, writes the traces of tears; the spring mountains, faint and hazy, lock the soul of sorrow. Without reason, the writing brings up sad memories, breaking the heart in the southern village of black alders.

    153

    复赠史子小峰一绝云：
    孤桐山下老名家，憔悴穷经鬓有华。黄叶江南秋水句，吟毫依约梦中花。
    小峰答云：
    姓氏仙班定几家，红笺佳句扫铅华。可怜一笑拚憔悴，徒对春风咏落花。
    冰夫人谓慕娥曰：“今日众姊妹相邀赴会，午后当回，可往矣。”小峰云：“会已，可能偕过小齐乎？”曰：“可耳。”
    小峰辞出，飘忽至一所。层楼面水，晶帘螺槅，掩映交辉。回忆前约，犹在目前，怦怦冀望，坐以待之。俄异香馥郁，众姝联袂至，霞裳云裾，绮丽非常。小峰屏息晋接焉。娥瑰姿艳逸，翩若惊鸿，宓妃之出洛波也。一冷艳，全输幽芳自赏，号素仙，即冰夫人也。一为椒青，姿容美丽，如蕉乍粉，柳舒眉，花睡初醒。一则小鬟鸦青，婉媚有林下风致，为谢妙香，侍立娥侧。既坐，众姝即事联句。各书姓名，起句云：
    一炉香篆袅于花(谢妙香)，隐映群仙出绛霞(椒青)。题句曾留芝液馆(梅素仙)，生香不断蔡经家(吴慕娥)。

    Explanation

    153

    A Final Translation of the Source Text

    In response to Shi Zixiao Feng, he composed a quatrain:

    An old scholar from under the Solitary Pine Mountain,
    Worn out from studying the classics, with graying hair.
    Yellow leaves and autumn water in Jiangnan,
    The brush strokes of poetry are like flowers in a dream.

    Xiao Feng replied:

    How many families have surnames in the celestial registry?
    Beautiful verses written on red paper, sweeping away the mundane.
    Pitifully willing to sacrifice one's weariness for a smile,
    Only to stand in the spring breeze and sing of falling flowers.

    Madame Bing said to Mu'e, "Today the sisters invited me to a gathering. I will return after noon. You may go." Xiao Feng said, "After the meeting, can we go together to Xiao Qi's place?" She replied, "It is possible."

    Xiao Feng left, and in a moment, arrived at a place. A multi-story building faced the water, with crystal curtains and spiral partitions, shading and reflecting each other in mutual brilliance. Recalling the previous agreement, it was still vivid in his mind, with a fluttering hope, he sat and waited. Suddenly, a fragrant aroma filled the air, and the beautiful women came together, their robes of clouds and gossamer, very splendid. Xiao Feng held his breath and greeted them. Mu'e was graceful and elegant, like a startled swan, like the goddess of the Luo River emerging from the waves. One was cold and beautiful, entirely lost in her own subtle fragrance, called the White Immortal, that is Madame Bing. One was Pea Green, with beautiful appearance, like a banana just powdered, willow eyebrows relaxed, and flowers just waking from sleep. One was a small maid with a black hairpin, graceful and charming with the elegance of a forest, Xie Miaoxiang, standing by her side. After sitting down, the beautiful women immediately composed verses based on the occasion. Each wrote their name, and started with a particular verse:

    A censer of incense curls around the flower (Xie Miaoxiang),
    Shading and reflecting each other in mutual brilliance, the immortals emerge from the crimson clouds (Pea Green). Verses once left at the Hall of Jade Dew (Mei Suxian),
    Fragrance never ceases at Cai Jing's house (Wu Mu'e).

    154

    名山得会还驱鹤(梅素仙)，胜地相逢且住车(妙香)。遮莫闲谈消永昼(椒青)，笑看红日又西斜(慕娥)。
    众姝咏罢，翩翩皆起，梅、椒先去，吴与妙留，娥复书词一首：
    无端说起沧桑事，谈笑共嬉游。山中博果，花前索句，竹里弹楸。/九馨宫阙，三清玉宇，百尺琼楼。半漳秋水，千层峭壁，数点云流。
    调寄《人月圆》。
    时恍惚有陈子香廊在侧。香廊已故，盖砚席友也。娥跌宕风流，性颇游戏，顾频与香廊谑。小峰曰：“何香浓而峰淡耶？”娥复书《孤鸾调》一词云：
    何须促迫，俺丹管花司，寒笙贵客。碎玉零香，不过游戏闲笔。博得数番酬唱，料先生，笑娥羞忒。便道是，香浓峰淡也，天青水白。/某从今去也，烟霞隔。再休问武陵，桃花颜色。看云笺缥缈，洒珠泪狼藉。一段灵风妙想，都教人，怎生消得。空留千秋佳话，落几行残墨。

    Explanation

    154

    In the renowned mountains, a reunion was held, and the crane was driven back (by Mei Suxian). At a famous place, they met and paused their carriages (by Miao Xiang). Perhaps idle talk would pass the long day (by Jiao Qing), and they smiled as they watched the red sun set (by Mu E).

    After the ladies finished their recitations, they all rose gracefully. Mei and Jiao left first, while Wu and Miao stayed behind. Mu E then wrote a poem:

    "Without reason, we spoke of the vicissitudes of life, laughing and playing together. In the mountains, we played fruit games, sought poetic lines by the flowers, and played chess in the bamboo grove. / Nine fragrant palaces, three pure celestial halls, a hundred-foot jade pavilion. Half the river in autumn water, a thousand layers of cliffs, a few points of cloud flow."

    Set to the tune of "Human and Moon Round."

    At that moment, there was a fleeting presence of Chen Zi Xianglang by the side. Xianglang was deceased, a friend of the inkstone and writing table. Mu E was free-spirited and elegant, with a playful nature, and often teased Xianglang. Xiao Feng said, "Why is the fragrance strong and the peak faint?" Mu E then wrote a poem in the tune of "Lonely Crane" in response:

    "Why rush? I am the red tube flower official, the noble guest of the cold bamboo flute. Crumbled jade and scattered fragrance, nothing more than playful and idle writing. I earned several rounds of mutual recitation, but the master must have laughed at me, too shy. Even if it were said, the fragrance is strong and the peak faint, the sky is blue and the water is white. / I depart from now on, separated by smoke and mist. No longer ask about Wuling, the color of peach blossoms. Watch the ethereal letters, scattered tears like pearls. A segment of spiritual and ingenious thoughts, how can they be exhausted? Only a thousand-year-old tale remains, a few lines of leftover ink."

    155

    小峰因问妙香何人。妙曰：“妾亦姑苏人，早折。遇娥，爱妾才姿，纳为常侍。因绮语致千仙戒，今已责谴无叶堂，皈依授记花坛。待海棠着雨再生枝耳。”小峰曰：“无叶堂安在？”妙香曰：“天无际，水无际，心生即是。”小峰曰：“花史佳咏，已香盈怀袖矣。何不一倾珠玉，光映后先。”妙云：“大巫在前，小巫自阻。”因集古咏对镜一绝云：
    卿须怜我我怜卿(小青)，午夜凭栏百感生(元稹)。碧落堂中钟定后(定空)，无人知是此时情(白傅)。
    自此啜茗清画，剪烛深宵，恍若数晨夕者然。
    一日，西池使来，捧赤锡召归。仍复旧职，为香案玉史。娥匆匆别，意甚不怿。妙香亦随行，既而妙香复回。小峰讶问之，妙曰：“权代理司花史，今在九馨宫，盘桓尚有日也。然异境离奇，遥念故山，忽忽既久，思归殊切。”小峰赠妙香云：

    Explanation

    155

    Xiaofeng then asked who Miaoxiang was. Miaoxiang replied, "I am also from Suzhou, but I died young. I met Lady E, who admired my talent and beauty, and took me in as a constant attendant. Because of my flowery speech, I was warned by the thousand immortals, and now I have been sent to the Leafless Hall, where I have taken refuge and received the Flower Terrace dharma seal. I am waiting for the rain to make the crabapple tree sprout new branches again." Xiaofeng said, "Where is the Leafless Hall?" Miaoxiang said, "Heaven has no boundaries, water has no boundaries, and the mind creates them." Xiaofeng said, "The beautiful verses of the Flower Historian have already filled my sleeves with fragrance. Why not pour out your pearls and jade, to shine upon both the past and the future?" Miaoxiang said, "The great sorcerer is ahead, and the small sorcerer naturally holds back." She then composed a verse inspired by ancient poetry, facing the mirror:

    "You must pity me, and I will pity you (Xiaoqing). At midnight, leaning on the railing, a hundred feelings arise (Yuan Zhen). After the bell in the Blue Sky Hall has settled (Dingkong), no one knows this feeling at this moment (Bai Juyi)."

    From then on, they drank tea, painted, and trimmed candles through the deep night, it felt as if they had spent many mornings and evenings together.

    One day, a messenger from the Western Pool came, holding a red tin to summon her back. She returned to her old position as the Perfumed Altar Jade Historian. Lady E left in a hurry, and she was very unhappy. Miaoxiang also followed, but soon she returned. Xiaofeng was surprised and asked her why. Miaoxiang said, "I am temporarily acting as the Flower Historian, and I am now in the Nine Fragrance Palace, where I will stay for a while longer. However, the strange and wondrous place, far from my hometown, has been a long time now, and my longing to return is very strong." Xiaofeng gave Miaoxiang a gift, saying:

    156

    博得飞琼下九霄，云窗雾阁话迢迢。华灯夜夜分娇面，风柳年年见细腰。
    一卷新词傅绿绮，经年苦忆觅红绡。眼前已恨蓬山隔，况是东西路几条。
    妙香即答云：
    风动长沙冷绛霄，乱山寒木路迢迢。已经碧海弹红泪，难把青山折素腰。
    鹤驾千巡归大域，灵幡一片荡轻绡。劳君诗思深如许，投别渐无珂玉条。
    又有菱粉者，妙香侍人也。家红桥，性俊逸。前此未之见，后往来通问，能诗，屈于主人，弗炫也。小峰强之，书一绝曰：
    露下银河海色苍，青鸾鹤背足徜徉。交梨火枣非吾好，逢着麻姑素酒尝。
    赞好诗，曰：“戏君耳。此录玉史旧作也。”字特工妙，索之，为书数纸。遒健秀媚。小峰立赠以诗，曰：
    叠叠飞来足几弓，杨家罗袜闪惊鸿。泥中雅羡康成婢，林下真披道韫风。
    瘦硬通神书最贵，娇羞作样眼偏空。佳人廿四桥边住，十二阑干亚字红。

    Explanation

    156

    Winning the flying jade from the ninth heaven, in the ethereal dwelling of cloud windows and misty pavilions, we converse on distant matters. The splendid lamps night after night divide the beautiful face, and the graceful willow trees year after year reveal the slender waist.
    A new scroll of poetry inscribed on green silk, suffering for years in longing to find the red silk. Even now, the sea of sorrows separates us; how much more so when there are countless paths east and west.
    Miao Xiang immediately replied:
    The wind stirs the cold crimson heavens of Changsha, through the winding mountains and cold woods, the path stretches far and wide. Already, red tears have been shed in the azure sea, and it is hard to bend the white waist against the green hills.
    The crane carriage makes a thousand rounds to return to the great realm, the spirit banner gently sways the light silk. Your poetic thoughts are so profound, farewell, the jade-like paths are fading.
    There is also Ling Fen, the attendant of Miao Xiang. From the Red Bridge, her nature is elegant and unrestrained. Until now, I had not seen her, but later we exchanged messages. She could compose poetry, but she remained humble and did not show off. Xiao Feng insisted on it, and she wrote an absolute verse:
    Dew falls on the Milky Way, the sea's color is deep and blue. On the back of the green phoenix and crane, enough to wander. Peaches and fire dates are not my preference, but when meeting Magu, I would taste the pure wine.
    Praising the good poem, she said, "I was just teasing you. This is a recording of an old work by the Jade Historian." Her writing is particularly skilled and exquisite. When asked, she wrote several sheets. The calligraphy is vigorous, elegant, and charming. Xiao Feng immediately gifted her a poem:
    The layers of clouds fly by, covering several bows. The Yang family's silk stockings flash like startled swans. In the mud, I admire the servant of Kang Cheng; in the woods, she truly embodies the style of Dao Yun.
    The slender and hard writing connects with the divine, the most precious. Her shy and coy gaze is empty. The beautiful woman lives by the Twenty-Four Bridges, twelve railings in the shape of '亚' are red.

    157
