    小峰不觉狂喜大叫，洒然而寤，遍体汗浃，栩栩犹弗能也。为之凝思，复忆情景宛然，诗词朗朗心目。咄嗟咋舌，呜呼奇哉！
    (与刘碧环同一笔仗，即《聊斋》之十八姨等耳。)

    钟子慕
    蓬莱钟子慕者，负奇志，有胆气。尝曰：“胸有万卷书，眼不见名山水，下笔时焉得有真境耶？”
    善琴，有年，航海而南，将游百粤。舶艘晴霁，风帆大驶，顷刻数百里。既而飓虹四起，舟师震恐，乃泊港中。子慕大快志，以为纵观狂溯，生平第一快事。因之抚弦动操，宫徽相生，清澈和伦。一舟之人，移情定志，皆不知身在波涛上，而声之感人深也。俄见舟傍鱼出水面，舟人异之，争相捞取。得一金色小鲤，目间有芒，子慕爱之。盎水养诸几上，夜间相对鼓琴。曲尚未终，忽而海水澒洞，巨舰簸扬，但闻裂帛一声，人琴欲碎，漂没于洪涛沉浸之间。有物自其背缚之者，张目谛视，如在琉璃瓶内，上下澄澈，水波不侵肌肤。絷之者，状皆狰恶。

    Explanation

    157

    小峰不禁狂喜大叫，猛然醒悟，全身汗湿，仿佛置身梦境，难以言表。他凝神思索，再次回忆起那情景，诗词在心中朗朗回响。他惊叹不已，感叹道：“真是奇哉！”

    （与刘碧环同一笔仗，即《聊斋》之十八姨等耳。）

    钟子慕
    蓬莱的钟子慕，胸怀奇志，胆气过人。他曾说：“胸中有万卷书，眼不见名山大川，下笔时怎能有真境呢？”
    他擅长弹琴，已有多年，航海南下，准备游历百粤。船在晴朗的天空下扬帆疾驶，转眼间便航行了数百里。不久，飓风骤起，船员们惊恐万分，只得停靠在港口。钟子慕大感欣慰，认为这放眼狂奔的旅程，是他生平第一快事。于是他抚琴演奏，音符和谐，清澈动听。船上的人都被音乐所感染，移情定志，仿佛忘却了身处波涛之上，而那音乐深深打动了他们的心。忽然，他看到船边有鱼跃出水面，船员们感到奇怪，纷纷争着捞取。他们捕获了一条金色的小鲤鱼，鱼眼间闪烁着光芒，钟子慕非常喜欢它。他将鱼养在桌上的水碗中，夜晚与它相对弹琴。曲子尚未结束，突然海水汹涌，巨船颠簸，只听一声如丝帛撕裂般的声音，人与琴仿佛要碎裂，被洪涛席卷而去。他感到有人从背后紧紧绑住他，睁开眼睛仔细看去，仿佛置身于琉璃瓶中，上下澄澈，水波不侵肌肤。那绑住他的人，面目狰狞恐怖。

    158

    钟固豪士，不之怖。至一城，闤阓繁华，珠光贝锦，比户连甍。见之者犀分翅展，遥立以观。继至一第，金碧交辉，光华四射，朱门洞启，阁道分驰。内则五云荫盖，六马金根，鸾旗映日，云罕从星。但听警跸传呼，静鞭甫动，众即絷钟阶下。钟立而不跪，睨视。殿上坐一王者，紫髯方额，怒形于色。以手指钟曰：“何物鲰生，几时浪迹，妄窃浮名，簧鼓淫哇之曲。随波逐流，致使孤掌珠覆盆，罪难渊测。速赐鱼肠，驱诸枯肆，毋赦。”众絷之出。

    Explanation

    158

    Zhong Gu, a man of great spirit, was not afraid. Upon arriving at a city, the marketplaces were bustling, the streets were lined with luxurious shops, and the houses were densely packed. Those who saw it spread their wings and stood afar to watch. Then he arrived at a mansion, where the golden and green colors interwove, the radiance shone in all directions, the red gate was wide open, and the pavilion paths were spacious. Inside, the five clouds shaded and covered, the six horses drew a golden carriage, the phoenix flag reflected the sun, and the formation resembled stars. As the guards and barriers announced and called, the silence whip just moved, and the crowd immediately bound Zhong Gu at the bottom of the steps. Zhong Gu stood upright and did not kneel, looking sideways. On the throne sat a king with a purple beard and a square forehead, whose anger was visible on his face. Pointing at Zhong Gu, he said, "What kind of small-minded person are you, how long have you been wandering aimlessly, falsely obtaining empty fame, playing lewd and vulgar music. Following the current, causing the loss of a single pearl, your crime is difficult to measure. Quickly grant the fish intestine sword, drive him to the dry market, no pardon." The crowd bound him and led him out.

    159

    钟仰天大笑，格格有声。王者令之返，曰：“何以笑为？”钟曰：“一笑大王之不达，又笑臣之不遇也。王试思之，深居九重，密勿之中，恶声不入。即使臣有洋洋流水之志，刺舟海上，岂同过阙驎驎，便尔声传禁闼？况鱼游潜听，关雉起朝飞？昔司马相如能文善琴，致卓王孙之奇遇。今臣亦能文善琴，而为大王之见杀。何古今之不相及，士之幸不幸，固如是乎？”王曰：“尔既能文，将面试。”命作《成连移情赋》。钟索笔札，文不加点，顷刻而就。赋曰：

    Explanation

    159

    Zhong looked up to the sky and laughed aloud, the sound echoing clearly. The king commanded him to return and asked, "Why are you laughing?" Zhong replied, "I laugh at the king's lack of understanding and also at my own misfortune. Please consider this: the king is deeply secluded in the innermost part of the palace, where unpleasant news cannot reach. Even if I have grand ambitions like flowing water and sail on the sea, how can I compare to passing by the palace gates and thus making my voice heard in the inner palace? Even if fish swim and listen secretly, and the roosters of the palace gates rise and fly in the morning, how can I reach the king? In the past, Sima Xiangru was skilled in writing and playing the qin and encountered the extraordinary favor of Zhuo Wensun. Now, I too am skilled in writing and playing the qin, but I am killed by the great king. How can the present be compared to the past, and the luck of scholars is indeed like this?" The king said, "Since you are skilled in writing, I will test you in person." He commanded Zhong to write "The Ode of Emotion Transfer by Cheng Lian." Zhong requested paper and ink, and wrote the text without revision, completing it in a short time. The ode begins:

    160

    六律昭宣，最关性情；六音并奏，首重丝桐。胡七弦之善抚，羌三载而未工。悟在寰中，浑无言于至教；神游象外，待响叶夫天风。厥有成连，工良心苦。方子春之传薪，楚伯牙之化雨。刺舟而去，何殊面命耳提；入海以求，即是引商刻羽。盖艺进乎道，理析微茫；技入于神，教通幽杳。云和一抚，居然流水汤汤；樊路非遥，尽是元音渺渺。尔乃俯窥地轴，仰瞩圆灵。烟凝山紫，岚拥风青。云起水穷，既迷茫而莫辨；气蒸波撼，亦鞺鞳其堪听。手挥目送之余，神归冲漠；海阔天空之际，思入窈冥。况复迷漫日月，鼓怒鼋鼍。既神惊而色变，弥心敏而手和。弟子来斯，希赏音于向若。先生休矣，将雅意之云何。

    Explanation

    160

    The Six Laws are clearly proclaimed, most closely related to nature and emotion; the Six Tones are played together, with the emphasis first on the zither and harp. The Hu people play the seven-stringed zither well, but the Qiang people have not mastered it after three years. Enlightenment lies within the world, yet there is no word for the highest teaching; the spirit wanders beyond the image, waiting for the sound to harmonize with the wind of heaven. There is Cheng Lian, whose skilled heart is filled with pain. Just as Zi Chun passes on the torch, so does Boya of Chu with his transformative rain. He rows away, no different from face-to-face instruction and ear-to-ear guidance; entering the sea to seek, it is like leading the shang and carving the yu. Art advances to the realm of the Dao, the principles are analyzed in the subtlest details; skill enters the realm of the divine, teaching reaches the deepest and most remote places. With a single stroke of the cloud and harmony, the flowing water sounds like rushing rapids; the path of the fence is not far, filled with the distant sounds of the original tones. Then you look down at the axis of the earth, and up at the circular spirit. Smoke condenses on purple mountains, mist embraces green winds. Clouds rise at the end of the water, becoming迷茫 and indistinguishable; the vapor rises and the waves shake, also producing a sound that is worth listening to. After waving the hand and looking away, the spirit returns to the void; at the moment of vast sea and sky, thoughts enter the depths. Moreover, the sun and moon are obscured, and the turtles and crocodiles are angry. The spirit is shocked and the color changes, the heart becomes more sensitive and the hands more harmonious. The disciples come here, hoping to hear the sound from the direction of Xiang. The master rests, what will be the refined intention?

    161

    王览毕，大喜，延之偏殿，赐麟脯之宴，歌“鹿苹”之章。与之言论，日昃不辍。并赐珠瑶奇宝，以示稽古之荣。由是公卿百寮，争延至家，宴饮款待无虚日，屡蒙召问。
    钟以老母，乞归终养。王许之，遣使持镇海神犀护送出海。两旁水皆壁立，中平夷如坦道，直达涯岸。抵界，众皆遗其捆载，并囊琴故物。倏然不见，海水顷合。烟波万里，渺渺而已。

    十八娘外传
    余幼随先大人宦游闽峤、粤海之间二十余年，得啖荔枝佳品，不一而足。如郎官红、黄玉、陈家紫，不莫饱嚼老饕。后余入蜀，又得味此。今返里十年，时深渴想，所谓鸟啼花落，水绿山青，足增悲悼者。古人不我欺也。因忆幔亭羽客有《十八娘外传》一通，书之以志想慕。曰：

    Explanation

    161

    King Wang, upon viewing it, was greatly pleased. He invited Zhong to a side hall and hosted a banquet with deer meat, singing the chapter of "Deer and Grass." He engaged in conversation with Zhong, continuing until late afternoon. He also bestowed upon Zhong rare pearls and precious treasures, to show the honor of studying ancient texts. From then on, high officials and ministers vied to invite Zhong to their homes, entertaining him with feasts and hospitality without a break, and he was repeatedly summoned for consultation.

    Zhong, citing his elderly mother, requested to return home to care for her. King Wang agreed, sending an envoy with a divine sea ox to escort him safely to sea. The waters on both sides stood like cliffs, while the middle was flat and smooth like a road, reaching the shore directly. Upon reaching the border, everyone left behind their bundles and the bag containing his old musical instruments. Suddenly, they disappeared, and the sea closed up quickly. The misty waves stretched for ten thousand miles, fading into the distance.

    18-Niang's Extranotes

    I grew up traveling with my father in the regions of Fujian and Guangdong for over twenty years, tasting many fine varieties of lychees. Such as the Langguan Red, Yellow Jade, and Chen's Purple, I have been a glutton for these fruits. Later, when I entered Sichuan, I once again enjoyed these delicacies. Now, returning to my hometown for ten years, I often feel a deep longing for them. The sight of birds singing and flowers falling, green waters and blue mountains, only adds to my sorrow and lamentation. As the ancients said, they were not mistaken. I recall that the celestial guest of the Man Pavilion had written a "18-Niang's Extranotes," and I have written this to express my longing and admiration. It reads:

    I grew up traveling with my father in the regions of Fujian and Guangdong for over twenty years, tasting many fine varieties of lychees. Such as the Langguan Red, Yellow Jade, and Chen's Purple, I have been a glutton for these fruits. Later, when I entered Sichuan, I once again enjoyed these delicacies. Now, returning to my hometown for ten years, I often feel a deep longing for them. The sight of birds singing and flowers falling, green waters and blue mountains, only adds to my sorrow and lamentation. As the ancients said, they were not mistaken. I recall that the celestial guest of the Man Pavilion had written a "18-Niang's Extranotes," and I have written this to express my longing and admiration. It reads:

    162

    明皇既幸蜀，失贵妃于马嵬。十八娘亦归里中，居晋安城东报国院。至德三载，无疾而终，遂就院旁之隙地瘗焉。
    万历中，有东海生者，闽人也。一日，出游东郊，少憩于报国院。昼长假寐，梦至一所，朱户红楼，丹栏紫阁，极其壮丽。徘徊间，一双髻侍儿，红裙翠袖，揖生而进，曰：“奉十八娘命，敬邀郎君。”生从之入，未及百步，香气袭人。行至一室，匾曰“扶离别馆”。少顷，见绿纱侍儿导一女郎，年可十八九，衣绛绡衣，颜色殊艳，冉冉而至。生进曰：“偶因休暇，驾言出游。既昧平生，敢逢胜果？”女郎曰：“妾开元皇帝侍儿也，以江采蘋之荐，得幸于上。今归于闽，似与郎君有夙缘，故相屈耳。”因出金钟，贮琼液，以酌生。生饮之，甘如醍醐丰酪。酒酣，姬容色转丽，因歌《菩萨蛮》一阕曰：

    Explanation

    162

    Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, having visited Shu, lost his beloved concubine Yang Guifei at Ma嵬. Miss Eighteen, as she was known, also returned to her hometown and lived in the East Gate Temple in Jin'an City. In the third year of the Zhide era, she passed away without illness and was buried in an empty plot of land near the temple.

    During the Wanli era, there was a man from Fujian known as Donghai Sheng. One day, he went out for a walk in the eastern suburbs and took a short rest at the East Gate Temple. As the day was long, he took a nap and dreamed of a place with red doors, red-painted buildings, red railings, and purple pavilions, all extremely magnificent. While wandering, he saw a pair of girls with hair buns, wearing red skirts and green sleeves, who bowed to him and led him in. They said, "On the order of Miss Eighteen, we respectfully invite you, sir." Following them, he walked in and, not even a hundred steps later, was overwhelmed by the fragrance. He entered a room where a sign read "Fuli Pavilion." A little while later, he saw a girl servant in green silk leading a young lady who was around eighteen or nineteen years old, wearing crimson gauze clothes, with an exceptionally beautiful appearance. She approached slowly. The man said, "I just happened to have free time and went out for a walk. Never having met before, I dare to meet such a wonderful result." The young lady said, "I was a servant of Emperor Xuanzong of Tang, thanks to the recommendation of Jiang Caiping, I was favored by the emperor. Now I have returned to Fujian, it seems we have a predestined connection, so I have invited you." She then took out a golden cup, filled it with fine liquor, and offered it to the man. The man drank it, and it was as sweet as clarified butter and rich milk. When he was drunk, the girl's appearance became even more beautiful, and she then sang a verse of "Pusaman."

    163

    “妾身本是琅琊种，当年曾被君王宠。艳态斗红妆，人称十八娘。绛绡笼玉质，纤手金盘擘。驿路起尘埃，骊山一骑来。”
    生闻之，愈加叹赏。因请闻开元遗事。姬曰：“妾忆在宫中时，正月十五夜，上御长春殿，张灵光宴。白鹭啭花，黄龙吐水。遣妾撒闽中锦丸于地，令宫人竞拾之。多者赏以红圈披绿晕衫。又一日，上幸长生殿，奏新曲，未有名，值妾为贵妃称觞，上大悦，遂以妾名其乐。左右欢呼，声动山谷。此皆妾受宠当时，不闻人间者。”生闻之，愈惊骇，既而侍儿报江、周、陈三姬至。江衣绿，周衣红，陈衣紫，种种妖丽。三姬曰：“闻吾姊今日有佳宾，故来相贺。”三姬各集古诗二章，江吟曰：

    Explanation

    163

    My body is originally from the land of Langya, once I was favored by the emperor. My beauty competed with the red makeup, and people called me the Eighteenth Lady. Crimson gauze enveloped my jade-like body, and my slender hands broke the golden dish. The road was dusty, and a single horse came from Mount Li. Upon hearing this, the young man admired her even more. He then asked to hear stories from the Kaiyuan era. The lady said, "I recall when I was in the palace, on the night of the Lantern Festival, the emperor presided over a banquet in the Changchun Hall. White herons sang among the flowers, and yellow dragons spewed water. He ordered me to scatter brocade balls from Minzhong on the ground, and the palace ladies competed to pick them up. Those who picked up the most were rewarded with red circles and green-striped robes. On another day, the emperor visited the Changsheng Hall, where a new piece of music was played, but it had no name. When I, as the favorite concubine, offered him a toast, he was very pleased and named the music after me. The courtiers cheered, and the sound echoed through the mountains. These were the times when I was favored, things unknown to the common people." Upon hearing this, the young man was even more amazed. Soon after, a servant announced the arrival of the three ladies Jiang, Zhou, and Chen. Jiang wore green, Zhou wore red, and Chen wore purple, all of them exquisitely beautiful. The three ladies said, "We heard that our sister has a distinguished guest today, so we came to congratulate her." Each of the three ladies composed two ancient poems. Jiang recited:

    164

    百般红紫斗芳菲(昌黎)，隔水残霞见画衣(曹唐)。别有玉杯承露冷(裴潾)，红妆飞骑向前归(武元衡)。
    野人相赠满筠笼(杜甫)，时以开元天宝中(杜牧之)。火树风来翻绛艳(白傅)，树头树底觅残红(王建)。
    凌晨并作新妆面(昌黎)，玉碗盛来琥珀光(李白)。饱食不须愁内热(王维)，已分甜雪饮琼浆(司空曙)。
    陈姬吟曰：
    何处横钗带小枝(秦韬玉)，可怜妖冶正当时(白傅)。曾缘玉貌君王宠(刘得仁)，莫比潘家大谷梨(崔兴宗)。
    可爱深红爱浅红(杜甫)，离离朱实绿丛中(周元范)。不知多少开元寺(谭用之)，香气潜来紫阳风(袁不约)。
    三姬吟毕，十八娘亦集古二首云：
    遥指红楼是妾家(李白)，琼枝日出晒红纱(白傅)。摘时正带凌晨露(白傅)，应服朝来一片霞(秦系)。

    Explanation

    164

    A Hundred Varieties of Red and Purple Compete in Beauty (Han Yu), Across the Water, the Setting Sun's Glow is Like a Painted Garment (Cao Tang). There is a Jade Cup Holding the Cold Dew (Pei Lin), The Red-clad Riders Fly Forward to Return (Wu Yuanheng).
    The Wild Man Presents a Full Bamboo Cage (Du Fu), It is in the Reigns of Kaiyuan and Tianbao (Du Mu). The Fire Tree, Swirling with Crimson Beauty as the Wind Blows (Bai Juyi), Seeking the Remaining Red Blossoms at the Top and Bottom of the Trees (Wang Jian).
    At Dawn, They Put on New Makeup (Han Yu), The Jade Bowl Holds the Amber-like Light (Li Bai). After a Full Meal, There is No Need to Worry About Internal Heat (Wang Wei), Already Divided into Sweet Snow and Drinking Nectar (Sikong Shu).
    Chen Ji Sings:
    Where is the Hairpin with a Small Branch (Qin Taoju), How Lovely, the Seductive Beauty is Right Now (Bai Juyi). Once Adored by the Emperor for Her Beauty (Liu Deren), Do Not Compare Her to the Pears of the Great Valley of Pan (Cui Xingzong).
    Loving the Deep Red and the Light Red (Du Fu), The Red Fruits Clustering in the Green Thicket (Zhou Yuanfan). Who Knows How Many Temples of Kaiyuan (Tan Yongzhi), The Fragrance Comes Stealthily with the Purple Yang Wind (Yuan Buyue).
    The Three Ladies Finish Singing, and Shiba Niang Also Recites Two Ancient Poems:
    Pointing to the Red Building Far Away is My Home (Li Bai), The Jade Branch in the Sun Dries the Red Gauze (Bai Juyi). When Picked, It Still Carries the Morning Dew (Bai Juyi), Should Wear the Morning's Cloud of Rosy Light (Qin Xi).

    165

    晓漱琼膏冰齿寒(包信)，一生长对水晶盘(李义山)。香随翠笼擎初到(昌黎)，长得君王带笑看(李白)。
    四姬吟就，十八娘出红绣鞋一双赠生，且隅曰：“愿君以此传之人间。”既而江姬出射囊一函，周姬出真珠一颗，陈姬出紫琼一枚为赠。
    生蘧然惊觉，惟见荔枝垂熟，繁星离离。询其旁，果有十八娘冢云。因赋诗曰：
    骊山一骑红尘起，七日能行数千里。丹荔飞来色正新，金盘满注华清水。
    花外遥闻百步香，寒冰一片剖罗囊。长生殿上连枝进，太液池头半醉尝。
    乐工初制梨园曲，小部音声听不足。佳名新赐荔枝香，左右欢呼动山谷。
    一声鼙鼓震渔阳，西幸銮舆道路长。娥眉宛转含情死，马上君王掩面伤。
    炎方仍进青丝笼，垂涕还思当日宠。丹实犹然贡上方，朱颜久已归荒冢。
    妃子妖魂去渺茫，千秋何处识红妆。梦中细说前朝事，不及王家十八娘。

    Explanation

    165

    A Morning Routine of Chewing on Ice (Bao Xun), a Life Spent Admiring Crystal Dishes (Li Yishan). The Fragrance Follows a Green Basket as It Arrives (Changli), and the Ruler Smiles and Watches (Li Bai).

    Four Ladies Compose Poetry, and Shiba Nü Gives a Pair of Embroidered Red Shoes as a Gift to the Recipient. She Says, "I Wish You Would Share This with Others." Then Jiang Gives a Case Containing Arrows, Zhou Gives a Pearl, and Chen Gives a Piece of Purple Jade as Gifts.

    The Recipient Suddenly Awakens, Seeing Ripe Lychees Hanging and Many Stars Scattered in the Sky. He Asks Someone Nearby, and Confirms the Presence of a Tomb for Shiba Nü. He Then Composes a Poem:

    From Lishan Mountain, a Rider on a Red Dust-Covered Horse Starts, Traveling Thousands of Miles in Seven Days. Fresh Red Lychees Are Brought, and a Golden Bowl Is Filled with Clear Water.

    The Fragrance of Flowers Is Smelled from a Distance, and a Piece of Ice Is Cut from a Silk Bag. Branches Are Presented at the Hall of Everlasting Life, and Something Is Tasted While Slightly Intoxicated Near Taiye Pool.

    Musicians Create New Songs in the Pear Garden, but the Sounds of a Small Ensemble Are Not Enough. A New Name Is Given to the Fragrance of Lychees, and the Surrounding Area Is Filled with Cheers.

    The Sound of Drums Shakes Yuyang, and the Emperor's Journey Westward Is Long. A Beautiful Woman Dies with Emotion, and the Emperor Covers His Face in Grief.

    A Cage Made of Green Silk Is Brought from the South, and Tears Are Shed While Thinking of Past Favors. Red Fruits Are Still Offered to the Imperial Court, but the Beauty of a Person Long Since Returned to a Grave.

    The Spirit of a Concubine Disappears into the Unknown, and After Many Years, It Is Difficult to Recognize the Beauty of a Woman. Stories of the Past Are Told in a Dream, but They Are Not as Interesting as Those of a Woman Named Shiba Nü from the Wang Family.

    166

    郧阳太守俭约文
    俭，美德也，过则鄙矣。故诗剌褊心，谓其不衷于度也。
    余犹子省轩，本闽籍，归吾大宗。壬申，举北皿考学。录为国子先生二十余年。工书法，刻青《集古滋蕙》帖，行于世。
    忆其助教成均，日常不给。课读之余，则为人缝纫，易钱钞。每晨买豆腐渣一块，或豆芽三斤，煮熟饱食。出门去，则传食糊口，至暮始归。坛中清水，不计冬夏，饮数杓；或又作纫事，或抄书。如是者习以为常。风窗雨屋，破絮悬鹑，泊如也。炕一白毡，日读书写字，墨沈淋漓，夜则束身其中。
    己丑，升刑曹主政，乃车。车无帏，用高丽纸糊，一骡御之。每五更早起，开炕炉，煮老米饭半锅，食然后入衙。衙中故有公厨，每顿银二钱，不肯费。御车者去城外半日，为人载，午翘其主人返，其得所钞，可办两日蒭豆。每日晨，散衙后，省轩一人兀兀坐，捉笔点画律例，八年成一书，名《律表》，亦梓行。前大学士舒，以其勤慎，列保荐。蒙恩以繁府用。

    Explanation

    166

    On Frugality and Simplicity of the Governor of Yunyang

    Frugality is a virtue, but excess makes one vulgar. Therefore, the Book of Songs criticizes narrow-mindedness, saying that it does not align with propriety.

    My younger brother, Shengxuan, originally from Fujian, has joined our family. In the year Ren Shen, he passed the northern examination and was appointed as a scholar at the Imperial College for over twenty years. He excelled in calligraphy and carved the green Ji Gu Zhi Hui collection, which circulated in the world.

    Recalling his time as an assistant teacher at the Imperial College, his daily life was often insufficient. In his spare time after teaching, he would sew clothes for others to earn money. Every morning, he would buy a block of tofu residue or three pounds of bean sprouts, cook them, and eat until full. When he left home, he would find food to eat, returning only at dusk. In his bowl, he drank clear water regardless of winter or summer, consuming several ladles; sometimes he would sew or copy books. Such was his routine, and he became accustomed to it. Living in a house exposed to wind and rain, with tattered clothes, he remained content and tranquil. On a kang, he had a white felt mat where he read and wrote every day, with ink stains all over, and at night he would wrap himself in it.

    In the year Ji Chou, he was promoted to the position of Chief of the Criminal Department and began using a carriage. The carriage had no curtains and was covered with Korean paper, pulled by a single donkey. Every day at the fifth watch, he would get up early, light the kang stove, cook half a pot of old rice, eat, and then go to the office. Although there was a public kitchen in the office, costing two taels of silver per meal, he refused to spend it. The driver would leave the city for half a day, carrying passengers, and return in the afternoon, receiving payment that could cover two days' worth of fodder and beans. Every morning, after the court session ended, Shengxuan would sit alone, writing and marking legal examples. After eight years, he completed a book, titled Table of Laws, which was also published. The former Grand Secretary Shu recommended him because of his diligence and prudence, and he was honored with a complex and important post.

    167

    丙申，放湖北郧阳太守。莅任之日，相随一仆一骡。仆即伺骡者。逾岁，眷属至，其少子年九岁。会冬冷，子无风帽，欲为之购，不肯，曰：“小儿当练头，不必冠。”遂伤脑，以鼻涕死。其妾，京中人也，足不弓，尝着其破朝靴，其家丁皆敝衣决踵，邋遢而环伺。夫然后顾而乐之，固不知其背面时，皆狐裘煌煌也。不宴客，即宴客，亦不饮酒。有同城副将马某，回教，与省轩早契。三年之中，不肯到一羊相邀宴。会以审案赴省，谒各上司衙门。日昃，不得返。尝以炊饼纳袖，自舆中啖之。人问：“食饼时，逢途中共耳而目之乎？”曰：“我食之，以袖笼口，不令人知。人或见我颊动，不过谓嚼槟榔、吸鼻烟耳。”初秋，着一蓖麻布袍，染作米色，衣以示人，云：“其质有类于羽毛纱，其色不亚于程乡茧。”署荆州府。署有楼，相传有妖物凭之。凡新守至，必牲牢音乐以祭，否则祟。省轩不祭，遂病喑。有劝之者，辄摇手，不行。至卸篆，病亦寻瘥。

    Explanation

    167

    In the year of Bing Shen, he was appointed as the governor of Yunyang Prefecture in Hubei Province. On the day he took office, he was accompanied by one servant and one mule. The servant was also the mule driver. After a year, his family arrived, including his young son who was nine years old. During a cold winter, the child did not have a wind hat, and he wanted to buy one for him, but he refused, saying, "The child should wear a simple cap, not a hat." As a result, the child injured his brain and died from nasal discharge. His concubine, who was from the capital, had unbound feet and once wore her old official boots. Her household servants all wore tattered clothes and looked untidy, surrounding him. He looked at them and was pleased, not realizing that they were all wearing splendid fox fur coats when he was not looking. He did not entertain guests, and even when he did, he did not drink alcohol. There was a Muslim vice-general named Ma, who had a long-standing friendship with him. For three years, he refused to attend a banquet invited by one sheep. When he went to the provincial capital to review cases and visit the offices of his superiors, it was late afternoon, and he could not return. He once carried a steamed bun in his sleeve and ate it from the carriage. Someone asked, "When you ate the bun, did people see and hear you on the road?" He replied, "I ate it, covering my mouth with my sleeve so no one would know. People might see my cheeks moving, but they would just think I was chewing betel nuts or sniffing snuff." In early autumn, he wore a coat made of castor bean fabric dyed in a rice color. He showed it to others, saying, "Its texture is similar to feather gauze, and its color is no less than that of Chengxiang silk." He served as the governor of Jingzhou Prefecture, whose office had a building said to be inhabited by an evil spirit. Whenever a new governor arrived, they must offer sacrifices with livestock and music, otherwise, they would suffer misfortune. He did not offer sacrifices and thus became mute. When someone advised him, he would shake his head and refuse. When he resigned from his post, his illness soon improved.

    168

    余过武昌，与省轩遇，相留弥月。每日苦蘖粝餐，不可耐。我欲归。是夜人静，省轩持金二百，置余床头，云：“不腆为叔赆，且为祖母寿。区区饮宴欢聚，比处皆然。一旦骊驹将驾行者，不足为一日之春。有黯然令人伤心者，吾叔以负米计，跋涉千里外，谅不为餔餟来也。”因受其金，且辞其言焉。
    逾年，省轩告归闽，年已八十矣。
    噫！俭则固，省轩之谓与。然其不为淫祀，不作浪费，赠远人，安淡泊，其矫世励俗之行，又当世士大夫中所难能而可贵者也。
    (省轩有《俭约》一篇云：
    盖闻崇俭去奢，本属持躬之要；辞华就朴，尤为训俗之宜。自世尚虚浮，人鲜樽节。侈于自奉，争羡何曾之食万钱；骄以成风，辄夸孔融之客满座。肆筵张乐，笙歌不绝于华堂；开阁延宾，珍羞日罗于绮席。虽隆札异数，徒费锱铢；而实意真怀，有何裨益？

    Explanation

    168

    As I passed through Wuchang, I met Shengxuan, and we stayed together for a month. Every day, we had coarse and bitter meals, which were difficult to bear. I wanted to return home. That night, when it was quiet, Shengxuan held two hundred gold coins and placed them by my bed, saying, "A small gift for my uncle, and also as a birthday gift for my grandmother. Small gatherings and feasts, it is the same everywhere. When the horse is ready to be driven away, it is not enough to make one day spring. There are those who are sad and heartbroken. My uncle, counting on carrying rice, traveled thousands of miles, surely not for the sake of food." I accepted the money and also declined his words.

    After a year, Shengxuan informed me he was returning to Fujian, and he was already eighty years old. Ah! Frugality is indeed the hallmark of Shengxuan. However, he does not engage in excessive rituals or waste. He gives gifts to distant people and finds contentment in simplicity. His actions to correct the world and inspire the customs are rare and valuable among the scholars and officials of the world. (Shengxuan has an essay titled 'Frugality' which says:
    It is said that promoting frugality and eliminating extravagance is the essence of self-cultivation; rejecting华丽 and embracing simplicity is especially suitable for teaching and guiding customs. Since the world has become superficial, people lack moderation. Excessive self-indulgence, competing to envy He Zeng's meals costing ten thousand coins; pride has become a trend, often boasting about Kong Rong's guests filling the room. Setting up feasts and music, music and singing never cease in the splendid hall; opening the gate to welcome guests, delicacies are daily arranged on the fine table. Although grand etiquette and special honors, only waste small amounts of money; but the true intentions and genuine feelings, have any benefit?)

    169

    吾辈从大夫后，为士庶先。淡泊相期，志何取乎大快；纷华奚事，情不用以过隆。敢敬告我同僚，共守清规。单刺可以通名，何烦全柬；片词即能达意，岂必庄陈。至于宴会住还，惟期伸我积素；觥筹交错，止宜浃彼常情。小酌不嫌于四两，屈量为佳；大脔仅可以三斤，过饱不取。非必为矫情之举，聊以表惜福之规。此约。)

    奸淫变相判
    嘉靖间，杭州书生游西湖断桥下。当暑热，醉后卧舟尾。夜不寐，凉月如水，可鉴毫芒。遥见二人，长不盈尺，徘徊沙际。其一多髭，其一妇人。相与语曰：“百十轮回，诘旦为殃。鼎煎刃解，折体裂肠。我倾炎刘，尔覆李唐。千秋万劫，莫可逃亡。”两人并肩，相与痛哭而入水中。书生异之。

    Explanation

    169

    Final Translation

    On Frugality and Modesty

    We follow the gentlemen, but precede the commoners. We aim for simplicity, so why seek great pleasure? Why pursue luxury, when emotions should not be overly indulged? I dare to respectfully advise my colleagues to jointly uphold the clear rules. A simple card can introduce names, why bother with a full letter? A few words can convey meaning, why insist on formal speech? As for banquets and gatherings, we only hope to express our long-standing friendship. Drinking cups and chopsticks intermingling, it is only appropriate to express ordinary emotions. A small drink of four ounces is not too little, moderation is best. A large piece of meat up to three pounds is enough, overeating is not desirable. It is not necessarily a pretentious act, but rather a way to show the rule of cherishing blessings. This agreement.

    The Transformation of Debauchery

    During the Jiajing era, a scholar from Hangzhou was boating under the Broken Bridge in West Lake. It was the height of summer, and after drinking, he lay down at the stern of the boat. Unable to sleep at night, the cool moonlight was like water, reflecting even the finest details. From afar, he saw two figures, no more than a foot tall, wandering along the sandbank. One had a thick beard, and the other was a woman. They spoke to each other, saying, "After a hundred or so lifetimes, disaster will come at dawn. Boiling in a cauldron, cut apart, the body shattered, the intestines torn. I will bring down the Han dynasty, and you will overthrow the Tang. Through a thousand autumns and ten thousand ages, there is no escape." The two stood side by side, wept bitterly, and then plunged into the water. The scholar found this strange.

    170

    次日，见渔者钓于桥下，得二鳖焉。径皆尺许。其一腹有“王”字，一腹有“天”字。生乃悟曰：“此曹、武余孽之深也。其一书爵，其一书姓名。至于今，犹颠倒磨折于鳞虫介羽之中，以大快天下万世之人心。谁谓苍苍莽莽间漫无真宰也哉？”生尝戏为判曰：
    诛已往之奸回，愤余殃之厉气。阿瞒安在，武氏为谶。或为君而为臣，济恶皆同一辙；即成男而成女，厥罪亦可为均。炎鼎移来，继篡于王莽之后；中官乱始，倡淫于韦后之先。居然统魏妄尊，竟尔伪周僭号。滥举孝廉之目，徒成才人之名。带剑入朝，汉相实为汉贼；垂帘听政，唐后即是唐妖。迹其欺妄之罪不殊也。幽二帝于深宫，揽权自附；迁储君于远戍，窃柄为奸。献帝将啮指而降诏，掖门之衣带频看；高宗乃病目以临轩，内寝之声闻益厉。挟天子而为令，汉廷之遗老被戕；窥神器以肆凶，唐室之诸宗几绝。炬北宫于八十万，犹夸元相阿衡；乱天纪于十三年，漫拟金轮天册。

    Explanation

    170

    The next day, the scholar saw a fisherman fishing under the bridge and caught two turtles. Their diameters were about a foot. One had a "Wang" character on its belly, and the other had a "Tian" character. The scholar then realized and said, "This is the deep-rooted legacy of Cao and Wu. One was inscribed with a title, and the other with a name. To this day, they are still being tormented in the realm of fish, insects, crustaceans, and birds, greatly pleasing the hearts of people for generations to come. Who would say that the vast universe is devoid of a true ruler?" The scholar once playfully wrote a judgment:

    To punish past treachery and to vent the lingering evil influence. Where is Cao Cao now, while the Wu clan is a foretold sign. Whether as a ruler or a minister, all who aid in evil follow the same path; whether male or female, their sins are equally grave. The imperial power was transferred, following the usurpation of Wang Mang; the eunuchs caused chaos, leading to the debauchery of Empress Wei. They arrogantly unified the state of Wei, falsely claiming respect; they eventually usurped the Zhou dynasty's name. They falsely claimed to be filial and upright, merely earning the reputation of talented individuals. Carrying swords into the court, the Han prime minister was actually a traitor to the Han; ruling behind the curtain, the Tang empress was an evil figure in the Tang. Their crimes of deceit and falsehood are no different. They imprisoned the two emperors in the inner palace, seizing power for themselves; they exiled the crown prince to a distant frontier, taking control through treachery. Emperor Xian bit his finger to issue an edict, frequently looking at the door; Emperor Gaozong became blind to ascend the throne, with the sounds from the inner chambers growing louder. They held the emperor hostage to issue commands, causing the old scholars of the Han court to be killed; they coveted the imperial throne to commit atrocities, nearly exterminating the Tang imperial family. They burned the Northern Palace with eighty thousand people, still boasting of Yuan Xian's role; they disrupted the heavenly order for thirteen years, rashly claiming the Golden Wheel and Heavenly Decree.

    171

    而其狡狯之心相类也。欲要荣于势位，父虽死而亦可共天；思固宠于宫闱，女即杀而不留余地。威能震主，射许田之鹿，万岁曾叨；功竟贪天，催上元之花，一诗敢冒。喜扶头而勿药，曾闻读檄于陈琳；惊顿足于失笑，犹讶讨文于骆子。上马提金，关壮缪之羁留，几欲牢笼贤圣；当朝赐翠，狄梁公之宰辅，真能束缚英雄。回忆祝发，空王曾下长门之泪；堪笑割须，渭水空惊孟起之军。宴铜雀于春深，老当益壮；比莲花于年少，耄而愈淫。孽由己作，罪有同条。十八狱之幽囚应遍，三十道之轮转备尝。惟是瘖彰有定理，从来生化岂无权。旷千秋而立案，坠诸畜遭而犹轻；惩大恶而从苛，律以介虫而允当。

    曹月帆
    自古金阊，繁华第一，至今吴会，风月无双。通略约以垂虹，香流桥下；步山塘于响屧，花满廛间。船回消夏之湾，几见霜寒枫冷；人动悲秋之念，犹思蓴美鲈肥，是以到处笙歌，竞传南部。而一时粉黛，固无不艳。

    Explanation

    171

    Their cunning and deceitful hearts are similar. They seek honor through power and position, even if it means their father's death; they desire to secure favor within the imperial harem, and will kill without leaving any room for mercy. Their authority can shake the ruler, as seen when Cao Cao hunted deer in Xu Garden, and they have claimed credit for divine favor, causing the early blooming of spring flowers, and dared to compose a poem. They are so weak they need support to hold their heads up, yet they refuse to take medicine, and have heard of reading a proclamation by Chen Lin; they are startled by laughter, and still find it surprising to write a complaint by Luo Ziqi. They mount a horse and hold a gold whip, like Guan Yu's capture and imprisonment, almost as if to cage the wise and virtuous; they are granted green jade, like Di Lianggong's role as a high-ranking official, truly able to bind heroes. Recalling the act of cutting off one's hair, the monk shed tears at the Longmen Temple; it is laughable to cut one's beard, as the Wei River was surprised by Meng Yi's army. They hold feasts in the Copper Swallow Pavilion during the spring, old yet more vigorous; they compare themselves to young lotus flowers, aged yet more lustful. The sin is self-made, and the punishment is the same. They should have been imprisoned in the eighteen hells, and experienced the cycle of reincarnation. Only there is a fixed law for both silent and obvious matters, the transformation of life has always had its own authority. Setting a precedent for a thousand years, the fate of animals is still lighter; punishing great evil with severity, the law for insects is fitting.

    Cao Yu
    From ancient times, Jin Gang has been the most prosperous, and even now, Wu is unmatched in beauty and culture. Connecting the bridges and piers of Chuihong, the fragrance flows under the bridge; walking the Shantang Street with the sound of footsteps, flowers fill the market. Returning to the bay of the summer retreat, one sees the cold of frost and the cold of maple leaves; people are moved by thoughts of autumn, still thinking of the beauty of chun and the richness of lu, so everywhere there is music and singing, competing to spread the fame of the southern part. And at that time, the beauty of the women is truly unparalleled.

    172

    说吴姬者也，江西曹塘字月帆，贵公子也。年二十，秀彦轶群，风华自诩。一日，买妾姑苏，觅舟南泛。十万钱缠，不是载将明月；三生愿重，但求嘒彼小星。越旬抵苏，客寓胥门。日事流观，渐且往来稠密，门馆喧阗。桃花坞，莺脰水，曾留逸少之名；沧浪馆，可中亭，遂有建安之目。其地狭斜最多，既云买姬，则媒红络绎。醉洞庭之春色，面带桃花；饶鹤市之风光，巷穿杨柳。而曹公子素挥霍如粪土。
    苏固有游手之徒，俗名“蔑片”，为人帮闲买笑，设阱伏机，以利曹之资。其初也，但鼓翼而附羶，挥之不去；继也，便含沙而射影，中之即伤。乃设一局，倩青楼四人，悉擅诗书琴棋，名瘦马者，充为良家女子，以绐曹，且大索见面钱、遮羞费。是日也，彩羽齐来，谁识铜街之丽；襜帏并启，严同金屋之娇。曹惊喜欲狂，延之入室。四人并列，一曰环风，一曰素珠，一曰夜兰，一曰碧湘。莫不修眉妙目，素体轻莲。

    Explanation

    172

    The One Who Spoke of Wu's Concubine

    In Jiangxi, there was a nobleman named Cao Tang, whose courtesy name was Yuefan. At the age of twenty, he was outstanding among the elite, boasting of his风采. One day, he bought a concubine from Suzhou and set sail southward. With ten thousand coins in hand, he did not intend to bring back the bright moon; rather, he sought only the faint stars of his three lifetimes. After ten days, he arrived in Suzhou and stayed at the Xumen Inn. He spent his days leisurely observing the sights, and gradually, his visits became more frequent, with the inn becoming bustling. The Peach Blossom Hut and the Swallow's Neck Water, once frequented by the famous calligrapher Wang Xizhi, were now adorned with the names of the Jian'an period. The area was known for its numerous narrow alleys, and since he had come to buy a concubine, matchmakers came in a continuous stream. Drunk on the spring scenery of Dongting Lake, his face flushed like peach blossoms; abounding in the sights of the Crane Market, the lanes were lined with willows. The nobleman Cao was known for his reckless spending, treating money as if it were dirt.

    In Suzhou, there were idle people, commonly known as "mepian," who helped others in trivial matters, set traps, and laid snares, all for the sake of profiting from Cao's wealth. At first, they flattered him with their wings, and he could not shake them off; later, they spread rumors and made false accusations, and he was deeply hurt by them. They then devised a plan, hiring four courtesans from the pleasure quarters, all skilled in poetry, books, chess, and music, one of whom was named Thin Horse, pretending to be a virtuous woman, to deceive Cao, and demanding large amounts of见面钱 and遮羞费. On that day, colorful feathers came together, who would recognize the beauty of the copper street; the curtains were drawn open, and the courtesans appeared as娇as the golden house. Cao was overwhelmed with surprise and joy, inviting them into his room. The four courtesans stood side by side, one named Huanfeng, one named Suzhu, one named Yelan, and one named Bixiang. None of them lacked beautiful eyebrows and eyes, light as lotus petals.

    173

    四人乃各致殷勤而拜曹曰：“公子万福，妾辈寒微陋质，自分缘悭，未卜谁为有幸，得以常侍左右。晷影犹早，愿作晓妆。请公子凭几而观之。”乃各调脂弄粉，启盒开奁，盘鸦髻挽，还惊蝉鬓之如涡；坠马妆成，更并螺云之低起。至若绣衣施粉，素袜凌波，自难备述。四人又复挽长袖，携素手，谓公子食性未谙，愿作羹汤，同入厨下，验异时中馈之助。一作金橙缕脍，一作红虬兰桂脯，一作芍药酱凫，一作红绫饼馅。诚闻香而口嚼，见色而心迷者矣。

    Explanation

    173

    The four women each showed their utmost courtesy and bowed to Cao, saying, "Your Excellency, we are mere humble and unattractive women, and we thought our fate was poor. We did not know who would be fortunate enough to serve you on a daily basis. It is still early in the day, so we wish to do our morning makeup. Please let us sit by a table and watch." They then applied makeup, opening boxes and drawers, winding their hair into buns, and the hair was as smooth as a cicada's wings and curled like a whirlpool. Completing the horse-riding makeup, the makeup was low and raised like cloud formations. As for the embroidered clothes and applied makeup, the white socks seemed to dance on water, and it is hard to describe in full. The four women then pulled their long sleeves and held hands, saying that since the nobleman's eating habits were not well-known, they wished to make soup and entered the kitchen together to test their future assistance in the kitchen. One made golden orange thread slices, one made red dragon and orchid meat, one made peony sauce duck, and one made red silk filling pastries. Indeed, those who taste the fragrance and see the colors will be enchanted.

    174

    食顷，四人复进技以尽其长，为公子寿。环执云阳板，素吹子晋笙，夜弹赵女筝，碧拨太真檀槽。而靡靡之音，宜风宜雅，听之如身在竹林秋晓间，魂与俱销。又复拈霜毫，舒素翰，各尽梅兰竹菊一幅，以赠公子，皆题一绝。
    咏梅云：
    搅得江南胜，为君画一枝。殷勤犹迨吉，好咏二南诗。
    咏兰云：
    空谷凭谁到，王孙尚未归。不知经服媚，有梦征燕妃。
    咏竹云：
    一径柔条嫩，萧萧倚碧流。漫夸湘水节，敢护鹿门秋。
    咏菊云：
    淡尔偏多韵，轻描却有神。秋风歌一曲，如对李夫人。
    公子斯时静睹芳容，饮餐佳味，繁弦调急，妙制情深。琉璃屏，孙亮之风流，不让丽姝洛杰；翡翠帏，魏文之爱幸，无殊莫段薛陈。陋赵家之广袖，一妹偏单；比杨氏之玉环，三姨并集。诚哉美不胜收，乐且莫极。无何，肩舆促驾，夕照衔山。四人移步裣衽云谢，叮咛而去。公子四顾踌躇，皆期满志；一时怜爱，尽结同心。

    Explanation

    174

    After a meal, the four women resumed their performances, showcasing their talents to celebrate the young lord's birthday. Huan held the Yunyang board, Su played the Zi Jin flute, at night she plucked the Zhao girl's zither, and Bi plucked the Taizhen lute. The soft, gentle music was suitable for both the Book of Songs and the Book of Odes, and listening to it was like being in a bamboo forest on an autumn morning, where the soul was also entranced. They then picked up their frosty brushes, spread out their white paper, and each completed a painting of plum, orchid, bamboo, and chrysanthemum to give to the young lord, all writing a quatrain.

    For the plum blossom, they wrote:
    Stirring up the beauty of the Jiangnan region, I paint a branch for you. With sincere diligence, still waiting for good fortune, good to recite the poems of the Two Nan.

    For the orchid, they wrote:
    Who will come to the empty valley? The prince has not yet returned. Not knowing the charm of being favored, there is a dream of the Yan palace.

    For the bamboo, they wrote:
    A path with tender, flexible branches, the sound of the wind through the trees, leaning against the green stream. Lightly boasting about the integrity of the Xiang River, daring to protect the autumn of Lu Gate.

    For the chrysanthemum, they wrote:
    Modestly yet rich in flavor, lightly painted yet full of spirit. Singing a song in the autumn wind, like facing Lady Li.

    At this moment, the young lord calmly observed their beautiful appearance, enjoying delicious food and drink, listening to the rapid and intricate melodies of the music, and marveling at the wonderful creations filled with deep emotion. The glass screen, associated with the elegance of Sun Liang, was no less graceful than the beauty of Luojie; the jade curtain, associated with the favor of Wei Wen, was no different from the favor of Mo Duan and Xue Chen. The wide sleeves of Zhao's family were modest, but one sister was alone; compared to Yang's jade ring, three aunts gathered together. Truly, the beauty was overwhelming, and the joy was boundless. Soon, they hurriedly left in a sedan chair, the sunset touching the mountain. The four women stepped back, bowed, and said their goodbyes, then left with instructions. The young lord looked around hesitantly, all achieving their full wishes; at this moment, all feelings of love and affection were united.

    175

    数日之后，议聘计售，价增人杳。心逾急者事多左，望过殷者遇偏疏。而来往诸人，固疑其迹，以阴耗其用焉。既而黑貂裘敝，囊空买玉之资；绿绮琴亡，身乏点金之术。日复一日，池榭萧条，馆庭阒寂。公子羁旅，寡情乡关，动念诸旧游。又私嘱当途，潜通胥吏，闻有游客曹姓，招摇于市，几被访缉。曹不得已，竟狼狈归。呜呼！风情顿减，好事全非，片帆高挂，人归五老之峰；故道重经，泪洒九江之水。
    迄今事隔年淹，曹君迈老，与二三良友每一谈及，竟成笑柄。话到不堪回首处，空萦公子之肠；只今方是点头时，漫拾佳人之翠。悬遗芳于素壁，对墨痕笔意而狁怜；想往事于他年，拟舞态歌声而欲绝。

    Explanation

    175

    After a few days, the negotiations for the marriage and the sale of the bride were underway, but as the price increased, people disappeared. The more one is in a hurry, the more likely one is to make mistakes; those who hope too much often meet with disappointment. The people who came and went were suspicious of his actions and secretly undermined his efforts. Soon, his black sable fur coat was worn out, and his purse was empty of the money needed to buy jade; his valuable green qin was lost, and he lacked the ability to turn base metals into gold. Day after day, the garden and courtyard became desolate, and the hall and courtyard were quiet and empty. The nobleman, away from home, lacked affection for his hometown and thought of his past friends and companions. He privately instructed influential people and secretly communicated with low-ranking officials, hearing that a traveler named Cao was showing off in the market and almost caught by the authorities. Cao, unable to avoid it, finally returned in a disheveled state. Alas! His romantic feelings suddenly diminished, and all good things turned bad. A single sail raised high, he returned to Mount Wugong; retracing old paths, tears were shed into the waters of the Nine Rivers.

    Until now, the matter has been delayed for years, and the elderly gentleman Cao, with a few good friends, every time they talk about it, it becomes a laughingstock. When talking about things that are too painful to recall, it leaves the nobleman's heart full of longing; only now is the time to nod in agreement, gathering the green hairpins of beautiful women. Hanging the lingering fragrance on a plain wall, looking at the traces of ink and brush strokes and feeling pity; thinking of past events in future years, imagining the dancing and singing and feeling as if one would die.

    176

    (七如不善四六，勉就一篇，尚不俗恶。)

    讨蜘蛛网檄
    雒城令某，贪而酷，助其虐者多鹰犬之才，爪牙之卫。一髯奴，名摩诃，是长安友人所寄。知文墨，善裁答，令不能物色之也。摩诃尝居静室，终日出，趁两顿饭。归则捉笔书蝇头字至今，夕辄焚之。
    一日，令与幕僚群集，因书屋尘封，蜘丝满架。戏作《讨蜘蛛网檄》，不就。适摩诃自园中执花枝一捆，代作薪炭。令呼至前，曰：“闻尔亦能文，试作此题。”给纸笔。摩诃构思敏捷，一挥而就，曰：
    原夫厉气所钟，毒虫斯螫。贪心遂逞，众物为殃。既罔惜夫众生，但徒供其一饱。从未有凶暴贪噬如蜘蛛结网者。迹其矜善识之名，号无肠之目。画阁雕甍，巧为陷阱；疏篱淡月，暗伏危机。丝丝入扣，晴冒几片红英；密密排空，冷缀半林黄叶。燕子楼中，任作成灰之恨；春晖阁里，谁传惹絮之词。檐前之细雨霏霏，据要津而陇断；树底之轻风习习，立常道以横施。

    Explanation

    176

    If one is not skilled in the four-six style, they may still manage to write a piece that is not vulgar or unpleasant.

    Proclamation Against Spider Webs

    The magistrate of Luocheng, being greedy and cruel, had many capable and loyal assistants who aided in his tyranny. One bearded servant, named Mahakala, was sent by a friend from Chang'an. Mahakala was knowledgeable in literature and skilled in drafting replies, which the magistrate could not find in anyone else. Mahakala often stayed in a quiet room, coming out only for meals. Upon returning, he would take out his brush and write in fine, small characters until evening, when he would burn his work.

    One day, the magistrate and his advisors gathered together. The bookshelf was dusty, and spider webs covered the shelves. They jokingly attempted to write a proclamation against spider webs but failed. Just then, Mahakala returned from the garden with a bundle of flower branches, using them as firewood. The magistrate called him forward and said, "I hear you are also skilled in writing. Try this topic." He gave Mahakala paper and ink. Mahakala thought quickly and finished it in one go, saying:

    By nature, this creature is fierce and venomous. Its greed grows unchecked, causing harm to all living things. It shows no mercy to any creature, only feeding itself. There has never been a more ferocious and greedy creature than the spider that weaves its web. It boasts of its cleverness and cunning, known as the "spider with no guts." It decorates its lair with intricate designs, setting traps in the corners of the room. In the dim light of the moon, it hides its deadly threats. Each thread is carefully placed, as if adorned with a few red flowers; they are densely woven, like a cold tapestry of yellow leaves. In the Yanzilou, it spreads sorrow like ashes; in the Chunhui Pavilion, who will spread the words of its entanglements? The fine rain before the eaves, blocking the path; the gentle breeze beneath the trees, spreading its influence.

    177

    且也杂花幌而左右交通，缘锦屏而远近相属。逞机心而入彀，作私智以拼吞。粉蝶无猜，谩拟四维之举；绿蚁何罪，不为一面之开。一天花事空虚，断送怜香之侣；到处蜂房零落，伤心采蜜之踪。蠛蠓飞来，好似伤弓之鸟；蜻蜒点去，还惊漏罟之鱼。刻以相绳，疏而不漏，啄余血食，竭被脂膏。居然万目齐张，巧布漫夭之计；咸思一网打尽，竟无余地之留。为尔茧丝，到无辜而被逮；多方罗织，纵有翅而难飞。虽在缧绁之中，非其罪也；既入牢笼之内，何所逃焉。尔其食甚于蚕，恶盈夫贯。休夸十里之雾，速撤三匝之围。将灭尔跳梁，且剪尔犄角。画叉轻卷，寸丝不挂于风尘；芳径无翳，败类悉归于剿逐。

    Explanation

    177

    In this network of intertwined flowers and screens, cunning schemes are laid, and private intelligence is used to devour. White butterflies, innocent and unsuspecting, are mistakenly caught in the four corners; green ants, what crime have they committed, that they are not given a chance to escape? The beauty of flowers fades, leaving behind those who once loved their fragrance; bee hives scatter, and the paths of honey gathering are broken. Midges fly in, like birds that have been shot and now fear the bow; dragonflies dart away, still startled by the net. Bound by strict measures, yet not entirely tight, they scavenge the remnants of blood and fat, exhausted and covered in grease. Suddenly, all eyes are open, and a grand plan is carefully laid; everyone thinks of netting everything up, leaving no place to remain. You, with your innocent silk, are captured without cause; from all sides, you are trapped, even with wings, you cannot fly. Though in chains, it is not your fault; once inside the cage, where can you escape? Your consumption is more than that of silkworms, evil has filled the string. Do not boast of a dense mist ten miles thick, quickly break the three layers of encirclement. Your jumping and climbing will be extinguished, your horns will be cut off. The paintbrush lightly rolls, not a thread left hanging in the wind and dust; the fragrant path is clear, all the degenerate ones are completely eliminated.

    178

    众览其词，皆相惊愕。令知其谤己也，遂恶之，既而去。
    后令坐事系御史台狱。亲友无一人致饷问者。忽摩诃来，裘马甚都，旦夕至台门，给饮食。六十余日，令贬敦煌，摩诃送之关外。
    呜呼！摩诃贱役也，抱非常之才，遭非常之困。览其文，虽学问士不过是也。一言之失，其过亦小，继而终始周旋，依依急难，诚有赂医纳玉之风，岂不贤哉。
    (此文本不纯净，然出自驵狯之手，成于俄顷之际，颇非易易，故存其真。)

    种痘说
    种痘不知始于何时。相传昔有善士，虔奉观音。得一子，遇道人授种痘法。伊子出痘数粒，圆润坚好，不药而憩。因传于世，名曰“观音痘”。是种痘之方，原本天授，悯婴儿之遭厄，乃消患于未萌。有回天转日之功，无短折夭亡之祸。相传已久，奏效甚奇。

    Explanation

    178

    Translation:

    All who read his words were greatly surprised. The official realized that he was being slandered, so he disliked him and subsequently left.
    Later, the official was imprisoned in the Imperial Censorate for some offense. No relatives or friends brought him food or inquired about his well-being. Suddenly, Moha came, dressed in fine fur and riding a splendid horse. He visited the court gate every day and night, providing him with food and drink. After more than sixty days, the official was demoted to Dunhuang, and Moha saw him off beyond the pass.
    Alas! Moha was a lowly servant, yet he possessed extraordinary talent and suffered extraordinary hardship. Reading his writings, even scholars are not superior to them. A single word of mistake, though the fault is also small, but then he continued to support him throughout, with deep affection in times of trouble. He truly had the virtue of bribing doctors and accepting jade, isn't that virtuous?
    (This text is not pure, but it was written by a cunning hand, completed in a short time, and it is not easy, so it is preserved as true.)

    Treatise on Vaccination
    It is unknown when vaccination began. It is said that in the past, there was a good man who devoutly worshipped Guanyin. He had a son who met a Taoist who taught him the method of vaccination. His son developed several pustules, which were round, smooth, and firm, and he rested without medication. This method was passed down to the world and was called "Guanyin Pustules." This is the method of vaccination, originally given by heaven, out of compassion for the suffering of infants, it eliminates the disease before it arises. It has the power to turn the sky and the sun, and it avoids the misfortune of premature death. It has been passed down for a long time and has shown remarkable results.

    179

    奈世人不察，或议其矫强，或虑其复出，率多疑阻。既有深信者，亦因循怠惰，迁延时日。迨至天行忽发，燥热外侵，火毒内炙，远近蔓延。一经传染，无论为险为逆，命在须臾。即幸遇顺症，亦劳心竭力，几费经营，始获保全。倘有疏失，悔之无及。若早种痘，决无虑此。
    盖种痘与时痘利害悬殊，时痘猝然而至，种则可待其时，择冷暖调适之候举行。天时既正，自无否塞之忧。时痘一染便发，种则可观其质，俟神气健旺之候下苗。精力既充，自无虚馁之患。时痘之发，人不及知。未熟之时，或冷暖失宜，或饮食失节，或风寒不谨，或跌扑不防，始既失于保护，后遂多其变更。若种痘，则未种之先已为调度，方种之候即投药石，火预清矣，毒预解矣。按期奏绩，保无他虞。

    Explanation

    179

    Alas, the world fails to understand; some criticize it as being artificial or forced, some fear its resurgence, and most are skeptical and obstructive. Even those who deeply believe also become complacent and lazy, procrastinating and delaying. Until a sudden outbreak occurs, external heat invades, internal fire burns fiercely, and it spreads far and wide. Once infected, whether dangerous or adverse, life is at risk in an instant. Even if one encounters a mild case, one still must exert great effort, after much effort and planning, one finally manages to survive. If there is any mistake, it is too late to regret. If one had vaccinated early, there would be no need to worry about this. The benefits and risks of vaccination versus natural infection are vastly different. Natural infection strikes suddenly, while vaccination can be scheduled at a suitable time when the weather is appropriate. The weather is favorable, there is no risk of obstruction. Natural infection occurs immediately upon contact, while vaccination allows for careful observation when the body is strong and healthy. With sufficient energy, there is no risk of weakness. Natural infection occurs without warning; before it fully develops, there may be improper temperature control, or improper diet, or improper care against wind and cold, or accidents go unnoticed, leading to many complications. If one vaccinates, preparation begins before the procedure, and during the vaccination, medicine is administered. The heat is pre-emptively cleared, and the toxins are pre-emptively resolved. It performs as expected, ensuring no other dangers.

    180

    况时痘之感，有邪有正。正者尚虚其险，邪者必至于逆。若种痘之苗，则美中求美，受气之初，既得其正，则见形之后，自无不顺。且所费有限，贫乏者亦可勉为，所出甚稀。人少者亦易照管。种种妥便，难以枚举。而世之迟疑未决者，亦谓种痘不无偶失耳。不知不种而失者十有二三，种痘而失者十或一二。而此一二者，又缘时痘已萌于内，而种痘又施于外。夹杂感发，以致疏虞。若非时痘之际，断不坏事。故种痘者，必当时痘未发，择其苗之泽润圆厚者，择吉种之，自百无失一，永不再出也。其或庸医，知谋利，不审婴儿有无疾病。痘症未现前疾先增，或病家止贪安逸，竟谓种痘不必谨慎，致外感杂投，变起仓猝。此皆人事之误，非种痘之咎也。若果择名医，选嘉种，慎药食，谨风寒，相天之时，因儿之质，依法种治，则婴孩咸免夭折，而登仁寿之域矣。

    Explanation

    180

    In the case of smallpox, there are both harmful and benign forms. The benign form is less dangerous, while the harmful form inevitably leads to complications. However, the smallpox vaccine is superior even among other good options. If administered correctly, it will lead to a smooth and uncomplicated outcome. Moreover, the cost is limited, and even the poor can afford it. The vaccine produces very few side effects, and it is easy to manage, especially for children. The benefits are numerous and too many to list. Yet, those who hesitate to vaccinate often believe that the vaccine can sometimes fail. In reality, the failure rate of natural infection is much higher than that of vaccination. The vaccine will not cause harm unless there is a natural smallpox outbreak. Therefore, the vaccine should be administered before a natural outbreak, choosing the best and most appropriate vaccine and administering it at the right time. This will ensure a hundred percent success rate and prevent future outbreaks. However, some unskilled doctors may prioritize profit over patient care, failing to examine the child for any underlying illnesses. If the child has an underlying illness, it may worsen before the vaccine takes effect, or the family may be too lazy to take proper care. Improper care or external influences can lead to sudden complications. These complications are due to human error, not the vaccine itself. If one chooses a skilled doctor, selects a good vaccine, and follows proper care and precautions, the vaccine will be successful. This will prevent premature death and lead to a long, healthy life.

    181

    今南方多行之。吾乡咸以为伪，盖痘症最盛于南，又起于中古，亦气数之积，渐沉溺使然也。犹之乎五谷之熟，上古无树艺之法而亦熟。自树艺之法行，而五谷未有不树艺而熟者矣。今日之视谷焉，知非后人之视痘。故据所见，为未知种痘者劝。
    (按《医宗金鉴》载：古有种痘一法，起自江右，达于京畿，究其所源，自宋真宗时，峨眉山有神人出，为丞相王旦之子种痘而愈，遂传于世。)

    秦桧墓诗
    秦桧墓，在建康。岁久榛芜，有盗窃发之，被执，赴部鞫。末减其罪，盖恶桧也，非纵盗也。时有诗快之曰：
    权奸构陷孤忠残，二帝中原不复还。恨无英主即显戮，至今遗臭江皋间。
    当时殉葬多奇宝，玉童金绳恣工巧。荒芜无主野人耕，狐兔为群石羊倒。

    Explanation

    181

    In the south, this practice is widely followed. Everyone in my hometown considers it fake, as smallpox is most prevalent in the south and originated in the middle ages. It is also due to accumulated fate and gradual indulgence. Just as grain matures naturally without cultivation methods in ancient times, but since cultivation methods were introduced, no grain matures without them. Today, our view of grain differs from how we view smallpox. Therefore, based on what I have seen, I advise those who do not know about vaccination.

    (According to Medicine's Golden Mirror: There was an ancient method of vaccination that originated in Jiangxi and spread to the capital region. Its source can be traced back to the reign of Emperor Zhenzong of Song, when a divine being appeared on Mount Emei and vaccinated the son of Chancellor Wang Dan, who then recovered. The method was subsequently passed down through the ages.)

    Poem about Qin Hui's Tomb

    Qin Hui's tomb is located in Jiankang. Over the years, it has become overgrown with weeds. Thieves broke into it, but they were caught and taken to the authorities for questioning. Their punishment was reduced, as the authorities detested Qin Hui, not because they were condoning theft. At the time, a poem was written to express this sentiment:

    Powerful villains framed loyal officials, leaving the two emperors of the Central Plains unable to return. It is regrettable that there was no wise ruler to execute him immediately, so that his name would be forever disgraced in the Jiang River area. At that time, many precious treasures were buried with him, crafted with exquisite workmanship. Now, the tomb is overgrown and abandoned, with wild people tilling the land, and foxes and rabbits gathering in groups, while stone sheep lie overturned.

    182

    一朝被发无全躯，若假盗手得天诛。于戏浙土鄂王墓，松柏森森天壤俱。
    三字狱不报于子孙，而乃假手于异代之盗贼，显暴其身。报不爽矣。诗亦高古。

    寺壁诗
    丙午之岁，江南大荒，流亡殆尽。有姑嫂二人，不知何许人，且不知其姓氏。乞食吴门，题诗寺壁二首。其一已自涂抹，仅留一首云：
    萧然行李此经过，只为年荒受折磨。踏破绣鞋穿竹径，吹残云鬓入风涡。
    叩门乞食推恩少，仰面求人忍辱多。欲赋归与归未得，夕阳回首泪滂沱。
    读之怆然欲绝。妇人能诗，其亦穷而后工乎！

    上寮翁焙鸭论
    上寮翁，不知其姓氏，广东顺德人。后东北上寮里，年最高，因呼“上寮翁”。性恬静，常独坐凝思，终日不与人交一语。又或蒙被卧一日两日，废饮食。人或问之曰：“翁何思而何虑耶？”翁曰：“古人多所造作，以利后进，吾亦渺然中处，岂剧不能创一事，名一技，辟独出之新裁。离前人之窠臼，使天下后世皆知有上寮翁哉！”

    Explanation

    182

    One day, when the hair is unbound, the entire body is exposed; if one allows a thief's hand to gain heavenly punishment. Alas, the tomb of the King of E in Zhejiang, with pines and cypress trees standing tall and firm in the world.

    A case involving three characters is not reported to descendants; instead, it is used by thieves from a different era, exposing the body publicly. The retribution is certain and accurate. The poetry is both lofty and ancient in style.

    Temple Wall Poetry

    In the year of Bingwu, there was a great famine in southern Jiangnan, and almost everyone had fled. There were two women, a mother-in-law and her daughter-in-law, whose origin and names are unknown. They begged for food in Wumen and inscribed two poems on temple walls. One of them had already been defaced, leaving only one poem, which says:

    Desolate travelers pass through here, only because of the famine year and suffering. Worn out embroidered shoes walk through bamboo paths, and disheveled hair is blown apart by the whirlwind. Knocking on doors to beg for food, receiving little kindness; looking up to ask for help, enduring much shame. Wanting to write about returning home but unable to, tears flow at sunset when looking back.

    Reading it causes intense grief. Can women write poetry? Perhaps they become skilled at it after experiencing poverty!

    Discussion of Roasting Ducks by Old Man Shangliao

    Old Man Shangliao, whose surname and name are unknown, is from Shunde, Guangdong. Later, he moved to Shangliao Village in the northeast, where he is the oldest in age, thus called "Old Man Shangliao." He is calm and peaceful in nature, often sitting alone and thinking deeply, not speaking to anyone all day. Sometimes, he sleeps under a blanket for one or two days, abandoning food and drink. Someone asked him, "What are you thinking and worrying about, old man?" He replied, "Ancient people created many things to benefit future generations. I am also in a humble position, but how can I not create something, name a skill, and innovate a unique style that breaks away from the conventions of previous generations, so that people in the future will all know of Old Man Shangliao!"

    183

    后翁得焙鸭法，遂以为业。因传其论曰：“造化一炉锤耳。惟大力者操之，则生气磅礴，随处可流衍推暨。苟得其主宰，即返之径寸而不诬。古人志其大者远者，我小人也，则务织悉而亦有合焉。
    “吾思以雌伏之不多孳也，因集卵千百，为筐数十，置之暖房，承之土炉，覆以衣被，环以木屑，种火其下，候气于旁，文煨武爆，各有其道，或设虚筐，或列椸架，得火小温，翌日自热。寒则闭户，燥则揭窗。由是三日而之上，六日复下之，转徒圆周。十一朝，乃复灯影照而日光映，去其蠹而溃者。又悉登之床第，至所藉之绵草，按时递减。入其室则童童然，抚其孕则煦煦然。通之月计，而雏孽孳，声唼唼，啄壳出振毡鸣矣。

    Explanation

    183

    After my father learned the method of roasting ducks, he took it up as his profession. He then passed down his theory, saying: "Creation is like a furnace and hammer. Only those with great power can control it, and then the vital energy becomes grand and expansive, able to flow and spread everywhere. If one can master it, one can reduce it to a small size without losing its essence. Ancient people aimed for grand and distant goals, while I, as a common person, focus on the details and still align with these broader principles.

    "I thought about the fact that female ducks do not reproduce frequently, so I collected thousands of eggs and made dozens of baskets. I placed them in a warm room, supported by a clay stove, covered with blankets, and surrounded by wood shavings. I lit a fire below and waited for the heat to spread. I used a method of cooking that involved both gentle heating and intense bursts of heat, each with its own technique. Sometimes I set up empty baskets or arranged racks. Once the fire was slightly warm, it would continue to heat up the next day. When it was cold, I closed the door, and when it was dry, I opened the window. In this way, I moved the eggs up and down in a circular motion over three and six days. On the eleventh day, the eggs were illuminated by both lamp and sunlight, and I removed the rotten or damaged ones. I then placed all the eggs on the bed, gradually reducing the amount of soft grass used as padding. When I entered the room, it felt lively, and when I touched the eggs, they felt warm and nurturing. By the end of the month, the chicks hatched, making sounds and pecking out of their shells."

    184

    “业是者如稳娘，如衽妇。其心不杂，其身欲亲，其志须勤，其火候宜匀，其有事于左右之卑幼役力，皆毋许其哗噪而悖戾。盖和而群，然后蕃以息也。至耳目官骸为之收视返听，若澄潭古井之在前。如是乎雏肥而多育，且速长。凡畜养者，所以挟笯肩栅竞趋吾门之若鹜也。吾用是获利，以衣食与庐。今传是法已广云。”
    焙鸡较焙鸭尤易。上寮翁，康熙时人。

    骨种羊考
    羊皮有骨种称者，春裘也，百金一裼，时人贵之。色纯而螺缜，可为冠缘。或曰是一羊也，何以骨种名？从来卉植之类，丽土而生，蠕动之物，含气以育。昔人有种米植羊之喻，谓事理之必无也。

    Explanation

    184

    "Those who excel in their craft are like a steady mother and a gentle wife. Their mind is focused, their body desires closeness, their will must be diligent, and their timing must be even. When dealing with younger or lower-ranking helpers, one should not allow them to be noisy or rebellious. Harmony and unity lead to prosperity and rest. When one's senses and body are drawn inward, like a clear, ancient well in front of them, the young will grow fat, multiply, and mature quickly. All who raise animals will flock to the door, eager to learn the method. I use this method to profit, providing for my clothing, food, and shelter. Now the method has been widely spread. Roasting chickens is easier than roasting ducks. The Elderly Man from the Kangxi era."

    "Study of the Bone-Seed Sheep"

    Sheep whose wool is prized for its quality are called "bone-seed" sheep. Their wool is used for spring coats, and a single coat can be worth a hundred gold coins, highly valued by people of the time. The wool is pure and fine, suitable for trimming the edges of hats. Some ask, if it is just one sheep, why is it called "bone-seed"? From ancient times, plants grow from the soil, and living creatures are nurtured by their essence. There is an analogy of planting rice and raising sheep, suggesting that such things are impossible.

    185

    然以骨种之名，则又似有可据者。按北齐高昂《从征行》曰：“垄种千口羊，泉连百壶酒。朝朝围山猎，夜夜迎新妇。”浦江吴立夫，有种羊皮书褥歌：
    尝道刳刀羊可食，土城留种羊胫骨。四围筑垣闻杆声，羊子还从胫骨生。青草丛抽脐未断，马蹄踣绝绕垣行。
    楚石大师还古诗有“自言羊可种，不信茧成丝”之句。人以问师，师曰：“大漠迤西，人能种羊。取羊骨以初冬末日，埋地中；初春末日为吹笳咒语，即有小羊从地中出。凡埋骨一具，可得子羊数只。双槐岁杪：西域人杀羊而食，埋其胫骨，举杆坚筑，久之羔从胫骨而生，脐未断。时马旁踏振之，即跳跃而起。入馔肥腴，其皮宜作书褥。西使记垄种羊出西海，以术脐乃断，即能啮草。至秋可食。脐内复有种。《异物志》：“大秦国北有羊子生于土中，秦人候其欲萌，为垣绕之，其脐连地不断，以刀截，击鼓惊之而绝，因跳鸣食草”。

    Explanation

    185

    However, by the name of bone-planting, it seems to have some basis. According to Gao Ang of the Northern Qi Dynasty's From the Campaign, it is said: "Planting a thousand sheep in the ridges, connecting a hundred pots of wine with springs. Surrounding the mountains for hunting every day, welcoming a new bride every night." Wu Lifu of Pujiang composed a song about planting sheepskin book cushions:

    Once said that a sheep can be eaten with a knife, leaving the shin bone of the sheep in the earth wall. Surrounding the wall, hearing the sound of the pole, the young sheep still grow from the shin bone. Green grass growing, the umbilical cord not yet cut, the horse's hooves trampling around the wall.

    The Master Chushi's ancient poem has the line: "He says sheep can be planted, but he doesn't believe silk can be spun from cocoons." People asked the master, and he said: "In the vast desert to the west, people can plant sheep. Taking the sheep bone on the last day of early winter, burying it in the ground; on the last day of early spring, blowing the horn and reciting incantations, and then small sheep appear from the ground. For each buried bone, several young sheep can be obtained. At the end of the year, Westerners kill sheep and eat them, burying the shin bones and firmly building the pole. After a while, the lambs grow from the shin bones, with the umbilical cord not yet cut. When the horse steps beside them, they immediately jump up. When eaten, they are fat and delicious, and their skin is suitable for making book cushions. The Western envoy recorded that the ridge-planted sheep come from the Western Sea, and with the technique, the umbilical cord is cut, and they can immediately eat grass. By autumn, they can be eaten. There is another kind inside the umbilical cord. The Record of Strange Things says: 'In the north of the Great Qin Kingdom, there are sheep born from the earth.' The Qin people wait for it to sprout, building walls around it. Its umbilical cord is not cut from the ground, cut with a knife, strike the drum to startle it, and it jumps and eats grass."

    186

    今闽粤有种蛎房，有种蛏田，以壳为灰，按时撒之，则翌岁蛏蛎丛生其间。由是言之，固然无足怪者。而实则出于四生胎化之外也。

    贾凫西鼓词
    木皮散客，曲阜贾凫西也。少负辩才，好说鼓词。尝于诸生塾宰官厅，及稠人广众中，持小鼓木板掀髯开喉为快。自明经迁部曹，明鼎革不仕。恒笑骂人，不容于乡，移滋阳县。尉挟之，贾怒起。旧官会奉使过里门，执县尉扑于阶下，曰：“此桓侯鞭挞督邮故事也。”不数月引病，不得，乃密属当事劾以说稗词，废政务，果免归。科头跣足，自如也。
    凡与臣言忠，与子言孝，无不以稗词，正不屑屑于寻章摘句，效老生常谈。其摹拟古人处，莫不须眉毕现。又别出蹊径，独抒胸臆。能使古帝王卿相哲愚贤奸是非，由我自定，真操乎物所不遁，而沉郁顿挫，亢坠疾徐之间，环而观听者，尽为咋舌。

    Explanation

    186

    In Fujian and Guangdong, there are oyster beds and clam fields. The shells are ground into lime and spread periodically, resulting in an abundance of clams and oysters the following year. By this account, it is indeed nothing to be surprised about. Yet, in reality, it transcends the conventional categories of biological reproduction (四生胎化).

    Jia Fuxi's Drum Ballad
    Wooden Skin Recluse, Jia Fuxi of Qufu. Young and gifted with eloquence, he enjoyed reciting drum ballads. He would often perform in the study halls of scholars, official halls, and in crowded public gatherings, holding a small drum and wooden board, raising his beard, and opening his throat for entertainment. After being promoted from a scholar-official to a departmental official during the Ming Dynasty, he chose not to serve during the upheaval of the Ming. He was known for his sharp wit and often mocked people, which made him unwelcome in his hometown, leading him to move to Ziyang County. When the county magistrate detained him, Jia Fuxi became enraged and stood up. An old official who happened to be passing through the village arrested the county magistrate and beat him on the steps, saying, "This is the story of Prince Huan beating the postal officer." Within a few months, Jia Fuxi claimed illness, but was not allowed to leave. He secretly instructed those in power to accuse him of reciting trivial ballads, neglecting official duties, and was eventually dismissed and returned home. He went bareheaded and barefoot, remaining completely at ease.

    In all matters, whether speaking to a minister about loyalty or to a son about filial piety, he used ballads. He did not bother with searching for and quoting classical texts, imitating the usual clichés. In his imitation of ancient figures, he vividly portrayed their characteristics. He also carved out his own path, expressing his own thoughts. He could determine the right and wrong of ancient emperors, ministers, wise and foolish, good and evil, entirely at his own discretion. Truly, he held the reins of all things, and in his profound and restrained style, with its rises and falls, fast and slow rhythms, those who gathered around to listen were all amazed.

    187

    晚岁著书数十卷，文字雅俚不伦，与沛县阎古古、诸城丁野鹤，亡命时，往来最密。其论语稗词，为东塘采入《桃花扇》中，历代史略。
    余尝听人唱演，今于李山亭处，又见“孟子齐人”一段，附录于后。
    话说孔圣人周游列国，用世情殷。王孙贾劝他媚灶，他又说获罪天；弥子瑕要送他卫卿，他又说得失有命。虽是美玉思沽，到底不肯诡遇求合。这是个万代宗师，能守出处之正。竟有一班游说之徒，不以为法，执鞭欣慕。甚且舔痔吮痈，甘心乐受。在他自己，觉得处世原该如此。那想有几个睁着眼的，看了替他一阵阵的脸上出火。所以晏平仲家使车的，何尝不洋洋得意。到捱了他老婆一顿臭骂。你看这个妇人，到还有些志气。我们男子汉大丈夫，为甚么不挽起眉毛成一个人？在下因取《盂子》中齐人一篇，编成几句鼓词儿，要在列位搢绅先生之前，聊为聒耳。

    Explanation

    187

    In his later years, he wrote dozens of volumes, the style of which was a mix of elegant and vulgar, and during his exile, he had the closest relationship with Yan Gugu of Pei County and Ding Yeha of Zhucheng. His miscellaneous words in the style of the Analects were incorporated into The Peach Blossom Fan and a brief history of past dynasties. I have often heard people perform and recite these works, and now at Li Shanting's place, I have seen a section from the Mencius titled "The Man from Qi," which I will append here.

    The story goes that Confucius, the great teacher, traveled among the various states with a strong desire to serve in the world. Wang Sun Jia advised him to curry favor with the gods, but Confucius said that such actions would bring him divine retribution. Mi Zi Xia offered him the position of prime minister in the state of Wei, but Confucius said that success and failure are matters of fate. Although he sought to sell his talents like a precious jade, he would not compromise his principles to gain favor. This is why he is considered the foremost teacher of all time, able to maintain his integrity in both public and private life. Yet there were some who did not follow his example, eagerly seeking positions and flattering those in power. Some even went so far as to lick sores and suck pus, gladly accepting such treatment. From their own perspective, they believed that this was how one should conduct oneself in the world. However, there were a few who, with their eyes open, watched and felt a burning anger at such behavior. Thus, Yan Ying, who was satisfied with his position, was once scolded by his wife. Look at this woman; she still has some spirit. Why can't we men lift our eyebrows and become someone? I have taken the story of the man from Qi in the Mencius and composed a few verses to serve as a distraction before the scholars and officials.

    188

    自古英雄命运艰，(就如那)孔、孟原来一脉传。(到处里)秉政当朝扬着脸，(谁肯向他)下巴底下吸吐涎。(第一个)梁惠王(就是)钱痨鬼，(再看他)养的儿子更不堪。(又有个)滕国(里，井田才合起)了局，(来了个)太荒唐(的，许行散)了班，(笑盈盈)荐贤有了乐正子，(又遇着)兔羔灭仓打醋坛。(遥望着)地广民稠齐国好，(无奈他)掉蛋齐宣性不长。(都只为)好货好色还好勇，(一说要)发政施仁不上前。(教一班)狗头狗脑胡揭弄，(苦煞了)执古搊板邹峄贤。

    Explanation

    188

    Since ancient times, heroes have had a difficult fate, just like Confucius and Mencius, who were originally part of the same lineage. Everywhere, those in power in the court look down on others, but who would lick their lips under their chin in flattery? The first was King Hui of Wei, a miserly person, and his son was even more unworthy. In the state of Teng, the well-field system was just established, but then came Xu Xing, who was very absurd and disbanded the class. With a smile, he recommended the virtuous man Le Zhengzi, but then encountered chaos as the rabbit destroyed the granary and the vinegar jar. Looking afar at the state of Qi, with its vast land and dense population, it seemed good, but it was short-lived under Duke Xuan. It was all because he loved wealth, beauty, and bravery, and when it came to implementing benevolent policies, he did not move forward. A bunch of foolish people acted foolishly, greatly troubling the ancient and strict Zou Yi.

    189

    前言按下不提，单说齐国有一个人，他的姓名不著，里居不详。只因他八字里喜的是双妻压命，又坐着一层狠旺的食神。所以在家有妻妾陪伴，出外就有酒肉饮食。若不扒着他的根子，看破他的行藏，只看他驴屎蛋外面光，那知这个齐人是丢德败行，真乃不作而不堪之至者。
    (是谁人)教会了(这个情现成的)法，(管保你)走遍天涯饿不煞。(整日里)东蹭西蹭瞎打混，(这行子)守(什么)田园顾(什么)家。(半边瓢)就是他的传家宝，(打狗棒)还仗(着是)他护身法。(只看他)一上门来先惹怪，(还在那)十字街头弄死蛇。(只都是)好吃懒做馋狗嘴，(积作作)赶着人家叫爹妈。

    Explanation

    189

    Skipping the introduction, let us speak of a man from the state of Qi. His name and place of residence are unknown. It is said that his birth chart indicated a preference for having two wives, and he also had a strong and prosperous "food star" in his chart. Therefore, he had concubines at home and access to fine food and drink when he went out. If one does not delve into his background and see through his true nature, one might only notice his superficial appearance, not realizing that this man from Qi was morally corrupt and utterly unworthy.

    Who taught him the ways of taking advantage of others' kindness? You can travel far and wide without starving. He wanders aimlessly and does nothing productive, pretending to care for his fields and family. Half a ladle is all he has to pass down, and the stick used to beat dogs is also his defensive tool. As soon as he enters a place, he starts causing trouble, making a scene in public. He is a lazy, greedy person who always looks for free food, always trying to get people to treat him like a son or daughter.

    190

    这齐人终日浪游，乞丐为生。一出门来，必然讨个醉饱。若是他妻妾知道来由，怎肯与他干休。谁知道齐妇并无有个耳报神，那齐人却到有了障眼法。那一日，吃的醉醺醺的从外昂然进门，一腔排下厉声高叫“快看茶来”。这齐妇不敢怠慢，不多一时，小婆子捧过茶来。齐人吃了，接去杯子。他二人坐着，就刮拉闲话起来。齐妇开言道：“尊声孩子达，凡你出门去，醉饱才还家。我且问问你，都是和谁呀？”
    (待说是)邻里乡党闲话客，(也不过是)一半遭儿话桑麻。(似你这)天天有酒天天醉，(我不懂)摆席人家为甚吗。(待说是)一家一日车轮会，(你也该)一来一往把锯拉。(总就是)男儿慷慨尊常满，(你对我说)也好(见他媳妇)谢谢他。
    这齐妇圆圆款款问了一遍。看起齐人，急忙里难以登答。谁知他早已料着有此一问，预先编就一套瞎话。有枝有叶便应声，答道：你要问我的朋友，都不是寻常小户人家哩。你且站在一旁听我道来。

    Explanation

    190

    This Qi man wandered aimlessly all day, making a living by begging. Whenever he went out, he would inevitably drink to his fill. If his wife and concubines knew the reason, they would surely not let it go. Who would have thought that the Qi woman had no ear for gossip, while the Qi man had a trick up his sleeve. On that day, he came home drunk and entered with a proud and confident demeanor, shouting loudly, "Quick, bring me some tea!" The Qi woman dared not slack off and, not long after, a maid brought over the tea. The Qi man drank it and took the cup. The two of them sat down and started chatting idly. The Qi woman spoke up, "Dear child, when you go out, you return home only after drinking and eating your fill. Let me ask you, who were you with?"

    (Perhaps it was) neighbors and fellow villagers chatting, (not much more than) half the time talking about farming and mulberry cultivation. (Like you) who drinks every day and gets drunk every day, (I don't understand) why there is a banquet at the house. (Perhaps it was) a family gathering where everyone takes turns speaking, (you should) back and forth like sawing wood. (In short) a man should always be generous and full of spirit, (you told me) it's also good to thank him (in front of his wife).

    The Qi woman asked him gently and patiently. Looking at the Qi man, she was unable to answer quickly. Who would have thought that he had already anticipated this question and prepared a set of fabricated stories. With a detailed and elaborate response, he answered, "If you want to know about my friends, they are not ordinary commoners. Stand aside and listen to me."

    The Qi woman asked him gently and patiently. Looking at the Qi man, she was unable to answer quickly. Who would have thought that he had already anticipated this question and prepared a set of fabricated stories. With a detailed and elaborate response, he answered, "If you want to know about my friends, they are not ordinary commoners. Stand aside and listen to me."

    191

    (这齐人)未曾开口样先梭，(高叫声)他娘们(住站)听我说。(那都象)下等之辈穷朋友，(怎么能)整日弄酒蒸馍馍。(头一个是)王欢右师齐国相，(他与我)朝暮相见饮宴多。(还有那)副相储子把我请，(着管家)骑着马来牵着驴。(又有了)驸马淳于好酒量，(他与我)论斗论石加班驳。(吃了些)刍豢悦口秦人炙，(吃了些)四境邻家鸡几窝。(吃了些)鲜鱼熊掌真我欲，(吃了些)胡龁羊羹陈戴鹅。(遇着那)庄暴见了往家拉，(走到了)沈同门前向里拖。(那一日)陈贾求我(去辟)王惭，(耽搁下)距心蚳鼍酒许多。(至于那)时子景丑不须说，(最厚的)惯弄嘴头盆成括。(我昨日)公行子家去吊孝，(说不尽)酒席宴前宾客多。(可惜我)没有这些闲腿跑，(那一天)不接帖子一大摞。

    Explanation

    191

    (Though he) hadn't even opened his mouth yet, he already began to chatter, (shouting loudly) "Listen to me, you lot!" (These were) lowly people and poor friends, (how could they) spend the whole day making wine and steaming buns. (The first one was) Wang Huan, the Right Minister and Prime Minister of Qi, (who) saw me morning and evening with many feasts. (And then there was) Deputy Prime Minister Chu Zi who invited me, (with his) steward riding a horse and leading a donkey. (Then there was) Prince Chunyu, who was known for his good drinking capacity, (who) drank in large quantities with me, adding to the confusion. (I ate some) roasted meat from Qin that was pleasing to the taste, (some) chickens from neighboring households, (some) fresh fish and bear paws, truly what I desired, (and some) dishes made from roasted sheep and a dish made from roasted goose. (When I encountered) Zhuang Bao, he pulled me home, (and when I walked) to Shen Tong's door, he dragged me inside. (That day) Chen Jia asked me to avoid the King's embarrassment, (and we) delayed the heart and mind with many toad and crocodile wines. (As for that time) Zi Jingchou does not need to be mentioned, (the most intimate one) always talking and playing with words, Peng Chengkua. (Yesterday) I went to Gong Xing's house to mourn, (there were) too many guests at the banquet to count. (Unfortunately, I) don't have the free time to run around, (that day) I received a stack of invitations.

    192

    你说这妇人家是最好哄，昕了齐人这一席话说，直喜得抓耳挠腮，批牙裂嘴。就如受封赠的一般，不由得在齐人面前加意奉承，无可不可。点上灯，铺了床，撮拥着齐人睡下。自己坐在一旁，辗转思量，不觉有几分狐疑起来。俗语说得好，肩膀齐的是亲戚，三钱不合二钱的拱手。我那良人如何就有那些富贵人合他相与，到底想个法儿扒扒他的根子才好。便抽身来厨房，找着小婆子说道：“我有一句话合你说哩。”其妻唤其妾说件事：“你听：提起咱良人，本来是穷精。如何出门去，回家醉酩酊。殆说买着吃，腰里没半文。方才问他道，他把大话烹。

    Explanation

    192

    The woman said, "Women are the easiest to deceive. When she heard what the man from Qi said, she was so delighted that she scratched her ears and cheeks, showing her excitement. It was as if she had received an official title or honor. She couldn't help but flatter him in front of him, agreeing to whatever he said. She lit the lamp, made the bed, and helped the man from Qi to bed. She sat by herself, thinking over and over again, and began to feel a bit doubtful. As the saying goes, 'People who share the same level of social status are considered relatives,' and 'People with different levels of social status cannot be close friends.' How could my husband have dealings with those wealthy and powerful people? I must think of a way to investigate his background. She then got up and went to the kitchen, found the maid, and said, 'I have something to tell you.' The wife called her concubine and said, 'Listen: My husband was originally very poor. How could he go out and come home drunk? Probably he bought something to eat, but he had no money at all. Just now I asked him, and he spoke boastfully.'

    193

    (他就说)同桌食的无贫贱，(尽都是)官宦人家富贵翁。(都是些)骑驴压马有势力，(都是些)穿袍戴帽大乡绅。(我想来)富贵人家眼眶大，(为什么)待咱良人这样亲，(要说是)贵而忘势富好礼，(为什么)全然不到咱家中。(虽然是)柴门难容车驷马，(须知道)相交何论富和穷。(这其间)不知真来不知假，(只恐怕)良人是个瞎话精。(我安排)偷出兰房看一看，(科子呀)是备(的休要给俺走)了风。”
    这齐妇对着小婆嘱咐一回，转到卧房，自觉心中有事，一夜不曾合眼。忽听鸡叫，他便一骨碌爬将起来。裹了裹脚，拢了拢头，札刮的停停当当，单看良人如何举动。
    话说齐人睡到天明，慌忙起身，披了衣服，对妇道：“今日是某老大人请吃早饭，须当速去。”遂迈步出门，徜徉而走。他那里知道大令正随后跟将来也。

    193

    Translation

    (He said) All eat the same regardless of social status, (all are) officials' families and wealthy elders. (All are) those who ride donkeys and horses with power, (all are) those who wear robes and hats, great gentry. (I think) the wealthy have a high opinion of themselves, (why) treat our good man so kindly? (If it's because) the rich forget their power and the wealthy are good at etiquette, (why) not visit our home at all? (Although) our humble home cannot accommodate a carriage and four horses, (one must know) that friendship does not distinguish between rich and poor. (In this case) I don't know if it's true or false, (only fear) that the good man is a liar. (I arranged) to sneak out of the inner chamber to take a look, (servant, don't let me go) the wind.”

    This woman of Qi instructed her maid for a while, then returned to her bedroom, feeling something on her mind, and did not sleep all night. Suddenly hearing the rooster crow, she sprang up in one go. She wrapped up her feet, combed her hair, and dressed herself neatly and tidily, just to see how the good man behaved.
    It was said that the man of Qi woke up at dawn, hurriedly got up, put on his clothes, and told his wife, “Today is an invitation from a certain old gentleman for breakfast, we must go quickly.” He then stepped out of the door and walked leisurely. He did not know that the magistrate was following him.

    194

    (那齐人)伸头缩脑前边走，(这齐妇)跷蹄捏脚在后跟。(那一个)只怕晚了赶不上，(这一个)只怕慢了看不真。(眼见的)雪宫门口不歇脚，(眼见他)驸马府前不留停。(早来到)斗鸡市上无人问，(又过了)庄狱街市谁欠身。(只见他)临淄走遍三万户，(没一个)路上行人叫一声。(看一看)湫尘隘巷人烟少，(难道他)结识了(晏相的旧)儿孙。(说不尽)妇人(满怀痴想)筹思意，(你看那)齐人(一溜迸星出了东郭)门。
    且说齐人放开大步，头也不抬，一直出了东门，走尽关厢，已是墦闾的所在。这齐妇跟到此处，把一腔热肠也就冷了一半，想来也无好处。待要回去，却是来做什么。犹豫一回，把鞋提了一提，牙根咬了一咬，道既到宝山，那有空回之理，少不得跟他走上一走。
    说起这齐妇，也算放的泼。一路跟了来，何曾住住脚。挨肩擦膀子，不知有许多。无人问一问，一直出东郊。

    Explanation

    194

    The man from Qi peeked out cautiously in front, while the woman from Qi walked with small, careful steps behind him. The man was afraid he would be late and miss something, while the woman feared she was moving too slowly to see clearly. They hurried past the Snow Palace without stopping, and did not pause in front of the Imperial Son-in-Law's Mansion. They arrived at the Cockfighting Market where no one paid attention, and passed by the Street of the City Prison where no one bowed. They walked through three thousand households in Linzi, but not a single passerby called out. They saw dusty, narrow alleys with few people, and wondered if the man had made friends with the descendants of Yan Xiang. The woman was full of foolish thoughts and careful planning, while the man darted out of the Eastern Gate like a shooting star.

    The man from Qi took long strides, his head down, and went straight through the eastern gate, walking through the outskirts until he reached the area of Fanli. The woman from Qi followed him to this place, and her warm-heartedness cooled by half, thinking there was no benefit to it. She considered going back, but wondered what she had come for.
