After hesitating, she pulled up her shoes, bit her teeth, and thought, "Having come to such a treasure trove, how could one return empty-handed? She must follow him for a while."

    Speaking of the woman from Qi, she was quite bold. She had followed him all the way without stopping. She brushed against many people's shoulders and arms, but no one asked her anything. She went straight out of the eastern suburbs.

    195

    (望一望)松林黄土，到处是(你向这)荒冢麒麟做什么(待说是)明日出吊东郭氏，(都怎的)昨夜枕边没提掇。(待说是)东门祖帐饯行客，(怎没个)良朋折柳坝桥河。(又只见)几家坟上哭声哀，(他那里)摆开一抬大祭盒。(这齐人)一见喜的旋风转，(来了他)五脏庙里救命佛。(大叉步)直到纸(钱蝴蝶灰)飞去，(只见他)咕咚倒地半截矬。(那些人)看不上他(那花)子相，(给了他)一壶奠酒(两个)供馍。(这人)饿狗抢食尽着吃，(却不道)气杀听风俊俏婆。(他这里)扑簌簌(泪珠儿不住的)掉，(他那里)刮搭(着嘴皮还四下)里睃。(天杀的)实指望(华堂开宴吃大)酒，(谁知道)乱葬岗头扳剩馍。(我悔恨)当初不该(来看)这看，(到弄的)进退两难无奈何。(这齐妇)跢(了跢)金莲回去罢，(他还在)坟(子旁里嚼着骨头就)酒掉。

    Explanation

    195

    (To take a look) at the pine forest and yellow earth, everywhere are (these) desolate graves with stone beasts, what are you doing here? (It seems) that tomorrow you will go to mourn the East Gate family, (but) how come nothing was mentioned by your side last night? (It seems) that there is a farewell banquet at the East Gate to bid farewell to travelers, (yet) why is there no good friend breaking off willow branches by the dam and river? (Again, I see) several graves with sorrowful cries, (there) is a large offering box set up. (This man) turns around with joy upon seeing someone, (he) is like a savior in the temple of his five organs. (He takes a large step) until the ashes of burnt paper money fly away, (then) he collapses to the ground, half-bent. (Those people) did not like his beggar-like appearance, (they gave him) a bottle of wine and two offerings of bread. (This man) eats voraciously like a starving dog, (yet) the pretty woman was angry and killed by hearing the wind. (Here) tears fall continuously, (there) with a sullen expression, looking around. (Damn it) I really hoped for a grand banquet in a beautiful hall, (but) who knew it would be scavenging for leftover food at a desolate burial ground. (I regret) that I should not have looked at this place in the first place, (now I am) in a difficult position, unable to decide. (This woman) walks away with a light step, (he) is still chewing bones and drinking alcohol by the grave.

    196

    却说齐妇原当他良人是个人物，看了回去好对小婆子说说，大家着实欢喜。谁知是这副嘴脸，只得扭身就走。正是乘兴而来，倒做了败兴而返。踉踉跄跄，到了自己门首，一推而进。说道“可了不的了”。
    齐妇把门进，气的脸焦黄。未曾张开口，擎着泪两行。说起那孩子，教人痛断肠。
    (每日里)擎着他当做(男儿)汉，(谁料他)连(狗底子孩牙也)看不上。(满城里)无人和他说句话，(直走到)东门以外乱葬岗。(谁知他)去跪人前讨着吃，(叫不了)剩菜剩饭好爷娘。(你不信)趁着此时(去看)一看，(未必不)还掇着(半碗豆)腐汤。(这齐妇)诉罢良人前后事，(只红了)两对眼眶泪四行。(这个说)管这营生没志气，(那个说)从今顾他什么娘。(这个说)强人杀的死了罢，(那个说)见人怎好把嘴张。

    Explanation

    196

    It is said that the woman from Qi originally thought her husband was a respectable person, and she wanted to tell the young maid about it, and everyone would be very happy. However, she found out he was not what she thought, so she had to leave angrily. This is how she came with high hopes but left in disappointment. She staggered to her own door and pushed it open, saying, "What a shame, what a shame."

    The woman from Qi entered her home, her face ashen with anger. She had not even opened her mouth when tears streamed down her face. She recounted the good and bad of her husband, making one's heart break.

    Every day, she held him as if he were a man, but who would look down on him even as a dog. Nobody in the city would speak to him, and he went straight to the outskirts of the east gate, the place of disordered graves. He knelt before others to beg for food, oh dear parents, leftover food and scraps. Take this opportunity to have a look, perhaps he is still holding a half-bowl of bean curd soup.

    After recounting the good and bad of her husband, only her eyes were red with four streams of tears. Some said that this kind of life is without dignity, and from now on, forget about him. Others said that kill the strong ones and die, but how can one speak in front of people?

    197

    (且不说)二人家中打碟碗，(又来了)装模作样那不良。(咳你这)不觉(死的鬼儿还起什么)调(粗喉咙)大噪子，(还叫孩们)的娘。(正待要)端起身子弄大款，(看了看)一家哭的好凄惶。(住了脚)支蒙起(耳朵才听)一听，(说了个)东郭墦间就心慌。(一煞时)毛遂没了隐身草，(可罢了)火焰山前小猴王。(没奈何)学了一个缩头法，(按下了)无名装那忘八腔。(但凭你)千声万骂全不理，(倒做了)司马懿(甘受巾帼韬略)长。(这就是)齐人干的无廉耻，(最可笑)冲的什么楚霸王。
    说这齐人初时怎么样得意，到后来何等扫兴结局。这也是孟夫子遍观世道，参透人情，咨嗟太息，把这人做一个求富贵利达的榜样，岂不可笑，岂不可叹！

    Explanation

    197

    Not to mention the commotion made in the two households, there came a pretentious person who was not aware of their own death, still teasing with a hoarse and loud voice, and even calling out to the children's mothers. Just as they were about to stand up straight and act as if they were wealthy, they saw a family in tears, looking very sorrowful. They stopped and raised their ears to listen carefully, and when they heard the mention of "The Man from Dongguo," they became anxious. Suddenly, they lost their hiding place, and they were no longer able to hide their true nature. They were like the bold Sun Wukong in front of the Flame Mountain, but helpless, they had to retreat and pretend to be foolish. No matter how much you scold them, they would not listen, and they ended up submitting to a woman's strategy. This was the shameless behavior of a person from Qi, and it was most laughable to challenge someone like Xiang Yu.

    This is the story of a person from Qi who was initially very successful but ended up with a very disappointing outcome. This is also what Mencius observed in the world, understanding human nature, sighing and lamenting, and using this person as an example of someone who sought wealth and status. How ridiculous and tragic it was!

    198

    (孟夫子)欷歔欲绝叹世情，(都只看)求利求名(是什么营)生。(见几个)轰轰烈烈没下稍，(见几个)巍巍峨峨(弄了精打)精。(见几个)娇妻美妾顾不住，(见几个)蟒玉腰金半截人。(可笑那)作法商鞅自丢白，(可笑那)范雎当年被溺泚。(可笑那)推打的张仪(舌头)强，(可笑那)不下机(的汉子去)相秦。(一个个)没头没脸胡钻干，(全不管)露出马脚怎充鹰。(以这些)不识羞的还打挣，(都该去)齐人家里认弟兄。

    卷六 珍宝部(器用附)
    宝藏库
    章邱“东陵晓月”为八景之一。山容翠峭，石壁兀立。
    有樵人某，至崖间，见一孔。近似目觑，内敞亮洞达，别有天地。遍满皆铺黄金白玉，种种灿烂，莫名其宝，如市中所玩西洋景。以手探之，仅容二指，得古钱十八枚，余不及。

    Explanation

    198

    (Shi Xiangshan) sighed deeply, lamenting the world's affairs, all of which were driven by the pursuit of profit and fame, what kind of life was this? He saw some who made a great show but failed to achieve anything meaningful, others who worked extremely hard but still fell short. He saw some who neglected their wives and concubines, others who were wealthy and powerful but half-dead. It was laughable that Shang Yang, who implemented reforms, ultimately lost his life, and it was laughable that Fan Ju, who was drowned in a toilet by the king of Wei, met such a fate. It was laughable that Zhang Yi, who was beaten but survived, managed to become a chancellor of Qin, and it was laughable that a man who was not allowed to leave the loom still managed to become a chancellor of Qin. One by one, they acted recklessly without regard for propriety, and no one cared about revealing their true nature. Those who didn't know shame still struggled, and they should all go to Qi to seek refuge and support.

    Volume Six: Treasures (with Appended Utensils)
    Treasure Repository
    "Eastern Hill Dawn Moon" is one of the Eight Scenic Views of Zhangqiu. The mountain's appearance is emerald and steep, with cliffs standing tall.
    There was a woodcutter who came to a cliff and saw a hole. It seemed as if he could see through it with his eyes, and inside was a bright and open space, a different world. Everywhere was covered in gold and white jade, all kinds of which were dazzling, and he could not identify the treasures, like the Western-style scenery played with in the market. He reached in with his hand, and it could only fit two fingers, finding eighteen ancient coins, and nothing else.

    199

    遂取土塞其孔，归。持凿往，将洞开而指其藏。至则迷其处，但闻崖内人呼：“宝藏库后垣有漏隙。”一人应曰：“去十八钱，觅工补之。”其声欲震陵谷，樵者惊怖返。
    (有此一处小洞天，更是多宝崖。安得到彼，掉臂游行其中，一开眼界。若到宝山，那有空回？奈何无问津者。)

    熟卵石
    粤香山小揽卖药林氏家，有大叶榕，高十余寻，可半亩园。上有鹳巢数处。林子幼稚好戏，尝猿步能至树巅。
    忽一日，见巢内二卵如柚，携而下。入釜燃薪，以待剖食。其母瞥见，骂之曰：“此异物，不可以啖。且鹳巢吾家，盖亦有年。”乃令其子仍归诸巢。阅数月而树间啾啾，又数日而小鹳立枝头学飞，既而随老鹳翔于云表。林子惑焉，复缘上以觇其异。巢间余粪败草中，贮一黑珠盈掬，林拾之。非金非石，黝光可鉴。怀归藏之药笼，久而尝陈几端。

    Explanation

    199

    The woodcutter then took earth to plug the hole and returned home. He carried a chisel with him, intending to open a hole and reveal the treasure. When he arrived, he was lost in the place, but heard people inside the cliff calling, "There is a leak in the wall of the treasure house." One person responded, "Spend eighteen coins, find a worker to fix it." The sound was so intense that it seemed to shake mountains and valleys, and the woodcutter was terrified and returned.

    (There is a small paradise here, it is even a treasure cliff. How can one reach there, waving arms to stroll freely in it, opening one's eyes. If one reaches the treasure mountain, there would be no empty return? Why is there no one asking the way?)

    In the small village of Xiaoluan, selling medicine in Lin's family in Guangdong, there was a large banyan tree, tall over ten xun, covering about half an acre of garden. There were several stork nests on it. Lin's son, young and fond of play, once climbed like a monkey to the top of the tree. One day, he saw two eggs inside the nest as big as a pomelo, carried them down, and put them in a pot, lighting firewood to wait to cut and eat them. His mother glanced and saw, scolding him, "This is a strange thing, cannot be eaten. And the stork nest is in our home, has been there for many years." She then ordered her son to return them to the nest. After several months, there were chirping sounds in the trees, and after several more days, small storks stood on branches to learn to fly, then followed the old storks to soar in the clouds. Lin's son was puzzled, and again climbed up to observe the strange phenomenon. In the nest's leftover feces and rotten grass, he found a black pearl that could be held in one's palm. It was neither metal nor stone, with a dark luster that could be used as a mirror. He carried it back and stored it in the medicine box, and after a long time, it was placed on the table.

    200

    后有番贾见之，不忍释手，问林卖否。林固知其异，而不知所以异者，乃昂其值曰：“三万金。”贾曰：“价亦太昂，然适当用，诚不敢吝。请同行而取价。”林怀宝以往，登巨舶。贾出白镪，如数兑收。林曰：“物已属君，将焉用之？”贾曰：“此青泥珠也。隋时曾有一枚入中国，后高丽使以六十万售之，将以入海求珠者。此珠出西海外，可以疗痨疾。兹闻交南王有是疾，余将往治之，可以倍蓰。”
    林别贾，携重资还，称富足焉。
    (乾隆辛丑，有吴侬某，在济宁王牧署作记室。游于市，见乡人负半段玉罗汉，色深碧，以四缗售归。命工开琢，宝光灿烂，掩映几案，竟为宝石。成搬指一，牧进之，国抚大喜，以为宝石从未有此色。成指环四，多为牧夫人所得。一花插，携归苏。遇胡贾，出万金买去，云：“此祖母绿，中华何尚有耶？”吴侬亦富。)

    Explanation

    200

    A foreign merchant later saw it and could not bear to let it go, asking Lin if he would sell it. Lin knew it was unusual but did not understand why it was special, so he raised the price, saying, "Three hundred thousand gold coins." The merchant replied, "The price is also too high, but it is suitable for my use, and I truly do not wish to be stingy. Please come with me to fetch the price." Lin took the pearl on board a large ship. The merchant paid in silver coins, exchanging the exact amount. Lin said, "The object now belongs to you. What will you use it for?" The merchant said, "This is an 'qingni' pearl. During the Sui Dynasty, one such pearl entered China, and later a Goryeo envoy sold it for six hundred thousand gold coins, intending to enter the sea to seek more pearls. This pearl comes from the western sea and can be used to treat tuberculosis. I have heard that the King of Jiaonan has this illness, and I will go to treat him, which can multiply its value several times over."

    Lin parted ways with the merchant, carrying a large sum of money back home, and was now considered wealthy.

    (In the year Xinchou of the Qianlong era, there was a man from Wu who worked as a secretary at the Wang magistrate's office in Jinan. While walking in the market, he saw a villager carrying half a jade statue of a Buddhist arhat, which was deep blue in color, and bought it for four thousand copper coins. He ordered a craftsman to carve it, and the stone revealed its brilliant luster, shining brightly on the desk, and was ultimately identified as a precious gem. From it, he made one finger ring, which the magistrate presented to the governor, who was delighted, thinking that no such color of gemstone had ever been seen before. He made four finger rings, most of which were given to the magistrate's wife. One flower holder, he took back to Suzhou. He met a foreign merchant who bought it for ten thousand gold coins, saying, "This is an emerald. How is there still such a thing in China?" The man from Wu also became wealthy.)

    201

    雨钱
    献县民家王氏，诞一儿，娩之夕，闻屋铮铮响，皆启户出视，满院钱落如雨，如自瓦陇滚滚下者。家人争取，得数十缗，咸惊喜。三日，洗儿盆中铢镪溢浮水上，于是里巷皆哄为异。邻妇来观，有抱之者，则青蚨突出襟袖间，如酬其劳，虽多寡不一，未尝虚其襁褓。
    不晬岁而殇，入殓时，阿堵物满布床箦。人家痛伤，盖诚爱其多财，而悼其夭折也。葬城中西门里隙地，年余，其家落。家人往哭于墓，辄于冢旁得数贯归，如是者，其母恒接踵告匮，而所与遂不能继。
    久之，即举家来奠，躄踊泣血，亦一文不舍矣。咸以为此子钱神也。子则曰：“耗鬼也。何也？耗尽则精散，禄绝则命促，何神之为？”闻其母至今尚在，年已七十矣。

    Explanation

    201

    Money Rain

    In the household of the Wang family in Xian County, a son was born. On the night of his birth, they heard a clattering sound in the house and opened the doors to look out. Money was falling like rain, as if rolling down from the roof tiles. The family members rushed to collect it, gathering several tens of strings of cash, and were all filled with surprise and joy. Three days later, the basin used for washing the baby overflowed with coins floating on the water. Thus, the neighborhood buzzed about the strange event.

    Neighboring women came to see the child, and those who held him had coins appear in their sleeves, as if as a reward for their effort. Though the amounts varied, the baby's swaddling clothes were never left empty.

    The child died before reaching one year old. When he was being prepared for burial, the coins covered the bed. The family mourned deeply, loving his wealth and grieving his untimely death. He was buried in a vacant plot within the city's west gate. A year later, the family fell into poverty. Whenever family members went to mourn at the grave, they would find a few coins near the tombstone and take them home. Soon, the mother repeatedly complained of their scarcity, and the coins stopped appearing.

    Eventually, the entire family came to pay their respects, kneeling and crying with blood, and not leaving a single coin behind. All believed the child to be a money god. His father, however, said, "He was a ghost of consumption. Why? When the money is all spent, the essence disperses, and when the fortune is exhausted, the life is shortened. How can he be a god?" It is said that his mother is still alive today, now seventy years old.

    202

    (此乾隆十八年事，为文学王廷家次子宝儿云。)

    琉璃
    博邑颜山产琉璃，其用广，其利薄。可以为玉、为晶、为宝石、为翡翠、为车磲，种种不一。人皆望而知之，实则人皆不知琉璃之所以为琉璃，与夫制造各种器物之法。有聂姓业于此，为余言曰：
    “君博物君子也，请为一申其所由来，并我数十年工业之苦心。夫琉璃者，石以为质，硝以和之，礁以锻之，铜铁丹铅以变之。非石不成，非硝不行，非铜铁丹铅则不精。三合然后生。白如霜，廉削而四方，马牙石也；紫如英，札札星星，紫石也；棱而多角，其形似璞，凌子石也。白者以为干也，紫者以为软也，凌子以为莹也。是故目以为干，则刚紫以为软，而斥之以薄而易张；凌子以为莹，则镜物有光。硝，柔物也，以和内；礁，猛火，人以攻外。

    Explanation

    202

    This is an event from the eighteenth year of Qianlong's reign, attributed to the second son of Wang Ting, a man of letters, named Bao'er.

    Lapis Lazuli
    The city of Bo produces lapis lazuli from Yanshan Mountain. Its uses are wide-ranging, but its profits are thin. It can be made into jade, crystal, gemstones, emeralds, and mother-of-pearl, among many other forms. Everyone knows it at a glance, but in fact, no one knows what makes it what it is and the method of manufacturing various objects from it. There is a person surnamed Nie who is engaged in this craft, and he told me:
    "Sir, you are a learned gentleman. Please allow me to explain its origin and the decades of hard work and dedication I have put into this industry. Lapis lazuli is made from stone as its base, nitrate to blend it, and reef to forge it, with copper, iron, cinnabar, and lead to refine it. Without stone, it cannot be formed; without nitrate, it cannot be processed; without copper, iron, cinnabar, and lead, it cannot be refined. After combining these three elements, it comes into being. White like frost, sharp and square, it is called horse-tooth stone; purple like flowers, with star-like patterns, it is called purple stone; with many angles and a shape similar to unpolished jade, it is called lingzi stone. White ones are used for dryness, purple ones for softness, and lingzi ones for luster. Therefore, when it is seen as dry, it is considered hard and purple as soft, and is dismissed as thin and easy to stretch; when it is seen as lustrous, it reflects objects with light. Nitrate is a softening agent, used internally; reef is a fierce fire, used externally.

    203

    “其始也，石气浊，硝气未澄，必剥而争，故其火烟涨而黑。徐恶尽矣，性未和也，火得红；徐性和矣，精未融也，火得青；徐精融矣，合同而化矣，火得白。故相火齐者，以白为候。其辨色也，白五之，紫一之，凌子倍紫，得水晶。进其紫，退其白，去其凌子，得正白。白三之，紫一之，凌子如紫，加少铜及铁屑焉，得梅萼红。白三之，紫一之，去其凌进其铜，去其铁，得蓝。法如白焉，钩以铜碛，得秋黄。法如水晶，钩以画碗石，得映青。法如白，加铅焉，多多益善，得牙白。法如牙白，加铁焉，得正黑。法如水晶，加铜焉，得绿。法如绿，退其铜，加少碛焉，得鹅黄。凡皆以焰硝之数为之程。

    Explanation

    203

    At the beginning, the sulfur is impure, and the saltpeter is not yet clarified; they must peel and compete, so the fire produces dense, black smoke. Gradually, all the impurities are exhausted, and the nature of the substances is not yet harmonized; the fire turns red. Gradually, the nature becomes harmonized, but the essence is not yet melted; the fire turns blue. Gradually, the essence is melted, and the substances combine and transform; the fire turns white. Therefore, those who control the fire take the white color as a sign. In distinguishing colors, five parts white and one part purple, with the amount of lingzi (a type of mineral) doubled that of purple, one gets crystal. Increase the purple, reduce the white, and remove the lingzi, and one gets pure white. Three parts white and one part purple, with the amount of lingzi equal to that of purple, and add a small amount of copper and iron filings, one gets plum blossom red. Three parts white and one part purple, remove the lingzi, increase the copper, and remove the iron, and one gets blue. The method is the same as for white, using copper and sand to refine, one gets autumn yellow. The method is the same as for crystal, using painting bowl stone to refine, one gets reflected green. The method is the same as for white, add lead, the more the better, and one gets tooth white. The method is the same as for tooth white, add iron, and one gets pure black. The method is the same as for crystal, add copper, and one gets green. The method is the same as for green, reduce the copper, add a small amount of sand, and one gets goose yellow. All are measured by the amount of saltpeter.

    204

    “其贵青帘，取彼水晶，和以回青。如箸斯条，若水斯冰。纬为幌簿，传于朱棂。瑞烟徐起，旭日始升。影动几筵，光浮御屏。凄神象之，以合窈冥。用之郊坛焉，用之清庙焉，隶于司空，以称国工。
    “次为佩璜，连珠缀缨，绛纱作盛，弁冕盈庭，乃球锵鸣。古者百僚朝祭之法服也。
    “其次又为华灯、屏风、礶合、果山，皆穿珠之属，口则无功，错采雕龙。
    “又其次为棋子、风铃、念珠、壶顶、簪珥、料方，皆实之屋。围棋滴之，风铃范之，料方亦如之，条珠缠之、细珠泻之、大珠缠之戛之，簪弭惟错。车渠者，杂二色药而糅之；玛瑙者，珐琅点之；缠丝者，以药夹丝待其融也，引而旋之。

    Explanation

    204

    Its precious green curtain, taking that crystal, mixed with indigo. Like a chopstick in its slenderness, like water in its clarity. The green curtain becomes a thin curtain, transmitted through the red lattice. Auspicious smoke slowly rises, the morning sun begins to rise. Shadows move on the tables and mats, light floats on the imperial screen. It makes the spirit feel desolate, to match the profound darkness. Used in the suburban altar, used in the clear temple, under the jurisdiction of the Minister of Works, to be called a national artisan.

    Next are the jade pendants, linked pearls and ribbons, red silk as a container, hats and crowns fill the hall, then the jade pendants sound. Anciently, it was the ceremonial dress for officials during court and sacrifice.

    Next are the ornate lamps, screens, vases, and fruit mountains, all made of strung pearls, but without function, decorated with colorful carvings of dragons.

    Next are the chess pieces, wind chimes, prayer beads, pot tops, hair ornaments, and small boxes, all filled with beads. Chess pieces are strung with beads, wind chimes are shaped with beads, small boxes are also like this, thin beads are wrapped around, fine beads are poured out, large beads are wrapped and struck, hair ornaments are only varied. For the tortoise shell, mix two colors of medicine and blend them; for the agate, enamel is dotted on it; for the twisted silk, use medicine to hold the silk until it melts, then draw and twist it.

    205

    “再则为泡灯、鱼瓶、葫芦、砚滴、佛眼，轩辕镜、火珠响器、鼓珰之属，皆空。凡制之法，必先为琉璃，为管焉，必有铁杖刀剪焉，非是弗工。石在冶，焕然流离，犹金在镕而出之，杖之力也。受之者管也，授之以隙，纳气而中空，使口得为功，管之力也。乍出于火，焕然流离，就管矣，未就口也。急则流，缓则凝，旋而转之，授以风轮，使不流不凝，手之力也。施气焉，壮则裂，弱则偏，调其气而消息之，气行而喉舌皆不知，则大不裂，小不偏，口之力也。吹圆球者，抗之；吹胆瓶者，坠之。一俯一仰，满气为圆，微气为长，身如朽株，首如鼗鼓，项之力也。引之使长，截之使短，拗之使屈，突之使高，抑之使凹，刀剪之力也。

    205

    Furthermore, for objects such as bubble lamps, fish bottles, gourds, ink droppers, Buddha eyes, the Yellow Emperor's mirror, fireball resonators, and drum pendants, all are hollow. In the general method of making them, one must first use glass, and make a tube. One must have iron rods, knives, and scissors, for without these, one cannot be skilled. When the glass is in the furnace, it shines brilliantly, like gold being refined and emerging from the molten state, which is the power of the iron rod. The receiver is the tube, and it is given an opening to admit air and make it hollow, allowing the mouth to be effective, which is the power of the tube. As it first emerges from the fire, it shines brilliantly, and it is ready for the tube but not yet for the mouth. If it is rushed, it flows; if it is slow, it coagulates. To rotate it and apply a wind wheel, to prevent it from flowing or coagulating, is the power of the hand. To apply air, if it is strong, it will crack; if it is weak, it will be uneven. To adjust the air and balance it, when the air flows and the throat and tongue are unaware, then it will not crack if large, nor be uneven if small, which is the power of the mouth. When blowing a round ball, one must push it; when blowing a pear-shaped bottle, one must pull it down. One up and one down, full air makes it round, slight air makes it long, the body is like a rotting tree, the head is like a hand drum, which is the power of the neck. To stretch it to make it long, to cut it to make it short, to bend it to make it curved, to push it to make it high, to press it to make it concave, is the power of the knife and scissors.

    206

    凡为葫芦，先得提，后得腹，接处为腰。为含子葫芦，先得子，次得提，纳子焉，后得腹。凡为鱼瓶，先得口，次得腔，次得山，后得果枝。凡为花簪，先得茎，后得顶，断而殊之，身手而燎之，后得蜂末。凡为响器，先得下口，后得上口。凡为砚滴，先为顶口，次得腹、次得提，后得吐水。凡为磴碗，先得圆球，吸其下，按其上，断其脐而坐之，上反为底，下反为面。凡为鼓珰，先得葫芦，旋绕其底而四流之，以均其薄。为而不平，使微枉焉，以随气之动，乃得鸣。鼓珰者，响葫芦也。言微气鼓之，而珰鸣也。辟之为鼓也，声者其面也，响之应者，其腔也。实则其空也，故大空则大鸣，小空则小鸣。此老氏之说也。当其无有有之用也。凡为空者，先养气，气圆而体圆。此学书之说也，心正则笔正。”
    余闻其说，遂笔记之。
    (此篇文理甚古奥，可传也。[太冲林鹗］

    Explanation

    206

    In the making of gourds, one first obtains the handle, then the body, with the connection forming the waist. For gourds that contain seeds, one first obtains the seeds, then the handle, places the seeds inside, and finally obtains the body. In the making of fish-shaped bottles, one first obtains the mouth, then the cavity, followed by the mountain-like shape, and finally the fruit branches. In the making of flower hairpins, one first obtains the stem, then the top, cuts and separates it, shapes it with hands, and burns it, finally obtaining the bee-like end. In the making of sound-producing instruments, one first obtains the lower opening, then the upper opening. In the making of ink droppers, one first obtains the top opening, then the body, followed by the handle, and finally the spout. In the making of stepped bowls, one first obtains a round ball, sucks the bottom, presses the top, cuts the umbilicus and places it, with the top becoming the bottom and the bottom becoming the face. In the making of drum-like objects, one first obtains a gourd, winds around the bottom and flows out in four directions to make it evenly thin. When making it uneven, one makes it slightly curved to follow the movement of the air, and it can then produce sound. Drum-like objects are sound-producing gourds. They say that when the air is slightly agitated, the drum-like object produces sound. Comparing it to a drum, the sound comes from the surface, and the response to the sound comes from the cavity. It is the emptiness that gives it its use, so a larger emptiness produces a louder sound, and a smaller emptiness produces a softer sound. This is the saying of Laozi. When it is empty, it has its use. For those that are empty, one first cultivates the air, and when the air is round, the body is round. This is the saying about learning calligraphy, where when the heart is upright, the brush is upright." I heard this and then wrote it down. (This piece of writing is very ancient and profound, and it is worth transmitting. [Taichong Lin'e])

    207

    读之如翻《考工冬官记》，古色斑斓，非时代物。[七如］)

    水晶眼镜考
    水晶为水精，《山海经》：“堂庭之山多水玉”；《拾遗记》：“孙亮作琉璃屏风，莹澈内外。”此类是也。今闽广出产水晶，好丑颜色，各有不同。其白而无绵者为上。为器玩最多。
    明三保太监出西洋，携烧玻璃人来中国，制如水晶。用以硝礁，无所不烧，如灯、瓶、珠、簪之属。镜之制，本范铜为之，粉以元锡，磨以白茹，则须眉毫发可得而察。移之玻璃，愈倍其光，因之以有玻璃之镜。更即镜收之于目，为眼镜焉。昏者亦可借镜而视，故玻璃遂缀于眉睫间。充其类，为老花，为少花，为短视，因人而施，量力而厚薄之以为的。是眼之有镜，实创于明。《庶物异名疏》：“叆叇”，今俗名眼镜是也。若壮岁用之，则反昏暗伤目。时人复以水晶之无绵者作眼镜，更较玻璃而著明。是眼镜之初作于假，而今乃变为真。玻璃之犹嫌于火，而水晶则实取于水也。

    Explanation

    207

    Reading it is like flipping through the Kao Gong section of the Rites of Zhou, ancient and colorful, not a product of its time. [Seven As]

    The Examination of Crystal Eyeglasses
    Crystal is the essence of water; the Shanhaijing states: "Mount Tangting is abundant in water jade"; the Shiji records: "Sun Liang created a crystal screen, transparent inside and out." Such are the examples. Today, Fujian and Guangdong produce crystal, with varying qualities and colors. The pure white ones without flaws are the best, used most often as decorative objects.
    During the Ming Dynasty, Eunuch Zheng He went to the Western Seas, bringing glass-blowing artisans to China, who made glass like crystal. Using nitrate and salt, they could make all kinds of glass objects, such as lamps, bottles, beads, and hairpins. The making of mirrors originally involved casting them in copper molds, powdered with tin and polished with white cloth, so that even the finest details of facial hair could be seen. Transferring the process to glass makes it even more brilliant, leading to the creation of glass mirrors. And thus glasses were born, where the mirror is held in front of the eyes. Those with poor vision can also use them to see, hence glass is attached to the eyelashes. There are different types of glasses: for presbyopia, myopia, and hyperopia, made according to individual needs, with varying thicknesses. The invention of glasses for the eyes actually began in the Ming Dynasty. The Shuowen Jiezi states: "Aicai," the modern common name for glasses. If used in middle age, they can actually harm the eyes by making things appear dimmer. People also make glasses from pure white crystal without flaws, which are even clearer than glass. Thus, glasses were initially made of glass, but now they have become more genuine. Glass is still somewhat associated with fire, while crystal is truly derived from water.

    208

    又有养目镜，虽少年戴之，无损于目。明人有诗云：“西洋眼镜规璧圆，玻璃为质象并缘。”可考也。国朝查慎行诗：“隙光分日月，宿障扫云烟。”阮芸台应制诗：“眸目何须尔，重瞳不用他。”考第一。余有绝句云：
    眼前物障视难明，物障安能明更生。有物照同如无有，眼光取入水中晶。

    Explanation

    208

    There are eyeglasses for the eyes, even if worn by young people, they do no harm to the eyes. A Ming dynasty poet once said: "Western eyeglasses are circular like jade disks, made of glass with a shape that fits the face." This can be verified. A Qing dynasty poet, Zha Shenxing, wrote: "The light divides the sun and moon, clearing away the clouds and smoke." Ruan Yuan, in his poem for the court, wrote: "Why do you need such eyes? No need for double pupils." This is the first to be examined. I have a quatrain that says:
    Objects in front are hard to see clearly; how can obstacles make things clearer? There is something that shines as if it were nothing; the eye takes in the clarity of water crystals.

    209

    铁人
    高密阴城，居民耕地，获一铁人。高尺许，左手擎钵，大于碗。注水，移时自沸，数易皆然。民实爱过甚，不以示人。耕余辄摩挲把玩，搬弄不已。忽误触手钵，脱底，复盛水，其下镌“诸葛亮造”四隶字。铁人掌心铸一“火”字，再注之水，则冷然也。

    卷七 僧道(女道士附)
    再来人
    太白为谪仙人，东坡是戒禅师后身，定非臆说。昭文金芗谷，老而无子，游于浙之西湖灵隐寺，默祝三宝，祈求子嗣。长老与之散步廊间，过香积寮，见一蹩僧顾金而笑。长老点首，金不知其故。及归，金妾有娠，是夜梦一僧直入寝所，醒告金，异之。生子。
    逾年，金复来杭。至寺，长老贺曰：“公子无恙。”金问何以预知，长老乃引金入厨下，见一龛云：“内则当时相视而笑之执爨僧也。伊圆寂时嘱勿化其身，俟伊自来。故留以待。”长老书龛际一联云：“此去有缘凭夙慧，归来好认旧菩提。”金出资为之甃砌。归告其家，皆呼“小和尚”。

    Explanation

    209

    Iron Figure
    In Yincheng, Gaomi, a farmer plowed the land and found an iron figure. It was about a foot tall, holding a bowl in its left hand, larger than a bowl. When water was poured into it, it would boil after a while, and even if the water was changed multiple times, it still boiled. The farmer loved it too much and did not show it to others. After finishing his work, he would fondle and play with it, handling it without end. Accidentally, he knocked the bowl with his hand, and the bottom fell off. He refilled it with water, and on the bottom were carved four clerical script characters "Zhuge Liang made." On the palm of the iron figure was cast a "fire" character. When water was poured into it again, it became cold.

    Chapter 7: Monks and Daoists (with Female Daoists)
    Reincarnation
    Li Bai was a谪仙人 (transferred celestial being), and Su Dongpo was the reincarnation of a戒禅师 (abstaining Zen master), which is not a mere guess. Jin Xianggu, an old man without a son, traveled to Lingyin Temple on West Lake in Zhejiang and silently prayed to the Three Jewels, asking for offspring. The abbot walked with him along the corridor and passed the kitchen, where he saw a hunchbacked monk looking at Jin and smiling. The abbot nodded, but Jin did not know why. When he returned home, Jin's concubine became pregnant. That night, Jin dreamed of a monk entering his bedroom directly. He woke up and told Jin, who found it strange. They had a son.
    A year later, Jin came back to Hangzhou. When he arrived at the temple, the abbot congratulated him, saying, "Your son is fine." Jin asked how he knew in advance. The abbot then led Jin into the kitchen and showed him a niche, saying, "Inside is the monk who looked at you and smiled when we passed the kitchen. He instructed not to cremate his body and to wait for him to return. So we kept it here." The abbot wrote a couplet on the side of the niche: "With karmic connections, rely on innate wisdom; upon return, recognize the old bodhi." Jin paid for the construction of a brick and stone foundation. Upon returning home, everyone called the child "Little Monk."

    210

    及长，名葆。茹素，强以荤酒，辄呕吐。读书聰慧。父死，事母孝。十五入泮，十六领乡荐，联捷南宫。博闻强识，精通释典，授中翰。
    京师慈仁寺有浮屠大善知识，能说无上《妙法》诸经。金往诣之，僧傲慢不为礼，金竖一指，罣叱之曰：“天地间亦知有我否？”僧惊之，延至方丈，与之言一真二谛、三摩四大、五蕴六欲、七心八垢、九根十行，无不了了。僧曰：“君原非阶下汉，故能作此过来语。”
    后出为荆州守，恬静无为，郡人颂之。金尝曰：“圣贤功用，主敬主静；道释两家，何以外此。即于中庸极致间有偏倚，亦非浅尝者所可訾病，奈何群聚讼为？是故今之释子，古之佛氏之罪人也；今之道士，古之老子之罪人也；今之秀才，古之圣人之罪人也。”

    Explanation

    210

    When he grew up, his name was Bao. He abstained from meat and alcohol, and whenever forced to consume them, he would vomit. He was intelligent and wise. After his father's death, he was filial to his mother. At fifteen, he entered school, and at sixteen, he was recommended for the provincial examination, subsequently passing the imperial examinations and entering the Hanlin Academy. He had a broad knowledge and strong memory, and was proficient in Buddhist scriptures, eventually being appointed as a Hanlin scholar.

    In the capital city, the Ciren Temple housed a great wise teacher, a Buddhist monk who could expound the supreme and wonderful sutras. Bao went to visit him, but the monk was arrogant and did not show him proper respect. Bao raised one finger and scolded him, saying, "Do you know that there is me in the world?" The monk was startled and invited him to his study, where they discussed the two truths, the four elements, the five aggregates and six desires, the seven minds and eight impurities, the nine faculties and ten practices. Bao understood everything without difficulty. The monk remarked, "You were never a commoner, which is why you can speak such profound words."

    Later, Bao served as the governor of Jingzhou, where he governed with tranquility and non-action, and the people of the prefecture praised him. Bao once said, "The functions of sages and virtuous people lie in maintaining reverence and tranquility. The Daoist and Buddhist schools cannot be separated from this. Even if there is a slight bias in the highest point of the Doctrine of the Mean, it cannot be criticized by those who have only superficially tasted it. Why then do people gather to dispute over it? Therefore, today's Buddhist monks are the sinners of ancient Buddhism; today's Daoist priests are the sinners of ancient Daoism; today's scholars are the sinners of ancient sages."

    211

    郡城外有一古寺，内有泥鬼，忽出野中立。乡人咸惊异，祀以香花，日盛其事。金舆往视，曰：“只这是泥是土，何圣何灵？速毁而瘗之。”夜一青衣来拜曰：“我乃山鬼，久受孽报。蒙君打脱一切障碍，如听无生大乘。”谢而去。
    三年，母死归葬，庐于墓旁，服阕不仕。有僧自杭来，门隶呵之。僧遗扇一柄，门者呈金，金曰：“长老命我归矣。”夫人李氏，旧家阀阅之女，闻金欲之杭为僧，乃从容而进词曰：“妾闻达者明理而通变，愚人守暗而抱拙。人生世上，不过忠孝节义诸大端。今欲去先人之墓庐，可谓孝乎？当此承平，不思鼓吹休明以和其盛，而乃遁逃枯槁，可谓忠乎？况乎里闾推重，后生矜式，正赖父兄之董，率为乡先生之规，以绵世泽，以熏善良。愿夫子不出家而成教于国也。”金怃然为间，深以为是。乃告夫人作杭州之游，以了前因，仍归故里，遂不为世外想。

    Explanation

    211

    Outside the city wall stood an ancient temple, within which was a clay demon. Suddenly, it appeared in the wild, standing upright. The villagers were all amazed and began to worship it with incense and flowers, and day by day, the rituals became more elaborate. Gold Cart went to see it and said, "It is just mud and earth, what saint, what spirit? Quickly destroy and bury it." That night, a figure dressed in green came to pay respects and said, "I am the mountain ghost, long suffering under the curse of sin. Thank you for freeing me from all obstacles, as if I have heard the great vehicle of the Buddha." He thanked and left.

    Three years later, Gold Cart's mother died, and he returned to bury her. He built a hut by her grave and, after the mourning period, did not take up office. A monk came from Hangzhou, and the gatekeeper rebuked him. The monk left behind a fan, and the gatekeeper presented it to Gold Cart, who said, "The elder has ordered me to return." Lady Li, a daughter of a prominent family, heard that Gold Cart wanted to become a monk in Hangzhou and calmly presented her words: "I have heard that those who are enlightened understand reason and adapt to change, while foolish people cling to darkness and hold onto clumsiness. In life, there are only the great principles of loyalty, filial piety, integrity, and righteousness. Now you want to leave the tomb of your ancestors, can this be called filial? In these peaceful times, you do not think of promoting peace and prosperity to harmonize the flourishing times, instead, you flee to a life of poverty and hardship, can this be called loyal? Moreover, the community respects you, young people look up to you, you rely on the guidance of your father and elder brothers, and follow the rules of the village elders to continue the family's legacy and inspire goodness. I hope that you will not leave home but teach the country." Gold Cart was deeply moved and paused, deeply agreeing with her. He then told Lady Li about his trip to Hangzhou to resolve the previous cause, and return to his hometown, no longer thinking of the outside world.

    212

    抵杭询长老，寺僧云：“三月前不知何处卓锡去矣。”金乃启龛视之，面目如生。火化之期，妙香四闻，祥光烛天。封之后山，题其塔曰“再来人”。
    金归家后，修身立行，二子皆成进士。遂入程朱之室，著《潜修录》十卷，语皆精粹可宝也。

    了拳
    粤之潮属有山，名曰阴那。其开山和尚俗姓潘，号惭愧，闽之沙县人。初生左拳曲，因名拳。弥月，一游僧至，父抱儿视之，僧书拳上一“了”字，指立伸，更名“了拳”。

    Explanation

    212

    Upon arriving in Hangzhou, Jin consulted with the elder monks. The temple monk said, "Three months ago, I don't know where he went with his staff." Jin then opened the niche to inspect it, and the face looked as if alive. During the cremation, a fragrant aroma spread far, and a bright light illuminated the sky. He was sealed in the back mountain, and the stupa was inscribed as "One Who Comes Again."

    After returning home, Jin cultivated himself and established his conduct, and both of his sons became jinshi (imperial scholars). He then adopted the teachings of Cheng and Zhu, wrote Qianxiu Lu in ten volumes, and all the words are exquisite and precious.

    In the region of Chaozhou, there is a mountain named Yinnuo. The founding monk of the mountain, surnamed Pan, was known by the name "Cankui" and was from Shaxian, Fujian. He was born with his left hand curled into a fist, so he was named "Quan." After a month, a wandering monk arrived, and his father held the child for the monk to see. The monk wrote the character "le" on the fist, and the child's finger straightened. He was then renamed "Lequan."

    213

    幼颖悟，不茹荤。年十二，丧父母。依叔，叔母不能容。十七去潮之黄砂社车上村，认寡妇游氏为母，今大埔县地。日与牧童登赤嶡岭，旷观天云，若有所得。令放牛山麓，拳以杖画地，牛不他逸。以烹鱼啖之，受而投诸水，鱼复活。今黑质白章，尾上焦，其遗种也。
    岭左溪潭有大石，如蹲虎，一老僧趺坐其上，尝以指甲写“大生石头”四字，大可巴掌。历风雨剥落，点画宛然。爱山水之胜，欲结茅于此，不果。迨游母既没，拳营窀穸，后遂去。里人为之筑灵觉寺，继之砌上。莆田有二寺：一名清泉，一名龙泉，相传皆拳卓锡取泉处。至神泉市，欲济无舟，折苇以渡。登黄龙献爪山，循顶西行，抵平沙社之楠树坑，依袁姓三年。后人因其地为高砌寺。爰乃涉芒州岗之巅，西望阴那五峰蝉联，耸峙云表，神赏者久之，欣然欲往。过浒梓村，求水弗得，乃卓锡成井，有石龟，至今存焉。虽亢旱，泉不竭。后亦建灵山亭，拳像塑其中。

    Explanation

    213

    From a young age, he was quick-witted and insightful, abstaining from eating meat. At twelve years old, he lost his parents. He relied on his uncle, but the uncle's wife could not tolerate him. At seventeen, he left for Huangsha She Checun in Chaozhou, acknowledging a widow named You as his mother, who lived in what is now Dabu County. Each day, he climbed Chixi Ridge with the herders, gazing at the sky and clouds, as if gaining some insight. He was ordered to graze cattle at the foot of the mountain, drawing in the ground with his fist and a stick, ensuring the cattle did not wander off. He cooked fish and fed it to them, taking it and throwing it back into the water, and the fish revived. Today, there is a type of fish with a black body, white patterns, and a burned tail, which is said to be the remaining offspring.

    On the left side of the mountain, there was a large stone resembling a crouching tiger, upon which an old monk sat in meditation. The monk had once written the four characters "Dashi Shitou" (Great Birth Stone) with his fingernail, each character about the size of a palm. Despite the wear from wind and rain, the strokes remained clear. He loved the beauty of the mountains and waters, wanting to build a thatched hut there, but he did not succeed. After the mother You passed away, he prepared the grave with his own hands and later left. The villagers built the Lingjue Temple for him and later added a platform. In Putian, there are two temples: one named Qingquan (Clear Spring) and the other Longquan (Dragon Spring). It is said that both were places where he obtained water by striking the ground. Arriving at Shenquan Market, he wanted to cross but had no boat, so he broke reeds to make a raft to cross. Climbing Mount Huanglong Xianzhua, he followed the top westward, reaching the Nan Shu Pit in Pingsha Village, where he lived with the surname Yuan for three years. Later, people built the Gaoqi Temple at that place. He then climbed to the top of Mangzhou Hill, looking westward at the five peaks of Yinna, linked like cicadas, towering high in the clouds. He admired it for a long time and gladly wanted to go there. Passing through Huzi Village, he tried to find water but failed, so he struck the ground with his staff to create a well. There was a stone turtle, which still exists today. Even during severe drought, the spring does not dry up. Later, they built the Lingshan Pavilion, and a statue of him was placed inside.

    214

    至阴那，斲石刊木，建道场为修真地，日说法，众多不省。曾赋诗曰：
    行脚腰包廿载游，一天花坠雨成秋。
    指禅未觉羞拳了，顽石因何不点头？
    住阴那又三十年。一日，语其徒曰：“从前佛祖皆宏演法乘，自度以度人。我未能也，心甚愧之。圆寂后，藏我骸于塔，当颜其额曰‘惭愧’。”因偈曰：“四十九年，无系无牵。如今撒手归空去，万里云开月在天。”语毕，端坐而逝。

    Explanation

    214

    At Yinna, he chopped stones and carved wood, building a temple as a place for cultivating the true self. He preached daily, but many did not understand. Once he composed a poem saying:

    Wandering for twenty years with a simple pack,
    A day's flowers fall, and rain turns to autumn.
    Pointing to Zen, I did not fully convey the truth,
    Why does a stubborn stone not nod?

    He stayed at Yinna for another thirty years. One day, he said to his disciples, "In the past, all Buddhas widely preached the Dharma, saving themselves to save others. I could not do this, and I feel deeply ashamed. After my passing, bury my remains in a stupa, and mark the top of the stupa with the words 'candkui' (shame and gratitude)." He then composed a verse saying:

    Forty-nine years, without attachment or entanglement.
    Now, letting go, I return to the void,
    A thousand miles of clouds part, and the moon shines in the sky.

    After speaking these words, he sat upright and passed away.

    215

    没后屡显灵异。明御史梅鼎臣舟过蓬辣滩，水汹涌，舟几覆，见老僧于岸，隐约指点，舟得无恙。又三饶寇乱，时过阴那，将肆虏掠，忽云雾四塞，咫尺不辨人，贼迷失道路，各村赖以全。每至三月，山中必风雨，相传洗殿。盖了拳生于元和十二年三月也。
    余十五岁游阴那，见冥然僧时挂搭于此，相与谈宴弥日，亦闽人。山多大笋，径围尺许，削其尖，刓空，贮以盐豉腐干煨熟，连笋断之，陈于俎，甘脆异常。寺中古柏三株，合抱两人，苍翠参天，为拳所手植云。

    懊上人
    吴端玉，直隶举人，家赀巨万。端玉一妻三妾，二子一女。有伯兄端履，亦二子，其一子士煌，与端玉同榜举人。端玉名下士，性豪爽不羁，与端履最友爱。工诗文辞。年三十，公车屡踬。

    Explanation

    215

    After his death, Leiqüan often manifested miraculous phenomena. During the Ming dynasty, the censor Mei Dingchen was traveling by boat over Pengla Tuan when the water became surging and the boat nearly capsized. He saw an old monk on the shore, who vaguely pointed, and the boat remained unharmed. In the Three Ráo region, during a period of bandit troubles, when passing through Yīn Nà, the bandits were about to commit plunder. Suddenly, clouds and mist filled all around, making it impossible to see even at arm's length. The bandits lost their way, and each village was saved. Every March, there must be rain and wind in the mountains, and it is said that the temple is washed. It is said that Leiqüan was born in the third month of the twelfth year of Yuanhe.

    When I was fifteen, I traveled to Yīn Nà and saw a monk named Míng Rán who was staying there. We talked and feasted for the whole day, and he was also from Fujian. The mountains have many large bamboo shoots, with a circumference of about a foot. Peeling off the tips, hollowing them out, and filling them with salted soybeans and dried fermented beans, then roasting them, cutting the bamboo shoots in half and placing them on the platter, they were extremely sweet and crisp. There are three ancient cypress trees in the temple, each with a circumference that could embrace two people, tall and green, said to have been planted by Leiqüan himself.

    The Disciple Awakened
    Wu Duanyu, a juren from Zhili, had a fortune in the millions. Wu Duanyu had one wife and three concubines, two sons and one daughter. He had an elder brother named Duanyu, who also had two sons, one of whom, Shihuáng, passed the juren examination in the same year as Wu Duanyu. Wu Duanyu, known for his scholarly achievements, was a person of great generosity and unconstrained spirit, and he was closest to his elder brother. He was skilled in poetry and prose. At the age of thirty, he repeatedly failed the imperial examinations.

    216

    其兴致颇高，与人谈集，竟日夜不倦。短于髯，尝取优伶须一具挂面上，欣欣自得，欲效苏长公雄视一切也。时人慕之，以为吴子之雅量宏才也。复得安兹顺境，天之爱才，可谓不负矣。
    而天下事，有顺必有逆。会岁疾疫行，端玉之妻妾子女，不旬日而死亡无孑遗。端玉素笃闺房之爱，更萦儿女之怀，一旦遭此惨痛，愤不欲生。尝欲引颈自决，不则投缳以尽，家人恐怖，百计防守。其兄哭泣相劝，而端玉终以死誓。兄复诸凡慰藉，开布大义，继而端玉曰：“弟岂不知死不如生？即弟之死，亦于已死之人无益于事，转与生者徒增悲悼。惟是柔肠寸断，实所难忍。”兄曰：“骨肉关情，弟死我不独生。”玉曰：“兄冢子也，以弟之故，俱死曷济？”于是兄弟痛楚，屡日悲泣。玉曰：“兄必不容弟死，当有一生法。”兄曰：“生法安在？”玉曰：“无已，请度为僧。”兄不得已许之，并以其子士煌嗣焉。

    Explanation

    216

    His enthusiasm was very high, and he would talk and discuss with others day and night without tiring. He was short of beard and once took a set of actors' beards and hung them on his face, feeling happy and content, wanting to emulate Su Chánggōng (Su Shi) in dominating everything. People of the time admired him, thinking he was a person of broad-mindedness and great talent. He again found peace and smooth circumstances, and it can be said that heaven's love for talent was not wasted.

    However, in the world, there is always a mix of good and bad fortune. One year, an epidemic broke out, and within a few days, Duan Yu's wives, concubines, children, and even his own children all died, leaving no survivors. Duan Yu always had a deep affection for his family, and even more so, he was deeply concerned about his children. Once faced with such a tragic loss, he was so angry that he did not want to live. He once wanted to cut his own throat, or hang himself to end it. His family members were terrified and tried every means to protect him. His brother cried and tried to persuade him, but Duan Yu ultimately swore to die. The brother then tried various ways to comfort him, explaining the great principles, and subsequently Duan Yu said, "Brother, do you not know that death is worse than life? Even if I die, it will not benefit those who have already died, but only add sorrow to the living. It is only that my heart is torn apart, which is truly unbearable." The brother said, "We are related by blood and emotion, if you die, I will not live alone." Duan Yu said, "Brother, you are the eldest son, if I die because of me, what good would it do to both of us?" Thus, the brothers were in pain and cried for many days. Duan Yu said, "Brother, you will not allow me to die, there must be a way to live." The brother said, "Where is the way to live?" Duan Yu said, "If there is no other way, I will become a monk." The brother, unable to refuse, agreed and also made his son Shihuang his heir.

    217

    玉乃择日哭于祖祠，剃发剃披。吴故大族，一时亲党戚友，数百余里皆来唁慰。而玉豪气未除，虽经磨蝎，讵能一袭毘卢，顿改初度。因欲傲游海内名胜，多金盛装，宛如贵胄。翩翩舆马，连镳蔽道而行，号为“懊上人”。凡遇佳山妙境，盘桓弥月不去。

    Explanation

    217

    Yü chose a day to mourn at the ancestral temple, shaving his head and cutting off his hair. As a member of an ancient noble family, relatives and friends came from hundreds of miles away to offer condolences. Despite having gone through difficult experiences, Yü still retained his boldness and had not suddenly transformed into a more spiritual state. He then decided to travel proudly through famous scenic spots within the realm, dressed in fine clothes and appearing like a noble. His carriages and horses moved gracefully in a line, blocking the road, and he was called "the person who is always dissatisfied." Whenever he encountered beautiful mountains and wonderful scenery, he would linger for a month without leaving.

    218

    至五台遇喇吗某，颇相契。玉因其地苦寒，思南下。喇吗多贵交，为之致书浙抚，荐为西湖灵隐方丈。端玉儒者也，今弃儒而逃禅，不过为一时愤急之行。因是而遂欲置之空虚寂灭之乡，以戕其性、死其心，则玉又不愿也。故端玉必饮酒食荤，且衣锦而好色，其穷奢甚于王公。南方之人谄鬼佞佛，以玉不能守规戒，共诽之，乃去杭州。去之曰，贻书留别当道诸公云：“余数年甘心栏入水牯牛队，本不欲被绣为牺，亦不愿服耒于田。便做和尚，原不必担着枷，逢人苦乞。堪笑瞿昙，今日舍卫大城，明日室罗筏城，平白教坏法门也。终不见有天人送供，何殊癞狗作生天想？那个持一口钵，如捧十丈珊瑚，放手不得？比归来，臀也强，踝也酸，何苦何苦！兹者闻焦山可以结茅，我欲出京口去。前途舟大舟小，不能前定，再来西湖，不知何年月日。无一点由人打算，尽若斯耶。懊书。”

    Explanation

    218

    Upon reaching Wutai Mountain, Yue met a certain Lama with whom he got along well. Feeling the place was cold and harsh, Yue thought of moving south. The Lama had many wealthy friends and wrote letters on his behalf to the governor of Zhejiang, recommending him as the abbot of Lingyin Temple in West Lake. Yue, a scholar, had abandoned his scholarly pursuits to become a monk, but it was just a momentary act of frustration. As a result, he wanted to place himself in a place of emptiness and extinction, to harm his nature and kill his heart, but Yue did not want that. Therefore, Yue must drink alcohol and eat meat, and dress in fine clothes and enjoy beauty, his extravagance being even greater than that of princes. People in the south flatter ghosts and deceive the Buddha because Yue could not follow the rules and precepts, and they all criticized him, so he then left Hangzhou. As he was leaving, he said, he left a letter to bid farewell to the officials in charge, saying: "For several years, I willingly entered the ranks of water buffalo, never wanting to be dressed in silk to be a sacrifice, nor did I want to plow the fields. So I became a monk, I didn't have to wear chains and beg from people. It's laughable that the Buddha, today in Savatthi, tomorrow in Shravasti, teaches the Dharma carelessly. In the end, there are no celestial beings offering food, how different is it from a leprous dog thinking about being reborn in heaven? Whoever holds a single bowl, like holding a ten-foot coral, can't let go. When I returned, my buttocks were sore, and my ankles were stiff, how painful, how painful! Now I hear that Jiaoshan can be used to build a hermitage, I want to leave through Jingkou. The size of the boats ahead cannot be determined in advance, when I come back to West Lake, I don't know when that will be. There is no way for people to plan, is it all like this? A regretful letter."

    219

    吴过苏阊买四僮，所谓清客者，悉令剃发作小沙弥。至焦山，大建浮屠，土木之工，三万有奇，皆其兄端履自家寄来也。又与其兄一札云：“大兄安好。弟今为释子，与诸方不同，原不类守昆尼博通经论者，异时修定修慧，且都搁起。近来卓锡焦山，揽海门洲岛之胜，令人举头天外。但住处狭隘，不可下榻。弟欲广布黄金，难得现成，算檀施无主，便当自舍。此亦一大好事，不求报于人，天作不朽功德，希冀将来缘法。大兄不可不一相扶持，携资来，为我度画得千稳百当，使大众一齐安乐。不则峰前独立，松下徘徊，或执疏沿门，虽走遍赵州，八十犹未能驻足也。兄以为何如？”端履得书，即来焦为之建置云。

    Explanation

    219

    During his travels through Suzhou, a man named Wu purchased four young men, known as cultured companions, and had them shave their heads to become young monks. Upon reaching Mount Jiaoshan, he undertook the construction of a grand Buddhist pagoda, involving more than thirty thousand laborers, all of whom were sent by his brother, Duanli. Wu then wrote a letter to his brother, saying: "Dear Elder Brother, I hope you are well. I am now a Buddhist monk, different from others, and not like those who study the scriptures and commentaries extensively. I have set aside the pursuit of meditation and wisdom. Recently, I have settled on Mount Jiaoshan, enjoying the scenic beauty of the sea gates and islands, which makes one look up to the heavens. However, the living space is narrow and cramped, making it unsuitable for staying. I wish to widely distribute gold, but it is difficult to find ready funds. Since there is no one to manage the donations, I will have to sacrifice myself. This is also a great good deed, not seeking reward from others, hoping for eternal merit from heaven and future karmic connections. Elder Brother, you must not hesitate to support me. Bring the funds, help me plan and execute everything perfectly, ensuring everyone's happiness and well-being. Otherwise, I will stand alone at the peak, wander aimlessly under the pines, or go door-to-door collecting alms, even though I have traveled extensively and still cannot settle down after eighty years. What do you think, Elder Brother?" Upon receiving the letter, Duanli immediately came to Mount Jiaoshan to assist with the construction.

    220

    而玉为诗亦复奇横，如：“水国白鱼恣口孽，空山黄叶打头陀。”又：“浮绿杯中千日酒，拂青槛外六朝山。”又：“听潮分子午，入定失朝昏。”又：“满山云是无心出，半夜钟因得意撞。”

    禹城道人
    禹城道人王真成，尝游海滨，值亢旱，居人以祈雨央之真成，曰：“天无雨，当借之龙王耳。”乃令一人携一瓶，从入海，至深处，令执瓶者弃瓶返。瓶即随真成俱没。久之，携瓶出，欣然曰：“借得雨来矣。”注于盆。众视之，色白而味甘，与海水殊。乃设坛，倾其盆，风雨骤至，遂获秋成焉。晚年居马山，自言其寿五百三十三岁焉。羽化于康熙七年。

    烧丹
    刘向苦心力学，为一代儒宗，乃得淮南黄白之法，上之天子。后以无验，下狱论死。幸兄阳成侯乞入国以赎，方得减死。唐白乐天亦为方士所惑。惟子瞻得方于扶风僧，程明道得书于鱼腹中，而皆不为。可知世无此术也，明矣！

    Explanation

    220

    Wang Zhēnchéng, the Daoist practitioner of Yǔchéng, composed poetry that was also strange and bold, such as: "In the watery land, white fish indulge in the pleasure of eating; in the empty mountains, yellow leaves hit the head of a monk." Another example: "Green floating in the cup, a thousand-day drink; green beyond the railing, the mountains of the Six Dynasties." Another: "Listening to the tide, distinguishing between noon and midnight; entering a meditative state, losing track of morning and evening." Another: "Clouds cover the mountains, coming out without intention; at midnight, the bell rings happily."

    Wang Zhēnchéng, the Daoist practitioner of Yǔchéng, once traveled by the seaside during a severe drought. The locals asked him to pray for rain. He said, "If there is no rain, we should borrow it from the Dragon King." He then instructed one person to carry a bottle and follow him into the sea. They reached a deep part of the sea, and the person carrying the bottle was told to discard it and return. The bottle followed Wang Zhēnchéng underwater. After a long time, they brought the bottle back, and he happily said, "We have borrowed the rain!" He poured the contents into a basin. Everyone looked at it; it was white and sweet, different from seawater. They set up an altar, poured the basin, and suddenly, wind and rain arrived, leading to a successful harvest that year. In his later years, he lived on Mǎ Mountain and claimed to be 533 years old. He passed away in the seventh year of the Kangxi era.

    Alchemical Practices
    Liu Xiang, a scholar of the Han dynasty, diligently studied and became a leading figure in Confucianism. He obtained the alchemical techniques described in the Huainanzi and presented them to the emperor. However, since there was no proof of their effectiveness, he was imprisoned and sentenced to death. Fortunately, his brother, the Marquis of Yangcheng, pleaded for him, and he was spared. During the Tang dynasty, Bai Juyi was also deceived by a Daoist practitioner. Only Su Shi obtained a formula from a monk in Fufeng, and Cheng Hao found a book in the belly of a fish, but neither of them practiced it. It is clear that there is no such alchemy in the world.

    221

    汶上有孔姓者，父子惑于此。其先世家素丰给。有青城道士精其术，谒孔。孔一见大悦，信如钟、吕，率其子弟从之。为之洁庐安鼎，焚香设帷，更出多金以为炼汞之具。道人亦时时指点火候，传授心法。孔因自号为神仙，名其子曰小神仙。固以为丹成指顾，将一切飞升脱体事似已先为布置者。
    其妻问之曰：“丹，何物也？”孔曰：“至宝也，人服之而成仙，物点之而成金。”妻曰：“诚如是，则异日丹成，将何以谢道人？”孔曰：“汝何藐视渠？点石成金，何所不遂，岂区区为谢仪来哉！”妻曰：“不此之故，天下宁少求丹者？道人何必以丹传之汝？”孔曰：“渠谓我有仙骨。”妻曰：“仙骨何在？”孔乃自耸其臀，曳妻手而抠之曰：“此一节是也。”妻笑曰：“即使成仙，将来亦是屁精，姑不具论。今看汝垂涎铅汞，亦不过平空欲得横财耳。然则蓬莱三岛，昆仑千仞之上，尽皆是几个守钱虏盘踞住乎？”

    Explanation

    221

    In We, there was a man surnamed Kong, whose father and son were fascinated by this. His family had been wealthy and well-provided for. There was a Taoist from Qingcheng who was skilled in his art, and he visited Kong. Kong was delighted upon seeing him and believed in him as much as in Zhong and Lv. He led his sons and disciples to follow him. He prepared a clean dwelling and set up a tripod, burned incense, and set up a canopy, spending more money on equipment for refining mercury. The Taoist also frequently pointed out the timing and heat, teaching the inner methods. Kong then called himself a god and named his son "Little God." He firmly believed that the elixir would be completed at a glance, and he seemed to have already arranged for all matters of flying and leaving the body.

    His wife asked him, "What is the elixir?" Kong replied, "It is a treasure; people who take it become immortals, and objects touched by it turn to gold." His wife said, "If that is true, then when the elixir is completed, how will we thank the Taoist?" Kong said, "Why do you look down on him? Turning stones to gold, what cannot be achieved? Is it just for a token of gratitude?" His wife said, "Is it not for this reason that there are so many people seeking the elixir? Why did the Taoist have to pass the elixir to you?" Kong said, "He said I have the bones of an immortal." His wife said, "Where are the bones of an immortal?" Kong then raised his buttocks, pulled his wife's hand, and said, "This part is it." His wife laughed and said, "Even if you become an immortal, you will still be a fart spirit, but let's not discuss that. Now see you drooling over lead and mercury, just wanting to get rich out of nowhere. Then are the islands of Penglai and the thousand-foot heights of Kunlun all occupied by a few money-grabbing bandits?"

    222

    孔自妻讪后，不惟不听其言，且信道人益笃。一日婿来，妻谓孔曰：“婿贫，丹成之后，幸毋谓传子不传女也。”孔嗫嚅有难色。妻曰：“愚哉夫也！汝尚不肯以未成之丹私汝婿，道人岂遂肯以必成之丹私与汝？汝其为道人之子耶？不然道人何独厚于汝？”次日晨起，仆入告曰：“道人于昨夜不知所往。”孔披衣出视，则已踢倒丹炉，空无火焰。乃慨然曰：“吾师想服丹入九天矣。”妻曰：“恐窃金过别县耳。”孔摇首以为不然。
    后其妻死，无人匡救。父子二人，始则同心合火，以望其成；继且分炉另灶，而私其秘。忽其子曰：“鼎中已见黄芽。”孔喜，欲一见，其子吝不与。孔日泣随之后。其子以之点红铜，不验，乃服之，遍身肿发，气结于喉，而睛突于眶。急服生绿豆，置身凉井中，浸之一日，而金石之毒乃解。由此家愈落。而年逾老，犹鳃鳃然日望大丹之成也。

    Explanation

    222

    Confucius, after being scolded by his wife, not only refused to listen to her but also believed the Taoist even more firmly. One day, his son-in-law came, and his wife told Confucius, "Your son-in-law is poor. After the elixir is completed, please do not say that you will pass it on to your son but not to your daughter." Confucius muttered hesitantly, looking uneasy. His wife said, "How foolish you are! You still refuse to give your incomplete elixir to your son-in-law. How could the Taoist then give you the completed elixir? Are you the Taoist's son? Otherwise, why would the Taoist be so generous to you?" The next morning, a servant came in and reported, "The Taoist disappeared last night without a trace." Confucius put on his clothes and went out to see, and found that the alchemy furnace had been kicked over, with no flame left. He sighed and said, "My teacher must have taken the elixir and ascended to the heavens." His wife said, "Perhaps he stole the gold and left for another county." Confucius shook his head, thinking otherwise.

    After his wife died, there was no one to help or rescue him. The father and son, at first, worked together with the same goal, hoping for success; later, they separated the furnaces and stoves, keeping their secrets to themselves. Suddenly, his son said, "The cauldron shows signs of the yellow sprout." Confucius was happy and wanted to see it, but his son was stingy and wouldn't show him. Confucius cried every day, following his son. His son tried using it to turn red copper into gold, but it didn't work. He then took it himself, and his whole body swelled, his breath was blocked, and his eyes bulged out. He quickly ate raw mung beans and placed himself in a cool well, soaking for a day, and the poison from the alchemical substances was finally neutralized. From then on, his family fell even further into decline. Even when he was old, he still anxiously hoped for the completion of the great elixir every day.

    223

    余尝过汶阳之墟，式其居，见其人颇长厚。惟是倖获之念锢于中，遂至失其所向，流于邪僻而不知返，为可悲也。
    (七如氏曰：甲辰家居穷窘，为孔道士所惑，严冬风雪中，脱皮裘，质典库，而候炉火。一日汞走烟飞，道士故作懊悔之状。余挥拳痛击，道士伏地妆鳖爬而去，余则相鼠无皮矣。此亦孔道士实事。)

    高道士
    江阴有高道士，与常州潘烂头友善。潘能敕勒之术。高受业于潘，潘能高亦能之。自是呼吸风云，指挥雷雨，如探之囊中易易也。尝榜其户曰：“出卖风云雷雨。”海舟有欲风者，得其符焚之，则片帆如驶，数百里可一日至。途人恐日炽，思云作盖，售以金，则幢幢然覆之而行。儿童欲雷雨为戏，书之符，令合其拳，一撒手而声响骤发。田夫望雨，得其资，隔陇与之，大约钱多则多与，钱少则少与，其价皆不相若。高尝夜拥群妓，醉中拘遣神将云。如是有年。

    Explanation

    223

    I once passed by the ruins of Wenyang, visited their dwellings, and found the people to be quite kind and honest. Yet, the thought of unjustly obtaining something was deeply rooted in their minds, leading them to lose their direction, drift into evil, and not know to return, which is pitiful.

    (Qiru said: In the year Jiachen, I was living in poverty at home and was deceived by a Taoist named Kong. In the severe winter wind and snow, I took off my fur coat and pawned it in the pawnshop, waiting by the stove. One day, the mercury ran away and the smoke flew, and the Taoist pretended to be regretful. I punched him fiercely, and the Taoist fell to the ground and crawled away like a turtle, while I became a rat without skin. This is also a true story of the Taoist Kong.)

    High Taoist
    In Jiangyin, there was a Taoist named Gao, who was friendly with a man named Pan from Changzhou. Pan could perform the technique of controlling spirits. Gao learned from Pan, and if Pan could do it, Gao could do it too. From then on, he could breathe wind and clouds, command thunder and rain, as if they were in his pocket, easy as pie. He once posted a sign on his door saying, "Selling wind, clouds, thunder, and rain." If a sea vessel wanted wind, they got his talisman and burned it, then the ship sailed like a speeding arrow and could reach hundreds of miles in one day. A traveler feared the sun would scorch him and thought to use clouds as an umbrella, sold it for money, and walked under it, covered by the cloud. Children wanted thunder and rain for play, wrote a talisman and told them to clench their fists, and as soon as they opened their hands, the sound of thunder erupted. A farmer hoped for rain, got his money and gave it across the field, roughly, the more money, the more rain; the less money, the less rain, and the prices were all different. Gao once hosted a group of courtesans at night, in a drunken state, he commanded the gods to send spirits. Like this for many years.

    224

    高游豫章，与当事诸公登滕王阁。是日江波震荡，风浪拍天。遥望远际，一小舟平稳徐徐而来。高指曰：“此中固大有人在也。”乃取盆水，折阶前竹叶置水上，指拨而口嘘之，叶左舟随之左，叶右舟亦随之右。集者正在环视，高忽曰：“彼飞剑来斩我，将奈何？”急取一鸡，乃自蹲几下，觉冷光旋绕，鸡断其首而去。高起，仍戏叶弄水，忽又曰：“彼已知非人，血剑又来！”高复欲蹲，而高首已落，滚首于阁板上，格格有声。高手摸而戴于颈，曰：“可恨也！”捺叶碎盆，而江上之舟已渺无踪矣。
    噫，高之术神，而其心忍甚！夏，高当午浴，天无片云，雷霆遽裂，殛之而死。背有一行云：“带血登坛犹可恕，隔田施雨最难饶。”

    Explanation

    224

    Gao traveled to Yuzhang and, along with the officials of the time, ascended the Tengwang Pavilion. On that day, the river waves were shaking, and the wind and waves were beating against the sky. Looking far into the distance, a small boat was coming steadily and slowly. Gao pointed and said, "There are certainly great people here." Then he took a basin of water, broke off a bamboo leaf from the steps, and placed it on the water. He pointed and blew, and when the leaf moved to the left, the boat followed to the left; when the leaf moved to the right, the boat followed to the right. The gathered officials were watching in awe, when suddenly Gao said, "That sword is flying to cut me, what can I do about it?" He quickly took a chicken and then squatted down on the mat. He felt a cold light swirling around, and the chicken cut off his head and flew away. Gao stood up again and continued to play with the leaf and water, when he suddenly said, "He has already realized that it is not a human, and the bloody sword is coming again!" Gao wanted to squat again, but his head had already fallen. His head rolled on the floor of the pavilion, making a clattering sound. He picked up his head and put it back on his neck, saying, "How hateful!" He pressed the leaf, breaking the basin, and the boat on the river had already disappeared without a trace.

    Alas, Gao's magic is divine, but his heart is extremely cruel! In summer, when Gao was bathing at noon, with no clouds in the sky, thunder suddenly split, and he was struck dead. On his back was a line of text: "Even if he climbed the altar with blood on his hands, it could still be forgiven, but it is hardest to forgive him for causing rain to fall across fields."

    225

    (余谓圣人之教，师表乎万世者也。若释、道两门，亦足以感人善心，外此皆邪教也。高道士之妖术邪法，致遭天谴，固无论已。乃有奸徒，诬民惑众，可惜蠢尔愚众，偏易煽动，听其引诱，以致牵朋联伍而奔聚矣，挟女带妇而偕往矣。谬言敛物，实则敛祸；妄托升天，实则渎天；诡称行善，实则行淫。迨人聚日众，邪谋一败，遂服上刑，皆无漏网。如明之白莲教、清水教、天主教。国初亦有无为教倡于浙郡，大被教起于海宁。今东省逆匪王伦之神拳法、直隶大名段逆之八卦教，济南新城又有一炷香教，莫不身罹重法，搜剔根株，一无噍类。或幸逃宪刑，而阴罚亦随之而立至，可不慎哉！)

    残菊诗
    莱阳学士李端，为道士而颠，周游无定，又名风道人。忽一日哭，忽一日嘻，忽一日酒，忽一日诗。不住庙，不诵经，更不茹素。与赵遂抡、王大椿相倡和，尝咏残菊一联云：“憔悴根下无时雨，冷落枝头有众星。”了无俗韵。

    Explanation

    225

    Translation

    Writings of the Sage: A Cautionary Tale and the Poem of the Withered Chrysanthemum

    I believe that the teachings of the sage serve as a model for all generations. The Buddhist and Daoist traditions are also capable of touching the hearts of people with kindness, but anything beyond these is heretical. The sorcery and witchcraft of the Daoist master Gao led to divine retribution, which is a given. There are also wicked individuals who deceive the people and mislead the masses, and it is a pity that the foolish and ignorant masses are easily incited. They listen to their inducements and end up dragging their friends and associates along, gathering women and wives to follow. They spread false words to gather wealth, but in reality, they accumulate disaster; they falsely claim to ascend to heaven, but in reality, they offend the divine; they falsely claim to do good deeds, but in reality, they engage in immoral sexual behavior. When their numbers grow, their evil schemes inevitably fail, and they all face severe punishment, none escaping. For example, the White Lotus Sect, the Clear Water Sect, and the Catholic Church during the Ming Dynasty. At the beginning of the Qing Dynasty, there was the Wuwei Sect in Zhejiang, and the Dabi Sect in Hai Ning. In today's Shandong, the rebel Wang Lun's Shenquan technique, the Baigua Sect led by Duan in Zhili's Daming, and the Yizhuang Sect in Jining's Xincheng—all faced severe punishment, and their roots were thoroughly investigated, leaving no survivors. Some may escape legal punishment, but unseen retribution will inevitably follow, so one must be cautious!

    The Withered Chrysanthemum Poem

    The scholar Li Duan of Laiyang, who became a Daoist and went mad, wandered without a fixed abode, and was also known as the Wind Daoist. One day he would cry, the next day he would laugh, the next day he would drink, and the next day he would write poetry. He did not stay in temples, did not recite scriptures, and did not eat vegetarian food. He exchanged verses with Zhao Suilun and Wang Dachun, and once composed a couplet about the withered chrysanthemum: "Withered roots beneath the ground, no timely rain; cold branches above, stars abound." It has no vulgar charm.

    226

    常静莲
    岱岳斗姥宫多女道士，俗朝山者多认亲家。初至庙，盏茶佳果，而客则以祈嗣为名，神前拜祷之后，若以为其嗣自庙中实与之也，遂姻娅焉。客择其美而亲之，再至，则旧婚媾焉。
    肥邑有郑法坤，字宏宇，美而文，知名士。尝曰：“自古沙门固当女流，何也？禅榻留云，较胜西厢待月。”有年，郑登岱，谒斗姥宫。女尼数辈谦喜承迎，通问姓氏。中一少者，鬓边才剃，头皮青如抹黛，着藕色道服，小眉丝靸，白庞如月，额正中有痣一点比凝脂。侪诸群偶，真无其伦。生心好之，而睛不转。少尼笑指曰：“个人贼目刺人，当是贼。”生曰：“尔帏后一小龛贮伪器，我曾窃得一具来。”小尼以袖掩口，笑中带骂而出。生问他尼，告曰：“此常静莲，肥城人，挂搭于后石坞，今去矣。”舆者促生，生不得已怅怅下山。归里时怀念之，冀续旧，不果也。

    Explanation

    226

    Final Translation

    Chang Jinglian

    The Temple of the Great Star Mother on Mount Tai is home to many female Daoists. Ordinary pilgrims who come to the mountain often pretend to be related to each other. Upon their first visit to the temple, they are offered tea and fruit, and the guests often claim to be praying for descendants. After praying before the deity, they believe that their descendants will come from the temple itself, thus forming a kinship. The guests choose the most beautiful and select them as partners, and upon subsequent visits, they become established marital partners.

    In Fei County, there was Vang Fakun, courtesy name Hongyu, who was both beautiful and cultured, a well-known scholar. He once said, "Since ancient times, monks and nuns have been women, why is that? The serene atmosphere of a temple, with clouds lingering over the meditation seats, is more refined than the romantic life depicted in The Western Chamber." One year, Vang ascended Mount Tai and visited the Temple of the Great Star Mother. Several female nuns greeted him with humility and joy, asking about his name and surname. Among them was a young nun whose sides of hair were just shaved, her scalp as blue as if it were smeared with black ink. She wore a light pink or pale purple robe, her eyebrows finely drawn, her hair slightly disheveled, her face as white and round as the moon, with a mole on her forehead as smooth and white as congealed fat. Among the group of women, she was truly without equal. Vang liked her in his heart, and his eyes did not move away from her. The young nun laughed and pointed, saying, "Your eyes are sharp and piercing, you must be a thief." Vang replied, "There is a small shrine behind your curtain, storing something improper, and I have stolen one already." The young nun covered her mouth with her sleeve, laughing while scolding, and then left. Vang asked another nun, who told him, "This is Chang Jinglian, from Fei County, who stays temporarily in the back stone hut, and she has left now." The bearers of the sedan chair urged him to leave, and Vang, unable to resist, reluctantly descended the mountain with a sense of regret. Upon returning home, he often thought of her, hoping to renew their acquaintance, but he never did.

    227

    会生妻有香愿，即邑境余邱之白华庵。入庵，见一女冠绝色。生妻与之语，通乡籍，女冠曰：“旧岁有郑生朝岱者，得非府上郎君乎？”郑妻曰：“然。”女属其归致问。郑妻归，果述焉。生喜，犹忆其肥人，即奋骑驰五十里，到庵，日尚未下舂。叩扉，老尼出。生问常，常即自殿中出，笑谓生曰：“何传命之速耶！”相与入室，备道思慕。生问莲何以至此，莲曰：“我博山人，曾寄养于西乡山后姑家。后我病，仍归博，遂为尼。”谓老尼曰：“此师叔，为我姑姊妹行。”饭生，生以香资与老尼，老尼喜曰：“郎君我师侄友也。今薄暮，盍与吾侄作抵足谈？”生喜，老尼且为之办刍秣。

    Explanation

    227

    Coincidentally, the wife of the scholar had a wish to burn incense, and she went to the White Flower Temple in the county of Yuqiu. Upon entering the temple, she saw a beautiful nun. The wife spoke with the nun and shared their hometown. The nun said, "Last year, there was a man named Zheng who came to Mount Dai. Isn't he the son of your family?" The wife replied, "Yes." The nun asked her to convey a message when she returned. The wife returned and indeed told the story. The scholar was delighted and still remembered the nun as a fat person. He immediately rode his horse for fifty miles to the temple, and the sun had not yet set. He knocked on the door, and an old nun came out. The scholar asked about Chang, and Chang came out from the hall, smiling and saying to him, "How quickly you conveyed the message!" They entered the room together and fully expressed their longing. The scholar asked Lotus why she was there, and she said, "I am from Bo Mountain. I was once fostered at my aunt's house in the west of the mountain. After I fell ill, I returned to Bo and became a nun." She told the old nun, "This is my aunt, she is my sister or cousin." They fed the scholar, and he gave her incense money. The old nun happily said, "You are my nephew and friend. Now it is late afternoon, why don't we have a long talk?" The scholar was delighted, and the old nun prepared fodder for him.

    228

    莲与生在禅室设榻，老尼去厨下寝。生乃与莲备极燕好。莲曰：“郎君一宵之情，尚为我图百年之好乎？”生曰：“容暇谋之。”莲遂不言。翌旦，生归，不能置，复来。见老尼爨灶下，问之，曰：“渠云游，卓锡无定踪。”生固问，老尼不答。生乃知前日之陈词仓猝矣。从此音耗遂隔。
    后六年，生于试后登岱，步行，欲细访静莲所在。至斗姥宫，问伊消息，不得，乃独行。至后石坞，崎岖难行，草深风大，树木丛杂。峰下微露梵烟一缕。生抵山门，门半掩，野鸟格辀，小犬嘷嘷。生直入，殿无人，顾左廊，则静莲坐蒲团上作缝纫。及莲见生，莲面转里。生趋入室，见莲身畔一小儿哝哝。生曰：“卿何忍为此态耶！”莲曰：“孰忍？孰不忍？必有辨之者！”生跪，继以泣。小儿曰：“若拜佛子，当往殿中去。”莲笑而起曰：“小儿笑尔矣。

    Explanation

    228

    In the Zen room, 莲 and 生 set up a bed, while the elderly nun went to sleep in the kitchen. 生 then indulged in the utmost pleasure with 莲. 莲 said, "My dear, do you still want to plan for a lifetime together after one night's affection?" 生 replied, "I will plan it when convenient." 莲 then remained silent. The next day, 生 returned home but could not bear to leave and came back. He saw the elderly nun cooking in the kitchen and asked her about her whereabouts. She replied, "I am a wandering monk, with no fixed trace." 生 persisted in asking, but the elderly nun did not answer. 生 then realized that his previous words had been spoken in a hurry. From then on, they became separated and lost contact.

    Six years later, after passing an examination, 生 climbed Mount Tai on foot, intending to carefully find out where 静莲 was. He reached the Temple of the Queen Mother of the North Star and inquired about her news but found none, so he continued alone. He arrived at the area known as 后石坞, where the terrain was rugged and difficult to traverse, with deep grass, strong winds, and dense trees. From the mountain peak, a faint incense smoke could be seen. When 生 reached the temple gate, it was half-open, with wild birds chirping and little dogs barking. He entered directly, and the main hall was empty. Looking to the left corridor, he saw 静莲 sitting on a mat, engaged in sewing. When 莲 saw 生, she turned her face inward. 生 rushed into the room and saw a small child making soft sounds beside 莲. 生 said, "How can you bear to act like this?" 莲 replied, "Who can bear it? Who cannot bear it? There must be someone who can distinguish this!" 生 knelt down and then wept. The child said, "If you want to pay respects to the Buddha, you should go to the main hall." 莲 laughed and stood up, saying, "The child is laughing at you."

    229

    今日是何向风，吹得到此？自君一夕之淹，何期得此赘累，本欲弃此榛莽，又思为留嗣息。”指儿曰：“此尔父来也。”小儿果扑生，生抱之，遂依生膝下。是夕，生留宿，并计与生同归。莲曰：“郎君以我为何如人也？人贵适意耳，况闲云野鹤，性成脱略。岂能向足缠绺发阵中效奔走、充下陈耶？前在白华庵中，曾得一睹尊夫人阃范，察其意旨，虽不至即下逐客之令，亦未必遂开延揽之门。我有褊心，是以不敢请耳。”终不许。早，生别，并携子下山。生妻无出，得子甚喜。后常竟绝迹焉。

    Explanation

    229

    "Where is the wind blowing me today, that I find myself here? Since your stay for one night, who would have thought I would be burdened with this. I originally wanted to abandon this wilderness, but then thought to leave behind an heir. I pointed to the child and said, 'This is your father here.' The child indeed ran to me, and I held him, so he clung to my knees. In the morning, we parted, and I took the child down the mountain. My wife had no children, and I was very happy to have a son. Afterward, she completely disappeared."

    "Sir, what kind of person do you think I am? The value of a person lies in being content. I lead a carefree and unburdened life, by nature, I am unrefined or unconventional. How could I possibly serve in the bustling world, running errands and being part of the crowd? Before, in the White Lotus Temple, I once had the chance to see your esteemed wife's manner, observing her intentions. She did not immediately order me to leave, but she also did not open the door to invite me back. I have a narrow-mindedness, that is why I dare not ask. She finally refused. Early in the morning, we parted, and I took the child down the mountain. My wife had no children, and I was very happy to have a son. Afterward, she completely disappeared."

    230

    子名芳，幼慧，十岁能文，举神童，十五领乡荐。大设喜筵，牵羊担酒，宾朋沓至，瓜葛盈门。忽一女道士，年三十余，来贺。延之堂上，芳问曰：“大师从何处来？”女道士曰：“贵人莫问我来处，当先自问贵人来处。知贵人从何处来，即知我之来处矣。”芳茫然曰：“识家君否？”曰：“十五年前，似曾相识。”芳乃告父。生倒屣曰：“尔母也！”果静莲。相与悲喜交集，入内与夫人相见。生缅述而告其子，芳大恸。五六岁时，如梦寐中。生劝常享子之荣，莲曰：“泡影浮沤，久不作尘中想矣。”拂衣欲去。生与子泣，苦留之，乃许。于村前里许建刹，曰“慈云庵”。莲清修其间，生日过从。庵中竹最盛，秋夏多凉，谈宴棋酒，往来不绝，生与莲相敬爱，若良友云。

    Explanation

    230

    Zi was named Fang. He was young and intelligent, able to write at ten years old. He was selected as a prodigy and at fifteen, he was recommended for the provincial examination. A grand banquet was set up, and guests arrived with sheep and wine. Relatives filled the door. Suddenly, a female Taoist priest, over thirty years old, came to congratulate. She was invited to the hall, and Zi asked, "Where does the master come from?" The female Taoist priest said, "Do not ask me where I come from; you should first ask yourself where you come from. Knowing where you come from will reveal where I come from." Zi茫然曰："识家君否？" "Do you know my father?" The female Taoist priest said, "It seems familiar." Zi then told his father. His father hurriedly said, "She is your mother!" Indeed, she was Jinglian. They were filled with mixed feelings of joy and sorrow and went inside to meet the lady. His father recounted the story to his son, and Zi was deeply moved. When he was five or six years old, it was like a dream. His father encouraged him to enjoy the honor of being a son, but Jinglian said, "Like a bubble or a floating foam, I have long stopped thinking about the world." She waved her sleeves and wanted to leave. His father and son begged her to stay, and she finally agreed. A temple was built in front of the village, called 'Ciyun Monastery.' She practiced her faith there, and they visited on her birthday. They engaged in conversation, banquets, chess, and wine, with constant visits. His father and Jinglian respected and loved each other like good friends.

    231

    (按：此条实一乡先生事，特隐其名。盖以事之无关劝惩，适足以扬人之过耳。况女冠比尼，悉为阴类，犹当痛绝，奈何引而近之？郑子之行固无足道，而尼之或隐或见，或有情或无情，其踪迹又诡异不测。卒之村外留云，转令为之子者，几无地以容身。故刘畏所省躬之语、姚端恪传家之训，未尝不严以为戒。而世之靡靡者，咸以为利于科名，交往愈密。呜呼，岂一郑子乎哉！)

    卷八 闺阃部(姬妾妓女附)

    Explanation

    231

    (Commentary: This matter is actually about a village scholar, but his name is concealed. It is because the matter has no relevance to moral instruction or punishment, and it only serves to expose someone's faults. Moreover, female Taoist priests and Buddhist nuns are all of the 'yin' class, and they should be utterly rejected; how can one bring oneself close to such people? Zhenzi's conduct is indeed not worth mentioning, but the nuns may be hidden or visible, and may have feelings or not, and their traces are again strange and unpredictable. Finally, a cloud lingers outside the village, turning out that his son has no place to stand. Therefore, Liu Wei's self-examination and reflection, as well as Yao Duanke's family teachings, have never failed to take it as a strict warning. However, those in the world who are morally lax believe it is beneficial for passing exams, and their interactions become more frequent. Alas, is this only about Zhenzi?)

    Chapter Eight: The Department of Women (with Appendices on Concubines and Prostitutes)

    232

    董子玉一家言
    董子玉祖籍北方，而生长南地。其先人官于吴，遂家松江。为人宽厚和平，年少老成。道逢裙衩，常以扇障面，或俯首疾趋。又慷慨好施。读书不达，而货殖焉，遂商旅于闽广间。贩丝丝贵，贩米米昂，不五六年，奇赢十倍。妻盛氏，美而贤。有婢暖云，幼鬻于董，年及笄，艳丽无俦，针黹绝伦，遂纳为姬，盛氏雅爱之。
    会子玉过维扬，又买一妾张氏，以二百金得之。年十七，亦韶秀，名兰绡，善烹饪之法。其父尝为鹾商供刀匕，故精于味。归董后，每郁郁叹闷，如有隐忧。董问之，兰曰：“奴得侍郎君，又闻夫人不妒，终身愿足。但有义妹阿进，我邻何姓女也。其父赤贫，与奴垂髫闺友，誓相爱顾。今天各一方，恐将来所适非人，用是悲耳。倘郎君能爱屋及乌，亦为罗致，则合璧连珠，共事君子，实为万幸！”言罢，泪下如雨，敛衽以请。董曰：“荐贤者受上赏，卿不愧为君子姬，当论首功。”遂允。亦二百金买。

    Explanation

    232

    Dong Ziyu's Family Anecdotes
    Dong Ziyu was born in the south, though his ancestral home was in the north. His ancestors served in the state of Wu and eventually settled in Songjiang. He was broad-minded, kind, and peaceful, displaying the maturity of a young man. When he met women on the road, he often used a fan to shield his face or quickly lowered his head. He was also generous and fond of giving. Despite not achieving success in his studies, he turned to business and traveled as a merchant in Fujian and Guangdong. He sold silk when it was expensive and rice when it was high-priced, making a tenfold profit within five or six years. His wife, Sheng, was beautiful and virtuous. He had a maid named Nuanyun, who was sold to Dong at a young age. When she reached adulthood, she was exceptionally beautiful and skilled in needlework, and he took her as a concubine, whom Sheng loved dearly.
    When Dong Ziyu passed through Weiyang, he bought another concubine named Zhang for two hundred gold coins. She was seventeen years old and also graceful and beautiful, named Lanxiao, and skilled in cooking. Her father had once worked as a salt merchant's chef, so she was skilled in flavors. After joining the Dong family, she often sighed and seemed troubled, as if she had hidden worries. Dong asked her, and Lan replied: "I have been fortunate to serve Lord Dong, and I have heard that Lady Sheng is not jealous, so my lifelong wishes are fulfilled. But I have a sworn sister, A Jin, who is a neighbor's daughter. Her father is very poor, and we were childhood friends, vowing to care for each other. Now we are separated, and I fear she may marry an unsuitable man, which is why I am sad. If Lord Dong could extend his kindness to her as well, and bring her into the family, it would be like two pearls joining together, and we would both serve a gentleman. That would truly be a great fortune!" She finished speaking, and tears streamed down her face as she bowed respectfully and pleaded. Dong said: "Those who recommend good people receive the highest reward. You are not unworthy to be a lady of a gentleman, and you should be credited with the main achievement." He agreed and bought her for two hundred gold coins as well.

    233

    阿进年十六，杭人，父幕死于扬。亦娟好，兼通书算、弹丝。喜不自胜，携归，如载宝而来。盛氏闻之，先舆来迎而归。盛氏因劝董曰：“风涛雨雪，带水环山，适可而止。今家迄小康，不劳更作行旅想矣。”董遂鬻田百亩，构一精舍，环以竹木，经岁不出，悠然怡乐。一妻三妾，爱若同胞。衣则易着，食则共器，既偶俱之无猜，更相期以共济。一家之中，盛氏总其成，暖云司纫事，兰绡佐中馈，阿进操会计书写，分任焉。皆擅其长，即错综焉，亦无废事。凡有妇人入董门者，皆薰其德而化为善，一时子玉之邻无诟谇声。
    每见夫妻燕好，一有小星，顿起参商；甚至林间狮吼，岭上鹃啼。况一再至三，连袂交枝。借使三善能称，而一夫作难，又安得保此庸庸之福也哉！说者谓董子善齐家，余谓董子有修身之道。想其被面障扇时，其气象固已异矣。使董子出而为仕，化家为国，正有可观。噫！董子不以轩冕之荣，易此闺房之乐也。

    Explanation

    233

    Ah Jin, aged sixteen, was from Hangzhou, and his father had died in Yangzhou. He was also graceful and proficient in arithmetic, calculation, and playing stringed instruments. Overjoyed, he was brought back, as if carrying a treasure. The Sheng family heard of this and sent a carriage to welcome him back. The Sheng family then advised Dong, saying, "The winds, waves, rain, and snow, with water and mountains surrounding, stop at the right moment.
