Huang once told others, "Since Hui came to our home, we no longer know how to prepare food; we just open our mouths for food and move our hands for clothing."

    273

    胳瘩老娘
    湖州有婺妇，号胳瘩老娘。能刀笔，为讼师，远近皆耳其名。凡有大讼久年不结者，凭其一字数笔，皆可挽折，虽百喙不能置辩。因之射利，计利厚则蔑理甚。
    邑有富甲之媳，早孀，欲改适。翁不许，强其贞守。媳丐于老娘。老娘索其一千六百金，弁其状十六字曰：“氏年十九，夫死无子，翁壮而鳏，叔大未娶。”官遂令其他适。会江北岁不登，人皆贩米江南。江南之人闭籴。构讼汹汹，贩者蜂拥，莫可为计。有知老娘者，恳其一词。索以三千金。词今日入，而明日遂放籴焉。其全词不录，中有一联云：“列国分争，尚有移民移粟；天朝一统，何分江北江南。”

    Explanation

    273

    Translation:

    The Old Woman with the Nickname "Gādā"

    In the city of Huzhou, there was a woman from the Wu region, known as Gādā Lǎoniáng. She was skilled in legal writing and served as a lawyer, and her name was well-known throughout the region. For any major lawsuit that had been unresolved for many years, relying on her to write a few words could persuade the parties to settle, even if a hundred people tried to argue against it. Because of this, she pursued profit, and when the profit was great, she disregarded reason to a great extent.

    In the city, there was the daughter-in-law of a wealthy man who had become a widow at a young age and wished to remarry. Her father-in-law refused and forced her to remain chaste. The widow sought help from Gādā Lǎoniáng. Gādā Lǎoniáng demanded one thousand six hundred gold pieces and wrote a sixteen-character legal document: “She is nineteen years old, her husband has died and left no children, her father-in-law is strong and unmarried, and her younger brother-in-law is grown but not yet married.” The official then ordered her to remarry.

    Coincidentally, there was a poor harvest in the northern regions, and people from the north traveled to the south to buy rice. The people in the south refused to sell rice. This led to widespread legal disputes, and the buyers rushed to buy rice, leaving no one with a solution. Someone who knew Gādā Lǎoniáng sought her help. She demanded three thousand gold pieces. The legal document was submitted today, and the next day, the sale of rice was allowed. The entire document is not recorded here, but it includes a couplet that says: “In the divided states of the Warring States period, there was still immigration and the movement of grain; in the unified empire, how can one distinguish between the north and the south?”

    274

    浙人吴姓，家富有，蓄优伶。有伶人问吴曰：“如捉得窃贼，将何法而痛惩之？”吴曰：“有一法最妙，当倒悬之，用陈醋灌鼻孔中，则窃苦甚，诘其事，可无遁词。”适外村有监生某，太戆生也，不懂人事。一日观剧于村，值夜人散，监独立场下。伶以为窃，絷而问，不答，遂如吴法，灌醋而死。鸣于官，验之，为某村监生。官鞫伶，伶以为受之于吴，复拘吴刑之，遂承招焉。吴之子幕于豫，闻父难，遄归。百词而莫赎其父，乃往湖州求老娘。奉以多金，遂为捉刀，立就一词。其词中用意，引孟子言燕可伐一节，“伐燕固在齐而不在孟子”云云。词入乃释吴，而罪定灌醋者。

    Explanation

    274

    A man from Zhejiang named Wu was wealthy and kept actors. An actor asked Wu, "If we catch a thief, what method should we use to punish him severely?" Wu replied, "There is one method that is most effective: hang him upside down and pour vinegar into his nostrils. The thief will suffer greatly, and when questioned, he will have no way to evade the truth." Just then, there was a monitor from a village named Wu, who was extremely naive and did not understand human affairs. One day, he was watching a play in the village when the crowd had dispersed at night. The monitor stood alone in the field. The actor thought he was a thief, bound him, and questioned him. When the monitor did not answer, they followed Wu's method and force-fed him vinegar, causing him to die. The incident was reported to the authorities, who investigated and found that the victim was the monitor from the village. The authorities questioned the actor, who claimed that he had learned the method from Wu. They then arrested Wu and subjected him to the same punishment, and Wu confessed. Wu's son, who was serving as an advisor in Henan, heard of his father's trouble and hurriedly returned home. He tried every possible argument but could not save his father. He then went to Huzhou to seek out a female lawyer. He offered her a large sum of money, and she wrote a statement on his behalf. The statement cited a passage from Mencius about the possibility of attacking Yan, arguing that "the attack on Yan was the responsibility of Qi, not Mencius." The judge released Wu, and the one who force-fed vinegar was punished.

    275

    吁，是妇亦奇矣！奈何以胳瘩名？盖亦厉气之结也。天之生才，往往令人不可测有如此者。

    二妙
    褚文兴，吴贾也。贸于粤，往来十易春秋，计利倍蓰。而蛮烟瘴雨，经尝备至。粤有黎姓者，褚之旧馆人也。黎有女名二妙，多姿且慧，年十三，甫垂髫。尝于盘鸦后束短发，缕丝作辫，披肩际。褚每南来，多携奁具脂粉赠之。其初也，褚爱之而怜其稚；继也，妙感之而情为移。逆旅之中，双环么凤，借以消遣，而褚亦愈久而不及乱。
    会南归三载，值广南诸郡流疫，商贾断绝。黎氏素无产，萧条贫惫，家遂以落。二妙年十六，母死，其父鳏，尝贷为炊，日不举火。父出不家，妙固茕茕掩双扉也。一日，褚忽至，黎老见之，备道苦况，二妙亦羞以为容。褚不忍去而之他，仍假馆焉。

    Explanation

    275

    Ah, this woman is indeed extraordinary! How is she named Gēdā? It seems to be a result of a malevolent energy or spirit. Heaven's creation of talent is often unpredictable, as seen in such cases.

    Er Miao
    Zhu Wenxing, a merchant from Wu, traded in Guangdong, traveling back and forth for ten years, making profits several times over. He endured the harsh conditions of barbaric smoke and miasma. In Guangdong, there was a man surnamed Li, an old acquaintance of Zhu. Li had a daughter named Er Miao, who was graceful and intelligent, just entering adolescence at thirteen. She would sometimes tie her hair into a short braid after the style of a young girl, with strands hanging over her shoulders. Whenever Zhu came south, he would often bring cosmetics and other gifts for her. At first, Zhu loved her but pitied her youth. Later, she felt his affection and her emotions were swayed. In the inn, they would play games like "Double Rings" and "Little Phoenix" to pass the time, and Zhu, over time, did not act on his feelings.

    After three years of returning south, a plague broke out in the southern regions, cutting off trade. The Li family had no property, and their life became impoverished, leading to their decline. Er Miao was sixteen, her mother died, her father was widowed, and they often borrowed to cook, barely managing to eat. Her father went out and did not stay at home, leaving Er Miao alone, closing the double doors. One day, Zhu suddenly arrived, and the old man of the Li family told him about their hardships. Er Miao was also embarrassed to show her face. Zhu could not bear to leave and go elsewhere, so he continued to stay in the inn.

    276

    粤有大麻疯，人中之，肉溃死，人皆屏弃，不与同巷。男子不治；女有之，与人交接可疗。客粤者往往中其毒，俗名“卖疯”，亦曰“过癞”。时二妙传染是疾，其父使妙移于褚。黎假出，妙至褚所。褚喜求合，女愀然曰：“我不忍祸君也。”遂告以故，且令褚速去，并乞异日病发，望藁葬于道路之旁，言已呜咽。褚曰：“卿无悲泪！”乃出橐金贻妙，“倘果不治，卿即南来，当养卿以天年。”妙拜谢，褚匆匆别。
    后半年创剧，溃出肌肤，众共弃之。妙乃流丐而南，形益秽。十阅月至吴阊，访褚门而告。褚收之，居以废圃。家人日投食，皆掩鼻。圃中有老槐，空其腔，蛇虺凭以为窠。妙食庋于牖上，蛇尝来食妙食，而妙亦食蛇所食之食。妙一日忽收脓结痂，脱然以起。回视荐上，如败鼓皮数十片。

    Explanation

    276

    In Guangdong, there is a severe form of leprosy. When people contract it, their flesh rots and they die, and everyone shuns them, not letting them live in the same alley. Men cannot be cured; if women have it, they can be cured by sexual intercourse. Travelers to Guangdong often contract this disease, which is locally known as "selling madness" or "passing leprosy." At that time, the two wonders (妙) were infected with this disease. Their father made one of the wonders move to Chu (褚). When Li (黎) left, one of the wonders went to Chu's place. Chu was happy to seek union, but the woman sadly said, "I cannot bear to bring disaster upon you." She then told him the reason and asked him to leave quickly, and also begged that if she fell ill in the future, he would bury her beside the road. She wept as she spoke. "You must not shed tears!" He then took out some gold from his bag and gave it to the wonder. "If you indeed cannot be cured, come to the south, and I will support you until your natural end." The wonder bowed in thanks, and Chu left hurriedly.

    After half a year, her condition worsened, her skin rotted, and everyone abandoned her. The wonder then became a beggar and moved south, her appearance becoming increasingly filthy. After ten months, she reached Wu Chang and went to Chu's door to inform him. Chu took her in and housed her in an abandoned garden. The family provided food daily, but they all covered their noses. There was an old locust tree in the garden, hollowed out, which snakes and venomous creatures used as a nest. The wonder placed her food on the window sill, and snakes often came to eat her food, and the wonder also ate the food that the snakes had eaten. One day, the wonder suddenly recovered, her sores healed, and she rose up. Looking back at the mat, there were dozens of pieces like the skins of rotten drums.

    277

    家人异之，褚亦来视，如剥瓠。褚问妙，亦不解其故。更阅月，发理颐丰，居然佳丽。褚妇颇贤，移之闺。况褚本不能忘情于妙，而妙且感情于褚者，遂纳为姬。后葺圃，见大蛇出树中云。

    颠当
    侯文智，天津人。多财，为海舶估，后为引鹾商。酷好声技，多姬妾，悉善弹吹。有门伙某自晋来，送侯一婢，名颠当。年十三，发垂髫而黝黑可照，眉目如水，侯喜自不胜，如获拱璧。一年而百技皆通，妙于音律。每度一曲，不惟能作新声，更多媚态。有时一手支颐，以目流盼，无不与曲中情景绘画而出。房帏间娇容缓步，对之如在消魂桥上，烦渴胥蠲。群婢效之，终莫得其形似。侯尝秘诸密室，虽至戚难睹其面也。

    Explanation

    277

    The family found it strange, and Chu also came to see, as if peeling a gourd. Chu asked about the beauty, but could not understand the reason. After a month, her hair and facial features became full and beautiful, and she suddenly became a beauty. Chu's wife was very virtuous, and moved her to the inner chambers. Since Chu could not forget his feelings for the beauty, and the beauty also felt affection for Chu, he adopted her as a concubine. Later, while repairing the garden, they saw a large snake emerging from a tree.

    DianDang
    Hou Wenzhi, a man from Tianjin, was wealthy, engaged in maritime trade, and later became a salt merchant. He was extremely fond of music and performance, and had many concubines, all skilled in playing and blowing instruments. A servant from Jin came and gave Hou a maid named DianDang. She was thirteen years old, with hair hanging down like a child's, dark and shiny, and eyes like water. Hou was overjoyed, as if he had gained a precious treasure. Within a year, she had mastered a hundred skills, excelling in music and rhythm. Whenever she played a piece, not only could she create new sounds, but she also displayed more charming grace. Sometimes, she would rest her chin on her hand, her eyes flowing with glances, and everything in the music seemed to come alive. In the bedroom, her charming and slow steps made him feel as if he were on the bridge of soul, and his desires were all satisfied. The other maids tried to imitate her, but none could match her. Hou often kept her in secret chambers, even close relatives could not see her face.

    278

    语云“佳人一顾，可以倾城”，况侯生无晋文公之识，而有石季伦之癖，宜乎金屋成而玉山颓矣。五年中商欠累积，一败涂地。始也飘零珠履三千，继也流散金钗十二，触目痛心。侯将不支，遂渐以病。独颠当相依不去。侯曰：“我贫将死，卿当先去，以自为谋。”颠当曰：“妾祸水也，此天遣以祸君家者也。君已及祸，妾将焉往？但妾见君生平虽贪声技，蓄姬妾，尚少淫恶。若断君嗣，未免太惨。妾今娠五月，或得一子以延侯氏后，但不能光大门闾耳。”侯泣谢而逝。家人以颠当美，欲鬻之，颠当骂曰：“我不去，将奈我何？倘他族实逼处此为嫌，则侯家尚有旧楼，我独不能效绿珠碎首耶！”家人又以无可分产，遂听之。乃居侯氏旧园，败屋一区。有恶少夜欲窥之，及其篱藩，即觫栗不敢前。日常闭门，邻家亦不见其有炊烟起，叩户入视，颠当俨然且突黔而釜未生尘也。

    Explanation

    278

    The saying goes, "A glance from a beautiful woman can topple a city," but how much more so for Hou, who lacked the wisdom of Duke Wen of Jin and had the debauchery of Shi Chong? It was only natural that his golden house fell and his jade mountain crumbled. Over five years, his business debts accumulated, leading to his complete ruin. First, he lost his three thousand jade shoes, and then his twelve gold hairpins scattered, causing him great pain. Hou could no longer bear it and gradually fell ill. Only Dian Dang stayed by his side. Hou said, "I am poor and will soon die; you should leave first to take care of yourself." Dian Dang replied, "I am a disaster, sent by heaven to bring misfortune to your family. Since you have already suffered, where else can I go? But I have seen that you, though fond of music and women and having many concubines, were not excessively immoral. If cutting off your lineage is too cruel, I am five months pregnant and may give birth to a child to continue the Hou family line, though I cannot bring glory to the family." Hou wept and thanked her before passing away. The family, seeing Dian Dang's beauty, wanted to sell her. Dian Dang scolded, "I will not leave; what can you do to me? If another family finds it unbearable to live here, then the Hou family still has its old mansion; I cannot follow Green Pearl's example and commit suicide?" The family, finding no other way to divide the property, let her stay. She then lived in the old Hou garden, in a dilapidated house. One night, a wicked youth tried to peek in, but as soon as he reached the fence, he trembled and dared not go further. She usually kept the door closed, and the neighbors did not see any smoke rising from her house. When they knocked on the door and entered to look, Dian Dang was composed, and her chimney was blackened, but the pot had not accumulated dust.

    279

    半年，果产一男，其貌酷类母。及长，人见其韶秀，劝入塾。颠当曰：“几见浪荡子孙有读书成名者？非必其子若孙之果不肖，其所由来非一朝夕之故。”至十岁，梳丱髻，着犊鼻裈，妙丽如脂。其母教之词曲，伊即能曼声莺语，呖呖可听。又令其习妖态，作愁眉啼、折腰步、龋齿笑，大有母风。母令其游于昔日之门下客———皆今日之堂上翁，为之献技醵金。诸人见之，无不颠倒。一时声价，重若千金。

    Explanation

    279

    Half a year later, she gave birth to a son who bore a striking resemblance to his mother. As he grew up, people admired his graceful and handsome appearance and encouraged him to attend school. His father, however, remarked, "How often do we see reckless descendants who become accomplished scholars? It is not necessarily that my son or grandson lacks virtue; the reasons behind such outcomes are not the result of a single day or night." By the time he was ten years old, he wore his hair in a topknot and donned short trousers, appearing as smooth and delicate as fat. His mother taught him the arts of music and poetry, and he quickly developed a soft, melodious voice, clear and pleasant to hear. She also trained him in various gestures and expressions, such as frowning with tears, bending his waist, and grinning with a gap-toothed smile, which greatly resembled his mother's style. She arranged for him to perform for her former disciples, who were now elders or patrons, and he performed for them, collecting money. The audience was captivated by his performance, and his reputation and value became as heavy as a thousand gold pieces.

    280

    咸曰：“颠当不可得而见之矣，得见当子也斯可矣！”于是缠头彩掷，不计其数。颠当乃为之娶妻，而侯氏之嗣，赖以不斩。颠当告其子曰：“是道也，可以歌，不可以娈；可以卜昼，不可以卜夜。总使其若远若近，若有情若无情。取前人所未有之心思，创而新时人之耳目，然后可以惊庸流之闻见，可以移贤智之性情。绣帘文榻间，立红氍毹，正如三神山，可望而不可即，斯其术乃工矣。”所以吴伶避席，越女停桡，名公巨卿乃独噪“当子”之名也。因是始传，至今有此一戏。又云当子狐也，不然，当子不能有是媚。
    (近日在郎牧之宴会中，偶来挡子一班，演唱不终席，闻者皆倦，如对古乐。人情变易，一至于此！)

    Explanation

    280

    All said, "It is impossible to see Diandan, but to see Dangzi is enough!" Thus, they threw headscarves and colorful objects, without counting the number. Diandan then married a wife, and the Hou family's lineage depended on this to continue. Diandan told his son, "This way can be sung but not made into a song, can be predicted for the day but not for the night. Sometimes distant, sometimes close; sometimes emotional, sometimes indifferent. Taking the thoughts that previous people did not have, creating and refreshing the ears and eyes of the present people, then it can shock the ordinary people's knowledge, capable of changing the nature and emotions of the wise and intelligent. In the embroidered curtains and literary couches, standing on a red carpet, just like the three divine mountains, visible but unattainable, this is the skill that is truly accomplished." Therefore, the Wu musicians avoid their seats, the Yue women stop their paddles, and the famous officials and great nobles only clamor for the name of 'Dangzi.' Because of this, it began to be transmitted, and until now there is this one play. Some say that Dangzi is a fox; otherwise, Dangzi could not have this charm.

    (Recently, at a banquet hosted by Lang Muzhi, the Dangzi troupe performed, but their singing did not last the entire banquet, and the listeners were all tired, as if they were listening to ancient music. Human feelings have changed so much that it has come to this!)

    281

    紫欢
    金陵妓紫欢者，自言晋人。十七岁来南，自鬻为娼。河房有富鸨，视其貌无伦比，声技称绝，咸以为钱树子，八百金买之。欢一入院，遂空其群。欢乃毁旧馆垣，嫌其湫隘。自画图式，鸠工庀材，移竹栽花，临水建阁。落成，自题曰“鹦鹉荼蘼之阁”。一切器用服食，穷极工巧。欢尝独置一室，悬纯阳像。人曰：“何不供大士？”欢曰：“论其普渡众生一也，吾尤喜回道人之胆气粗豪，襟期磊落，于我心窃有慕焉。”欢不着隔日衣，文锦绣舄满笥簏。自奉奢侈，贵介大贾之所不能比拟，正所谓“日费万钱，尚无下箸处”也。时之来交紫欢者，但得蒙一盼，虽盈千累万，亦所不惜，且以为荣。
    会当秋风桂子之年，人文聚萃，长板桥头、莫愁湖畔，无不蜂屯蚁附。而紫欢乃大开园亭，广列华筵。预访某州邑名下之士，以及寒单布衣，延之益力。是日，欢盛装华服，曲尽殷勤。试后如有贫不能归者，悉赆焉。

    Explanation

    281

    A Courtesan Named Zihuan

    Zihuan, a courtesan from Jinling, claimed to be from the Jin Dynasty. At the age of seventeen, she came south and sold herself into prostitution. A wealthy madam in the brothel saw her beauty as unparalleled and her skills as unmatched, considering her a source of wealth, and bought her for eight hundred gold coins. Upon Zihuan's arrival, the brothel was immediately filled with admirers. Unsatisfied with the cramped space, she demolished the old courtyard walls and rebuilt it. She designed the layout herself, gathered workers and materials, transplanted bamboo and planted flowers, and built a pavilion by the water. Upon completion, she named it the "Parrot and Tea Rose Pavilion." All the furnishings and decorations were of the finest craftsmanship. Zihuan often kept a room for herself, hanging a portrait of Lü Dongbin. Someone asked, "Why not hang a statue of the Bodhisattva?" Zihuan replied, "In terms of saving all beings, they are the same. I particularly admire Lü Dongbin's boldness, his straightforwardness, and his noble spirit, which I secretly admire." She never wore clothes from the previous day, and her chests and baskets were filled with embroidered silk shoes. She indulged in luxury, surpassing even the most extravagant merchants, truly embodying the saying, "Spending ten thousand coins a day, yet still having nowhere to place the chopsticks." Those who came to visit Zihuan were willing to spend thousands or even tens of thousands of coins just for a glimpse of her, and they considered it an honor.

    During the year known for its pleasant autumn weather and the fragrance of osmanthus, a gathering of talented people took place at the Long Board Bridge and Mochou Lake, where crowds gathered like bees and ants. Zihuan opened her garden and pavilions, setting up elaborate banquets. She pre-selected scholars from famous states and counties, as well as poor commoners, and invited them with great earnestness. On that day, Zihuan dressed in splendid and luxurious clothing, showing utmost hospitality. After the event, if anyone was too poor to return home, she gave them gifts.

    282

    温汝砺，溧阳人。好学能文，苦贫，来试，寓穷邸。欢招温，温辞。一日，欢软舆至温邸，入，温惊问，欢笑曰：“诸君子皆与欢言，先生何以独不与欢言耶？闻先生固穷，谨以百金助膏火。”命从人取置几上。温方逊谢，而欢已出门匆匆去。温于是年登榜首。芜湖大贾汤廷楷艳之，以三千金赎欢为妾。欢从之数月，见汤鄙吝，辄病恶。汤不得已，听欢所欲，以博欢心。欢则为之华屋宇、美衣食、蓄婢仆、延宾客，优伶技艺日满庭除，欢犹以为无可消遣者。二年，汤不能供币赋。汤贫欢去，复归金陵旧院，车填马溢，震动一时。巷中人曰：“欢姑娘回，穷人之福，富人之灾也。”当紫欢之在金陵也，十年之中，富人为所倾败者二十五家；而里巷编氓之间，望之举火者日三百人。

    Explanation

    282

    Wen Ruli, a man from Liyang, was a scholar who loved learning and was skilled in writing. Despite his poverty, he came to take an examination and lodged in a humble inn. Huan invited Wen, but Wen declined. One day, Huan arrived in a carriage to Wen's residence, entered, and Wen was surprised, asking questions. Huan smiled and said, "All the gentlemen have spoken with me, but why haven't you, Master Wen? I have heard that you are steadfast in your poverty, so I respectfully offer a hundred gold pieces to help with your studies." Huan then ordered someone to fetch the money and place it on the table. Wen was about to humbly decline, but Huan had already left hurriedly. That year, Wen ranked first in the examination. A wealthy merchant from Wuhu, Tang Tingkai, was attracted to Huan and offered three thousand gold pieces to take her as a concubine. Huan lived with Tang for several months, but seeing his stinginess, she fell ill with disgust. Tang, unable to help it, complied with Huan's wishes to win her favor. Huan then lived a life of luxury, with beautiful houses, fine clothing and food, servants, and guests. Actors and musicians filled the courtyard every day, but Huan still felt there was nothing to do. After two years, Tang could no longer afford the expenses. Poor and with Huan leaving, she returned to her old quarters in Jinling, where carriages and horses filled the streets, causing a great stir. People in the neighborhood said, "Huan the lady has returned, a blessing for the poor, a disaster for the rich." During Huan's time in Jinling, over ten years, 25 wealthy families were ruined; and among the common people, 300 people hoped to benefit from her presence.

    283

    一日，有老尼至欢门，欲见欢。欢出迎，尼曰：“事完否？”欢曰：“妾已了之矣。”尼乃按欢顶上，应手而匾，纳入一荷囊而去。今秦淮河诸妓，尤有爱士之风，其紫欢之遗耶！

    阿嫱
    阿嫱，广东肇庆女子也。年十四，母死。父好樗蒲，多负，鬻女，女遂落狭邪。有富舍喜之，以番钱五百购为小星，贮之别室，昵爱逾常。几年为大妇觉，大妇固悍妒，操刀往逐之，无见怜意，舍不得已，遣之。媒居为奇货，携之番禺。

    Explanation

    283

    One day, an elderly nun arrived at the door of Huan, intending to see her. Huan came out to greet her, and the nun asked, "Is the matter finished?" Huan replied, "I have already taken care of it." The nun then pressed Huan's head, and with a sudden gesture, she placed something in a small bag and left. Nowadays, the courtesans along the Qinhuai River are especially known for their affection for scholars—could this be the legacy of Zihuan?

    Ah Qiang
    Ah Qiang was a woman from Zhaoqing, Guangdong. At the age of fourteen, her mother passed away. Her father enjoyed playing a gambling game called chupu and often lost, so he sold her. As a result, she fell into the world of courtesans. A wealthy patron took a liking to her and bought her for five hundred foreign coins as a concubine, keeping her in a separate room and showing her more affection than usual. After several years, the main wife discovered this and, being violent and jealous, took a knife and chased her away, showing no pity. Reluctantly, the patron let her go. The matchmaker kept her as a valuable commodity and took her to Panyu.

    284

    番禺为省会首邑，沙面皆蛋户，厮养娃妓，不下千百。蛋户为粤之不齿类，以舟为屋，沙水聚族为里，捕鱼虾为业，如晋之乐户、浙之惰伻、楚之渔户也。媒卖之蛋艇。阿嫱一至，遂空其群，嫱复落烟花。嫱智慧不凡，所见辄通，艺技入手，无不精妙，间亦涉书词。粤中有《摸鱼歌》最雅，嫱信口占之，皆妙句。如云：
    二月南风莫怕寒，阿嫂行上望夫山。云横云断浈江水，情郎贩米下梧关。
    芭蕉取丝不呷果，丝丝织作千孔罗。落尽木棉花如锦，一身縠薄好郎摸。
    至“水调”、“南词”，真又歌喉一串珠矣。粤女不缠脚，方履、绣红绫波瘦袜，有一种别韵。蛮音多不可辨，嫱之苏白京腔，登答尤工，河下倾动，声价千金。贵介达官，放浪于珠江烟水间者，舟中无阿嫱，如座上少油木梳也。

    Explanation

    284

    Panyu is the capital city and the foremost county of the province, with Shatoujiao inhabited by boat-dwelling people, including servants, courtesans, and entertainers, numbering in the hundreds. The boat-dwelling people are despised in Guangdong, living in boats as their homes, gathering in clusters along the river, and making a living by fishing, much like the musicians of Jin, the idlers of Zhejiang, and the fishermen of Chu. They are often sold as brides. When A Qiang arrives, they all disappear, and Qiang then becomes a courtesan. Qiang is exceptionally intelligent, understanding everything she sees, mastering any skill she takes up to perfection, and occasionally delving into literature. In Guangdong, there is a song called "The Fishing Song" which is the most elegant. Qiang composes it off the cuff, and all the lines are exquisite. For example:
    In February, the southern wind is not to be feared for the cold, as the elder sister walks up to the Hill of Waiting Husband. The clouds stretch and break over the waters of the Ting River, and the lover sells rice down to Wuguan.
    Bamboo fibers are not eaten as fruit, but woven into a thousand-holed silk cloth. The cotton trees have fallen, their flowers like brocade, and a light gauze dress is perfect for the lover to touch.
    As for "Water Melody" and "Southern Lyrics," her singing is like a string of pearls. Women in Guangdong do not bind their feet, wearing square-toed shoes and embroidered red silk stockings, which have a unique charm. The local dialect is often difficult to understand, but Qiang's Suzhou and Beijing accents are especially skilled in responding, stirring the crowd along the river and earning a reputation worth a thousand gold pieces. Noble and influential officials who indulge in the misty waters of the Pearl River find that without A Qiang in the boat, it's like being without an oil comb on the seat.

    285

    余友谢伯庄，宦家子，少有老成之目。饶资，客广南，尝与诸同人游。粤城卑湿，绝少游观，惟海上驾一叶舟，最足娱人心目。谢之游志在流水，而同人之游心在美人。然其趋不同，而又不能不与之合，所以花楼小艇中，有谢之迹，遂又有嫱之遇。嫱又每视诸裙屐多勿睇，而谢因家书促归，心旌摇摇，对此倚门娼，若恐浼，谈笑间直不知有美丽在侧也，惟以平淡遇之。乃嫱之视谢独挚，自入门以及酒阑，嫱之目惟谢、心惟谢，谢左则嫱随之左，谢右则嫱随之右，谢歌则嫱执板以和，谢握管则嫱磨隃糜以进。同人举觞，谢固豪饮；偶为拇战所困，而嫱素涓滴，辄为谢进三爵，意甚得也。同人皆庆谢之诚愿有当于嫱之特赏云。日斜，谢欲归。粤滨海多盗，管钥不至酉，谢呼船傍岸，嫱若有所失，依依惜别。去时犹伫望渡烟，不转盼也。

    285

    My friend Xie Bozhuang, a son of a wealthy and influential family, was young but already seen as mature and responsible. He had ample resources and traveled or resided in the southern regions of Guang. Once, he went on a trip with his fellow scholars and friends. The city of Guang was low-lying and humid, with very few places to visit or enjoy. The only place worth visiting was the sea, where one could sail on a small boat, which was particularly enjoyable.

    Xie's desire to travel was focused on flowing water, while his companions' desire was centered on beautiful women. Despite their different interests, they could not help but join together. Thus, on the small boat by the flower-lined path, there was Xie's presence, and there was also the encounter with Qiang. Qiang, however, looked at the other women with little interest, but Xie, whose heart was wavering and unsettled, treated her with simplicity and lack of excitement, as if he feared being defiled by her beauty. He talked and laughed with her, but did not show much enthusiasm.

    Qiang, on the other hand, was deeply attached to Xie. From the moment they entered the boat until the end of the evening, Qiang's eyes and heart were always on Xie. Whenever Xie moved to the left, Qiang followed him to the left; whenever Xie moved to the right, Qiang followed him to the right. When Xie sang, Qiang would hold a board and sing along; when Xie picked up a writing brush, Qiang would grind the inkstone for him. Whenever the companions raised a cup, Xie drank heavily, but when he was tired from the game of finger-pointing, Qiang, who rarely drank, would offer him three more cups. The companions all celebrated Xie's sincere wish being rewarded by Qiang's special favor.

    As the sun began to set, Xie wanted to return. The coastal areas of Guang were known for piracy and banditry, and the locks were not secured until late afternoon. Xie called for a boat to dock, and Qiang seemed to lose something, parting with reluctance and affection. Even after Xie left, she continued to stand and gaze at the mist over the river, without turning her gaze away.

    286

    翌日，前友至，复约，谢辞以冗。及归装三日前又至，谢不得已，往。甫入舱，而嫱已在舟中久候矣，华饰炫然，曰：“谢公子将促归，小女子无以为赠，敢置杯酒蒸豚，为公子祖饯于舟中。倘公子异日飞腾，重游五岭，节旄到处，而小女子门前冷落，残质风涛，人生若梦，生死莫必，正不知清明麦饭，司马青衫，公子能重续苏白二公佳话否？”言已，呜咽欲绝。谢怃然曰：“邂逅相逢，不意卿爱若斯也。仆本恨人，钟情绝少，今竟于蛮烟瘴雨中得一知己。

    Explanation

    286

    The next day, the previous friend arrived, renewing the invitation, but Xie refused, citing his busy schedule. By the third day before departure, he arrived again, and Xie, unable to refuse, went. As soon as he entered the cabin, Qiang, already waiting in the boat for a long time, was adorned in a dazzling manner and said, "Young Master Xie is about to return soon, and I have nothing to offer as a gift. I dare to prepare a cup of wine and a steamed piglet to bid farewell to you on the boat. If you rise to prominence in the future and revisit the Five Ridges, wherever the staff of authority goes, and if I am left alone, withered by the winds and waves, life is like a dream, and life and death are uncertain. I wonder if you will be able to continue the fine stories of Su and Bai, as they once did?" After speaking, she sobbed uncontrollably. Xie replied thoughtfully, "A chance meeting, I did not expect you to care so deeply. I am a person of many regrets, rarely having deep feelings. Yet in this place of mist and rain, I have found a true friend."

    287

    嘻，可以不恨。”乃入席尽欢。既而酒阑灯炧，嫱挽谢，谢亦心醉嫱，遂枕藉乎舟中矣。诸同人亦各有乐地。衽席间，嫱之曲承缱绻，如不胜衣，丰肌腻骨，发拥乳香，无一不可人怀抱。即足趾雪凝，握之直似一双软璧。至口脂鸡舌，吞吐风情，更出凡想。当其柔词细语，道述生平，并问伯庄踪迹家事。嫱知谢之不能偕嫱也，而谢亦实以不能偕嫱告。嫱偎倚叹息，呼天缘之不假，终夜泪汐，与子潮并长，鸳枕为之尽渍。晨起，谢帆亦挂，嫱送之清远峡口，欷歔而别。谢德之。
    辛丑，余南行，谢嘱余访之，并托寄茧缎等物。余抵粤，次年始在羊城。亦为友人招饮舫中，座间二十余，一妓云系阿嫱，然已半老佳人，昔年风味，犹觉娇仍在目。余因举谢伯庄以问，而嫱若或忘之者。余诖甚，因以其所寄物归而赵焉，且告以悔。

    Explanation

    287

    Ah, there is no need to regret. They then sat down and enjoyed themselves. After the wine had run out and the lamps had gone out, Xiang held Xie, and Xie was also captivated by Xiang, and they lay intertwined in the boat. All the other guests also found their own enjoyment. In the bed, Xiang's gentle and affectionate behavior, as if she could not bear the weight of her own clothes, with plump flesh and smooth bones, hair embracing the fragrance of milk, nothing was unsuitable for human embrace. Her feet were like snow, and holding them felt like two soft jade disks. Her lip balm and chicken tongue, swallowing and exhaling charm, were even more extraordinary. When she spoke softly and gently, recounting her life, and asked about Brother Zhuang's whereabouts and family matters, Xiang knew that Xie could not accompany her, and Xie also truly told her that he could not accompany her. She leaned close and sighed, calling out that fate was not kind, tears flowed all night, like the tide of the child, and the鸳 pillow was soaked through. In the morning, Xie's boat was also hoisted, and Xiang saw him off at the mouth of the Qingyuan Gorge, sighing and parting. Xie thanked her.

    In the year Xinchou, I traveled south, and Xie asked me to visit her, and entrusted me with silk and satin. I arrived in Guangdong, and the following year I was in Yangcheng. I was also invited by a friend to drink on a boat, where there were over twenty people, and one prostitute claimed to be A Xiang, but she was already a middle-aged beauty, yet the charm of her youth was still visible in her eyes. I then mentioned Xie and Brother Zhuang to ask, but Xiang seemed to have forgotten. I was very confused, so I returned the items he had sent and explained, and told him about my regret.

    288

    (余友伯庄之不智也，向非以家事遄归，几为蛮女儿所困。余甫闻谢言，亦感，孰知十年之后而嫱之谋始败。嫱亦狡狯矣哉！谚云“少不入广”，盖其世世相传，设此陷阱，牢笼天下，卒令身死蛮乡，鬼成异域，甘心祸水而不悟者，什千百也。伯庄其幸耳。
    粤省风土最异，如鱼姊蚬妹，舵花舟草详矣。今沙面一带，自靖海门起，群娃聚族，以木庋水，结篷曰“寮”，所居之舟曰“高尾艇”，延客之舟曰“花楼”，亦曰“黑楼”，如“大沙飞”，“满江红”之类。千艘分列，中留甬水之道，以便游观，曰“水心街”。客观妓曰“打水围”，妓接客曰“来瞭”。客至则进槟榔，入口若红绛点唇；继则吸鸦片烟，诸女伴相与叠股而醉昏昏。呼小者曰“阿姑”，及长发分拢者曰“横梳”，有夫曰“阿嫂”，主事者曰“事头婆”，统而言之曰“老举”。此名色又异，故附于此。)

    Explanation

    288

    (As for my friend Berzhuang, his lack of wisdom almost led him to be ensnared by a girl from a remote area, had he not hastily returned home due to family matters. I just heard his words of gratitude and was moved, but who would have thought that ten years later, Qiang's plan would finally fail. Qiang is indeed very cunning! The saying goes, "Do not enter Guangdong when young," for it is a trap that has been passed down through generations, ensnaring the world. It ultimately makes one die in a remote place and become a ghost in a foreign land, with thousands upon thousands willingly becoming trouble and not realizing it. Berzhuang is lucky.

    The customs and scenery of Guangdong province are the most unusual, as seen in the playful terms for girls like "Fish Sister" and "Clam Sister," and the poetic descriptions of boat decorations like "Steering Flower" and "Boat Grass." Nowadays, in the Shama area, starting from the Jinghai Gate, a group of girls gather in clans, building tents called "huts," living on boats called "high-tail boats," and entertaining guests on boats called "flower towers" or "black towers," such as "Great Sand Fly" and "Full River Red." Thousands of boats are arranged in rows, leaving a waterway path in the middle for convenient sightseeing, called "Water Center Street." Visiting a courtesan is called "surround the water," and a courtesan receiving guests is called "come and see." When the guest arrives, betel nut is offered, which looks like red lips when it enters the mouth; then opium is smoked, and all the girls cross their legs together and become drunk and drowsy. The younger ones are called "aunt," those with long hair parted are called "horizontal comb," married ones are called "sister-in-law," the person in charge is called "mistress," and in summary, it is called "old lady." These names and titles are quite unusual, so I have added them here.)

    289

    卷九 仙狐部
    红叶
    瓯宁范一湖，为人诚笃好善，年三十，不获一芹，遂恣情山水。一日，游武夷十六洞，至铁笛亭。见二人对坐，执榼酒相与酣饮。范至，二人让之坐，问范，告以姓氏。范问二人，曰：“彭武、彭夷兄弟也。”劝范饮。二人曰：“佳客邂逅，曷出美馔。”乃启椟，中一蒸儿。范惊掩面，不敢下箸。二人笑视，遂相啖食殆尽，范只饮一盏酒。一人问范何所长，范曰：“愿学医而未逮也。

    Explanation

    289

    Chapter Nine: The Immortal Fox
    Red Leaf
    Fan Yihu from Ou Ning was a man of sincere and kind-hearted nature, who loved doing good deeds. At the age of thirty, he had not achieved any official position, so he indulged in the pleasures of nature. One day, he traveled to the Sixteen Caves of Wuyi and reached the Iron Flute Pavilion. There, he saw two men sitting opposite each other, drinking wine from a jug together. When Fan arrived, the two men offered him a seat and asked about his name. Fan told them his name, and then asked about theirs. They replied, "We are brothers named Peng Wu and Peng Yi." They invited Fan to drink with them. The two men said, "It's a pleasure to meet a good guest like you. Why don't we serve you some delicious food?" They then opened a box, revealing a steamed dish inside. Fan was surprised and covered his face, too embarrassed to take a bite. The two men laughed and proceeded to eat almost all of the dish, while Fan only drank a cup of wine. One of the men asked Fan what his talents were. Fan replied, "I wish to learn medicine, but I have not yet succeeded."

    290

    ”武出一书贻范曰：“君曾读此否？”范视之，皆奇方脉诀、针灸经络。过十余页，武即夺而藏之袖中。范求终读，武曰：“足下得之，已可名世。”忽二人足底云生，冉冉直上，遗落红叶一片，鲜艳可爱，插以金针。范乃悟其为仙，深悔失之觌面，遂怀叶藏针返。途中默诵所见书，一字不遗，归录之秘箧，而红叶经久不枯。于是设市肆，蜂窠鹿角、药臼青囊，居然一小杏林。有患脑后疮者，一年不愈，不容人抚动。其亭前有柳树，范度其尺寸针之，树中出血升余，而人遂瘥。从此范之名噪，而范之究心于医也益力。

    Explanation

    290

    Wu took out a book and gave it to Fan, saying, "Have you ever read this?" Fan looked at it, and it contained strange formulas, pulse rules, and acupuncture meridians. After more than ten pages, Wu took it away and hid it in his sleeve. Fan asked to finish reading it, but Wu said, "If you obtain it, you will already be renowned in the world." Suddenly, clouds formed beneath their feet, rising slowly upward. They left behind a single red leaf, which was fresh and beautiful, inserted with a golden needle. Fan realized that they were immortals and deeply regretted missing the opportunity for face-to-face interaction. He then kept the red leaf and the golden needle hidden.

    On his way back, Fan silently recited the book he had seen, not missing a single word. He recorded it in a secret box, and the red leaf remained fresh for a long time. Fan then set up a shop, using honeycombs, deer antlers, a medicine mortar, and a blue pouch, and became a small apricot garden. There was a person suffering from a cyst behind the ear, which had not healed for a year and could not be touched. There was a willow tree in front of the shop, and Fan measured its dimensions and pierced it with a needle. Blood flowed from the tree in several liters, and the person was cured. From then on, Fan's fame spread, and he devoted himself to medicine even more diligently.

    291

    有邻某不服范术，当盛暑见范来，于当途日炽土上滚，作霍乱之状以试之。范脉其关寸，惊曰：“此冷热相激，肺已裂矣，不可救药。”邻笑其妄，归家果暴卒。建宁太守某公得一症，忽视人物无不倒覆，众医不知何症。范至诊视，问其从人曰：“贵官尝从事于曲糵否？”从者曰：“豪于饮。”范曰：“是矣。”密嘱家人疾舆载之，至十里外，覆其舆。太守仆，自舆盖坠出，后视物遂正。众医问其病之故，范曰：“此酒后气不统血，床头倒呕，心挂胞络，不得下垂耳。”

    Explanation

    291

    A neighbor, who did not accept Fan's medical methods, once saw Fan on a hot summer day and, on the road, acted as if suffering from cholera by rolling on the scorching ground. Fan diagnosed his pulse at the cun and guan points and, shocked, said, "This is a case of cold and heat colliding, the lungs have already ruptured, and there is no remedy." The neighbor laughed at Fan's diagnosis as absurd, but upon returning home, he indeed died suddenly. In Jianning, a certain governor contracted an illness where he could not see anything without it being overturned. Many doctors were puzzled by this condition. When Fan came to examine him, he asked the attendant, "Has the governor ever been known for his drinking?" The attendant replied, "He is excellent at drinking." Fan said, "That is the cause." He secretly instructed the family to quickly transport the governor in a carriage to a place ten miles away, and then to overturn the carriage. The governor fell from the carriage, and upon getting up, he found that everything was normal again. The other doctors asked Fan about the cause of the illness, and Fan explained, "This was due to the qi not controlling the blood after drinking, leading to violent vomiting near the bed, which caused the heart to hang and the pericardium to not descend properly."

    292

    富甲某母病，医者误用参芪，濒死。范至诊脉，并素所服众方遍阅一过，乃书曰“人参一两煅灰”，余苏解数味而已。投之霍然。前医多人曰：“先生诚卢扁，治某太夫人可谓以针投芥，应手而得。但参用煅灰，伊古未有此制也，愿先生教我。”范笑曰：“某太夫人本无甚二竖之忧，诸君子遽加以七年之艾，膈于中而不相下，复益补剂，何异负薪救火？倘余不用是参，则数品草根木叶，不特为诸公所轻，亦为主人所不屑用，故用之以煅。正所以置有用以无用之权而用之，乃得其无用之妙；观者可以从同而见赏，病者即获投症而有喜。不然，《肘后方》恐覆瓿久矣！”众惭服。

    Explanation

    292

    A wealthy man's mother fell ill, and the physician mistakenly prescribed ginseng and astragalus, nearly causing her death. Doctor Fan examined her pulse and thoroughly reviewed all the prescriptions she had taken. He then wrote, "One ounce of ginseng calcined into ash," and added a few other ingredients. The patient suddenly recovered. Many of the previous physicians said, "Sir, you are truly like Lu and Bian, the ancient physicians. Treating the noblewoman can be compared to hitting a small object with a needle—your treatment was precise and effective. However, using ginseng calcined into ash is a method that has never been used before. Please teach us." Doctor Fan smiled and said, "The noblewoman originally did not have a serious illness. You all hastily applied prolonged and excessive treatment, which was not absorbed by her body and led to further complications. Adding more tonics is like adding fuel to a fire. If I had not used this ginseng, the other herbs would have been looked down upon by you gentlemen and also by the patient. Therefore, I used it in this unconventional way. By treating the useful as if it were useless, I achieved the subtle effectiveness of the seemingly useless. Observers can agree with this approach and appreciate it, and the patient will be pleased with the treatment. Otherwise, the Elbow Reserve might be forgotten for a long time!" The physicians were ashamed and admitted their mistake.

    293

    一夜，范听雨危坐，闻窗外有呻吟声甚惨。范问之，一女子应曰：“我鬼也。生前病骨蒸死，今虽为鬼，痛亦如生。闻先生名医，故来求治。但我无形，未知如何而可？”范曰：“可治也。”乃缚一茅草人形，按穴针之，计日而瘳。女来谢曰：“蒙君疗我痼疾，泽及枯骨，愿为先生婢，以报大德。”后时依刀圭前后，名曰“桃胶”，呼之即至；或相随囊履，百里不离。过人闺阁暧昧之处，桃悉知之，而范固无俟望、闻、问、切，已了若指掌，人皆不知也。

    Explanation

    293

    One night, Fan Tingyu sat upright, and heard a very painful groan from outside the window. Fan asked, and a woman replied, "I am a ghost. Before my death, I suffered from a severe illness, and even now as a ghost, I still feel the pain. I have heard of your reputation as a renowned doctor, so I came to seek treatment. But I am intangible, and I do not know how you can treat me." Fan said, "It can be treated." He then tied a straw doll, inserted needles into specific points, and within days, the woman was cured. The woman came to thank him, saying, "I am grateful to you for treating my chronic illness, blessing even the dry bones. I wish to serve you as a maid, to repay your great kindness."

    Afterward, whenever Fan needed assistance, he would call out "Taojiao," and she would come immediately; or she would follow him, never leaving his side for a hundred miles. She knew all the intimate details of Fan's life, and although Fan did not need to observe, listen, ask, or diagnose, he understood everything as if it were in his palm, and no one else knew this.

    294

    或劝其著书垂世，范曰：“医之为言，意也。腠理之微，随气亦巧；针石之介，毫芒即乖。神存心手之间，心可得而解者，口不可得而宣也，言之适足以误人耳，何益之有？”二彭相传为彭祖之子云。
    (苏州叶天士，名医也。夏日与友人偶在梧桐树下对弈，忽一叶落枰间，叶拾起。适有以难产告者，叶即以桐叶与之，令其煎服。后胎果下。众问曰：“桐叶固可催生乎？”叶曰：“非也。”众曰：“先生何以用之？且用之而效若是。”叶曰：“适桐叶落时，正值立秋之候耳。《淮南子》谓一叶落而天下皆秋，独不可通于医乎？”此亦意也。七如)

    Explanation

    294

    Someone advised him to write a book to be passed down to future generations, but Fan replied, "The term 'medicine' implies intention. The subtle nature of the body's tissues requires skill to treat, and the precision of using needles and stones demands accuracy. The spirit resides between the heart and hands; what can be understood in the heart cannot be expressed with words. To speak of it would only mislead people, what benefit is there?" The two Pings are traditionally said to be sons of Peng Zu.

    (Suzhou's Ye Tienshi was a renowned physician. One summer, he was playing chess with a friend under a Chinese parasol tree when a leaf fell onto the chessboard. Ye picked it up. At that moment, someone came to him with a case of difficult childbirth. Ye gave the leaf to the woman, instructing her to boil and drink it. Later, the baby was successfully delivered. The others asked, "Can the Chinese parasol leaf really induce labor?" Ye replied, "No." They asked, "Why did you use it, and why did it work so effectively?" Ye said, "It was just that the Chinese parasol leaf fell when it was the beginning of autumn. The Huainanzi says that when one leaf falls, the whole world knows it is autumn. Why can't this be applied to medicine?" This too is a matter of intention. Seven as such.)

    295

    庄仙人
    武进刘紫村先生，为大学士时，请乩，有仙主于其家。仙能断谋，公事之唯谨，凡国家大计、生民休戚，必谘于仙而后入告，即接物居官，一举一动，亦必请命于仙而后行。构净室以奉之，唯扶乩者某与仙居其中。凡乩之所示，凛于弟子之于先生也。
    一日，扶乩某以事将归，公即请于仙曰：“某今将归，侍侧者谁代其职？”仙云：“公之中表庄培封与我有缘，可代也。其人曾于数月前来都，欲谒公而未果，公可询之同乡官京师者，当知其行止也。”公询之，以不愿干谒，恐蹈奔竞之嫌，复归于吴。公乃致书常州太守招庄。庄北上，谒仙，仙降乩与庄叙旧云：“三百年前与君讲道庐山，临别时我赠君玉环，犹相忆否？”庄茫茫莫对，唯唯而已。刘令居仙室中。如是者瓣香清供，相与共晨夕者，两易寒暑。

    Explanation

    295

    The Immortal of the Family

    Mr. Liu Zicun from Wujin, when he was a university scholar, summoned a spirit tablet (乩) and had an immortal preside over his household. The immortal could make decisions on strategies, and Mr. Liu treated official matters with utmost care. For all major state affairs and the welfare of the people, he would consult the immortal before reporting to the emperor. Even in his daily interactions and official duties, he would seek guidance from the immortal before taking any action. He built a clean and sacred room to honor the immortal, and only the person who performed the divination and the immortal resided there. All the messages conveyed by the spirit tablet were treated with the same reverence as a disciple would show to his master.

    One day, the person who performed the divination was about to leave for personal matters, and Mr. Liu immediately asked the immortal, "I am about to leave. Who will take my place at your side?" The immortal replied, "Your distant relative, Zhuang Peifeng, has a connection with me and can take your place. He came to the capital several months ago, intending to pay you a visit but did not succeed. You can inquire about him from officials from your hometown who are currently serving in the capital, and they will know his whereabouts." Mr. Liu inquired, but Zhuang Peifeng declined, saying he did not wish to seek an audience, fearing it might be seen as pursuing office through improper means. He returned to Wu. Mr. Liu then wrote a letter to the governor of Changzhou to invite Zhuang Peifeng. Zhuang traveled north and paid respects to the immortal. The immortal descended on the spirit tablet and reminisced with Zhuang, saying, "Three hundred years ago, I taught you the Dao on Mount Lu. At the moment of parting, I gave you a jade ring. Do you still remember?" Zhuang, confused and unable to respond, simply nodded in agreement. Mr. Liu then lived in the room with the immortal. For two years, they shared incense offerings and meals, living together day and night.

    296

    庄故江南茂才，会省试欲归。仙示庄曰：“君科第中人也。君相寒俭，余将为君表而出之。”是夜，庄忽病狂，一室若哗，向隅而奔。家人告公以状，公以禁闼，戒勿扬，键户而俟之。家人隙而窥，见庄上跽，以指圈面曰：“脸要大。”庄面遂如满月。以手画眉曰：“眉要高。”庄眉遂如起蚕。由是耳、目、口、鼻莫不得手而应心矣。夜半乃倦，方自就寝。明日视之，则方面大耳，广颡丰颐，非复当日负郭庄生也。刘公披衣来认，亦几觌面相失。庄因揽镜自照，亦哑然笑其形容之顿改耳。

    Explanation

    296

    Zhuang, formerly a talented person from southern Jiangnan, was about to return home after the provincial examination. A supernatural being appeared to Zhuang and said, "You are destined for success in the imperial examinations. Your appearance is simple and humble, and I will reveal your true potential." That night, Zhuang suddenly became mad, and the room seemed like a commotion, with him running to a corner in confusion. The family informed the public about the situation, and the public, fearing it would be spread, warned not to disclose it, and locked the door while waiting. The family peeped through a crack and saw Zhuang kneeling upright, with his finger circling his face, saying, "Your face should be larger." Zhuang's face then became like a full moon. With his hand drawing his eyebrows, he said, "Your eyebrows should be higher." Zhuang's eyebrows then became like raised silkworms. Thus, his ears, eyes, mouth, and nose all responded to his hands as if guided by his heart. By midnight, he grew tired and finally went to bed. The next day, when he looked at himself, he then had a broad face, large ears, a wide forehead, and a full jaw, no longer the Zhuang from the outskirts of the city. Liu Gong, dressed in his clothes, came to recognize him, almost recognizing him face-to-face. Zhuang then took a mirror to look at himself and laughed silently at the sudden change in his appearance.

    297

    越日，庄戒行，仙又示云：“君归困乏，何以为谋？刘公清廉，难为君壮行色。予授君一符咒，焚而饮，可代刀圭，以济人，因而自济。君其宝之，勿贪勿吝。”庄拜受，辞仙行。仙居刘第，无可与者，而仙亦辞刘而归蓬壶矣。庄归途迟迟行，试其术于天津、山左、维扬间，效如响，名振，而仙人之号自此始。庄不索谢，贻之亦不却，抵舍而客囊颇裕。是科获中后，以符术治人，辄不验。

    Explanation

    297

    The next day, Zhuang prepared to depart, and the deity appeared again, saying, "If you return in a state of poverty and distress, what will you do? Liu Gong is so upright that he cannot provide a grand send-off. I will give you a talisman. Burn it and drink it, and it can serve as a substitute for medicine to heal others and yourself. Treasure it, and do not be greedy or stingy." Zhuang bowed and accepted the talisman, then left. The deity stayed at Liu's house but found no one to stay with, so the deity left Liu and returned to Penglai.

    Zhuang traveled slowly and tested his magic in Tianjin, Shandong, and Yangzhou. It worked effectively, gaining him fame, and the title of "immortal" began here. Zhuang did not ask for payment, and those who gave him gifts did not refuse, making his journey quite prosperous. After passing an examination, using the talisman to treat people became ineffective.

    298

    会试屡踬南宫，截取江西瑞金令。庄性慈和，政务德化，邑大治。当事以其迕执憎之。会抚军过境，家人索勒不遂，诟庄。庄不受，杖之，怀绶见抚军曰：“职虽卑，是朝廷官，非大人厮养所可辱者，将何以蒞民？”请解任。抚慰庄，还其印绶，逐其家人。旋以他事中伤之，遂罢官。寓南昌陈善人家，归资莫措，日给不周。
    陈豫章之长者，富而好施，为庄力办捐复，后仍发江西补某县，调临川。庄居官早起，案无留牍，政暇则诗酒弈棋而已。终以圭稜不合时宜，又六年告归。年七十，目炯炯，声如铜钟，益健步履。亲故访之，即留与手谈，终日不倦。卒时，预知撤瑟之期。庄名橚，字培封，人呼为仙人云。

    Explanation

    298

    Zhuang repeatedly failed in the metropolitan examination and was eventually appointed as the magistrate of Ruìjīn County in Jiangxi. Zhuang had a kind and gentle disposition, governing with moral influence, and the county was well-governed. The officials resented him for opposing them. When the governor-general passed through the area, his family members demanded something from Zhuang and failed, insulting him. Zhuang refused and beat them. The governor-general's retainer went to see the governor-general and said, "Although my position is low, I am an official of the imperial court and cannot be disrespected by the governor-general's servants. How can I govern the people?" He requested to resign. The governor-general consoled Zhuang, returned his seal and official insignia, and expelled his family members. Shortly after, he was slandered with other matters and dismissed from office. He stayed at the home of a benevolent person named Chen in Nanchang, unable to arrange funds for returning home, with daily expenses insufficient.

    A respected elder in Nanchang, Chen, was wealthy and generous. He did his best to arrange for Zhuang's reinstatement. Later, Zhuang was still sent to Jiangxi to fill a vacancy in a certain county and then transferred to Lichuan. Zhuang got up early when in office and had no unfinished documents on his desk. When he had free time from official duties, he would only engage in poetry, wine, chess, and games. Eventually, because of his sharpness or integrity, he was out of step with the times. Another six years later, he resigned and returned home. At seventy years old, his eyes were bright, his voice was like a bell, and his steps were even more vigorous. Relatives and friends visited him, and he immediately stayed to play chess with them, all day without tiring. At the time of his death, he foreknew the time for the removal of the zither. Zhuang's name was Shu, his courtesy name was Peifeng, and people called him the Immortal.

    299

    (作宦不得志于大官，强于得罪子民。千古一辙，良可寄慨！七如是作，岂自道耶？袁硕夫)

    石帆
    登州卞京家贫，三十失伉俪，奇士也。尝浪游南北，糗粱断绝，因借舟过浙江，渡海盐。忽遭飓风，舟覆桅折，卞即遵海而下。既乃飘至一岛，扳岸直登。翠峰百仞，高插云表，下皆平石，周围作坡陀，而潮水震荡，如坐艨艟。衣湿如洗，风飕飗至。
    无何，月出海上，照耀波光，似火飞金涌，身不自主。方骇异间，又觉目前渺渺，亘天又起一峰，冲风破浪而来，与己坐之峰，若相低昂鞺鞳于其间。卞蹲伏一角，莫敢仰视。近则双峰并峙，屹然而立。忽见前峰下有红灯数十对，度石而来，月色灯光，杳不可辨，至后峰脚下而没。顷刻灯复出，较倍于前，又度前峰，渐隐。乃见前峰如挂帆饱风以去。一天星月，澄然无际。回顾后峰间，尚余一灯如杏，明灭来前。

    Explanation

    299

    In serving as an official, it is better to be unsuccessful in a minor position than to cause harm to the people. Throughout history, this pattern has remained the same, and it is fitting to express one's feelings about it! Is this how he acted? Is this what he did? (袁硕夫)

    Stone Sail
    Bi Jing from Dengzhou was poor and lost his wife at thirty, but he was a remarkable man. He often traveled north and south, and once his food supplies ran out, he borrowed a boat to cross the Zhejiang River and the Haiyan River. Suddenly, he encountered a hurricane, the boat capsized, and the mast broke, and Bi followed the coast south. He then drifted to an island and climbed ashore. The island had a green peak hundreds of feet high, rising into the clouds, with flat stones below, sloping all around, and the tide surging, like sitting in a warship. His clothes were soaked like they had been washed, and the wind blew coldly. Soon, the moon rose over the sea, illuminating the waves, like fire and gold leaping, and he felt helpless. As he was startled, he suddenly felt that the horizon seemed endless, and another peak rose up, breaking through the wind and waves, and it seemed to rise and fall in harmony with the peak he was sitting on. Bi crouched in a corner, too afraid to look up. As he got closer, two peaks stood side by side, firmly upright. He suddenly saw dozens of red lanterns in pairs at the base of the front peak, coming across the stones, and the moonlight and lantern light were indistinct, disappearing at the base of the rear peak. In a moment, the lanterns reappeared, more numerous than before, crossing the front peak again, and gradually disappearing. He then saw the front peak as if it were a sail filled with wind, drifting away. The sky was filled with stars and the moon, clear and boundless. When he looked back at the rear peak, there was still one lantern like a plum, flickering and coming forward.

    300

    将近身所，俨一美女，披云氅，持灯上下照卞曰：“客从何来？”卞告以风坏舟故。女曰：“空山无人，罡风可畏。曷随吾灯往？”卞随之至峰下，有门洞开。入内则朱檐碧瓦，万户千门，类王者居。至东北隅，复入重门一小苑中。女令卞入室，绣帏锦幔，几席半彩缋，器用多漆画。女乃出新衣衣之，衣皆绣缘。曰：“郎君何方人？”卞曰：“登州人。”女曰：“奴与君有夙缘也。”遂与卞解带入帏。女固无异常人，独其足下则绣袜绘舄。枕间谓卞曰：“奴顾英也，石帆夫人之侍女也。今夜陀矶夫人来请押事，故府中无人。辰起当置郎君衣壁中，毋怖也。”卞应之。而自以为一生奇遇，不为之苦。如是日伏夜出，女尝馈食壁中，皆珍味，多不识。至夜深，携卞出，或罗酒浆，或评局谈棋，备极欢笑。

    Explanation

    300

    As Bian approached the place, a beautiful woman appeared, wearing a cloud-like cloak and holding a lamp, illuminating up and down as she asked, "Where have you come from?" Bian explained that the wind had damaged his boat. The woman said, "The empty mountain has no one, and the fierce wind is to be feared. Why not follow my lamp?" Bian followed her to the foot of the peak, where a door was open. Inside, there were red eaves and green tiles, ten thousand households and a thousand doors, similar to the residence of a king. They arrived at the northeast corner and then entered through a heavy gate into a small garden. The woman told Bian to enter the room, where the curtains and drapes were embroidered and brocade, the tables and seats half covered with colorful paintings, and the utensils mostly painted with lacquer. The woman then took out new clothes for Bian to wear, all of which were embroidered. She asked, "Where do you come from, sir?" Bian replied, "I am from Dengzhou." The woman said, "I have a predestined connection with you." She then undressed with Bian and entered the curtains. The woman was indeed no different from a normal person, except that her feet were in embroidered socks and painted shoes. Between the pillows, she said to Bian, "I am Gu Ying, the servant of Lady Shi Fan. Tonight, Lady Tuo Ji has come to request matters, so the mansion is empty. In the morning, I will place your clothes in the wall; do not be afraid." Bian agreed. He felt that this was a remarkable encounter and did not find it difficult. Thus, they would hide during the day and go out at night. The woman often provided food in the wall, all of which were delicacies, many of which he did not recognize. By nightfall, she would take Bian out, sometimes preparing wine and refreshments, sometimes discussing games and chess, and they were thoroughly happy and joyful.

    301

    一日，正卧壁中，忽又有女子入，年次于英，而丰腻肌肤，若有馀脂，其下乃翘然似凤也。生抱其颊曰：“卿为谁耶？”女曰：“我盼华也，英妹我。”二人偎亵备至。顾英忽掀帏进食，瞥见女，怒曰：“室无人，汝行窃耶？”女笑曰：“姊幔藏也，诲我以盗，于我何尤？”英转哂曰：“妹无惫赖，恐属垣有耳，请以卜夜。”女乃出。英反关户，相携去。卞揭衣壁亦出，见室中陈设与夜间无异，独枕边一黄册。窃视，皆诸神号署押其上，并书明季甲申之变云云。卞骇，不敢动，归壁中。

    Explanation

    301

    One day, while he was lying hidden in the wall, suddenly another woman entered. She was younger than Ying, with plump and smooth skin, as if with extra fat, and her lower part was raised like a phoenix. Sheng embraced her cheek and said, "Who are you?" The woman replied, "I am Pan Hua, Ying's younger sister." The two of them were intimate to the fullest extent. Then Ying suddenly lifted the curtain to eat and saw the woman, angrily saying, "There is no one in the room, are you stealing?" The woman smiled and said, "Sister is hiding behind the curtain, you accuse me of theft, what do I have to do with it?" Ying turned and smiled and said, "Sister, don't be so lazy, there might be someone listening at the wall, let's wait until night." The woman then left, and Ying locked the door and they left together. Bian also emerged from the wall and saw that the room was arranged the same as at night, only a yellow book was on the pillow. He secretly looked, and saw the names of various gods written on it, along with notes about the events of the late Ming dynasty's Jia Shen year. Bian was shocked and dared not move, returning to the wall.

    302

    至夜二女偕来，觥筹交错，如双隗，如二乔。英长而修雅，华次而肥黠，二美悉具。卞亦乐此不疲。衽席之间，或英先而华后，或英左而华右，几令人应接不暇也。侵晓俱去，皆整农如承值状。一夜，华独至。卞问英，华曰：“奉使押黄册去矣。”卞乃把华臂，如秋日藕；玩其足尖，如解结锥。因问英何以不弓，华曰：“伊西北产，其俗然也。”遂相与狎。华露玉体，覆以锦茵。乃脱其贴肤淡黄夹衣衣卞，如云縠而轻暖异常。

    Explanation

    302

    At night, the two women came together, toasting and drinking merrily, like the twin beauties of the Han dynasty and the two famous sisters from the Three Kingdoms period. Ying was tall and elegant, while Hua was plump and clever, both possessing all the qualities of beauty. Bian also found joy in this without tiring. In their intimate moments, sometimes Ying would take the lead and Hua would follow, or Ying would be on the left and Hua on the right, almost leaving Bian unable to keep up. Just before dawn, they all left, dressed as farmers, as if they were working. One night, Hua came alone. Bian asked Ying, and Hua replied, "I was sent on a mission to deliver the yellow register." Bian then took Hua's arm, which felt smooth and cool like a lotus root in autumn; he played with her toes, which were sharp like a needle. He asked Hua why she did not wear a bow, and Hua said, "She is from the northwest, and that is their custom." They then engaged in intimate play. Hua revealed her beautiful body, covered with a brocade mat. She then removed her light yellow inner garment and gave it to Bian, which was like a cloud-like gauze, light and exceptionally warm.

    303

    卞忽闻窗外落叶声繁，瑟瑟阶除，三秋动念，千里他乡，固乐未已，而悲又起矣。因告华以意，华曰：“久客思归，人情也，我不忍以爱昵淹君也。尘世兴废变迁，莫保功名富贵，君其淡漠置之。君归后倘遇危急，即拆夹里衣，便可为计，请以赠君。容俟姊来，当共图之。此地郎君亦无分久居也。”卞问：“此间仙乎？人乎？”华曰：“人即可以为仙，仙亦非天外之人，仙亦人而已矣。但郎君福籍所注，非终老于此。百二十年后，尚有好运。”

    Explanation

    303

    Zheng suddenly heard the sound of many falling leaves outside the window, rustling on the steps, and the passage of three autumns stirred his thoughts. A thousand miles away from home, his joy had not yet ended, but sorrow arose again. He then told Hua of his feelings, and Hua said, "It is natural for a long-term guest to long for home. I cannot bear to keep you here with my affection. The rise and fall of the world, nothing can guarantee fame and wealth. You should treat it with indifference. If you encounter danger upon returning home, simply tear the lining of your garment, and you will have a plan. Please accept this as a gift. I will wait for my sister to come, and we will discuss it together. This place is not meant for you to stay long." Zheng asked, "Are the people here gods or humans?" Hua replied, "A person can become a god, and gods are not beings from beyond the heavens; they are also humans. However, your destiny is not to end your days here. In a hundred and twenty years, you will still have good fortune."

    304

    逾夕英至，华告以故。英不忍舍，华告以数不可违，英乃许。而离情顿起，别绪萦怀。卞复含英咀华，左顾右盼，为之泣数行下。英与卞一书云：“君究心于此，较胜恒产。”英、华袭之出甬道，卞不敢视。至洞外，见前峰又泊岸间。英手掖卞登彼岸，华呜呜送之。
    倏忽之间，峰起而北。卞回望旧山，于波涛间一点如豆，霎时而失。但觉山当人面，波撼崖头，震荡水天，飘然竟止。身颇不寒，仍著旧衣，内有黄甲并书在焉。晓见渔舟呼载之，皆乡人也。问其地，则登之陀矶岛也。已去家三载矣。闲时翻阅所赠书，即常行星算书。为人推吉凶，多奇应。
    明末贼蜂起，将薄城。卞闻警，即拆夹里衣，无所见，而绵絮如云布，出十里之外。贼迷所在以去。后人德其守城之功，祀以乡贤。计其年，百有二十，而盼华“好运”之说，信不诬也。

    Explanation

    304

    After the night, Ying arrived, and Hua informed her of the reason. Ying could not bear to part, but Hua told her that the numbers could not be defied, so Ying agreed. Suddenly, feelings of separation arose, and thoughts of parting lingered in her heart. Bian again held Ying and savored her, looking left and right, and she wept tears in several rows. Ying wrote a letter to Bian, saying, "You have dedicated yourself to this, more valuable than constant property." Ying and Hua escorted her out of the corridor, and Bian dared not look. To the outside of the cave, she saw the front peak again anchored in the bay. Ying gently helped Bian onto the other shore, and Hua sobbed as she saw her off.

    In an instant, the peak rose to the north. Bian looked back at the old mountain, a speck like a bean in the waves, which instantly disappeared. She only felt the mountain facing her, waves crashing against the cliff, shaking the water and sky, and suddenly stopped. She was not particularly cold, still wearing her old clothes, with yellow armor and a book inside. In the morning, she saw fishing boats calling for her to board, and they were all villagers. She asked about the place, and it was the island of Dengji. She had been away from home for three years. In her spare time, she read the book she had been given, which was a book on celestial calculations. She used it to predict good or bad fortune, and it often had strange and accurate predictions.

    At the end of the Ming dynasty, bandits rose up like a swarm of bees, about to attack the city. Bian heard the alarm and immediately tore open the lining of her clothes, seeing nothing, but the cotton floss spread like clouds, going out ten miles. The bandits were confused and left. Later generations praised her for her role in defending the city, honoring her as a local virtuous person. Counting her years, she was one hundred and twenty years old, and the saying that she hoped for good fortune was indeed true.

    305

    小青
    王生行本，字雨人，武城人，父官于南。长，丰神俊逸，眉目如画，时人比之璧人。有相者谓生眼睫有芒角，后当配一仙女。生风度端凝，言笑不苟，官家争欲婚之，生皆力拒。又以其父宦迹萍踪，多所未遇。
    生尝于市肆见骨董铺中有画美人卷，装潢蚀剥而容貌端好、神情妙丽，似人小照，无款识。以金购之，更为重装，曰：“人但知礼大士像，犹不知慈悲心亦变作春梦婆，度一切冷落众生也。”日夕焚香瞻拜，对画如对人。虽传纸上人，而意中缘常涉幻想。奈何近在咫尺，邈若山河，令人形影徒吊，空想见而不相识耶。尝有二绝云：
    春日无端去住闲，湘裙碧水鬓青山。何时一枕荒唐梦，总在云云雨雨间。
    虹驾不愁天汉阔，星槎那怕鹊桥空。应知人亦能仙去，会向蓬莱第几宫。

    Explanation

    305

    小青

    Wang Xingben, courtesy name Yu Ren, was from Wucheng. His father served in the southern region. As Wang grew up, he possessed a graceful and elegant demeanor, with eyebrows and eyes as beautiful as a painting. People of his time compared him to a jade person. A fortune-teller once said that Wang's eyelashes had a sharp edge, and he would later be matched with a celestial maiden. Wang had a dignified and composed demeanor, speaking and laughing with seriousness and propriety. Officials competed to marry him, but Wang resisted all their proposals. Moreover, because his father's official career was like a wandering cloud, he had not yet found his place.

    One day, Wang saw a scroll of a beautiful woman's portrait in an antique shop in the marketplace. The frame was worn and damaged, but the woman's appearance was still beautiful and her expression was charming, as if it were a miniature portrait. There was no signature or mark on the painting. Wang bought it with gold and had it remounted. He said, "People only know the statue of the revered figure, but they do not know that a compassionate heart can also transform into a dream-like state, guiding all lonely and neglected people." He burned incense and paid respects to the painting every day, treating it as if it were a real person. Although the woman was depicted on paper, his heart's connection often involved a lot of imagination. How could she be so close yet so distant, like mountains and rivers, leaving him alone with his shadow, imagining seeing her but not recognizing her? He once wrote two poems:

    Spring days come and go idly, her green skirt and blue hair like mountains and rivers. When will I have a dream of folly, always in the clouds and rain?
    A rainbow bridge does not fear the vastness of the Milky Way, a celestial boat does not fear the emptiness of the magpie bridge. One should know that people can also ascend to immortality, meeting in the palace of the mythical island of the immortals.

    306

    又题画一词，调寄《声声慢》云：
    还羞又怯，似爱偏惊，真个娇娇滴滴。带笑含颦模样，谁人描出。轻轻淡淡几笔，好比如、春花三月。想一会，画中人、恰似梦中相识。/丰韵天然各别。恼着他、为何恁般老实。对这一人儿、只是向伊凭说。朦胧一钩儿月、挂窗前，不清不白。看屋内、灯儿又明又灭。
    一夕挑灯夜读，忽举首，见女子从画中下。生惊起致问，女曰：“感君缱绻，不能自已，故不避孽海，又落尘缘。想君丰韵，岂少佳偶，何必终日坐清净蒲团，伴飘泊影，郁郁久居此耶？”生喜促坐，女殊不羞涩，拥之也不甚拒，遂与为欢，备极燕婉。每至夜静阖户便来，雅谈诗文。翻案头诗稿，至生好句，辄低声吟哦，意态蕴藉。西窗剪烛之馀，亦复谁能遣此？宜乎有甚于画眉者矣。见壁上悬琴，曰：“郎君知音乎？”生曰：“愿学焉。”女乃下，而以纤指轻揉，其音袅袅。生曰：“请终其曲。”女曰：“但得其趣，固不必托于音也。”

    Explanation

    306

    Another poem titled "Echoing Slowness" was inscribed on the painting:
    Shy and timid, yet seeming to love and suddenly startled, truly charming and delicate. The face, with a smile and a frown, who could have depicted such a complex expression? With light, subtle strokes, it is like the delicate beauty of spring flowers in March. Thinking for a moment, the person in the painting seems like someone I've met in a dream.
    Each has their own natural grace. What is it about him that makes him so honest and straightforward? With this person, I just talk to him freely. A faint, crescent moon hangs before the window, creating an ambiguous, indistinct atmosphere. Looking inside, the lamp is flickering on and off.
    One night, while reading by the light of a lamp, I suddenly looked up and saw a woman stepping out of the painting. The man was startled and asked her. She said, "Moved by your affection, I cannot help myself, so I have not avoided the sea of suffering and have again fallen into worldly entanglements. Thinking of your grace, you must have many good matches, why should you sit all day on a quiet meditation cushion, accompanied by the shadow of a wandering person, feeling depressed and staying here for a long time?" The man was delighted and sat close to her, the woman was not at all shy, and did not resist much when he embraced her, so they became intimate, experiencing the utmost tenderness. Every night when it was quiet and the door was closed, she would come, and they would have elegant discussions about poetry and literature. She would look through the man's poetry drafts, and whenever she found a good line, she would whisper it, her demeanor being graceful and restrained. After trimming the candle in the west window, who else could spend the time like this? It is fitting that there is something even more intimate than painting eyebrows. Seeing a qin (a traditional Chinese instrument) hanging on the wall, she said, "Do you understand music, sir?" The man replied, "I wish to learn." The woman then played, using her slender fingers to lightly stroke the strings, producing a lingering sound. The man said, "Please finish the piece." The woman replied, "As long as one gets the essence, it is not necessary to rely on the sound."

    307

    一日正欢笑间，忽见狸奴来扑女裙，作呜呜响。女惕然投生怀曰：“郎为我驱之。”生以拂尘击之去。女曰：“狮吼之威犹在耶？”生问其故。女曰：“妾生前遭悍妇，心胆惧碎。今见狸奴，犹令我毛骨都悚。”生详诘之，女曰：“妾小青也，郎即冯郎。当时见逐孤山，此照曾经三易。其二为悍妇所焚，此则郎君所匿，流在人间者。妾死后，冥司令我再生，以了夙缘。妾固乐死，不愿忧生，遂悠忽随风，不受拘束。因见杨夫人告我，乃知郎君恋恋也。有时谈前生事，念及慈亲，不能成咽。”生曰：“杨夫人从何处来？”女曰：“蕊珠宫侍值班也。”生曰：“卿生时诗文，十绝一书焚馀之外，犹能记忆否？”女曰：“杳如梦寐，强半遗忘。但零膏剩粉，触处酸辛耳！”尚记三绝云：

    Explanation

    307

    One day, while they were laughing and enjoying themselves, a cat suddenly jumped onto the girl's skirt and made a "呜呜" sound. Startled, the girl threw herself into her husband's arms and said, "Please drive it away for me." The husband used his whisk broom to chase the cat away. The girl said, "Is the power of your lion's roar still intact?" The husband asked her why she was so afraid. The girl said, "I suffered at the hands of a domineering wife in my previous life, and my heart and courage were shattered. Even now, seeing this cat makes me feel my hair stand on end." The husband inquired further, and the girl explained, "I am Xiaoxing, and you are Fenglang. When I was driven away from Gushan, this photo has gone through three changes. Two of them were burned by the domineering wife, and this one was hidden by you, circulating in the human world. After my death, the underworld ordered me to be reborn to fulfill our predestined fate. I was content to die, but I did not wish to suffer in life, so I drifted away with the wind, unconstrained. It was only when I met Lady Yang that I realized you still hold onto me. Sometimes, when we talk about our past lives, I think of my mother and cannot speak." The husband asked, "Where did Lady Yang come from?" The girl replied, "She is on duty as a servant in the Rui Zhu Palace." The husband said, "Can you still remember the poems and essays you wrote in your previous life, aside from the ten poems and one book that were burned?" The girl said, "They are as vague as a dream, and most of them have been forgotten. But whenever I see remnants of them, they bring me a bitter taste!" She still remembered three of them:

    Note: The translation provided is based on the given source text and aims to convey the original meaning and emotional tone while ensuring clarity and naturalness in English. The names "小青" (Xiaoxing) and "冯郎" (Fenglang) are kept as they are, assuming they are proper nouns referring to specific individuals. If these names have specific cultural or historical significance, further research might be needed to provide more context or alternative translations.

    308

    病里沉沉怯又娇，合欢花发独眠宵。起看一径忘忧草，移向孤山亦恨苗。
    酿得前溪一片云，闭门春雨乱纷纷。愁眉更掬西泠水，却画扬州月二分。
    晚妆无力杏花残，瓣瓣沾泥糁作团。一把柳丝扶不起，轻盈搭在玉栏干。
    生为之笔记焉。人之见之，皆疑鬼而疑狐，生力白其无。后其父诘之，生以实告。父启户摘画，投于火，登时而尽。生肝肠寸断，较伊生前之炬，更为惨切。至晚入帏，而画里小青固在枕簟间也。生喟然曰：“天衣有缝因风剪。”女即对曰：“花影无根向月栽。”生因反涕为笑。女曰：“适为大人所逐，竟而庐舍荡然，无所依栖。告大人另以闲所置我，我非祸君者。”生告父。父不得已，除西舍为之成礼。夫人来，女出见，则婉而多风，艳绝人世。夫人曰：“真佳妇也，无怪我儿魂依而梦绕焉。”

    Explanation

    308

    In the depths of illness, I am weak and delicate, the hehuan flower blooms while I sleep alone. Rising to look at a path of wangyoucao, I move it to the solitary mountain, yet it remains a source of sorrow.
    I brew a cloud over the stream before the closed door, as spring rain falls chaotically. My furrowed brows hold the water of Xiling, yet I divide the moon of Yangzhou into two.
    My evening makeup is fading, the apricot blossoms are withering, their petals falling into the mud and forming clumps. I cannot support the willow branch, and it rests lightly on the jade railing.
    The man wrote this down in his notes. Those who saw it all suspected it was a ghost or a fox, but the man insisted it was not. Later, his father questioned him, and he told the truth. His father opened the door, tore the painting, and threw it into the fire, and it was consumed instantly. The man was heartbroken, more so than when the painting was burned before his eyes. That evening, as he entered the bedchamber, the girl Xiaoqing from the painting was indeed there on the bed. He sighed and said, “Even the finest garment has a seam, cut by the wind.” The girl replied, “The flower shadow has no roots, planted toward the moon.” The man then turned his tears into laughter. She said, “Just now, I was chased away by the master, and my humble abode was destroyed, leaving me with no place to stay. Please tell the master to find a quiet place for me; I do not intend to harm you.” The man relayed this to his father. His father, having no choice, arranged for the girl to move into a western room and married them. When the wife arrived, the girl came out to greet her, and she was graceful and charming, surpassing all women in beauty. The wife said, “She is truly a fine wife, no wonder my son was so devoted to her in life.”

    309

    女善事翁姑，常不食，虽严冬皆着纱縠，未尝寒栗。或制裘服，力不胜披。逾年，觉颦眉交促，暂数腰围，乃告夫人曰：“儿有怀矣。”遂食烟火。一日，生入闻儿啼，视之，床上绷两儿，生大喜。后两子名仙照、仙图，貌皆类母，往往不辨伯仲。以五彩线一系其臂，一系其足云。女生平不作一笔墨事，但勤针黹。生以为嗜好之异，何前后判若两人耶？女曰：“诗以穷而后工，故劳人思妇之作，大抵皆不得志之所为。其感喟不平，根于心者，悉露于言。而坎坷丛集，富于文者益穷其遇。况内仪志美，中馈称贤，更非丈夫可比。何必咏柳絮于风前，颂椒花于元日。至隔墙待月之词，花里闭门之句，又乌足挂人齿颊也哉！即不然，如妾生前，亦当为女流握管，永垂龟鉴耳。”

    Explanation

    309

    The woman was good at serving her in-laws, often not eating, even in severe winter she wore thin gauze, never feeling cold. Sometimes she made fur clothes, but her strength could not bear to wear them. After a year, she felt her eyebrows frown and her waist tighten, and then she started to eat regular food. One day, the husband heard the baby crying and looked, seeing two children swaddled on the bed, he was greatly pleased. Later, the two sons were named Xianzhao and Xiantu, both resembling their mother, often making it hard to distinguish between them. They were tied with a five-colored string on their arms and feet. The woman never wrote a single word in her life, but was diligent in needlework. The husband thought her tastes were so different, how could she be so different before and after? The woman said, "Poetry becomes refined through hardship, so the works of laborers and thinking women are mostly the result of unfulfilled aspirations. Their feelings of resentment and dissatisfaction, rooted in their hearts, are all expressed in their words. And those who encounter many hardships and are rich in writing become even more impoverished in their circumstances. Especially when it comes to inner beauty and being a good wife, she is far beyond what a man can achieve. Why write about willow catkins in the wind or praise pepper flowers on New Year's Day? As for poems about waiting for the moon through a wall or closing the door in the midst of flowers, these are not worth mentioning. Even if not, if I were alive, I would still write for women, leaving a lasting example for future generations."

    310

    后生父以致仕归老，生夫妻厮守，终身田园之乐。忽女一日谓生曰：“妾当先去，为郎君除新舍。”倏忽不见，生亦寻卒。后二子贵显，以为事涉不经，故讳言之。
    (可以作如是观。
    或谓是祝允明手笔，他手不能作。七如)

    Explanation

    310

    The young man's father retired from office and returned to a private life. The couple stayed together, enjoying the simple pleasures of rural life for the rest of their days. Suddenly, the wife told the husband one day, "I will go first, to prepare a new dwelling for you." She vanished in an instant, and soon after, the husband also passed away. Their two sons later became prominent, but they avoided speaking about these unusual events, likely due to social or cultural taboos.

    Final Translation

    The young man's father retired from office and returned to a private life. The couple stayed together, enjoying the simple pleasures of rural life for the rest of their days. Suddenly, the wife told the husband one day, "I will go first, to prepare a new dwelling for you." She vanished in an instant, and soon after, the husband also passed away. Their two sons later became prominent, but they avoided speaking about these unusual events, likely due to social or cultural taboos.

    311

    刘祭酒
    平阴朱太史言，有祭酒刘公娶狐一事：刘公家世以曲糵为业。年十二，失怙恃，主肆无人，倩其中表某司酒政。一日，告刘曰：“每晨观作房，辄空一瓮，迨无虚夕。将逾月，不解其故。”刘不之信，恐酒工所窃，乃封识去。早起验视，果如所言。群以为此狐仙也，不可以制。刘不服，夜外宿作房逻守之。众皆寝，刘不寐，微闻唼咂声。刘潜近听之，声在坛中。乃脱衣覆瓿口，呼曰：“捉之矣！”众来听，坛内寂然，皆以为遁。刘曰：“此黠鼠之故智也，毋堕其术。”乃抱瓮归，阖户火而俟。漏四下，瓮中忽曰：“胡为乎此中？”刘曰：“谁请君入耶？”又曰：“曷放吾归休？”刘曰：“谈何容易！若放尔，数十瓮酒价，向何处索取？”曰：“此债寻常，出当倍偿。”刘曰：“吾今不欲你偿。如欲出，当奉吾约，否则立炬尔将为醢。”曰：“请言所约。”刘曰：“吾欲尔卜夜与吾嬉。”曰：“可。”乃要以誓。

    Explanation

    311

    The Master Liu
    Zhu Taishi of Pingyin recounted an incident involving Master Liu, who married a fox spirit: Liu's family had long been engaged in the brewing business. At the age of twelve, he lost his parents, and the shop was without a manager. He entrusted his cousin to oversee the wine shop. One day, the cousin told Liu, "Every morning when I check the workshop, one jar is always empty, and this has continued every night without fail. For over a month, I don't understand why." Liu did not believe him, suspecting that the workers might be stealing the wine, so he sealed and marked the jars. The next morning, he checked, and indeed, it was as his cousin had said. Everyone thought it must be a fox spirit, which could not be controlled. Liu was不服 (unconvinced), and he stayed outside the workshop at night to guard it. Everyone else went to sleep, but Liu did not, and he faintly heard the sound of sucking and licking. Liu crept closer to listen, and the sound came from the altar. He then took off his clothes and covered the mouth of the jar, shouting, "I've caught you!" The others came to listen, but the altar was silent, and they all thought the fox spirit had escaped. Liu said, "This is just the old trick of a cunning rat; don't fall for it." He then carried the jar back to his room, closed the door, lit a fire, and waited. At the fourth watch of the night, the jar suddenly said, "Why am I in here?" Liu replied, "Who invited you in?" It said again, "Why don't you let me go back to rest?" Liu said, "What's so easy about that! If you let you out, where will I get the price of dozens of wine jars from?" It said, "This debt is ordinary; if you let me out, I will double repay it." Liu said, "I don't want you to repay me now. If you want to come out, you must follow my agreement; otherwise, I will light a fire and turn you into meat." It said, "Please tell me what the agreement is." Liu said, "I want you to predict the night and play with me." It said, "Okay." They then made an oath.

    312

    刘揭瓮，出不知所在。次夜洗盏以待，果至。一少年约十四五岁，头挽双髻，身着花绣锦团短袭，云镶犊鼻裤，小朱履，项系金络索圈，手挽宝钏，朗朗然姣好无比，与刘相亚。少年曰：“来赴嬉约。”刘喜，问其姓氏，曰：“于姓。”刘遂呼为兄，与之共嬉。有时或说新奇小传，令人听之娓娓不倦；或作百戏，皆有妙想，障人眼目；或歌艳曲，则莺喉宛转，轻若游丝；或作旋风之舞，垂手折腰，无不入妙。倦则举杯觞饮。二人深相投契，如形随影，靡夕不至，至无不嬉。

    Explanation

    312

    Liu lifted the jar, but the location was unknown. The next night, he washed cups in anticipation, and the young boy indeed arrived. The boy was about fourteen or fifteen years old, with his hair tied in two buns, wearing a short jacket embroidered with colorful patterns, pants with cloud-like decorations, small red shoes, a gold chain around his neck, bracelets on his hands, and he was clearly and beautifully, incomparably lovely, comparable to Liu. The boy said, "I came to fulfill the playful agreement." Liu was delighted and asked for his surname, and the boy replied, "My surname is Yu." Liu then called him "Brother" and played with him. Sometimes he told new and strange stories, making people listen with great interest and without tiring; sometimes he performed various plays, all with ingenious ideas, deceiving or distracting the eyes; sometimes he sang beautiful songs, with a voice like a nightingale, melodious and graceful, light as a spider's thread; sometimes he performed a whirling wind dance, with hands hanging down and waist bending, all of which were perfect. When tired, they would lift cups to drink. The two deeply understood and trusted each other, like shadow following form, no night without coming, and when he came, there was always play.

    313

    一夕，于窗前剪纸照影，手提口演，刘自外视，与场上俳优声情毕肖，为之叫绝。既而相与入坐。少年持其剪纸云：“一片热肠，空费裁成为纸戏。”命刘属对，刘曰：“我未读书，焉能作对？”少年曰：“荒为嬉，何如勤尔业耶？”刘曰：“即欲读书，谁其教我？”少年曰：“吾日间在家，以读书为事。今后勿嬉，我以平旦之所得者，清夜而授尔，何如？”刘曰：“固所愿也。”自此相与正字校书，咿唔灯火，鸡鸣而散。

    Explanation

    313

    One night, at the window, he cut paper and reflected his shadow, holding and performing orally. Liu watched from outside and saw that the performer's voice and emotion were perfectly lifelike, and he was amazed to the point of exclaiming. They then sat down together. The young man held the paper figures and said, "With a heart full of enthusiasm, one's efforts are wasted in cutting and shaping paper into a play." He commanded Liu to compose a couplet, and Liu replied, "I have not studied, how can I compose a couplet?" The young man said, "How about working hard on your studies?" Liu asked, "If I want to study, who will teach me?" The young man replied, "I study at home during the day. From now on, do not play; I will teach you what I learn in the morning at night, how about that?" Liu said, "That is certainly what I wish for." From then on, they corrected characters and proofread books, read by the light of a lamp, and dispersed at the crow of the rooster.

    314

    刘生而聪慧，不二年冠童子试，逾年领乡荐。人咸以为有仙授。时年已十六，知识渐启，与少年情好愈笃。刘尝曰：“吾观天下女子，未有如兄美者。”少年曰：“尔诚少见而多怪也。吾有一妹，饶有姿容。若令尔见，当不知如何诧异。”刘曰：“能一见否？”少年曰：“呼之立至。”刘喜跃曰：“望兄移玉邀来一晤，幸勿稽迟。”少年微笑而起，将手揭帘，向外一转即入，果一女子。宝髻云鬟，娉婷如画，侧立不语。刘执烛凝眸，良久曰：“非兄也耶？”女曰：“痴子，尔兄亦缠足乎？”刘乃视其裙下双钩，翘然三寸。

    Explanation

    314

    D Liu was born with exceptional intelligence, passing the young scholar's examination within two years and being recommended by the local authorities the following year. People believed he had received divine assistance. By the time he was sixteen, his knowledge was gradually awakening, and his affection for the young man grew even stronger. Liu once said, "I have seen no woman as beautiful as my brother." The young man replied, "You truly see little and are full of wonder. I have a younger sister who is exceptionally beautiful. If you were to see her, you would not know how surprised you would be." Liu asked, "Can I meet her?" The young man said, "Just call her, and she will come immediately." Liu was overjoyed and said, "I hope you will invite her to meet me. Please do not delay." The young man smiled and stood up, raising his hand to pull aside the curtain. He turned and entered, and indeed, there was a woman. She had a jeweled hairpin and cloud-like hair, graceful like a painting, standing sideways in silence. Liu held a candle and fixed his gaze on her for a long time before saying, "Is this not your brother?" The woman said, "Foolish boy, does your brother also have bound feet?" Liu then looked at her feet under her skirt, which were prominently three inches long.

    315

    曰：“兄将何往？”女曰：“归去矣，嘱奴来与尔作伴。”又笑向帘外取男履一双，向刘曰：“此尔兄之留遗也。”刘接视，见其棉絮楦满帮内，不觉泪下如雨。女笑曰：“毋悲，我固尔兄，非妹也。”刘泣曰：“我亦知尔非妹，即兄也。惟其兄，是以悲耳。何不早令我知之？”女乃自袖中出花巾，为刘拭面曰：“尔生也晚，非余言之不欲早也。况羊未亡而牢可补，我两人犹小夫妇也。”刘乃破涕为喜，遂相与绸缪。女曰：“毋躁，三年前灯影对对来。如不能就，今宵尚得分床。”刘应声曰：“几回苦口，漫劳点拨助膏灯。”女点首，遂成夫妇。次日，女亦不去。
