One day, while Yang was bathing, his brother secretly peeped and saw a large turtle, which was spitting out water from the jars. He was greatly shocked and did not dare to reveal it. After the agreed time, he reported back and returned home, and Yang held him back and gave him a generous gift.

    426

    弟思：“贵溪龙虎真人敕勒可以制怪，盍往求之？”负资而至，具申以故。真人叠指默坐，半晌曰：“吾当亲往歼焉，否则不可制也。”乃作道装，著棕鞋，负葫芦，命其弟肩蒲团从之。迤逦至湖，投谒，送长生丹。传谕：“云冠羽流，素所鄙夷。不得逗留境内，宜速去，勿见逐也。”当太守出，真人遮于路，手掷一物入舆，舆裂以遁。真人拂袖入云表，一郡皆哗。其弟于稠人广众之中，悉述其异。郡之人素怨毒之，恒乐其速就诛也。真人追至府署，始就擒捉。乃告其弟曰：“伏之矣！”遂探袖中，出一小金钱龟，被道冠簪刺透胸盖，缩项如伏罪囚。真人曰：“孽畜生杀人之身，窃人之位，败乃国法，糜烂我庶民，宜暴之以明正其辜。”随人弃去，则霹雳震起，电光闪烁。忽一铁柱自天而落，直插地上，柱上符勒皆不可辨。后作亭以纪其异。当时，其弟尽散其宦资于湖民，遣其姬还京师，乃自归滇。闻其后亦为道士云。

    Explanation

    426

    The younger brother thought, "The Dragon and Tiger True Man of Guixi, with his divine command, can subdue strange phenomena. Why not go and seek him out?" He brought resources and explained the reason. The True Man remained silent, pointing his fingers, and after a while said, "I shall personally go to subdue it. Otherwise, it cannot be controlled." He then put on Taoist attire, wore brown shoes, carried a gourd, and instructed his brother to carry a prayer mat and follow him. They traveled windingly to the lake, where he paid respects and offered the elixir of immortality. The True Man issued a decree: "Cloud-crowned and feathered hermits, I have always despised you. You are not to linger in my domain; leave quickly, lest you be expelled." When the governor emerged, the True Man blocked his path, throwing an object into the carriage, which split open and fled. The True Man waved his sleeves and ascended into the clouds, causing a commotion throughout the county. His brother recounted the strange events in front of a large crowd. The people of the county, who had long harbored resentment, were pleased that the True Man would be swiftly punished. The True Man pursued him to the government office, where he was finally captured. He then told his brother, "I have subdued it!" He reached into his sleeve and produced a small gold-crowned turtle, its chest pierced by a Taoist hairpin, its neck drawn back like a guilty prisoner. The True Man said, "This wicked beast has killed people, usurped positions, undermined the national law, and corrupted the common people. It should be exposed openly to clearly punish its guilt." As they threw it away, a thunderclap shook the sky, and lightning flashed. Suddenly, an iron column fell from the heavens, plunging straight into the ground. The inscriptions on the column were all illegible. Later, a pavilion was built to commemorate the strange event. At that time, his brother distributed all his official wealth to the people of the lake, sent his concubine back to the capital, and returned to Yunnan on his own. It is said that he later became a Taoist priest.

    427

    (七如氏曰：今人一入仕途，顿丧生平之素，所谓上台便换面孔者，岂皆鳖嗑之乎？不宁惟是，而其趋奉势利，莫不古今一辙。试观饮黄龙汤[和氏开客]、嗅病马脓[赵元楷]、尝便溺[郭宏霸]、奉溺器[朱之问]、拂大参须[丁谓]、拭相公带尾垢[崔公度]、为太尉濯足[彭逊]、作篱边犬[赵师择]，皆足令千载人冷齿。况赵孟所贵，赵孟能贱。吾人穷达皆有定数，初何必变本加厉，卒令妻子朋友诧异，前后判若两人者，抑独何也！)

    Explanation

    427

    Final Translation

    Seven Ru said: When people enter the official world, they suddenly abandon their lifelong nature, as if they change their face when they come to work. Are they all just flattering? Not only that, but their flattery of power and wealth is no different from what has been throughout history. Let us look at those who drink Yellow Dragon Soup [He Shi Kai Ke], smell the pus of a sick horse [Zhao Yuankai], taste excrement [Guo Hongba], serve a chamber pot [Zhu Zhiwen], stroke the mustache of a high-ranking official [Ding Wei], wipe the dirt from the end of a high-ranking official's belt [Cui Gongdu], wash the feet of a high-ranking official [Peng Xun], and act like a dog on the fence [Zhao Shize]. All of these actions are enough to make people laugh for a thousand years. Moreover, what Zhao Meng values, Zhao Meng can despise. Our fortunes and misfortunes are determined by fate. Why go beyond the original and finally cause one's wife, children, and friends to be surprised, making one seem like a different person before and after? What is the reason for this?

    428

    石氏妻
    平阴石绍孔，佣奴也。娶妻，年十七，颇美。成婚后，辄不食，甚至水不下咽。其初家人以为新妇羞，继则以为新妇病。积有日，总绝粒，且经岁如是，而颜色肌肤更丰脆。又一年，生一子，终岁操井臼、勤纺绩弗辍。迄今年五十馀，了不异人，惟夜寝则浑身悉冷，惟胸前一点微热，晨必扑其鼻端乃醒，否则竟日长眠。每询之，则云：“彼处另有家，丰衣食。今此梦中耳。几见梦中人必饮食哉？”可亭居停田公言之。石佣，田公之老仆也。

    Explanation

    428

    A Wife of the Shi Family

    Shi Shaokong from Pingyin was a hired servant. He married a wife who was seventeen years old and quite beautiful. After the wedding, she would not eat, even water. Initially, the family thought the new bride was shy, and later they believed she was ill. After some time, she completely stopped eating and continued like this for a year, yet her complexion and skin became more plump and delicate. Another year passed, and she gave birth to a son. She carried out household chores and diligently spun and wove without stopping. Until now, she is over fifty years old and is no different from others. Only at night, when she sleeps, her entire body is cold, but her chest has a slight warmth. In the morning, she must be gently patted on the tip of her nose to wake up; otherwise, she would sleep all day. Every time she is asked, she says, "There is another family there with abundant clothing and food. Now this is just a dream. How often do people in dreams need to eat?" Mr. Tian, who stayed at the Pavilion, said this. Shi, the hired servant, is Mr. Tian's old servant.

    429

    曹公洞
    益都金岭之南为公泉峪，其山有洞曰“曹公洞”，下有潭，深不可测。洞方阔数尺，止容三五人。入则渐狭幽窅，宛转无尽。
    有姓张者，曩日浴潭中，整衣入洞，久之不出。其家觅之。有见其入者，试呼之，辄应，问之，曰：“吾见洞门大开，高堂广厦。既深入，忽昏闇逼窄，石簇簇束吾身，不能动转。”乃令人侧身以竿探之，云：“是我发髻。”即以竿杪递食。一日后云：“石渐束吾腹，不能食矣。”更呼之，不应。人遂以石塞其洞，无复入者。
    (七如氏曰：何武陵渔者得入桃源，与避秦人遇，话桑麻，具鸡黍，出入绰绰然有馀裕哉？今张姓探奇，遂致陷身石窦，进退维谷之际，其间不容以寸，岂不痛伤。实偪之惨，自取咎耶？)

    Explanation

    429

    Cao Gong Dong
    South of Jinling in Yidu is Gongquan Valley, and on the mountain there is a cave called "Cao Gong Dong." Below the cave is a pool so deep that its depth cannot be measured. The entrance to the cave is several feet wide and can only accommodate three or five people. As one enters, the cave gradually narrows and becomes increasingly dark and winding, seemingly endless.
    There was a man surnamed Zhang who, on a certain day, bathed in the pool and then entered the cave fully dressed. He did not emerge for a long time. His family searched for him. Someone who saw him enter called out to him, and he responded. When asked, he said, "I saw the cave's entrance wide open, with grand halls and spacious rooms. After going deeper, it suddenly became dark, narrow, and constricting, with rocks pressing against my body, making it impossible to move." They then sent someone to probe the cave with a pole, and he reported, "It hit my hair." They used the pole to pass food to him. After one day, he reported, "The rocks are gradually squeezing my abdomen, and I can no longer eat." They called out again, but he did not respond. They then sealed the cave with stones, and no one else entered.
    (Qi Ru said: How is it that the fisherman from Wu Ling was able to enter the Peach Blossom Spring, meet those who had fled the Qin dynasty, talk about mulberry and hemp, prepare chicken and millet, and live comfortably? Now, the man surnamed Zhang, seeking adventure, became trapped in a narrow stone cave, unable to move forward or backward, with no room to turn. Is this not a tragic and painful situation? The real tragedy is that he brought this upon himself.)

    430

    场中儿啼
    读王文简《居易录》，会试外帘，说“贡院”中，忽闻小儿啼声，迹之，在“明远楼”上。登楼视之，果有小儿如初生者，卧而啼哭，莫知所从来，诚异事也。
    余于己亥乡试东省二场，明月如洗，甬道上并无一人。两行号舍，灯火相连，三鼓后“明远楼”上人哗曰：“甬道中有一妇人，抱一儿，携一子，随一犬。呜呜咽咽，往来甬路，出入号舍，自‘巨’字号出，今入‘虞’字号矣。”余正此号，方欲假寐，悚然而起。时各号大半皆息，及闻声出视，真如传警。汹汹之声，戒旦不绝。亦奇矣。

    口中吐火
    康熙三十二年，潍县北乡一老妪口中吐火，自焚毙。有刘以贵记一诗，云：
    忆昔甲戌春，新正才十日。离城廿里遥，老妪色如漆。倏忽出火光，怂涌口鼻出。绿烟冲九天，比邻争造室。
    救火火愈炽，幻成瞿昙质。异事哄城市，焚黄金成镒。咄咄村间妇，疑得三昧术。荏苒历十年，此理无从识。

    Explanation

    430

    Translation

    Field of Crying Babies

    Reading Wang Wenjian's Juyi Lu (The Record of Ease), it is recorded that during the outer examination hall of the imperial examination, someone suddenly heard the cries of a baby in the "Gongyuan" (Examination Hall). Tracing the sound, they found it coming from the "Mingyuan Lou" (Bright Distant Tower). Climbing the tower to look, they indeed saw a newborn baby lying and crying, with no one knowing where it had come from. Truly, it was an unusual event.

    During my second examination in the eastern province in the year Jihai (1693), the moon was as clear as washed, and there was no one on the corridor. On both sides were examination rooms, with lights connected in a row. After the third drum strike, people on the "Mingyuan Lou" shouted, "There is a woman in the corridor, holding a baby, carrying a child, and followed by a dog. She is sobbing and crying, moving back and forth along the corridor, entering and exiting the examination rooms, coming out of the 'Ju' number room and now entering the 'Yu' number room." I was in this room, about to take a nap, when I suddenly woke up in alarm. At that time, most of the examination rooms were silent, and when I heard the sound and went out to see, it was as if an alarm had been raised. The loud sounds continued until dawn. It was also a strange occurrence.

    Spitting Fire

    In the thirty-second year of the Kangxi era (1693), an elderly woman in a village in the northern part of Weixian county suddenly began spitting fire and burned herself to death. Liu Yigui wrote a poem about this, which reads:
    Remembering the spring of Jia Xu (1684), only ten days into the new year. Twenty miles away from the city, the old woman's skin was as dark as ink. Suddenly, firelight emerged, surging out of her mouth and nose. Green smoke rose to the heavens, and neighbors rushed to their homes.
    The fire grew even more intense, and she appeared to transform into a Buddha-like figure. Strange events spread through the city, and gold was burned, turning into ingots. Astonishing village woman, it seems she had mastered a secret technique. Over ten years have passed, and this reason is hard to understand.

    431

    疖溃出蝉
    莱阳县南高家庄梁氏妇，背生一疖，半月而溃，无脓血，但出荆棘数枝，一蝉振羽曳声以去，遂愈。
    (吾邑北乡梁家海一梁姓，踝生疮，如豆隆起，抓破出烟一缕，袅袅不断。合村来观，不辨名症。三日后，烟炽有焰，入水不灭，夜炤床席。病者呼痛，如炮烙肌肤间，五日乃死。闻此人素无他嗜，惟饮烧酒后，吃烟无算云。)

    黄玉山
    潜山黄玉山,慧巧，读书而贫不能继膏油，以写真求利，擅名一时。会游山右。有平阳太守桂公，东海荣城人。其太夫人年登七十，延黄写照。
    时当初春，是日阴晦。太夫人貂裘凤帽出，群婢环列。旁坐则太守之女，亦戴紫貂搭头，著锦花团绣天马氅，系百鸟裙，艳丽夺目。四围兽炭香麝竞烧。黄炫目移神，濡毫下笔，不知所为。逾刻而粉地先成，进阅，群婢曰：“此女公子也。”黄愕顾，自以为误，因复易一图以进，佥谓神似太夫人矣。画成，太守谢之多金。

    Explanation

    431

    Boil Erupts and a Cicada Emerges

    In the southern village of Gaojia, Laixiang County, there was a woman surnamed Liang. She had a boil on her back that ruptured after half a month. There was no pus or blood, but instead, several branches of thorns emerged, and a cicada fluttered its wings and chirped before flying away. She then healed.

    (There was a governor of Pingyang named Gui, who was from Rongcheng, Donghai. His dowager was seventy years old. She wore a fur coat and phoenix hat, surrounded by maids. Sitting beside her was the governor's daughter, who also wore a purple fox fur hat, a brocade embroidered with floral patterns and a celestial horse cloak, and a skirt adorned with a hundred birds, all of which were dazzling and beautiful. Animal charcoal and musk burned around them. Huang Yushan was dazzled and shifted his gaze, dipped his brush, and started writing, but he didn't know what to do. After a while, the white ground of the painting was completed first. Upon inspection, the maids said, "This is the young lady." Huang Yushan was startled and thought he had made a mistake, so he changed the painting and presented it again. Everyone agreed that it resembled the dowager in spirit. The painting was completed, and the governor thanked him with a large sum of money.)

    Huang Yushan, from Qianshan, was wise and skilled. He studied but was poor and could not continue with lamp oil, so he painted portraits for profit and became famous for a time. He once traveled to Shanxi. The governor of Pingyang, Gui, invited him to paint a portrait of his dowager.

    It was early spring, and on that day, it was overcast. The dowager, wearing a fur coat and phoenix hat, came out, surrounded by maids. Sitting beside her was the governor's daughter.

    432

    生归寓，取其初画女公子像，足而完之。令其赤身斜立，左手执一纨扇，独蔽下体，悬寝室中。一日，黄饮夜半，酬曰：“公子盍饮一杯醹醖？”言讫，觉画上面颊頳红，笑容可掬，黄甚异之。自此每饭不忘。会晚雨，黄出窗外伫立，闻室中簌簌响。舐棂偷觑，一娟好女子依几支颐，俨若画中。黄启帏入，四无踪迹，怅怅就寝。一檠相对，默祝其来。既而倦寝，女忽揭帐，钩响，生醒，以手探之，温如软玉，遂揽入怀。女曰：“春雨凝寒，逼人肌肤，奈何终日置屏间？”生曰：“明旦当藏之绣衾中矣。”生起求欢，女曰：“姑徐徐，不当唐突西子。君风雅人，请试一对。如不能就，何止酒数。”生请之，女曰：“多晴今得雨。”生即应曰：“有杏不须梅。”遂成伉俪。生问其名，女曰：“非非。”生曰：“太守为谁？”女曰：“我大人也。”生曰：“信如是，安能到此？”女曰：“昔韩寿偷香，女中岂无似丈夫者耶？”鸡鸣遂去。

    Explanation

    432

    Huang returned to his lodging and took the initial sketch of the young lady, completing it. He made her stand half-naked, leaning slightly, holding a silk fan in her left hand, covering her lower body, and hung it in his bedroom. One day, at midnight, Huang replied to himself, "Why don't you drink a cup of fine wine?" After saying this, he noticed the face on the painting blushing and smiling, and Huang was very surprised. From then on, he never forgot it during meals. On a rainy evening, Huang stood by the window. He heard a rustling sound from the room. Peering through the window bars, he saw a beautiful young woman leaning on a table with her chin resting on her hand, as if from a painting. Huang opened the curtain and entered, but found no trace of her. Feeling sad, he went to bed. Facing a single lamp, he silently wished for her to come. Soon after, he fell asleep. The woman suddenly pulled the curtain, and the hook made a sound. Huang woke up and touched her, finding her as warm as soft jade. He then embraced her. She said, "The spring rain is cold, chilling my skin. Why keep me in the screen all day?" Huang replied, "Tomorrow, I will put you in a brocade quilt." Huang tried to make love to her, but she said, "Let's take it slowly; I don't want you to be too forward. You are a refined person; let's try a couplet. If you can't, then no more wine for you." Huang asked her, and she said, "Many sunny days have finally brought rain." Huang immediately responded, "If there is a plum, there is no need for a plum." They became husband and wife. Huang asked her name, and she said, "Nono." Huang said, "Who is the magistrate?" She said, "I am my own master." Huang said, "If that's true, how could she be here?" She said, "In the past, Han Shou stole perfume; is there no woman who acts like a man?" At dawn, she left.

    433

    自是至无虚夕，与生谈诗文，皆远过生。戏题其照曰：“好个丫头，寸丝不挂。因不是我，用扇蔽下。若还是我，连扇去罢。”又有题词数阕，女皆喜纳云。女曰：“妾以怜才，终蹈私奔之丑，倘一经侦觉，势难镜合。郎君诚相错爱，尚效女红，双骑共逸耳。”生曰：“良佳，但难得此脚力。”女曰：“何难之有？”晨起，有二马立于门，踶趹昂嘶。生即束装，与女并辔而驰，倏忽不计道里。既而山径偪仄，叠嶂迂回，而女之先驱甚驶。

    Explanation

    433

    From then on, every night without fail, the girl and the student discussed poetry and literature, and she surpassed the student in skill. She playfully inscribed on her portrait: "Such a lovely girl, not a thread on her body. Because it's not me, she uses a fan to cover herself. If it were me, I would even take away the fan." She also wrote several poems, which the girl happily accepted. The girl said, "I did this out of admiration for your talent, but I ended up taking a disgraceful step of eloping. If anyone were to discover this, it would be impossible to maintain our perfect union. If you truly cherish me, let's continue to learn needlework and ride together in freedom." The student replied, "That sounds wonderful, but where would we find such horses?" The girl said, "What's difficult about that?" In the morning, two horses stood at the gate, kicking, rearing, and neighing. The student immediately prepared to leave, and the girl rode alongside him. In a flash, they traveled without counting the distance. The paths grew narrow and winding, with towering peaks and twisting terrain, but the girl's advance was very swift.

    434

    至一处，重垣兽脊，木植阴翳，女与生驰而入。下骑，系树间。登堂，焕然丹垩，独无一人承应。生问此何地，女曰：“故园也。家大人游宦多年，久经荒芜耳。”俄一老媪送茗至，继以烛。女曰：“妾爱楼居。”生曰：“可。”妪执灯前导，胡梯而上。生登楼，楼颇轩敞。生翻邺架旧帙，悉系桂家。及问其妪，亦荣城故里，无异词也。女居常只用妪一人进食，馀无溷至。每劝生读，勉图上进。奈黄之为黄也，固半途而辄废，复见异而思迁。往往于鸡鸣咿喔之际，女抱绣相对，生辄倦欲寝。女长叹不怿。

    Explanation

    434

    They arrived at a place with high walls and animal-like roof ridges, surrounded by dense trees. The woman and the man galloped into the place. They dismounted and tied their horses to trees. Upon entering the hall, it was brightly painted but devoid of any response. The man asked where they were, and the woman replied that it was their old garden, which had been neglected for many years due to the father's long-term official duties. Shortly after, an elderly woman brought tea and then a candle. The woman said, "I prefer staying in a tower." The man agreed. The old woman held the lamp and led the way up the ladder. The man climbed the tower, which was quite spacious. He flipped through old books on the shelves, all of which belonged to the Gui family. When he asked the old woman, she confirmed that they were from the same hometown, with no discrepancy. The woman usually ate with only the old woman present, and no one else visited. She often encouraged the man to read and strive for advancement. Despite her efforts, the man often abandoned his studies halfway and was easily distracted by other interests. Often at the crowing of the rooster, the woman held a brocade quilt and sat beside him, but the man became tired and wanted to sleep. The woman sighed deeply, showing her disappointment and dissatisfaction.

    435

    会秋宵，方假寝，忽闻排闼破扉声，继入以炬，多人执械。生方欲喊，一人以白刃加生颈，不敢声。但见数人卷女于衾，缚而舁之，并所有什物，席空而去。又系生送诸四十里之外，弃生。生狼藉凄怆，觅路遄归。至园门则扃锁重缄，荒草寂寂。问诸邻人，果为桂公旧宅，十馀年无人居住，闻近日桂公将归田，欲葺此屋宇而未果也。问：“数月前女公子来否？”皆曰：“无之。”生惘然若失，知其为魅。即魅，亦切恋恋不能置。荣城固海僻，生举目无识，乞于道，瘦惫已甚。

    Explanation

    435

    On an autumn night, the protagonist was just taking a brief nap when suddenly he heard the sound of someone breaking down the door. Following this, several people entered with torches, armed with weapons. The protagonist was about to shout, but one of them held a knife to his neck, silencing him. He saw several people wrap the woman in a quilt, bind her, and carry her away, along with all her belongings, leaving the place empty-handed. They then tied up the protagonist and took him forty miles away, abandoning him. The protagonist returned home in a state of disarray and sorrow, quickly finding a way back. Upon reaching the garden gate, he found it tightly locked and sealed, with overgrown grass and silence all around. He asked the neighbors, who confirmed that it was once the old house of Guì, abandoned for over ten years. They heard that Guì was planning to retire and renovate the house but had not yet done so. He asked, "Did a daughter come here a few months ago?" The neighbors all said, "No." The protagonist felt lost and confused, realizing that the woman was a spirit. Even as a spirit, he was deeply attached and could not let go. The town of Róngchéng was a remote coastal area, and the protagonist looked around but found no one familiar, begging for help on the road, feeling extremely exhausted.

    436

    一月而抵济南，乃以画鬻于市，仅得易食，而衣粗不完体也。重阳，济南千佛寺游女甚盛，生随往观。见一女子手持红叶一枝，身欲登舆，揭帘频频顾生。生甫觉而舆已飞去，望之俨似非非。生曳追之，但见暮烟四起，夜色迷漫。正踌躇间，拾得红叶一枝，上有钗画一诗云：
    莫非非即是，今既是非非。既识非非是，非非是耶非？
    生泪下，穷力踪迹，遥盼笋舆，竟入山谷。生即攀陟嵢岈，不顾颠仆。约二更，抵一村，石垣垒门，嶔崎凸凹，惟板扉内一灯荧荧，坐一老叟。生入问叟曰：“适一肩舆，将何所抵？”叟怒曰：“尔远方人，深更叩户，与君无萍水交，适小女归，尔问之，何所见闻？请闻教焉。”生口塞，半晌曰：“路迷，求假宿。”叟目视生，曰：“老夫非逆旅主人也。”逐生出，扃其户。生不得已，乃于门前席地趺跏。

    Explanation

    436

    One month later, he arrived in Jinan, where he sold his paintings in the market, only managing to exchange them for food, and his clothes were coarse and incomplete. On the Double Ninth Festival, the Thousand Buddha Temple in Jinan had many women visitors, and the man followed to watch. He saw a woman holding a branch of red leaves, who was about to board a sedan chair, lifting the curtain and looking at him repeatedly. Just as he realized it, the sedan chair had already flown away, and looking at it, it seemed like something other than what it was. The man pulled and chased after it, but he only saw the evening smoke rising all around, and the night was dim and hazy. Just as he was hesitating, he picked up a branch of red leaves, on which there was a hairpin and a poem written:
    Is not what is not, now is what is not.
    Having recognized what is not, is not what is not.
    The man wept, and with all his might, he traced the path, looking far into the distance where the sedan chair had gone, but it had already entered the mountains. The man climbed the steep and rugged terrain,不顾颠仆 (without regard for falling). Around two o'clock at night, he reached a village, with stone walls and a gate, rough and uneven, with only a dim light glowing inside a wooden door, where an old man was sitting. The man entered and asked the old man, "Where was the sedan chair heading?" The old man angrily said, "You, a distant traveler, knocking on my door late at night. I have no acquaintance with you. My daughter has just returned, and you ask me what you have seen and heard? Please tell me." The man was speechless, and after a moment, he said, "I am lost and seeking a place to stay for the night." The old man looked at the man and said, "I am not an innkeeper." He then pushed the man out and locked the door. The man, having no choice, sat on the ground in front of the door in a cross-legged position.

    437

    凉风带霜，夜静石冷，生乃抱叶呜咽，真不啻虫鸣阶井也。门顿启，一女持球灯出照，曰：“此非黄郎乎？”生起欲认，而灯已灭。生持女欲泣，女曰：“慎勿悲凄，响则丧尔生矣！”女牵生悄入，闩户，室中几上设一灯。生相见，泪下如雨，不敢仰视。女掖之，亦泣曰：“奴负郎矣！郎自不长进，不克自立，徒以一艺碌碌天涯，何以为家？适宜偿以今日之厄也。妾本非太守女，因怜君孤孑，故冒名求匹。实欲玉汝于成，何期甘心暴弃，坐废居诸？妾即与郎厮守终身，不过一画士妻，奚贵哉？”生告以悔。又听有剥啄声，翁出，女灭灯。生问，女戒勿扬。生于窗隙窥见一紫金冠者，如贵官状，入中堂。女指生，以足击地曰：“此小姨夫也。汝措大能不相形见绌耶？”遂掩袖涔涔。生曰：“我愿自立，不贻尔羞。”二人共枕，各诉离衷。生忽朦胧。甫觉，人舍已空，独卧石上。惊起，身旁有画一轴，黄金一铤。画即所画太守女，照上题词宛然，凄恻难名。

    Explanation

    437

    Cool wind carries frost, the night is quiet and the stone is cold, the man embraces a leaf and weeps, truly no different from the chirping of insects near a well. Suddenly the door opens, a woman holds a lantern and steps out to illuminate, saying, "Isn't this Huang Lang?" The man stands up to recognize her, but the lantern has already gone out. The man holds the woman and wants to cry, the woman says, "Beware of sorrow, if you make a sound, you will lose your life!" The woman leads the man in quietly, locks the door, and sets a lamp on the table in the room. They meet, and tears fall like rain, the man dares not look up. The woman supports him, also crying, saying, "I have failed you! You have not made progress, unable to stand on your own, only with a single skill, wandering aimlessly in the world, how can one make a home? It is appropriate to repay today's misfortune. I am not actually the daughter of the governor, because I pity your loneliness, so I took on a false name to seek a match. I truly wanted to refine you into success, how did I expect you to willingly give up, sitting idle and wasting time? I will stay with you for life, no more than the wife of a painter, what is valuable about it?" The man confesses his regret. They hear a knocking sound, the old man comes out, the woman extinguishes the lamp. The man asks, the woman warns him not to reveal anything. The man looks through a crack in the window and sees a man with a purple gold crown, like a high-ranking official, entering the main hall. The woman points to the man and hits the ground with her foot, saying, "This is your brother-in-law. Can you not be outshone by this scholar?" She then wipes away her tears. The man says, "I wish to be self-reliant and not bring shame to you." The two share a pillow, each expressing their inner feelings. The man suddenly becomes drowsy. Just as he awakens, the room is empty, and he is alone lying on a stone. He starts up in surprise, beside him is a scroll of painting and a bar of gold. The painting is of the governor's daughter, and the inscription on it is clearly visible, evoking a sense of sorrow that is hard to describe.

    438

    幸有贻金，可以办行装。返寓易其金，南归，攻举业，不复言画。时悬女照于帏中，如临师保。往往读罢对之而哭，哭罢复读。后入京师，乡、会联捷，入词林。有山东张进士者，与生同校录，知生未娶，欲以桂姓表妹妻生。生问里居，果荣城桂守之女，异之，遂许婚。
    先是桂公任满，告休入都，见中表张庶常，托为择婚，恐荣城僻地，无可坦者，并留女于都。一日，女与诸姊出奇华门，游二闸，泛小舟。忽岸旁一女子呼共济，舟近载之。女登舟，欻然不见，众以为怪。归，女之丰神顿改，灵敏异常。女先通书词，今一时造艺，殊堪刮目。文词书籍，考之不能屈。至涓吉，张公主婚，行亲迎礼。生到门，吹擂喧填，内呼曰：“索新贵人催妆诗。”生笑应之。偕归交拜，揭面盖，视女与非非无异，心知其非非非也。然不知今日之非非，果有异于前日之非非否也？

    Explanation

    438

    Fortunately, there was a gift of gold, which could be used to prepare for the journey. Returning to his residence, he exchanged the gold, returned to the south, pursued scholarly studies, and no longer talked about painting. At times, he hung the girl's portrait in the curtain, as if in the presence of a teacher or guardian. Often, after reading, he would cry in front of it, and after crying, he would read again. Later, he entered the capital, succeeded in both the provincial and metropolitan examinations, and entered the literary establishment. There was a Mr. Zhang from Shandong, who was a successful candidate in the imperial examination, and worked together in the same office. Knowing that the student was unmarried, he wanted to marry his cousin named Gui to the student. The student asked about her place of origin, and indeed, she was the daughter of the governor of Rongcheng. He found it extraordinary and agreed to the marriage.

    Previously, Governor Gui's term was over, and he retired and went to the capital. He met his cousin Zhang, a successful candidate in the imperial examination, and asked him to find a suitable match, fearing that Rongcheng was a remote place with no suitable prospects, and left his daughter in the capital. One day, the girl and her sisters went out through the Qihua Gate, visited the Second Sluice, and rowed a small boat. Suddenly, a woman on the shore called to join the boat, and the boat approached and took her on board. The girl boarded the boat and suddenly disappeared, and everyone found it strange. Returning, the girl's appearance suddenly changed, and she became extraordinarily sharp-witted. She was already well-versed in letters, and now, in a short time, she had developed remarkable skills, truly worthy of admiration. Her literary works and books could not be challenged or refuted. When the auspicious day was chosen, Mr. Zhang oversaw the marriage, and the bridegroom personally came to fetch the bride. The student arrived at the door, and the sound of drums and gongs filled the air. Someone inside called out, "Demanding a poem to urge the bride to dress." The student smiled and responded. They returned together and bowed to each other, lifted the veil, and saw that the girl was no different from the ghost, but the student knew that she was not the same ghost. However, the student did not know if today's ghost was truly different from yesterday's ghost.

    439

    花烛之夕，共女入帏，女见画曰：“画则犹是也，恐黄金费尽矣。”生惊曰：“是非非耶？非非非耶？”女含颦曰：“非非苟非我，我何以知非非？我诚非非非，我固知非非也。”生乃问女，夜雨联床，楼头课读，以及山谷遗金，历历不爽。复详诘之，女曰：“奴与郎初会时，见郎落笔凝思，心为所感，因之情与俱移。至若与子同乘，原是意中之马；取怀相赠，何殊囊里之金。今幸鹏程万里，相期璧合一双，而郎终以二心歧视。窃恐非非一去，非非复来，而非非则诚非矣。”生因不敢置喙。后女归荣城，其父母不能辨，问闺中幼小时事，无不记忆。

    Explanation

    439

    On the night of the wedding, as they entered the bridal chamber together, the bride saw a painting and said, "The painting is still the same, but I fear all the gold will be spent." The groom was startled and asked, "Is this not you? Is this not not you?" The bride, with a frown, replied, "If it is not me, how can I know it is not not me? I truly am not not not me, and I indeed know that it is not not me." The groom then asked the bride about their shared experiences, such as studying together on rainy nights, reading books on the upper floor of a building, and finding unexpected treasures in the mountains and valleys. She recounted these events without error. He further inquired, and the bride said, "When I first met you, I saw you writing with deep thought, and my heart was moved, and thus my feelings were also affected. As for riding together, it was like riding a horse I had long desired; when I gave you a gift, it was no different from giving you gold from my purse. Now, I am fortunate to have a promising future, and we have promised to be a perfect match, but you have ultimately discriminated against me due to two hearts. I fear that what is not not will leave and not return, and that what is not not is indeed not." The groom dared not speak a word. Later, the bride returned to Rongcheng, and her parents could not recognize her. When asked about her childhood memories and experiences in the inner quarters of her home, she remembered everything clearly.

    440

    (文笔甚奇警。)

    卷十三 杂技类
    指画渴笔创始
    铁岭高少司寇其佩，字韦之，号且园。自谓且道人，又号古狂，名重天下。数十年来，莫不竞以司寇之指画称。
    夫以指作画，古未尝有，有之，自公始。公八岁学画，遇稿辄摹，积十馀年，盈二簏，每恨不能自成一家。倦而假寐，梦一老人引至土室，四壁皆画，理法无不具备。而室中空空，不能摹仿，惟水一盂，爰以指蘸而习之。觉而大喜。奈得于心不能应之于笔，辄复闷闷。偶忆土室用水之法，因而以指蘸墨，仿其大略，尽得其神，信手拈来，头头是道，乃投笔而不复用。有印章云：“画从梦得，梦自心成。”又自有句云：“笔画今为指画掩，须知指笔互相因。”

    Explanation

    440

    Translation:

    The writing is remarkably strange and striking.

    Volume Thirteen, Miscellaneous Skills
    The Origin of Finger Painting and Dry Brushwork
    Gao Qipei, the lesser justice of Tieling, courtesy name Wei Zhi, style name Qiaoyuan, and also known as Qudaoren and Gu Kuang, was renowned throughout the world. For several decades, no one failed to praise his finger painting.
    Using fingers to paint was unprecedented in ancient times; if it existed, it began with him. At the age of eight, he began learning to paint, and whenever he encountered a draft, he would copy it. After accumulating for more than ten years, he had filled two baskets, often lamenting that he could not develop his own style. Tired, he fell into a light sleep and dreamed of an old man leading him to a earthen room. The walls were covered with paintings, all of which demonstrated complete principles and techniques. Yet the room was empty, leaving nothing to copy, except for a single basin of water. He then dipped his fingers in the water and practiced. Upon waking, he was overjoyed. However, though he understood the technique in his heart, he could not replicate it with his brush, and he became disheartened. Suddenly remembering the method of using water in the earthen room, he then dipped his fingers in ink and imitated it roughly, capturing its essence perfectly. He could pick up a brush and create freely, everything he did was flawless, and he then put down his brush and never used it again. He had a seal that read: "Painting comes from dreams, dreams come from the heart." He also had a saying: "Brushwork is now overshadowed by finger painting; one must know that finger and brush are interdependent."

    441

    公于唐宋元明诸大家中钻研探讨，集其大成，将诸家之用意用法悉归于指，允称独步。其章法不拘前人，主客阴阳，自有阅历真境。其指法则各指有单用、双用、三指、满手、拳用之异。其染法则青、赭、红、黄，随意烘用，皆有神味趣机。其皴法则披麻、荷叶、斧劈，各有巨细，难名其妙。其用色如胭脂宜淡而偏浓、赭不宜赤而偏重，青绿加于重墨、硃粉施之金箑，皆古人之所不敢。其用墨至五色而无痕，于无痕而有象，尤觉自然。故见公画者，莫不知其天资高、学力到、胸襟阔大也。

    Explanation

    441

    The subject has studied and explored the works of major authors from the Tang, Song, Yuan, and Ming dynasties, synthesizing their best techniques and methods. He has gathered the essence of their intentions and practices, attributing them to his own finger techniques, truly deserving the title of unparalleled. His compositional methods are not bound by traditional constraints, balancing dominant and subordinate elements with his own genuine experience and insight. His finger techniques vary in application, including single, double, triple, full hand, and fist uses. His coloring techniques employ blue, ochre, red, and yellow, applied freely to create a sense of spirit, flavor, and interest. His textural techniques include "披麻" (wrinkled like hemp), "荷叶" (like lotus leaves), and "斧劈" (like axe cuts), each with its own variations and subtleties. His use of color, such as light shades of rouge but preferring darker tones, ochre that is not too red but heavy, and adding blue-green to dark ink or applying red powder to gold fans, were unconventional for ancient painters. His use of ink reaches a level where it appears to have five colors but is seamless, and yet it still conveys form and imagery, making it seem particularly natural. Therefore, anyone who sees his paintings will recognize his high natural talent, extensive learning, and broad-mindedness.

    442

    公画钟进士像，不下二百馀本。有文像武像、善威喜怒、壮老仙佛鬼怪，粗工钩勒之不同，神奇变幻，在当时即多显应。天津人持一画像，求售于查俭堂，查未信为真。忽其妾发狂如中祟状，云：“目中有长髯绿袍大汉！”惊怖欲绝。查移置画像于床榻被上，急焚香默祷，病辄愈。故宁国太守翟宅厅事，每夜不宁，后悬公所画像，即静谧。
    公画龙独开生面。曾于京口赴永宁观察时，虔祷雨中，得睹真容，故画龙有角有耳，独无所谓无碍者。画虎，头大而胯细，尝曰：“画工之虎，得其形似，不若吾虎之威也。”谓公乘醉以头画虎者，是齐东语。画狮不以长毛大尾，似虎非虎，黄色面方，两耳白毫拖地，尾结成球，人多不识，乃雍正年间公在御园亲见也。至若山水之兼众妙，人物之得真神，翎毛花卉，梅柳丛树，或仿古、或没骨、或白描，莫不各极其精。而且写照传神，诧为阿堵，是指画之能事毕矣！

    Explanation

    442

    Mr. Gong painted portraits of the scholar-official Zong Jinshi, creating no fewer than two hundred copies. These portraits included civilian and military figures, depicted with expressions of virtue, awe, joy, and anger, and portrayed young and old, immortals, Buddhas, ghosts, and monsters. The level of detail varied from rough outlines to fine brushwork, showcasing the artist's wondrous and ever-changing skills. During his time, these paintings were said to have many miraculous effects. A man from Tianjin brought one of these portraits to Zha Jiantang, hoping to sell it. Zha did not believe it was genuine. Suddenly, Zha's concubine went mad, claiming she saw a man with a long beard and wearing a green robe in her eyes! She was terrified to the point of death. Zha then moved the portrait to the bed and immediately burned incense and prayed silently. The concubine's illness immediately recovered. Therefore, in the hall of Zhai's residence, the governor of Ningguo, there were often restless nights until they hung Gong's portrait, after which it became peaceful.

    Gong's dragon paintings were uniquely innovative. Once, while traveling to Yongning as an observer, he sincerely prayed for rain and saw the true form of a dragon. Thus, his dragon paintings featured horns and ears, unlike others who claimed their dragons were unobstructed. His tiger paintings depicted large heads and slender hips, and he once said, "The tigers painted by other artists may resemble the form, but they do not match the majesty of my tigers." The claim that Gong drew tigers while drunk is a fabricated story. His lion paintings did not feature long fur and large tails; they resembled tigers but were not tigers. They had yellow faces, square features, white hair on both ears reaching the ground, and tails forming balls. Many people did not recognize them, as they were based on Gong's personal observation in the imperial garden during the Yongzheng era.

    Furthermore, his landscape paintings combined many wonders, his figure paintings captured true spirit, and his depictions of feathers, flowers, plants, plum, willow, and grove trees used various techniques, including imitating ancient styles, boneless techniques, and line drawings. Each technique reached the highest level of skill. Moreover, his ability to capture likeness and spirit was so surprising that it was considered a treasure, marking the pinnacle of his artistic achievements.

    443

    公画自供奉大内，以及海内缙绅家，无不索求。公惟日染指，自壮而老，未尝一刻释手。约在人间不下数万纸也，宜乎为一家之冠冕矣。同时如李天涛之指墨焦笔小品，后有朱伦瀚之山水、傅凯亭之人物，虽亦各有所长，是皆分公一体，或具体而微者也。
    孔衍栻，字石村。为稼部公之从子，曲阜人，圣裔也。贡生，官济宁训导。善画，以渴笔名，独辟蹊径。晚年学愈进，寿八十九。自著有《石村画诀》，云：
    古今画家，用水渲染，不易之法也。渴笔烘染，古人未创此境。余幼师石田，一树一石，必究其用意处，久之稍有所得。因静心自思，笔笔石田，终在古人范围。乃穷日夜之思，忽结别想。偶以渴笔烘染，似觉别有意趣，脱却俗态。久乃益精，幸不为鉴赏家所鄙。实由苦心，未尽自泯，因志画诀藏箧中，以俟同志。

    Explanation

    443

    The Artist's Brushstrokes Spanned the Imperial Court and Beyond

    The artist painted for the imperial court and was sought after by scholars and officials throughout the land. He dedicated himself to painting daily, from youth to old age, never putting down the brush for a moment. It is estimated that he produced no fewer than tens of thousands of works, fittingly earning him the title of the crown of a school or style. Among his contemporaries were Li TianTao, known for his small works done with finger painting and dry brush techniques, followed by Zhu Lunhan, who excelled in landscape painting, and Fu KaiTing, who specialized in figurative painting. Although each had their strengths, they were all part of the same school or style, or smaller versions of it.

    Kong YanShi, style name Shicun, was the nephew of JiaBu Gong, a native of Qufu, and a descendant of Confucius. As a tribute scholar, he served as a tutor in Jining. He was skilled in painting, particularly known for his dry brush technique, and pioneered a new path in his art. His studies advanced further in old age, and he lived to be 89. He wrote his own treatise on painting, titled The Painting Principles of Shicun, which stated:

    Throughout history, painters have used water to blend colors, an unchanging method. The dry brush technique, however, was not created by ancient masters. In my youth, I studied under Shitai, carefully examining each tree and rock, studying the intention behind them. After a long time, I gradually gained some understanding. By quietly reflecting, I realized that every stroke was like Shitai's, still within the bounds of ancient masters. Then, I exhausted days and nights thinking, and suddenly came up with a different idea. Occasionally using the dry brush technique, I seemed to have a different charm, shedding the mundane. Gradually, it became more refined, fortunately not despised by connoisseurs. Truly due to hard work, not completely lost, I recorded the painting principles and kept them in a box, to await fellow enthusiasts.

    444

    按画诀十则：一曰立意，二曰取神，三曰运笔，四曰造景，五曰位置，六曰避俗，七曰点缀，八曰渴染，九曰款识，十曰图章。其渴染法云：墨少着水，重磨。用秃湖颖，不着水，即蘸焦墨。先用别纸试，微润，轻拂画上，笔笔匀，可染二三次，惟无笔痕为妙，颇有秀色。凡五叶树，俱用渴笔实染双钩，叶白者不染。房舍有瓦草处染，无瓦草处空白。室内人物器具空白，周围俱用渴笔剔清。每一石止渴染皴处，石顶空白，石根宜用重染。大山平坡皆然。远山先用炭为轮廓，外用渴染，天气渐与之接。远山空白，山根用渴染。波水溪江，俱用平直笔密画出，有聚有散，俱用渴染托出。云烟断续，须轻染，渐渐不见乃妙。非有定，惟画者自裁。有墨画处，此实笔也；无墨画处，以云气衬，此虚中之实也。树石房廊等，皆有白处，又实中之虚也。实者虚之，虚者实之，满幅皆笔迹到处，却又不见笔痕，但觉一片灵气浮动于其上。

    Explanation

    444

    According to the Ten Rules of Painting: First, establish the intention; second, capture the essence; third, handle the brushwork; fourth, compose the scenery; fifth, arrange the elements; sixth, avoid clichés; seventh, add details; eighth, use the dry brush technique; ninth, inscribe the signature; tenth, apply the seal. The dry brush technique is described as follows: grind the ink with less water to make it thicker. Use a worn brush without water and dip it in concentrated ink. First, test the brush on a separate piece of paper to achieve a light, moist effect, then lightly apply it to the painting, ensuring each stroke is even. This can be done two or three times, with the best result being when no brushstrokes are visible, giving a refined appearance. For all five-leaf trees, use the dry brush technique to outline and shade the leaves, leaving white leaves untouched. Shade the parts of houses that have tiles or grass, leaving the rest blank. Leave the interior of rooms and objects blank and use the dry brush technique to define the edges. For each rock, use the dry brush technique on the texture, leaving the top blank and heavily shading the base. Large mountains and flat slopes follow the same technique. For distant mountains, first outline them with charcoal and then use the dry brush technique to blend them into the background. Leave distant mountains blank and shade their bases. Use straight, dense strokes to depict water bodies, with clusters and spreads, and use the dry brush technique to define their edges. Lightly shade clouds and mist so that they gradually fade away, creating a natural effect. There is no fixed rule; the artist must decide based on their own judgment. The areas with ink are solid brushwork, while the areas without ink are virtual space filled with cloud and mist, which is a form of solid within the void. Trees, rocks, and buildings all have blank areas, which represent the void within the solid. Balance solid and void elements in the painting. The entire painting is covered with brushstrokes, yet no brushstrokes are visible, creating a sense of ethereal energy.

    445

    其论如此。此石村变化前人之法，所谓遗貌而取神者也。
    (七如氏曰：书法以右军为圣，至颜鲁公而一变。诗以少陵为宗，至退之而亦一变。古人谓文有变，而不止于文也，且不止书与诗也，即画亦然。画凡不知其几变，或变南北之宗，或变大小之体。盖其所不变者，理与法；而其所变者，势也，亦运会之使然也。代有人焉，翘然崛起，推陈出新，卓卓自立于数千百年。后先相望之顷，其名不以贵胄掩，迹不以穷约晦。如且园石村者，不多觏也。

    Explanation

    445

    His theory is as follows. Shicun changes the methods of his predecessors, which is what is meant by abandoning the outward appearance and grasping the inner spirit. (Sevenlike says: Calligraphy was considered sacred with Wang Xizhi, but changed with Yan Zhenqing. Poetry was considered authoritative with Du Fu, but also changed with Han Yu. Ancient people said that literature changes, but not only in literature, and not only in calligraphy and poetry, but also in painting. Painting has undergone countless changes, some changing the northern and southern schools, others changing the large and small styles. What remains unchanged is the principles and methods; what changes is the style, influenced by the times. Throughout history, there have been individuals who rise up, innovating and standing out over thousands of years. Their names are not overshadowed by noble birth, and their deeds are not concealed by poverty or low status. Such as Shicun of And Garden, one does not often encounter them.

    446

    袁行川曰：七如精于画，故言之委曲详尽如此。己酉余于厂市购得且园先生《钟进士伏鬼图》一幅，见之能令人畏。七如有石村手迹，不轻示人。余素不愿夺人之所好，亦惟有心艳而已。)

    王浩
    王浩，江夏秀才。性常逸而不放，情多喜而忤，少年不检，褫其巾。娶妻有色，每出必鐍其户，恐邻人窥其内美。妻死，遂垂帘于市，卖卜于三佛阁下，语多奇中。虽敝衣破履，作衣冠之容，跬步不苟。道上拾只字，必衲诸袖，归而焚之，积而成捆，投诸江，再拜而送焉，曰：“古圣古贤，济世心血。”路遇庙寺，必拜，群儿环而笑之，毫不以为怪。
    楚十万户，凡人家寿辰，必登堂祝寿，人皆称之“生日王”。王必具寿仪，仪何？则面筹数十，悉其平时拜寿所得，食不暇给，而蓄之者。拜毕而献曰：“为某某公某夫人添筹。”计十筹亦值半两。其腰缠之筹，盖尝数百云。王生家不举火者四十年，终日醉啖。卜则在寅卯，炊时已之寿家作宾去矣。

    Explanation

    446

    Yuan Xingchuan said: Qiu Ruchuan is skilled in painting, hence his descriptions are so detailed and thorough. In the year Jiyou, I purchased a painting titled "The Scholar-official Zhu Subduing a Ghost" by the esteemed artist Mr. Qiuyuan from a market in the city. Seeing it, one cannot help but feel a sense of awe. Qiu Ruchuan also has calligraphy by Shi Cun, which he does not easily show to others. I have never wished to take away others' pleasures, and so I only admire it from afar.

    Wang Hao
    Wang Hao, a scholar from Jiangxia, is usually carefree but not unrestrained, often joyful yet sometimes defiant. In his youth, he was undisciplined and once had his headband removed. He married a beautiful wife who would lock the door every time she went out, fearing that neighbors might see her inner beauty. After her death, he hung a curtain in the market and sold divination services at the Three Buddha Pavilion, often predicting things accurately. Despite wearing tattered clothes and worn-out shoes, he maintained the appearance of a gentleman, every step deliberate. On the road, he would pick up scraps of paper, sew them into his sleeves, and upon returning home, burn them in bundles and cast them into the river, bowing twice before sending them off, saying, "These are the blood and sweat of ancient sages and virtuous men, dedicated to saving the world." Whenever he encountered temples or shrines, he would bow, and children would gather around and laugh at him, but he did not consider it strange.

    In the Chu region, among ten thousand households, anyone celebrating a birthday would invite Wang Hao to their home to bless them, and people called him "Birthday Wang." Wang Hao would bring a set of birthday offerings, which consisted of dozens of wooden sticks (筹), all collected from his previous birthday celebrations, too many to eat himself. After the blessings, he would present them, saying, "For Master So-and-so and Lady So-and-so, add to your sticks." Ten sticks were worth half a tael of silver. The number of sticks he carried around, it is said, could amount to several hundred. Wang Hao lived a life of poverty for forty years, drinking and eating continuously. His divinations took place between 3 AM and 5 AM, and after cooking, he would go to the birthday celebrant as a guest.

    447

    余宰江邑，舆出，时见王生立道旁，恭而且敬。余心识其非常，而狃于街评，不便与之通讯。后被议，兼之有荆监河工三年，去省垣。归而觅之，庄岳闤闠之间，绝无王生之迹。偶与邑人谭子道及，云已作古。其传闻有乡人在安徽省遇诸途，欢然作别云：“为人家作笔佣。”并寄书其家门。归而知其已死，甚为骇异。其家书中云“已为安徽某县城隍”，并示其房某处有藏面筹数十，作谢寄书者酒资云。

    黔中儿
    江国瑞，黔之威宁州人，家贫业屦。娶妻张氏，三乳而举五子，不十年皆龆龀。一人屦，遂为八口累，于是困惫滋甚。夫妻着败絮，五子倮焉。终日饮粥糜，且不重食。继而妻病瘵死，遂鳏。父兮兼母职，更难以堪。长次曰万清、永清，三四曰长清、庆清，五曰福清。冬则五子群卧草中，江视日之蚤暮，抱五子而就曝。日出东，则列其子皆墙西；日转西，则移其子于墙东。呱呱杂沓，几不可耐，亦无如何。馀暇犹织屦。

    Explanation

    447

    During my tenure as the magistrate of Jiang County, I would travel in a carriage, and I often saw Wang Sheng standing by the roadside, showing both respect and reverence. I recognized him as an extraordinary person, but I was restrained by the gossip of the streets and did not find it convenient to communicate with him. Later, I was summoned for an inquiry, and coincidentally, I had been involved in river construction projects in Jing for three years, so I left the provincial capital. Upon returning, I searched for him in the bustling marketplaces of the city, but there was no trace of Wang Sheng. I happened to mention this to a local named Tan Zi, who told me that Wang Sheng had already passed away. There was a rumor that a villager from Anhui Province had met him on the road, and they parted happily, with Wang Sheng saying, "I am working as a hired scribe for a family." He also sent a letter to his family. Upon receiving the letter, I learned that he had already died, which greatly surprised me. In his letter, he wrote, "I have become the city god of a certain county in Anhui," and he mentioned that there were hidden face tokens in a certain room, which were given as a token of gratitude for the messenger's expenses.

    In the region of Guizhou, there was a man named Jiang Guorui from Weining Prefecture. He was poor and made shoes for a living. He married a woman surnamed Zhang, who gave birth to five sons over three pregnancies. Within ten years, all the children had grown into toddlers. With one person making shoes, the family of eight became increasingly burdened, and their hardships grew more severe. Both husband and wife wore tattered clothes, and the five children were often naked. They ate porridge all day, and there was never enough food. Later, his wife died of tuberculosis, leaving him a widower. Taking on the role of both father and mother, he found it even more difficult to bear. His eldest and second sons were named Wanqing and Yongqing, the third and fourth were named Changqing and Qingqing, and the fifth was named Fucheng. In winter, the five children would often sleep together in the grass. Jiang watched the sun rise and set, carrying the children to bask in the sunlight. When the sun rose in the east, he arranged the children on the west side of the wall; when the sun set in the west, he moved them to the east side. The children's cries were incessant, almost unbearable, and there was nothing he could do. In his spare time, he still wove shoes.

    448

    万清年十五而伟，永清亦如之，遂樵于山。日得柴两担，售于市，可敌其父五日屦，如是江稍裕。三年而长清亦峥嵘起，亦能樵。万清兼猎事，獐麂野豕，偶一得之，可易贯钱斗粟。江室中有大布之衣，干糇之粟，自今日始。
    城西坪忽有虎患，官捕不能得，断樵路。万清乃谓永清曰：“兄会须格杀此獠，恐其猛，弟当助一臂力。”永即应。万往，而长、庆亦欲与俱，兄诃之返，乃阴随之。万、永至，俟于嵎。虎来万出，虎扑万，万以手握其腋下皮，举而立，虎亦立，永即出，曳其尾。于时虎不得奋，相视而雄。忽长、庆猝至，左右各捉一虎蹄扭之。虎怒而起，众复按，虎仆，以虎口置地上揉捺之。虎大怒，腾而奔。众方欲逐，虎颔下忽贯一矢，大吼如雷，声震陵谷，跃入危崖而毙。但闻树杪一儿呼曰：“诸兄酣斗时，打成一片，弟无处下手。幸而纵去，乘隙而中之。”乃知其为五弟福清也。

    Explanation

    448

    When Wan Qing was fifteen, he had grown tall and strong. Yong Qing was the same, and so they began to chop wood in the mountains. Each day, they could gather two loads of firewood, which they sold in the market, earning enough to match what their father earned in five days making shoes. Thus, the Jiang family gradually became more prosperous. After three years, Chang Qing also grew rapidly and could also chop wood. Wan Qing also engaged in hunting, occasionally catching wild animals like roe deer, muntjacs, and wild boars, which could be exchanged for strings of coins or a measure of grain. The Jiang household now had coarse cloth clothing and dry grain, starting from today.

    Suddenly, there was a tiger menace in the western field of the city. The authorities tried to capture the tiger but failed, cutting off the path for chopping wood. Wan Qing then said to Yong Qing, "Brother, you must kill this beast. I fear it is fierce, so I will lend you a hand." Yong Qing agreed. Wan Qing went, and Chang Qing and Qing also wanted to join, but their brother scolded them and sent them back, though they secretly followed. When Wan Qing and Yong Qing arrived, they waited in a secluded spot. When the tiger approached, Wan Qing stepped out. The tiger pounced on Wan Qing, but he grabbed the tiger by the skin under its armpit, lifting it up, and the tiger stood up. Yong Qing then stepped out and grabbed the tiger's tail. At this point, the tiger could not struggle, and they looked at each other, both appearing fierce. Suddenly, Chang Qing and Qing arrived, and they each grabbed one of the tiger's hooves and twisted them. The tiger became furious and tried to stand up again. The group held it down again, and the tiger fell. They placed the tiger's mouth on the ground and rubbed and pressed it. The tiger roared loudly, like thunder, shaking the mountains and valleys, and then jumped into a dangerous cliff and died. Only then did they hear a child calling from the treetops, "When my brothers were fighting fiercely and everything was in chaos, I didn't know where to start. Fortunately, I let them go and took the opportunity to shoot it." They realized that the child was Wu Di, the fifth brother, Fu Qing.

    449

    会川苗骚扰，威镇剿捕，万清兄弟皆入伍。万清首登苗寨，破其碉，得其首级九颗，悬之腰间而返。威镇曰：“好男儿！”擢为裨将。请于上，迁参戎，褒赐有差。其昆弟四人，累立军功，皆官守御。每出战，五人蝉联而入，势若长蛇，而福之药机，犹百发百中。今国瑞年七十，健饭，五子迎养于官，终日憨憨笑，以为少年时所念不到有今日也。

    常正吾
    常正吾，不详其乡贯，率其二子以锻铁，居即墨。工于射，往往为旅客护装资，号为“保镖”。偶登镇阅兵即墨，正吾旁睨之，少所许可。时老矣，或强其一射。正吾选弓矢，植弱条百步外，三发三中。
    又述其出游时，一老贾聘与俱，途遇不类，遽止逆旅中。使贾伪为师，教之射。悬鸡街衢，扬言曰：“贯左目。”乃故中右目，贾佯怒，正吾唯唯承教，不类者咋舌去。人由此奇之。后知其为前明开平侯常遇春之裔，其在即墨，盖避地云。寻卒，葬城东。

    Explanation

    449

    In the region of Huichuan, the Miao people caused disturbances, and General Wei led the suppression efforts. The brothers Wan Qing and others joined the military. Wan Qing led the first assault on a Miao stronghold, breached their fortifications, and captured nine heads, which he hung around his waist before returning. General Wei remarked, "What a fine man!" and promoted Wan Qing to the rank of deputy general. He requested a promotion from the emperor, who elevated him to the rank of colonel and bestowed rewards according to his merit. The four brothers, including Wan Qing, accumulated numerous military achievements and were appointed as local military officers. Whenever they went into battle, the five brothers entered in succession, their formation resembling a long snake, and their archery was always accurate, hitting the target every time. Today, Guo Rui is seventy years old, strong and able to eat well, and his five sons support him through official means. He spends his days smiling foolishly, thinking that the things he dreamed of in his youth have come to pass.

    Chang Zhengwu
    Chang Zhengwu, whose hometown is unknown, led his two sons in forging iron and lived in Jimo. He was skilled in archery and often protected travelers' belongings, earning him the nickname "bodyguard." Once, he attended a review of troops in Jimo. Chang Zhengwu observed the proceedings from the side, rarely approving of others. By then, he was old, and someone forced him to demonstrate his archery skills. Chang Zhengwu selected a bow and arrow, set up a weak target at a hundred paces away, and hit it three times in a row. He also recounted a story from when he was traveling. An elderly merchant hired him to accompany him, and they encountered someone suspicious, so they stopped at an inn. Chang Zhengwu had the merchant pretend to be a teacher and instruct the suspicious person in archery. He hung a chicken in the street and declared, "Shoot through the left eye." Instead, he deliberately hit the right eye. The merchant pretended to be angry, and Chang Zhengwu nodded in agreement and accepted the instruction. The suspicious person left with their tongue hanging out, astonished. People found him remarkable. Later, it was discovered that Chang Zhengwu was a descendant of Chang Yuchun, the Prince of Kai Ping from the Ming dynasty, and he had come to Jimo to seek refuge. Soon after, he died and was buried in the east of the city.

    450

    康熙甲申之前岁，其二子语所厚曰：“大祲将至，不可留！”负其锻具以行。

    霍璟燕
    休宁汪某藏书，家有阁十所，环以水，盖恐祝融回禄之劫，故人迹罕到，鬼狐遂凭之以为居。尝登阁视，则缥缃卷帙不理也，即理之而仍乱。
    霍璟燕豪气磊落，与汪固戚党，有书癖，遂假榻于其阁。有小舫度之，朝发而夕返。霍于是偃仰其中，如在瑯环洞府也。如是者非一日。忽当亭午，闻架上书簌簌响，霍睨视，乃一小狐如犬而人，手持一册累累行，力不胜书。霍叱之，狐弃书去。霍起拾书，则《龟筴传》。霍笑曰：“彼绥绥者，亦留心于数学耶？”移时，一白须叟扶杖来前，霍起，延之坐，知其为狐。询之，叟曰：“秦中白姓。”倾谈颇蕴藉，霍敬礼焉。见案头置《周易》，曰：“善此乎？”霍曰：“然。”叟举一卦问霍，霍为述其师说。叟曰：“章句之学也。至于义蕴则全非。

    Explanation

    450

    In the year before the Jia Shen year of the Kangxi era, two sons told someone close to them, "A great calamity is about to come, we cannot stay!" They carried their blacksmithing tools and left.

    Ho Jingyan
    Wang, from Xiuning, had a vast collection of books. His home had ten pavilions, each surrounded by water, presumably to prevent the disaster of fire, which made it rarely visited by people. Thus, ghosts and fox spirits inhabited them. Once, Ho climbed up to one of the pavilions and found the books in disarray, even when he tried to organize them, they remained chaotic.

    Ho Jingyan was a man of great courage and broad-mindedness, and he was closely related to Wang. He had a passion for books and often spent the night in Wang's pavilion. There was a small boat that would take him there, and he would leave in the morning and return in the evening. In this way, Ho would recline and relax within the pavilion, feeling as if he were in a heavenly abode. This continued for several days. Suddenly, at noon, he heard a rustling sound from the bookshelves. He glanced up and saw a small fox, resembling a dog and human-like, holding a bulky book and struggling to carry it. Ho scolded the fox, and it abandoned the book and fled. Ho picked up the book and found it to be the Book of Changes (I Ching). He smiled and said, "That wandering creature, is it also interested in mathematics?" After a while, an elderly man with white hair and a cane approached. Ho stood up, invited him to sit, and realized he was a fox spirit. He asked the man, and he replied, "I am Bai from the region of Qin." They engaged in a refined conversation, and Ho showed him great respect. He noticed a copy of the Yijing (Book of Changes) on the table and asked, "Are you good at this?" Ho replied, "Yes." The man then presented a hexagram to Ho and asked him to explain it. Ho recounted the teachings of his master. The man said, "That is the study of individual words and phrases. As for the deeper meaning, it is entirely different."

    451

    ”霍曰：“先生诚精于《易》，能先知否？”叟曰：“试指一字。”霍即指与天地合其德“德”字。叟曰：“子欲问行人乎？”霍曰：“然。何时当至？”叟曰：“十四日当至。”霍曰：“恐他事羁绊。”叟曰：“心为身主，渠一心要来。”霍问故，叟曰：“德字双立人，固行人也。有‘十四’字，故云其日。下‘一心’字，固知其必来。”霍大悦服，拜求其学。曰：“可斋戒四十九日，拜老夫。”四十九日，霍如其言，叟曰：“孺子可教。”乃为剖析《河》、《洛》精义，皆出程朱之外。因及天文乐律、奇门太乙、六壬诸术，曰：“此不过《易》之一端耳。”
    居阁中五年，霍尽得其秘。叟曰：“技至此，缘亦尽。我将移去，慎斯术也。非其人而误传，与得其人而不传，皆失之。后十年戊申，汝游北豫间，当三月扃户，不见一人，否则祸及身。”霍谨奉教。自此谈数学多奇中。

    Explanation

    451

    Translation:

    Huo asked, "Sir, are you truly skilled in the I Ching and able to foresee the future?" The old man replied, "Try pointing to a character." Huo pointed to the character "德" (virtue) in the phrase "与天地合其德" (harmonizing virtue with heaven and earth). The old man said, "Do you wish to inquire about a traveler?" Huo replied, "Yes. When will he arrive?" The old man said, "He will arrive on the fourteenth day." Huo said, "I fear he may be detained by other matters." The old man said, "The heart is the master of the body; he is determined to come." Huo asked for the reason, and the old man explained, "The character '德' (virtue) has two '立人' (standing people), which signifies a traveler. The characters '十四' (fourteen) indicate the day. The characters '一心' (one heart) show that he is determined to come." Huo was greatly pleased and convinced, and he bowed to seek the old man's teachings. The old man said, "You may fast for forty-nine days and come to pay respects to me." After forty-nine days, Huo followed his instructions, and the old man said, "The child is worthy of being taught." He then explained the profound meanings of the Book of the Yellow River and Book of the Luo River, which were beyond the teachings of Cheng Yi and Zhu Xi. He also discussed astronomy, music theory, the Yi Men Tai Yi (Mysterious Gate Supreme One), and the Liu Ren (Six Celestial Stems) systems, saying, "These are merely one aspect of the I Ching."

    Huo stayed in the study for five years and mastered all the secrets. The old man said, "Your skills have reached this point, and our缘分 (fate) has also come to an end. I will depart, so be cautious with this knowledge. Misapplying it to the wrong person or failing to pass it on to the right person would both be a mistake. In ten years, during the year of Wu Shen, when you travel to the northern Henan region, you should lock the door in March and not see anyone, or else disaster will befall you." Huo carefully followed his instructions. From then on, his discussions of mathematics often hit the mark.

    452

    十年，旅寓河汴，果有大名妖逆八卦教之变，多所刑诛，半年始定。霍不及于难，叟之力也。霍游京师，缙绅与之游，言数奇验。有李某从之，得其术不精，能预知人姓名，亦奇也。噫！人为万物之灵，苟专心一志，将希圣希贤，有何不可？辟之灵明，彼巢居知风，穴居知雨之伦，尚可臻此，人奈何自画为耶？

    水烟技
    韩文懿公慕庐，有烟酒之癖。或问曰：“必不得已而去，于斯二者，何先？”文懿踌躇半晌曰：“去酒。”掌翰林时，曾命门人作淡巴菰诗，诗多不传。海宁陈文贞有句云：“似吐仙人火，初疑异草熏。”又：“味从无味得，情岂有情牵。”又：“吸虚能化实，尝苦有馀甘。”今又有兰州水烟，余曾有句云：“猩唇气吸西江水，彤管云蒸北固潮。”盖须眉巾帼，嗜好约略相似。

    Explanation

    452

    During the tenth year, he wandered and resided in the regions of the Yellow River and Bian River, where indeed a major rebellion involving the Eight Trigrams Sect occurred, resulting in many executions, and it took half a year to stabilize the situation. Ho was not affected by the difficulties, thanks to the efforts of the old man. Ho traveled to the capital, where the elite socialized with him, and his predictions were proven accurate. There was a man named Li who followed him, but he did not master the technique perfectly; however, he could predict people's names, which was also remarkable. Alas! Humans are the most intelligent of all creatures; if one focuses one's mind and will, one can aspire to be like sages and virtuous people—what is there to prevent it? Like a luminous spirit, even those animals that live in nests and know the wind, and those that live in holes and know the rain, can reach such heights; why then do humans limit themselves?

    The Art of Smoking Water Pipes
    Han Wenyi, the Duke of Mu Lu, had a fondness for smoking and drinking. Someone asked him, "If you must give up one of these two habits, which would you choose first?" After hesitating for a while, Han replied, "I would give up alcohol." When he was in charge of the Hanlin Academy, he once commissioned his disciples to write poems about tobacco, but most of these poems did not survive. Teng Wen-zhen of Hai Ning wrote, "It seems like the breath of a fairy, at first one suspects it is the scent of exotic herbs." He also wrote, "The flavor comes from nothing, and the emotion is not bound by conventional ties." And, "The act of smoking can transform the void into substance, and the bitter into something sweet." Nowadays, there is also the water pipe from Lanzhou, for which I once wrote, "The breath of a red-lipped creature sucks in the water of the Western River, and the vapor rises from the northern tide like a vermilion tube." It seems that both men and women share similar tastes and preferences.

    453

    有楚人周子畏者，好水烟，其技遂以水烟名。年六十游京师，饮器高三四尺许，白铁为之，腹可容升水。日常不嗜，嗜必尽八两，呼呼欲移晷。周吸罄，初不见口鼻中出一缕也。必择静空一间，纸垩光洁，无漏罅处，亦无风入处。周入室，观者随之。周踞坐，先伸颈垂首，张口照地，一吻吐落一圈，大如簸。再以舌抵腭上，出齿际，则成一大蝠。如是再，再而三。但见蝠飞圈外，圈套蝠中，愈出愈多，真如月晕日环，幻化出百千万亿圈子。或粘壁间，或施地上，或印人衣履，或套人头项，不可思议。

    Explanation

    453

    A Man from Chu Named Zhou Zhiwei

    There was a man from Chu named Zhou Zhiwei who loved smoking water pipes, and his skill eventually became known as water pipe smoking. At the age of sixty, he traveled to the capital city, where he had a drinking vessel that was three to four feet high, made of white iron, and capable of holding a sheng of water. He did not smoke every day, but when he did, he would consume eight taels, smoking continuously for a long time. When Zhou finished smoking, no smoke came out of his mouth or nose at first. He must choose a quiet, empty room with paper walls that were smooth and clean, with no gaps or cracks, and no wind should enter. When Zhou entered the room, spectators followed him. Zhou sat cross-legged, first extending his neck and lowering his head, then opening his mouth to the ground, and with one breath, a circle fell out, as large as a winnowing basket. Then he touched his tongue to the roof of his mouth, and it emerged between his teeth, forming a large bat. He did this again, and then a third time. What was seen was a bat flying outside the circle, and the circle enclosing the bat. The more he did it, the more they appeared, truly like the halo around the moon or the rings around the sun, transforming into countless circles. Some stuck to the walls, some landed on the ground, some printed on people's clothes and shoes, and some encircled people's heads and necks. It was inconceivable.

    454

    既而淙淙然，直蒸屈槅，又复羃历而下，钩旋宛转。虽有精于绘云者，无其象；精于绘水者，无其色。及至地，色较淡，而丝缕倍多于前，然而一平如掌，几榻不能碍以高下。观者已置身叆叇之上，又若泛舟波涛之面也。逾时，中忽高起如浮屠，旁若屋宇。淡处乱处，历历直上者，则丛树修柯，掩映阴翳。室四隅烟复连蜷裹入，俨然雉堞连亘，女墙睥睨，其间往往如人马旗帜，点点如豆。约一炊刻，然后霏微敛散，城薄人稀，马行帜拔，屋舍荒落。独一塔危然耸峙，居中直上，乃愈起而愈细，飘飘乎无纤尘之留坐隅也。
    昔苏公登蓬莱阁，快睹海市，虽曰大观，亦未必如周子今日之呼吸三昧也，幻化一室。噫，技至此乎！

    454

    Shortly after, a continuous, flowing sound was heard, rising directly through obstacles and then gradually descending in a winding, twisting motion. Even those skilled in painting clouds could not capture its form, and those skilled in painting water could not depict its color. By the time it reached the ground, the color was lighter, and the threads or strands were more numerous than before, yet it was as flat as a palm, with neither tables nor chairs obstructing its flatness. The observer felt as if standing in the midst of clouds and also as if sailing on the surface of waves. After some time, a central structure suddenly rose like a pagoda, surrounded by buildings. In the light and chaotic areas, those that rose clearly and directly were tall trees with long branches, shaded and partially hidden. Smoke from the corners of the room wrapped around and entered, creating a continuous wall with battlements and watchtowers, and small walls and watchtowers. Often resembling people, horses, and flags, small as beans. About the time it takes to cook rice, the scene gradually dissipated, with the city becoming sparse and less populated, and houses becoming desolate. A single tower stood tall and prominent, rising straight up in the center, becoming higher and finer, floating away without leaving the slightest dust.

    In the past, Su Shi ascended the Pavilion of Penglai and quickly saw the sea market, although it was called a grand view, it may not be as profound as Zhou Zi's today's mastery of the essence, transforming a room. Ah, has the skill reached this level!

    455

    陈抱拙
    瞽者陈抱拙，东平人。先是秀才，少工诗，善琵琶。又癖于拳勇，贫不给，遂弃此一领巾。
    会乡有斗狠者，陈负气往，以梃伤其目。邻欲诉诸官，陈惧，以灰自迷二目，遂瞽，事乃寝。晚年益困，乃善其指拨之妙，游缙绅间。又系一教诗小牌于琵琶轴上，人目为狂瞽。然其食志也亦雅。其诗无存稿，佳者同人口志之。如咏半钱诗云：
    制来九府一钱兮，圆样如何仅半圭。留得看囊终是涩，纵教入市不成提。
    用将鬼使难推磨，持去酬君也弃泥。空对弯弯残月影，好同破镜落窗西。
    瘿道人一绝云：
    道人何事气豪粗，欲比骊龙挂一珠。自窃长生丹药后，项间长带火葫芦。
    咏菊花枕云：
    谁把零星傲骨香，寒来收拾入缥缃。篱边一醉三秋月，爱煞渊明不下床。
    咏芦笔云：
    几点寒芦未吐芳，恰如彩笔倚方塘。撩波影似含毫思，载雪花如入梦香。
    秋水一泓溪砚古，碧霞千尺锦笺长。有时被雁偷衔去，写破蓼天一两行。

    Explanation

    455

    Blind Man Chen Baocuo

    Chen Baocuo, a blind man from Dongping, was once a scholar. As a young man, he was skilled in poetry and proficient in playing the pipa. He was also obsessed with martial arts. Due to poverty, he abandoned his status as a scholar.

    When there was a fight in the village, Chen went out of anger and struck someone with a stick, causing injury to their eyes. Neighbors wanted to report it to the authorities, but Chen was afraid. He blinded himself with ash, became blind, and the matter was then dropped. In his later years, he became even more impoverished, but he became skilled in the subtle movements of his fingers and roamed among the gentry. He attached a small sign advertising teaching poetry to the axis of his pipa, and people called him a mad blind man. However, his way of living was also elegant. His poems have no surviving manuscripts, but the good ones were memorized by others. For example, his poem about a half coin says:

    "A coin made of nine parts of metal, how can it be only half a gu? Even if kept in a bag, it remains difficult to use. Even if taken to market, it cannot be picked up. Using it would be like trying to push a millstone with a ghost. Holding it to pay someone would be like throwing mud. Empty, facing the curved shadow of a crescent moon, like a broken mirror falling on the west side of the window."

    The poem of a hunchbacked Taoist says:

    "What business does the Taoist have to be so bold and rough? Wanting to compare himself to a dragon hanging a pearl. After stealing the elixir of immortality, a fire gourd hangs around his neck."

    His poem about a chrysanthemum pillow says:

    "Who put the fragrant bones of pride together? In winter, they are collected into silk books. Drunk by the fence under the autumn moon, love it so much that even Tao Yuanming wouldn't get out of bed."

    His poem about a reed pen says:

    "A few cold reeds have not yet bloomed, just like a colorful brush leaning against a square pond. The ripples seem to contain the thoughts of a brush, carrying snowflakes like entering a dreamy fragrance. The autumn water in the ancient inkstone, thousand-foot green clouds on the long brocade paper. Sometimes stolen by geese, writing a few lines on the sky of water chestnut."

    456

    (曲阜颜幼客，有《怀抱拙》一绝云：“白发新声贾扣哀，赵官明月寺门苔。诗名不合谢榛并，也作人间眇秀才。”)

    孔小山
    曲阜孔小山，圣裔也。善鼓琴，慕音者恒不得一聆其操。孔有十绝、二十四忌，稍不当可，则拂弦而起，是小山之音之希也。尝抱琴于空山阒静、人迹罕到之区，然后一弹再鼓。同人恶之，莫能伊何。
    汶上赵子釐性诙谐，多力有胆气，长鬣盈腮，因自号为“小虬髯”。曲多葭莩亲，当宴谈，辄言小山事，而小山固未与赵觌面也。一日，闻小山游石门寺，石门即子美访张氏隐居处。山深藤萝满峪，春尽迷望，如锦步障十里许。赵悄行，腰间悬椎，跨骡往。抵寺问，头陀告曰：“适携焦琴，并奚童山后去了。”赵絷骡，步入山。满岚翠滴，香气袭人。盘曲五六里，微闻指拨声。继见一人坐石，横琴膝上，旁立一奴，执杖系葫芦，飘然如仙。赵捉椎咤叱，响应陵谷。

    Explanation

    456

    In Qufu, Yan Youke composed a short poem titled "Embracing Simplicity" which reads: "New sounds of white hair, knocking on sorrow, the moonlit temple gate in Zhao. Poetic reputation not matching Xie Zhen, also becomes a mediocre scholar in the human world."

    Kong Xiaoshan, a descendant of Confucius from Qufu, was skilled in playing the qin (a traditional Chinese zither). Those who admired his music could never hear him play, as he had ten absolutes and twenty-four taboos. If even slightly out of place, he would stop playing and leave, making his music rare and elusive. He often played his qin in remote, quiet mountains where few people ventured, and after playing once or twice, his fellow musicians would be dissatisfied but could not do anything about it.

    Zhao Zili from Weishang was known for his wit and boldness, with a long beard that gave him the nickname "Little Dragon Beard." He had many relatives in Qufu and often spoke about Kong Xiaoshan during gatherings, although Kong Xiaoshan had never met Zhao. One day, he heard that Kong was visiting Shimen Temple, which was where Du Fu once visited Zhang's hermitage. The mountain was deep, covered with vines and lush foliage, and in the spring, it was so dense that it seemed like a ten-mile-long brocade screen. Zhao quietly walked, with a mallet hanging at his waist, riding a donkey to the temple. When he arrived, a monk told him, "He just left with his qin and a servant boy, heading into the mountains." Zhao tied his donkey and entered the mountain. The air was filled with the scent of pine and the sound of birds. After winding through the mountain for several miles, he faintly heard the sound of fingers plucking the strings. Then he saw a person sitting on a rock, a qin lying across his knees, with a servant standing beside him holding a staff and a gourd, looking ethereal like a celestial being. Zhao grabbed his mallet and shouted, and the echo resonated through the valleys.

    457

    小山惊起，奴亦弃杖。赵曰：“取买山钱献我，否则敲断狗骨子！”赵以椎击岩边石，磞然而坠，火星滚滚落山隅中。孔泣跪曰：“野游至此，未曾携得一文。”赵踞坐，喝曰：“脱剥尔皮，以代钞用。”二人觳觫，自褫其衣，堆于赵前。赵指葫芦曰：“何物？”小山曰：“酒。敬进大王。”赵提饮，一吸而尽，又指琴曰：“黑漆漆者复何物？”小山曰：“琴。曷为大王鼓之？”赵曰：“鼓。”小山跪而奏“淋零”之曲。赵不乐，以椎指其头，令再鼓。小山又为“涂山大会”诸曲。

    Explanation

    457

    Xiao Shan jumped up in surprise, and his servant dropped the staff. Zhao said, "Bring me the money to buy a mountain, or I'll break your dog's bones!" Zhao struck the rock at the edge of the cliff with a mallet, and it fell with a loud sound, sparks rolling down into the corner of the mountain. Kong wept and knelt, saying, "I was out in the wild and didn't bring any money." Zhao sat down and shouted, "Strip your skin to use as currency!" The two men trembled with fear and removed their clothes, piling them in front of Zhao. Zhao pointed to the gourd and asked, "What is this?" Xiao Shan said, "It's wine. I respectfully offer it to Your Majesty." Zhao took a drink and finished it in one gulp. Then he pointed to the qin and asked, "What is this black, shiny thing?" Xiao Shan said, "It's a qin. Why not play it for Your Majesty?" Zhao said, "Play it." Xiao Shan knelt and played the "Lín Líng" tune. Zhao was not pleased and hit Xiao Shan on the head with the mallet, ordering him to play again. Xiao Shan then played the "Grand Gathering at Mount Tu" and other tunes.

    458

    久之，夕阳在山，而孔犹顾影效《广陵散》，真不啻嵇康之就刑时也。赵起，大吼，轮椎沉沉，若电转霆惊，排奡穿藤花而去。孔狼狈归。后孔微闻其事，碎琴裂囊，誓不复弄。

    聂小玉
    聂小玉，蜀人也。为优伶游京师。艳绝，眉间有媚风，姣女子不及其冶。所演多秦腔。即村俚剧唱，登场必另开生面，于是群噪一时。王孙贵戚，相与持赠，缠头盈千累万，人不愿封万户侯，但愿一登首邱而死也。

    Explanation

    458

    After a long time, as the sun set behind the mountains, Kong continued to play the Guangling San, looking back at his own reflection, no less than Ji Kang at the moment of his execution. Zhaohui stood up, roared loudly, pounded rhythmically, and left forcefully, like lightning turning and thunder startling, pushing through the vine flowers. Kong returned in a disarray. Later, when Kong heard of the incident, he smashed his zither, tore his bag, and swore never to play again.

    Nie Xiaoyu
    Nie Xiaoyu was from Shu. She traveled to the capital as a performer. She was extremely beautiful, with a charming breeze in her eyebrows, and even the most beautiful women could not compare to her allure. She mostly performed Qin opera. Even in village and rustic plays, when she took the stage, she always brought a fresh and unique style, and thus, she became a sensation for a time. Aristocratic and noble families gave her gifts, with thousands upon thousands of items, and people preferred her performances over being granted a fiefdom, wishing only to die after just one performance.

    459

    苏州翟秋山，以不第留滞京都，名士也。日者观剧，见聂心喜。归寓，驰想不置。由是戏上有聂，园中有翟。聂出而翟则昂首而盼，聂入而翟则掩面而卧。如是者非一日。聂于场上，未尝不转盼留神，异其钟情之独挚。
    某日演戏于翡翠园，日未昃，聂入，见翟已徘徊于众几间。聂前致词，曰：“晨餐也未？何来恁早耶？”翟欣然答曰：“秀色可疗人饥，恐迟—刻少见一刻耳。”遂告姓氏居址。曲终人散，翟归。晚闻剥啄声，则一车在门，毡帏晶窗，驾以骏骡。门焉者以为贵公子，及下车登堂，翟始知其为聂。聂则貂冠狐裘，翟颇形寒俭。聂曰：“郎君旅馆亦寂寞否？”翟曰：“客邸萧条，大抵如是。”聂曰：“长安米不易索。我意屈驾过我屋，颇不僦；而饮食调护，自以为颇不粗粝。将请励志攻苦，来春雷甲可乘也。”翟起谢曰：“邂逅相逢，过蒙不弃，何敢居停坐扰？”聂再三致请。坐良久，嘱以明辰来枉驾也，遂登舆去。

    Explanation

    459

    Zhai Qiushan of Suzhou, having failed the imperial examinations, remained in the capital, where he was a renowned scholar. One day, while watching a performance, he saw Nie Xinxi. Upon returning to his lodging, he could not stop thinking about him. Thus, in the theater, there was Nie, and in the garden, there was Zhai. When Nie appeared on stage, Zhai would look up eagerly; when Nie left the stage, Zhai would cover his face and lie down. This pattern continued for many days. Nie, on stage, never failed to turn and pay attention, marveling at Zhai's unique and devoted affection.

    On a certain day, a performance was held at the Green Jade Garden. Before noon, Nie entered, and saw Zhai already wandering among the tables. Nie approached and said, "Have you had breakfast yet? Why did you come so early?" Zhai replied cheerfully, "The beauty here can cure hunger; I fear that if I wait even a moment longer, I might miss it." He then told Nie his name and address. After the performance ended and the audience dispersed, Zhai returned home. That evening, he heard a knock at the door, and outside stood a carriage with a fur-lined canopy and crystal windows, drawn by a fine mule. The gatekeeper thought it must be a nobleman, but upon descending from the carriage and entering the hall, Zhai realized it was Nie. Nie was wearing a sable hat and a fox fur coat, while Zhai appeared rather shabby. Nie said, "Is your inn also lonely, young sir?" Zhai replied, "The inn is rather desolate, as you can imagine." Nie continued, "Rice in Chang'an is not easy to come by. I would like you to grace my humble abode, which is not for rent; we can provide you with proper food and care. I hope you will work hard and persevere, and perhaps next spring, you can ride in a carriage like this." Zhai stood up and thanked him, saying, "We met by chance, and you have shown me such kindness. How dare I impose on your hospitality?" Nie insisted repeatedly. They sat for a long time, and Nie asked Zhai to visit the next morning. Finally, Nie got into the carriage and left.

    460

    次早，车已在门，翟即收拾书剑随往。至大宅，聂出，延入书舍。潇洒精致，铺陈皆细软。辰餐美馔。食罢，聂出门去。晚归，已带微醺。烹苦茗，夜谈，细诉衷曲，彼此爱慕。深更人退，聂复晚妆，如妇人，同翟共寝。翟偎抱温柔，如怀至宝。聂之娇容媚态，肌肤滑泽，更非脂粉裙钗所得方其万一。从此二人厮守，如夫如妇。有人为聂言婚，聂笑曰：“我赋男形，实有女心，乾道变化，将不知其已也。”悉却之。翟于是往来声气，聂与有力焉。
    逾年成进士，胪唱第一人。后聂亦弃其业。翟以观察滇南，聂随往。燕台当道，祖饯相望，不知者以为为翟也，其知者以为为聂耳。抵任后，内外事悉决于聂。会边戍，聂随之军需。旁午时，野人居一带土酋结连缅匪入寇，抵铁门关。翟率偏师袭之，深入重地，为酋所获，聂亦被虏。缅酋女长也，悦聂美，因说聂降而释翟。聂大骂请死。女酋怒，二人遂与难。死之日，聂大呼曰：“吾得与秋山死，死得所矣！”

    Explanation

    460

    The next morning, the carriage was already at the door, and Zhai packed his books and sword, ready to depart. Upon arriving at the grand mansion, Nie greeted them and led them into her study. The room was elegant and tastefully decorated, with luxurious furnishings. For breakfast, they enjoyed a sumptuous meal. After eating, Nie left, and in the evening, she returned slightly tipsy. They brewed bitter tea and engaged in a late-night conversation, sharing their deepest feelings. They were mutually enamored. Deep into the night, after everyone had gone to bed, Nie applied her evening makeup and, dressed like a woman, shared her bed with Zhai. Zhai embraced her tenderly, cherishing her as if she were a precious treasure. Nie's charming beauty and smooth, lustrous skin were far beyond what any cosmetics or women's attire could achieve. From then on, the two lived together as husband and wife. When someone suggested marriage to Nie, she laughed and said, "Though I am born with a male form, I have a woman's heart. The ways of heaven are ever-changing, and I do not know where they will lead." She rejected all proposals. Zhai benefited greatly from Nie's influence and support.

    After a year, Zhai passed the imperial examination and was announced as the top-ranked candidate. Later, Nie also abandoned her career. Zhai was appointed as an observer in southern Yunnan, and Nie accompanied him. At the capital's gate, officials gathered for a farewell banquet. Those who did not know thought it was for Zhai, while those who did know believed it was for Nie. Upon arriving at their post, all internal and external affairs were decided by Nie. When there was military service on the frontier, Nie accompanied Zhai with the military supplies. At noon, a group of bandits led by a local chieftain joined forces with Burmese bandits to invade, reaching the strategic pass of Iron Gate. Zhai led a small force to attack them, penetrating deep into enemy territory, where he was captured by the chieftain, and Nie was also taken prisoner. The female chieftain was attracted by Nie's beauty and persuaded her to surrender, promising to release Zhai. Nie cursed and requested death. The female chieftain became angry, and the two died together. On the day of their death, Nie cried out, "I am happy to die with Qiu Shan, and die in the right place!"

    461

    翠柳
    维扬汪本，以手谈自诩。尝游于京洛缙绅间，曾见赏于吴桥某大司马，因称“棋汪”。由是一枰之上，方罫之间，闻汪生之风者，可以不战而先馁。一日，游三楚，寓武昌。太守张公，高手也，癖于木野狐。因与汪弈，三战三北，汪胜气临之。太守衔汪，因欲得一胜汪者以快意，而卒寥寥。
    张于静夜，灯前覆汪胜局，反覆凝思，计无所出。一婢年十五，名翠柳，慧而能。捧茗在张公侧久立，乃曰：“莲漏三滴，犹抱石子不寐，夫人将不耐等矣。”张不答。翠柳指局曰：“但此间争一着先耳。”张恍然。遂命与弈，终局翠胜，张大喜，抱之膝间，曰：“可儿，明日当与汪弈，为我一洗前辱。”

    Explanation

    461

    Cui Liù
    Wang Ben of Weiyang prides himself on his skill at Go. He has traveled among the elite in the capital region and was once praised by a certain Grand Marshal of Wuqiao, earning him the nickname "Go Wang." As a result, on the Go board, those who have heard of Wang's reputation can be intimidated before the battle even begins. One day, he traveled through the Chu regions and stayed in Wuchang. The governor, Zhang Gong, was a skilled player and addicted to Go. He played against Wang and lost three times in a row, bearing a grudge against Wang. Zhang wanted to find someone who could defeat Wang to satisfy his desire, but no one came forward.
    One quiet night, Zhang Gong reviewed Wang's winning games by the lamp, pondering repeatedly, but could not find a solution. A maid named Cui Liù, who was fifteen years old, was clever and capable. She stood by Zhang Gong for a long time, holding tea, and then said, "Three drops from the lotus clock, still holding chess pieces without sleeping, the lady will be impatient." Zhang did not answer. Cui Liù pointed to the board and said, "It's only a matter of competing for one move ahead." Zhang suddenly understood. He then ordered her to play with him, and in the end, Cui Liù won the game. Zhang was greatly pleased and hugged her on his knee, saying, "Dear, tomorrow I will play with Wang to wash away my past humiliation."

    462

    辰起，请汪及众宾至，复布局，曰：“今日有小女子学步者，愿先生教之。”汪漫应焉。张公呼翠柳出，汪视之，垂髫丫髻儇婢也。立案前，入局即持白子曰：“棋让一先。先生请下黑子，可以前驱胜我也。”汪颔之。甫三四着，汪色变。翠曰：“先生面頳矣。”翠上下嬉顾，略不经意，而子落枰间，一座皆惊。翠又曰：“先生汗出矣。”汪頳颜沉思，下子愈迟。翠随手掷之，疾若鹘落。既而翠柳棋声乃与笑声丁丁格格相酬答；汪如木偶，子更无着处。翠以手自捏其凤翘曰：“先生坐，亦知立者苦否？”众粲然。而汪神丧志沮，辙乱旗靡。忽为翠柳于西北角上劫去十数子，如方塘一鉴，白鹭数点而已。翠乃以长袖自掩其口，胡卢曰：“先生负矣！先生负矣！”零碎连步以入。汪目望洋，不知所为，是局固未终也。汪蹩躄返寓，明日遂行。

    Explanation

    462

    At dawn, Wang was invited to the gathering along with all the guests. The board was rearranged, and it was said, "Today, there is a young girl who is learning to play, and I wish Master would teach her." Wang replied vaguely. Mr. Zhang called out Cui Liu, who stood in front of the board. She had a childlike hairstyle and a playful demeanor, appearing as a mischievous servant. She entered the board and immediately took a white piece, saying, "The game allows one move advantage to the opponent. Master, please take the black piece, and you can lead and win against me." Wang nodded. Just after three or four moves, Wang's face changed color. Cui said, "Master's face has turned red." Cui looked around playfully, not paying much attention, but the pieces fell on the board, and everyone was surprised. Cui said again, "Master is sweating." Wang, with a red face, pondered deeply, and his moves became slower. Cui casually placed the pieces, swiftly like a falcon descending. Then, the sound of Cui Liu's moves mingled with her laughter. Wang was like a puppet, with nowhere to place his pieces. Cui pinched her phoenix hairpin with her hand and said, "Master, sit down. Do you know how hard it is to stand?" Everyone laughed heartily.
