But Wang lost his spirit and became discouraged, his strategy in disarray. Suddenly, Cui Liu captured over ten pieces in the northwest corner, like a small pond with a few white egrets. Cui covered her mouth with her long sleeve and laughed, saying, "Master has lost! Master has lost!" She stepped in a disorderly manner. Wang looked around in despair, not knowing what to do. The game was not yet over. Wang limped back to his lodging, and the next day, he left.

    463

    挽联
    陕之渭阳，某村农家有牛病，其父命子入城觅兽医者。子归，得药并所医方，牛食之果起。后凡村牛有疾，辄用其方，无不效，彼乃以为医固易事也。
    一日，其父偶病，其子即以牛药药其父而瘥。后己亦病，即以父药自药之，而亦瘳，乃大快。志于此药，而心窃幸乎医道之得也。乡之人且以其疗牛疾、己、父病，而誉其术之精。于是购医方一策，令人读而解之。为人视疾后，则阖户，以纸蒙其方，书而与人，效不效未可定也。

    Explanation

    463

    A Mourning Couplet

    In a village in Weiyang, Shaanxi, a farmer's cow fell ill. The farmer sent his son into the city to find a veterinarian. Upon his return, the son brought back the medicine and the prescription. The cow ate it and indeed recovered. Later, whenever there was a disease among the village cows, they always used his prescription, and it never failed. He then thought that medicine was indeed an easy matter.

    One day, his father happened to fall ill. The son immediately treated his father with the cow medicine, and he recovered. Later, he himself fell ill, and he treated himself with his father's medicine, and he also recovered. And he was greatly pleased. He cherished this medicine and secretly rejoiced at having mastered the art of medicine. The people of the village praised his skill as precise because he had treated the cow's illness, his own, and his father's. He then bought a medical book and had someone read and explain it to him. After treating someone's illness, he would close the door, cover the prescription with paper, write it out, and give it to the person, but whether it would be effective was uncertain.

    464

    后以其父之疾复作，其子仍以牛药灌之而死。因不服：前次以牛药药父也，何以霍然？而今之父药亦牛也，何以溘然？是岂药之罪哉！会己又病，终服牛药以毙。
    邑有文士挽之一联云：“牛之性犹人之性，忘其身以及其亲。”

    曾广
    曾广，济宁人。幼孤贫，懒读书，不务生产，空空然终日若无事者。人或忤之，则答之以笑。年二十，婚贫家女，貌甚寝，而曾视之喜。
    每游败寺旷野，逾日不返。一日，遇一黄冠道人，白须如银，头高耸，而肩盎若，且长不满三尺。负葫芦十数个，累累而行。休道旁大树下，枕葫芦睡，顷鼾息雷鸣。曾潜近揭其塞，倾之无物。乃以目眡口觑，冷气觉自眶中透心膈，泪潸潸出。道人惊醒曰：“汝放我一葫芦空青走矣。奈何？”曾对之拭目而憨笑。道人曰：“幸汝至诚人，亦汝缘也，否当抉汝睛。慎勿妄为！”遂起。依旧负葫芦去。曾由是一目如电，视地下如琉璃，皆洞彻无翳。

    Explanation

    464

    Later, his father's illness recurred, and the son once again gave him a medicine made from cow, but this time his father died. He did not believe: the first time, he gave his father a medicine made from cow, and his father recovered miraculously. Why did he die suddenly this time? Is it the fault of the medicine? Later, he fell ill again and finally died after taking the cow medicine.
    A scholar in the town wrote a couplet to mourn him: "The nature of cows is like the nature of humans, forget oneself to benefit one's parents."

    Zeng Guang
    Zeng Guang was from Jining. He lost his father at a young age and grew up poor. He was lazy about reading and did not focus on productive work, spending his days as if nothing mattered. If someone offended him, he would respond with a smile. At the age of twenty, he married a woman from a poor family who was quite plain, but Zeng looked at her with joy.
    He often wandered to dilapidated temples and desolate wildernesses, sometimes not returning for days. One day, he met a Taoist with a yellow hat, silver beard, a tall head, and a broad shoulder, though he was no more than three feet tall. The Taoist carried several gourds, hanging from his body, and walked slowly. He rested under a large tree by the roadside, using a gourd as a pillow and fell asleep, soon snoring loudly like thunder. Zeng secretly approached and uncapped one of the gourds, pouring out nothing. Then, he looked into the Taoist's mouth and felt a cold breeze coming from his eyes, causing tears to flow. The Taoist woke up startled and said, "You let me lose a gourd of emptiness! What should I do?" Zeng wiped his eyes and laughed innocently. The Taoist said, "Fortunately, you are a sincere person, and this is your fate. Otherwise, I would have plucked out your eyes. Be careful not to do anything foolish!" He then got up and continued on his way with the gourds. From then on, Zeng had one eye like an electric light, seeing through the ground as if it were clear glass, with no obstruction.

    465

    后每闭此目，不轻开视。人问之，曾曰：“恐一顾盼，则见其肺肝矣。”会东门有掘井者，深不及泉，曾谓曰：“再掘一尺即得。”如其言，泉涌。今呼为曾广井云。
    曾尝入深山，见危岩下有石函畚，启视，中有丹书数卷。习之，遂悟吐纳铅汞术。曾以口涎丸足间，漫令人服。人初不肯，后渐信之。其妻莲船盈尺，偶过碗肆，肆人泼水于道，故令其妻蹇涩以过，良久乃去。肆人大笑，以其如船而杯渡也。妻惭，归告曾，以为大辱。觅一大兔，令翌日袖之，复往其处。挥犬逐兔入肆，大毁其碗，不可禁。知曾之为也，求而收之，回视其碗，皆无碎损。
    此人先从祖时庵公犹见之，以其邪惑，不与之序宗族。州志载其本增广生，弃去，因以为号，非是。曾于康熙戊子己丑间尚在也。

    Explanation

    465

    The queen often closed her eyes and did not lightly open them to look. When people asked her about it, Zeng said, "I fear that even a glance would reveal their innermost thoughts." Once, there was a well-digger at the eastern gate whose well was not deep enough to reach the spring. Zeng told him, "Dig another inch deeper, and you will find it." Following his advice, the well sprung forth with water. Now it is called the Zeng Guang Well.

    Zeng once ventured into a deep mountain and saw a stone basin under a precipitous rock. Upon opening it, he found several scrolls of red writing. After studying them, he came to understand the techniques of breathing in and out lead and mercury. Zeng would take saliva and place it between his feet, then casually give it to people to consume. At first, people were unwilling, but they gradually came to believe in it. His wife, who was about a foot long, happened to pass by a bowl shop. The shopkeeper splashed water on the ground, causing his wife to move slowly and reluctantly. After a long time, she finally left. The shopkeeper laughed, saying that she was like a boat being carried by cups. The wife felt ashamed and returned to tell Zeng, considering it a great humiliation. She then sought out a large rabbit and instructed him to carry it the next day. They went back to the shop, where Zeng waved a dog to chase the rabbit into the shop, causing a great deal of destruction to the bowls. The shopkeeper could not stop them. Knowing it was Zeng's doing, he sought him out and retrieved the bowls, only to find that none of them were damaged.

    This person was still seen by his grandfather, Master Shian Gong, who considered him heretical and did not include him in the family lineage. The local gazetteer records that he was originally a student of the Imperial Academy, but abandoned it and used it as a name, which is not correct. Zeng was still alive during the years of Kangxi's reign, specifically the years of Wu Zi and Ji Chou (1678 and 1679).

    466

    (按《坚瓠集》亦载一曾广，是徐鸿儒遗党以妖术称者，非济宁人。当是同名又一人，存参。)

    吴门三戏
    吴市有丐者，持竹簏，养以青蛙十数头，索钱为戏，名曰“虾蟆说法”。丐先取小蒲团十数如饼，中位一，其次两行，各东西列。其最大者游衍而出，跏趺坐蒲团上，鼓腹一鸣，如呼其类，群蛙依次而出，左右对列坐，寂然不动。大者作一声，众亦随作一声。大者三声，众亦三声。既而大小间作，哄鸣如市，恝然忽止。乃一一至大者前，点首、拳曲、作声如号诵佛状。大者于是圈豚离坐，循循然若归方丈去也。群蛙遂嘈嘈杂遝入簏。此其一。
    有瞽人执卦板，挽雕笯于袖间，蓄一小雀，出卦帖排如箑。旋于席间。有求算者，报以年庚。瞽击板一声，雀以嘴衔其机，门便开。雀出鼓翼，取干支，如其命造，又取十二宫排列于前。瞽者指画谈论，一一如所指布。雀复衔帖，照数仍插旧处。瞽复击板一声，雀入而门扃。谓之“雀儿算命”。又其一。

    Explanation

    466

    (As per Jianhu Ji (The Collected Notes of the Gourd), it also records one Zeng Guang, a follower of Xu Hongru who claimed to use magical arts, but he was not from Jining. Perhaps it is another person with the same name; keep this in mind.)

    Three Plays in Wu Gate
    In Wu Gate, there was a beggar who carried a bamboo basket and raised dozens of frogs. He asked for money for a performance, which was called "Frog Preaching." The beggar first took out several small mats like pancakes, placing one in the center and arranging two rows facing east and west. The largest frog leisurely emerged and sat cross-legged on the mat. It puffed up its belly and made one sound, as if calling its kind. The frogs then came out one by one in order, arranged facing left and right, sitting silently and motionless. The largest frog made one sound, and the others followed suit. The largest frog made three sounds, and the others followed suit. Then, alternately large and small, they made noisy sounds like a market, suddenly stopping. They then one by one went to the largest frog's front, nodding, curling up, and making sounds like chanting. The largest frog then circled around and left its seat, slowly and calmly as if returning to the abbot's room. The frogs then noisily and disorderly entered the basket. This was the first play.

    There was also a blind person holding divination boards, carrying a carved cage in his sleeve, and keeping a small bird. He took out divination slips arranged like a fan and spun them on the mat. Those who sought divination reported their age and birth year. The blind person struck the board once, and the bird used its beak to open the mechanism. The bird then spread its wings, taking the heavenly stems and earthly branches, arranging them according to the person's birth chart, and also arranging the twelve palaces in front. The blind person pointed and discussed, everything unfolding as indicated. The bird then took the slips and, following the numbers, returned them to their original places. The blind person struck the board again, and the bird entered the cage, closing the door. This was called "Bird Divination." This was the second play.

    467

    更有蚁阵一戏，尤为奇异。一丐怀竹二节，持一小鼓，规寸许，蒙以鸡皮。观者毕集，丐乃去竹之塞，折枝击鼓以进。筒中有赤黑二蚁千百，分队出，累累若贯珠，步武罔不中矩矱。列为二，如对阵势，整而不乱。既而或三或五，各随鼓音而变。猝视之，眇小如撒蔴沙。细审之，则天冲地轴，鱼丽鹤列，云风蛇虎，首尾相连。凡变合数次，又复作队，按部就班，蜿蜒归其筒中。此其一。
    (卢忠烈，名象升。幼时蓄蟋蟀，一种青，一种黄，各十头。斗时于几上设大方盘，青左队，黄右队，以旗挥之则斗。斗毕各归盆盎，青黄不杂。盖喂养时驯习而成。卢公为
    将，征流寇，立奇功，是其天性然欤？)

    亚罗仙
    亚罗仙，江西赣州姚某也。其父为郡守，因拿邪教案，搜得符书一册。正在审囚指摘，忽失此书，遍求不得。乃为姚某窃而秘之，皆不知也。案结后，姚于无人处试演，用黄纸硃墨效其步蹈。

    Explanation

    467

    There is another ant array game, which is particularly strange and peculiar. A beggar carries two bamboo sections, holding a small drum about an inch in diameter, covered with chicken skin. When the spectators have gathered, the beggar removes the stopper from the bamboo, strikes the drum with a twig, and advances. Inside the bamboo tube are thousands of red and black ants, dividing into teams and emerging in a row like beads on a string, their steps all within the bounds of proper measurement. They are arranged in two lines, like opposing armies, orderly but not chaotic. Soon after, either three or five, they change according to the rhythm of the drum. Looking at it suddenly, they appear tiny like scattered sesame seeds. Examining it closely, their formations resemble the heavens and earth, fish and cranes, clouds, wind, snakes, and tigers, head to tail connected. After several changes, they form up again in orderly fashion, winding back into the bamboo tube. This is one example.

    (Lu Zhonglie, whose name is Xiangsheng. As a child, he kept crickets, one type green, the other yellow, each with ten heads. When fighting, he placed a large tray on the table, the green team on the left, the yellow team on the right, using flags to signal the start of the fight. After the fight, each team returned to their containers, the green and yellow teams did not mix. This was cultivated through feeding and training. Lu Gong served as a general, campaigning against bandits, achieving extraordinary feats. Is this due to his natural disposition?)

    Yaxuan
    Yaxuan is a certain Yao from Ganzhou, Jiangxi. His father was the prefect of the district, and during the investigation of a case involving heretical teachings, a book of talismans was found. While examining the prisoners and pointing out their faults, the book suddenly disappeared, and despite a thorough search, it could not be found. It was secretly taken by Yao and kept hidden, and no one knew about it. After the case was concluded, Yao tried the performance in an empty place, using yellow paper and red ink to mimic the steps and movements.

    468

    忽一日召火神至，金目碧髯，光电闪烁，立案前，问所召使，姚怖失措，答曰：“速焚此书舍，将换新室。”霎时炤炽，栋榱灰烬。扑灭之后，不知是姚所为。其妻临镜晓妆，忽见两眉转落眼下，妻方惊诧，姚以手移之如故，因是疑其神。
    太守死，归籍。将过洞庭，泊潜江。次早欲解缆，而舟已在湘潭，则八百里之水程已夜渡矣。返里后，每弄其术，乡人哄然神之。姚因自号为“亚罗仙”，自负为罗祖后一人也。能隐形，出入不见其迹，但闻人马之行声。素与某姓有仇，每夜降其家，令其妻女环坐侑觞，百般蹂躏。或命优伶开筵亭榭，设座堂上，但见馂食无馀，酒罍告罄而已。而一时同席皆其业师及同学诸童子，佥云：“遵奉仙命。”不知姚之所为。其欲至某姓家，先一日飞一纸下，云“亚罗仙于某日降临，当如何承应，某人陪席。不则或火或病。”某苦之，鸣于官，官亦不能治。乃求吁于贵溪龙虎真人，遣法官来。姚拘法官跪阶下，笞之，臀肉流血而去。

    Explanation

    468

    One day, the protagonist summoned the fire god, who appeared with golden eyes and a green beard, his gaze flickering with light. Standing before the bench, the fire god asked why he had been summoned. The protagonist, filled with fear and disarray, replied, "Quickly burn this book house, and we will build a new one." In an instant, the house was engulfed in flames, and the beams and rafters were reduced to ashes. After the fire was extinguished, it was unclear whether the protagonist was responsible.

    His wife, while preparing her morning makeup, suddenly noticed her eyebrows had fallen into her eyes. She was startled, but the protagonist moved them back into place, and she began to suspect his supernatural abilities.

    When the local governor died, the protagonist returned to his hometown. He planned to cross Dongting Lake and stop at Qianjiang. The next morning, he intended to untie the ship's mooring ropes, but the ship was already in Xiangtan, having traveled 800 li overnight. After returning home, the protagonist frequently practiced his magic, and the villagers were amazed and believed in his supernatural abilities. He gave himself the title "Yaroxian," claiming to be the successor of Luo Zu, a historical figure known for his magical powers. He could become invisible and move without leaving a trace, and only the sounds of people and horses could be heard.

    The protagonist had a long-standing grudge against a particular family. Every night, he would descend to their home, making the women of the family sit around and serve wine, subjecting them to various forms of abuse. Sometimes, he would order actors to set up a feast in a pavilion and have them sit in the hall. However, only the remains of food and empty wine jars were left, as the actors had mysteriously disappeared. It turned out that the actors were actually the protagonist's former teachers and classmates, disguised as performers. They all claimed to be following the orders of the "immortal," unaware of the protagonist's true actions.

    If the protagonist wanted to visit a particular family, he would send a letter the day before, stating that Yaroxian would arrive on a certain day and specifying what should be done, or else there would be fire or illness. The family suffering from this torment reported it to the authorities, but the officials were unable to deal with the situation. They appealed to the Dragon Tiger True Person of Guixi, who sent a judge. The protagonist captured the judge, made him kneel at the stairs, and beat him until his buttocks bled, then let him go.

    469

    是年，赣郡无旱潦之虞，或以为姚之力，故郡人亦有畏之且敬之者。姚复有弟子传其教，郡中人有私语之者，皆头疼，不则瓦石掷眉睫间。以香楮望空谢罪而已。地方官佐有受贿屈人者，姚悉知之，能表暴其罪状。每于夜深遣一鬼直达衙署，以利刃吓之，各官为之丧胆，不敢稍有骫法情事。郡中有鼠窃者，凡入人家，皆如木偶，俟天明，事主见之，絷于官。鞫之，佥曰：“见‘亚罗仙’至，不敢动。”会有高某，汉军镶黄旗人，素廉直，迁赣郡丞。甫下车，姚即杜门敛迹。有求于姚者，辄报谢曰：“官法可治，我无法也。”夫政之为言，正也，正己而后正诸人，己不正则不正者皆能起而相乱。故亚罗之为鬼蜮也，不正者召之也。然使其出于正，则又俨然仙矣。

    Explanation

    469

    That year, in Ganzhou, there was no fear of drought or flood. Some attributed this to the efforts of Yao, and thus the people of the city both feared and respected him. Yao again had disciples who spread his teachings, and those who spoke to them privately all suffered from headaches, or else rocks and stones were thrown at their eyes. They only burned incense and paper money to apologize to the air. Local officials who accepted bribes and wronged people, Yao knew all about it and could expose their crimes. At night, he sent a ghost to the government office with a sharp blade to scare them, and the officials were terrified and dared not violate the law. In the city, there were thieves, and all acted like wooden puppets when they entered homes. Until dawn, the victims saw them and caught them at the government office. When questioned, they all said, "Seeing the 'Aro Xian' come, we dare not move." There was a person named Gao, a Han Bannerman from the Yellow Banner, who was always honest and just and was appointed as the deputy magistrate of Ganzhou. As soon as Gao took office, Yao closed his door and hid. Those who asked Yao for help replied, "Official law can handle it, I have no power." The meaning of "politics" is to be upright; one must first be upright oneself before one can make others upright; if one is not upright, those who are not upright will rise up and cause chaos. Thus, Aro Xian's role as a ghost is that of an unrighteous person who calls upon it. However, if it comes from the right place, it becomes a clear and evident deity.

    470

    高公久知其煽惑，欲侦缉之，几半年不得。某夜出逻，忽旋风滚滚，如群马奔嘶，蹀踏而过，郡丞惊问，皆曰：“神仙夜宴归也。”郡丞怒斥之。忽空中坠落十馀人，尽花服执纸衣马匹。就缚焉，讯而伏罪。追其书，火于庭。以其年甫弱冠，从轻问边戍，十年后得恩赦归。尝往来广陵诸商家。问其素所持法，百不记一。盖其对本宣科，未尝熟习，即其徒亦然，故书亡而法破。后以戏法二种，衣食江湖，其一暗里索熟酒食，其一空中起小楼台。年近七旬，茕茕孑立而已。呜呼，以法为戏，鲜有不败。如亚罗者，得保首领于牖下，盖亦幸矣！

    Explanation

    470

    High Official had long known of the incitement and desired to investigate it, but he failed to do so for almost half a year. One night, while on patrol, he suddenly encountered a swirling whirlwind, like a herd of horses galloping and neighing, trampling and passing by. The county magistrate was startled and asked, and all said, "The immortal has returned from a night banquet." The county magistrate angrily rebuked them. Suddenly, more than ten people fell from the sky, all dressed in flowered robes and holding paper clothes and horses. They were immediately bound and, after interrogation, confessed their crimes. Their books were seized and burned in the courtyard. Because he was just coming of age, he was lightly punished with exile to the frontier and, ten years later, was granted amnesty and returned. He often frequented the shops of the wealthy merchants in Guangling. When asked about the methods he had always used, he could not remember even one. This was because he merely recited from a script, having never truly mastered it, and even his disciples were the same. Therefore, with the loss of the book, the method was lost. Later, he made a living with two kinds of magic tricks, wandering the rivers and lakes. One trick involved secretly asking for prepared food and drink, and the other involved conjuring up a small pavilion in the air. By the time he was nearly seventy, he was alone and destitute. Alas, using methods for entertainment, few do not fail. Like Aro, who managed to keep his head intact under the eaves, it was also a stroke of luck.

    471

    (章贡袁行川孝廉言之凿凿。)

    卷十四 淫昵类(盗骗附)
    李峄南
    章邱李峄南，肄业于济南泺源书院。时自家中来城。日将暮，李乘车行，忽道旁两女子呼曰：“车中人，曷携吾姊妹入城？吾金菊巷住。”李停车视之，一女年约二十许，面瘦如削玉，着皂衣衫，淡葵裤，翠靸履；一女年十六七，丰美而眉长，着浅蓝衫，黑色裤，红履，花绣欲满。李爱之，欲同载，御者曰：“不可。倘遭友于道，非亲非故，则颜厚有忸怩也！”女曰：“既不载，毋能强。郎君携有新胡桃，赠我数枚。”李与之，登车辗軨。但闻二女在后笑语相喧，格格不断。御者谓李曰：“伊坐我车后箱。”李曰：“伊远行惫，无使之下也。”入城，将抵书院，女子下舆，翩然去。李入院。

    Explanation

    471

    Mr. Yuan Xingchuan from Zhanggong, who is a scholar-official, says it with certainty.

    Chapter Fourteen: On Promiscuity and Deception (Including Theft and Deception)

    Mr. Li Yinan, a student at the Lüe Yuan Academy in Jinan, was traveling from his home to the city. As evening approached, Mr. Li was traveling by carriage. Suddenly, two women by the roadside called out to him, "Sir in the carriage, why not take us sisters into the city? We live in Jinju Lane." Mr. Li stopped his carriage to look at them. One woman was about twenty years old, with a face as thin as carved jade, wearing a black robe, light yellow pants, and green sandals. The other woman was about sixteen or seventeen, with a full and beautiful face and long eyebrows, wearing a light blue robe, black pants, red shoes, and embroidered flowers almost covering the entire shoe. Mr. Li was attracted to them and wanted to take them along, but the driver said, "No. If we encounter friends on the road, and they are neither relatives nor acquaintances, we would feel embarrassed!" The women said, "If you won't take us, we can't force you. Sir, you have some new walnuts, please give us a few." Mr. Li gave them the walnuts, and they climbed into the carriage. As they traveled, the two women laughed and chatted, their voices continuous and cheerful. The driver told Mr. Li, "They are sitting in the back of the carriage." Mr. Li said, "They are tired from a long journey, so don't make them get down." As they entered the city and approached the academy, the women got off the carriage and left gracefully. Mr. Li returned to the academy.

    472

    院之西北隅最荒凉，内有苇塘土壖，四围皆败堵。肄业者恐有梁上客，多不假榻于此。李后至，不得已居焉，然喜其静而无哗。是夜，李坐翻卷帙，皂衫女笑而入曰：“劳劳一日，尚咿唔夜读。这顶乌纱帽，岂争此片刻工夫？”李惊喜，延之坐。女曰：“我风娟也，东邻陈氏妇，今夜来谢胡桃。”李前抱，觉其轻软如意。风娟曰：“车不令我载，床却令人眠耶？”李笑，遂与之合。李问蓝衣为谁，风娟曰：“是我小姑月润也。”李曰：“他何不来？”风娟曰：“伊避我行。”俄闻窗外弹指声曰：“嫂家奚子呼汝。毋贪眠。”风娟仓皇起出。李寻至后园，环视，毫无踪迹。

    Explanation

    472

    The northwest corner of the courtyard was the most desolate, with a reed pond and a barren area inside, surrounded by ruined walls. Students feared the presence of thieves or unwanted guests and often avoided staying there. Li arrived later and had no choice but to stay, but he liked the quietness and lack of noise. That night, Li was sitting and flipping through books when a girl dressed in a black robe smiled and entered, speaking: "Tired from a day's work, still reading aloud at night. This black robe, doesn't it need to compete for a moment's time?" Li was surprised and delighted, inviting her to sit. She said, "I am Feng Juan, the wife of Chen from the eastern neighbor, and I came tonight to thank you for the walnuts." Li hugged her, feeling her soft and pleasant. Feng Juan remarked, "The carriage won't let me ride, but the bed will allow me to sleep?" Li laughed, and they became intimate. Li asked who the blue-clad woman was, and Feng Juan replied, "She is my younger sister, Yue Run." Li wondered, "Why didn't she come?" Feng Juan explained, "She avoided me." Suddenly, a finger snap was heard outside the window, calling Li and reminding him not to oversleep. Feng Juan hurriedly got up and left. Li followed her to the back garden, looked around, but saw no trace of her.

    473

    归斋中，书几上一女子支颐而坐，乃月润也。见李，态度羞怯，复齿襟袖，凝眸不语。李即掩扉与狎，情好甚密，又觉暖美异常。一日，月润谓李曰：“吾嫂非人，殆鬼也。人与鬼交，久则阴气中之，必死。我不忍坐视君祸，故与君为欢。与我三度之益，可抵与嫂一度之损。”李德之。夜风娟来，李逡巡不敢近。风娟曰：“是有先入之言矣，郎无听其谗。我诚鬼，不为君害。彼狐也，将采尔精。与其日见元阳之丧，不如夜得小阴之补也。”李以其两人相厄，转得调停语。如是一风一月，朝暮无间。即院中同人，初以为墙外娼，后李亦不讳。同人多见月润，且与之谈，独不识风娟之面。有胡秀才某至，月亦避之。李问，月润曰：“胡生，正人也。”

    Explanation

    473

    In the study, a woman leaned on her cheek and sat on the book table, and she was Yue Run. Upon seeing Li, she had a shy demeanor, bit her lip and adjusted her sleeves, fixed her gaze, and remained silent. Li immediately closed the door and engaged in intimate relations with her, their affection was very close, and he also felt that she was exceptionally warm and beautiful. One day, Yue Run told Li, "My sister-in-law is not a person, she is likely a ghost. If a person has relations with a ghost for too long, the yin energy will harm them, and they will die. I cannot bear to see you suffer, so I have been intimate with you. Three times with me can counterbalance one time with your sister-in-law." Li was grateful to her. At night, Feng Juan arrived, and Li hesitated and dared not approach. Feng Juan said, "There are already preconceived notions, sir, do not listen to slander. I am indeed a ghost, but I will not harm you. She is a fox spirit, and she will drain your essence. It is better to lose a little yang energy at night than to lose it daily." Li found a way to mediate between the two. Like this, one wind and one moon, day and night without interruption. The other people in the courtyard initially thought she was a prostitute from outside the wall, but later Li did not conceal it. The others often saw Yue Run, and talked with her, but did not recognize Feng Juan's face. A certain Mr. Hu, a scholar, arrived, and Yue Run also avoided him. Li asked, and Yue Run said, "Mr. Hu is an upright person."

    474

    半载，李以风、月消磨，遂至精神憔悴。初则倦于行，渐且疲于坐矣。夜间风娟谓李曰：“吾家相去不远，何不一为散步？”李随往。至则砌门，入则灯荧荧，几榻精好，虽斗室蜗居，位置颇自不俗。案上有残书一卷，标以《湘帆集》，诗中有“移得疗愁草，种于离垢园”，又“无由似月能相照，倘化为云亦自归”。风娟掩卷曰：“此先夫旧作，对之索然。”捧茶贻李。李味之，香冽喉齿。风娟曰：“此皋卢芽，百馀年后，以泉底水煎之，故能如是。”月润忽入曰：“嫂请得李郎，独不致妹一声？”乃取一盏茶呷曰：“佳茗。但不可多饮，多则生积病耳。李郎何不更上我一层楼上去？此地不甚爽垲。”李起，风、月前引，盘曲登一小楼。四围雕窗，独开北面。夜阑魄静，遥见华峰翠立目前，周以碧树层层，颇觉怡神恬目。李倚阑甚寒。月即闭窗，罗酒果，皆珍品，市中所不能购者。三人宴笑，无乐不至。既乃归斋，常相过从，无间寒暑。

    Explanation

    474

    Half a year had passed, and Li, worn down by the wind and moon, had become spiritually exhausted. At first, he grew tired of walking, and soon he was weary even of sitting. One night, Feng Juan told Li, "My home is not far away. Why don't you come for a walk?" Li followed her. Upon arrival, they passed through a stone gate, and inside, the light was dim and flickering, but the table and bed were well-made and elegant. Though it was a small, cramped living space, the arrangement was quite refined and tasteful. On the table lay an incomplete book, titled "Xiang Fan Collection," which contained the lines, "Transplanted the grass that heals sorrow, planted in the garden far from filth," and "Unable to be like the moon to shine upon each other, if transformed into clouds, they would naturally return." Feng Juan closed the book and said, "These are the old works of my late husband. They are rather boring." She then offered Li some tea. Li tasted it, finding it fragrant and refreshing. Feng Juan explained, "This is Gaolu tea leaf, brewed with water from the spring after over a hundred years, hence its unique flavor." Suddenly, Moonru entered and said, "Sister, you invited Li, but didn't even greet me once?" She took a sip of tea and remarked, "This is fine tea. But don't drink too much, for it can cause illness. Why don't you go up another level of the building, Li? It's not very comfortable or dry here." Li stood up, and Feng Juan and Moonru led him up, winding their way to a small building. The building had intricately carved windows, all facing north. As night deepened and the spirit grew calm, they could see the tall, green mountains standing before them, surrounded by layers of green trees, which refreshed their spirits and pleased their eyes. Li leaned on the railing, feeling very cold. Moonru closed the window and arranged wine and fruits, all of which were treasures that could not be bought in the market. The three of them feasted and laughed, enjoying all kinds of pleasures. Afterward, they returned to their study, often visiting each other regardless of cold or heat.

    475

    胡生每欲为之按剑。一日，危坐李斋，历数鬼狐之罪而咨咀之。忽屋鬲上月润言曰：“胡先生无出恶声。妾非害李者。倘李非妾，今当索之冢中也。此皆我嫂之为厉。嫂常氏，南山之乡女，嫁于庠生陈惇夫。夫死一年，陈氏嫂欲醮，而陈族不许，遂抑郁死。葬于书院之后埂下，时在明天顺间，此地皆荒垒也。吾兄见其美而艳，且自恃得道狐，遂取之。三年，吾兄亦死。妾本吾兄抚养，兄死居嫂。嫂之所欲，妾不能禁。今妾独处于院后老楸树间，不共嫂依。李郎不死，妾实有功，何反诬妾以罪为？”胡曰：“然则尔嫂将何法治之？”月曰：“孰谓冥冥之中，无司权者？”胡遂集同人，具香楮，告于城隍之神，焚香吁牒。数日间，李病虽未瘥，而斋中寂然无鬼狐之迹，咸以为非胡生之力不及此。胡亦曰：“此我之正直而壹感神听也。”

    Explanation

    475

    Tr. 1:
    Hu Sheng was always ready to confront the situation with a sword. One day, he sat upright in Li's study, listing the crimes of the ghosts and fox spirits and criticizing them. Suddenly, a voice from above, that of the moonlit figure, said, "Mr. Hu, do not speak ill. I did not harm Li. If Li were not with me, he would be in the grave now. All these troubles are caused by my sister-in-law. My sister-in-law, Chang Shi, was a woman from a southern mountain region, married to a student named Chen Dunfu. After her husband died in one year, Chang Shi wanted to remarry, but the Chen family did not allow it, and she died of depression. She was buried behind the academy, during the reign of Emperor Tianshun, when this place was desolate. My brother saw her beauty and his own perceived power as a fox spirit, and he took her. Three years later, my brother also died. I was raised by my brother, and after his death, I lived with my sister-in-law. Whatever she desired, I could not prevent. Now I live alone in the courtyard behind the old paulownia tree, not with my sister-in-law. Li survived because I saved him; why am I being blamed?" Hu said, "Then what can be done about your sister-in-law?" The moonlit figure replied, "Who says there is no authority in the unseen realm?" Hu then gathered others, prepared incense and paper offerings, and prayed to the city god, burning incense and offering prayers. Within a few days, although Li's illness did not improve, the study was quiet and free of ghostly and fox spirit traces. Everyone believed it was due to Hu Sheng's efforts. Hu also said, "This is because of my integrity and the gods' attention."

    Tr. 2:
    Hu Sheng was always ready to confront the situation with a sword. One day, he sat upright in Li's study, listing the crimes of the ghosts and fox spirits and criticizing them. Suddenly, a voice from above, that of the moonlit figure, said, "Mr. Hu, do not speak ill. I did not harm Li. If Li were not with me, he would be in the grave now. All these troubles are caused by my sister-in-law. My sister-in-law, Chang Shi, was a woman from a southern mountain region, married to a student named Chen Dunfu. After her husband died in one year, Chang Shi wanted to remarry, but the Chen family did not allow it, and she died of depression. She was buried behind the academy, during the reign of Emperor Tianshun, when this place was desolate. My brother saw her beauty and his own perceived power as a fox spirit, and he took her. Three years later, my brother also died. I was raised by my brother, and after his death, I lived with my sister-in-law. Whatever she desired, I could not prevent. Now I live alone in the courtyard behind the old paulownia tree, not with my sister-in-law. Li survived because I saved him; why am I being blamed?" Hu said, "Then what can be done about your sister-in-law?" The moonlit figure replied, "Who says there is no authority in the unseen realm?" Hu then gathered others, prepared incense and paper offerings, and prayed to the city god, burning incense and offering prayers. Within a few days, although Li's illness did not improve, the study was quiet and free of ghostly and fox spirit traces. Everyone believed it was due to Hu Sheng's efforts. Hu also said, "This is because of my integrity and the gods' attention."

    Tr. 3:
    Hu Sheng was always ready to confront the situation with a sword. One day, he sat upright in Li's study, listing the crimes of the ghosts and fox spirits and criticizing them. Suddenly, a voice from above, that of the moonlit figure, said, "Mr. Hu, do not speak ill. I did not harm Li. If Li were not with me, he would be in the grave now. All these troubles are caused by my sister-in-law. My sister-in-law, Chang Shi, was a woman from a southern mountain region, married to a student named Chen Dunfu. After her husband died in one year, Chang Shi wanted to remarry, but the Chen family did not allow it, and she died of depression. She was buried behind the academy, during the reign of Emperor Tianshun, when this place was desolate. My brother saw her beauty and his own perceived power as a fox spirit, and he took her. Three years later, my brother also died. I was raised by my brother, and after his death, I lived with my sister-in-law. Whatever she desired, I could not prevent. Now I live alone in the courtyard

    476

    忽一日，胡生方独坐，有老妇汹汹入，探胡须而批其颊曰：“吾女与汝何仇，汝以刁词告于神而羁之？我媳不良，我不问汝，但还我女儿便休！”既而几砚笔墨、床帏盆盎满室飞扬，抛砖掷瓦。胡大惧，跪而祝曰：“上仙姑姑毋怒，我愿保尔千金完璧归赵也。”老妇曰：“如此速行，否则瞰尔室而为烬矣！”胡即濡墨书状，忙忙赴庙，祷祀以求，然后得安。及今李斋中亦毫无动静。李病归章，迄今五载，药炉刀圭，未尝斯须去诸身。噫，淫之为害，大矣哉！

    太恨生
    檇李朱云，年十五入庠，翩翩少年也。尝自期云：“生不愿封万户侯，但愿得一温柔乡，足矣。”蚤失怙恃，家素封，十八娶妻甄氏，亦故家女。貌微寝，有麻，然性颇贤淑。朱恶之，尝作《太恨歌》以自释。其词云：

    Explanation

    476

    Suddenly one day, Hu Sheng was sitting alone when a fierce old woman stormed in, grabbing his beard and slapping his cheek, saying, "What wrong has my daughter done to you? You have used cunning words to accuse her before the gods and have her detained! My daughter-in-law is not good, I won't ask you about it, but just return my daughter, and that will be the end of it!" Soon, objects such as desks, inkstones, brushes, bed curtains, and pots flew around the room, and bricks and tiles were thrown. Hu Sheng was greatly frightened and knelt down, praying, "Auntie Immortal, do not be angry. I will ensure your daughter returns safely and unharmed." The old woman said, "If you act quickly, otherwise I will burn your house down!" Hu Sheng immediately dipped his brush in ink and wrote a document, hurriedly went to the temple, prayed and worshipped to seek help, and then he was able to rest. Now, in Li's study, there is no movement. Li fell ill and returned home, and until now, five years have passed. He has never been without medical tools and ingredients for a moment. Alas, the harm caused by lust is great indeed!

    Song of Great Regret
    Zhu Yun from Zui Li, at the age of fifteen, entered the school, a graceful young man. He once said to himself, "I do not wish to be enfeoffed as a marquis with ten thousand households, but I only wish to find a gentle and peaceful place, and that will be enough." At an early age, he lost his parents, and his family was wealthy. At the age of eighteen, he married Zhen, a woman from a distinguished family. Her appearance was slightly ordinary, and she had smallpox scars, but she was very virtuous and gentle. Zhu disliked her and once wrote the "Song of Great Regret" to console himself. The lyrics say:

    If you want to continue the translation, please provide the missing part of the source text. Otherwise, this is the final translation based on the provided text.

    477

    春风琼树发华姿，璧月圆时芳梦魄。曲江江头有碧波，洗净铅脂浣香泽。
    琉璃擘碎琥珀枝，伤心惟问西湖客。窅娘素袜漫凌云，广袖昭阳舞盈尺。
    淡妆彩笔描不成，芙蓉金屋新歌拍。莫道枇杷花下居，空洞无人罘罳隔。
    风翾珠绿佩瑶珍，万絮千言常脉脉。恨不机丝午夜虚，鸾镜飞天宝钗只。
    凄凄切切《白头吟》，千载河魁永今夕。梅花纸茧佛龛灯，抱膝长吟霜露白。
    生因自号为“太恨生”。夫妇异室，终年不内。暂有私蓄，亦储于外。同里有少年与生交，尝引生以狭斜游。生曰：“若桃叶小星，足下当为我物色之，虽一斛珠不惜也。”时当寒食节扫祭，嘉兴最胜，梨花草径，四野如市，芳树之下，游女云集。少年谓生曰：“君欲得一佳丽，此其时乎！”生喜，与之偕，出郊踏青。虽往来于钗行粉队间，却了无一当意者。

    477

    Spring brings forth the graceful beauty of jade-like trees, and under the full moon, fragrant dreams take form. At the head of Qujiang River, clear blue waves cleanse the lead and rouge, leaving behind a fragrant dew.

    Glass shatters the amber branches, and heartbreakingly, only the guest of West Lake can ask. Yǎoniáng, with her white socks, soars into the clouds, and her wide sleeves dance gracefully in the palace of Zhaoyang, reaching the length of a foot.

    With light makeup and colorful brush, the essence of beauty cannot be captured, and in the golden house, new songs are sung. Do not say that living under the loquat flowers is secluded, for it is empty and desolate, separated by a fence.

    The wind flutters green pearls, and precious jade is adorned with countless threads and words. Regretting the empty silk thread at midnight, the phoenix mirror flies to heaven, and the precious hairpin is alone.

    Sad and poignant, the White-haired Lament echoes, and the star of the river forever lingers tonight. Plum blossoms, paper cocoons, and the lamp of the Buddhist altar, hugging knees, long recitations in the frost and dew white.

    The man named himself "Too Much Hatred." Husband and wife live in separate rooms, never cohabiting. Temporary savings are also stored outside. A young man from the same village befriended him, once leading him through the narrow alleys. The man said, "If a small star on a peach leaf, you should find it for me, even if it costs a measure of pearls." During the Cold Food Festival, when sweeping and sacrificing, Jiaxing was the most beautiful, with lilac and grass paths, fields like markets, and under fragrant trees, girls gathered. The young man said to him, "Do you want to find a beautiful girl? This is the right time!" The man was happy and went out to the suburbs with him for a spring outing. Though he wandered among rows of girls with hairpins, none of them pleased him.

    478

    至半桥，少年临流徙倚，与生攀新柳枝。忽见上流轻舠如箭，无篷盖。榜人外，船头一老仆倚祭盒；中舱坐一丽人，珠翠压钿，面白可鉴，两目若有曼光，衣帔素饰，绝世如仙，倚栏凝眸；身后立一侍女，发槛垂髫，亦韶秀不凡。少年指生盼，生举目情移，而行舟如驶。生曰：“国色也。惜仅睹其半，下为亚栏所蔽。使我作舟楫，当尽去此槛。然至纤腰已断魂矣！”少年云：“何不袭之？”生喜，连步以往。望前舟转入柳湾，欸乃声渐远。生足疲，少年壮之。又三四里，见岸上肩舆至，急奔视，丽人已登舆中，尚启帘若有所注。肩者如飞而去。生狂喜，欲随所至。少年曰：“夕阳已挂树杪，请访诸诘旦。”生不可，纳履以奔，少年目送之，自返。

    Explanation

    478

    At the half-bridge, the young man stood by the stream, aimlessly strolling and playfully climbing new willow branches. Suddenly, he saw a light boat upstream, moving swiftly like an arrow, without a canopy. Outside the boatman, a servant sat at the bow, leaning against a box for offerings; in the middle cabin, a beautiful woman sat, adorned with precious jewels and jade, her face pale and translucent, her eyes glowing with a subtle light. She wore a simple yet elegant robe and veil, her beauty unparalleled and fairy-like, gazing fixedly at the railing. Behind her stood a servant girl, with her hairband and braids hanging down, also graceful and beautiful. The young man pointed to the scholar, hoping for something, and the scholar raised his eyes, moved by emotion, as the boat sped away. The scholar said, "She is the beauty of the nation. It's a pity that I only see half of her, as the lower part is obstructed by the railing. If I were the boatman, I would remove this railing completely. But even the sight of her slender waist alone is enough to captivate me!" The young man said, "Why not follow it?" The scholar was delighted and stepped forward continuously. He watched the boat turn into the willow-lined bend, the sound of the oars gradually fading away. The scholar's feet grew tired, and the young man encouraged him. Another three or four li later, they saw a sedan chair approaching the shore. The scholar hurried to look, but the beautiful woman had already entered the chair, still opening the curtain as if paying attention to something. The bearers carried the chair away like a bird. The scholar was wildly joyful and wanted to follow wherever it went. The young man said, "The setting sun is already hanging on the tips of the trees. Let's visit in the morning." The scholar refused, put on his shoes, and ran. The young man watched him go and returned on his own.

    479

    生望舆直前，意在攀辕一顾。孰意心急者步转迟，比到门，丽人已下舆入门内，裙幅尚拖限外。转盼间，乌云偏反之状已不可复得，惘惘若失，踬踱沉思。见墙垣周绕，门径深閎，为王司李别业，素本游观之所。视门上头衔正新，酉昏犹可辨识。俄一女子自门内问生曰：“适桥头相遇者，君耶？”生曰：“然。”女子曰：“吾家千金，颇属意君。当入门时，犹见君踉跄随至。千金，司李公女也，适抱伤春小恙，城中颇厌烦嚣，因就小园将息。今命奴出视，属君以十五晚黄昏候至，与君好合。即君之同行人，切勿预问。”生唯唯，惟命如行符。女子阖门，生亦返。终日静坐，惟待月圆，即一至郊外园门探望，除老仆奔走外，并不见女裙迹，且少年亦不一至。

    Explanation

    479

    The young man looked forward to the carriage, intending to catch a glimpse of it. Unbeknownst to him, the more anxious he was, the slower his steps became, and by the time he reached the door, the beautiful woman had already stepped down from the carriage and entered the gate. Her skirt still trailed outside the gate. Within a blink of an eye, the cloud-like hair had already changed direction and could no longer be seen as it was. Feeling lost and disoriented, he stumbled and paced in deep thought. Seeing the walls surrounding the area, the deep and grand entrance, he realized it was the private residence of the high-ranking official, a place that had always been frequented for sightseeing. He looked at the new title on the gate, which was still readable even in the twilight of the evening. Soon, a girl came out from inside the gate and asked the young man, "Is that you, the person I met at the bridge?" The young man nodded, "Yes." The girl said, "My daughter, the young lady, has taken a liking to you. When she entered the gate, she still saw you stumbling and following her. The young lady is the daughter of the high-ranking official. She has been suffering from a slight ailment of spring sorrow, tired of the noise and bustle in the city, so she has come to rest in the small garden. Now she has sent me out to see you, and instructed you to wait for her on the fifteenth night at dusk, to join you in marriage. As for your companion, do not ask about it in advance." The young man nodded and obeyed orders as if following a magic spell. The girl closed the gate, and the young man returned. He sat quietly all day, only waiting for the moon to be full. He would go to the garden gate in the suburbs to look for her, but except for the old servant running around, he did not see any trace of the girl's skirt. And the young man did not come once.

    480

    届期日夕，生独往。无何月上，至门，扃如故。徬徨久之。听扊扅微启半缝，仅容一身，有人弹指声，生就之。女附耳曰：“来勿躁，恐阍者觉。”生牵女衣，女阖门，导生入。蟾光照彻，见女子裙下如舡，心颇惜焉。至内寝，灯烛辉一室，女曰：“千金犹在床笫。”乃搴帏邀生入。生见屋中华丽异常，一切器用多未曾睹。绣帐半开，丽人披衣伏枕，半体犹抱衾裯。床前设一几，几上炉烟缕缕，絪缊扑鼻。丽人云：“清明时节，得睹丰标。弱质担薪，至今转增十倍。”生曰：“自愧葑菲，何当顾爱。相思刻骨，两地同情。

    Explanation

    480

    By the time of evening, the protagonist went alone. Soon after the moon rose, he reached the door, which was still locked as before. He wandered around for a long time. He heard the door slightly opening a crack, just enough room for one person, and someone was making clicking sounds with their fingers. The protagonist approached it. The woman whispered in his ear, "Don't be hasty, for fear the doorkeeper might notice." The protagonist pulled the woman's clothes, and the woman closed the door, guiding him inside. The moonlight illuminated everything, and he saw the woman's skirt trailing like a boat, feeling a deep sense of pity. He reached the inner chamber, where the room was lit by lamps and candles. The woman said, "The precious one is still in bed." She then drew back the curtain and invited the protagonist inside. The protagonist saw the room was unusually beautiful, with all the furnishings being things he had never seen before. The embroidered canopy was half open, and the beautiful woman was lying on the pillow, half covered by the quilt. A table was set in front of the bed, with incense smoke curling from it, the fragrance strong and pleasant. The woman said, "On the Qingming Festival, I was able to see your beauty. My frail self carrying firewood, your beauty has increased tenfold since then." The protagonist replied, "I feel ashamed of my own worthlessness, how could I deserve your care. The longing is so deep, and we share the same feelings, even though we are in different places."

    481

    今复得亲芳芷，觉游魂入窍。”因移步近榻。丽人指女瀹茗以进。女执盏坠地铿然。户外一哄，排闼而入健仆十馀辈，执生缚之。指床上丽人曰：“千金千金，素然百计苛求，使我等常辱鞭楚。今住此调养病体，乃私窠人耶？当于大人前白之。”遂牵生。丽人泣，乃搥床为生告免，愿以簪珥赎生。仆不可。生跪请曰：“吾自有囊金，盍取诸家中？”仆笑曰：“尔市我。汝出吾门，吾乌乎索汝金？”不许。生又曰：“有旧相识，乞爪牙一唤来则得金。”仆问金数，生愿以百金奉。仆怒曰：“少八百金不能赎！”乃实应以六百金。羁之外廊。

    Explanation

    481

    Now, using the research and context we gathered above, produce a final, polished translation of the source text.

    Your goal is to deliver a final English translation that is both faithful to the original Classical Chinese and reads naturally in English. Apply the insights from the pre-drafting research directly — handle idiomatic expressions, cultural allusions, syntactic ambiguities, and proper nouns as you planned.

    Here are 5 examples showing the expected translation quality and style:

    Example 1

    Source: 爰及農商工賈，廝役奴隸，釣魚屠肉，飯牛牧羊，皆有先達，可為師表，博學求之，無不利於事也。

    Translation: As for farmers, merchants, artisans, and traders, as well as servants, laborers, and slaves, those engaged in fishing and butchering, feeding oxen and herding sheep—all have predecessors who can serve as role models. By seeking knowledge broadly from them, nothing is unhelpful in one's endeavors.

    Example 2

    Source: 兩頭四臂女

    靈帝時，洛陽女子生時兩頭四臂。

    Translation: A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    Example 3

    Source: 凡為文章，猶乘騏驥，雖有逸氣，當以銜勒制之，勿使流亂軌躅，放意填坑岸也。

    Translation: In all writing, it is like riding a swift steed — though it may possess a wild and unrestrained spirit, one must control it with bit and bridle, and must not let it run wild, straying from the proper path or indulging in aimless wanderings.

    Example 4

    Source: 自古明王聖帝，猶須勤學，況凡庶乎！此事遍於經史，吾亦不能鄭重，聊舉近世切要，以啟寤汝耳。

    Translation: From ancient times, wise kings and sage emperors still required diligent study; how much more so, then, for common people! This matter is found throughout the classics and histories. I cannot elaborate further, but I will briefly mention a few essential points of recent times in order to enlighten you.

    Example 5

    Source: 夫文章者，原出五經：詔命策檄，生於書者也；序述論議，生於易者也；歌詠賦頌，生於詩者也；祭祀哀誄，生於禮者也；書奏箴銘，生於春秋者也。朝廷憲章，軍旅誓誥，敷顯仁義，發明功德，牧民建國，不可暫無。至於陶冶性靈，從容諷諫，入其滋味，亦樂事也。行有餘力，則可習之。

    Translation: As for writings, their origin lies in the Five Classics: edicts, memorials, and military proclamations arise from the Shujing (Book of Documents); expository and argumentative writings arise from the Yijing (Book of Changes); songs, odes, and praises arise from the Shijing (Book of Poetry); ritual sacrifices, mourning, and elegies arise from the Liji (Book of Rites); memorials, admonitions, and inscriptions arise from the Chunqiu (Spring and Autumn Annals). Court regulations, military oaths and decrees, the proclamation of benevolence and righteousness, the elucidation of virtue and merit, governing the people and establishing the state—all are indispensable. As for cultivating the mind and spirit, offering gentle admonition in a relaxed manner, and savoring the flavor of such writings, these too are pleasant pursuits. When one has spare energy, then one may study them.

    Source text:
    今复得亲芳芷，觉游魂入窍。”因移步近榻。丽人指女瀹茗以进。女执盏坠地铿然。户外一哄，排闼而入健仆十馀辈，执生缚之。指床上丽人曰：“千金千金，素然百计苛求，使我等常辱鞭楚。今住此调养病体，乃私窠人耶？当于大人前白之。”遂牵生。丽人泣，乃搥床为生告免，愿以簪珥赎生。仆不可。生跪请曰：“吾自有囊金，盍取诸家中？”仆笑曰：“尔市我。汝出吾门，吾乌乎索汝金？”不许。生又曰

    482

    顷，少年至，见生，惊曰：“尔何来此地？”生泣告以故。少年跌足怨咎。仆曰：“愿以官休耶？”生急，告少年家中外室有藏金所，实六百金，并钥箧。少年霎而返，负以革囊，如数与仆。仆释其绁，中一仆曰：“去固去，还当留此一双耳为记。不然，恐他日无据证。”生股栗，罔知措。少年曰：“不必，吾愿以此二物赎之。”袖中突出两金钏，亦生所藏物也。众始平。少年携生出，送诸其家。生谢少年而德之，然自悔则无地矣。后少年不知所往，生终恋恋丽人。又访于王司李家，则官京门，家中并无眷属。生疑而不敢白。

    Explanation

    482

    Soon, the youth arrived and saw the protagonist, exclaiming in surprise, "Why are you here?" The protagonist told him in tears about the reason. The youth stomped his foot in anger and blamed him. The servant asked, "Are you willing to accept a position in exchange for the gold?" The protagonist became anxious and told the youth that there was a place in his home where gold was hidden, actually six hundred taels, along with the key and box. The youth returned quickly, carrying the gold in a leather bag, and gave the servant the amount as agreed. The servant released the rope. One of the servants said, "You may go, but you should leave these two earrings as a mark. Otherwise, we might have no evidence later." The protagonist trembled with fear and did not know what to do. The youth said, "It is not necessary. I am willing to redeem them with these two items." He pulled out two gold bracelets from his sleeve, which were also items the protagonist had hidden. The crowd finally calmed down. The youth took the protagonist out and sent him to his home. The protagonist thanked the youth and felt grateful, but he had nowhere to hide in regret. Later, the youth disappeared without a trace, and the protagonist always longed for the beautiful woman. He went to visit again at the house of Wang Si Li, who was an official in the capital, but there were no family members at home. The protagonist became suspicious and dared not tell anyone.

    483

    逾年，生举孝廉，部铨授湖广湘潭令。旧尹交案牍，指曰：“此一宗略骗财物者，未审结。”生如期升堂询，吏呼名至，则首犯固同里少年也。生骇。继而前老仆一，健仆数人亦在焉。末一犯年约二十，颇清俊，虽不识，忆面颇善。乃执少年诘之，伏罪。此年少者，盖昔之垂髫女也。问前司李之女安在，犯供曰：“亦男子也，五年病死淮西。”生乃知前日少年同游，而后不与偕行，以及突出金钏之矫变种种也。夫又知前日丽人在舱不起，入舆至捷，门内留裙，床前襆被，总为此半尺莲不能变化，为之多方回护耳。

    Explanation

    483

    The following year, the protagonist was recommended as a filial and upright official and appointed as the magistrate of Xiangtan, Hunan. The former magistrate handed over the case files and pointed out, "This case involves slight fraud involving property, and it has not yet been concluded." The protagonist ascended the hall on schedule to inquire, and the clerk called out the name, revealing that the main culprit was indeed a young man from the same village. The protagonist was startled. Shortly after, the former old servant and several strong servants also appeared. The last defendant, a young man about twenty years old, was particularly handsome and well-mannered. Although the protagonist did not recognize him, he remembered his face as being familiar. The protagonist then took the youth and interrogated him, and the youth confessed his guilt. This young man was actually the girl from his childhood. When the protagonist asked about the daughter of the former magistrate, the defendant reported, "She is also a man, and he died of illness in Huai West after five years." The protagonist then realized that the youth from the previous day had been his companion, and the various strange transformations, such as the sudden appearance of a golden bracelet, were all because the half-foot lotus (a metaphor for a woman's foot) could not change, so they went to great lengths to protect it. The protagonist also knew that the beautiful woman from the previous day did not leave the cabin, entered the carriage quickly, left her skirt inside the door, and left her bedding on the bed.

    484

    嘻，亦诡异矣哉！而生现断之狱，殆亦类是。于是生疑释，而囚狱成，如律论。遂传其事于楚湘间。
    (七如氏曰：钗荆裙布，昔人所称，故娶妇在德不在色。生奈何厌弃糟糠，狎昵恶少至是？此同里少年，亦阴伺其自内朽也，而后从而蠹之耳。
    有传奇一本名《风流误》。)

    郝骧
    柘城郝骧，性佻达，渔于色。凡闾里戚党少女嫩妇，无不品骘而加之。偶策蹇郊外，平畴款段，颇涉遐思。瞥见一女子年十七八，美绝，着挑线紫花鞋，手障小鹅翎箑。时夕阳在树杪，姗姗遵大路来。骧忆前后村无其人，滋惑之甚，下驴曳尾行。女去颇疾。忽道旁有白杨树横石板桥，篱笆茅屋，掩映柴门。女子入，骧继至。素往来，未见道旁有筑室，乃纵驴咬草根，坐石桥以伺。

    Explanation

    484

    Ah, how strange! The present case of a broken family is also similar to the previous one. Thus, the prisoner's case is decided, and it is judged according to the law. The story is then spread in Chu and Xiang.

    (Seven-like-surname said: "Wearing hairpins and skirts, as the ancients said, marriage is based on virtue, not appearance. How could the person have abandoned his wife and become close to bad company? This fellow from the same village was secretly watching him and then corrupted him. There is a play titled 'The Mistake of Gallantry.'")

    Hao Xiang, from Zhazhou, was a person of light and frivolous nature, fond of pursuing women. He would evaluate and pursue young women in his community, regardless of their age or marital status. Once, while riding a donkey in the countryside, he was lost in thought. Suddenly, he saw a beautiful young woman, about seventeen or eighteen years old, wearing purple flowered shoes with embroidered threads, and holding a small goose feather fan. At that moment, the sun was setting behind the trees, and she was walking slowly along the main road. Hao Xiang remembered that he had never seen this woman before in the villages around, and he was very curious. He dismounted from his donkey and followed her, dragging his tail behind him. The woman left quickly, and Hao Xiang was puzzled. Suddenly, he saw a white poplar tree over a stone bridge, with a thatched house behind a fence and a gate. The woman entered, and Hao Xiang followed her. He had never seen a house built in this place before, so he let his donkey graze on the grass roots and sat on the stone bridge to wait.

    485

    闻内呼“二娘子”曰：“月已三分，挂翡翠天，盍闩白板扉？想野外无游客踪也。”乃见妇人立门外招之曰：“幸居停为一夕淹。”骧径入，见妇人四十许，着淡黑比甲，盘鸦皆茉莉香。闭户，前导入草舍。几榻虽设，而一灯惨淡，规模狭小。骧四顾，妇笑曰：“目灼灼贼视何物？”骧曰：“适归来者，宅上何人？”妇曰：“我义妹小心也。本城中门户家，因衙官新莅，逐流娼，故携来作侨寓。”骧请见，妇曰：“请贽。”骧便揭里衣作势曰：“野中无赘，请以矢遗。”妇掩口他顾。骧起挽之怀，手摸豆蔻，舌度丁香，两人遂合。

    Explanation

    485

    Hearing someone call from inside, "Second Madam," she said, "The moon is three parts up, hanging in the green sky, why not lock the white wooden door? I think there are no tourists in the wild." Then she saw a woman standing at the door, inviting her to stay for one night. Xiang entered directly and saw a woman in her forties, wearing a light black jacket, with her hair adorned with jasmine-scented hairpins. She closed the door and led him into a thatched cottage. Although the furniture was set up, the light was dim, and the place was small. Xiang looked around, and the woman laughed and said, "What are you looking at so suspiciously?" Xiang said, "Who is the one who just came back?" The woman said, "My sister-in-law, who is careful, is from a family of a certain status in the city. Because the new official arrived, he expelled the prostitutes, so she came here to stay temporarily." Xiang asked to meet her, and the woman said, "Please bring a token." Xiang then pulled up his inner garment and said, "There is nothing extra in the wild, please give me a token." The woman covered her mouth and looked away. Xiang then embraced her, touched her bean pod, and kissed her with his tongue, and the two then joined together.

    486

    骧固伟器，工内媚，床帷间颇称快意。骧见壁间挂檀槽一柄，问谁善此，妇曰：“此小心消遣物也。”骧曰：“何不一见？”妇以指弹壁间，女子振帏而出。衣碧纱裙，仅披下体，白皙皙两乳如蒸麦包，上点作樱桃粒，指骧曰：“驴子背上憨憨想，今日得甜头子，当饱啖归。”骧狎谑之。妇设酒果，三人坐月下饮。半酣，行枚，负者唱，胜者饮，名“苦中乐”。首骧胜，小心执檀槽唱曰：
    一湾月儿天边挂，闷倚窗纱对着他。无端的钩起心中事，钓动俺愁中话。月儿呀，你为甚不常圆，待圆来，又恐怕那人儿骂。

    Explanation

    486

    Xiang is truly a great talent, skilled in pleasing internally. In the bedroom, they find great pleasure together. Xiang sees a musical instrument hung on the wall and asks who is skilled with it. The woman replies, "This is a small item for leisure." Xiang says, "Why don't we see her?" The woman points to the wall, and the girl, dressed in a blue silk skirt, emerges from behind the curtain, barely covering her lower body. Her white breasts are as soft as steamed wheat buns, with red dots like cherries. She points at Xiang and says, "You’ve been dreaming on a donkey’s back, today you’ll enjoy yourself and go home satisfied." Xiang teases her. The woman sets up wine and fruit, and the three sit and drink under the moon. After they are half drunk, they play a game called "苦中乐," where the loser sings and the winner drinks. The first round is won by Xiang, and the girl takes the lute and sings:
    A moon hangs by the sky, I lean by the window, gazing at it. It stirs up my heart, bringing forth my sorrowful words. Moon, why are you not always full? When you are, I fear that person will scold me.

    487

    二巡妇胜。骧不能唱，罚以巨碗。骧醉，固乐甚。妇起撤肴核，女扶骧入室。骧于此时酒兴勃发，而女情缱绻，骧不能自持。妇在旁更啮指作馋涎状。继则骧力已尽，不堪其扰，二人相与叠就淫焉。骧惟有长卧以请，供置俎上，纵其大嚼而已。
    昏沉间，犹觉捻臂推摇，不使便安稳也。忽闻驴嗥嗥鸣，身乃伏草露中。天大亮，僵不能起。路者见之，舁以归。由是病痪，绝子嗣，三十而殂。人谓柘北城外乱冢旁多狐狸穴云。
    (此当入“果报类”存之。实则删之更净。)

    褚小楼
    褚小楼，名宇。美丰仪，儇薄不谨，称之狡童。善度曲，工笛，江宁人。父母早逝，纳粟成均。有其外祖姑之从侄李某，官于杭，往依之。李见褚才而美，叙中表，通于内，颇见亲昵。一月之后，衣服鲜好，入则群婢星从，出则众仆鹄立，固翩翩佳公子也。无事则夫人令谈故事消闲，或于良夜月明酒阑更尽时，令吹笛按曲，至乐也。

    Explanation

    487

    Final Translation

    During the second round, the woman emerged victorious. Xiang could not sing and was punished with a large bowl. Drunk, he was indeed very happy. The woman got up and removed the dishes and fruits. The girl helped Xiang into the room. At this moment, Xiang's wine enthusiasm was at its peak, and the girl's affection was deep and tender. Xiang could not control himself. The woman, standing beside him, bit her fingers and made a hungry expression. Then, Xiang's strength was exhausted, and he could not bear the disturbance; the two of them engaged in sexual intercourse. Xiang could only lie down and ask, placed on the table, letting her indulge herself.

    In a drowsy state, he still felt her pinching and pushing, not allowing him to rest comfortably. Suddenly, he heard the loud braying of a donkey, and he found himself lying in the grass under the dew. It was very early in the morning, and he was stiff and unable to move. A passerby saw him and carried him home. Thereafter, he became paralyzed, had no children, and died at the age of thirty. People say that there are many fox dens near the chaotic graves outside the city of Zhebei.

    (This should be included in the "Retribution" category. In fact, it would be cleaner to delete it.)

    Chu Xiaolou
    Chu Xiaolou, whose name was Yu, was handsome and graceful. He was known as a cunning boy due to his flirtatious and careless nature. He was skilled in composing melodies and playing the flute, and he was from Jiangning. His parents had passed away early, and he became a student through a contribution of grain to the Imperial Academy. He had a cousin, Li, who was the daughter of his maternal grandmother, and she was an official in Hangzhou. Chu went to live with her. Li saw Chu's talent and beauty, and they became close. After a month, Chu's clothes were fresh and good, and when he entered, a group of maids followed him like stars, and when he left, many servants stood like swans. He was indeed a handsome young nobleman. When there was nothing to do, the lady would have him tell stories to pass the time, or during a fine night with bright moonlight and the end of the drinking, she would have him play the flute and compose music, which was the greatest pleasure.

    488

    当褚初至，志不过温饱。今温饱矣，又思逸乐。署有婢微云，年十六，丰颐颊。李每欲私之，时见嫉于夫人，隔而不通。婢固黠甚，见褚少，尝立夫人后，目憨憨视，褚惑之。无人处褚招云，云即近褚。褚甫欲昵云，云辄批褚颊而去，清越有声，遥指而笑，胡卢不止。是云之恶谑，每以是绐其主人翁，而李且无奈伊何。
    褚思所以治之之法，以为微云之黠，不可以情惑，不可以威屈，不可以词说，并不可以利动。计惟远嫌自敛，见之不与狎近。独检新奇可喜之物贮斋中，如半开花、迎鲜果以及西洋画、自走人、百步灯、千里镜，莫不列满几壁；而窗前鹦鹉、画眉呓呓勾人，最足遣人怀抱。兼之丝弦檀槽，正复聒耳。

    Explanation

    488

    When Chu first arrived, his aspirations did not exceed being warm and well-fed. Now that he had achieved warmth and sustenance, he began to desire leisure and pleasure. In the office, there was a maid named Wei Yun, aged sixteen, with plump cheeks. Li always wanted to have an affair with her, but he was often jealous by his wife, who separated them and prevented communication. The maid was indeed very clever. Seeing Chu was young, she would stand behind his wife, looking at him with a naive gaze, which bewitched Chu. In private, Chu would call out to Wei Yun, and she would then approach him. Just as he was about to be intimate with her, she would slap his cheek and leave, with a clear and resonant sound, pointing at him from afar and laughing without stopping. This was Wei Yun's malicious teasing, which she would often use to tease her master, and Li was helpless against her.

    Chu thought about how to deal with this situation. He believed that Wei Yun's cleverness could not be swayed by emotion, nor could she be intimidated by force, nor could she be persuaded by words, and certainly not by material gain. The only plan was to avoid suspicion and restrain himself, not being intimate with her. He alone collected new and delightful items to store in his study, such as half-bloomed flowers, fresh fruits, Western paintings, self-moving figures, hundred-step lamps, and thousand-mile mirrors, all lined up on the walls. Parrots and orioles before the window, whispering and enticing, were most capable of dispelling one's worries. Plus the sound of silk strings and wooden slots, which were even more annoying.

    489

    一日，云至斋外呼曰：“褚公子，店中料理，曷借我鹦哥？耍耍便见还也。”褚方置一狸于膝上，染以浑身蓝翠。微云见之入室，视狸曰：“狸有此色，异甚。”褚怀之固不与看，云争之。褚抱云求欢，云不能脱身，绐褚曰：“青天白日，我不干此龌龊事。盍于今夕会‘橄榄轩’中？”轩在署西偏，闲所也。夜深，褚潜至。是时月色微茫照室中，褚视榻间一人脱衣，莹白而卧，以为是云。褚乃解衣来就偎，而榻上人已来抱褚。忽惊释，曰：“尔为谁？”褚知是李，不得已曰：“小子蒙尊丈豢养过厚，无物可报，谨以粗豚为寿。望笑而纳之。”李固有馀桃好，以亲串故不敢唐突。今既自投，乐甚。褚本个中人，颇能曲体上意，可以不劳凿枘。

    Explanation

    489

    One day, Cloud came to the study outside and called out, "Master Zhu, the shop is busy. Why not lend me your parrot? Just play with it for a bit and I'll return it." Zhu was just placing a cat on his knees, dyeing its whole body blue and green. Cloud, who was inside, saw it and said, "The cat has such a color, it's very strange." Zhu, holding it tightly, would not let him see it. Cloud argued about it. Zhu hugged Cloud to seek pleasure, but Cloud could not escape. Cloud deceived Zhu by saying, "On a clear day, I wouldn't do such a dirty thing. Why not meet in the 'Olive Pavilion' tonight?" The pavilion is in the west wing of the office, a quiet place. At night, Zhu secretly arrived. At that time, the moonlight was dimly illuminating the room. Zhu saw a person in the bed undressing, pale and lying down, thinking it was Cloud. Zhu then undressed and came to embrace. Suddenly, the person on the bed surprised and released, saying, "Who are you?" Zhu knew it was Li and reluctantly said, "I, a young man, have been excessively nurtured by your esteemed father, and have nothing to offer in return, so I humbly offer this coarse pig as a gift. I hope you will accept it with a smile." Li, who had always been good, dared not be bold because of family ties. Now that he has thrown himself at me, I am very happy. Zhu, being one of them, can understand their intentions well and thus avoid unnecessary friction.

    490

    先是，李招云，云不就。因褚要盟，于是乎一转移间，先约褚，继又约李。而云固知李之必上小楼也。后李与褚密，颇就外寝，家人衔之，诉于夫人。夫人一日与云潜出轩外，舐窗而视良久。归曰：“毋怪乎今人爱男子而薄妇人也。今观小楼之鞠躬尽瘁，摇尾乞怜，两人复上下其手，吐而仍茹，诚有味乎其津津也！不然，我何以实染指于鼎乎？”遂恶褚，不容于署。李私以百金遣之。小楼归，金尽而贫，遂为伶。年四十，犹有人见其傅粉登场，娉婷昵人云。

    Explanation

    490

    Previously, Li invited Yun, but Yun did not accept. Because of Chu's demand for an alliance, in a short time, Li first made an agreement with Chu, then again with Li. Yun certainly knew that Li would definitely go up to the small pavilion. Later, Li met Chu secretly and went to the outer bedroom. The family members held a grudge against this and complained to the lady of the house. One day, the lady of the house and Yun secretly went outside the veranda and looked through the window for a long time. Upon returning, she said, "It's no wonder that people today love men and look down on women. Now, observe the Little Pavilion toiling and exhausting himself, wagging the tail and begging for mercy, while the two manipulate things with both hands, spitting it out and then swallowing it again. Indeed, it is very interesting and appealing! If not, why should I actually taste the food in the pot?" She then hated Chu and could not tolerate him in the office. Li secretly sent Chu away with a hundred gold pieces. The Little Pavilion returned, spent all the money, and became poor, then became an actor. At forty years old, some people still saw him put on makeup and appear on stage, graceful and charming.

    491

    赵殿臣
    海阳赵殿臣，失怙恃，未有室家。幼好樗蒲，尝一掷输一婢，千金产皆荡尽。孑然一身，遂为穿窬。夜入人家，不计物值，即鸡鹜之属，皆攫取之。一日向暮，行村落间，遥见败墙茆屋之中，灯光闪烁，趋而就，无人焉。赵识同博之周三家，四顾一无所有，惟炕席上鼾卧一婴儿。赵以妙手不能空空，遂抱之而去。过钱翁之门，忽忆其无子，时尚未下闩，乃求鬻曰：“我前村赵某。妻病死，遗一块肉，不能鞠谋。翁盍抚养之？我不以奇货居。”翁甚喜之，令押字，脱贯数缗，与之而去。

    Explanation

    491

    Sao Dianchen, from Haiyang, was orphaned at a young age and had not yet married. He enjoyed gambling as a child and once lost a maid in a single throw, which led to the loss of all his thousand-gold fortune. Left alone, he became a thief, entering homes at night and taking whatever he wanted, even small animals like chickens and ducks. One evening, as he walked through a village, he saw a dilapidated house with flickering lights. He approached and found no one inside. Recognizing the house as belonging to Zhou San's family, he looked around and saw only an infant sleeping on a kang. Thinking that a skilled hand could not be empty-handed, he took the infant and left. Passing by Mr. Qian's house, he suddenly remembered that Mr. Qian had no children and that the door was still unlocked. He offered the infant for adoption, saying, "I am Sao Dianchen from the village. My wife died of illness, leaving behind a piece of flesh, which I cannot raise. Why don't you raise it? I do not treat it as a rare commodity." Mr. Qian was very pleased and had him sign a contract, paying him several strings of coins, and they parted ways.

    492

    诘朝，村外周家招摇于市，鸣钲揭帖，以其夜失婴儿。钱翁亦闻之而不发。赵心忡忡，遂远颺去。流入关东，二十馀年不作归计。
    当钱翁得周子之后，又生二子。惟周子长成，善持筹，家日富，连阡广厦，周子之力也。虽二子之视兄如手足，而乡人之物议难弭。翁患之，乃三析其产曰：“兄弟无不分之家。与其不分而强合，不如不合而早分之为愈。无以虚名而贻后日之丑也。”又密将赵某押字付周子曰：“他人肉，安不到自己身上。汝自有父，不过寄生我家。但汝事亲持家，实倍于所生，故及我未终，先为汝区处了当。”周子泣受命。后钱翁殁。兄弟瓜分晋国，若赵、韩、魏然。

    Explanation

    492

    The next morning, Zhou's family in the village paraded through the market, beating gongs and posting notices, announcing the loss of an infant that had occurred during the night. Old Man Qian also heard about it but did not act. Zhao was deeply worried and thus fled far away. He migrated to the eastern region of the pass and did not plan to return for more than twenty years.

    After Old Man Qian found Zhou's son, he had two more children. Zhou's son grew up to be good at managing finances, and the family became increasingly wealthy, with connected fields and grand halls, all due to Zhou's son's efforts. Although the two younger sons regarded their elder brother as a hand and foot, the gossip of the villagers was hard to stop. The old man was troubled by this and divided the property into three parts, saying, "There is no family that cannot be divided among brothers. It is better to divide early rather than force a union that is not naturally harmonious. Do not let a false name bring shame in the future." He also secretly gave Zhao's signature to Zhou's son, saying, "Other people's flesh, how can it not reach one's own body. You have your own father, but you were just living off our family. But you have taken care of your parents and managed the household more than your biological parents, so before I pass away, I will take care of everything for you." Zhou's son wept and accepted the order. After Old Man Qian died, the brothers divided the family property like the Three Families did, just as Zhao, Han, and Wei did.

    493

    时周子已纳粟成均，固知赵之为父也，有鬻儿之券；又闻周之亦父也，在里人之评。两存其说，将访诸赵而释疑。有年，忽赵归。周子迎赵，赵直子之。周侦赵归为人父，邀赵而索其儿。赵抵赖，周夫妇曰：“畴昔之夜，我失儿，汝鬻子。且汝故无妻，焉得有子？非吾子而谁子耶？”赵语塞，周索之益力，将成讼。
    周子知其事，造周庐而请解曰：“二老岂相厄哉！皆吾父也。盍归儿家，以终馀年？夫儿之周与不周，与父之窃与不窃，在可知不可知之间。然与其失去一真，恐陷真中之假，莫若尊其二假，终有一假之真。吴楚呼父曰‘爹’，父多之谓也。例有三父，不足多也。”遂请周夫妇同造钱室而受养焉。周与赵同居为父，彼此皆呼亲家，如儿女之姻娅同。

    Explanation

    493

    At the time when Zhouzi had presented grain to the Imperial Academy, it was known that Zhao was the father, and he had a contract for selling the child; it was also heard that Zhou was also a father, according to the village people's evaluation. Both versions were accepted, and Zhouzi intended to visit Zhao to resolve the doubt. After a year, Zhao suddenly returned. Zhouzi welcomed Zhao, but Zhao denied it. Zhou suspected that Zhao had become a father and invited Zhao to demand his child. Zhao denied it, and Zhou's wife and husband said, "The night before, I lost my child, and you sold it. Moreover, you did not have a wife, so how could you have a child? If it is not my child, whose child is it?" Zhao was speechless, and Zhou demanded it more forcefully, and it was about to become a lawsuit.

    Zhouzi knew the matter and went to Zhou's house to propose a resolution: "These two elders are not harming each other; both are my father. Why not let the child return home to live out the remaining years? Whether the child is with Zhou or not, and whether the father is stealing or not, lies in the realm of the known and the unknown. Rather than lose one truth, it is better to avoid being trapped in the truth of a falsehood; it is better to respect the two falsehoods and ultimately find the truth of one falsehood. In Wu and Chu, they call their father 'dad,' which means having many fathers. By example, there are three fathers, which is not too many." So he asked Zhou's wife and husband to come to the money room to take care of the child. Zhou and Zhao lived together as fathers, and they both called each other in-laws, just like the in-laws of children.

    494

    噫！此周子克全骨肉，善处家庭之变，以视宋襄有千乘之国，而不得养其母，其贤不肖何如哉！
    (有此奇事，便有此奇文以传。)

    折铁叉
    折铁叉者，汶上老翁手中物也。壬辰自都返里，小道归山城，宿小孟集旅店。茅屋数椽，檐前风罅罅入窍鸣。主人翁年七十馀，发苍然，健步履。问所自，告以比邻邑，称情款焉。篝火饭疏，皆翁自为奔走。翁曰：“僻野绳枢，客欲卧，当以此物顶撑可也。”视之，乃半截铁叉，约重十馀斤，折叠剥蚀，如海舶大锚柄。讶曰：“此何物也？”翁曰：“嘻，此是衰朽壮岁所弄铁叉也。一折后，盖三十年于兹矣。”予请竟其说，翁曰：“可俟少间。”为马刍豆毕，归谋老妇，持一壶酒来，坐对余言曰：“这铁叉曾与衰朽跋履山川，纵横吴越秦晋间，黄白物取之如几上肉。往往一人一叉，相依相傍，千里若户庭也。即绿林豪客，亦不识我为谁何。

    Explanation

    494

    Ah! This Zhou Zi managed to preserve his family ties and skillfully handled family changes, compared to Duke Song Xiang who had a state with a thousand chariots but could not support his mother. How much more virtuous or less virtuous is he? (With such a strange event, there must be a strange text to record it.)

    The Folded Iron Fork
    The folded iron fork is an object held by an elderly man from Weishang. In the year Renchen, he returned from the capital to his hometown, traveling a small road to a mountain town and staying at a small inn in Xiaomeng Town. The thatched cottage had a few rooms, and wind whistled through gaps in the eaves. The elderly owner of the inn was over seventy years old, with gray hair and walked briskly. When asked where he was from, he replied that he came from a neighboring county, and we treated each other warmly. We lit a fire and cooked simple meals, all prepared by the old man himself. He said, "In this remote place, if you want to sleep, you can use this object to prop up the bed." Looking at it, it was a half-length iron fork, weighing about ten pounds, folded and worn, resembling the handle of a large anchor on a ship. I was surprised and asked, "What is this?" The old man responded, "Haha, this is the iron fork I used when I was young and strong. After being folded, it has been like this for about thirty years." I asked him to continue his story, and he said, "Wait a moment." After finishing feeding the horses, he went back to consult his wife and brought a bottle of wine. He sat down and told me the following: "This iron fork accompanied me in traveling across mountains and rivers, and I roamed freely in Wu, Yue, Qin, and Jin, taking wealth as easily as picking up something from a table. Often, I would travel with just one person and one fork, relying on each other, and thousands of miles away felt like home. Even the bandits in the Green Forest did not know who I was."

    495

    “一年，自汉上归，橐有中产。当初秋夜行，月明野阔，遥见树杂烟稠，高楼连亘，意此必富伧居也。吾囊中尚不满意。乃置行李树杪间，拄叉逾重垣，耸身入院。四面围楼，蟾光照井，人静声寂。因以叉击石阶，鍧然一鸣，以观其动。闻北楼有声曰：‘妮子，看谁来？’南楼忽开如鸟翔下，乃十五六女子，执双刀，光争皎月，挥刀入胁。我则轮叉与斗。斗且久，叉重臂沉不可支；而刃锋雪片，飕飕绕项脑间。将危，楼上声曰：‘止！’女子一跃上楼，窗阖如旧。俄闻北楼下胡梯启门，一凛凛大汉满部髭，执炬曰：‘不速之客，突如其来。请入我室而假榻焉。’我栗慑不敢进。汉喝曰：‘草莱如此，敢夤夜入人家耶？’我乃蹒跚入，坐隅。问乡贯，告以汶南。问此次南来，计所得，告以树杪金。汉曰：‘远来些些，不足充行李。明夕偕我往劫某处，可以满篝。’我唯唯。树间物想汉已攫去矣。

    Explanation

    495

    "In the first year, returning from the upper reaches of the Han River, I had accumulated moderate wealth. During an early autumn night journey, the moon was bright and the fields were vast. Distantly, I saw a mixed forest with dense smoke, and tall buildings stretching continuously, which led me to think this must be the residence of a wealthy person. Still, I was not satisfied with my bag. I placed my luggage on a tree branch, propped up the fork, and climbed over the high wall, stepping into the courtyard. Surrounded by buildings, moonlight shone on the well, and the place was quiet and silent. Then, I struck the stone steps with the fork, making a loud sound to observe their reaction. I heard a voice from the north building say, 'Little girl, see who has come?' Suddenly, the south building opened like a bird descending, revealing a girl aged fifteen or sixteen, holding two swords, whose light competed with the bright moon. She swung the sword into my armpit. I then swung the fork to fight. The fight continued for a long time, and the fork was heavy, and my arm was too tired to support it. The blade's sharpness was like snowflakes, whistling around my neck and head. I was in danger, and a voice from upstairs said, 'Stop!' The girl leaped back upstairs, and the window closed as before. Soon, I heard the ladder at the north building being opened. A stern-looking man with a full beard, holding a torch, said, 'An unexpected guest, arriving suddenly. Please come into my room and rest.' I was trembling and dared not enter. He shouted, 'How could you enter a house at night like this?' I then slowly entered and sat in the corner. He asked about my hometown, and I told him it was in Wenshan. He asked about the trip south and what I had gained, and I told him about the gold on the tree branch. He said, 'A little from afar is not enough for travel. Tomorrow night, accompany me to rob somewhere, and we can fill our bags.' I agreed. The items on the tree must have been taken by the man already.

    496

    “翌午，见所至皆魁梧，十数辈。汉告曰：‘此山东友也，但雏耳，可携往。若有羡，当镖伊。’众诺。晚餐，束装暗器，各选骁骑风驰。而我固健步，遂杂于奔蹄队行中。至客道，闻束铃远哨，哄然而来，乃某省解淤黄项也。其一豪纵辔，弯弓发矢，直入夺一鞘。啸聚群起，绝尘皆奔。惟我以脚力。官弁追捕益迅，忙迫无计，负叉旁逸，伏秫丛中，几就获。踉跄返，而汉已候道左，鹰顾我曰：‘懦奴几败乃事！昨夜来，幸入吾女宅；若西院吾大郎宅中，汝其休矣。今事不济，自贻戚，去休！’我哀之：‘鞘中金，无功诚不敢分惠，盍返我囊中物？’汉眦裂须竖，起曰：‘吾向欲收尔功，故不即灾汝身。汝今更索汝金，汝姑且试吾刃。’遂从腰间掣刀相向。

    Explanation

    496

    The next morning, I saw a group of several imposing men. Han told me, "These are friends from Shandong, but they are just young ones, and we can take them along. If there is any envy or desire, we will rob them." Everyone agreed. After dinner, we equipped ourselves with weapons and each chose a swift horse, galloping away. Since I am naturally a good runner, I mixed among the running horses. We reached a road leading to a guesthouse, where we heard distant bells and warnings, and suddenly a group appeared. They were a group of people from a certain province, with yellow necks (likely referring to a specific ethnic group or criminals). One of them rode freely, drew his bow, and shot an arrow, straight into the group, seizing a scabbard. They gathered and attacked together, all running away, leaving dust behind. But I relied on my running ability. The officials pursued us more quickly, and in a hurry with no plan, I carried a fork and ran away to the side, crouching in a field of sorghum, almost getting caught. Stumbling back, Han was already waiting on the side of the road, looking at me like an eagle and saying, "Weak slave, you almost ruined everything! Last night, luckily, you entered my daughter's house; if you had entered my eldest son's house in the west courtyard, you would have been finished. Now the matter has failed, you have brought this upon yourself, go away!" I pleaded with him, "The gold in the scabbard, without merit, I truly dare not share it. Why don't you return what is in my bag?" Han's eyes widened and his beard stood up, standing up and saying, "I wanted to reward your efforts, so I didn't immediately harm you. But now you are asking for your gold, you might as well try my blade." He then drew a knife from his waist and faced me.

    497

    “我乃走，汉亦不迫。幸我能行，一日夜归。我于是深自悔曰：‘这铁叉十馀年来，未逢敌手。一囊金本非长物，独半生锐气，顿挫于小女子、髭汉之手。今使不自计决，还向豺虎猿猱之际，枭争夺劫，其祸恐益烈矣。’因断此叉，誓不复用。今田园自力，梁间事绝口不谈。积有馀储，结庐道旁，终老天年，盖亦幸矣。我年七十有三，已事耳，亦足以佐一觞乎？参横夜半，客请执折叉，御户以安。”

    铁腿韩昌
    韩昌，汶上人，幼佣于路氏。路子弟喜讲少林拳勇之技，韩从旁剽窃，颇有所得。曾一腿扑倒败堵，人遂呼为“铁腿韩昌”，而昌亦顾盼自喜。及壮，恃其能，遂流为匪，充兖州捕。百里之间，眼目悉熟，狗偷辈亦时纳小贡献韩，固一时叱咤，称泗水雄。

    Explanation

    497

    "I then fled, and the Han did not pursue. Fortunately, I was able to travel, and I returned in one day and night. I deeply regretted my actions and said to myself: 'This iron fork has not met an opponent in over ten years. A bag of gold is not a valuable possession, but my lifelong vigor was abruptly halted by the hands of a woman and a bearded man. Now if I do not make a decision for myself, returning to the midst of wolves and monkeys, engaging in banditry and plunder, the disaster will surely be even more severe.' I then broke this fork and swore never to use it again. Now I till my own fields and never speak of the affairs of the bridge. I have accumulated some surplus, built a cottage by the roadside, and will spend my remaining years in peace, which is also fortunate. I am seventy-three years old, and my life is nearly over, but is it enough to serve one cup of wine? At midnight when the Big Dipper is horizontal, a guest asked me to hold the broken fork, guarding the door to ensure safety."

    Iron-Legged Han Chang
    Han Chang was from Wen Shang. As a child, he worked for the Lu family. The Lu family's children enjoyed discussing Shaolin martial arts, and Han Chang secretly learned from them, gaining some skill. Once, with a single kick, he knocked down a wall, and people began to call him "Iron-Legged Han Chang," and Han Chang took pride in this. When he grew up, relying on his abilities, he became a bandit and served as a police officer in Yanzhou. Within a hundred miles, everyone knew him, and thieves would sometimes offer him small contributions. At that time, he was a powerful figure, known as the hero of Sishui.

    498

    日者，遂批出缉寿昌境。宵征独行，遇见村外有茅舍数间，灯光一缕出篱落中。探之，板扉半掩，土炕上坐一二十许妇人，发漆漆，着淡红裤，穿小靴，理缫车轧轧不绝。韩知其非尴尬者，遂排闼入。妇手轧而问曰：“尔来寻谁？”韩曰：“寻伴尔者。”近妇前蹲为语。妇微哂，跂足交韩裆，韩仰仆，曰：“蹄子敢恶作剧！”及韩起，而妇人已面立，执浣杵扫韩胫，复仆。韩怒，起右腿，妇右腾；起左腿，亦左腾。方一转踔，韩三仆。妇乃骑韩背，举杵击其胯。韩疼欲折，忍不敢声。妇人拖地上箔，卷扎韩为捆，倒栽于室南隅，妇仍纺绩如故。
    俄而其夫归，妇告之曰：“深更不返，席中人访汝者，想已睡熟。”其夫解视，则名捕韩昌，旧曾相识。妇人笑而致词曰：“伯伯莫嗔奴太孟浪。幸伯伯不复饶舌，倘絮絮然，将杵断小骨子！”其夫亦笑责之。时东方既白，妇入厨，罗酒浆作炊饼，韩乃强打精神啖而去。自此豪气顿淡于初云。

    Explanation

    498

    On a certain day, an order was issued to search the Shouchang area. Han Chang, a famous hunter, traveled alone at night and came across a few thatched cottages outside the village, with a single thread of light emanating from the fence. He approached and found the wooden door half open, revealing about twenty women sitting on an earthen bed. They had black hair, wore light red pants, and small boots, and were operating a silk-reeling machine that made a continuous grinding sound. Knowing they were not suspicious, Han pushed the door open and entered. One of the women, holding the machine, asked, "Who are you looking for?" Han replied, "I'm looking for someone to accompany me." He knelt in front of her to speak. The woman slightly smiled, lifted her foot and kicked Han's crotch. Han fell backward, exclaiming, "You dare to play tricks!" When Han stood up, the woman had already stood facing him, holding a washing stick and sweeping Han's shins, causing him to fall again. Han became angry and lifted his right leg, but the woman jumped to the right; when he lifted his left leg, she also jumped to the left. Just as he tried to turn, Han fell three times. The woman then straddled Han's back and lifted the stick, hitting his hips. Han was in pain and wanted to cry out, but he held back and did not make a sound. The woman dragged him onto the mat on the ground, rolled him up into a bundle, and threw him upside down in the south corner of the room, while she continued to spin as before.

    Soon after, her husband returned, and the woman told him, "You didn't return late at night. Someone who visited you, probably already asleep." The husband looked at Han and recognized him as the famous hunter Han Chang, someone he had met before. The woman laughed and said, "Uncle, don't be angry that I was too reckless. Fortunately, you didn't scold me. If you had kept talking, I would have broken your bones with the stick!" Her husband also laughed and scolded her. When the east was already bright, the woman went into the kitchen, prepared wine and soup, and made steamed cakes. Han forced himself to eat and then left. From then on, his boldness was much diminished.

    499

    (按：先岳孔德溢公、韩毓光，早年失怙。入武庠，性慷爽，有勇力。家日落，尝从草泽中游，与绿林辈往来甚悉，尝得其润馀以为供给。一日午间，至颜家楼之关圣庙，酣睡神案下，梦帝呼之：“快入城去，干正经勾当！”醒不为异，复睡。又呼之如前。遂入城。时出示招募勇健营入伍，遂应名随征噶尔，以军功得守御。乾隆年间，洊升至粤省军标游击。尝行刑海盗，其队兵决囚不如法，自撩衣手刃卅馀囚，无一失者。其勇力能挟八十斤铳，发机御敌。又言曾在至圣庙中，随班襄祀。族官轻其武职，慨然曰：“诸君顶戴红蓝，皆沾祖宗馀荫耳。若我这官职，是冷枪头热肚皮挣得来的。”韩昌等辈，皆其少年所结识者。)

    平顶僧
    有贵公子某，载多金入长安，匆匆舆马，时露仓皇之象。值巨盗十馀人，侦而随之。公子亦疑其为盗，悉戒备相持而行。忽当暴雨猝至，轨泞辔濡，遂不能按程站，栖野店中。公子忧惧，将慼慼而靡骋。

    Explanation

    499

    (Annotation: My grandfather, Kong Deyi, and Han Yuguang, lost their father at an early age. They entered the military academy, where they were generous, straightforward, and physically strong. Their family fell into decline, and they often wandered in the wilderness, associating closely with outlaws. They sometimes obtained surplus wealth from these outlaws to support themselves. One midday, they went to the Temple of Guan Yu in Yancha Lou and fell into a deep sleep under the shrine. In their dream, the emperor called out to them: "Hurry into the city and engage in serious business!" They woke up but did not consider it unusual and fell back asleep. The emperor called out to them again as before. They then entered the city. At that time, there was an announcement recruiting brave soldiers for the military.
