They responded to the call and joined the campaign against the Gar people, earning military merit and becoming defenders. During the Qianlong era, they were repeatedly promoted to the rank of military commander in Guangdong. They once executed pirates, and when the soldiers in their unit carried out the executions improperly, they personally executed over thirty prisoners by hand, none of whom were mishandled. Their physical strength allowed them to carry an eighty-pound gun and fire it to repel enemies. They also mentioned that they once participated in rituals at the Temple of Confucius, accompanying the ceremonies. Clan officials looked down on their military rank, and they said with a sigh: "All of you wear red and blue insignia, which you inherited from your ancestors. As for my rank, it was earned through hard work and risk." Han Chang and others were acquaintances from their youth.)

    A wealthy young nobleman traveled to Chang'an with a large sum of money, his carriage and horses moving hurriedly, often showing signs of panic. Coincidentally, he was followed by a group of over ten large bandits. The young nobleman also suspected them of being bandits and kept himself on high alert as they traveled together. Suddenly, a heavy rainstorm struck, making the roads muddy and the reins wet, so they could not stay on schedule and had to take refuge in a wild inn. The young nobleman was filled with fear and worry, feeling anxious and unable to move forward.

    500

    先是店中有一人居西屋中，倚门望雨。公子入，见其昂藏修伟，异之。通询，问曰：“贵客途中未遭淋耶？”客答曰：“幸而免。”公子遂邀与坐谈，颇倾肝胆。二人共饮，公子忽郁郁不乐。客问故，公子以盗伺告。客毅然曰：“今夜公子但请高枕，吾将俟之而甘心焉。”公子起谢，就安置，并令从人皆寝，凡有声息勿哗。
    客亦闭户独坐，舐窗外视。月照院庭，净洗如水，光芒可鉴毫发。闻壁垣间，如鸟隼飞落，乃一人逾垣入院。客窗罅以气吹之，其人首落地上。逾时，又一人至，客又吹之，凡十馀吹，而尸已枕藉庭阶。客忖曰：“殛盗何必尽灭其口，使即不留遗类，谁知吾刃之有馀。何如存一不必胜诛之人，令其试吾锋之若顿。”又一人入四顾，客但以气微嘘其顶，似切瓜一片，其人抱头跳出，自是寂然。

    Explanation

    500

    Previously, in the inn, there was a man staying in the west room, leaning against the door and watching the rain. When the nobleman entered, he saw the man standing tall and majestic, and was surprised by him. The nobleman greeted him and asked, "Did you get rained on during your journey, esteemed guest?" The man replied, "Fortunately, I was spared." The nobleman then invited him to sit and talk, and they shared their thoughts deeply. The two drank together, but the nobleman suddenly became gloomy. The man asked why, and the nobleman told him about the thieves waiting outside. The man resolutely said, "Tonight, my lord, you may rest easy. I will wait for them and satisfy my heart." The nobleman stood up and thanked him, then settled down and ordered his attendants to sleep, instructing them not to make any noise. The man also closed the door and sat alone, peering through the window. The moonlight illuminated the courtyard, washing everything clean like water, and its brightness could reflect even the finest details. He heard a sound from the wall, like a bird of prey landing, and saw a man climbing over the wall into the courtyard. The man used breath to blow through a crack in the window, and the thief's head fell to the ground. After a while, another man arrived, and the man blew again, and after more than ten blows, the bodies were lying strewn on the courtyard steps. The man thought to himself, "Why kill all the thieves? If no one is left alive, who would know that my blade has more to spare. How about leaving one who is not worth killing, to let him test my sword's sharpness." Another man entered and looked around, and the man gently blew on the top of his head, like slicing a piece of melon, and the man grabbed his head and jumped out, and everything became silent.

    501

    及曙，公子起，客启户，见尸大惊。客乃告以歼之之故，且言有一后至者，但去其头而逸，想此人或未至死。继出一金盒，以指匙药弹尸上，皆化为水。公子乃知其为侠，厚赠之，不受，问其姓名亦不答。送之出，客跨卫拱手，遂去。
    后十年，公子在京师红寺与一僧友善，尝对弈，往来过从。每至盛暑，僧汗流不脱帽，公子固请，僧坚不肯除。一日，又对弈，公子戏以扇柄挥之，僧帽落，见平顶如劈瓠，不生毛发，惟斑疤类大莲蓬。公子笑问故，僧踟蹰曰：“十馀年前，未尝不头角峥嵘也。缘无行为盗，夤夜入人家，不知被何冷气吹去顶皮，濒死，许久创合。至今犹不敢脱然于王公大人之前也。”公子曰：“是某年月日雨后旅店事乎？”僧惊栗。公子曰：“我即载金人也。兹汝已逃禅，且为我友，不汝究矣。”遂释然。僧每问，公子亦含糊应之，自此僧之棋，顿挫于公子云。

    Explanation

    501

    At dawn, the young lord awoke, and the guest opened the door. Seeing the corpse, he was greatly shocked. The guest then told him the reason for the massacre, and mentioned that there was one who came later, but only removed his head and fled, perhaps this person was not yet dead. The guest then took out a golden box, used a spoon to sprinkle medicine on the corpse, and all turned to water. The young lord then knew that the guest was a hero. He offered him a generous gift, which he refused, and when he asked for his name, he did not answer. The young lord saw him off, and the guest crossed his hands and bowed, then left.

    Ten years later, the young lord became friendly with a monk at the Red Temple in the capital. They often played chess and visited each other frequently. Every time during the hot summer, the monk sweated profusely but did not remove his hat, and the young lord repeatedly asked, but the monk firmly refused to take it off. One day, they played chess again, and the young lord playfully swung the fan handle at him. The monk's hat fell off, and he saw a flat top like a split gourd, with no hair growing on it, only scars like large lotus pods. The young lord laughed and asked the reason. The monk hesitated and said, "More than ten years ago, I was not without a prominent forehead. Because I had no moral principles and became a thief, I sneaked into people's homes at night. I did not know that a cold wind blew off the top of my head, nearly killing me. After a long time, the wound healed. I still cannot act carefree in front of nobles and officials." The young lord said, "Is this the incident at the inn after the rain on that particular day?" The monk was startled. The young lord said, "I am the man who carried the golden box. Now that you have become a monk, and you are my friend, I will not investigate you further." And thus he felt relieved. The monk often asked, and the young lord responded vaguely. From then on, the monk's chess skills were no match for the young lord.

    502

    放鹰
    今北省有一种撞骗之人，往往以己之妻女，充为嫠妇室女，待售于人。中其术者，廉其值而得之。归不旋踵，稍失防范，即乘隙而逸。实则返其夫若父家。而其夫若父，转至买其妻与女之门，百般诈索，名曰“放鹰”。盖言鹰得兔，而鹰亦能还；放之云者，有收之之道在焉。
    直隶南宫艾姓，年二十，未娶。值岁祲，入京师，为人佣仆。数年积中人产，遂欲作归计。辞主人，囊资市一小驴，迢迢策蹇，官道扬鞭，意甚得也。时当春暮，行至献县界，遥见新柳成行，绿荫掩映。道旁有老翁与一少女，丰姿娟好，着浅翠衣、黑绸裤、小白鞋，坐树根呜咽而泣。旁羁一大腹牝驴，咬地上草。艾驴过，见之嘶鸣。艾随下骑休息，向翁乞火吸烟。

    Explanation

    502

    Translation:

    Plucking the Eagle

    In the northern provinces, there is a kind of swindler who often passes off their own wife or daughter as a widow or unmarried girl, selling them to others. Those who fall for their trick buy them at a low price. But the moment they return home, if they fail to guard themselves properly, the swindler seizes the opportunity to escape. In reality, the wife or daughter returns to her husband's or father's home. And then, the husband or father turns up at the door of the person who bought their wife or daughter, demanding exorbitant compensation in various deceptive ways. This is called "plucking the eagle." The term means that just as an eagle catches a rabbit, it can also return; the phrase "plucking the eagle" implies that there is a way to recover what was lost.

    There was a man named Ai from Nángōng, Hebei Province, who was twenty years old and had not yet married. During a famine, he went to the capital to work as a servant. After several years, he had saved enough money to return home. He left his master, packed his belongings, and bought a small donkey. He traveled a long distance on the donkey, galloping along the official road, feeling very pleased with himself. It was late spring when he reached the border of Xiàn County. From afar, he saw rows of new willows, with green shadows covering the road. Beside the path, there was an old man and a young woman, both of whom were beautifully charming. The woman was wearing a light green dress, black silk pants, and white shoes, sitting on the ground by a tree and weeping softly. Beside her was a pregnant female donkey, nibbling at the grass. As Ai's donkey passed by, it neighed. Ai dismounted to rest and asked the old man for a light to smoke.

    503

    翁击火递艾。艾问翁何往，翁告以接女归宁者。女拭泪睨艾，面转之他。翁忽叹低首，艾曰：“令爱归家当欢聚，何以悲为？”翁曰：“客何处人？”艾曰：“南宫。”翁曰：“乡里也。客有所不知，吾女适献人某，贫甚。去年婿死，又无儿女。吾家又屡空，老妻又下世。今我父女如无主孤魂。我又衰迈，今日不知明日事，是以相对欷歔耳！”艾曰：“何不择一婿家？”翁曰：“我本穷困，女又在献，谁复有问蹇修者？”艾曰：“天下男子而无妻者多，未有女子而无夫者。翁不为之急，择斯已耳！”翁曰：“曾娶否？”艾曰：“固未有室家也。”翁曰：“我皆井里，如不以弱息丑陋，愿结褵焉。”艾喜动眉宇，曰：“我方远归，匆匆道途，何以为情？”翁曰：“会向前行，旅店中再作计议。”

    Explanation

    503

    The old man struck the fire and passed the mugwort to Ai. Ai asked the old man where he was going, and the old man told him that he was going to fetch his daughter for her visit home. The daughter wiped her tears and glanced at Ai, then turned her face away. The old man suddenly sighed and lowered his head. Ai said, "Your daughter should be happy at home when she returns; why be sad?" The old man replied, "Where are you from, guest?" Ai answered, "From Nangong." The old man said, "We are fellow villagers. You may not know, but my daughter was married to a man named [X], who is very poor. Last year, her husband died, and they had no children. Our family is also often in dire straits, and my old wife has passed away. Now my daughter and I are like soulless spirits without a master. I am old and weak, and I do not know what will happen tomorrow; that is why we are sighing together!" Ai said, "Why not choose another husband for her?" The old man said, "I am originally poor, and my daughter is already married. Who else would ask about a matchmaker?" Ai said, "There are many men in the world who do not have wives, but there are no women who do not have husbands. You should not be in such a hurry; just choose one." The old man asked, "Have you ever been married?" Ai replied, "I have not yet married." The old man said, "We are both from the same village. If you do not consider my daughter to be ugly or unattractive, I would be willing to marry her to you." Ai's expression brightened, and he said, "I am just returning home, and I am in a hurry on the road. How can I discuss this now?" The old man said, "We will continue on our way, and we can discuss further at an inn."

    504

    翁牵驴掖女乘，艾随牵其驴，随女驴后，与翁同行。女曼视艾，艾不转睛，而翁若不闻不见。翁渐落后，女忽回首，据鞍微笑，艾以目承之。无何，白日西匿，牛羊下来。至一村墟，翁与艾入旅店，有房三间，一堂屋，东西两夹室。翁曰：“僦住不必更分彼此，但是一家人，吾与尔东西住可耳。”艾首肯。女入进西间，艾以行李进东间。翁时出，或与馆人计刍豆，或办晚餐。艾在室襆衣被，女挑之曰：“中堂有门，东西何以不设？”艾曰：“既可同室，自无庸隔阂矣。”女嗤然作声。既翁入，陈馔。店主人持灯至，翁与艾同食，又分其半与女在东间食。艾与翁且饮，半酣，艾又提女事。翁作醉状曰：“一诺千金，何悔之有？”呼女出，入东室与艾成偶。曰：“旅中不事繁文。明日归，尔即偕女至家可也。”艾起欲行半子礼，翁曰：“不必也，我醉欲眠。”遂起身入西间，作酣鼾响。

    Explanation

    504

    The old man led the donkey and guided the woman to mount it, while Ai followed, leading his own donkey behind the woman’s. They traveled together. The woman looked at Ai slowly and lingeringly, and Ai stared back without blinking, though the old man acted as if he neither noticed nor cared. Gradually, the old man fell behind. Suddenly, the woman turned her head, leaning on her saddle and smiling. Ai reciprocated with a look. Soon, the sun set in the west, and cattle and sheep began to descend from the hills. They arrived at a village market, where the old man and Ai entered an inn. The inn had three rooms: a main hall and two side rooms. The old man said, “There’s no need to divide the space further; we’re like one family. You and I can live in separate rooms.” Ai nodded in agreement. The woman entered the western room, and Ai brought his luggage into the eastern room. The old man occasionally stepped out, either discussing fodder and feed with the innkeeper or preparing dinner. Inside his room, Ai packed his clothes and bedding. The woman called out, “There’s a door in the middle hall, but why aren’t there doors between the east and west rooms?” Ai replied, “Since we can share the same room, there’s no need for separation.” The woman laughed softly. When the old man returned, he set out the food. The innkeeper brought a lamp, and the old man and Ai ate together, sharing half their meal with the woman in the eastern room. As they drank, the old man became slightly tipsy, and Ai brought up the matter of the woman again. The old man, feigning drunkenness, said, “A promise worth a thousand pieces of gold—what regret could there be?” He called the woman out and entered the eastern room with Ai. “In travel, we don’t need to go through formalities,” he said. “Tomorrow, you can bring her home with you.” Ai rose to perform a half-brotherly courtesy, but the old man waved him off. “There’s no need,” he said, “I’m too drunk to care.” With that, he got up and went into the western room, making sounds of deep sleep.

    505

    艾收酒盏，阖户，持灯入。女乃坐炕沿，视艾，连点其首，以足敲床枨登登然。艾即解衣偎女，欲接其吻，女箝口不与。艾曰：“此天缘配定。”女曰：“恐是人谋算就耳。”艾即与女解衣，见其白馥双乳，十分酥软可爱，及解下体，裈带纠结，牢不可开。艾急曰：“安得并州快剪，割此幅巾。”既而以口啮带，艾即昏然仆地，盖翁以迷药置结上，俟其啮脐而中之也。翁此时过东间，扶艾卧床，取艾资。侵晓，翁起备驴，见艾驴伟，遂牵艾驴驮其行载，命女骑。呼店主人曰：“吾婿尚寝，留其脚力，我与女先行。”主人不知，遂听其戴星而去。

    Explanation

    505

    Ai collected the wine cups, closed the door, and carried the lamp inside. The woman sat on the edge of the kang, looking at Ai, nodding her head repeatedly, and tapping the bed frame with her feet, making a clattering sound. Ai immediately undressed and leaned toward the woman, trying to kiss her, but she clamped her mouth shut and refused. Ai said, "This is a fate ordained by heaven." The woman replied, "It might just be a human plot." Ai then tried to undress the woman, seeing her soft, white breasts, which were extremely lovable. When he tried to untie her undergarment straps, they were tightly knotted and impossible to untie. Ai exclaimed, "Where is the sharp knife from Bingzhou to cut this wide band?" Suddenly, he fainted and fell to the ground. It turned out that the old man had placed a love potion on the knot, waiting for her to bite it and fall under its effect. Early in the morning, the old man got up and prepared the donkey. Seeing that Ai's donkey was strong, he took Ai's donkey and loaded it with their belongings, telling the woman to ride it. He called the innkeeper and said, "My son-in-law is still asleep, so I will leave his donkey behind. I and the woman will go first." The innkeeper, unaware of the trick, let them leave under the stars.

    506

    日至午，主人不见客起程，隔窗呼不应，惊而入视，犹睡。摇弄逾时，醒曰：“失睡矣。”问妻与岳安在，主人曰：“令正与泰山半夜行矣。”艾仓皇起，搜其箧荡然一空。问主人：“何以令贼窃吾金去？”主人曰：“畴昔之夜，客以为妻与岳也；今去矣，即以为贼。倘令岳与尔妻不去，将妻与岳乎？抑贼乎？”艾不能对。乃整其疲牝驴，挂被囊，丧气出村。驴忽而抵西行，艾挽勒，驴奋不遵道，踶趹而奔。艾愤，追而骑之，任其往，颇驶。

    Explanation

    506

    The sun had reached midday, but the host did not see the guest preparing to leave. He called through the window, but there was no response. Startled, he entered the room to find the guest still asleep. After shaking and coaxing him for a long time, the guest woke up and said, "I overslept." The host asked where his wife and father-in-law were, and the guest replied, "My wife and father-in-law left at midnight." The guest, now in a state of panic, searched his box and found it completely empty. He asked the host, "How did thieves manage to steal my gold?" The host responded, "Last night, you thought your wife and father-in-law had left; now that they have gone, you assume they are thieves. If your father-in-law and wife had not left, would it be your wife and father-in-law? Or thieves?" The guest could not answer. He then groomed his tired female donkey, hung his travel bag, and left the village in a dejected state. Suddenly, the donkey headed west. The guest pulled and restrained it, but the donkey struggled and did not follow the path, kicking and galloping. Angered, the guest chased and mounted the donkey, letting it go, and it moved quite fast.

    507

    如道已经，羁之返则不可，心异甚。过数村，不少停。约三十里，抵一庄，半掩山麓，草茨数椽。驴忽入一柴门，艾方欲下，见女立院中。女见艾曰：“郎来耶！甚好，吾将与尔偕行。”艾欲争诘，女曰：“甚毋哗。我父放鹰，常以此诳少年行旅，非止一次。我诚不愿为此。今趁我父远集去，至暮始回，尔金在笥，尔驴在厩，我将怀细软随郎去。此则郎之所谓天缘配合，有非人谋所及料者也。迟则生变矣！”艾乃喜，女入室，怀资，艾即备其驴，随以牝驴满载。

    Explanation

    507

    The journey had already begun, and turning back was impossible, the heart greatly changed. Passing through several villages, they did not stop at all. About thirty miles, they arrived at a village, partially hidden at the foot of a mountain, with a few thatched cottages. The donkey suddenly entered a wooden gate. Ai was just about to get off when he saw a woman standing in the courtyard. The woman saw Ai and said, "You came, Sir! Very good, I will go with you." Ai wanted to argue, but the woman said, "Do not make a fuss. My father used to let his hawk fly, often using this to deceive young travelers, not just once. I truly do not want to do this. Now that my father is far away, he will return at dusk. Your money is in the chest, your donkey is in the stable. I will carry my valuables and follow you. This is what you call a heavenly match, something beyond human planning. If delayed, things will change!" Ai was then happy. The woman entered the room, carrying her belongings. Ai then prepared his donkey, and they loaded it with a female donkey.

    508

    女与艾出村，跨镫扬鞭上路。牝驴与艾驴俱，亦复驯然就道，遂归南宫完娶焉。后翁半载得女耗，来访艾。艾告女，女即出见翁曰：“鹰其脱鞲，随狗走矣。东门之故智，此后不必复想。翁其归乎，毋落我女红。”女遂入，不复与见。
    (七如曰：放鹰老翁，可谓揣摩家第一上乘，馀智皆出其下。想其操术也常，其行事也易，其用人也则床头膝下，不必株连党羽；其攫资也则探之囊而取诸宫。即使其谋败也，妇口可以出走，较之白撞、念秧、打絮巴种种，变幻险幸皆不若。计出房中，挟此旖旎袅娜一具，单刀直入，稍假裙带滋味，则垂垂者自然脱贯而入我篝，亦巧矣！而翁竟何如？是可慨耳！)

    Explanation

    508

    The girl and Ai left the village, stepped onto the stirrup, raised the whip, and started on the road. The female donkey and Ai's donkey followed, quietly and willingly following the path. They then returned to the southern palace to complete the marriage ceremony. After half a year, the father-in-law heard news of his daughter and visited Ai. Ai told the daughter, and she came out to see the father-in-law and said, "The eagle has broken free from its glove and follows the dog around. The old strategies of the eastern gate are no longer needed. Father-in-law, please return, and do not interfere with my domestic affairs." The daughter then went inside and did not see him again.
    (Comment: The father-in-law, known as the "old eagle handler," could be considered the best in his craft, surpassing others in his methods. His techniques were common, his actions were easy, and he used people close to him without involving party members. He could easily obtain wealth by reaching into the treasury and taking from the palace. Even if his schemes failed, the woman could leave with her mouth intact, unlike other methods such as robbery, fraud, and deception, which were more risky and uncertain. His plan involved using a charming and graceful woman to directly approach the target, and with a little help from her connections, the desired outcome would naturally fall into his hands. What happened to the father-in-law? It is truly a matter of regret!)

    509

    卷十五 物类
    人参考
    参之为用也神，故其为物也贵。所以有非常之用者，斯有非常之宝。士人寒素，淡泊自甘，无需乎此。然或进以奉亲，假以调摄，即不能少。至今大官贵贾，在所常御，且大内尤为珍重。依古以来皆有之。明季，沁州、高丽、邯郸、百济、泽州、箕州、并州、幽州、妫州、易州、平州并产焉，而上党山谷者为最。上党，今潞州太行紫团山，又出紫团参。高丽、新罗、百济，其参结子，十月下种，如种菜法。春苗于山阴，初生一丫五叶，四年丫二，十年丫三，再久丫四，各五叶，中一茎。三四月花细如粟，蕊如丝，色紫，秋结子类小豆，七八枚，自落。

    Explanation

    509

    Chapter 15: Classification of Objects
    Human Reference
    The use of ginseng is divine, hence the object itself is precious. The reason for its extraordinary use is that it possesses extraordinary qualities. Scholars who are content with a simple life and find satisfaction in modest living do not need it. However, if offered to honor one's parents or used for health maintenance, it cannot be spared. Even today, high-ranking officials and wealthy merchants commonly use it, and it is especially valued in the imperial court. Since ancient times, it has always existed. During the late Ming dynasty, ginseng was produced in various regions including Qin Prefecture, Korea, Handan, Baekje, Ze Prefecture, Ji Prefecture, Bing Prefecture, You Prefecture, Gui Prefecture, Yi Prefecture, and Ping Prefecture, but those from the mountain valleys of Shangdang are the best. Shangdang, now part of Lü Prefecture, includes the Purple团 Mountain, which also produces Purple团 Ginseng. In Korea, specifically in Goryeo and Baekje, the ginseng seeds are planted in October, similar to planting vegetables. The seedlings grow on the shaded side of the mountain, initially with one branch and five leaves. After four years, they have two branches, after ten years, three branches, and after a longer period, four branches, each with five leaves and one stem. In March and April, the flowers are small like millet, with silk-like stamens and a purple color. In autumn, the fruits resemble small beans, about seven or eight in number, and fall naturally.

    510

    我朝独重辽参，实乃神草，王气所重，味胜力洪，他皆不及。其产地则曰凤凰城，土人采取甚早。又有船厂，去凤凰城三四千里，稍坚实，六七月可采。又宁古台，地处极北，去船厂又五千里，地极厚，天极寒，深秋冰雪载道，采以八九月，其体坚实，少糙而多熟。盖产参之地，本不止于凤、厂、台，要得以凤、厂、台概之。如新城、旧城，地道杂，多不一，惟以参之色光、体圆、质熟、内湛为上，论货不论地也。

    Explanation

    510

    Our dynasty alone values ginseng from Liaodong, which is indeed a divine herb, valued by the royal aura, with a superior flavor and great power, unmatched by others. The place of production is called Fengcheng, where local people harvest it very early. There is also a shipyard, located about three or four thousand miles from Fengcheng, where the ginseng is slightly more solid and can be harvested in June and July. Another location is Ninggutai, situated at the extreme north, about fifty thousand miles from the shipyard. The land is very thick and the sky is extremely cold, with snow and ice covering the roads in late autumn. It is harvested in August and September, and its body is solid, slightly coarse but mostly mature. The places where ginseng is produced are not limited to Fengcheng, the shipyard, and Ninggutai, but they can be summarized by these three locations. For example, New City and Old City have mixed soil and vary greatly, but only the color, roundness, maturity, and inner clarity of the ginseng are considered the best, regardless of the place of origin.

    511

    参入中土，分等第，有五六十种名目。如拔顶、红塘、西货、统顶、二顶、次顶、大拣、中拣、中子、熟短、中大、二修尖、太参顶、条短、兼皮、顶糙、绉糙、白棍、片料、净须、条参、芦空、红箱底；或以枝干之强弱，颜色之鲜剥，皮芦须末，罔不别类分标，眩人耳目，借以高其价值。因而采取人多，滋长不及。售之者贵，用之者多，其货遂至日低一日，其价因之年长一年。即内府库货，亦无久贮，皆随收随发，以为匪颁。甚矣，人之趋利若鹜也！

    Explanation

    511

    The substance was introduced into the Central Land, where it was classified into grades, with fifty to sixty different names and categories. For example, there are terms such as "top pick," "red pond," "Western goods," "top tier," "second tier," "sub-tier," "large selection," "medium selection," "medium grade," "ripe short," "medium large," "second refined tip," "extra top tier," "strip short," "with bark," "top rough," "wrinkled rough," "white stick," "sheet material," "clean beard," "strip ginseng," "hollow root," and "red box bottom." These classifications are based on the strength of the branches and roots, the freshness of the color, the quality of the bark, and the condition of the hairs and tips, with each type distinctly labeled to dazzle the eyes and ears, thereby raising its value. As a result, more people are involved in harvesting, but the supply cannot keep up. Those who sell it charge a high price, and those who use it are many, causing the goods to gradually become cheaper day by day, while the price increases year by year. Even the goods in the imperial treasury are not stored for long, and they are all collected and distributed as needed. How intense is the desire for profit among people!

    512

    吾登莱一带，游手之民，往往跋涉数万里，偷挖私货。虽法有严禁，皆愍不畏惧。其为害也，害于苦寒，害于窎远，害于虎虫，官禁种种，而惟是营求，哀哉！
    (七如曰：余在边外四年，此条辨证最确，不特得之采访，亦复亲为考据。一物一地，曾无摭饰半字。)

    葫芦枣
    光州城外七里村有媪，家植枣二株。熟时，一道人过而求焉。媪曰：“任自取之。”道人摘食十馀枚。将行，取所佩葫芦挂树上，曰：“谢婆婆意，明年枣当作此状。”后如言。今吾邑亦有此种，是谁怀其核迁地而为良耶？

    Explanation

    512

    In the region of Dengzhou and Laizhou, there are idle people who often travel thousands of miles to secretly excavate private goods. Despite strict laws prohibiting such actions, they show no fear. The harm they cause comes from the harsh cold, the remoteness, the dangers of tigers and insects, and the various official prohibitions, yet they persist in their pursuit, alas! (Qiqi said: I spent four years in the border region, and this statement is thoroughly accurate. I did not obtain this information solely through interviews but also through personal investigation. Every detail is factual, with no embellishment.)

    Hulu Jujube
    In a village seven miles outside Guangzhou, an old woman had two jujube trees in her home. When the jujubes ripened, a Taoist passed by and requested some. The old woman said, "Take as many as you like." The Taoist picked and ate more than ten. As he was about to leave, he took the gourd he was carrying and hung it on the tree, saying, "Thank you, grandmother. The jujubes will be like this next year." As he predicted, the jujubes grew in that manner the following year. Today, in our county, there is also such a variety. Who brought the seeds to this place and made them thrive?

    513

    (曲阜有枣大如杯盏，味亦甘脆。余幼时随先君官粤之南雄，食枣，有名磨盘者，形扁若干柿饼。至于乐陵出无核枣，则人人皆知。至于火枣，又不知如何仙品也？)

    龙[三则]
    乾隆十六年，肥城邱姓家，夏日与二三友坐前轩纳凉。闻雷声隐隐，阴云四布。邱忽抬头，见西墙上一小孔，中挂一小赤蛇，蠕蠕出。群皆指视。欻而坠地，长丈馀，浑身金灿，冷气逼人。继乃霹雳大震，昂首鬐角崭然。雨骤倾注，而身已与一院围径相若，腾升直上。约一炊时，复晴霁，院水深尺许，屋舍树木，了无损伤。人以为瑞，乃于壁上鎸“龙穴”二字。
    又
    诸城某村后有某山，山之中大木蓊葱，有数百年者。某年秋，夜大雷雨，闻巨雷一声，如劈山岳，其尾声如一串金铃朗朗而杳。次日樵者入山，见一大树剖裂，似大刀划开，中夹一爪，大如犀，弯而且利，连以肉筋，血涔涔。佥曰：“此秃爪龙，当入牙牌谱矣。”闻此爪尚在某绅家。

    Explanation

    513

    Qufu has dates as large as cups and saucers, with a sweet and crisp flavor. When I was young, I followed my father to Nanxiong, Guangdong, where we ate dates, one of which was called "mòpán" (磨盘), flat and resembling a persimmon cake. As for the seedless dates from Le陵, they are well-known to everyone. But as for the "huozao" (火枣), I wonder what kind of rare delicacy it is?

    Dragon [Three Stories]

    In the sixteenth year of the Qianlong reign, a family surnamed Qiu in Fancheng spent the summer sitting in the front hall to cool off. They heard distant thunder, and dark clouds spread all around. Suddenly, Qiu looked up and saw a small red snake hanging from a small hole in the west wall, slowly emerging. Everyone pointed and watched. Suddenly, the snake fell to the ground, measuring over ten feet in length, its body shining gold, and the cold air was chilling. Then came a loud thunderclap, and the dragon raised its head, its scales and horns clearly visible. The rain poured down heavily, and the dragon's body had already grown to the size of a courtyard, rising straight up. After about a bowl of rice in time, the rain stopped, and the courtyard was filled with water, about a foot deep. The houses, trees, and everything else were undamaged. People considered this a sign of good fortune and inscribed the words "Dragon's Lair" on the wall.

    Another story:

    Behind a village in Zhucheng, there was a mountain with large trees that had been there for several hundred years. One autumn, during a heavy thunderstorm, a loud thunderclap was heard, like a great mountain being split in two, followed by a series of clear, bell-like sounds that faded away. The next day, when a woodcutter entered the mountain, he saw a large tree split open, as if cut by a great sword. Inside, there was a claw, as large as a rhinoceros horn, curved and sharp, connected to flesh and tendons, with blood flowing. Everyone said, "This is a dragon with a bald claw, it should be recorded in the 'Ya Pai Pu' (a collection of strange phenomena)." It is said that this claw is still in the possession of a certain gentleman.

    514

    又
    即墨宋海月，于雷震之处，拾龙鳞二片，金色，坚厚，大如蛤壳。底面有肉丝如条筋。周如锦赠宋试济南，有诗云：
    里选先居第一人，解名应缀榜头频。山东弟子终童妙，世上文章宋玉真。
    驿路槐花联桂萼，{山昔}湖秋水接天津。君行知作龙门客，昨日攀龙得二鳞。
    是秋果捷。

    大沽桥
    沽水民某姓者，河涨见浮木近岸，欲取之，跨其上，则鳞甲齿齿然。民知为龙，急呼其兄于岸。语未竟，随波去。弥漫无际，水高于顶，而身不沾濡，瞬息飞腾，不计道里。三至大沽河，皆望桥而返。以手扪之，苍苔遍体，螺蛳累结入鳞中，时开时闭。窥其肉红白滑腻，为之摸挲搜剔，意甚得也。

    Explanation

    514

    Final Translation

    Again

    In the vicinity of Jimo, Song Haiyue discovered two pieces of golden dragon scales, hard and thick, as large as clam shells. The underside had meat threads like tendons. Zhou presented them as a gift to Jinan, and there was a poem:

    The village election first placed him as the top candidate, his name should frequently appear at the top of the list. The disciples of Shandong are skilled like Zhong Tong, the world's writings are truly like Song Yu's.

    The willow road is lined with locust flowers and osmanthus buds, the lake of Shanshi connects with the autumn waters of Tianjin. You know you are a guest at the Dragon Gate, yesterday you climbed the dragon and got two scales.

    That autumn, he indeed succeeded.

    Da Gu Bridge

    A certain surname from the Gu Water people saw a floating log near the shore during a river flood. He wanted to pick it up and stepped on it, but the scales were like those of a dragon. The people knew it was a dragon and quickly called his brother to the shore. Before they could finish speaking, it went with the current. Vast and boundless, the water was higher than the top, yet his body was not wet. Instantly, he flew and rose, without counting the distance. He went to the Da Gu River three times, all looking back at the bridge and returning. He touched it with his hand, finding green moss covering its body, snails piled up in the scales, sometimes open, sometimes closed. Peering at the red and white smooth and slippery meat, he rubbed and searched it, feeling very satisfied.

    515

    居水中三日夜，自分无生理，思母而恸，因失声。忽龙腾身，掷民于岸上。归，其母哭之已两旬馀矣。民以思母得生，诚能动物，理固有之。大沽桥在即墨，为道士李常明所建，其或有驱灵伏怪之术也欤？

    南山猎
    东抚某公，将卜某日畋于岱山之阴，以讲武事，实记胜游也。先一日，南山之柳市村农夜归，宿山庙中。未艾，闻庙外有呼殿声，农惧匿龛下。见数人入，执灯烛煌煌，皆五色髯，三目鬼面，抱簿庋案上。一长喙鸠形者持小钲，向外三击，俄而虎狼狐兔，雉雁雀鹞，环骈而来，群伏阶下。髯者曰：“奉命来传集汝百族，来朝抚某猕于此。汝辈若者死于箭，若者死于枪，若者鹰犬，若者网罗。”言讫，按簿而呼，群队俯应。旁一白狐曰：“抚军某公，何等人也？”髯者曰：“不知也。

    515

    For three days and nights, he was in the water, thinking himself doomed, grieving for his mother with deep sorrow, and losing his voice. Suddenly, a dragon rose, and threw him onto the shore. Upon returning, his mother had been crying for him for over two weeks. The man was able to survive because of his thoughts of his mother, truly able to move animals, which is indeed natural. The Great Gou Bridge is in Jimo, built by the Taoist priest Li Changming, who may have possessed the ability to exorcise spirits and subdue strange beings?

    South Mountain Hunt
    A certain official from the east, planning to predict a certain day for hunting in the shade of Mount Dai, to discuss military affairs and to commemorate a successful journey. One day before, a farmer from the village of Liushi in the South Mountain returned home at night and stayed in a mountain temple. Not yet fully recovered, he heard a bell ringing outside the temple. Frightened, he hid under an altar. He saw several figures enter, holding bright torches, all with five-colored beards and three-eyed ghostly faces, carrying a ledger on a table. One with a long beak and a pigeon-like shape held a small gong, striking it three times outward. Soon, tigers, wolves, foxes, rabbits, pheasants, geese, sparrows, and hawks came in a circle, kneeling at the bottom of the steps. The bearded one said, "I have been summoned to gather your hundred clans. Come to pay homage to Governor Fang on this day. Some of you will die by arrows, some by guns, some by hawks and dogs, and some by nets." After speaking, he consulted the ledger and called out, and the groups knelt and responded. A white fox beside him asked, "What kind of person is Governor Fang?" The bearded one replied, "I do not know."

    516

    我但宣示刑名，至于汝等之应死与不应死，抚军之当死汝与不当死汝，吾不与闻。”狐曰：“吾闻某抚贪而残，为众所弃，即宜为神所不佑。况又以游戏之馀，作此鹰鹯之逐，而使者且为之前驱，无乃不可乎？”髯者曰：“我无能也，任自为之。”狐曰：“抚耽酒而渔色。择一人而中伤之，使罢而不猎。吾有妹媚珠，令其今夕往纵淫之，借无良酝投之为饵。”旁有虎头者曰：“我于青州从事处，得苦露一罂，殆六十甲子矣，当助为力，成兹无量功德。”众兽忻舞，各有喜色。髯者执炬皆出。逾时寂然，惟古柏间鸡啼啁啁。翌朝，畋事果寝，又相传开府瘫，不出视事矣。

    Explanation

    516

    I merely announce the names of punishments, but whether you should be punished or not, and whether the military governor should be punished or not, I do not interfere. The fox said, "I have heard that the military governor is greedy and cruel, abandoned by the people, and therefore should not be favored by the gods. Especially since he uses leisure time to engage in such a fierce pursuit, and the messenger will lead the way, is this not inappropriate?" The bearded one said, "I am incapable, let him do as he pleases." The fox said, "The military governor indulges in wine and women. Choose one person to harm, causing them to stop and not hunt. I have a sister named Mei Mie who can go tonight to indulge in lust, using no good wine as bait." A person with a tiger's head beside him said, "I obtained a jar of bitter dew from my service in Qingzhou, it is almost sixty years, I will assist and help to complete this great merit." The beasts were delighted and each had a joyful expression. The bearded one held a torch and they all went out. After a while, it was silent, only the old cypress trees had the sound of chickens chirping. The next morning, the hunting affair was indeed abandoned, and it was also said that the governor-general was paralyzed and did not leave his office.

    517

    (可为耽酒渔色者戒。)

    瞽者搏虎
    诸城一瞽目男子，曳杖独行郊外。遇一虎，以尾扫其面，瞽初不知为虎，误以为人之击己也。弃杖，双手擒之。其尾有毛，始骇大呼。虎亦惊奔，瞽愈不敢稍放松，相与奔。路旁有眢井，虎坠，瞽仆，尾脱手。行人闻而至，扶瞽起于甃，面磕伤；窥虎纳诸穽，身扼毙。

    义鸟亭
    宜兴陆某，善士也。宅多树木，百鸟咸集。亭午夕阳之顷，观其投林如归市焉，更不许人弹射。遇雨雪严冬，取米谷散布林中饲之。一仇家陷陆逆党，絷之公庭，时集讯多人，忽而百鸟盈庭，喧噪震天。讯至陆，一鸟翔下，衔其首辞片纸而去，群鸟飞散。问官惊异，刑其首者，始辨其诬。
    陆构义鸟亭以识异，今在毘陵地方城内。好事者有《禽言》五首云：
    鹁鸪鸪，鹁鸪鸪，声声相唤声声呼。南山有羊我不食，东村晒谷我不图。大家飞入衙堂去，替我恩人雪冤苦。雪冤苦，哀鸣众口皆呶呶。

    Explanation

    517

    Translation

    (To serve as a warning for those who indulge in wine and women.)

    The Blind Man Grappling with a Tiger
    In Zhucheng, there was a blind man who was dragging a cane and walking alone in the outskirts. He encountered a tiger, which swept his face with its tail. The blind man initially did not know it was a tiger, mistaking it for someone hitting him. He dropped his cane and grabbed it with both hands. When he felt the tiger's tail, he was terrified and shouted. The tiger was also startled and ran away, but the blind man was even more afraid and did not dare to let go. They ran together. There was a dry well by the road, and the tiger fell into it, while the blind man fell down and lost his grip on the tiger. Passersby heard the commotion and came to help the blind man up from the well, where he had a bruised face. They looked at the tiger and threw it into a pit, where it was strangled to death.

    The Pavilion of Righteous Birds
    In Yixing, there was a man named Mr. Lu, who was a good person. His house had many trees, and all kinds of birds gathered there. At noon when the sun was setting, he watched the birds fly into the forest as if returning home. He did not allow anyone to shoot them. During rainy, snowy, and cold winters, he took rice and grain and scattered them in the forest to feed the birds. An enemy framed Mr. Lu for being a rebel and tied him in the public court. Many people were gathered for questioning. Suddenly, hundreds of birds filled the courtyard, noisy and loud, shaking the sky. When Mr. Lu was questioned, a bird flew down, took a piece of paper from his head, and flew away. The other birds flew away. The judge was surprised. The person who had his head shaved, then realized he was framed. Mr. Lu built the Pavilion of Righteous Birds to commemorate this strange event. It is now in the city of Piling. A well-meaning person wrote five poems about birds:
    Cuckoo, cuckoo, cuckoo, cuckoo, calling to each other sound by sound. There are sheep in the south mountain, but I do not eat them. There is grain being dried in the east village, but I do not take it. Let us all fly into the courthouse, to clear the injustice and suffering of my benefactor. Clear the injustice and suffering, the mournful cries of many mouths are noisy.

    518

    泥滑滑，泥滑滑，把好人，受冤屈。我喳喳，他咄咄，不把仇人快打煞。欲救恩人真没法，拚将一纸偷衔去，飞飞诉于天公说。
    脱却布绔，脱却布绔，布绔脱却一半破，换来不够衙门数。打通上下谁相助，沉冤若诉还无路。脱绔脱绔，冬冬衙鼓斜阳暮。
    不如归去，不如归去，堂上一呼阶下匍，一声声屈谁相顾。陷人坑阱茫无据，黄绸被底大蒙头。杜鹃啼老庭前树，看看血泪如红雨。
    秦吉了，秦吉了，树间鸣，阶前噪。恩人莫上锁，恩人莫打拷，水落石出寒云散，青天白日自分晓。安得唤醒镜台前，把个官司完结了。
    (平阴田怡亭言：其叔祖母孀居，年七十馀，住楼曾蓄一泰山红脚鸟，六七年能言。以笼为巢，晓出晚归。乾隆甲申，春风异前。一夕鸟忽云：“老奶奶，我明日出去，恐不能回来。”其祖母云：“何如不出去？”鸟云：“数不能逃。”次日果出不归，母思之，竟以疾终。)

    Explanation

    518

    Slippery, slippery, good people suffer unjustly. I chatter, he scolds, do not let the enemy escape unscathed. There is no way to save the benefactor, daringly take a letter and fly away, tell the heavens.
    Take off the cloth trousers, take off the cloth trousers, the cloth trousers are half broken, it is not enough for the official records. Who will help to connect the upper and lower levels, even if the deep injustice is reported, there is no way. Take off the trousers, take off the trousers, the official drum beats in the evening twilight.
    It is better to return, it is better to return, a single call from the hall causes the people below to bow, who will care for the cries? Fall into a pit without any basis, covered with yellow silk, completely hidden. The cuckoo cries under the old tree in the courtyard, look at the blood and tears like a red rain.
    The Qin jili bird, the Qin jili bird, singing in the trees, noisy at the steps. Do not lock the benefactor, do not torture the benefactor, the water recedes, the stones appear, the cold clouds disperse, the blue sky and bright sun make things clear. How to wake up before the mirror, end the case.
    (Tian Yiting from Pingyin said: His great-grandmother was a widow, over seventy years old, lived in a tower and kept a Taishan red-footed bird for six or seven years, which could speak. It used a cage as a nest, leaving early and returning late. In the year of Jia Shen during the Qianlong reign, the spring wind was different from before. One night, the bird suddenly said, "Old grandmother, I am going out tomorrow, I fear I will not return." Her grandmother said, "Why not stay home?" The bird said, "The number cannot escape." The next day, it indeed went out and did not return, and her mother thought about it, and eventually died of illness.)

    519

    鹦鹉辞
    兖州之金乡，有太学生李某，性好音，落魄无聊。畜一鹦鹉，教之逾年而能歌，按板针腔，清婉合律。尝肩负小架，栖鹦鹉于上，跨蹇驴出游，逍遥山水。得意时则命之歌，而自吹笛以和之。久之，邑令麦君子亭，强纳百金以买。生不能辞，听持去，而捐金于途，歌哭尽日乃去。令得之甚喜。明日大会宾客，开筵命歌，而鹦鹉喑然不出一声。不食，数日死。任城王生伯敏言之，因缀以诗云：
    新词自谱教鹦哥，玉笛低吹慢倚歌。倘遇垂虹桥畔路，风流争似小红多。
    人间何事足欢场，策蹇逍遥云水乡。曲子相公真雅韵，按歌犹带雪衣娘。
    教曲经年费苦吟，相依为命更劳心。珍珠一斛倾喉出，金谷无缘惠好音。
    羞向华筵唱渭城，相思一夕顿捐生。吟魂莫恋知音者，安否难传陇上声。

    Explanation

    519

    The Parrot's Lament

    In Jinxiang, Yanzhou, there was a student named Li, who studied at the Imperial Academy. He had a fondness for music and was often aimless and bored. He kept a parrot and taught it to sing for over a year, until it could sing in rhythm and pitch, clear and graceful, in harmony with the rules. He would carry a small frame on his shoulder, place the parrot on it, and ride a slow donkey on outings, roaming through mountains and waters. When he was in high spirits, he would command the parrot to sing, and he would play a jade flute to accompany it. After a while, the county magistrate, Master Mai, forcibly bought the parrot for a hundred gold pieces. Li could not refuse and let him take it, but he discarded the money on the way, singing and crying all day before leaving. The magistrate was very pleased with his acquisition. The next day, he hosted a grand banquet for his guests, and commanded the parrot to sing. However, the parrot remained silent and refused to sing. It refused to eat and died after several days. Wang Bomin from Renshou spoke of this, and composed a poem:

    New songs self-composed to teach the parrot, a jade flute softly played to accompany the singing. If we meet by the side of the Chuihong Bridge, the elegance would surpass that of Xiao Hong.
    What in the world is worth celebrating? Riding a slow donkey to roam the clouds and waters. The gentleman who loves music truly has refined taste, still carrying the memory of Snowy Girl while singing.
    Teaching songs for years, spending countless hours in laborious effort, pearls poured out from the throat. No chance to hear good sounds in Jin Gu.
    Ashamed to sing 'The Song of the Wei River' at a grand banquet, missing someone so much that one gives up life in a single night. The soul of the poet should not cling to a true appreciator, it's hard to convey news of safety from the western border.

    520

    金蚕蛊
    滇中有养蛊家，杀人渔利，利得亦自杀，名曰“金蚕”。大约以端午日，取蛇蝎蟆诸毒物，聚于一器，听其自咬。将尽死，独一物生，则毒之尤者矣。以时饲之，雏匹三年，杂以五色绫锦，裂而饵之。此物最灵，奉之者凡一动一作，皆尊承而不敢稍狎于心，否将不利。暂将日变月化，形遂隐。俾其行毒，必先试一人，若无过客，则以家人当之。中毒，绞毒吐逆，十指如墨，嚼豆不腥，含矾不涩，是其验也。夫而后祈求粮米银钱，无不如意。然按月必蛊一人以为飨蛊者。盖以其粪纳饮食中云。
    宜良章姓夫妇赤贫，三女一子，无以为生，遂蓄一蛊。蛊成，家巨富。尝置厮仆，多夭死。初人不知，后知为蛊，章虽多金，而门致可罗雀。乃设酒肆于通衢，渐亦有侦察之者，解貂人虽过门不入也。蛊之索食甚急，章于此时求之去而不得矣。章大女荷珠已适人，二女莲珠、三女露珠年皆及瓜，咸以蛊故，乡里评旦焉，遂一妁不至。后年馀，其大倩死蛊也。

    520

    The Golden Silkworm

    In Yunnan, there were those who raised silkworms for malevolent purposes, killing people for profit, and even taking their own lives when the profit was made, and these were called "Golden Silkworms." They were typically raised on the Dragon Boat Festival, gathering snakes, scorpions, frogs, and other poisonous creatures into a single container, allowing them to bite each other. When most of them had died, only one creature remained alive, which was the most poisonous of all. This creature was then fed regularly for three years, with colorful silk fabrics torn into pieces and given as food. This creature was extremely intelligent, and those who cared for it had to show utmost respect in every action, avoiding any familiarity or carelessness, for fear of bringing misfortune. Over time, it could change its form and eventually become invisible. To allow it to carry out its poisonous actions, it would first be tested on a single person. If there were no strangers passing by, it would target someone from the household. The symptoms of poisoning included convulsions, vomiting, and nausea, with the victim's fingers turning black. The victim could chew beans without tasting them and hold alum without feeling its bitterness—these were the signs of the creature's poison. Afterward, people would seek wealth or resources, and all their desires would be fulfilled. However, the creature had to be fed monthly by poisoning someone, likely as a ritual offering, with its excrement added to food.

    In Yiliang, there was a poor couple surnamed Zhang, with three daughters and one son, unable to make a living. They decided to raise a Golden Silkworm. After raising it, the family became wealthy. They often employed servants, but many of them died under mysterious circumstances. Initially, people were unaware of the cause, but later it was discovered that the deaths were caused by the creature. Despite their wealth, the Zhang family became so feared that even birds avoided their house. They opened a wine shop in a busy street, but people began to investigate or observe the family, and even wealthy people who passed by the house would not enter. The creature demanded food urgently, and at this point, the Zhang family could no longer find a way to escape or resolve the situation. The eldest daughter, Hezhu, was already married, and the second and third daughters, Lianzhu and Luzhu, were both of marriageable age. Due to the creature, the daughters were criticized or judged by the community, making it difficult for them to find husbands, and no matchmakers came. After some time, the eldest daughter died due to the creature.

    521

    会有楚人毕路者，字蓝峰，贸于滇，为斫苓业，三十而鳏。章欲赘之次女，将以饲蛊。毕不知，遂婚焉。毕见莲珠美而岳多金，窃自喜。独女视毕则点首嗟呀。毕问之，辄不答，久而荷珠、露珠见之亦如是。毕曰：“大姨、小姨，何觌面黯然而神伤也？”亦不答。一日，毕入室醉，女问谁与饮，毕曰：“是大人强以酒。”女惊。逾时曰：“万幸！”毕次日问女，女泣告以蛊故：“今岁蛊将及我。父母爱我不忍割，乃以我为饵，将得汝以代牲也。”毕问计于女，女曰：“盍去诸？”毕曰：“我不去。我死则卿活，我去则卿死。卿既不忍我死，我遂竟去以听卿死，是为不情。不情必有天殃，反不如蛊死之为得也。无已，请就衅焉，我不之悔。”于是女为之百计防检，且若姊妹亦与有维持之力，故章父母不能行其毒。然女实忧之。女欲与之偕去，而父母亦如女之防其蛊之防其去。如是遂皆不安。而章又急思为蛊供。

    Explanation

    521

    A Man from Chu

    There was a man from Chu named Bi Lu, with the courtesy name Lanfeng. He engaged in commerce in Yunnan, specializing in the processing or sale of a type of plant or medicine. At the age of thirty, he became a widower. His father-in-law, a man of wealth, wanted to marry his second daughter to Bi Lu, intending to use her in a ritual involving venomous creatures. Unaware of this, Bi Lu married the girl. He found his wife, Lotus Pearl, beautiful and was pleased by her father's wealth. However, his wife alone looked at him and nodded, lamenting in silence. When Bi Lu asked her why, she did not answer. After some time, his sisters-in-law, Hezhu and Luzhu, also reacted similarly when they saw him.

    Bi Lu asked his sisters-in-law, "Why do you look so gloomy and sad when you see me?" They did not answer. One day, Bi Lu returned home drunk. His wife asked who he had been drinking with. He replied, "It was my father-in-law who forced me to drink." His wife was shocked. Later, she said, "Thankfully, it was not worse!"

    The next day, Bi Lu asked his wife about the incident. She wept and told him the truth: "This year, the ritual will involve me. My parents love me and do not want to harm me, so they plan to use me as bait to get you to take my place in the ritual." Bi Lu asked his wife for advice. She said, "Why don't you leave?" Bi Lu replied, "I will not leave. If I die, you will live; if I leave, you will die. You cannot bear for me to die, so I will leave to let you die, which would be unreasonable. Unreasonable actions will bring divine retribution, and it is better to die by the poison. If there is no other way, please accept the sacrifice, and I will not regret it."

    Thus, the wife took every measure to protect him, and even her sisters-in-law helped in maintaining this protection. Therefore, the father-in-law could not carry out his plan. However, the wife was truly worried. She wanted to leave with him, but her parents also guarded against his departure as they had guarded against the ritual. In this way, both were filled with anxiety. Meanwhile, the father-in-law urgently sought to fulfill the ritual.

    522

    日者偶持笔椠，命毕作一札致人。毕吮笔而书，附之去。女拍案曰：“郎休矣！”毕曰：“无他。”女曰：“含毫濡墨时，我何念不到此耶？”相与痛哭，移时而死，女悲怆甚。遂藁葬于野。夜女私往奠。欻见寒星一点，奕奕来前。女以为燐，近女身则毕也。女惊曰：“汝鬼也？我欲与汝偕行。”毕曰：“卿不必尔。向我死后抵冥司，稽我并非籍中数。我将返舍，又恐岩岩者不相容。姑俟至子日，有新官过境，汝诉之，自能救我。无悲啼也。”

    Explanation

    522

    A diviner once held a brush and tablet, ordering someone named Bi to write a note to another person. Bi carefully wrote the note and sent it off. The woman slammed the table and exclaimed, "My lord is finished!" Bi replied, "There is nothing else." The woman said, "When I held the brush and dipped it in ink, how could I have not thought of this?" They both wept bitterly, and after some time, Bi died. The woman was extremely grief-stricken. She then hastily buried him in the wilderness. At night, the woman secretly went to pay her respects. Suddenly, she saw a cold star, bright and clear, approaching her. She thought it was a ghostly light, but as it drew closer, it was Bi. The woman was startled and said, "Are you a ghost? I want to accompany you." Bi replied, "You do not need to do that. After I died, I went to the underworld, where they checked and found me not to be among the recorded numbers. If I return home, I fear the stern ones will not tolerate me. Wait until the day of the child, when a new official passes through. You can appeal to him, and he will be able to save me. Do not weep."

    523

    如期，昆明令朱某，直隶人，名进士，道遇女。案之其家，实遭金蚕之害，欲去之而不得。令示期往勘，携竹笯，笼两刺猬。入门，令见其屋瓦无纤毫尘土，曰：“是也。”乃启笼，猬出，入其家周遭寻剔，凡榻下、墙孔，稍可匿之处，莫不闻嗅。后至其大厅左柱间，钻穴以下。约三时，两猬擒一虫出，如赤蛇一圈，无头，臂大可围，俗呼绊之绊蛊钏也。乃籍其家，章拘拷掠。其所掠骗毒杀，不可胜计，后死于狱。
    令乃开毕棺验之，尸未损。以瓮莱汁并死蛊烹而灌之，遂苏。女掖之归，肠作痛，泻三日。视其秽，而死蛊大小纠结相缠，如锁子环。毕乃欲携女返楚。女，章之中女也。其大女孀，三女未字，章母悉以委毕，遂皆归里。毕归楚，有三妻焉。君子曰：“终非毕生之幸也，得三妻亦蛊也。”

    Explanation

    523

    On schedule, the magistrate of Kunming, Zhu, a native of Zhili and a named jinshi, encountered a woman on the road. Upon investigating her home, it was discovered that she had indeed suffered from the harm of a golden silkworm, but efforts to remove it were unsuccessful. The magistrate set a date to conduct a thorough investigation and brought along a bamboo cage containing two hedgehogs. Upon entering the house, the magistrate noticed that the roof tiles were free of even the slightest dust and remarked, "This is it." He then opened the cage, and the hedgehogs were released to search the house thoroughly, sniffing and probing every corner, including under the beds, through wall holes, and any place where something might be hidden. They eventually found the malevolent spirit in the space between the left pillar of the main hall, digging down into a hole. After about three hours, the hedgehogs captured a creature that resembled a red snake, without a head, with a body that could be wrapped around something. It was commonly known as a "golden silkworm" or "golden silkworm charm." The magistrate then confiscated the family's property and detained them for interrogation. The family was accused of numerous crimes, including theft, fraud, and poisoning, and ultimately died in prison.

    The magistrate then opened the coffin to examine the body, which was found to be undamaged. He used a medicinal drink made from watercress and the dead malevolent spirit to revive the woman. The woman returned home but experienced severe intestinal pain and diarrhea for three days. Upon examination, it was found that the dead malevolent spirits were intertwined like a chain. The magistrate then wanted to take the woman back to Chu. The woman was the second daughter of the family named Zhang. Her eldest daughter was widowed, and her third daughter was unmarried. The mother entrusted all her daughters to the magistrate, and they all returned home. The magistrate returned to Chu and married three wives. One might say, "Ultimately, it was not fortunate for him, as getting three wives was also a form of witchcraft."

    524

    猴诉
    潮州刺史署，大门槛柱皆刻木猴而饰，不知其故。古梅杨夫子告余曰：先是，市中有蓄猴丐者，豫章人，飘零韩水。尝养一猴，教傀儡铃索，以给朝夕。食则与猴共器，寝则与猴共处。村烟墟雨，凄其之况。怜猴者丐，而知丐者猴，两两相依，知己正在不言之表。丐有赢馀，积餽箱中，猴若为守虏者然。一日，有无赖丐扳饮，猴见之，即变面作吼，怒形声色。丐斥之，回顾指画，若识其不可与接者。
    丐固耽曲糵，一杯在手，便刺刺成心腹交。后二丐寝处合一，猴终不释然。尝同往村落戏，乞馀钱，则二丐卯饮熏熏。从此丐亦不复更有馀资也。每日牵担同行。忽至一郊原，前后市廛较远，山凹松杉，蔽翳道左。二人同行，无赖丐袖石扑丐，丐应声中颅而仆，复掣担连挥数十，丐遂殒。

    Explanation

    524

    Monkey's Lawsuit

    In the governor's office of Chaozhou, the threshold and pillars were adorned with carved wooden monkeys, but the reason for this decoration was unknown. Mr. Yang of Gu Mei told me: Previously, there was a beggar in the marketplace who kept a monkey. He was from Yuzhang and had wandered the Han River. He once raised a monkey, teaching it puppetry and acrobatics to provide for his daily needs. He ate from the same dishes as the monkey and slept in the same place. The scene was desolate, with smoke rising from villages and rain falling on ruins, evoking a sense of loneliness and hardship. The beggar pitied the monkey, and the monkey understood the beggar, the two relying on each other, their friendship unspoken but profound. The beggar saved his surplus in a box, and the monkey acted as a guardian. One day, a lazy beggar joined them for a drink. The monkey saw him and immediately changed its expression, roaring in anger, its fury evident in its voice and demeanor. The beggar scolded the monkey, turning back to gesture, as if recognizing that the lazy beggar was not to be trusted.

    The beggar was fond of alcohol, and with a cup in hand, he quickly formed a close bond with the lazy beggar. Later, the two beggars began to sleep and eat together, but the monkey remained uneasy. They often went to villages together, where the beggar would beg for extra money, and the two beggars would drink until they were drunk. From then on, the beggar no longer had any surplus. Every day, they walked together, carrying their burdens. Suddenly, they reached a remote suburban area, with shops and houses far behind, and pines and cypresses hidden in the mountain hollows, obscuring the path. As they walked together, the lazy beggar secretly took a stone and struck the beggar, hitting him on the head and causing him to fall. The lazy beggar then swung his burden repeatedly, striking the beggar dozens of times, and the beggar died.

    525

    猴乘隙断锁缘松顶，无赖丐恨指猴曰：“毛团狡甚，幸生汝！”乃掘浮土瘗其尸，荷担而去。盖其醉后曾告其箱有储也。无赖去远，猴下树悲鸣欲绝。入村人户中，长跪凄凄，俯首堕泪，人与之食，食毕复号，又去他村如前村状。人习而怜之，皆不忍羁系，听其往来。暂随乡人入城市，市人始异之，继亦怜而饲之，人终不知其故。会太守出舆过，猴忽拦舆嘶号，若有所指。隶人鞭扑，猴嘶益厉。守止之曰：“毋！”令人随之。猴悲而先导，人止，则猴若招之状。十里许，至松间浮土处，旋绕捶胸如躄踊。隶标，返告诸守。守诣其地，挖而见尸，猴哀不胜。验毕，返署，而杀人者毫无踪迹。

    Explanation

    525

    The monkey seized the opportunity to break the lock and climbed to the top of the pine tree. The rascal beggar, filled with hatred, pointed at the monkey and said, "The furry ball is very cunning, fortunately you were born!" Then he dug up loose soil to bury the monkey's body and shouldered his burden to leave. It was because, after drinking, he had mentioned that there was something valuable in the box. The beggar went far away, and the monkey descended from the tree, crying out in grief, almost to the point of death. He entered a village and knelt for a long time in a pitiful state, bowing his head and shedding tears. People gave him food, and after eating, he cried again, then went to another village in the same manner as before. People became accustomed to his behavior and pitied him, so they did not restrict his movements, allowing him to come and go as he pleased. Occasionally, he followed the villagers into the city, where the city dwellers initially found him strange but later pitied and fed him. No one knew the reason behind his actions. One day, when the magistrate passed by in his carriage, the monkey suddenly blocked the carriage and cried out, as if pointing to something. The servants beat him, and the monkey's cries grew louder. The magistrate stopped them and said, "No!" He ordered someone to follow the monkey. The monkey, filled with sorrow, led the way, and when people stopped, the monkey seemed to be beckoning them. After traveling about ten miles, they arrived at a spot among the pines where loose soil had been dug up. The monkey circled and pounded his chest, as if jumping up and down in grief. The servant marked the spot and returned to report to the magistrate. The magistrate went to the location, dug up the area, and found the body. The monkey's sorrow was overwhelming. After the inspection, the magistrate returned to his office, but the murderer left no trace.

    526

    守素神明，亦一时计无所出。即牵猴问之，猴不能言。守沉思之曰：“古人覆盆之下，尚为雪冤。况尸证在前，凶身岂难缉获？因类以求，缘情而起。”遂呼吏胥于附近会赛处牵猴纵往，听其到。
    一月之间，而无赖丐以丐馀资又弄一猴，即以是猴之箱之傀儡之铃索而招摇于市。猴见眦裂，前攫，豕啼而人跃，爪牙交错于丐人衣履之间，捕者就而缚焉。无赖丐曰：“我猴戏者，何冤我？”捕曰：“有戏猴冤者，故及汝。”絷至庭，一讯而服，罪以抵。太守令牵猴至前，问之曰：“汝仇报矣。盍归乎山林？”猴乃取向时傀儡衣衣之，冠冠之，如人鞠躬俯伏毕，复登大门揭阳楼之顶，长号数声，坠地以死。太守哀之，郡人义之，葬于揭阳楼下。故至今槛角楼头，不饰以狮象而猴之者，形其义也。
    (按：王慎旃《圣师录》中志汪学使尹金华，一猴诉冤，与此相类。)

    Explanation

    526

    The Wise Official and the Monkey

    The wise official, Shou Su, was also momentarily at a loss for what to do. He immediately took the monkey and asked it questions, but the monkey could not speak. Reflecting deeply, Shou Su said, "Even in ancient times, when one was hidden like a pot, they could still clear their name. How much more so when there is evidence before us? It is not difficult to apprehend the culprit. Seeking by analogy and acting according to the situation, we shall find the truth."

    He then called the local officials to a nearby market and released the monkey, observing its actions. Within a month, a vagrant beggar, using the money he earned from begging, bought another monkey and used the box, puppets, and strings to perform in the market. The monkey saw this and became enraged, making pig-like sounds and jumping around, causing people to jump in surprise. Its claws and teeth interlocked with the beggar's clothes and shoes, and the officials captured the vagrant.

    The vagrant said, "I am just a monkey performer. Why am I being blamed?" The official replied, "There is a monkey performer who has committed a crime, and thus you are implicated." The vagrant was brought to court, confessed, and was punished by death.

    The official then ordered the monkey to be brought forward and asked, "Your enemy has been avenged. Why not return to the mountains and forests?" The monkey took the puppet's clothes and hat, dressed itself like a human, bowed deeply, and then climbed to the top of the city hall, let out a long cry, and fell to the ground, dying.

    The official mourned the monkey, and the people of the county honored it, burying it at the base of the city hall. Therefore, even today, the corners of the city hall are adorned with monkeys rather than lions or elephants, embodying the moral lesson of the story.

    (As recorded in Wang Shen Zhan's Records of the Sage Teacher, there is a similar story of Official Wang Yu of Yinzhou, where a monkey sought justice.)

    527

    鹰
    泰安人李坦，性好鹰。之岱峪悬崖自缒，取鹰雏。将至巢而绳绝，落树岐间，上下皆壁立，进退维谷。大鹰见人，衔肉不敢至巢，遥放肉下，坦即取肉饲雏，馀者自充。越五六十日，雏能振翼，乃裂裳系雏鹰足，鹰飞掣其臂，比至涧底，一无所伤，仍絷鹰而归。或曰“放焉，将以报德”，坦乃脱鞲使去。

    狍
    博山西关李氏，家蓄一狍，最训，见人则呦呦鸣，或作抵角状。其家门外皆山，狍有时出，至暮必归，若牛羊之下来。属当秋祭，例用鹿，官督猎者急无所获。狍似鹿，短小而肉角，乃向李氏求之，李氏不与，狍亦如故。祭有日矣，猎者固请不已，李氏迟疑曰：“君且休，姑徐徐。”其日，狍去遂不归。
    (七如氏曰：万物愚于人，人愚于天。顾人发杀机，物或知之。天发杀机，人鲜知也。彼死权、死宠、死于声利，祸在燃眉，身罹阱坎，尚谓彼仍爱我，迷而不悟者众矣。况几先祸始动于萌蘖之间，隔于视听之表乎？呜呼，此狍智于临江之麋远甚！)

    Explanation

    527

    鹰

    A man from Taian named Li Tan had a fondness for eagles. He descended into the cliffs of Mount Tai's ravines by tying himself and lowering himself down, to capture a young eagle. Just as he was about to reach the nest, his rope broke, and he fell into a crevice between two trees, with sheer cliffs above and below, leaving him in a difficult situation where he could neither advance nor retreat. A large eagle, seeing a person, carried meat but dared not approach the nest. From a distance, it dropped the meat, and Li Tan immediately took the meat to feed the young eagle, using the remainder for himself. After about fifty or sixty days, the young eagle was able to flap its wings, so Li Tan tore a piece of his clothing to tie the young eagle's feet. As the eagle flew, it pulled on his arm, but by the time they reached the bottom of the ravine, there was not a single injury. He then tied the eagle and returned. Someone said, "Release it, to repay your kindness," and Li Tan released the eagle.

    狍

    In the western outskirts of Boxing Mountain, the Li family kept a roe deer that was very well-trained. It would make a soft "yōu yōu" sound when it saw people and sometimes assume a posture as if to butt heads. The area outside their home was all mountainous, and the roe deer would occasionally go out, but always returned by dusk, like a herd of cattle or sheep. It was time for the autumn sacrifice, and it was customary to use a deer. The official overseeing the hunt was in a hurry but found no deer. The roe deer looked like a deer, being short and small with meat-like horns, so they asked the Li family for it, but the Li family refused, and the roe deer continued as before. The day of the sacrifice arrived, and the hunters persisted in asking, so the Li family hesitated and said, "Please rest, let's take it slowly." On that day, the roe deer left and did not return.

    (Seven Like said: All things are dull to people, and people are dull to heaven. But when people release the killing mechanism, things may know it. When heaven releases the killing mechanism, people rarely know it. Those who die from power, those who die from favor, those who die from fame and profit—disaster is at the eyebrows, the body falls into traps and pits. They still say that they still love me, but those who are confused and do not understand are many. How much more so when the initial signs of disaster move in the early stages and are beyond the scope of sight and hearing? Alas, this roe deer is much wiser than the deer by the river!)

    528

    鱼跃
    饶州商人某，过鄱阳湖。见网户得一大鱼，重百馀斤。渔人索银一两，如数买之，投湖中。至越月，商人挟资归，夜过鄱阳，遇盗登其舟，移至芦苇中，劫其资，将刃而甘心焉。忽一鱼跳入横舱间，奋鬣扬鳞，泼剌格盗，盗刃不能伤。俄顷捕巡船至，闻苇中喧哄，就而盗获，鱼亦跃入江中。商因忆救鱼之事，今报德云。此康熙三十六年七月事。

    小虾子
    有楚客贸于象郡，夏月独行山箐间。山多大树，阴翳道左，皆不知名。偶憩坐树根，闻石罅泉涓涓响，倦方欲睡，又听树杪如蜂闹蝇薨。举首视有物皆如婴儿，首类小豆，身不满寸，互相牵附，续续垂下，百十为群，皆撒手饮涧畔。有沃面者，漱齿者，相荡为戏者，濯足者。观其具体虽微，而动作罔不犹人。客异，起近之，皆仓皇窜。遂手捉焉，如扑蝶蜥，共得男女老幼一十五人，置食簏中。

    Explanation

    528

    Translation

    Fish Leap

    A merchant from Raozhou passed through Poyang Lake. He saw a fisherman who had caught a large fish, weighing over a hundred pounds. The fisherman asked for one tael of silver, which the merchant paid and then released the fish back into the lake. After a month, the merchant returned home with his wealth, passing through Poyang at night. He encountered thieves who boarded his boat, moved it to the reed beds, and robbed him of his wealth, intending to kill him. Suddenly, a fish leaped into the cabin, flinging its fins and scales, splashing and fighting against the thieves, whose knives could not harm it. Shortly after, a patrol boat arrived, hearing the commotion in the reeds. The thieves were caught, and the fish also leaped into the river. The merchant remembered the incident of saving the fish and now saw it as a repayment of virtue. This occurred in July of the 36th year of the Kangxi Emperor's reign.

    Little Shrimp

    A guest from Chu was trading in Xiang Jun during the summer. He was walking alone through the mountains. The mountains were covered with large trees, and the shade covered the left side of the road. All the trees were unfamiliar to him. He happened to rest sitting at the base of a tree, hearing the sound of water dripping from a rock crevice. He was tired and about to sleep when he also heard the sound of bees and flies at the top of the tree. He looked up and saw things that looked like babies, with heads like small beans and bodies less than an inch long. They were hanging down one after another, clinging to each other, in groups of tens or hundreds. All of them let go and drank at the streamside. Some were washing their faces, some brushing their teeth, some playing together, and some washing their feet. Observing that despite their small size, their actions were all like those of humans, the guest was surprised. He got up and approached them, and they all panicked and fled. Then he caught them with his hands, like catching butterflies and lizards, and caught fifteen people of all ages and genders. He placed them in a food basket.

    529

    宿店出示，土人识之，呼为“都”，又名“小虾”，可烹而食。客爱之，饲以谷水，亦能饮啄。其一二白发髯者，多不食死。男女一日必三配合，视其状与人无异。以箸拨之则开，然亦不能诞育。一月之后，只剩一男二女。因隔其簏，使不得通。又审其左腋下皆有红点，突起如痣。后携归楚，冬寒不禁，为之制衣，辄啮去。铺毛絮伏暖具中，终以僵死。今其遗蜕尚存，视之如海菜中美人蛏干之类。
    《双槐岁钞》、《诺皋记》所载，信不诬也。因续小语数言以赠云：“么蔑之子，难形为象。晨登蚁埃，薄暮不上。朝炊半粒，昼复得酿。烹一小虱，饱于乡党。”

    卷十六 杂记

    Explanation

    529

    At an inn, the creature was presented, and the locals recognized it, calling it "Dū" and also naming it "small shrimp," as it could be cooked and eaten. The guest loved it, fed it with grain water, and it could drink and peck. Among them, one or two with white hair and beards often died without eating. Males and females must mate three times a day, and observing its appearance, it was not different from humans. When prodded with a chopstick, it would open, but it could not give birth. After a month, only one male and two females remained. They separated them in a basket to prevent them from mating. They also examined and found red spots on their left armpits, which were raised like moles. Later, they took it back to Chu, but it could not withstand the winter cold. They made clothes for it, but it would bite them off. They placed it in a warm bed with down and wool, but it eventually froze to death. Now its molted shell remains, which looks like a sea vegetable or a dried scallop.

    As recorded in Shuanghuai Suichao and Nuogao Ji, it is indeed true. Therefore, I add a few words as a gift: "The offspring of the tiny creature, hard to describe in form. It climbs onto ant hills in the morning but does not return by evening. It cooks half a grain in the morning and brews again during the day. Cooking a small flea, it satisfies its village."

    Volume Sixteen, Miscellaneous Notes

    530

    郑板桥
    板桥郑燮，兴化人也。康熙秀才，雍正举人，乾隆进士。工诗，有别裁。善画兰竹。精书法，隶草相杂，号“六分半书”。观者谓其创，而实则因钟繇碑而广之，唐时已有草隶之说，此类是也。
    性倜傥，好为苟难奇僻之行，又尝不矜小节，洒洒然狂达自放。如板桥者，使之班清华，选玉堂，摛词绘藻，相与鼓吹休明，岂不甚善？奈之何加以民社之任，颠倒于簿书鞅掌中哉！呜呼！造物生才不偶，有才者不能见用，用矣又违其才，均可惜也。
    后出宰范邑，自范而潍，每多废事。莅任之初，署中墙壁悉令人挖孔百十，以通于街。人问之，曰：“出前官恶习俗气耳。”郑素有馀桃癖。一日听事，见阶下一小皂隶执板遥立，带红牙帽，面白衣黑，颇觉动人，遂见爱嬖。有友戏问曰；“侮人者恒受侮于人。使其行反噬之谋，倒戈而相向焉，何以御之？”郑曰：“斯受之耳，亦未必其血流漂杵也。”其书室一联最可笑，云：“诗酒图书画，银钱屁股屄。”

    Explanation

    530

    Zheng Banqiao
    Zheng Banqiao, also known as Zheng Xie, was from Xinghua. He was a scholar during the Kangxi era, a successful candidate in the Yongzheng era, and a Jinshi (imperial scholar) during the Qianlong era. He was skilled in poetry, with a unique style. He was adept at painting orchids and bamboo. He was excellent in calligraphy, mixing regular script and cursive, and was known as "Six and a Half Points Script." Observers thought he had invented this style, but in fact, he expanded on the styles of Zhong Yao's steles. During the Tang Dynasty, there was already a concept of mixed cursive and regular script, which is this style.

    He had a free-spirited nature and enjoyed eccentric and unconventional behavior. He also did not care about minor details and acted freely and wildly. If someone like Banqiao were appointed to high office, he would write elegant and ornate prose, contributing to the flourishing of culture and virtue. Why was he given the responsibility of a local official, where he was overwhelmed by administrative duties? Alas! It is a pity that talented people are not given opportunities, or if they are used, they are not allowed to use their talents fully.

    Afterwards, he served as the magistrate of Fan County, and later in Weifang, often neglecting his duties. When he first took office, he had people dig hundreds of holes in the walls of his office to connect them to the street. When asked why, he said, "To get rid of the bad customs and habits of the previous official." Zheng Banqiao had a fondness for leftover peaches. One day, while presiding over court, he saw a low-ranking clerk standing far away with a board, wearing a red toothed hat and having a white face with black hair, which he found quite appealing, and thus he favored him. A friend jokingly asked, "Those who mock others often end up being mocked themselves. If he plans to turn the tables and attack you, how will you defend yourself?" Zheng replied, "I will just accept it, and it won't necessarily lead to blood flowing in the trenches." The couplet in his study room is the most amusing, saying, "Poetry, wine, books, paintings, and drawings; silver, money, and vulgar talk."

    531

    邑之崇仁寺与大悲庵相对，有寺僧私尼，为地邻觉，缚之官。郑见僧尼年齿相若，令其还俗配为夫妇。有诗云：
    一半葫芦一半瓢，合来一处好成桃。从今入定风规寂，此后敲门月影遥。
    鸟性悦时空即色，莲花落处静偏娇。是谁勾却风流案，记取当年郑板桥。
    又盐店商送一私贩求惩，郑见其人蓝缕，非枭徒，乃谓曰：“尔求责扑，吾为尔枷示之何如？”商首肯。郑即令役取芦席，编成一“枷”，高八尺，阔一丈，剪成一孔，令贩进首带之。郑于堂上取纸十馀张，用判笔悉画兰竹，淋漓挥洒，顷刻而就，命皆贴枷上，押赴盐店，树塞其门。观者如堵，终日杂沓，若闭门市。浃辰，商大窘，苦哀郑，郑乃笑而释之。
    郑尝因公晋省，各上司皆器重之。一日，会宴趵突泉，属诗于郑，郑应作曰：
    原原有本岂徒然，静里观澜感逝川。流到海边浑是卤，更谁人辨识清泉。

    Explanation

    531

    In the city, the Chongren Temple stood opposite the Great Compassion Temple. A monk from the temple had an affair with a nun, and the local neighbor discovered and reported it to the authorities. Zheng Banqiao, upon seeing that the monk and nun were of similar age, ordered them to return to secular life and marry as a couple. There is a poem about this event:

    Half a gourd and half a ladle, when combined, form a perfect peach. From now on, the rules of discipline will be silent, and after this, the moon's shadow will be distant when knocking on the door.
    The bird's nature is pleased when space and time are immediate, and the lotus falls where it is still and delicate. Who was it that entangled in this affair of passion? Remember the day of Zheng Banqiao.

    Additionally, a salt store merchant brought a private trader to be punished. Zheng Banqiao saw that the man was worn and tattered, not a bandit, and said to him, "If you seek punishment, I will have you publicly displayed in a wooden collar. What do you think?" The merchant agreed. Zheng immediately ordered his men to take reed mats and weave a wooden collar, eight feet tall and one zhang wide, with a hole cut in it, and made the trader put it on. Zheng took more than ten sheets of paper from the hall, used a judge's brush to draw orchids and bamboo, painting freely and abundantly, finishing in a moment. He ordered that all of them be pasted on the wooden collar and took the trader to the salt store, blocking the door with the collar. Spectators gathered like a wall, all day long bustling, as if the market was closed. After twelve days, the merchant was in great distress and pleaded with Zheng. Zheng then smiled and released him.

    Zheng Banqiao once went to the provincial capital on official business, and all the superiors esteemed him. One day, at a banquet at Baotu Spring, they asked Zheng to write a poem. Zheng responded with:

    There must be a reason and a basis, not in vain. In quiet contemplation, observing the waves and feeling the passing river. Flowing to the sea, it becomes brine. Who can distinguish the clear spring water?

    532

    诗成，满座拂然，佥谓郑讪诽上台。后因邑中有罚某人金事，控发，遂以贪婪褫职。嘻，板桥非百里才也，其贾祸以才故，而乃诬之以贝，冤矣！
    当其去潍之日，止用驴子三头。其一板桥自乘，垫以铺陈；其一驮两书夹板，上横担阮弦一具；其一则小皂隶而娈童者，骑以前导。板桥则风帽毡衣出大堂，揖新令尹，据鞍而告之曰：“我郑燮以婪败，今日归装，若是其轻而且简。诸君子力踞清流，雅操相尚，行见上游器重，指顾莺迁。倘异日去潍之际，其无忘郑大之泊也。”言罢，跨蹇，郎当以行。
    后寓维扬，以书画称，搢绅争为延誉，名重一时。有李秀才寄赠一联，首句云：“三绝诗书画。”板桥按纸沉思其下联不得，既而启视云：“一官归去来。”最妙。又有名幕某一诗，诮板桥亦佳。记其末二句云：“如何乞食天宁寺，不唱莲花唱竹枝？”盖以板桥有扬州竹枝百首，颇涉诮让，又自认为郑元和之后裔也。

    Explanation

    532

    The poem was completed, and everyone in the audience was displeased, all believing that Zheng was mocking and criticizing the higher authorities. Later, because of a case in the county where someone was fined a sum of money, the matter was brought to light, and Zheng was dismissed from office due to accusations of greed. Alas, Zheng Banqiao was not a talent fit for a hundred-li county, yet he brought disaster upon himself because of his talent, and then was falsely accused of being greedy for wealth, which is unjust!

    On the day he left Weifang, he only used three donkeys. One was for Zheng himself to ride, padded with bedding; one carried two book covers, with a lute on top; the third was a small servant and a young boy, riding ahead to lead the way. Zheng then came out of the main hall wearing a wind hat and felt coat, bowing to the new magistrate. Sitting on his saddle, he said to him, "I, Zheng Xie, failed due to corruption. Today, my return is so light and simple. May you not forget Zheng's simplicity." After saying this, he mounted his donkey and rode away with a jingling sound.

    Later, he settled in Weiyang, where he was known for his paintings and calligraphy. The gentry competed to promote his reputation, and he was famous for a time. There was a scholar named Li who sent a couplet, with the first line saying, "Three masterpieces in poetry, painting, and calligraphy." Zheng thought about the lower couplet but could not find it. Later, he saw it and it read, "A retired official returns home." It was the most wonderful. There was also a famous poet who sent a poem criticizing Zheng, which was also good. The last two lines of the poem said, "Why beg at Tianning Temple, instead of singing lotus flowers, sing bamboo branches?" This was because Zheng had written a hundred bamboo branch poems in Yangzhou, which involved some criticism, and he also considered himself a descendant of Zheng Yuanhe.

    533

    郑有印章数十方。如“橄榄轩”、“七品官耳”、“鹧鸪”、“二十年前旧板桥”，皆别致，大半吾乡朱文震所刻。其诗钞、词钞、家书、小唱，皆手自书之。其门人司徒文膏镂板亦精。又附“道情”数阕于左：
    老渔翁，一钓竿，靠山厓，傍水湾，扁舟来往无牵绊。沙鸥点点轻波远，荻港萧萧白昼寒，高歌一曲斜阳晚。一霎时波翻金影，蓦抬头月上东山。
    老樵夫，自砍柴，捆青松，夹绿槐，茫茫野草秋山外。丰碑是处成荒冢，华表千寻卧碧苔，坟前石马磨刀坏。倒不如闲钱沽酒，醉醺醺山径归来。
    老头陀，古庙中，自烧香，自打钟，兔葵燕麦闲斋供。山门破落无关锁，斜日苍黄有乱松，秋星闪烁颓垣缝。黑漆漆蒲团打坐，夜烧茶炉火通红。
    水田衣，老道人，背葫芦，戴袱巾，棕鞋布袜相厮称。修琴卖药般般会，捉鬼拿妖件件能。白云红叶归山径。闻说道悬崖结屋，却教人何处相寻？

    Explanation

    533

    Zhen possesses dozens of seals. Among them are inscriptions such as "Ganlan轩," "a seventh-rank official," "partridge," and "an old bridge from twenty years ago," all of which are unique, most of them carved by Zhu Wen Zhen from my hometown. His poetry collections, song collections, family letters, and short songs, all written by hand. His disciple Simu Wengao's engraved boards are also exquisite. Additionally, several pieces of "Daoqing" (religious songs) are appended below:

    An old fisherman, with one fishing rod, leaning against the mountain cliff, beside the water bend, a small boat coming and going without any hindrance. Sandpipers dot the light waves far away, the reed port is desolate in the cold daylight, singing a song at dusk. In a moment, the waves turn golden, suddenly looking up, the moon rises over the eastern mountain.

    An old woodcutter, cutting his own firewood, binding green pines, carrying green locusts, the vast wild grass beyond the autumn mountains. Monuments everywhere become deserted graves, the tall pillars lie covered in green moss, the stone horses in front of the tomb are worn down by the grindstone. It's better to spend idle money on wine, returning home drunk along the mountain path.

    An old monk, in an ancient temple, burning incense, ringing the bell, the rabbit and sparrow grass serving as offerings. The temple gate is dilapidated, no lock or bolt, the setting sun casts a pale light on the tangled pines, autumn stars flicker through the cracks in the ruined walls. Darkly, sitting on a meditation cushion, the night tea stove's fire burns bright red.

    A water field robe, an old Taoist, carrying a gourd, wearing a headscarf, the brown shoes and cloth socks match well. Skilled in repairing musical instruments and selling medicine, capable of catching ghosts and demons. White clouds and red leaves return along the mountain path, hearing that a house is built on a cliff, it teaches people where to find it.

    534

    老书生，白屋中，说黄虞，道古风，许多后辈高科中。门前仆从雄如虎，陌上旌旗去似龙。一朝势若成春梦。倒不如蓬门僻巷，教几个小小蒙童。
    尽风流，小乞儿，数莲花，唱竹枝，千门打鼓沿街市。桥边日出犹酣睡，山外斜阳已早归，残杯冷炙饶滋味。醉倒在回廊古庙，一凭他雨打风吹。
    掩柴扉，怕出头，剪西风，菊径秋，看看又是重阳后。几行衰草迷山郭，一片残阳下酒楼。栖鸦点上萧萧柳，撮几句盲词瞎话，交还他铁板歌喉。
    邈唐虞，远夏殷，卷宗周，入暴秦，争雄七国相兼并。文章两汉空陈迹，金粉南朝总废尘，李唐赵宋慌忙尽。最可叹龙盘虎踞，尽消磨《燕子》《春灯》。
    吊龙逢，哭比干，羡庄周，拜老聃。未央宫里王孙惨。南来薏苡徒兴叹，七尺珊瑚祇自残。孔明枉作英雄汉，早知道茅芦高卧，省多少六出祈山。

    Explanation

    534

    An Elderly Scholar in a Humble Abode, Discussing the Virtues of Ancient Rulers and the Styles of the Past, Many Younger Generations Achieve High Honors. The Servants Before the Gate Are as Strong as Tigers, and the Banners Along the Streets Like Dragons. One Day, the Power and Glory Will Fade Like a Spring Dream. It Is Better to Live in a Humble Lane and Teach a Few Young Children.

    Fully Embodied in Elegance, a Young Beggar Counts Lotus Flowers and Sings Bamboo Songs, Many Doors Beat Drums Along the Streets. By the Bridge, the Sun Rises While Someone Still Sleeps; Beyond the Mountains, the Slanting Sun Has Already Set. Leftover Food and Cold Dishes Still Have Flavor. Drunkenly Leaning Against an Old Temple Corridor, Letting the Rain and Wind Blow.

    Hiding Behind the Wooden Door, Afraid to Show Oneself, Cutting Through the West Wind, Walking in a Path of Chrysanthemums in Autumn. Soon It Will Be After the Double Ninth Festival. A Few Lines of Withered Grass Obscure the Mountain Town, a Setting Sun Descends on the Wine Shop. Perching Crows Mark the Desolate Willows, a Few Lines of Blind Words and Nonsense, Given to His Iron Plate Singing Voice.

    Far Back to the Era of Emperor Yao and Shun, and the Xia and Shang Dynasties, Through the Western Zhou Dynasty, Into the Qin Dynasty, Seven Warring States Vying for Supremacy. The Literary Achievements of the Han Dynasties Are Now Mere Traces, the Opulence of the Southern Dynasties Has Turned to Dust, the Tang and Song Dynasties Hurriedly Came to an End. Most Pitiable Is the Dragon Coiling and Tiger Crouching, All Consumed by the Yanzi and Chun Deng (Literary Works).

    Lamenting Long Feng and Crying for Bi Gan, Admiring Zhuang Zhou and Venerating Lao Dan (Laozi). In the Weiyang Palace, the Young Nobles Are in Sorrow. The Southward Journey of the Young Nobles Is Only for Sighs, the Seven-Foot Coral Is Only for Self-Destruction. Zhuge Liang in Vain Became a Hero, If He Had Known to Rest in a Thatched Cottage, He Would Have Saved Himself Many Expeditions to Mount Qishan.

    535

    拨琵琶，续续弹，叹庸愚，惊懦顽，四条弦上多哀怨。黄沙白草无人迹，古戍寒云乱鸟还，虞罗惯打孤飞雁。收拾了渔樵事业，任从他风雪关山。
    风流家世元和老，旧事翻新调，扯碎状元袍，脱却乌纱帽，俺唱这道情儿归山去了。

    朱高安
    朱轼，字可亭。巡抚浙江，有美政，外宽内严，以礼自律，复以礼律人。故有一檄而吏神明奉之，有一教而民父母依之，响应皆若枹鼓。虽往往有矫正之弊，人不以为非。
    一日，途中见嫁女者极华盛，朱公问之，曰：“秀才某妻也。”朱命彩舆移入节署，直达内堂。新妇出，见一老妇，钗荆裙布，方桔槔灌地，自菜畦来。令新妇入室，琴书外了无长物。妇谓新妇曰：“我起居八座，尚安粗粝。汝冬烘家，何奢侈乃尔？大人令汝进署，将以观我型，庶几训汝身也。”新妇谢而出。后归夫家，果能相夫成名，封淑人。

    Explanation

    535

    Plucking the pipa, continuing to play, lamenting the mediocre and foolish, shocking the timid and stubborn, on the four strings there is much sorrow and resentment. Yellow sand and white grass with no human tracks, ancient fortresses and cold clouds with birds returning chaotically, the虞罗 (a type of net) is accustomed to catching lone flying geese. Gathering up the fishing and woodcutting business, leaving the wind, snow, and mountains behind. A noble and elegant family from the Yuanhe era, old stories retold in new tunes, tearing apart the imperial robe of the top scholar, shedding the black cap, I sing this way of feeling and return to the mountains.

    Zhu Gaoan
    Zhu Shi, courtesy name Ke Ting. As governor of Zhejiang, he implemented beautiful governance, outwardly lenient and inwardly strict, self-disciplined by propriety, and disciplining others by propriety. Therefore, with one edict, officials serve like gods and spirits; with one teaching, the people rely on him like children on their parents; the response is as quick as a drumbeat. Although there are often corrective flaws, people do not consider them wrong.
    One day, on the road, he saw a woman being married with great splendor. Zhu asked about it, and was told, "She is the wife of Scholar So-and-so." Zhu ordered the colorful sedan chair to be moved into the provincial governor's residence, directly to the inner hall. The bride came out, saw an old woman, wearing a simple cap and a plain skirt, using a pulley to water the ground, coming from the vegetable plot. He ordered the bride to enter the room, which had only a zither and books, nothing else. The old woman said to the bride, "I, who live in a high-ranking official's residence, still eat coarse food. You, from a family that is not well-off, why are you so extravagant? The elder has ordered you to come to the office, so you can see my example, and perhaps learn from it." The bride thanked her and left. Later, when she returned to her husband's home, she indeed helped her husband achieve success and was posthumously honored as a virtuous lady.

    536

    杭俗无论贫富，妇女游春湖上，必不可已。虽父不能禁女，夫不能禁妻，盖沿习使然。朱公严禁之，闻其事阳奉而阴违焉。一日，朱公驰卫至西湖净慈寺，坐山门外，察寺中妇女百计，公选健僧百人驮之出。说者谓朱公：“此举大不近情，百人中岂无耻以自尽者？”而竟不然。数日后，但闻闺中语曰：“朱辣利好恶谑也。”公江西人，“辣利”，俗呼秃也。
    会郡亢阳，自夏徂秋，井泉涸竭，佥曰：“大人请诣天竺，迎大士入城，乃雨。”公曰：“大士不知何许人？又不知何如神？既曰菩萨，当必普救众生，何庸以一请为荣耶？”不许。郡人莫之为计。有道人许姓，能符术厌胜之道。从京师来，夤缘出入宫掖，遂号真人。适至杭郡，人曰真人至，旱魃不敢为灾矣。暨请，公敬礼之。公曰：“为民请命，苟有利死生以之，况区区下礼之微乎？但恐未必然也。”不得已，具旛盖，亲为控引，而道士骄恣傲慢。既至坛所，盛设供帐，自旦至夕，公立坛下。

    Explanation

    536

    The customs of Hangzhou are such that regardless of wealth or poverty, women must visit the lake in spring. This custom is so deeply ingrained that it cannot be stopped, even by fathers who cannot prevent their daughters or husbands who cannot stop their wives. Zhu, a government official, attempted to ban this practice, but people paid lip service while secretly disobeying. One day, Zhu rode out to the West Lake and sat outside the gates of the Jingci Temple. He selected a hundred strong monks to carry the women out. Some said that Zhu's actions were very good or very bad, depending on one's perspective. However, this was not the case.
